\"title\", \"description\", \"content\", \"author\", \"url\", \"guid\", \"media\", \"attachments\"\nparams: 163778663386687488,1211296081845936128,🧠 MLPs are Hebbian Memories: A Simple Recipe for Fact-Storing Transformers,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2026-07-22-mlps-are-hebbians,<h2 id=\"user-content--tldr\">⚡ TL;DR</h2>\n<p><strong>It sounds like sci-fi, but it's real: we can <em>instantly</em> build knowledge into a Transformer block, no training required!</strong></p>\n<p><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-28-mlps-p1\" target=\"_blank\">Previously</a>, we showed that MLPs can be constructed to store facts. Since then, we found a much simpler and more powerful explanation: a Transformer MLP is naturally a <a href=\"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hopfield_network\" target=\"_blank\"><em>Hebbian memory</em></a>. This view lets us develop the first closed-form <strong>fact-storing MLP construction</strong>, requiring no gradient descent 🤯, that <strong>packs facts at the information-theoretically optimal rate</strong> and is <strong>usable within Transformer blocks for factual recall</strong>!</p>\n<p><strong>Full paper team:</strong> Roberto Garcia*, Jerry Liu*, Ronny Junkins*, Sabri Eyuboglu, Atri Rudra, Chris Ré</p>\n<p>📄 <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.10034\" target=\"_blank\">Paper</a><br>\n💻 <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/hebbian-mlps\" target=\"_blank\">GitHub</a></p>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2026-07-08-mlps-are-hebbians/mlp-robot.png\" alt=\"The Hazy Research Bot constructing a fact-storing MLP\"><p><em>The Hazy Research Bot Constructing a Fact-Storing MLP.</em></p></div>\n<details><summary><strong>Beyond efficient fact-storing MLPs</strong> (click to expand!)</summary><p>Excitingly, our construction's impact goes beyond efficiently building MLPs! This post focuses on the MLP construction and its storage capacity, but in <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.10034\" target=\"_blank\">our paper</a> we discuss how it also:</p><ul>\n<li>Gives the first theoretical account of why LLMs can store facts at an information-theoretically optimal rate, as observed in <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.24832\" target=\"_blank\">prior work</a>.</li>\n<li>Offers a constructive, rather than mechanistic, interpretability of fact storage inside Transformers. Since we can construct fact storing MLPs in closed-form, we can exactly understand how facts live in them.</li>\n<li>Enables seamless fact editing in Transformer blocks <em>without retraining</em>.</li>\n</ul></details>\n<h2 id=\"user-content--fact-storage\">🔑 Fact Storage</h2>\n<p>Prior work (e.g., <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.05405\" target=\"_blank\">Allen-Zhu and Li 2024</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.24832\" target=\"_blank\">Morris et al. 2025</a>) has empirically found that LLMs store facts near the information-theoretically optimal rate of <span><span><span><math>Θ(Flog⁡F)\\Theta(F\\log F)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>Θ</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">F</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>lo<span style=\"margin-right:0.01389em\">g</span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">F</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> parameters for <span><span><span><math>FF</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">F</span></span></span></span></span> facts. Our goal is to match this rate <em>by construction</em> in our MLPs, and to show that Transformers can actually use them.</p>\n<p><strong>What do we mean by facts?</strong> We study facts through the lens of a fact set, which is simply a mapping from keys to values. Concretely, a fact set is defined by the key embeddings <span><span><span><math>K={k1,…,kF}K=\\{k_1,\\ldots,k_F\\}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">K</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>{</span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0315em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>1</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>…</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3283em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0315em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">F</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>}</span></span></span></span></span>, value embeddings <span><span><span><math>V={v1,…,vF}V=\\{v_1,\\ldots,v_F\\}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.22222em\">V</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>{</span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0359em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>1</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>…</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3283em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0359em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">F</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>}</span></span></span></span></span>, and a mapping between them <span><span><span><math>f:[F]→[F]f:[F]\\to[F]</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8889em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10764em\">f</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>:</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>[</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">F</span><span>]</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>→</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>[</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">F</span><span>]</span></span></span></span></span>. For example, the key embedding for \"France\" might map to the value embedding for \"Paris\" in the capitals fact set.</p>\n<p><strong>What do we mean by a fact-storing MLP?</strong> We say an <span><span><span><math>MLP\\mathrm{MLP}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span><span>MLP</span></span></span></span></span></span> <em>stores</em> the fact set if, when queried on key <span><span><span><math>kik_i</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8444em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0315em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>, its output has the highest dot product with the correct value <span><span><span><math>vf(i)v_{f(i)}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7858em;vertical-align:-0.3552em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3448em\"><span style=\"top:-2.5198em;margin-left:-0.0359em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10764em\">f</span><span>(</span><span>i</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.3552em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>:</p>\n<div><span><span><span><math>⟨MLP(ki),vf(i)⟩>⟨MLP(ki),vj⟩for all j≠f(i).\\langle \\mathrm{MLP}(k_i), v_{f(i)} \\rangle > \\langle \\mathrm{MLP}(k_i), v_j \\rangle \\quad \\text{for all } j \\neq f(i).</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.1052em;vertical-align:-0.3552em\"></span><span>⟨</span><span><span>MLP</span></span><span>(</span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0315em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>)</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3448em\"><span style=\"top:-2.5198em;margin-left:-0.0359em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10764em\">f</span><span>(</span><span>i</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.3552em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>⟩</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1.0361em;vertical-align:-0.2861em\"></span><span>⟨</span><span><span>MLP</span></span><span>(</span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0315em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>)</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0359em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2861em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>⟩</span><span style=\"margin-right:1em\"></span><span><span>for all </span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span><span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8889em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span><span><span></span></span></span><span></span></span></span></span></span><span>=</span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10764em\">f</span><span>(</span><span>i</span><span>)</span><span>.</span></span></span></span></span></div>\n<p>This matches how language models decode tokens: the model produces a hidden vector, then scores possible next tokens by dot product with output embeddings.</p>\n<h2 id=\"user-content--a-simple-mlp-construction\">🔧 A simple MLP construction</h2>\n<p>How do we build an MLP that stores a fact set? Surprisingly, a very simple closed-form recipe works.</p>\n<p><span id=\"user-content-construction\"><strong>The construction.</strong></span> Given keys <span><span><span><math>kik_i</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8444em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0315em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> and values <span><span><span><math>vf(i)v_{f(i)}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7858em;vertical-align:-0.3552em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3448em\"><span style=\"top:-2.5198em;margin-left:-0.0359em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10764em\">f</span><span>(</span><span>i</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.3552em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>, sample random matrices <span><span><span><math>A,G∈Rm×dA,G \\in \\mathbb{R}^{m\\times d}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8778em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span>A</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>G</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>∈</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"></span><span><span>R</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>m</span><span>×</span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> from a Gaussian distribution and construct the gated MLP:</p>\n<div><span><span><span><math>MLP(x):=B ϕ(x),ϕ(x):=(Ax)⊙(Gx),B:=1F∑ivf(i) ϕ(ki)⊤\\mathrm{MLP}(x):=B\\,\\phi(x), \\qquad \\phi(x) := (Ax)\\odot(Gx), \\qquad B := \\frac{1}{F}\\sum_i v_{f(i)}\\,\\phi(k_i)^\\top</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span><span>MLP</span></span><span>(</span><span>x</span><span>)</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>:=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05017em\">B</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>ϕ</span><span>(</span><span>x</span><span>)</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:2em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>ϕ</span><span>(</span><span>x</span><span>)</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>:=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>(</span><span>A</span><span>x</span><span>)</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>⊙</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>(</span><span>G</span><span>x</span><span>)</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:2em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05017em\">B</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>:=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:2.5991em;vertical-align:-1.2777em\"></span><span><span></span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.3214em\"><span style=\"top:-2.314em\"><span style=\"height:3em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">F</span></span></span><span style=\"top:-3.23em\"><span style=\"height:3em\"></span><span style=\"border-bottom-width:0.04em\"></span></span><span style=\"top:-3.677em\"><span style=\"height:3em\"></span><span><span>1</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.686em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.05em\"><span style=\"top:-1.8723em;margin-left:0em\"><span style=\"height:3.05em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span><span style=\"top:-3.05em\"><span style=\"height:3.05em\"></span><span><span>∑</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1.2777em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3448em\"><span style=\"top:-2.5198em;margin-left:-0.0359em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10764em\">f</span><span>(</span><span>i</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.3552em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>ϕ</span><span>(</span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0315em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span><span>)</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8991em\"><span style=\"top:-3.113em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>⊤</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></div>\n<p>That's the whole construction, <strong>no gradient descent!</strong></p>\n<details><summary><strong>Our Hebbian memory view of MLPs</strong> (click to expand!)</summary><p>A classical Hebbian memory stores key-value pairs as a sum of outer products, <span><span><span><math>W=∑iviki⊤W = \\sum_i v_i k_i^\\top</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">W</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1.1488em;vertical-align:-0.2997em\"></span><span><span style=\"position:relative;top:0em\">∑</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.162em\"><span style=\"top:-2.4003em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2997em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0359em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"><span style=\"top:-2.4413em;margin-left:-0.0315em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>⊤</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2587em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>, and retrieves values with <span><span><span><math>Wq=∑ivi⟨ki,q⟩Wq = \\sum_i v_i \\langle k_i, q\\rangle</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8778em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">W</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">q</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1.0497em;vertical-align:-0.2997em\"></span><span><span style=\"position:relative;top:0em\">∑</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.162em\"><span style=\"top:-2.4003em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2997em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0359em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>⟨</span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0315em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">q</span><span>⟩</span></span></span></span></span>. This is a weighted sum of stored values, where each value's weight is its key's similarity to the query. Our construction is exactly a Hebbian memory, but with the similarity measured in the MLP's feature space <span><span><span><math>ϕ\\phi</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8889em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span>ϕ</span></span></span></span></span> instead:</p><div><span><span><span><math>MLP(q)=B ϕ(q)⏟MLP=1F∑ivf(i) ⟨ϕ(ki),ϕ(q)⟩=1F∑ivf(i) K(ki,q)⏟Hebbian Memory,\\underbrace{\\mathrm{MLP}(q) = B\\,\\phi(q)}_{\\text{MLP}} = \\frac{1}{F}\\sum_i v_{f(i)}\\,\\langle \\phi(k_i), \\phi(q)\\rangle = \\underbrace{\\frac{1}{F}\\sum_i v_{f(i)}\\,K(k_i, q)}_{\\text{Hebbian Memory}},</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:2.3263em;vertical-align:-1.5763em\"></span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.75em\"><span style=\"top:-1.4237em\"><span style=\"height:3em\"></span><span><span><span><span>MLP</span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"top:-3em\"><span style=\"height:3em\"></span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3552em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">K</span><span>(</span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0315em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7479em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span><span>,</span></span></span></span></span></div><p>Intuitively, the kernel similarity <span><span><span><math>K(ki,q)K(k_i, q)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">K</span><span>(</span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0315em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">q</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> acts as a soft lookup. When we query with a stored key <span><span><span><math>q=kjq = k_j</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.625em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">q</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.9805em;vertical-align:-0.2861em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0315em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2861em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>, the matching term <span><span><span><math>K(kj,kj)K(k_j, k_j)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.0361em;vertical-align:-0.2861em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">K</span><span>(</span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0315em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2861em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0315em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2861em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> is large while the mismatched terms <span><span><span><math>K(ki,kj)K(k_i, k_j)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.0361em;vertical-align:-0.2861em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">K</span><span>(</span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0315em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0315em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2861em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> for <span><span><span><math>i≠ji \\neq j</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8889em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span>i</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span><span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8889em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span><span><span></span></span></span><span></span></span></span></span></span><span>=</span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.854em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span></span></span></span></span> are small, so the weighted sum collapses onto the correct value <span><span><span><math>vf(j)v_{f(j)}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7858em;vertical-align:-0.3552em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3448em\"><span style=\"top:-2.5198em;margin-left:-0.0359em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10764em\">f</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.3552em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>.</p><figure style=\"text-align:center;margin:1.5rem 0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2026-07-08-mlps-are-hebbians/hebbian-flow.png\" alt=\"Hebbian kernel memory retrieval flow from stored keys through kernel comparison to weighted sum of stored values\" style=\"width:100%\"><strong>MLPs are Hebbian memories.</strong> An MLP compares a query to stored keys in feature space, then returns a similarity-weighted sum of stored values.</figure></details>\n<p><span id=\"user-content-mlp-capacity\"><strong>📈 MLP storage capacity.</strong></span> How many parameters does this construction need to store a fact set?\nExcitingly, we find that our simple MLP construction with <span><span><span><math>WW</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">W</span></span></span></span></span> parameters stores a fact set of size <span><span><span><math>FF</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">F</span></span></span></span></span> exactly at the <strong>information-theoretically optimal rate</strong> when the key and value embeddings are isotropic (e.g. uniform-spherical):</p>\n<div><span><span><span><math>W=Θ(md)=Θ(Flog⁡F)W=\\Theta(md)=\\Theta(F\\log F)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">W</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>Θ</span><span>(</span><span>m</span><span>d</span><span>)</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>Θ</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">F</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>lo<span style=\"margin-right:0.01389em\">g</span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">F</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span></div>\n<p>We show an analogous result for non-isotropic key and value embeddings (like those from LLMs) in <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.10034\" target=\"_blank\">our paper</a>. There, the storage capacity scales at the same rate, up to penalization factors depending on the embedding geometry.</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;margin:1.5rem 0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2026-07-08-mlps-are-hebbians/isotropic-capacity.png\" alt=\"Standalone fact-storage capacity scaling under isotropic embeddings\" style=\"width:100%;max-width:460px\"><strong>MLP capacity scaling.</strong> Our constructed Hebbian MLPs achieve optimal <span><span><span><math>W=Θ(Flog⁡F)W=\\Theta(F\\log F)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">W</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>Θ</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">F</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>lo<span style=\"margin-right:0.01389em\">g</span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">F</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> capacity under isotropic embeddings, improving over prior constructions (NTK) by <span><span><span><math>1010</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>10</span></span></span></span></span>-<span><span><span><math>104×104\\times</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>104</span><span>×</span></span></span></span></span> while staying within <span><span><span><math>66</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>6</span></span></span></span></span>-<span><span><span><math>10×10\\times</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>10</span><span>×</span></span></span></span></span> of gradient descent (GD)-trained MLPs. We also include two enhanced flavors of our construction: a <strong>whitened</strong> variant that uses a covariance-whitened kernel, and a <strong>data-dependent</strong> variant that solves a least-squares problem to store the fact set. Check out <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.10034\" target=\"_blank\">our paper</a> to learn more!</figure>\n<p><span id=\"user-content-transformer-capacity\"><strong>🎯 Transformer block storage capacity.</strong></span> Storing facts in a standalone MLP is one thing; using them <em>inside a Transformer</em> is another. When an MLP sits inside a Transformer block, attention doesn't hand it the exact key <span><span><span><math>kik_i</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8444em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0315em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>, but a noisy version instead. Excitingly, our MLP construction can handle noisy fact queries gracefully.</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;margin:1.5rem 0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2026-07-08-mlps-are-hebbians/transformer-block-schematic.png\" alt=\"Transformer block schematic: attention produces a noisy query that a constructed MLP decodes to the correct value\" style=\"width:100%;max-width:360px\"><strong>Fact recall inside a Transformer block.</strong> Attention hands the MLP a noisy query <span><span><span><math>q=k1+εq=k_1+\\varepsilon</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.625em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">q</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8444em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0315em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>1</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>+</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span>ε</span></span></span></span></span> instead of the exact key; our constructed MLP, and thus the whole Transformer block, still decodes the correct value <span><span><span><math>v1=Emb(Paris)v_1=\\mathrm{Emb}(\\text{Paris})</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.5806em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0359em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>1</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span><span>Emb</span></span><span>(</span><span><span>Paris</span></span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span>.</figure>\n<p>Using our MLP construction, we derive that a Transformer block still retrieves the correct fact as long as the attention noise (i.e. the L2 error between a query vector and the exact key embedding of the fact being queried) stays below <span><span><span><math>d/(Flog⁡F)\\sqrt{d/(F\\log F)}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.24em;vertical-align:-0.305em\"></span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.935em\"><span style=\"top:-3.2em\"><span style=\"height:3.2em\"></span><span style=\"padding-left:1em\"><span>d</span><span>/</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">F</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>lo<span style=\"margin-right:0.01389em\">g</span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">F</span><span>)</span></span></span><span style=\"top:-2.895em\"><span style=\"height:3.2em\"></span><span style=\"min-width:1.02em;height:1.28em\"><svg xmlns=\"http://www.w3.org/2000/svg\" width=\"400em\" height=\"1.28em\" viewBox=\"0 0 400000 1296\" preserveAspectRatio=\"xMinYMin slice\"><path d=\"M263,681c0.7,0,18,39.7,52,119\nc34,79.3,68.167,158.7,102.5,238c34.3,79.3,51.8,119.3,52.5,120\nc340,-704.7,510.7,-1060.3,512,-1067\nl0 -0\nc4.7,-7.3,11,-11,19,-11\nH40000v40H1012.3\ns-271.3,567,-271.3,567c-38.7,80.7,-84,175,-136,283c-52,108,-89.167,185.3,-111.5,232\nc-22.3,46.7,-33.8,70.3,-34.5,71c-4.7,4.7,-12.3,7,-23,7s-12,-1,-12,-1\ns-109,-253,-109,-253c-72.7,-168,-109.3,-252,-110,-252c-10.7,8,-22,16.7,-34,26\nc-22,17.3,-33.3,26,-34,26s-26,-26,-26,-26s76,-59,76,-59s76,-60,76,-60z\nM1001 80h400000v40h-400000z\"></path></svg></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.305em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>. When it does, the block inherits the same information-theoretically optimal scaling as MLPs,</p>\n<div><span><span><span><math>W=Θ(md)=Θ(Flog⁡F).W = \\Theta(md) = \\Theta(F\\log F).</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">W</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>Θ</span><span>(</span><span>m</span><span>d</span><span>)</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>Θ</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">F</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>lo<span style=\"margin-right:0.01389em\">g</span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">F</span><span>)</span><span>.</span></span></span></span></span></div>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;margin:1.5rem 0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2026-07-08-mlps-are-hebbians/transformer-capacity-eval100.png\" alt=\"Transformer fact-storage capacity scaling\" style=\"width:100%;max-width:460px\"><strong>Transformer capacity scaling.</strong> Under bounded attention noise, Transformer blocks with our MLP construction achieve optimal <span><span><span><math>W=Θ(Flog⁡F)W=\\Theta(F\\log F)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">W</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>Θ</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">F</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>lo<span style=\"margin-right:0.01389em\">g</span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">F</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> capacity, with the data-dependent variant up to <span><span><span><math>63×63\\times</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>63</span><span>×</span></span></span></span></span> better than prior constructions. As in the MLP capacity figure, we also include our whitened and data-dependent constructions.</figure>\n<p>This is the first MLP construction demonstrating that <strong>Transformer blocks can store facts at the information-theoretically optimal rate</strong>. Compared to prior work, our MLP construction is not only simpler but scales better inside Transformers: our data-dependent construction stays within <span><span><span><math>3×3\\times</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>3</span><span>×</span></span></span></span></span> of GD-trained MLPs and is up to <span><span><span><math>63×63\\times</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>63</span><span>×</span></span></span></span></span> better than prior constructions as the number of facts grows at fixed model dimension.</p>\n<h2 id=\"user-content--whats-next\">🔭 What's next</h2>\n<p>We're excited about these results, since a simple mathematical object, a <em>Hebbian kernel memory</em>, allows us to develop fact-storing MLPs and Transformer blocks with provable capacity guarantees! But there's still a ton more to do:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>\n<p><strong>Understanding how pretrained LLMs store facts.</strong> Can we use our theory to understand the MLPs inside pretrained language models? Can we directly extract or edit the knowledge stored within these MLPs?</p>\n</li>\n<li>\n<p><strong>Studying memory in sequence mixers and multi-layer Transformers.</strong> Can we extend our theory to fact storage in the parameters of sequence mixers, such as attention's KV caches? How do attention and MLP layers cooperate to store knowledge in a multi-layer model?</p>\n</li>\n</ul>,⚡ TL;DR It sounds like sci-fi, but it's real: we can instantly build knowledge into a Transformer block, no training required!\n\nPreviously, w…,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2026-07-22-mlps-are-hebbians,<a href=\"https://roberto09.github.io/\">Roberto Garcia*</a>, <a href=\"https://jerrywliu.github.io/\">Jerry Liu*</a>, <a href=\"https://scholar.google.com/citations?user=KMRwMNIAAAAJ&hl=en\">Ronny Junkins*</a>, and <a href=\"https://cs.stanford.edu/people/chrismre/\">Chris Ré</a>,2026-07-22T18:37:38.426Z,2026-07-22T07:00:00.388Z,[{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2026-07-08-mlps-are-hebbians/mlp-robot.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":2614,\"height\":1886},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2026-07-08-mlps-are-hebbians/hebbian-flow.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2026-07-08-mlps-are-hebbians/isotropic-capacity.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2026-07-08-mlps-are-hebbians/transformer-block-schematic.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2026-07-08-mlps-are-hebbians/transformer-capacity-eval100.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"}],163778663386687488,1211296081845936129,From Minions to OpenJarvis: A Retrospective on Two Years in Local AI,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2026-05-15-minions-to-openjarvis-retrospective,<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2026-05-15-minions-to-openjarvis-retrospective/00-hero.png\" style=\"width:100%\"></figure>\n<p>For two years, the dominant story in AI has been the growing cloud: <em>bigger clusters, larger models, more gigawatts</em>. Another story is unfolding in the opposite direction: <em>on-device inference, smaller models, watts instead of megawatts</em>.</p>\n<p>Over the past two years, we've explored this through three projects, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.15964\" target=\"_blank\">Minions</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2511.07885\" target=\"_blank\">Intelligence per Watt</a>, and <a href=\"https://scalingintelligence.stanford.edu/blogs/openjarvis/\" target=\"_blank\">OpenJarvis</a>, each making a version of the same argument: <strong>the future of inference is hybrid by design, local-first by default</strong>.</p>\n<p><strong>TL;DR.</strong> Across three projects, we argue that capable AI inference can run on hardware people own, with cloud reserved for what genuinely needs it. <strong>Minions</strong> showed local and cloud are complements: cloud decomposes long-context tasks, local executes subtasks in parallel, recovering <strong>97.9%</strong> of GPT-4o's accuracy at <strong>5.7× lower cost</strong>. <strong>Intelligence per Watt (IPW)</strong> introduced task performance delivered per watt of power as a unified metric for local viability: IPW improved <strong>5.3× in two years</strong>, and <strong>88.7%</strong> of single-turn chat and reasoning queries are answerable locally. <strong>OpenJarvis</strong> decomposes the personal AI stack into five composable primitives that run on a user's own devices, treating efficiency and on-device learning as first-class capabilities alongside accuracy. A single shared deployment ties the arc together: <em>optimize with cloud at search time, run local at inference time</em>.</p>\n<p><strong>Sections:</strong> <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2026-05-15-minions-to-openjarvis-retrospective#state-of-play\" target=\"_blank\">State of Play</a> · <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2026-05-15-minions-to-openjarvis-retrospective#how-did-we-get-here\" target=\"_blank\">How did we get here?</a> · <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2026-05-15-minions-to-openjarvis-retrospective#impact\" target=\"_blank\">Impact</a> · <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2026-05-15-minions-to-openjarvis-retrospective#what-were-releasing-today\" target=\"_blank\">What we're releasing today</a> · <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2026-05-15-minions-to-openjarvis-retrospective#whats-next\" target=\"_blank\">What's next?</a> · <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2026-05-15-minions-to-openjarvis-retrospective#build-with-us\" target=\"_blank\">Build with us</a></p>\n<h2 id=\"user-content-state-of-play\">State of Play</h2>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2026-05-15-minions-to-openjarvis-retrospective/00-state-of-play.png\" style=\"width:100%\"></figure>\n<p>Local AI is having its moment, driven by two compounding trends.</p>\n<p><strong>On the demand side</strong>, users are pulling for on-device. Personal-AI runtimes like <a href=\"https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw\" target=\"_blank\">OpenClaw</a> and <a href=\"https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent\" target=\"_blank\">Hermes Agent</a> have built large user bases around personal AI leveraging cloud intelligence, building agents for their daily life. The local stack can now actually answer most of the queries those users send without sacrificing privacy, cost, latency, and offline operation.</p>\n<p><strong>On the supply side</strong>, the components are landing. M4-generation Mac Minis became developer favorites as unified-memory inference boxes. Open-weight families like <a href=\"https://huggingface.co/Qwen/Qwen3-235B-A22B\" target=\"_blank\">Qwen3</a>, <a href=\"https://huggingface.co/openai/gpt-oss-120b\" target=\"_blank\">gpt-oss</a>, <a href=\"https://huggingface.co/google/gemma-3-27b-it\" target=\"_blank\">Gemma</a>, and <a href=\"https://huggingface.co/ibm-granite/granite-4.0-h-small\" target=\"_blank\">IBM Granite</a> now <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2511.07885\" target=\"_blank\">trail frontier cloud models by 6 to 12 months on most personal-AI tasks</a>, rather than years. NVIDIA shipped <a href=\"https://nvidianews.nvidia.com/news/nvidia-unveils-dgx-personal-ai-supercomputers-powered-by-grace-blackwell\" target=\"_blank\">DGX Spark</a>, a desktop with 128GB of unified memory and a petaFLOP at FP4. The full stack from silicon to scaffolding has quietly become deployable.</p>\n<p>The two trends compound. Total LLM usage is expanding faster than cloud capacity can absorb, so routing inference workloads to local hardware lets cloud capacity concentrate on the queries that genuinely need frontier capability. <em>The world will be hybrid by design and local-first by default.</em></p>\n<h2 id=\"user-content-how-did-we-get-here\">How did we get here?</h2>\n<p>Three projects, three starting questions:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>How can local and cloud models collaborate on long-context tasks? (<strong>Minions</strong>)</li>\n<li>How much intelligence can local hardware actually deliver per unit of energy? (<strong>Intelligence per Watt</strong>)</li>\n<li>How do we run an entire personal-AI stack on-device? (<strong>OpenJarvis</strong>)</li>\n</ul>\n<p>Taken together, they trace a single line of inquiry: <em>how do we deliver capable AI on hardware people own, while still drawing on the cloud where it adds the most value?</em></p>\n<ul>\n<li><strong>Minions</strong> established that local and cloud models can collaborate productively.</li>\n<li><strong>Intelligence per Watt</strong> established that local hardware is closing the efficiency gap fast enough to matter.</li>\n<li><strong>OpenJarvis</strong> established that an entire personal AI stack can run on a user's own devices when efficiency is a first-class design constraint.</li>\n</ul>\n<h3 id=\"user-content-minions-local-and-cloud-models-are-a-match\">Minions: Local and cloud models are a match</h3>\n<p>Minions started from a specific observation. When you give a small on-device model and a frontier cloud model the same long-context document QA task, the cloud model crushes it and the local model fails. The failure has two compounding sources: <strong>long contexts and complex multi-step instructions</strong>. Decompose the task into small, simply-specified subtasks over short context shards, and each piece falls within the local model's reach.</p>\n<p>This suggested a protocol: the cloud model decomposes the task into small, local-shaped subtasks; the local model executes each subtask over a shard of the context, in parallel; the cloud model aggregates. Two variants emerged: <strong>Minion</strong> (a single local model in extended chat with the cloud) and <strong>MinionS</strong> (the cloud decomposes into parallel local subtasks).</p>\n<p>The numbers came out cleanly. On FinanceBench, ConvFinQA, MuSiQue, QASPER, and similar long-context benchmarks, MinionS recovered <strong>97.9%</strong> of GPT-4o's accuracy at <strong>5.7× lower cost</strong>; the cheaper Minion variant hit <strong>87.9%</strong> at <strong>30.4× lower cost</strong>.</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2026-05-15-minions-to-openjarvis-retrospective/01-minions-tradeoff.png\" style=\"width:75%\"><strong>Cost-accuracy frontier on long-context QA.</strong> Both Minion variants sit far inside the cloud-only Pareto frontier.</figure>\n<p>The shape of the gain (<em>cloud for planning, local for parallel grunt work</em>) generalized further than we expected. It is the same asymmetry we'll return to in OpenJarvis, one layer up: <em>cloud at search time to optimize the stack, local at inference time to run it.</em></p>\n<h3 id=\"user-content-intelligence-per-watt-local-compute-is-underutilized-and-getting-better\">Intelligence per Watt: Local compute is underutilized (and getting better!)</h3>\n<p>Minions showed that local participation pays off, but it did not answer the underlying measurement question: <em>how much intelligence can local hardware actually deliver per unit of energy, and how is that changing?</em> Benchmarks measured accuracy. <a href=\"https://github.com/mlcommons/inference\" target=\"_blank\">MLPerf</a> and <a href=\"https://ml.energy/zeus/\" target=\"_blank\">ML Energy</a> measured throughput or energy. Nothing measured them together at the granularity of a model-accelerator pair on a real workload distribution.</p>\n<p>So we built one. <strong>Intelligence per Watt (IPW) is task accuracy divided by mean power draw</strong>, measured per query, on real inference workloads. We instrumented a hardware-agnostic profiling harness across NVIDIA, AMD, Apple, and SambaNova accelerators, ran 1M+ queries across <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2405.01470\" target=\"_blank\">WildChat</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.13124\" target=\"_blank\">NaturalReasoning</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.01574\" target=\"_blank\">MMLU-Pro</a>, and <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.14739\" target=\"_blank\">SuperGPQA</a> through 20+ local LMs, and tracked how the numbers moved from 2023 to 2025.</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2026-05-15-minions-to-openjarvis-retrospective/03-ipw-overview.png\" style=\"width:100%\"><strong>Intelligence per Watt.</strong> Best-of-local win rate against frontier models rose from 23.2% (2023) to 71.3% (2025); IPW itself improved 5.3× over the same window.</figure>\n<p>We found three interesting findings from our IPW study. First, <strong>88.7%</strong> of single-turn chat and reasoning queries can be answered correctly by some local LM (≤20B active parameters), with coverage on the best single local model moving from 23.2% (2023) to 71.3% (2025). Second, <strong>IPW improved 5.3× over two years</strong>, roughly 3.1× from better models and 1.7× from better hardware. Third, local accelerators still trail cloud accelerators by <strong>1.4-7.4×</strong> on the same workload, but <em>routing inverts the per-query gap at the system level</em>: a hybrid local-cloud router delivers <strong>60-80% reductions</strong> in energy, compute, and cost compared to a batched cloud baseline, because the dominant effect is sending easier queries to local models rather than cloud models.</p>\n<p>Overall, we find that <em>efficient AI infrastructure comes from straddling local and cloud models and hardware</em>, rather than from waiting for local silicon to match cloud silicon per query.</p>\n<h3 id=\"user-content-openjarvis-a-local-first-stack-for-personal-ai\">OpenJarvis: A local-first stack for personal AI</h3>\n<p>By late 2025, open-weight 4B-32B models had become genuinely capable for personal AI workloads, with the deployment bottlenecks shifting to the engineering stack around them. In almost every personal-AI framework, the local component is a thin orchestration layer while the intelligence is leased from someone else's data center. Your most personal data (e.g. emails, calendars, documents, messages) routes through cloud APIs, with their associated latency and inference costs.</p>\n<p>We built <a href=\"https://scalingintelligence.stanford.edu/blogs/openjarvis/\" target=\"_blank\">OpenJarvis</a> as an opinionated framework for personal AI that runs <em>on your personal devices</em>. It exposes a personal AI system as five composable primitives: <strong>Intelligence</strong> (the on-device language models), <strong>Engine</strong> (the inference runtime), <strong>Agents</strong> (the reasoning loops), <strong>Tools &#x26; Memory</strong> (interfaces and persistent local state), and <strong>Learning</strong> (the optimizer that improves all of the above from local traces). Each primitive is an independent slot, benchmarkable and substitutable on its own.</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2026-05-15-minions-to-openjarvis-retrospective/04-openjarvis-overview.png\" style=\"width:100%\"><strong>OpenJarvis in one figure.</strong> Five composable primitives form a hardware-aware, local-first stack for personal AI.</figure>\n<p>Three design commitments fall out of this. First, <strong>efficiency is a first-class constraint, not an afterthought.</strong> On a laptop running on battery, constraints such as energy, dollar cost, FLOPs, and latency are hard requirements, not nice-to-haves. OpenJarvis ships a hardware-agnostic telemetry layer that profiles power draw across NVIDIA, AMD, and Apple Silicon, and treats those metrics as evaluation targets alongside accuracy. Second, <strong>learning happens locally.</strong> Because execution is local, traces stay on-device, and the same traces can drive closed-loop optimization across model weights (<a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2106.09685\" target=\"_blank\">SFT</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2106.09685\" target=\"_blank\">LoRA</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.03300\" target=\"_blank\">GRPO</a>), prompts (<a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.03714\" target=\"_blank\">DSPy</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2507.19457\" target=\"_blank\">GEPA</a>), engine configuration, and tools selected. Third, <strong>the cloud teaches the local stack at search time.</strong> A frontier model reads on-device traces, identifies failure clusters, and proposes coordinated edits across the four editable primitives. A held-out gate accepts only edits that don't regress. The resulting stack runs entirely on-device. We call this loop <strong>LLM-guided spec search</strong>, and it is how OpenJarvis closes the frontier-local gap on hard tasks without giving up local execution at inference time.</p>\n<p>Where local trails cloud, we lean on the cloud at search time to optimize the local stack, and let the optimized stack run on-device at inference time. <em>Cloud at search time, local at inference time.</em></p>\n<h2 id=\"user-content-impact\">Impact</h2>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2026-05-15-minions-to-openjarvis-retrospective/09-impact-collage.png\" style=\"width:90%\"></figure>\n<p>We are grateful that the metrics, methodologies, and libraries from this program are actively in use by an unusually wide range of partners, spanning chip manufacturers, local AI platforms, and LM training shops. Each community framed the value differently, but the underlying claim is shared: <em>local and cloud compute are complements, not substitutes.</em></p>\n<h3 id=\"user-content-minions\">Minions</h3>\n<p>Minions found an audience faster than we expected. <a href=\"https://ollama.com/blog/minions\" target=\"_blank\">Ollama</a> launched a same-day collaboration, making \"your local model and a cloud model, talking to each other\" a first-class workflow. <a href=\"https://www.amd.com/en/developer/resources/technical-articles/2025/minions--on-device-and-cloud-language-model-collaboration-on-ryz.html\" target=\"_blank\">AMD</a> brought the protocol to Ryzen AI through Lemonade, and <a href=\"https://www.docker.com/blog/hybrid-ai-and-how-it-runs-in-docker/\" target=\"_blank\">Docker</a> built MinionS into Compose and Model Runner under the framing \"Hybrid AI isn't the future, it's here.\" <a href=\"https://www.together.ai/blog/minions\" target=\"_blank\">Together AI</a> wrote it up as \"embracing small LMs, shifting compute on-device,\" which captured the thesis better than we did. <strong><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-05-12-security\" target=\"_blank\">Secure Minions</a></strong> then closed the loop on the obvious next question: <em>can you run this protocol if you don't trust the cloud provider?</em> We found that by using H100 confidential computing as a remote-attested enclave, even root users on the cloud side cannot read plaintext!</p>\n<h3 id=\"user-content-intelligence-per-watt\">Intelligence per Watt</h3>\n<p>IPW gave the field a shared name for something it had been measuring in disconnected pieces. NVIDIA built on the framing in their <a href=\"https://developer.nvidia.com/blog/scaling-token-factory-revenue-and-ai-efficiency-by-maximizing-performance-per-watt\" target=\"_blank\">developer blog on token-factory revenue and performance-per-watt</a>; IBM Research wrote up the work alongside <a href=\"https://research.ibm.com/blog/small-models-hazy-research-granite\" target=\"_blank\">Granite 4.0</a>; SambaNova published a <a href=\"https://sambanova.ai/blog/best-intelligence-per-joule\" target=\"_blank\">benchmark post on best intelligence-per-joule</a>. <a href=\"https://www.laude.org/updates/slingshots-two\" target=\"_blank\">Laude Institute</a> awarded a Slingshot grant for follow-on work, <a href=\"https://www.gartner.com/en/documents/7367330\" target=\"_blank\">Gartner</a> cited IPW in their efficiency reports, and the Computing Research Association hosted a <a href=\"https://cra.org/industry/2026/04/27/how-ai-will-reshape-computer-systems-by-2035-a-jeffersonian-dinner-in-san-francisco-about-our-10000x-future/\" target=\"_blank\">Jeffersonian dinner</a>, in which AI luminaries discussed IPW and other topics. Once you have a unified accuracy-and-power number, a lot of efficiency arguments that previously lived in disconnected dimensions snap into the same frame.</p>\n<h2 id=\"user-content-what-were-releasing-today\">What we're releasing today</h2>\n<h3 id=\"user-content-hybrid-capabilities\">Hybrid capabilities</h3>\n<p>OpenJarvis v1.0 makes three hybrid patterns from this research arc first-class:</p>\n<ol>\n<li><strong>Hybrid local-cloud collaboration.</strong> OpenJarvis supports a growing family of techniques for coordinating frontier cloud models with local models, including <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/minions\" target=\"_blank\">Minions</a>, <a href=\"https://github.com/az1326/advisor-models\" target=\"_blank\">Advisor Models</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.04388\" target=\"_blank\">Conductor</a>, and <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2511.21689\" target=\"_blank\">ToolOrchestra</a>. Each carves up the work differently; OpenJarvis exposes them as composable patterns so researchers can study existing protocols and build new ones without rewriting the stack.</li>\n<li><strong>LLM-guided spec search.</strong> A frontier model reads traces, proposes coordinated edits across all five primitives, and a held-out gate accepts only non-regressing edits. The resulting spec runs entirely on-device.</li>\n<li><strong>Per-query routing</strong> through a query-complexity analyzer: easier queries stay local while only queries that need frontier capability escalate. This is the routing pattern that recovers the 60-80% energy, compute, and cost reductions from IPW versus a batched cloud baseline.</li>\n</ol>\n<h3 id=\"user-content-openjarvis-prebuilt-agents\">OpenJarvis Prebuilt Agents</h3>\n<p>Each agent template is expressible because the five primitives sit behind a single typed interface; any slot is swappable without touching the rest. The v1.0 release ships:</p>\n<ul>\n<li><a href=\"https://github.com/open-jarvis/OpenJarvis/tree/main/src/openjarvis/agents\" target=\"_blank\"><strong style=\"color:#b63629\">Eight built-in agents</strong></a> across three execution modes (on-demand, scheduled, continuous), including a deep-research agent with inline citations, a CodeAct-style coder, and a continuous monitor with memory compression for long-horizon workflows.</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://github.com/open-jarvis/OpenJarvis/tree/main/configs/openjarvis/examples\" target=\"_blank\"><strong style=\"color:#b63629\">Seven starter presets</strong></a> that bundle an agent with a hardware-appropriate engine, connectors, and tools, installable with <code>jarvis init --preset &#x3C;name></code>. Variants cover Apple Silicon, Linux GPU servers, and CPU-only laptops.</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://github.com/open-jarvis/OpenJarvis/tree/main/src/openjarvis/engine\" target=\"_blank\"><strong style=\"color:#b63629\">Four local engines</strong></a> (Ollama, vLLM, SGLang, llama.cpp) and <strong>five cloud engines</strong> (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google Gemini, OpenRouter, MiniMax), all behind a single Engine interface.</li>\n</ul>\n<p><strong>Code:</strong> <a href=\"https://github.com/open-jarvis/OpenJarvis\" target=\"_blank\">github.com/open-jarvis/OpenJarvis</a></p>\n<p><strong>Website:</strong> <a href=\"https://open-jarvis.github.io/OpenJarvis/\" target=\"_blank\">open-jarvis.github.io/OpenJarvis</a></p>\n<h2 id=\"user-content-whats-next\">What's next?</h2>\n<p>We're particularly excited about three areas of future research.</p>\n<h3 id=\"user-content-closing-the-frontier-local-gap\">Closing the frontier-local gap</h3>\n<p>A persistent accuracy gap remains between closed-source frontier cloud models and open-source local models, especially on complex reasoning, agentic, and research-heavy tasks. The gap varies substantially by domain: chat and simple retrieval are nearly saturated locally, while browser-based agentic tasks and coding tasks still favor the cloud. Closing this gap matters because it determines how much of the workload can stay on-device without quality regressions, and methods that mitigate it (better local models, smarter routing, hybrid local-cloud protocols) are how local-first deployment becomes the default rather than a tradeoff.</p>\n<h3 id=\"user-content-local-first-model-architectures\">Local-first model architectures</h3>\n<p>Today's mixture-of-experts models decouple total parameter count (capacity) from per-token compute by activating only a small subset of experts per query, which works well for cloud serving at increasing batch sizes, but wastes resources on single-user local devices, where most experts sit cold across queries. Local devices have an opposite resource profile from datacenters: abundant memory capacity (128GB+ unified memory on modern workstations) paired with relatively constrained memory bandwidth (around 500 GB/s on Apple Silicon versus 4.8-8 TB/s on HBM3e datacenter accelerators), and per-device power budgets in the tens of watts rather than hundreds. The local-friendly architecture should look different: <em>dense rather than sparse, small active footprints, smart quantization, and trained with local serving as a first-class objective.</em> We expect the next generation of open-weight models tuned for local-first deployment to look substantively different from today's releases.</p>\n<h3 id=\"user-content-local-cloud-collaboration-as-a-research-area\">Local-cloud collaboration as a research area</h3>\n<p>Every project in this arc is an instance of the same underlying question: <em>how should a frontier cloud model and a local model divide labor on a single query, task, or workflow?</em> Minions answered it one way for long-context QA; <a href=\"https://github.com/ScalingIntelligence/Archon\" target=\"_blank\">Archon</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.02453\" target=\"_blank\">Advisor Models</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.04388\" target=\"_blank\">Conductor</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2511.21689\" target=\"_blank\">ToolOrchestra</a>, and <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19672\" target=\"_blank\">SkillOrchestra</a> answer it differently for other workloads. The field does not yet have a shared vocabulary for comparing these on equal footing. We need a <strong>taxonomy that maps workload characteristics to hybrid paradigms</strong> (coding wants a specific collaboration protocol, long-context retrieval wants another, agentic tool use wants a third), and we need to extend IPW's profiler to hybrid execution. This is the thread we are most excited to coordinate on with the broader community.</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2026-05-15-minions-to-openjarvis-retrospective/08-hybrid-landscape.png\" style=\"width:100%\"></figure>\n<h2 id=\"user-content-build-with-us\">Build with us</h2>\n<p>We're looking for collaborators across three fronts:</p>\n<ul>\n<li><strong>Researchers</strong> working on local-cloud collaboration, efficient local architectures, on-device learning, or intelligence efficiency in ML systems. The OpenJarvis evaluation harness covers 30+ benchmarks across accuracy, latency, energy, cost, and more; we'd love to compare notes.</li>\n<li><strong>Developers</strong> building on OpenJarvis or running it on hardware we haven't profiled yet. PRs, issues, and use-case reports all help us understand where the bottlenecks actually are.</li>\n<li><strong>Users</strong> willing to point OpenJarvis at their files, calendars, and messaging platforms and tell us what works and what breaks. (All it takes is <code>pip install openjarvis</code>, then <code>jarvis init</code>).</li>\n</ul>\n<p><strong>Resources:</strong></p>\n<ul>\n<li>Intelligence per Watt initiative: <a href=\"https://www.intelligence-per-watt.ai/\" target=\"_blank\">intelligence-per-watt.ai</a></li>\n<li>Intelligence per Watt codebase: <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/intelligence-per-watt\" target=\"_blank\">github.com/HazyResearch/intelligence-per-watt</a></li>\n<li>OpenJarvis codebase: <a href=\"https://github.com/open-jarvis/OpenJarvis\" target=\"_blank\">github.com/open-jarvis/OpenJarvis</a></li>\n<li>Discord: <a href=\"https://discord.gg/3DEGU5Ce\" target=\"_blank\">discord.gg/3DEGU5Ce</a></li>\n<li>Twitter: <a href=\"https://x.com/OpenJarvisAI\" target=\"_blank\">@OpenJarvisAI</a></li>\n</ul>,For two years, the dominant story in AI has been the growing cloud: bigger clusters, larger models, more gigawatts. Another story is…,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2026-05-15-minions-to-openjarvis-retrospective,<a href='https://jonsaadfalcon.com/'>Jon Saad-Falcon</a> and <a href='https://www.linkedin.com/in/avanika-narayan-a35a02a3/'>Avanika Narayan</a>.,2026-07-22T18:37:38.425Z,2026-05-15T07:00:00.666Z,[{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2026-05-15-minions-to-openjarvis-retrospective/00-hero.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1672,\"height\":941},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2026-05-15-minions-to-openjarvis-retrospective/00-state-of-play.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2026-05-15-minions-to-openjarvis-retrospective/01-minions-tradeoff.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2026-05-15-minions-to-openjarvis-retrospective/03-ipw-overview.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2026-05-15-minions-to-openjarvis-retrospective/04-openjarvis-overview.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2026-05-15-minions-to-openjarvis-retrospective/09-impact-collage.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2026-05-15-minions-to-openjarvis-retrospective/08-hybrid-landscape.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"}],163778663386687488,1211296081845936130,ThunderKittens 2.0: Even Faster Kernels for Your GPUs,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2026-02-19-tk-2,<p><strong>TL;DR: This is a release post for ThunderKittens 2.0, our cute little CUDA-embedded DSL, along with a technical deep dive for those who are interested.</strong></p>\n<p>Since releasing <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/ThunderKittens\" target=\"_blank\">ThunderKittens</a> (TK) two years ago, we've spent most of our time <em>adding</em> new features: for instance, <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-10-29-tk2\" target=\"_blank\">boilerplate templates</a>, <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-03-04-thundermla\" target=\"_blank\">custom on-device schedulers</a>, <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-03-15-tk-blackwell\" target=\"_blank\">Blackwell support</a>, <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-11-27-tk-fp8\" target=\"_blank\">FP8 support</a>, <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-17-pk\" target=\"_blank\">multi-GPU support</a>, and <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-09-28-tp-llama-main\" target=\"_blank\">Megakernels</a>.</p>\n<p>This release is different in that it's as much about <em>subtraction</em> as addition: we refactored the internals, hunted down unnecessary memory instructions and assembler inefficiencies, reduced build system complexities, and identified many surprising behaviors on modern Nvidia GPUs that guide how kernels should <em>not</em> be optimized.</p>\n<p>Thus, the goal of this post is to briefly announce the release, and then share some of our learnings. On the release side, ThunderKittens 2.0 brings:</p>\n<ul>\n<li><strong>New features</strong>: <strong>MXFP8/NVFP4 support</strong>, CLC scheduling, tensor memory controllability, many new utilities, PDL, and more.</li>\n<li><strong>Major refactor of the internal code</strong>, during which we found a number of subtle inefficiencies described throughout the rest of this post.</li>\n<li><strong>Much simpler build structure</strong> for all of our example kernels, so you (or your agent) can easily adapt them for your own use!</li>\n<li><strong>Contributions from industry</strong>: many companies have their own internal fork of ThunderKittens, and many were generous enough to contribute them back to us!</li>\n</ul>\n<p>These changes enabled us to write even faster kernels with new optimization strategies and fewer lines of code. As an example, we present the new state-of-the-art <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/ThunderKittens/tree/main/kernels/gemm/bf16_b200\" target=\"_blank\">BF16</a> / <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/ThunderKittens/tree/main/kernels/gemm/mxfp8_b200\" target=\"_blank\">MXFP8</a> / <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/ThunderKittens/tree/main/kernels/gemm/nvfp4_b200\" target=\"_blank\">NVFP4</a> GEMM kernels that surpass or match cuBLAS performance on Nvidia B200s:</p>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:75%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2026-02-19-tk-2/figure-1.png\" alt=\"New ThunderKittens kernels\"><p><em>Figure 1: New Kernels! All of the kernels (both TK and cuBLAS) were benchmarked using bitwise-identical random inputs with 500 warmup iterations, 100 profiling iterations, and L2 cache eviction. Details on the benchmarking method are described later in this post.</em></p></div>\n<p>We also updated all of our <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/ThunderKittens/tree/main/kernels\" target=\"_blank\">existing example kernels</a> to use the newer APIs, and are actively implementing more state-of-the-art kernels with TK (e.g., Flash Attention 4, grouped GEMMs, GEMV).</p>\n<p>That's it for the release! Please check out our <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/ThunderKittens\" target=\"_blank\">repository</a> for the details. The rest of this post cherry-picks some of the interesting technical details we found while optimizing TK. Specifically, we'll discuss:</p>\n<ul>\n<li><strong>Memory consistency</strong>. Can't escape it if you want to squeeze out the last few TFLOPs! Tightening memory synchronization with proper reasoning is crucial to getting peak performance.</li>\n<li><strong>Tensor core and memory pipelining</strong>. Some tensor core instructions are implicitly pipelined without proper documentation, and the best memory pipelining strategy for the given workload might not be so obvious.</li>\n<li><strong>Hinting the PTX assembler properly</strong>. It really doesn't trust us otherwise! Logically identical code can produce meaningfully different instructions depending on how it's written.</li>\n<li><strong>Occupancy</strong>. Don't trust what the code suggests; distributed shared memory does not work identically across all SMs, and tensor core instructions silently limit occupancy.</li>\n<li><strong>Benchmarking GPU kernels correctly</strong>, with L2 usage and power consumption in mind.</li>\n</ul>\n<p>We deliberately chose topics that are interesting and not well-covered elsewhere. These are the <strong>last</strong> bits to really squeeze out everything. For obtaining the first 90% of TFLOPs, we recommend reading <a href=\"https://www.modular.com/blog/matrix-multiplication-on-nvidias-blackwell-part-1-introduction\" target=\"_blank\">this great blog post</a> from Modular.</p>\n<h2>Memory consistency</h2>\n<p>CUDA/PTX provides many different kinds of memory consistency primitives. Missing memory synchronization leads to race conditions, while unnecessary ones lead to a loss of TFLOPs. In fact, tightening the acquire/release pattern usage was one of the last optimizations required for our <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-09-28-tp-llama-main\" target=\"_blank\">Megakernel</a> implementation to surpass SGLang: we observed that a few loose fence instructions caused <strong>over 10% loss</strong> in performance.</p>\n<p>Here, we'll demonstrate how we reason about using the memory fence and how it can lead to performance improvements. As a running example for this section, ThunderKittens previously included the following code in its Blackwell blockscaled tensor core matrix multiplication path (the <code>mma(...)</code> function):</p>\n<div><pre><code class=\"language-cpp\"><span>.</span><span>.</span><span>.</span> <span>// Prepare instruction and matrix descriptors</span>\n\n<span>asm</span> <span>volatile</span><span>(</span><span>\"tcgen05.fence::after_thread_sync;\\n\"</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n<span>asm</span> <span>volatile</span><span>(</span><span>\"fence.proxy.async.shared::cta;\\n\"</span> <span>::</span><span>:</span> <span>\"memory\"</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n\n<span>.</span><span>.</span><span>.</span> <span>// Perform tcgen05.mma</span>\n</code></pre></div>\n<p>To provide a brief background on what is happening here: this code is performing the 5th generation tensor core matrix multiplication. The operands A and B matrix tiles reside in shared memory, and the output is accumulated onto tensor memory. In addition, this performs blockscaled matrix multiplies (e.g., MXFP8 or NVFP4); thus, the input scales for A and B reside in the tensor memory as well. Fully understanding blockscaled matrix multiplication does not really matter here, but you can read more about it in this <a href=\"https://cursor.com/blog/kernels\" target=\"_blank\">other blog post</a> if you are interested.</p>\n<p>So why the two fence instructions? Before the tensor cores begin consuming data (upon issuance of <code>tcgen05.mma</code>), we need to ensure that all inputs are loaded to their respective locations (tensor memory and shared memory) and are visible to the tensor cores. These fences serve as safety measures guaranteeing that all preceding loads and copies have completed and their effects are visible.</p>\n<p>However, profiling this path revealed that the two fences cost roughly 20-30 TFLOPs in compute throughput of the GEMM kernel. So the natural question was: do we actually need them?</p>\n<h3>Understanding the PTX memory consistency model</h3>\n<p>To understand what these fences do in the first place, we must understand the PTX memory consistency model better.</p>\n<p>In PTX, a memory write to shared or global memory by one thread is guaranteed to be visible to reads by other threads, provided the write and read are ordered by <em>causality</em>. Two memory operations X and Y are causally ordered if:</p>\n<ol>\n<li>X and Y are issued by the same thread,</li>\n<li>X synchronizes with Y, or</li>\n<li>There is another operation Z between X and Y, where X-Z and Z-Y are ordered by causality (transitivity).</li>\n</ol>\n<p>We say “X synchronizes with Y” if either (1) X and Y are both barrier operations (<code>bar/barrier</code>) executed on the same barrier, or (2) if X is write-release, Y is read-acquire, and Y observes the value written by X.
