There are additional nuances and cases on this, but these should suffice for the purposes of this post.</p>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:50%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2026-02-19-tk-2/figure-2.png\" alt=\"Examples of causality in PTX\"><p><em>Figure 2: Examples of memory operations ordered by causality in PTX.</em></p></div>\n<p>There is also an additional concept of <em>memory proxy</em>. A proxy refers to a group of memory access methods. Most memory operations (e.g., <code>st</code>, <code>ld</code>) use the <em>generic proxy</em>. However, some asynchronous operations like <code>tcgen05.mma</code> or <code>cp.async.bulk.tensor</code> (TMA) use the <em>async proxy</em>. The above causality ordering only holds within the same proxy. In order for causality to hold between two different proxies, a proxy fence (<code>fence.proxy</code>) instruction must be inserted in between.</p>\n<h3>Can the tensor cores observe the inputs?</h3>\n<p>By the time the tensor cores begin matrix multiplication (via the issuance of the <code>tcgen05.mma</code> instruction), they must be able to observe:</p>\n<ol>\n<li>The input A and B tiles, loaded through TMA into shared memory (i.e., through the <code>cp.async.bulk.tensor</code> instruction).</li>\n<li>The input scales, loaded through the <code>tcgen05.cp</code> instruction into tensor memory.</li>\n</ol>\n<p>Note that the tensor cores must also observe that the epilogue threads are no longer accessing the accumulator, but we set that aside for this post.</p>\n<p>We must fully understand what happens between the writes (<code>cp.async.bulk.tensor</code> and <code>tcgen05.cp</code>) and the read (<code>tcgen05.mma</code>), and to determine whether the inputs are guaranteed to be visible to the tensor cores. Answering this requires collecting and combining information scattered across several parts of the PTX documentation, then reasoning about it carefully. Below, we cite the relevant sections as we go.</p>\n<p>First, can the tensor cores observe the shared memory filled by TMA? Let's work through this step by step.</p>\n<ol>\n<li>The first thing that happens is memory copy operation from global memory to shared memory. This happens as part of the TMA load instruction (<code>cp.async.bulk.tensor</code>) and is a weak memory operation (<a href=\"https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/parallel-thread-execution/#data-movement-and-conversion-instructions-cp-async-bulk-tensor\" target=\"_blank\">section 9.7.9.27.1.2</a>) performed through the async proxy (<a href=\"https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/parallel-thread-execution/#async-proxy\" target=\"_blank\">section 9.7.9.25.2</a>).</li>\n<li>As part of the same TMA load instruction, an <code>mbarrier</code> <code>complete-tx</code> operation follows immediately. This operation is <em>implicitly ordered</em> with regard to the preceding memory copy operation (<a href=\"https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/parallel-thread-execution/#program-order-async-operations\" target=\"_blank\">section 8.9.1.1</a>) and is a <em>release</em> operation (<a href=\"https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/parallel-thread-execution/#data-movement-and-conversion-instructions-cp-async-bulk-tensor\" target=\"_blank\">section 9.7.9.27.1.2</a>). Also, an implicit generic-async proxy fence is inserted right after completion (<a href=\"https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/parallel-thread-execution/#async-proxy\" target=\"_blank\">section 9.7.9.25.2</a>).</li>\n<li>The <code>mbarrier</code> <code>try-wait</code> operation that precedes the tensor core matrix multiplication is, by default, an acquire operation (<a href=\"https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/parallel-thread-execution/#parallel-synchronization-and-communication-instructions-mbarrier-test-wait-try-wait\" target=\"_blank\">section 9.7.13.15.16</a>), and ThunderKittens uses exactly this default behavior. Thus, this establishes a causality order with the TMA load.</li>\n<li>Finally, the <code>tcgen05.mma</code> instruction executes in the same thread that issued the <code>mbarrer try-wait</code> instruction, preserving the causality order, and reads from shared memory through the async proxy (<a href=\"https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/parallel-thread-execution/#tcgen05-memory-consistency-model-smem-access\" target=\"_blank\">section 9.7.16.6.5</a>), the same proxy used by <code>cp.async.bulk.tensor</code>.</li>\n</ol>\n<p>The verdict is that, for this specific scenario, causality order is already established between the TMA write and the tensor core read due to transitivity! No additional memory fences are needed for the shared memory read. One down, one to go.</p>\n<p>Second, can the tensor cores observe the tensor memory filled in by <code>tcgen05.cp</code>?</p>\n<ol>\n<li>According to <a href=\"https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/parallel-thread-execution/#tcgen05-memory-consistency-model-pipelined-instructions\" target=\"_blank\">section 9.7.16.6.2</a>, <code>tcgen05.mma</code> is implicitly pipelined with regard to <code>tcgen05.cp</code> (we discuss this in more detail in the next section).</li>\n<li>According to <a href=\"https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/parallel-thread-execution/#tcgen05-memory-consistency-model-canonical-sync-patterns-pipelined-same-thread\" target=\"_blank\">section 9.7.16.6.4.1</a>, implicitly pipelined instructions do not require a memory ordering mechanism.</li>\n</ol>\n<p>Combining these two facts, we can conclude that <code>tcgen05.cp</code> and <code>tcgen05.mma</code>, when issued by the same thread, are automatically well-ordered with respect to each other. As we will explain shortly, this is a canonical pattern for writing blockscaled GEMM kernels (and we have not found a case where issuing these instructions from different threads is beneficial). The fence that would otherwise ensure visibility of <code>tcgen05.cp</code> to <code>tcgen05.mma</code> (i.e., <code>after_thread_sync</code> in the example code above) is therefore unnecessary.</p>\n<p>This is great. Neither of the two memory fences were needed. Removing unnecessary fences like these here and there gave us roughly a 20 TFLOP/s boost across our GEMM and attention kernels.</p>\n<h2>Tensor core/memory pipelining</h2>\n<h3>Pipelining <code>tcgen05.cp</code> with <code>tcgen05.mma</code></h3>\n<p>For our MXFP8/NVFP4 kernels, the major bottleneck was loading scale values into tensor memory.</p>\n<p>Apologies in advance for throwing a bunch of numbers at you, but this is needed for the discussion: MXFP8 uses one scale value per 32 elements, and NVFP4 uses one per 16 elements. To fully utilize the tensor cores, Blackwell GPUs require a 128x128x32 GEMM shape per CTA for MXFP8 and 128x128x64 for NVFP4. The canonical way to pipeline <code>tcgen05.mma</code> is to provide enough data for 4 consecutive MMAs without interruption; that is, 128x128x128 per CTA for MXFP8 and 128x128x256 for NVFP4. We call these 4 consecutive MMAs one MMA stage.</p>\n<p>From these numbers, we can derive the following (we disregard scale swizzling for this post):</p>\n<ul>\n<li>We need <code>128 * 128 / 32 = 512</code> scale values per operand per MMA stage per CTA for MXFP8.</li>\n<li>We need <code>128 * 256 / 16 = 2048</code> scale values per operand per MMA stage per CTA for NVFP4.</li>\n</ul>\n<p>The problem is that blockscaled MMA needs the same scale values to be broadcasted to all 4 warps in tensor memory, and only one instruction supports this: <code>tcgen05.cp.32x128.warpx4</code>. As its name suggests, this copies only <code>32 * 128 / 8 = 512</code> values per invocation (the 128 refers to bytes). This is great for MXFP8: a single <code>tcgen05.cp</code> per operand is enough to feed one tensor core MMA stage.</p>\n<p>For NVFP4, however, this means you need 4 <code>tcgen05.cp</code> instructions per MMA stage just to supply one operand. With both A and B, that doubles. Then, due to a subtle layout detail on the B side, the B matrix requires an additional 2x factor, bringing the total to 12 <code>tcgen05.cp</code> instructions per MMA stage.</p>\n<p>That's a lot, and our original kernel design looked something like this:</p>\n<div><pre><code class=\"language-text\">if (warp_id == 0) {\n    Load A and B tiles with TMA (HBM -&#x26;gt; SMEM)\n} else if (warp_id == 1) {\n    Load A and B scales with TMA (HBM -&#x26;gt; SMEM)\n} else if (warp_id == 2) {\n    Wait for A and B scales to arrive at SMEM\n    Load A and B scales with tcgen05.cp (SMEM -&#x26;gt; TMEM)\n} else if (warp_id == 3) {\n    Wait for A and B tiles to arrive at SMEM\n    Wait for A and B scales to arrive at TMEM\n    Run 4 MMAs\n}</code></pre></div>\n<p>In the pseudocode above, <code>warp_id == 2</code> would need to issue 12 <code>tcgen05.cp</code> instructions per MMA stage, and <code>warp_id == 3</code> would then have to explicitly wait for all of them to complete. With these overheads, our kernel throughput suffered badly, roughly 10% lower than that of state-of-the-art kernels.</p>\n<p>However, after a few weeks of struggling, something caught our eyes:</p>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:75%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2026-02-19-tk-2/figure-3.png\" alt=\"PTX documentation excerpt\"><p><em>Figure 3: PTX documentation section 9.7.16.2.</em></p></div>\n<p>Notice how it describes that the <code>tcgen05.copy</code> instruction is implicitly pipelined with respect to <code>tcgen05.mma</code>. We had read the PTX documentation countless times, but only at this point did we <strong>realize that <code>tcgen05.copy</code> was a typo of <code>tcgen05.cp</code></strong>; the hypothetical <code>tcgen05.copy</code> instruction never appears again anywhere in the document. The lack of any example showing <code>tcgen05.cp</code> pipelined with <code>tcgen05.mma</code> compounded the confusion, preventing us from realizing this for a surprisingly long time.</p>\n<p>With this new knowledge, we arrived at a new design. By merging the copy and MMA work into the same thread and removing the now-unnecessary barrier waits, we recovered the missing ~500 TFLOP/s, roughly a 10% improvement for NVFP4 GEMM.</p>\n<div><pre><code class=\"language-text\">if (warp_id == 0) {\n  Load A and B tiles with TMA (HBM -&#x26;gt; SMEM)\n} else if (warp_id == 1) {\n  Load A and B scales with TMA (HBM -&#x26;gt; SMEM)\n} else if (warp_id == 3) {\n  Wait for A and B tiles to arrive at SMEM\n  Wait for A and B scales to arrive at SMEM\n  Load A and B scales with tcgen05.cp (SMEM -&#x26;gt; TMEM)\n  Run 4 MMAs\n}</code></pre></div>\n<h3>Pipelining tensor memory</h3>\n<p>A common pattern described in many writings is to pipeline MMA with tensor memory reads. The key idea is this: tensor memory is organized as a 128x512 array, and non-blockscaled <code>tcgen05.mma</code> instructions accumulate into at most 128x256 of the tensor memory at a time. So, the natural approach is to alternate between the two 128x256 slots, such that one is used by the tensor cores for accumulation, and the other is used by epilogue threads to read out the results of the previous MMA operation.</p>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2026-02-19-tk-2/figure-4.png\" alt=\"Tensor memory buffering with split slots\"><p><em>Figure 4: Tensor memory buffering. The 128x512 tensor memory per SM is divided into two slots: one accessed by tensor cores, the other accessed by epilogue threads simultaneously.</em></p></div>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2026-02-19-tk-2/figure-5.png\" alt=\"Pipeline visualization for split-slot buffering\"><p><em>Figure 5: Visualization of the resulting pipeline from the above buffering scheme.</em></p></div>\n<p>This is theoretically sound in that there exists no “pipeline bubble” where the tensor cores sit idle waiting for the epilogue threads to finish. In practice, we found this to be the most efficient choice for small GEMM sizes (roughly below 2048x2048x2048).</p>\n<p>For larger sizes, however, we found \"double accumulation\" pattern to work better. In this scheme, we run two MMA pipelines simultaneously, both sharing the same A tile while operating on different B tiles. This means we accumulate across the entire 128x512 tensor memory throughout, and the tensor cores wait while the epilogue threads drain it.</p>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2026-02-19-tk-2/figure-6.png\" alt=\"Alternative tensor memory buffering scheme\"><p><em>Figure 6: An alternative tensor memory buffering scheme. The 128x512 tensor memory per SM is divided into two slots, both accessed by either the tensor cores or the epilogue threads.</em></p></div>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2026-02-19-tk-2/figure-7.png\" alt=\"Pipeline visualization for double accumulation\"><p><em>Figure 7: Visualization of the resulting pipeline. Note that A x B0 and A x B1 are serialized internally at the tensor core hardware, but this is omitted from the diagram for simplicity.</em></p></div>\n<p>This introduces a slight bubble between MMAs: the tensor cores must wait for the epilogue threads to read all 256 KB (<code>128 * 512 * 4</code> bytes per element) from tensor memory before starting the next operation. But the reduced memory traffic from sharing the A tile compensates for larger GEMMs, giving us an additional ~100 TFLOP/s for our BF16 GEMM kernel.</p>\n<h2>PTX assembler behavior on SM90+ single-threaded instructions</h2>\n<p><em>Special thanks to <a href=\"https://www.linkedin.com/in/nashbrown1/\" target=\"_blank\">Nash Brown</a> for identifying this!</em></p>\n<p>A common pattern in modern GPU kernels is to perform warp specialization where each warp is assigned a single role. On Hopper and Blackwell GPUs, many of these roles only require a single thread issuing instructions within the warp. For instance, a \"loader\" warp issues TMA loads, which need only one thread. The usual ThunderKittens pattern looks like this:</p>\n<div><pre><code class=\"language-cpp\"><span>if</span> <span>(</span>warpgroup<span>::</span><span>groupid</span><span>(</span><span>)</span> <span>==</span> <span>0</span><span>)</span> <span>{</span>\n    <span>if</span> <span>(</span>warpgroup<span>::</span><span>warpid</span><span>(</span><span>)</span> <span>==</span> <span>0</span> <span>&#x26;&#x26;</span> warp<span>::</span><span>laneid</span><span>(</span><span>)</span> <span>==</span> <span>0</span><span>)</span> <span>{</span>\n        tma<span>::</span><span>expect_bytes</span><span>(</span>arrived<span>,</span> <span>sizeof</span><span>(</span>smem<span>)</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n        tma<span>::</span><span>load_async</span><span>(</span>smem<span>,</span> A<span>,</span> <span>{</span><span>0</span><span>,</span> <span>0</span><span>}</span><span>,</span> arrived<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>}</span>\n<span>}</span>\n</code></pre></div>\n<p>Here, <code>arrived</code> is an <code>mbarrier</code> object and <code>smem</code> is a shared memory tile. To those unfamiliar with ThunderKittens, this code selects the first warpgroup's first warp, then lane 0 within that warp. This makes sense at the source level since TMA only needs to be issued by a single thread.</p>\n<p>But when you inspect the SASS generated by this code, it looks like the following:</p>\n<div><pre><code class=\"language-text\">0x0260 @P0 ELECT P1, URZ, PT ;\n0x0270     UTMALDG.5D [UR8], [UR4] ;\n0x0280 @P1 PLOP3.LUT P0, PT, P1, PT, PT, 0x8, 0x80 ;\n0x0290     PLOP3.LUT P1, PT, PT, PT, PT, 0x8, 0x80 ;\n0x02a0 @P0 BRA.U.ANY 0x260 ;</code></pre></div>\n<p>Note that this code is already guarded by <code>warpgroup::warpid() == 0 &#x26;&#x26; warp::laneid() == 0</code>, so only a single thread can reach it. Yet the PTX assembler has inserted a loop: it elects a thread, issues the TMA load, removes that thread from the active set, branches back to <code>0x0260</code>, elects another from the remaining 31, and repeats, cycling through all 32 threads in turn. In practice, however, only one thread ever executes this section of code.</p>\n<p>Why does this happen? There are two reasons: (1) the <code>UTMALDG</code> instruction (SASS equivalent for TMA load) cannot be issued by multiple threads of the same warp simultaneously, and (2) the PTX assembler cannot prove that this code path is reached by only a single thread, so it conservatively inserts a serialization loop.</p>\n<p>How can we avoid this? We must use an instruction that the PTX assembler knows selects exactly one thread: the <code>elect.sync</code> instruction. This PTX instruction elects a single thread from the current warp. The effect is the same as <code>warp::laneid() == 0</code>, but the assembler recognizes the intent and avoids the loop.</p>\n<p>In ThunderKittens, you can use the <code>warp::elect_leader()</code> function to do this. If you change the code like the following:</p>\n<div><pre><code class=\"language-cpp\"><span>if</span> <span>(</span>warpgroup<span>::</span><span>groupid</span><span>(</span><span>)</span> <span>==</span> <span>0</span><span>)</span> <span>{</span>\n    <span>if</span> <span>(</span>warpgroup<span>::</span><span>warpid</span><span>(</span><span>)</span> <span>==</span> <span>0</span> <span>&#x26;&#x26;</span> warp<span>::</span><span>elect_leader</span><span>(</span><span>)</span><span>)</span> <span>{</span>\n        tma<span>::</span><span>expect_bytes</span><span>(</span>arrived<span>,</span> <span>sizeof</span><span>(</span>smem<span>)</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n        tma<span>::</span><span>load_async</span><span>(</span>smem<span>,</span> A<span>,</span> <span>{</span><span>0</span><span>,</span> <span>0</span><span>}</span><span>,</span> arrived<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>}</span>\n<span>}</span>\n</code></pre></div>\n<p>The generated SASS changes to:</p>\n<div><pre><code class=\"language-text\">0x01e0*/      ELECT P0, URZ, PT ;\n0x01f0*/ @!P0 BRA 0x2d0 ;\n... (address calculation)\n0x02b0*/      UTMALDG.5D [UR8], [UR4] ;\n0x02d0*/      BSYNC.RECONVERGENT B0 ;</code></pre></div>\n<p>No loops! By applying this pattern to all single-threaded instructions across our kernels, we were able to improve compute throughput by up to 10% for small-shaped GEMMs.</p>\n<h2>Occupancy</h2>\n<p>Here we describe a few of our failure modes, so you don't fall into the same traps :\\</p>\n<h3>Not all SMs support all cluster sizes</h3>\n<p><a href=\"https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/cuda-programming-guide/01-introduction/programming-model.html#thread-block-clusters\" target=\"_blank\">Threadblock clusters</a> larger than 2 can improve compute throughput by enabling distributed shared memory and reducing memory controller traffic. But there was a caveat: <strong>some cluster sizes prevent the scheduler from fully utilizing all SMs</strong>.</p>\n<p>What does this even mean? Suppose we want to implement a persistent grid kernel on a B200 GPU. The B200 has 148 SMs, so a natural pattern is to set the grid size to 148, have each threadblock consume most or all of an SM's shared memory, specify the cluster size via <code>__cluster_dims__(n)</code>, and launch.</p>\n<p>Now, this works well if you have a cluster size of 2: each SM pair forms a cluster, and you have a total of <code>148 / 2 = 74</code> threadblock clusters running.</p>\n<p>But what about a cluster size of 4? We would expect groups of 4 SMs to team up, giving <code>148 / 4 = 37</code> clusters, right?</p>\n<p>To test this, we can have a kernel print its cluster and block information, sleep for a noticeable duration, and return:</p>\n<div><pre><code class=\"language-cpp\">__global__ <span>__cluster_dims__</span><span>(</span><span>2</span><span>)</span> <span>void</span> <span>kernel</span><span>(</span><span>const</span> __grid_constant__ globals<span>&#x3C;</span>C<span>></span> g<span>)</span> <span>{</span>\n    <span>printf</span><span>(</span><span>\"num_clusters=%d, cluster_idx=%d, cluster_size=%d, cta_rank=%d, blockIdx.x=%d\\n\"</span><span>,</span> <span>nclusterid</span><span>(</span><span>)</span><span>.</span>x<span>,</span> <span>clusterIdx</span><span>(</span><span>)</span><span>.</span>x<span>,</span> <span>cluster_nctarank</span><span>(</span><span>)</span><span>,</span> <span>cluster_ctarank</span><span>(</span><span>)</span><span>,</span> blockIdx<span>.</span>x<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>for</span> <span>(</span><span>int</span> i <span>=</span> <span>0</span><span>;</span> i <span>&#x3C;</span> <span>500000</span><span>;</span> i<span>++</span><span>)</span> <span>__nanosleep</span><span>(</span><span>10000</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n<span>}</span>\n</code></pre></div>\n<p>If you run this code, you'll see that the result is quite surprising: only 132 SMs are active at a time. The remaining 16 threadblocks are scheduled only after the first 132 exit. This was quite strange, so we decided to see how it behaves for all powers-of-2 cluster sizes:</p>\n<table><thead><tr><th align=\"left\">Cluster Size</th><th align=\"left\">Active SMs</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td align=\"left\">2 (grid size 148)</td><td align=\"left\">148</td></tr><tr><td align=\"left\">4 (grid size 148)</td><td align=\"left\">132</td></tr><tr><td align=\"left\">8 (grid size 144)</td><td align=\"left\">120</td></tr><tr><td align=\"left\">16 (grid size 144)</td><td align=\"left\">112</td></tr></tbody></table>\n<p style=\"text-align:center\"><em>Table 1. Active SMs by cluster size on B200.</em></p>\n<p>This implies that blindly setting a cluster size greater than 2 on a persistent grid kernel produces a mysterious slowdown that can take some time to diagnose. Our hypothesis is that distributed shared memory requires internal wiring between the SMs and it was a hardware engineering decision to choose not to wire certain SMs for simpler implementation.</p>\n<p>Does this mean Nvidia fooled us with threadblock clusters and distributed shared memory? The <code>__cluster_dims__()</code> attribute is certainly misleading, but no. It turns out that by not using <code>__cluster_dims__</code> and instead launching kernels with <code>cudaLaunchKernelEx</code>, you gain the ability to specify two cluster sizes: a preferred size and a minimum size.</p>\n<p>The scheduler first fills SMs using the preferred cluster size (e.g., 132 SMs filled with 4-clusters), then uses the minimum cluster size to fill the remainder. <strong>The kernel must be written to support both sizes</strong>. In ThunderKittens 2.0, we added the <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/ThunderKittens/blob/9df1476fe54d56fcb255aa7cf3128cc9222827f3/include/common/util.cuh#L454\" target=\"_blank\"><code>LaunchConfig</code></a> utility for this purpose (it is a thin wrapper around the CUDA API, so feel free to use the API directly if you prefer).</p>\n<h3>TCGEN05 instructions hard-limit per-SM occupancy</h3>\n<p>Another surprising thing we found was that the moment a kernel accesses tensor memory, its <strong>maximum per-SM occupancy is hard-limited to 1</strong>. We can see this with a simple test code:</p>\n<div><pre><code class=\"language-cpp\">__global__ <span>void</span> <span>empty_kernel</span><span>(</span><span>)</span> <span>{</span><span>}</span>\n__global__ <span>void</span> <span>tcgen05_kernel</span><span>(</span><span>)</span> <span>{</span>\n    __shared__ <span>uint32_t</span> tmem_addr<span>;</span>\n    <span>asm</span> <span>volatile</span><span>(</span>\n        <span>\"{tcgen05.alloc.cta_group::1.sync.aligned.shared::cta.b32  [%0], %1;}\"</span>\n    <span>::</span>  <span>\"l\"</span><span>(</span><span><span>reinterpret_cast</span><span><span>&#x3C;</span><span>uint64_t</span><span>></span></span></span><span>(</span><span>&#x26;</span>tmem_addr<span>)</span><span>)</span><span>,</span> <span>\"n\"</span><span>(</span><span>128</span><span>)</span>\n    <span>)</span><span>;</span>\n<span>}</span>\n\n<span>void</span> <span>print_occupancy</span><span>(</span><span>const</span> <span>char</span><span>*</span> name<span>,</span> <span>const</span> <span>void</span><span>*</span> kernel<span>,</span> <span>int</span> block_size<span>)</span> <span>{</span>\n    <span>int</span> max_blocks_per_sm <span>=</span> <span>-</span><span>1</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>CUDACHECK</span><span>(</span><span>cudaOccupancyMaxActiveBlocksPerMultiprocessor</span><span>(</span>\n        <span>&#x26;</span>max_blocks_per_sm<span>,</span>\n        kernel<span>,</span>\n        block_size<span>,</span>\n        <span>0</span>\n    <span>)</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>printf</span><span>(</span><span>\"%s: Max blocks per SM: %d\\n\"</span><span>,</span> name<span>,</span> max_blocks_per_sm<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n<span>}</span>\n\n<span>int</span> <span>main</span><span>(</span><span>)</span> <span>{</span>\n    <span>const</span> <span>int</span> block_size <span>=</span> <span>256</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>print_occupancy</span><span>(</span><span>\"empty_kernel\"</span><span>,</span> <span><span>reinterpret_cast</span><span><span>&#x3C;</span><span>const</span> <span>void</span><span>*</span><span>></span></span></span><span>(</span>empty_kernel<span>)</span><span>,</span> block_size<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>print_occupancy</span><span>(</span><span>\"tcgen05_kernel\"</span><span>,</span> <span><span>reinterpret_cast</span><span><span>&#x3C;</span><span>const</span> <span>void</span><span>*</span><span>></span></span></span><span>(</span>tcgen05_kernel<span>)</span><span>,</span> block_size<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>return</span> <span>0</span><span>;</span>\n<span>}</span>\n</code></pre></div>\n<p>When you compile and run the above code, the output shows that <code>tcgen05_kernel</code> has a maximum occupancy of 1 block per SM. This is quite surprising since the kernel allocates only one quarter of the available tensor memory, and all tensor memory allocation and management instructions in PTX implicitly suggest that tensor memory is designed to be shared among multiple resident blocks. Nonetheless, it appears that increasing per-SM occupancy is not a viable optimization strategy when utilizing tensor cores in a Blackwell kernel.</p>\n<h2>Benchmarking GPU kernels properly</h2>\n<p>After weeks of benchmarking GEMM kernels, one thing that consistently bothered us was the seemingly unbeatable speed of CUTLASS GEMM kernels when benchmarked with their own profiler (the <a href=\"https://docs.nvidia.com/cutlass/latest/media/docs/cpp/profiler.html\" target=\"_blank\">CUTLASS Profiler</a>). They were consistently 100-150 TFLOPs faster than ours. At one point, <a href=\"https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=45458948\" target=\"_blank\">we were adding the prefix <code>\"cutlass\"</code> to our kernel names</a> and were half-convinced that Nvidia had private assembler optimization passes reserved for their own code.</p>\n<p>Fortunately, there was no such thing for this case. But it did turn out that the CUTLASS Profiler, by default, rounds input values to nearest integers before passing them to the kernels. Aside from the fact that this is a questionable design choice, this heavily affects the performance. Integer-like input matrices produce less bit-flipping in the GPU's transistors, which reduces power consumption and, consequently, the likelihood of clock throttling.</p>\n<p>This triggered us to investigate further, we realized just how many factors can influence observed TFLOPs. Every subtle benchmark design decision matters: using 2 CUDA events versus <code>N*2</code> (where <code>N</code> is the number of profiling iterations), how you clear L2 cache between iterations (explicit flush vs natural eviction through input groups), benchmarking from C++ versus PyTorch, what random distribution and seed you use, and so on.</p>\n<p>In our experience, the set of design choices described above can account for up to a 10% difference in results. After a series of experiments, we settled on the following convention for benchmarking our kernels and reporting performance numbers publicly:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>Use bitwise identical random inputs (usually from uniform distribution; range depends on the precision being used).</li>\n<li>If the total input size is less than 3x the L2 cache size (e.g., 128 MB on B200), use multiple input groups. This way, each group's data naturally evicts the previous group's residency in L2, simulating cold-cache conditions. <em>We found that explicit cache flushing meaningfully slows down the measured time even with proper CUDA event usage</em>.</li>\n<li>Run 500 warmup iterations before profiling, to reach a power-steady state.</li>\n<li>Run 100 profiling iterations, with the kernels launched back-to-back without intermediate synchronization.</li>\n<li>Measure time using 2 CUDA events recorded immediately before and after all of the profiling iterations.</li>\n<li>Give GPUs short idle period between benchmarking two kernels, to allow thermal cooldown.</li>\n</ul>\n<p>We encourage adopting and/or improving this convention. The pseudocode below illustrates the approach. Note that even though this code is in CUDA C++, it's also fully possible to do exactly the same in PyTorch through the <code>torch.cuda</code> API:</p>\n<div><pre><code class=\"language-cpp\"><span>int</span> l2_cache_size<span>;</span>\n<span>cudaDeviceGetAttribute</span><span>(</span><span>&#x26;</span>l2_cache_size<span>,</span> cudaDevAttrL2CacheSize<span>,</span> <span>0</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n<span>int</span> num_input_groups <span>=</span> <span>(</span>input_size <span>>=</span> l2_cache_size <span>*</span> <span>3</span><span>)</span> <span>?</span> <span>1</span> <span>:</span> <span>int</span><span>(</span>l2_cache_size <span>*</span> <span>3</span> <span>/</span> input_size<span>)</span> <span>+</span> <span>1</span><span>;</span>\n\n<span>// &#x3C;Prepare `num_input_groups` set of inputs, ensuring they are bitwise-identical for all kernels being benchmarked></span>\n\n<span>cudaDeviceSynchronize</span><span>(</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n\n<span>for</span> <span>(</span><span>int</span> i <span>=</span> <span>0</span><span>;</span> i <span>&#x3C;</span> <span>500</span><span>;</span> i<span>++</span><span>)</span> <span>{</span>\n    input_group_index <span>=</span> i <span>%</span> num_input_groups<span>;</span>\n    <span>launch_kernel</span><span>(</span>inputs<span>[</span>input_group_index<span>]</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n<span>}</span>\n\n<span>cudaEventRecord</span><span>(</span>start_event<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n\n<span>for</span> <span>(</span><span>int</span> i <span>=</span> <span>0</span><span>;</span> i <span>&#x3C;</span> <span>100</span><span>;</span> i<span>++</span><span>)</span> <span>{</span>\n    input_group_index <span>=</span> i <span>%</span> num_input_groups<span>;</span>\n    <span>launch_kernel</span><span>(</span>inputs<span>[</span>input_group_index<span>]</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n<span>}</span>\n\n<span>cudaEventRecord</span><span>(</span>end_event<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n<span>cudaEventSynchronize</span><span>(</span>end_event<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n\n<span>float</span> milliseconds<span>;</span>\n<span>cudaEventElapsedTime</span><span>(</span><span>&#x26;</span>milliseconds<span>,</span> start_event<span>,</span> end_event<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n\n<span>// &#x3C;Save time and throughput></span>\n\n<span>sleep_ms</span><span>(</span><span>500</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n</code></pre></div>\n<h2>What's next?</h2>\n<p>We're happy to say that ThunderKittens is now fully optimized for Blackwell and can still produce state-of-the-art kernels with significantly fewer lines of code than other CUDA DSLs. We're also happy to see TK 2.0 already being adopted by the industry! For instance, it powers training kernels for <a href=\"https://cursor.com/home\" target=\"_blank\">Cursor</a>'s Composer and inference kernels for <a href=\"https://www.together.ai/\" target=\"_blank\">Together AI</a>.</p>\n<p>Yet there is a variety of wild optimizations that could push these kernels even further. Most importantly, modern GPUs are composed of multiple chiplets, each with its own partition of L2 cache and HBM. The Nvidia B200, for instance, is two chiplets stitched together by a 10 TB/s chip-to-chip interconnect, significantly lower throughput than the L2 itself (roughly 21 TB/s by our measurement). This creates an unavoidable NUMA effect: memory throughput is limited by the interconnect bandwidth even on an L2 hit. If we can get this right, whether through kernel design or model architecture, there's a good chunk of speedup on the table.</p>\n<p>And we're not done with <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-09-28-tp-llama-main\" target=\"_blank\">Megakernels</a> yet! We've shown that Megakernels deliver significant speedups over state-of-the-art inference engines, even in compute-bound, GPU-friendly workloads like prefill. The remaining question is how to make them <em>accessible</em>. Rather than another DSL or standalone compiler, we believe what's needed is a fundamental change in ML infrastructure. PyTorch is a great frontend for representing compute graphs, and we're extremely grateful for its existence; but its 10+-year-old backend architecture may not be the best fit for extracting every last TFLOP from modern GPUs. We'll see!</p>\n<p>As always, if you'd like to learn more or contribute, feel free to reach out to Stuart at <a href=\"mailto:ssul@cs.stanford.edu\" target=\"_blank\">ssul@cs.stanford.edu</a> :)</p>\n<h2>Acknowledgements</h2>\n<p>We are grateful to <a href=\"https://cursor.com/home\" target=\"_blank\">Cursor</a> for generously providing the GPUs for this work. We also thank Simran Arora, Simon Guo, and Alex Waitz for their thoughtful feedback on this post.</p>,TL;DR: This is a release post for ThunderKittens 2.0, our cute little CUDA-embedded DSL, along with a technical deep dive for those who are…,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2026-02-19-tk-2,<a href=\"https://stuartsul.com/\">Stuart Sul</a>, <a href=\"https://cs.stanford.edu/people/chrismre/\">Chris Ré</a>,2026-07-22T18:37:38.424Z,2026-02-19T07:00:00.079Z,[{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2026-02-19-tk-2/figure-1.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":2400,\"height\":4500},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2026-02-19-tk-2/figure-2.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2026-02-19-tk-2/figure-3.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2026-02-19-tk-2/figure-4.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2026-02-19-tk-2/figure-5.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2026-02-19-tk-2/figure-6.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2026-02-19-tk-2/figure-7.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"}],163778663386687488,1211296081845936131,What Does Information Theory Say About Designing Agentic Systems?,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-12-29-agentic-it,<p><strong>TL;DR</strong>\nMany contemporary agentic systems exhibit a clear pattern: larger models interact with the user and orchestrate smaller models that directly interface with the data. In “Deep Research” systems (<a href=\"https://gemini.google/us/overview/deep-research/?hl=en\" target=\"_blank\">Google</a>, <a href=\"https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/multi-agent-research-system\" target=\"_blank\">Anthropic</a>), smaller worker models analyze targeted search results and provide summaries to a larger orchestrator model, which synthesizes a final research report. This separation of control and data plane is also evident in Claude Code and relatives (<a href=\"https://github.com/sst/opencode\" target=\"_blank\">OpenCode</a>), but it is unclear where scaling actually pays off. This compression-prediction structure isn’t new, an information-theoretic model helps us derive design principles that guide compute spending. Looking at the information actually communicated between the models showed that investing in compressors for relatively cheap mitigates the need for expensive frontier predictors.</p>\n<blockquote>\n<p>📄<a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.21720\" target=\"_blank\">Paper</a><br>\n💻<a href=\"https://github.com/shizhehe/agentic-information-theory\" target=\"_blank\">GitHub</a></p>\n</blockquote>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-12-29-agentic-it/minions-scaling.png\" alt=\"The Hazy Research Lab Cooking Juicy, Fact-Stuffed MLPs!\"><p><em>Picking the \"right\" workers is no easy task for Gru.</em></p></div>\n<h3>The Art of Designing Agentic Systems </h3>\n<p>Agentic systems are everywhere now—multiple language model agents (often running in parallel) help you <a href=\"https://www.claude.com/product/claude-code\" target=\"_blank\">debug your code</a>, <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/Minions\" target=\"_blank\">summarize a 200-page document</a>, or <a href=\"https://www.perplexity.ai/hub/blog/introducing-perplexity-deep-research\" target=\"_blank\">crawl the web for research papers.</a> </p>\n<p>Modern workflows routinely challenge the long-context capabilities of even the largest frontier models, resulting in a decline in model performance referred to as <a href=\"https://research.trychroma.com/context-rot\" target=\"_blank\">context rot</a>. Agentic systems split tasks across two models working in tandem. First, a larger <strong>orchestrator</strong> breaks a user query into focused sub-queries. Smaller <strong>workers</strong> go through large amounts of data to extract information that matters most for each query. Then, the <strong>orchestrator</strong> builds on those findings, either weaving them together into coherent outputs or sending out another round of queries. We observe that in these worker-orchestrator systems, the small model acts as a <strong>compressor</strong>—it reads the full context (<em>e.g.</em> search results) and provides a compressed view to the larger <strong>predictor</strong> model.</p>\n<p>But here’s the problem: we still don’t have a <strong>principled</strong> understanding of when to scale the compressor or the predictor. If we knew which component of a compression-prediction system actually drives performance, we could make surgical changes to the system and avoid paying for the wrong things.</p>\n<p>Thus, we ask:</p>\n<div style=\"text-align:center\"><blockquote><em>Which design decisions in a compression–prediction system are worth paying for?</em></blockquote></div>\n<h3>Not All Compute is Equal</h3>\n<p>We want to talk about where to spend compute, but <strong>not all compute is equal</strong>.</p>\n<p>Thanks to <a href=\"https://hai.stanford.edu/ai-index/2025-ai-index-report\" target=\"_blank\">fast-improving consumer hardware, and increasingly capable small</a> models, we forecast a world where near-SOTA compressors can run locally on laptops and phones in the coming years. These tailwinds point to an interesting future for distributed, hybrid agentic computing, which we’ve explored in our previous blogs (<a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-02-24-minions\" target=\"_blank\">Minions</a>, <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-11-ipw\" target=\"_blank\">Intelligence per Watt</a>): large cloud models orchestrate workers that run on user devices. </p>\n<p>Once you own the device, you can simply scale local models to the largest size the hardware can support, for free. Keeping computation local keeps data local, improving privacy and reducing the networking overhead required for cloud inference. </p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-12-29-agentic-it/scaling-trends.png\" style=\"width:100%\"><strong>Figure 1</strong>: Consumer laptops and phones can now run increasingly powerful large language models.</figure>\n<h3>Scale Smarter, not (always) Bigger</h3>\n<p>As compression-prediction systems grow larger and end-to-end sweeps are no longer feasible, there must be a smarter way than simply making every model bigger. We have to scale <strong>smarter</strong>—deciding where to spend compute and what information actually flows between models.</p>\n<p>Through large-scale experiments, we’ve distilled a few key principles for scaling compression-prediction systems efficiently:</p>\n<ol>\n<li><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-12-29-agentic-it#scaling-compressor\" target=\"_blank\"><strong>Compressors can be scaled at a sublinear computational cost</strong></a> — Larger compressors yield higher downstream QA accuracy, and output fewer tokens, so the total cost grows slowly. </li>\n<li><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-12-29-agentic-it#scaling-total\" target=\"_blank\"><strong>Spend on local compressors to save on remote predictors</strong></a> — It’s cheaper and more effective to scale compressors that can run on-device than to burn budget on ever-bigger predictors on the cloud. </li>\n<li><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-12-29-agentic-it#mutual-information-the-perplexity-for-agentic-communication\" target=\"_blank\"><strong>Optimize for information density</strong></a> — More intelligent models express more task-relevant information in fewer tokens; we can measure higher compression quality in their communication.</li>\n<li><strong>Compressor model family > compressor size > predictor size</strong> — Compressor model family is the most important knob to turn. Matching families doesn’t matter; some families are just better than others. </li>\n</ol>\n<p>In the rest of this post, we’ll unpack how we arrived at these principles. We’ll start from our information-theoretic perspective and trace empirical evidence across different domains and tasks.</p>\n<h3>An Information-Theoretic Problem in Disguise</h3>\n<p>Current compression-prediction systems are evaluated only on end-to-end performance on specific task-based evaluations. This conflates two distinct failure modes: information lost during compression, and information retained but poorly synthesized by the predictor.  We turn to information theory to unpack what information is actually communicated between the models, and do so in a way that is general across tasks and domains.</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-12-29-agentic-it/it-setup.png\" style=\"width:85%\"><strong>Figure 2</strong>: The <strong>compressor</strong> acts as a <strong>noisy channel</strong> transforming raw context X and into an efficient representation Z, which a <strong>predictor</strong> consumes to produce a final output Y.</figure>\n<p>Compression followed by downstream prediction is a classical setting in information theory. The compressor acts as a <strong>noisy channel</strong>—exactly how we view the <strong>worker</strong> models. You can actually measure compression efficacy—that is, how much signal survives the compression—between X and Z using <strong>mutual information (MI).</strong> You are able to evaluate the compression output independently from downstream performance, which allows us to come up with <strong>generalizable</strong> system design principles. </p>\n<h3 id=\"user-content-performance-lies-with-the-compressor\">Performance Lies with the Compressor</h3>\n<p>When your compression-prediction system struggles, <strong><em>should you invest in a larger compressor or predictor?</em></strong> </p>\n<p>To find out, we scaled both models across five realistic tasks that require extracting domain-specific information out of long context documents:</p>\n<ul>\n<li><strong>Medical QA</strong> on patient records over time (<a href=\"https://github.com/kbressem/LongHealth\" target=\"_blank\">LongHealth</a>)</li>\n<li><strong>Financial analysis</strong> of 100-page financial filings (<a href=\"https://github.com/patronus-ai/financebench/tree/main\" target=\"_blank\">FinanceBench</a>)</li>\n<li><strong>Question answering</strong> on academic research papers (<a href=\"https://huggingface.co/datasets/allenai/qasper\" target=\"_blank\">QASPER</a>)</li>\n<li><strong>Question answering</strong> over long web pages (<a href=\"https://huggingface.co/datasets/HuggingFaceFW/fineweb\" target=\"_blank\">FineWeb</a>)</li>\n<li><strong>Information recall</strong> across multi-session chats (<a href=\"https://wildchat.allen.ai/\" target=\"_blank\">WildChat</a>)</li>\n</ul>\n<p><span id=\"user-content-scaling-compressor\"><strong>Scaling compressors.</strong></span> We fix the predictor (GPT-4o) and scale the compressor from 1–1.5B up to 7–12B. We observe interesting scaling trends:</p>\n<ul>\n<li><strong>Downstream performance</strong> — Larger compressors—<a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/Minions\" target=\"_blank\">as expected</a>—improve downstream performance.</li>\n<li><strong>Compression length</strong> — Larger compressors produce outputs that are <strong>more concise</strong> (up to 4.6x !).</li>\n<li><strong>Compression compute cost</strong> — Because larger compressors generate <strong>fewer tokens,</strong> the number of math operations that occur with each compression (FLOPs-per-generation) scales <strong>sublinearly</strong> with model size; strikingly, Qwen-2.5 compressors scale <strong>almost “for free”</strong> (just 1.3% extra FLOPs-per-generation!).</li>\n</ul>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-12-29-agentic-it/compressor-scaling.png\" style=\"width:100%\"><strong>Figure 3</strong>: Compressors improve accuracy at little extra cost, validating principle 1.</figure>\n<p>And this holds even when we explicitly ask the compressor to output a fixed number of sentences!</p>\n<p><span id=\"user-content-scaling-total\"><strong>Scaling predictors.</strong></span> Now flip it: we fix the compressor, scale the predictor from 1B up to 405B. The accuracy curve flattens out quickly once a <strong>baseline predictor capacity</strong> is reached (8B–70B). While scaling a Qwen-2.5 compressor from <strong>1B</strong> to <strong>7B</strong> improves accuracy by <strong>60%</strong>, scaling the predictor from <strong>70B</strong> to <strong>405B</strong> adds only <strong>12%</strong>. No matter how big the predictor is, it cannot recover information that was never provided by the compressor. </p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-12-29-agentic-it/total-scaling.png\" style=\"width:85%\"><strong>Figure 4</strong>: Downstream accuracy plateaus while total FLOPs-per-generation scales with predictor size, confirming principle 2.</figure>\n<p>Looking at total compute makes this contrast even starker. Scaling predictors means burning FLOPs on cloud APIs for each query, while scaling compressors gives you steeper gains at <strong>nearly flat cost</strong>—at least up to 7–8B. </p>\n<h3 id=\"user-content-mutual-information-the-perplexity-for-agentic-communication\">Mutual Information: the Perplexity for Agentic Communication</h3>\n<p>Now that we’ve seen that compression capacity is crucial to downstream task performance, we ask: <strong><em>what sets one compressor apart from another</em>?</strong> A better compressor is one that communicates <strong>as much task-relevant information</strong> as possible in <strong>as few tokens</strong> as possible.</p>\n<figure style=\"float:right;width:45%;margin-left:1em;margin-top:0.5em;margin-bottom:0.5em\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-12-29-agentic-it/mi-correlation.png\" style=\"width:100%\"><strong>Figure 5:</strong> Mutual information correlates with perplexity (R^2 = 0.71).</figure>\n<p>We turn to <strong>mutual information</strong> to directly measure the information passed from compressor to predictor. Just as perplexity is an effective <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2001.08361\" target=\"_blank\">indicator for model performance</a>, mutual information can serve as  a task-agnostic indicator of compression quality. </p>\n<p>Our experiments confirm this hypothesis—mutual information correlates with both downstream accuracy (after sampling) and perplexity. It is an indicator for system performance that can be measured <strong>without actually running the prediction step</strong>. </p>\n<p>More importantly, larger models produce compressions that retain more mutual information—up to <strong>5.4x</strong> more! Since larger compressors are also <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-12-29-agentic-it#performance-lies-with-the-compressor-2\" target=\"_blank\"><strong>more concise</strong></a>, they don’t only communicate more information, but do it <strong>more efficiently per token</strong>.</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-12-29-agentic-it/mi-scaling.png\" style=\"width:85%\"><strong>Figure 6:</strong> Larger compressors carry more mutual information and are more bit efficient (mutual information per compression token), validating principle 3.</figure>\n<h3>Making Deep Research go Brrrrrrrr</h3>\n<p>We put these lessons to the test in something that looks closer to a <strong>Deep Research system</strong>. On <a href=\"https://deepresearch-bench.github.io/\" target=\"_blank\">DeepResearch Bench</a>, we scaled compute into larger compressors instead of scaling predictors. With compressors that could run locally, we were able to achieve <strong>102% of frontier-LM only performance</strong> at only <strong>28% of the cost</strong>.</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-12-29-agentic-it/deep-research.png\" style=\"width:70%\"><strong>Figure 7:</strong> Deep Research cost-accuracy tradeoff.</figure>\n<h3>Information Theory Paves a Path Forward </h3>\n<p>With our information-theoretic framing of compression-prediction systems, we take a <strong>first step</strong> towards a more principled approach to designing multi-model workflows. As compression-prediction systems become more and more complex, and as small models increasingly run on-device, the future won’t be about ever-larger models hosted on the cloud, but about more information exchanged between smaller models. Mutual information will become a standard metric of intelligence in compression-prediction systems, embedded into the training, routing, and evaluation of workflows. </p>\n<p><strong>What’s next?</strong> We see a number of opportunities to explore. Mutual information remains difficult to estimate for LMs in practice. Models can be jointly trained to optimize for compressor-predictor communication, and alternative communication avenues beyond natural language can be built (communicating features, not text). Information-theoretic concepts could guide routing decisions in complex compression-prediction systems.</p>\n<h3>Additional Resources</h3>\n<blockquote>\n<p>📄<a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.21720\" target=\"_blank\">Paper</a><br>\n💻<a href=\"https://github.com/shizhehe/agentic-information-theory\" target=\"_blank\">GitHub</a></p>\n</blockquote>\n<h3>Acknowledgements</h3>\n<p>We are incredibly grateful to <a href=\"https://modal.com/\" target=\"_blank\">Modal</a> and <a href=\"https://www.together.ai/\" target=\"_blank\">Together AI</a> for providing valuable compute resources and credits. We thank Simran Arora, Yasa Baig, Kelly Buchanan, Sabri Eyuboglu, Neel Guha, Simon Guo, Jerry Liu, Jon Saad-Falcon, Frederic Sala, Ravid Shwartz-Ziv for providing invaluable feedback during the writing of the paper.</p>,TL;DR Many contemporary agentic systems exhibit a clear pattern: larger models interact with the user and orchestrate smaller models that…,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-12-29-agentic-it,<a href=\"https://shizhehe.com/\">Shizhe He</a>, <a href=\"https://www.linkedin.com/in/avanika-narayan-a35a02a3/\">Avanika Narayan</a>, <a href=\"https://iskhare.github.io/\">Ishan S. Khare</a>, <a href=\"https://lindermanlab.github.io/\">Scott W. Linderman</a>, <a href=\"https://cs.stanford.edu/people/chrismre/\">Chris Ré</a>, <a href=\"https://dan-biderman.netlify.app/\">Dan Biderman</a>,2026-07-22T18:37:38.423Z,2025-12-29T07:00:00.773Z,[{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-12-29-agentic-it/minions-scaling.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1408,\"height\":768},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-12-29-agentic-it/scaling-trends.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-12-29-agentic-it/it-setup.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-12-29-agentic-it/compressor-scaling.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-12-29-agentic-it/total-scaling.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-12-29-agentic-it/mi-correlation.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-12-29-agentic-it/mi-scaling.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-12-29-agentic-it/deep-research.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"}],163778663386687488,1211296081845936132,Our In-House Recipe for Juicy, Fact-Stuffed MLPs,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-12-01-mlps-p2,<details><summary><strong>TL;DR (click to expand):</strong></summary><p>Transformers store much of their factual knowledge inside MLP layers, but how they do it is still unclear. Whereas most previous efforts to understand MLP fact storage have focused on probing trained LLMs, we take a <strong>generative approach</strong>: we <em>explicitly construct</em> MLPs that implement key-value fact mappings. This gives us provable guarantees of correctness, precise facts-per-parameter scaling laws, and new insights into how embedding geometry affects MLP parameter efficiency and how transformers can reliably use MLPs. Our construction is the first to be:</p><ul>\n<li><strong>Provably Efficient:</strong> for some embeddings, our construction matches the information-theoretic limit of <span><span><span><math>Θ(Flog⁡F)\\Theta(F \\log F)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>Θ</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">F</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>lo<span style=\"margin-right:0.01389em\">g</span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">F</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> bits to store <span><span><span><math>FF</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">F</span></span></span></span></span> facts.</li>\n<li><strong>General:</strong> our construction handles realistic, non-uniform embeddings.</li>\n<li><strong>Usable:</strong> Transformers can learn to use our MLPs for factual recall.</li>\n</ul><p>In this blog, we'll dig into the details of our construction.</p></details>\n<blockquote>\n<p>⏮️ <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-28-mlps-p1\" target=\"_blank\">Part 1 of the blog post</a><br>\n📄 <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.00207\" target=\"_blank\">Paper</a></p>\n</blockquote>\n<p>In <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-28-mlps-p1\" target=\"_blank\">Part 1</a>, we introduced our generative view of factual recall in MLPs. Here, we zoom in on the construction: we'll explain how to turn a set of key/value embeddings into a provably correct and efficient fact-storing MLP.\nWe’ll keep the math light and focus on the core intuitions throughout.</p>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-28-mlps/112625_banner_p2.png\" alt=\"The Hazy Research Lab Cooking Juicy, Fact-Stuffed MLPs!\"><p><em>The Hazy Research Lab Cooking Juicy, Fact-Stuffed MLPs!</em></p></div>\n<hr>\n<h2>Problem setup</h2>\n<p>First, let’s formalize what it means for an MLP to <em>store a set of facts</em>.</p>\n<p>We're given key embeddings <span><span><span><math>K∈R∣K∣×d\\mathbf{K} \\in \\mathbb{R}^{|\\mathbf{K}| \\times d}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7252em;vertical-align:-0.0391em\"></span><span>K</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>∈</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.888em\"></span><span><span>R</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.888em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>∣</span><span>K</span><span>∣</span><span>×</span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>, value embeddings <span><span><span><math>V∈R∣V∣×d\\mathbf{V} \\in \\mathbb{R}^{|\\mathbf{V}| \\times d}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7252em;vertical-align:-0.0391em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.01597em\">V</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>∈</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.888em\"></span><span><span>R</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.888em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>∣</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.01597em\">V</span><span>∣</span><span>×</span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>, and a fact map <span><span><span><math>f:[∣K∣]→[∣V∣]f : [|\\mathbf{K}|] \\to [|\\mathbf{V}|]</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8889em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10764em\">f</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>:</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>[</span><span>∣</span><span>K</span><span>∣</span><span>]</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>→</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>[</span><span>∣</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.01597em\">V</span><span>∣</span><span>]</span></span></span></span></span> that assigns each key its correct value.</p>\n<p>We'll say that an MLP <span><span><span><math>gθ:Rd→Rdg_\\theta : \\mathbb{R}^d \\to \\mathbb{R}^d</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.625em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">g</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3361em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0359em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">θ</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>:</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"></span><span><span>R</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>→</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"></span><span><span>R</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> stores this fact set if, for every key <span><span><span><math>ki\\mathbf{k}_i</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8444em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span>k</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>, its output is closer (under dot-product decoding) to the correct value than to any other:</p>\n<div><span><span><span><math>⟨gθ(ki), vf(i)⟩  >  ⟨gθ(ki), vj⟩for all j≠f(i).\\langle g_\\theta(\\mathbf{k}_i),\\, \\mathbf{v}_{f(i)} \\rangle \\;>\\; \\langle g_\\theta(\\mathbf{k}_i),\\, \\mathbf{v}_j \\rangle \\quad \\text{for all } j \\neq f(i).</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.1052em;vertical-align:-0.3552em\"></span><span>⟨</span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">g</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3361em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0359em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">θ</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>(</span><span><span>k</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>)</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.01597em\">v</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3448em\"><span style=\"top:-2.5198em;margin-left:-0.016em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span
