style=\"margin-right:0.01597em\">V</span><span>∣</span></span></span></span></span></td><td>Generic</td><td><span><span><span><math>ρ(V)>0\\rho(\\mathbf{V}) > 0</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>ρ</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.01597em\">V</span><span>)</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>0</span></span></span></span></span></td></tr></tbody></table>\n<hr>\n<h2>How close are we to optimal in practice?</h2>\n<p>So far we’ve focused on theory; how well does the construction work empirically?</p>\n<p>To test this, we compare several MLP families. We compare our construction (which we label \"Ours: Bin + JL\") against the following baselines:</p>\n<ul>\n<li><strong>GD MLPs:</strong> fully end-to-end MLPs trained directly on the dot-product decoding objective using gradient descent and a cross entropy loss</li>\n<li><strong>NTK MLPs:</strong> the construction from <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.06538\" target=\"_blank\">Nichani et al. (2024)</a></li>\n</ul>\n<p>In addition to the GD and NTK baselines, we evaluate <strong>ablations of our encoder-decoder framework</strong>:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>\n<p><strong>GD encoder (Ours: GD + JL):</strong><br>\nWe keep the JL decoder fixed, but instead of using the explicit encoder gadget, we <em>train</em> the encoder parameters <span><span><span><math>(G,A,E)(\\mathbf{G}, \\mathbf{A}, \\mathbf{E})</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>(</span><span>G</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>A</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>E</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> with gradient descent and a mean-squared-error loss so that</p>\n<div><span><span><span><math>enc(ki)≈cf(i)\\mathrm{enc}(\\mathbf{k}_i) \\approx \\mathbf{c}_{f(i)}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span><span>enc</span></span><span>(</span><span><span>k</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>)</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>≈</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.7996em;vertical-align:-0.3552em\"></span><span><span>c</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3448em\"><span style=\"top:-2.5198em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10764em\">f</span><span>(</span><span>i</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.3552em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></div>\n<p>for the target compressed codes <span><span><span><math>C\\mathbf{C}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6861em\"></span><span>C</span></span></span></span></span>.</p>\n</li>\n<li>\n<p><strong>GD decoder (Ours: Bin + GD):</strong><br>\nWe keep the encoder gadget fixed, but instead of using a random projection as the decoder, we train a linear map <span><span><span><math>D\\mathbf{D}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6861em\"></span><span>D</span></span></span></span></span> and compressed codes <span><span><span><math>C\\mathbf{C}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6861em\"></span><span>C</span></span></span></span></span> with gradient descent and a cross entropy loss on the logits</p>\n<div><span><span><span><math>zij=⟨Dci,vj⟩.z_{ij} = \\langle \\mathbf{D}\\mathbf{c}_{i}, \\mathbf{v}_{j} \\rangle.</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7167em;vertical-align:-0.2861em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.04398em\">z</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.044em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">ij</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2861em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1.0361em;vertical-align:-0.2861em\"></span><span>⟨</span><span>D</span><span><span>c</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.01597em\">v</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.016em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2861em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>⟩</span><span>.</span></span></span></span></span></div>\n</li>\n<li>\n<p><strong>GD encoder + GD decoder (Ours: GD + GD):</strong><br>\nWe keep the same encoder-decoder architecture as our construction, but with both the encoder and decoder parameters learned with gradient descent. The encoder and decoder are trained separately.</p>\n</li>\n</ul>\n<p>We fix the embedding dimension (<span><span><span><math>d=128d=128</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span>d</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>128</span></span></span></span></span>), sample uniform-spherical embeddings, and vary the number of facts. For each MLP family, we measure the <strong>minimum number of parameters needed to perfectly store a randomly chosen fact set</strong>.</p>\n<p>Three observations stand out:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>\n<p><strong>Our MLPs match GD MLPs' asymptotics.</strong><br>\nThe explicit construction (Bin + JL) follows the same asymptotic trend as GD-trained MLPs as the number of facts grows. In contrast, NTK MLPs exhibit asymptotically worse scaling with increasing number of facts and fixed embedding dimension.</p>\n</li>\n<li>\n<p><strong>We consistently outperform NTK.</strong><br>\nEven in this clean spherical setting, our explicit MLP is roughly <strong><span><span><span><math>4×4\\times</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>4</span><span>×</span></span></span></span></span> more parameter-efficient</strong> than NTK-based constructions — and the gap <em>widens</em> with more facts. Around <span><span><span><math>2142^{14}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"></span><span><span>2</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>14</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> facts, NTK MLPs fail to achieve perfect fact storage while ours continue working.</p>\n</li>\n<li>\n<p><strong>Our encoder-framework is performant when partially learned.</strong><br>\nUsing a GD-trained encoder together with a GD-trained decoder gets us most of the way to the parameter-efficiency of fully gradient-descent-trained MLPs (worse by <span><span><span><math>∼4×\\sim 4\\times</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3669em\"></span><span>∼</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>4</span><span>×</span></span></span></span></span>, better than NTK by <span><span><span><math>>16×>16\\times</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.5782em;vertical-align:-0.0391em\"></span><span>></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>16</span><span>×</span></span></span></span></span>). This suggests that our encoder-decoder framework captures most of the structure that gradient descent discovers.</p>\n</li>\n</ul>\n<div style=\"text-align:center\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-28-mlps/112525_construction_ablations.png\" alt=\"MLP fact-storage scaling\"><p><em>Our explicit construction (Bin + JL) matches the fact-storage scaling of GD-trained MLPs — and is asymptotically more parameter-efficient than prior constructions for large numbers of facts.</em></p></div>\n<h2>Conclusion</h2>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-28-mlps/120125-end-roadmap.png\" alt=\"The Hazy Research Recipe for Juicy, Fact-Stuffed MLPs!\"><p><em>The Hazy Research Recipe for Juicy, Fact-Stuffed MLPs!</em></p></div>\n<hr>\n<h2>Acknowledgements</h2>\n<p>Thank you to Yasa Baig, Kelly Buchanan, Sam Buchanan, Catherine Deng, Andy Dimnaku, Junmiao Hu, Herman Brunborg, Rajat Dwaraknath, Jonas De Schouwer, and Atri Rudra for helpful feedback on this blog post.</p>\n<h2>More Resources</h2>\n<blockquote>\n<p>📄 <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.00207\" target=\"_blank\">Paper</a><br>\n⏮️ <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-28-mlps-p1\" target=\"_blank\">Part 1 of the blog post</a></p>\n</blockquote>,TL;DR (click to expand): Transformers store much of their factual knowledge inside MLP layers, but how they do it is still unclear. Whereas…,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-12-01-mlps-p2,<a href=\"https://druidowm.github.io/\">Owen Dugan*</a>, <a>Roberto Garcia*</a>, <a href=\"https://scholar.google.com/citations?user=KMRwMNIAAAAJ&hl=en\">Ronny Junkins*</a>, <a href=\"https://jerrywliu.github.io/\">Jerry Liu*</a>, <a href=\"https://cs.stanford.edu/people/chrismre/\">Chris Ré</a>,2026-07-22T18:37:38.422Z,2025-12-01T07:00:00.402Z,[{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-28-mlps/112625_banner_p2.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":2816,\"height\":1536,\"blurhash\":\"LDKAcuNEIqmk~pR4t-9HD*M|-nIV\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-28-mlps/112625_construction_framework.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":2456,\"height\":1242,\"blurhash\":\"LXP6~+MeyDyYxuazaeoJ~VWYofsk\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-28-mlps/112725_mlps_rho.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1376,\"height\":1147,\"blurhash\":\"L2SigQRi%MD*yrRODiM|-p^+~qkr\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-28-mlps/112525_construction_ablations.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1368,\"height\":1156,\"blurhash\":\"LBSPX_D%?b%MNGt7s:Rj~pxaRkX8\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-28-mlps/120125-end-roadmap.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":2816,\"height\":1536,\"blurhash\":\"LEOe|gv}4UtQn+RPr?jsIAIVV{oM\"}],163778663386687488,1211296081845936133,Stuffing MLPs Full of Facts 🧠: A Generative Approach to Factual Recall,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-28-mlps-p1,<p><strong>TL;DR</strong><br>\nTransformers store much of their factual knowledge inside MLP layers, but how they do it is still unclear. Instead of probing trained LLMs, we take a <strong>generative approach</strong>: we <em>explicitly construct</em> MLPs that implement key-value fact mappings. This gives us provable guarantees of correctness, precise facts-per-parameter scaling laws, and new insights into how embedding geometry affects MLP parameter efficiency and how transformers can reliably use MLPs. Our construction is the first to be:</p>\n<ul>\n<li><strong>General:</strong> handles realistic, non-uniform embeddings instead of idealized spherical ones.</li>\n<li><strong>Efficient:</strong> matches the information-theoretic lower bound of <span><span><span><math>Ω(Flog⁡F)\\Omega(F \\log F)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>Ω</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">F</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>lo<span style=\"margin-right:0.01389em\">g</span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">F</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> parameters to store <span><span><span><math>FF</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">F</span></span></span></span></span> facts.</li>\n<li><strong>Usable:</strong> can be reliably used by Transformers for factual recall tasks.</li>\n</ul>\n<p>Finally, we show a proof-of-concept application of fact-storing MLPs: <strong>modular fact editing</strong> by <em>swapping whole MLP blocks</em>. Because the Transformer learns to cleanly use MLPs as fact stores, we can <em>swap in an entirely new MLP block</em> containing new facts and the Transformer immediately outputs them — no retraining required.</p>\n<p><strong>Full team</strong>: Owen Dugan*, Roberto Garcia*, Ronny Junkins*, Jerry Liu*, Dylan Zinsley, Sabri Eyuboglu, Atri Rudra, Chris Ré</p>\n<p><strong>Read the paper <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.00207\" target=\"_blank\">here</a>!</strong></p>\n<hr>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-28-mlps/banner.png\" alt=\"Illustration of MLP storing key-value mappings\"><p><em>Our in-house recipe for stuffing MLPs with factual knowledge.</em></p></div>\n<p>Recently, we've been thinking a lot about how Transformers store and retrieve <em>facts</em>. Ask any LLM \"What’s the capital of France?\", and the answer \"Paris\" emerges from a tangle of weights. But the mechanisms behind fact storage are still unclear.</p>\n<p>Most prior work takes a forensic approach: take a big pre-trained model, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2104.08696\" target=\"_blank\">poke at its neurons</a>, and try to <a href=\"https://www.alignmentforum.org/posts/iGuwZTHWb6DFY3sKB/fact-finding-attempting-to-reverse-engineer-factual-recall\" target=\"_blank\">reverse-engineer where the facts live</a>. These works have furthered our understanding of LLM factual recall, identifying the crucial role of MLPs as <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2012.14913\" target=\"_blank\"><em>key-value memories</em></a> within these models.</p>\n<p>Here, we take a different approach: <strong>can we directly construct fact-storing MLPs ourselves?</strong></p>\n<h2>🔍 Challenges in formalizing fact storage</h2>\n<p>Before introducing our construction, we first formalize how we think about <em>facts</em> and summarize recent progress in the study of factual recall in language models.</p>\n<p><strong>Definition (Fact Set).</strong> Given key embeddings <span><span><span><math>K∈RF,d\\mathbf{K} \\in \\mathbb{R}^{F, d}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7252em;vertical-align:-0.0391em\"></span><span>K</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>∈</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"></span><span><span>R</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">F</span><span>,</span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> and value embeddings <span><span><span><math>V∈RF,d\\mathbf{V} \\in \\mathbb{R}^{F, d}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7252em;vertical-align:-0.0391em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.01597em\">V</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>∈</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"></span><span><span>R</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">F</span><span>,</span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>, a fact set is a mapping <span><span><span><math>f:[K]→[V]f : [\\mathbf{K}] \\to [\\mathbf{V}]</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8889em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10764em\">f</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>:</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>[</span><span>K</span><span>]</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>→</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>[</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.01597em\">V</span><span>]</span></span></span></span></span>, which assigns each key <span><span><span><math>ki\\mathbf{k}_i</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8444em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span>k</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> a corresponding value <span><span><span><math>vf(i)\\mathbf{v}_{f(i)}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7996em;vertical-align:-0.3552em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.01597em\">v</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3448em\"><span style=\"top:-2.5198em;margin-left:-0.016em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10764em\">f</span><span>(</span><span>i</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.3552em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>. For example, in the \"capitals\" fact set, the key \"France\" would map to the value \"Paris\".</p>\n<p><strong>Progress on factual knowledge in LLMs.</strong> Recent works have examined how Transformers and MLPs store and retrieve large numbers of facts:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>One line of work has studied the scaling of factual knowledge in Transformers in synthetic <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2312.04927\" target=\"_blank\">associative recall</a> settings. Interestingly, several works (e.g., <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.05405\" target=\"_blank\">Allen-Zhu and Li 2024</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.24832\" target=\"_blank\">Morris et al. 2025</a>) have found that LLMs consistently store facts at the <strong>information-theoretically optimal</strong> rate of <span><span><span><math>Θ(Flog⁡F)\\Theta(F \\log F)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>Θ</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">F</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>lo<span style=\"margin-right:0.01389em\">g</span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">F</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> parameters for <span><span><span><math>FF</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">F</span></span></span></span></span> facts.</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.06538\" target=\"_blank\">Nichani et al. 2024</a> propose an MLP construction and provide a theoretical analysis of its parameter efficiency, where their MLPs come within a polylog factor of the observed scaling of LLMs.</li>\n</ul>\n<p>Prior MLP constructions make progress on understanding mechanisms for fact storage. However, these works have three major limitations:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>\n<p><strong>Restrictive assumptions.</strong> Prior constructions assume idealized inputs and outputs (specifically, that they're uniformly spherically distributed). However, we find that these constructions fail to store facts correctly when using messy, anisotropic embeddings like the internal representations of LLMs.</p>\n</li>\n<li>\n<p><strong>Inefficiency.</strong> Prior constructions remain asymptotically less efficient than the information-theoretic limit: theoretically, by up to <span><span><span><math>log⁡11(F)×\\log^{11}(F)\\times</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.1484em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span><span>lo<span style=\"margin-right:0.01389em\">g</span></span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8984em\"><span style=\"top:-3.1473em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>11</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">F</span><span>)</span><span>×</span></span></span></span></span> when storing <span><span><span><math>FF</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">F</span></span></span></span></span> facts! When implemented, they are more than <span><span><span><math>100×100\\times</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>100</span><span>×</span></span></span></span></span> less parameter efficient than gradient-descent-trained MLPs for spherically-distributed embeddings, and fail entirely for anisotropic embeddings.</p>\n</li>\n<li>\n<p><strong>Not evaluated within Transformers.</strong> Prior works didn't investigate whether Transformers could reliably use constructed MLPs for factual recall tasks.</p>\n</li>\n</ul>\n<p>Our work addresses each limitation, yielding constructions capable of handling realistic embeddings, optimally storing facts per parameter, and being usable within Transformers for factual recall:</p>\n<ul>\n<li><strong><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-28-mlps-p1#generality\" target=\"_blank\">Generality</a>.</strong> Our construction provably stores facts for all but a measure-zero set of feasible MLP inputs/outputs.</li>\n<li><strong><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-28-mlps-p1#efficiency\" target=\"_blank\">Asymptotic efficiency</a>.</strong> Under the spherical inputs/outputs setting from prior work, our MLPs match the asymptotic information-theoretic lower bound of <span><span><span><math>Θ(Flog⁡F)\\Theta(F \\log F)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>Θ</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">F</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>lo<span style=\"margin-right:0.01389em\">g</span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">F</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> parameters for <span><span><span><math>FF</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">F</span></span></span></span></span> facts. Beyond this idealized case, we show that fact-storage efficiency is governed by a single embedding-quality metric, the <em>decodability</em>, which quantifies how non-uniform or poorly structured embeddings reduce achievable efficiency.</li>\n<li><strong><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-28-mlps-p1#usability\" target=\"_blank\">Usability</a>.</strong> We identify a small set of architectural tweaks that enable Transformers to reliably use fact-storing MLPs for factual recall! On a synthetic factual recall task, 1-layer Transformers equipped with constructed MLPs achieve facts-per-parameter scaling comparable to the information-theoretically optimal observed in LLMs.</li>\n</ul>\n<h3>Roadmap</h3>\n<ul>\n<li>We'll first walk through the high-level intuition behind our construction, which combines a <em>encoder</em> gated MLP with a linear <em>decoder</em>. We then show that this construction achieves facts-per-parameter scaling that matches gradient-descent-trained (GD) MLPs — unlike prior constructions — and that for general embeddings, this scaling depends on our <em>decodability</em> embedding-geometry metric.</li>\n<li>Next, we demonstrate how to make Transformers reliably use these fact-storing MLPs for factual recall. In doing so, we'll identify a tradeoff between the fact-storage <em>capacity</em> of an MLP and its <em>usability</em> by Transformers.</li>\n<li>Finally, we show how our insights unlock a new capability: <em>modular fact editing</em> in Transformers by swapping out entire MLP blocks.</li>\n</ul>\n<h2>🧱 Our encoder-decoder framework for constructing MLPs</h2>\n<p>At a high level, the key insight in our construction is to decompose a single-hidden-layer MLP into two subproblems (Figure 1):</p>\n<ul>\n<li>An <strong>encoder</strong> that maps from <span><span><span><math>dd</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span>-dimensional inputs (key embeddings) to a <span><span><span><math>Θ(log⁡F)\\Theta(\\log F)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>Θ</span><span>(</span><span>lo<span style=\"margin-right:0.01389em\">g</span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">F</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span>-dimensional <em>compressed code</em>. We propose a gated MLP encoder that can represent all but a measure-zero set of keys and compressed codes.</li>\n<li>A <strong>decoder</strong> that maps from the compressed code to <span><span><span><math>dd</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span>-dimensional outputs (value embeddings). A simple way to produce a compressed code and its decompression method is with a random projection matrix, via a Johnson-Lindenstrauss-type argument.</li>\n</ul>\n<p>Intuitively, Johnson-Lindenstrauss tells us that although the <span><span><span><math>FF</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">F</span></span></span></span></span> value embeddings live in <span><span><span><math>Rd\\mathbb{R}^d</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"></span><span><span>R</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>, we can compress them into as few as <span><span><span><math>log⁡F\\log F</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8889em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span>lo<span style=\"margin-right:0.01389em\">g</span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">F</span></span></span></span></span> dimensions while still distinguishing them with high probability. Introducing this <span><span><span><math>log⁡F\\log F</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8889em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span>lo<span style=\"margin-right:0.01389em\">g</span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">F</span></span></span></span></span>-dimensional bottleneck is what makes the construction parameter-efficient: the encoder only needs to produce a short code, and the decoder only needs to ensure that the decompressed vectors approximately match the target values.</p>\n<div style=\"text-align:center\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-28-mlps/112625_construction_framework.png\" alt=\"Encoder-decoder structure\"><p><em>Figure 1: Our constructed MLP uses an encoder–decoder structure to achieve asymptotically optimal fact-storage capacity.</em></p></div>\n<p>This decomposition lets us analyze the encoder and decoder separately, which in turn allows us to prove correctness and optimal fact-storage capacity. We'll explore the construction in more detail in Part 2 of the blog: stay tuned!</p>\n<h3 id=\"user-content-generality\">📏 Output geometry fundamentally limits fact-storage capacity</h3>\n<p>While developing the construction, we discovered a metric on the output embeddings: <span><span><span><math>ρ\\rho</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.625em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span>ρ</span></span></span></span></span>, the <em>decodability</em>. This quantity summarizes how \"tightly packed\" the output embeddings are, with small <span><span><span><math>ρ\\rho</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.625em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span>ρ</span></span></span></span></span> values indicating tightly packed embeddings (Figure 2).\nSurprisingly, we found that <span><span><span><math>ρ(V)\\rho(V)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>ρ</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.22222em\">V</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> is predictive (<span><span><span><math>R2>0.97R^2>0.97</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8532em;vertical-align:-0.0391em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.00773em\">R</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>2</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>0.97</span></span></span></span></span>) of fact-storage capacity for both gradient-descent-trained and constructed MLPs — including ours and prior constructions!</p>\n<div style=\"text-align:center\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-28-mlps/112725_mlps_rho.png\" alt=\"Decodability of output embeddings\"><p><em>Figure 2: Lower decodability <span><span><span><math>ρ\\rho</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.625em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span>ρ</span></span></span></span></span> means MLPs need more parameters to store a fixed number of facts; this holds for gradient-descent-trained (GD) MLPs, for ours, and for the construction from (Nichani et al. 2024) (NTK).</em></p></div>\n<h3 id=\"user-content-efficiency\">📈 Our construction achieves asymptotically optimal scaling</h3>\n<p>We find that this simple encoder-decoder framework is enough to asymptotically match the facts-per-parameter scaling of gradient-descent-trained MLPs (Figure 3).\nIn contrast, prior constructions (NTK) exhibit asymptotically worse scaling as more facts are packed into a fixed dimension.</p>\n<div style=\"text-align:center\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-28-mlps/112825_fact_storage.png\" alt=\"MLP fact-storage capacity\"><p><em>Figure 3: Our MLP construction is the first to match the asymptotic scaling of GD MLPs.</em></p></div>\n<h3 id=\"user-content-usability\">🛠️ Transformers learn to use our constructed MLPs</h3>\n<p>Now that we know how to construct MLPs to store facts, can a Transformer actually <em>use</em> those fact-storing MLPs for factual recall tasks?</p>\n<p>Excitingly, the answer is <strong>yes</strong> — but only after a few architectural tweaks to ensure the Transformer is <em>reading</em> the MLP’s facts rather than memorizing them itself.</p>\n<blockquote>\n<p><strong>Changes we needed to make for Transformers to interface with fact-storing MLPs:</strong></p>\n<ul>\n<li><strong>Tying MLP and Transformer embeddings and freezing the pre-MLP RMSNorm.</strong> Ensures keys and values remain in the embedding geometry the MLP expects.</li>\n<li><strong>Removing residual connections.</strong> Forces information to flow <em>through</em> the MLP instead of bypassing it.</li>\n<li><strong>Freezing value and out-projection matrices to identity.</strong> Prevents the attention parameters from being used as an alternative fact store.</li>\n</ul>\n<p>We expect these constraints can be relaxed; exploring this is promising future work!</p>\n</blockquote>\n<p>To evaluate these modifications, we define a <strong>Synthetic Sequential Factual Recall (SSFR)</strong> task. Crucially, we needed a controlled setting that measures whether the Transformer is actually extracting facts from the MLP instead of storing them in its own parameters.</p>\n<p>In this setting, we find that a 1-layer Transformer can achieve <strong>>99% factual recall</strong> while matching the <strong>facts-per-parameter scaling</strong> exhibited by LLMs (Figure 4).</p>\n<div style=\"text-align:center\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-28-mlps/112825_fact_adaptive_frontier.png\" alt=\"Decodability of output embeddings\"><p><em>Figure 4: Fact-storing MLPs are <em>usable within Transformers</em>. Transformers equipped with our constructed MLPs exhibit scaling that closely matches the fact-storage capacity of MLPs in isolation.</em></p></div>\n<h3>⚖️ The capacity-usability tradeoff</h3>\n<p>Along the way, we uncovered a fundamental tension between the number of facts an MLP stores (its <em>capacity</em>) and how readily Transformers can access that MLP's facts (its <em>usability</em>).\nBuilding off our insight about embedding decodability, it's possible to increase the capacity of an MLP by manually spacing apart the output embeddings within fact-storing MLPs: we call this technique <em>embedding whitening</em>. Interestingly however, despite increasing MLP capacity, we found this procedure <strong>decreases MLP usability</strong> overall.</p>\n<p>The <em>Lipschitz constant</em> of the MLP turns out to be a key predictor (Figure 5): embedding whitening produces MLPs that store more facts, but it also destabilizes the training of Transformers that use them.</p>\n<div style=\"text-align:center\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-28-mlps/112825_usability_vs_conditioning_lip_blog.png\" alt=\"MLP usability vs. conditioning\"><p><em>Figure 5: MLPs with larger Lipschitz constants are less usable by Transformers.</em></p></div>\n<h2>🧪 A new unlock: modular fact editing</h2>\n<p>When used in 1-layer Transformers, fact-storing MLPs unlock an exciting new capability. As a proof-of-concept, we found we can <em>swap</em> a GD-trained fact-storing MLP with another one storing a different fact set, and the transformer immediately outputs the new facts – <em>with no extra training</em> and <em>almost no performance loss</em> (less than 3% difference in cross-entropy loss on off-target tokens).</p>\n<p>This doubles the editing score of prior state-of-the-art fact editing methods (MEMIT, ROME, AlphaEdit) when editing 10% of facts (Figure 6).</p>\n<div style=\"text-align:center\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-28-mlps/fact_editing_score.png\" alt=\"MLP swapping\"><p><em>Figure 6: Transformers can use GD-trained fact-storing MLPs for <em>modular factual recall</em>. Swap in a new MLP, get new facts — <em>with no additional training</em>.</em></p></div>\n<p>It's still incredibly early, but we're really excited about what this result hints at: memory that's truly <em>plug-and-play</em>. We're eager to explore how we can scale these ideas towards building LLMs whose knowledge is <em>robust, modular, and efficiently editable</em>.</p>\n<h2>🚀 A generative approach to understanding LLMs</h2>\n<p>We believe this work can unlock a fundamentally new way to study and understand LLMs. Instead of attempting to study pretrained models <em>descriptively</em>, by opening the black box of their weights and activations, why not study them <em>constructively</em>, by directly <em>building</em> systems with interpretable and provable mechanisms?</p>\n<p>We're excited to use these tools to investigate a new suite of questions:</p>\n<ul>\n<li><strong>Robust, adaptive memory systems.</strong> Can we design architectures whose internal memories are modular, interpretable, and support precise, localized updates?</li>\n<li><strong>More efficient training and inference.</strong> Can explicit MLP constructions help us pack more knowledge into smaller models or even shortcut parts of pre-training?</li>\n<li><strong>Beyond factual recall in LLMs.</strong> Can similar generative constructions shed light on higher-level LLM capabilities like reasoning or planning, or even extend beyond language to domains such as <a href=\"https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/science.ado9336\" target=\"_blank\">biology</a> and <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-11-18-evo-axioms\" target=\"_blank\">physics</a>?</li>\n</ul>\n<p>If you’re interested in understanding how models store, organize, or reason with knowledge — or want to help build new mechanisms for doing so — we’d love to talk!</p>\n<h2>🛠️ Code &#x26; Resources</h2>\n<blockquote>\n<p>📄 <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.00207\" target=\"_blank\">Paper</a><br>\n⏭️ <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-12-01-mlps-p2\" target=\"_blank\">Part 2 of the blog post</a></p>\n</blockquote>,TL;DR Transformers store much of their factual knowledge inside MLP layers, but how they do it is still unclear. Instead of probing trained…,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-28-mlps-p1,<a href=\"https://druidowm.github.io/\">Owen Dugan*</a>, <a href=\"https://x.com/garctrob\">Roberto Garcia*</a>, <a href=\"https://scholar.google.com/citations?user=KMRwMNIAAAAJ&hl=en\">Ronny Junkins*</a>, <a href=\"https://jerrywliu.github.io/\">Jerry Liu*</a>, <a href=\"https://cs.stanford.edu/people/chrismre/\">Chris Ré</a>,2026-07-22T18:37:38.421Z,2025-11-28T07:00:00.794Z,[{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-28-mlps/banner.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":2816,\"height\":1536,\"blurhash\":\"LAP%O*?b~qD%?It7f7Rj%MM{M{t7\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-28-mlps/112625_construction_framework.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":2456,\"height\":1242,\"blurhash\":\"LXP6~+MeyDyYxuazaeoJ~VWYofsk\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-28-mlps/112725_mlps_rho.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1376,\"height\":1147,\"blurhash\":\"L2SigQRi%MD*yrRODiM|-p^+~qkr\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-28-mlps/112825_fact_storage.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1374,\"height\":1150,\"blurhash\":\"L9SPR#%g%#IUWToyRjRj_MsVRQt7\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-28-mlps/112825_fact_adaptive_frontier.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1466,\"height\":1150,\"blurhash\":\"L2Sr},D%9ZIoR%Nw4Tso?^xb-V-;\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-28-mlps/112825_usability_vs_conditioning_lip_blog.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1619,\"height\":1148,\"blurhash\":\"L4SPR$%#Di~q=xNH8_M{_2IB_3M{\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-28-mlps/fact_editing_score.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1470,\"height\":1170,\"blurhash\":\"L2SPX_jF~q~qwOIURPWVE3aw_3-p\"}],163778663386687488,1211296081845936134,Maximizing American Gross Domestic Intelligence with Hybrid Inference,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-28-gdi,<figure style=\"text-align:center\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-28-gdi/banner.png\" style=\"width:100%\"></figure>\n<p>American leadership in AI has come under threat from a quiet but persistent strategy executed by the Chinese Communist Party. Locked out of high-end chips designed in the U.S. and fabricated in Taiwan with tools from American allies [2], China is making a three-way bet: a <a href=\"https://www.woodmac.com/blogs/the-edge/powering-chinas-data-centres/\" target=\"_blank\">massive build-out of cheap power and grid capacity</a>, a push to <a href=\"https://www.reuters.com/world/china/huawei-readies-new-ai-chip-mass-shipment-china-seeks-nvidia-alternatives-sources-2025-04-21\" target=\"_blank\">scale domestic accelerators</a>, and aggressive use of increasingly <a href=\"https://www.rand.org/pubs/perspectives/PEA4012-1.html\" target=\"_blank\">compute-efficient open-source models</a>. A year ago, that strategy looked almost quixotic. China's ability to train frontier-scale models was still limited relative to the U.S., and it was hard to see how pouring resources into power plants, <a href=\"https://ifp.org/the-b30a-decision/#china-cannot-manufacture-ai-chips-at-scale\" target=\"_blank\">inferior chips</a>, and open models would win a game that seemed defined by state-of-the-art training runs.</p>\n<p>Fast forward just a year, and it is clear the landscape has shifted. Demand for compute-intensive AI-enabled services (reasoning models, agents, etc.) is exploding, and we're entering an inference-centric phase where serving billions of queries will dominate compute demand and costs [6]. The center of gravity is moving from \"Who trains the biggest model?\" to \"Who can run these models everywhere, all the time?\" That shift changes what it means to win in AI. Victory no longer means running the biggest training job, but rather deploying the largest amount of computational intelligence to achieve scientific, economic, and military advantage.</p>\n<p>Maximizing deployed intelligence requires two simultaneous objectives: making computation more intelligent per unit of power (better models, better chips), and maximizing the power available to run that computation. Miss either dimension and you lose: efficient models without power sit idle; abundant power without efficient computation drives up costs with diminishing returns. In this framing, the absolute amount of intelligence a nation can deploy is the product of <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2511.07885\" target=\"_blank\">intelligence per watt</a> [1] and the amount of power usable for computation — what we call <strong>Gross Domestic Intelligence (GDI)</strong>.</p>\n<p>We argue that the United States should compete in AI by maximizing GDI rather than raw datacenter capacity. Viewed through this lens, one of the United States' most underappreciated competitive advantages becomes clear: because U.S.-based firms dominate operating systems, developer tools, and GPU/NPU hardware, American households and businesses own an unusually dense stock of AI-capable devices. Millions of laptops and local devices already contain AI accelerators that sit idle most of the day, and local AI models are now powerful enough to make good use of them. Activating this local compute as part of a hybrid local–cloud inference system would immediately add 30–40%<sup id=\"user-content-fnref-1\"><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-28-gdi#fn-1\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">1</a></sup> to the nation's usable inference capacity and boost GDI by roughly 2–4×<sup id=\"user-content-fnref-2\"><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-28-gdi#fn-2\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">2</a></sup> for single turn chat and reasoning queries, without building any new datacenters or grid infrastructure.</p>\n<h2>Introducing Gross Domestic Intelligence: A New Framework for AI Competition</h2>\n<p>To make this concrete, it helps to define terms. Think of GDI as the AI analogue of GDP: the total \"intelligence output\" a nation can bring to bear given its hardware, software, and energy system. At a first approximation, we can write:</p>\n<p align=\"center\"><strong>GDI = IPW × min(P<sub>A</sub>, P<sub>C</sub>)</strong></p>\n<p>where:</p>\n<ul>\n<li><strong>IPW (Intelligence per Watt)</strong> is how much useful model behavior you get per unit of power consumed. Higher IPW means your chips and models squeeze more intelligence out of each electron. In practice, we care about IPW at a fixed latency budget, since you can raise theoretical IPW by running cooler, slower chips—but only at the cost of much slower responses.</li>\n<li><strong>P<sub>A</sub> (Power Available for computation)</strong> is how much electrical power can actually be delivered to AI workloads at the point of use. In practice it's limited by both generation capacity (how much power plants can produce) and grid capacity (transmission and distribution lines, substations, and interconnections).</li>\n<li><strong>P<sub>C</sub> (Power Consumption of chips)</strong> is how much power your installed base of accelerators could draw if you ran them at 100% capacity.</li>\n</ul>\n<p>The \"min(P<sub>A</sub>, P<sub>C</sub>)\" term reflects that the system is only as strong as the weakest resource. If you have more power than chips (P<sub>A</sub> > P<sub>C</sub>) you're compute-bound: electrons waiting around for silicon. If you have more chips than power (P<sub>C</sub> > P<sub>A</sub>) you're energy-bound: racks of GPUs you can't fully feed.</p>\n<h2>China Is Compute-Bound; America Is Energy-Bound</h2>\n<p>Seen through this GDI lens, China and the United States face inverse bottlenecks. Because of U.S.– and ally–imposed export controls on advanced accelerators and the tools needed to manufacture them, <a href=\"https://ifp.org/the-b30a-decision/#appendix-1-estimate-of-2025-and-2026-us-production-of-ai-chips\" target=\"_blank\">China is increasingly compute-bound</a>: it can keep adding power plants, but it <a href=\"https://merics.org/en/report/chinas-drive-toward-self-reliance-artificial-intelligence-chips-large-language-models\" target=\"_blank\">cannot easily acquire or build large fleets of high-IPW accelerators</a> on par with Nvidia, AMD or Google's latest chips. Public U.S. estimates put <a href=\"https://www.reuters.com/world/china/us-says-chinas-huawei-cant-make-more-than-200000-ai-chips-2025-2025-06-12\" target=\"_blank\">Huawei's 2025 output of advanced AI chips at roughly a few hundred thousand units</a>, far short of the millions an unconstrained ecosystem would like to deploy [11, 12].</p>\n<p>The United States, by contrast, is <a href=\"https://foreignpolicy.com/2024/10/14/ai-artificial-intelligence-energy-demand-nuclear-solar-wind/\" target=\"_blank\">increasingly energy-bound</a> — not from lack of primary energy (it's an energy superpower) but from grid constraints on delivering electricity to datacenter sites. American firms still design the world's most capable accelerators and train the leading frontier models, but they are running into grid-side constraints: <a href=\"https://www.camus.energy/blog/why-does-it-take-so-long-to-connect-a-data-center-to-the-grid\" target=\"_blank\">long interconnection queues, limited local substation capacity, and slow permitting</a> for both gigawatt-scale data-center campuses and the transmission lines that would feed them [4]. Multiple planned datacenters have been cancelled due to community pushback and concerns over consumer electricity prices, with $18 billion in projects cancelled outright and another $46 billion delayed [13]. In most serious analyses today, power availability, not chips, is emerging as the single biggest bottleneck on U.S. AI data center build-outs [5].</p>\n<p>Beyond power constraints, other <a href=\"https://www.mckinsey.com/industries/private-capital/our-insights/scaling-bigger-faster-cheaper-data-centers-with-smarter-designs\" target=\"_blank\">structural factors</a> — shortages of skilled labor, electrical equipment (transformers, turbine blades), and raw materials, plus the risk of tariffs and supply chain disruptions — further complicate domestic datacenter buildouts. As a result, a growing share of marginal build-out is flowing to energy-rich hubs. Gulf states leverage their <a href=\"https://www.pwc.com/m1/en/media-centre/articles/unlocking-the-data-centre-opportunity-in-the-middle-east.html\" target=\"_blank\">freedom from green commitments, rapid infrastructure development efforts, and centralized governance</a> to dictate terms—enabling rapid deployment of firm fossil-fuel power backed by sovereign capital—while Southeast Asian markets such as Malaysia and Indonesia compete on lower land and electricity costs.</p>\n<p>Put back into the GDI identity, the logic is simple. In the near term, China cannot dramatically raise domestic IPW without better access to advanced manufacturing tools: while algorithmic advances (i.e., <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.09388\" target=\"_blank\">Qwen</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.19437\" target=\"_blank\">DeepSeek</a>) can boost intelligence, wattage is still dominated by chip- and system-level efficiency. Without leading-edge, high-efficiency accelerators, its straightforward play is to maximize P<sub>C</sub> through sheer volume — deploying massive quantities of lower-IPW accelerators — while keeping P<sub>A</sub> sky-high via its rapidly expanding power system. America, by contrast, is already saturated with high-IPW silicon but starved for new electrons, which means its unique advantage is the ability to push all three levers — IPW, P<sub>A</sub>, and P<sub>C</sub> — by continuing to improve chips, refine models, and redesign where and how AI workloads run.</p>\n<h2>The Grid-Aware Datacenter Fix—and Its Limits</h2>\n<p>An intuitive response to America's energy bottleneck is a grid-aware AI system: <a href=\"https://rmi.org/fast-flexible-solutions-for-data-centers/\" target=\"_blank\">make datacenters flex with the grid</a>, turning down or shifting workloads in time and space when the system is stressed. In principle, this kind of demand response could unlock significant capacity from the existing grid. By some estimates, nearly 100 GW of additional datacenter capacity could be added without breaching peak load limits if facilities reduced power draw by just 0.5% for roughly 200 hours per year [10]. AI workloads, particularly inference, are unusually well suited to this approach: unlike steel mills or residential cooling, they are one of the few major loads that can be re-routed across regions at the speed of light, shifting queries from constrained grids to areas with surplus power.</p>\n<p>But even a perfectly grid-responsive cloud still assumes that almost every watt of inference flows through hyperscale campuses. That keeps the U.S. constrained by the slow variables of physical infrastructure: siting fights, interconnection queues, transmission build-outs, and the pace at which we can pour concrete and stack transformers. In a world where America is increasingly energy-bound rather than chip-bound, a strategy that relies solely on ever more flexible datacenters still runs into the hard limits described above.</p>\n<h2>The Untapped Asset: America's Local Accelerator Bounty</h2>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-28-gdi/abundance_of_local.png\" style=\"width:100%\"><p>The United States has an unusually dense installed base of local AI accelerators in consumer devices, from smartphone NPUs to Apple M-series laptops. Activating this local compute as part of a hybrid inference system would immediately add 30–40% to the nation's usable inference capacity.</p></figure>\n<p>There is a quicker way out: treat the United States' installed base of local accelerators as a strategic asset. From smartphone NPUs to Apple's M-series laptops and emerging AI PCs, U.S. companies (i.e., Apple, AMD, Intel) already ship the chips that power the \"PC AI era\" [7]: local accelerators that can run models once confined to data centers within tight device power budgets. AI-capable PCs represented 14% of global shipments in Q2 2024, with the market expanding rapidly [8], and we estimate a total of 70–80M AI-accelerated PCs and Macs in the United States today<sup id=\"user-content-fnref-3\"><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-28-gdi#fn-3\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">3</a></sup>.</p>\n<p>Critically, these chipsets are now powerful enough to run local, open source AI models that have become good enough to matter. Sub-20B-parameter \"local LMs\" are now surprisingly capable: in a recent study, they correctly answered <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2511.07885\" target=\"_blank\">88.7% of single-turn chat and reasoning queries</a>[1], and they already run at interactive latencies on a single high-end laptop or phone.</p>\n<p>In terms of our simple GDI equation, lighting up the U.S. local accelerator base for inference does three useful things at the same time:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>\n<p><strong>IPW goes up.</strong> Local chips are engineered for tight battery and thermal envelopes, and small models are now good enough to answer the majority of everyday queries. When we route those easy queries to small local models instead of always waking up a giant frontier model in the cloud, we get more \"useful answers per joule\" at the system level. Cloud accelerators still win on raw efficiency for the same model, but using the right-size model on an energy-conscious local chip for the bulk of traffic raises overall intelligence per watt.</p>\n</li>\n<li>\n<p><strong>P<sub>C</sub> goes up.</strong> P<sub>C</sub> is \"how much power your installed base of accelerators could draw if you ran them flat out.\" Today, P<sub>C</sub> is effectively counted as \"datacenter GPUs only,\" and the accelerators in laptops and phones are mostly idle from an AI standpoint. Turning those consumer accelerators into inference targets increases the total chip power you can, in principle, put to work for AI. National P<sub>C</sub> becomes \"datacenter GPUs plus millions of client devices,\" — by our estimates 70-80M from AI PCs alone<sup id=\"user-content-fnref-3\"><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-28-gdi#fn-3\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">3</a></sup> — not just whatever is inside a few hyperscale campuses.</p>\n</li>\n<li>\n<p><strong>P<sub>A</sub> goes up in practice.</strong> P<sub>A</sub> is \"power that can actually be delivered to AI workloads,\" which for cloud inference is increasingly limited by siting, substation headroom, and transmission interconnections around a small number of hyperscale campuses. Local devices, by contrast, sit behind distribution infrastructure that is already built to serve homes, offices, and base stations. When a share of inference runs on those devices, it taps into spare distribution and behind-the-meter capacity that a single new datacenter cannot easily access. This does not magically increase national generation capacity, but it relieves grid and interconnection bottlenecks: more of the existing generation fleet can be used without waiting on new high-voltage interconnections. Aggregated across the country, this amounts to a large additional pool of deliverable compute power, even if not one more datacenter interconnection is approved.</p>\n</li>\n</ul>\n<p>By treating local accelerators as part of the national AI infrastructure, the United States can move IPW, P<sub>C</sub>, and P<sub>A</sub> together while also making its AI system more efficient, freeing up scarce training capacity, and hardening the inference layer, instead of fighting for marginal gains in datacenters alone.</p>\n<h2>How to Unlock Local Accelerators: Hybrid Inference</h2>\n<p>Local accelerators look like \"free\" compute sitting on every desk and in every pocket—but we can't just move all inference onto them. A meaningful slice of queries still need frontier models: they're unusually challenging reasoning tasks, or must hit strict service-level guarantees that today's small local models can't yet meet. The right architecture is therefore not \"all cloud\" or \"all local,\" but a hybrid system with a router that makes one decision per query: can a small on-device model answer at the required quality and latency, or do we escalate to the datacenter? Everyday tasks stay local; only the hardest or most data-hungry queries go to the cloud.</p>\n<p>Our <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2511.07885\" target=\"_blank\">Intelligence Per Watt study</a> gives the empirical foundation for building such a hybrid system. Across 1M real-world single-turn chat and reasoning queries, we find that ensembles of local LMs running on consumer accelerators can correctly answer about >80% of single turn queries, and their IPW has improved roughly 5.3× from 2023 to 2025—3.1× from model advances and 1.7× from accelerator advances [1]. Cloud accelerators, however, still deliver at least a 1.4× IPW advantage on the same models [1]. Together, these facts define the design space: local models are now capable enough to handle most of the load, but frontier hardware remains the best place to run the tail of extremely demanding queries.</p>\n<p>When we put these pieces together in a routing system, running over real-world traffic, a hybrid router that routes each query to the smallest capable local model — falling back to the cloud when needed — <strong>cuts energy by 64%, compute by 62%, and costs by 59%</strong> versus sending everything to the cloud [1]. As local accelerators and models continue to improve, an even larger share of queries can stay on-device, and these savings compound directly into higher Gross Domestic Intelligence.</p>\n<h2>Strategic Advantages of Hybrid Inference</h2>\n<p>Hybrid local–cloud inference delivers three distinct strategic advantages beyond maximizing Gross Domestic Intelligence:</p>\n<p><strong>First, it increases usable capacity and accelerates deployment.</strong> By delegating a large share of routine queries from frontier cloud models to capable local models on client devices, the U.S. can expand effective AI capacity while keeping hyperscale infrastructure focused on the hardest workloads. Because the chips and power infrastructure are already deployed—in smartphones, laptops, and desktops — a hybrid strategy can immediately raise the practical value of both P<sub>C</sub> and P<sub>A</sub>, increasing GDI by roughly 2-4x. This is a software change, not a hardware change: no interconnect queues, no construction lead times.</p>\n<p><strong>Second, it structurally improves both privacy and resilience.</strong> Keeping sensitive tokens and intermediate states on the device wherever possible reduces the volume of personal and mission-relevant data sent to centralized services. At the same time, distributing inference across millions of endpoints instead of concentrating it in a small number of hyperscale campuses lowers exposure to bulk data collection and single-point physical or cyber failures. In GDI terms, this makes the system less brittle with respect to localized shocks in P<sub>A</sub> (regional grid stress, datacenter outages) while maintaining a large and diverse pool of P<sub>C</sub> that can continue serving queries from unaffected devices. This matters most in environments where connectivity is intermittent or contested—from military and industrial edge operations to everyday AWS-scale cloud outages. Because local inference also reduces per-query cost, resilience effectively \"pays for itself\" rather than operating as a pure insurance premium: the same architectural choices that make the system harder to disrupt also make it cheaper to run.</p>\n<p><strong>Finally, it strengthens U.S. AI leadership abroad by exporting an American-aligned AI stack.</strong> The installed base of high-end phones and PCs already shipping with U.S.- or ally-designed application processors—paired with an emerging ecosystem of strong open-weight local models (<a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2508.10925\" target=\"_blank\">gpt-oss</a>, <a href=\"https://www.datocms-assets.com/64837/1763662397-1763646865-olmo_3_technical_report-1.pdf\" target=\"_blank\">Olmo</a>, <a href=\"https://www.ibm.com/new/announcements/ibm-granite-4-0-hyper-efficient-high-performance-hybrid-models\" target=\"_blank\">IBM Granite</a>, <a href=\"https://blog.google/technology/developers/gemma-open-models/\" target=\"_blank\">Gemma</a> and others)—creates a natural channel for pushing American models, tooling, and norms into foreign markets. Local models that are not developed in CCP-controlled state-owned enterprises are also more likely to provide users with a genuine privacy layer, reducing how often sensitive information needs to be sent to the cloud, not just in the United States but worldwide. Because <a href=\"https://www.reuters.com/world/china/huaweis-new-laptop-uses-older-china-made-chip-us-curbs-stall-smic-report-shows-2025-06-23/\" target=\"_blank\">Huawei still lags Apple and AMD</a> in efficient consumer silicon [9, 14, 15], hybrid local–cloud inference running on domestic hardware effectively exports an American-aligned AI stack: U.S. and allied chips on the device, U.S. and allied models in both local and cloud form, and an ecosystem that makes it easy for foreign users and governments to choose this stack over CCP-aligned alternatives.</p>\n<h2>Policy Levers: Making Hybrid Inference America's Default</h2>\n<p>Hybrid inference can help address the technical problem of maximizing American GDI; smart policy is what turns that design choice into a durable national advantage by shaping how much of national P<sub>A</sub> and P<sub>C</sub> actually translates into deployed intelligence.</p>\n<p><strong>External Policy:</strong> Export controls remain the primary tool for keeping China compute-bound. The simple GDI logic still applies: as long as the CCP can build power capacity faster than it can acquire high-IPW accelerators and advanced fab tools [3], controls on those tools are the main brake on China's ability to translate cheap energy into GDI. Maintaining and refining these controls — especially on cutting-edge accelerators and the lithography equipment needed to manufacture them — ensures that America's advantage in chip technology compounds over time.</p>\n<p><strong>Internal Policy:</strong> A range of policy levers can accelerate the transition to hybrid systems by making client-side inference the path of least resistance. Tax breaks for model providers who push inference to the client side, IPW standards for utility interconnection, and data privacy rules that incentivize local inference could all shift the economics in favor of distributed architectures. Procurement rules — especially in the federal government and defense ecosystem — that favor architectures defaulting to local inference when possible would further accelerate adoption. These policies work best when paired with targeted incentives for American labs to develop open-source models optimized for cloud integration, building on efforts like Llama, Gemma, Phi, gpt-oss to ensure the U.S. dominates both ends of the hybrid stack.</p>\n<h2>The Path Forward</h2>\n<p>The AI competition is entering a new phase. Victory will not be determined by who builds the most datacenters, but by who can convert energy into intelligence most efficiently.</p>\n<p>The United States doesn't need to win a gigawatt-for-gigawatt race against China's power plant and grid expansion. Instead, it should maximize Gross Domestic Intelligence by exploiting an asymmetric advantage China cannot easily replicate: hundreds of millions of high-performance local accelerators already deployed in homes, offices, and pockets both across America and around the world combined with world-leading efficiency gains from frontier labs and continued open-source innovation. The result: American hardware everywhere, running American local models, coordinated by American frontier models on American compute infrastructure, using distributed power to bypass grid constraints and keep sensitive data beyond CCP reach.</p>\n<p>Hybrid inference systems that redistribute workloads between local devices and cloud infrastructure can simultaneously raise all three components of GDI — intelligence per watt, chip capacity, and accessible power — while making America's AI infrastructure more resilient, privacy-preserving, and cost-effective.</p>\n<p>The path forward is clear: maintain export controls that keep adversaries compute-bound, accelerate the shift to hybrid inference architectures, and treat local compute as critical national infrastructure. In an inference-dominated era, the nation that best leverages distributed intelligence will shape the century. America has the chips, the models, and the ecosystem to win.</p>\n<h2>Acknowledgements</h2>\n<p>Thank you to Andrew Grotto, Akhil Iyer, John Hennessy, Ashray Narayan, Brad Brown, Yasa Baig, Jerry Liu, Catherine Deng and Mayee Chen for providing feedback on this draft.</p>\n<h2>References</h2>\n<p>[1] Saad-Falcon, Jon, et al. \"Intelligence per Watt: Measuring Intelligence Efficiency of Local AI.\" arXiv, arXiv:2511.07885v2, 14 Nov. 2025, arxiv.org/abs/2511.07885.</p>\n<p>[2] Allen, Gregory C. \"Understanding the Biden Administration's Updated Export Controls.\" Center for Strategic and International Studies, Dec. 2024, <a href=\"http://www.csis.org/analysis/understanding-biden-administrations-updated-export-controls\" target=\"_blank\">www.csis.org/analysis/understanding-biden-administrations-updated-export-controls</a>.</p>\n<p>[3] \"How US Export Controls Have (and Haven't) Curbed Chinese AI.\" AI Frontiers, 2025, ai-frontiers.org/articles/us-chip-export-controls-china-ai.</p>\n<p>[4] U.S. Department of Energy. \"DOE Releases New Report Evaluating Increase in Electricity Demand from Data Centers.\" Energy.gov, 20 Dec. 2024, <a href=\"http://www.energy.gov/articles/doe-releases-new-report-evaluating-increase-electricity-demand-data-centers\" target=\"_blank\">www.energy.gov/articles/doe-releases-new-report-evaluating-increase-electricity-demand-data-centers</a>.</p>\n<p>[5] Green, Alastair, et al. \"How Data Centers and the Energy Sector Can Sate AI's Hunger for Power.\" McKinsey &#x26; Company, 2024, <a href=\"http://www.mckinsey.com/industries/private-capital/our-insights/how-data-centers-and-the-energy-sector-can-sate-ais-hunger-for-power\" target=\"_blank\">www.mckinsey.com/industries/private-capital/our-insights/how-data-centers-and-the-energy-sector-can-sate-ais-hunger-for-power</a>.</p>\n<p>[6] Noffsinger, Jesse, et al. \"The Cost of Compute: A $7 Trillion Race to Scale Data Centers.\" McKinsey &#x26; (2025, September 8).
