(2025). $64 billion of data center projects have been blocked or delayed amid local opposition. <a href=\"https://www.datacenterwatch.org/report\" target=\"_blank\">https://www.datacenterwatch.org/report</a></p>\n<p>[14] Shilov, A. (2024, December 12). Huawei sticks to 7nm for latest processor as China's chip advancements stall. Tom's Hardware. <a href=\"https://www.tomshardware.com/tech-industry/huawei-sticks-to-7nm-for-latest-processor-as-chinas-chip-advancements-stall\" target=\"_blank\">https://www.tomshardware.com/tech-industry/huawei-sticks-to-7nm-for-latest-processor-as-chinas-chip-advancements-stall</a></p>\n<p>[15] Shilov, A. (2025, November). Huawei's Ascend AI chip ecosystem scales up as China pushes for semiconductor independence — however, firm lags behind on efficiency and performance. Tom's Hardware. <a href=\"https://www.tomshardware.com/tech-industry/semiconductors/huaweis-ascend-ai-chip-ecosystem-scales\" target=\"_blank\">https://www.tomshardware.com/tech-industry/semiconductors/huaweis-ascend-ai-chip-ecosystem-scales</a></p>\n<p>[16] Ong, I., Almahairi, A., Wu, V., Chiang, W.-L., Wu, T., Gonzalez, J. E., Kadous, M. W., &#x26; Stoica, I. (2025). RouteLLM: Learning to route LLMs with preference data. In Proceedings of the Thirteenth International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR 2025). <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.18665\" target=\"_blank\">https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.18665</a></p>\n<p>[17] Feng, T., Shen, Y., &#x26; You, J. (2025). GraphRouter: A graph-based router for LLM selections. In Proceedings of the Thirteenth International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR 2025). <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2410.03834\" target=\"_blank\">https://arxiv.org/abs/2410.03834</a></p>\n<p>[18] Jin, R., Shao, P., Wen, Z., Wu, J., Feng, M., Zhang, S., &#x26; Tao, J. (2025). RadialRouter: Structured representation for efficient and robust large language models routing. arXiv preprint arXiv:2506.03880. <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.03880\" target=\"_blank\">https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.03880</a></p>\n<div>\n<hr>\n<ol>\n<li id=\"user-content-fn-1\">Our \"local compute multiplier\" is obtained by explicitly comparing device counts and approximate per-device throughput for datacenter accelerators and AI PCs. We assume that by around 2025 U.S. datacenters host on the order of 1.4×10⁷ H100-class accelerators, based on CSIS/SemiAnalysis estimates that U.S. labs and hyperscalers will have access to about <a href=\"https://www.csis.org/analysis/securing-agi-laurel-export-controls-compute-gap-and-chinas-counterstrategy\" target=\"_blank\">14.31 million AI accelerators by the end of 202</a> and NVIDIA documentation showing H100-class parts deliver on the order of a few thousand INT8 TOPS each (<a href=\"https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/data-center/h100\" target=\"_blank\">∼2,000 INT8 TOPS each</a>). For local devices, we triangulate from NVIDIA's statement that there are already \"<a href=\"https://nvidianews.nvidia.com/news/generative-ai-rtx-pcs-and-workstations\" target=\"_blank\">over 100 million RTX AI PCs and workstations</a>\" in the installed base, market-research estimates that the global Mac user base is about <a href=\"https://www.spyhunter.com/shm/macos-stats\" target=\"_blank\">100.4 million with roughly 40.3% of Mac users in the United States</a> (implying ~40 million U.S. Macs, most now on Apple silicon), and Canalys/Intel guidance that AI-capable PCs will ship in the tens of millions per year with cumulative AI PC shipments exceeding <a href=\"https://www.lightreading.com/ai-machine-learning/ai-capable-pcs-forecast-to-make-up-40-of-global-pc-shipments-in-2025-canalys\" target=\"_blank\">100 million globally by the end of 2025</a>. On conservative assumptions this yields an installed base of roughly 7×10⁷ U.S. AI-capable PCs (RTX-class Windows machines plus Apple-silicon Macs and NPU-equipped \"AI PCs\"), each delivering O(10²) effective INT8 TOPS for LLM inference (we use 150 TOPS as a central average), so local PCs contribute about 30–40% as much raw INT8 inference throughput as the existing U.S. datacenter fleet, implying that turning them on for inference would raise total national usable compute by roughly one-third—about a 1.3× multiplier.<a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-28-gdi#fnref-1\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">↩</a></li>\n<li id=\"user-content-fn-2\">We compute the GDI multiplier as follows. This calculation applies to single-turn chat and reasoning queries workloads. We assume: (1) a 235B-total-parameter, 22B-active-parameter frontier model in the cloud (Qwen3-235B-A22B), (2) a 20B-total-parameter, 3.6B-active-parameter model running locally (GPT-OSS-20B), (3) local compute adds 35–40% to baseline U.S. datacenter FLOPs, (4) 88.7% of single turn chat and reasoning queries can be accurately serviced by ≤20B parameter model, and (5) account for hybrid local-cloud router accuracy ranges from 60-100% [16,17,18]. We define GDI as proportional to query throughput. In the baseline, all queries go to the cloud model, yielding throughput Qbase = C/Fbig, where C is datacenter capacity and Fbig is per-query cost. In the hybrid scenario, a router sends fraction αeff to the local model and (1 − αeff) to the cloud. Since FLOPs scale linearly with parameters, the 235B-A22B model costs ~6.1× more per query than the 20B-A3.6B model. Two capacity constraints apply: cloud capacity (1 − αeff) × Q × Fbig ≤ C; local capacity αeff × Q × Fsmall ≤ 0.35–0.40C. An oracle router achieves αoracle = 0.887; practical routers achieve αeff = a × αoracle for accuracy a ∈ [0.6, 1.0]. Solving yields Qhybrid/Qbase ≈2.1× at a = 0.6, ≈3.2× at a = 0.8, and ≈2.6× as a → 1. We report ≈3× as the central estimate; the full range is 2-4×.<a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-28-gdi#fnref-2\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">↩</a></li>\n<li id=\"user-content-fn-3\">We approximate the U.S. installed base of “AI PCs” as follows. The U.S. has roughly 133M households, and recent ACS-based analyses suggest that about 1 in 7 lack a large-screen computer, implying that ≈86–87% of households have at least one desktop or laptop (<a href=\"https://digitunity.org/data-highlights-persistent-u-s-computer-ownership-gap-in-detail\" target=\"_blank\">Digitunity, citing ACS</a>). That yields on the order of 115M PC-owning households. Allowing for more than one PC in many households plus workplace and education machines points to a total installed base of roughly 200–220M PCs and Macs in use; for shorthand we use 210M. U.S. desktop OS share data from Statcounter/Wikipedia (“<a href=\"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Usage_share_of_operating_systems\" target=\"_blank\">Usage share of operating systems</a>”) puts macOS at ≈28.5% of active desktop traffic, which, applied to that 210M figure, would suggest ≈60M Macs. Traffic-share tends to overstate macOS unit share, and external estimates of the global Mac base imply a lower U.S. count, so we conservatively assume that ≈40M of these are active Apple-silicon (M-series) Macs with on-device accelerators. NVIDIA reports “<a href=\"https://nvidianews.nvidia.com/news/generative-ai-rtx-pcs-and-workstations\" target=\"_blank\">over 100 million RTX AI PCs and workstations</a>” in the global installed base; combining this with a U.S. share of roughly one quarter of worldwide PC shipments (per <a href=\"https://www.gartner.com/en/newsroom/press-releases/2025-04-14-gartner-says-worldwide-pc-shipments-increased-4-point-8-percent-in-first-quarter-of-2025\" target=\"_blank\">Gartner PC shipment data</a>) and allowing for overlap with the Mac base yields ≈20–25M RTX-class systems in the U.S. Gartner and IDC forecast tens of millions of AI PCs shipping annually, and Intel states it is on track to ship more than “<a href=\"https://www.intc.com/news-events/press-releases/detail/1726/intel-reports-fourth-quarter-and-full-year-2024-financial\" target=\"_blank\">100M AI PCs by the end of 2025</a>”; scaling by U.S. share and excluding devices already counted in the RTX bucket suggests another ≈10–15M recent Intel/AMD NPU-equipped AI PCs. In shorthand: we assume ≈40M Apple-silicon Macs + ≈20–25M U.S. RTX systems + ≈10–15M additional NPU-based AI PCs, for a conservative, order-of-magnitude estimate of roughly <strong>70–80M AI-accelerated PCs and Macs in the United States today</strong>, before counting phones, tablets, or consoles.<a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-28-gdi#fnref-3\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">↩</a></li>\n</ol>\n</div>,American leadership in AI has come under threat from a quiet but persistent strategy executed by the Chinese Communist Party. Locked out of…,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-28-gdi,<a href=\"https://x.com/jdunnmon\">Jared Dunnmon*</a>, <a href=\"https://x.com/avanika15\">Avanika Narayan*</a>, <a href=\"https://jonsaadfalcon.com\">Jon Saad-Falcon*</a>, <a href='https://cs.stanford.edu/people/chrismre/'>Chris Ré</a>.,2026-07-22T18:37:38.420Z,2025-11-28T07:00:00.232Z,[{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-28-gdi/banner.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":2816,\"height\":1536,\"blurhash\":\"LMJ%,d_LxZRjxtr[ayX5=yxZkARQ\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-28-gdi/abundance_of_local.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":2816,\"height\":1536,\"blurhash\":\"LRKA$btRsmsn}@M|j@xamln$WCNG\"}],163778663386687488,1211296081845936135,ParallelKittens: Simple and Fast Multi-GPU AI Kernels,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-17-pk,<em>Note: This is an introduction post for our two longer blog posts, new ThunderKittens updates, new multi-GPU kernels, and our paper release. For more details:</em>\n<ul><li style=\"margin:2px 0\"><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-09-22-pgl\" target=\"_blank\">Part 1: How do we perform multi-GPU communication using NVLink/NVSwitch in our kernels?</a></li>\n<li style=\"margin:2px 0\"><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-17-fluffy-kittens\" target=\"_blank\">Part 2: What are the general principles for building efficient multi-GPU kernels on modern hardware?</a></li>\n<li style=\"margin:2px 0\"><a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/ThunderKittens\" target=\"_blank\">Code</a></li>\n<li style=\"margin:2px 0\"><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2511.13940\" target=\"_blank\">Paper</a></li></ul>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-17-pk/sp-kittens.png\" alt=\"Sequence-parallel kittens\"><p><em>Figure 1: \"Sequence-parallel\" kittens</em></p></div>\n<p>In the last few years, we have really put a lot of effort into making AI more efficient. We have worked on making AI use less compute (e.g., BASED), be more hardware-aware (e.g., FlashAttention), be more easy to map to hardware (ThunderKittens), overlap its execution sequence (Megakernels), and run efficiently on multiple vendors (ThunderMittens for Apple Silicon, HipKittens for AMD).</p>\n<p>At this point, we believe <em>GPU networking</em> offers many new and exciting opportunities for AI efficiency. This is especially true due to recent advancements in GPU networking hardware. For instance, with the introduction of NVSwitch 4th generation, we now have compute inside the networking fabric (<em>in-network compute</em>), we can perform asynchronous network transfers from the device-side with the Tensor Memory Accelerator (TMA) <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-17-pk#footnote-1\" target=\"_blank\">[1]</a>, and we are shifting from scale-out architectures to scale-up architectures (e.g., Nvidia planning a single system with 576 GPUs by 2027). That will open up new opportunities and challenges in both AI (e.g., how do we build models that are native to such systems?) and systems (e.g., fault tolerance). It will be more than just faster data or model parallelism!</p>\n<p>To lay early foundations for exploring these technologies, we did three things. First, <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-09-22-pgl\" target=\"_blank\">we extended ThunderKittens to support multi-GPU kernels</a>. Second, <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-17-fluffy-kittens\" target=\"_blank\">we explored hardware-driven principles that can be reused to write a variety of efficient multi-GPU kernels</a>. Third, <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/ThunderKittens/tree/main/kernels/parallel\" target=\"_blank\">we built a handful of new kernels to demonstrate the approach</a>. So far, our observations are simple:</p>\n<ul>\n<li><em>Transfer mechanisms</em>. There are multiple ways to initiate GPU networking, each with different costs. The right choice depends on the workload and scheduling strategy.</li>\n<li><em>Scheduling strategies</em>. We can overlap communication and compute at different levels: host-device, inter-SM, or intra-SM. Modern GPUs offer abundant execution resources and many ways to orchestrate them. The goal, as always, is to keep the tensor cores busy!</li>\n<li><em>Design overheads</em>. It turns out that off-the-shelf communication libraries (e.g., NCCL, NVSHMEM) are quite slow to adapt to new hardware features. Writing basic communication kernels from scratch (with fewer than 10 lines of device code in ThunderKittens) can easily outperform them.</li>\n<li><em>Tiles</em>. Tiles are still the way to go. Tile-granularity network communication not only saturates network bandwidth but also lets us keep the familiar ThunderKittens tile abstractions that make our kernel design life simple.</li>\n</ul>\n<p>While we continue exploring new directions, we are excited to share that we can already match or surpass state-of-the-art implementations across various parallel strategies with the updated ThunderKittens.</p>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-17-pk/all-reduce.png\" alt=\"BF16 all-reduce sum performance on 8xH100s and 8xB200s.\"><p><em>Figure 2: BF16 all-reduce sum performance on 8xH100s and 8xB200s.</em></p></div>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-17-pk/ag-gemm.png\" alt=\"BF16 all-gather + GEMM performance on 8xH100s.\"><p><em>Figure 3: BF16 all-gather + GEMM performance on 8xH100s.</em></p></div>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-17-pk/ring-attn.png\" alt=\"Ring Attention performance on 8xH100s (B = 16, H = 16, D = 128).\"><p><em>Figure 4: Ring Attention performance on 8xH100s (B = 16, H = 16, D = 128).</em></p></div>\n<p>We plan to keep adding new features, such as inter-node communication (as well as cleaning up the ThunderKittens repo and finally bringing proper documentation). We also have ideas for even more exciting applications (e.g., load-balancing MoEs). That said, the current APIs and kernels are stable, and the existing functionality isn’t expected to change. We are excited to release it to the public. Feedback is always welcome! Feel free to reach out to Stuart at <a href=\"mailto:ssul@cs.stanford.edu\" target=\"_blank\">ssul@cs.stanford.edu</a>.</p>\n<p><strong>Read more: <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-09-22-pgl\" target=\"_blank\">Part 1</a> | <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-17-fluffy-kittens\" target=\"_blank\">Part 2</a> | <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/ThunderKittens\" target=\"_blank\">Code</a> | <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2511.13940\" target=\"_blank\">Paper</a></strong> <br></p>\n<hr>\n<p id=\"user-content-footnote-1\">[1] It looks like AMD is also adding TMA-like hardware features (\"TDM\") as well! (<a href=\"https://github.com/triton-lang/triton/pull/8333/files\" target=\"_blank\">link</a>)</p>,Note: This is an introduction post for our two longer blog posts, new ThunderKittens updates, new multi-GPU kernels, and our paper release…,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-17-pk,<a href=\"https://stuartsul.com/\">Stuart Sul</a>, <a href=\"https://arorasimran.com/\">Simran Arora</a>, <a href='https://benjaminfspector.com/'>Benjamin Spector</a>, <a href=\"https://cs.stanford.edu/people/chrismre/\">Chris Ré</a>,2026-07-22T18:37:38.419Z,2025-11-17T07:00:00.109Z,[{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-17-pk/sp-kittens.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1536,\"height\":1024,\"blurhash\":\"LOQT1Day?bxu-;t7IURj~qRjWBxu\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-17-pk/all-reduce.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1358,\"height\":1186,\"blurhash\":\"L8S6Soo#?K$,^-juInRi-rs;WTNF\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-17-pk/ag-gemm.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":2658,\"height\":876,\"blurhash\":\"LTR{lR.AWAROyDRPROozyEMco#tR\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-17-pk/ring-attn.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1286,\"height\":768,\"blurhash\":\"LbRC^|xwxwo1tSf7WAae-@V?V=Wp\"}],163778663386687488,1211296081845936136,Loads and Loads of Fluffy Kittens,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-17-fluffy-kittens,<p><strong>Links: <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-17-pk\" target=\"_blank\">Intro</a> | <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-09-22-pgl\" target=\"_blank\">Part 1</a> | <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-17-fluffy-kittens\" target=\"_blank\">Part 2</a> (this post) | <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/ThunderKittens\" target=\"_blank\">Code</a> | <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2511.13940\" target=\"_blank\">Paper</a></strong> <br></p>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-17-fluffy-kittens/fluffy-kittens-1.png\" alt=\"The kittens gather for a family photo.\"><p><em>Figure 1: The kittens gather for a family photo.</em></p></div>\n<p>A couple of months ago, we shared a <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-09-22-pgl\" target=\"_blank\">post</a> demystifying how multi-GPU communication works, introducing new multi-GPU functionalities in <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/ThunderKittens\" target=\"_blank\">ThunderKittens</a>, and showing how these simplify the process of writing high-performance multi-GPU kernels. That post primarily focused on <em>enabling</em> the ability to write communication kernels that span multiple GPUs, which already let us write communication kernels up to 2.6x faster than NCCL using only a few dozen lines of code!</p>\n<p>Yet there is a breadth of parallelization strategies when we look across AI workloads. Each requires specific optimizations to preserve compute utilization by overlapping computation and communication. What we found is that there are fundamental, generalizable trade-offs in communication mechanisms and scheduling patterns that depend on the workload properties. By applying such principles with ThunderKittens, we can easily build efficient compute-communication kernels that match or surpass hand-optimized, low-level implementations.</p>\n<p>In this post, as a follow-up to our previous one, we share:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>Our findings on the principles for writing efficient compute-communication kernels, focusing on inter-GPU transfer mechanisms, overlapping schedules, and tiling.</li>\n<li>New compute-communication kernels built with ThunderKittens. We fuse common operators used in data, tensor, sequence, and expert parallelism: including fused collective (all-gather, reduce-scatter, all-reduce) + GEMM operations, distributed attention variants, and combined token dispatch with expert GEMMs. We show that we can match or outperform hand-optimized kernels, all with only a few dozen lines of device code.</li>\n</ul>\n<h2>Properly Overlapping the Kittens</h2>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-17-fluffy-kittens/fluffy-kittens-2.png\" alt=\"The kittens overlap for cuteness density.\"><p><em>Figure 2: The kittens overlap for cuteness density.</em></p></div>\n<p>Modern AI workloads run on multi-GPU platforms like HGX H100 (8xH100s), HGX B200 (8xB200s), or GB200 NVL72 (72xB200s). These systems interconnect datacenter-grade GPUs through NVLink and NVSwitch, providing up to 900 GB/s of unidirectional bandwidth between any two remote High Bandwidth Memory (HBM). As a quick reminder, the figure below from our previous post illustrates these systems’ high-level topology.</p>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-17-fluffy-kittens/hgx-b200.png\" alt=\"NVIDIA HGB B200 overview.\"><p><em>Figure 3: NVIDIA HGB B200 overview. The PCIe path handles kernel execution, host-device data movement, and inter-node communication over InfiniBand or TCP, while device-to-device communication occurs entirely through NVLink and NVSwitch.</em></p></div>\n<p>An important thing to remember is that these platforms come with <em>loads and loads of heterogeneous resources that must be carefully orchestrated to achieve high utilization</em>. These include the register file, CUDA and tensor cores, special function units (SFUs), load/store units, the Tensor Memory Accelerator (TMA), the memory controller, HBM and NVLink bandwidth, and in-fabric accelerators within the NVSwitch.</p>\n<p>That’s quite a mouthful, and finding the balance across these resources is not so easy. Naively splitting a workload and applying parallelism strategies like data, tensor, sequence, or expert parallelism can end up saturating only one or two resources while leaving the rest idle. Poor overlapping design leads to load imbalance, where one resource becomes a serialized bottleneck while others sit underutilized.</p>\n<p>We don’t want that. After all, <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-09-28-tp-llama-main\" target=\"_blank\">we bought the whole GPU, so we’d better use the whole GPU</a>. One obvious and valid approach is to design hand-optimized kernels with per-operator (e.g., all-gather fused with GEMM) overlapping strategies. A long line of excellent prior work (e.g., <a href=\"https://discuss.pytorch.org/t/distributed-w-torchtitan-introducing-async-tensor-parallelism-in-pytorch/209487\" target=\"_blank\">1</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.06858\" target=\"_blank\">2</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.01889\" target=\"_blank\">3</a>, <a href=\"https://github.com/deepseek-ai/DeepEP\" target=\"_blank\">4</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.19811\" target=\"_blank\">5</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.04667\" target=\"_blank\">6</a>, <a href=\"https://github.com/NVIDIA/cutlass/tree/main/examples/65_distributed_gemm\" target=\"_blank\">7</a>) does this, and it’s been a major source of inspiration for our work.</p>\n<p>For this post, though, we wanted to step back and ask: what are the fundamental principles that can guide us in writing any compute-communication kernel on any modern GPU? Through building and experimenting with many compute-communication kernels, we’ve identified a few that seem to matter repeatedly: using the right transfer mechanism, using the right scheduling strategy, and, of course, using the tiles! We’ll dive into each below.</p>\n<h4>Using the Right Transfer Mechanism</h4>\n<p>There are 3 ways to perform inter-GPU data transfers: using the per-GPU copy engine, the Tensor Memory Accelerator (TMA), or register-level instructions (e.g., <code>ld</code>, <code>st</code>, <code>red</code>, or <code>multimem</code>). Each comes with its own trade-offs, and naively relying on a single method leads to failures. For example, while the copy engine can deliver the highest bandwidth without involving any SMs (as shown in the previous post), it only reaches peak throughput for large message sizes. As illustrated in the figure below, moving 1 GB of data typically requires chunks of around 256 MB to fully saturate the link. Thus, the copy engine is not well-suited for fine-grained communication.</p>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-17-fluffy-kittens/nvlink-bandwidth-1.png\" alt=\"Observed memory bandwidth utilization 1.\"><p><em>Figure 4: Observed memory bandwidth utilization for a 1 GB peer-to-peer transfer over NVLink between 2 H100s.</em></p></div>\n<p>On the other hand, TMA is excellent for inter-GPU communication in many ways. It can saturate NVLink bandwidth with messages as small as 2 KB while using very few resources: about 15 out of 148 SMs on B200 GPUs, as shown in the figure below. With intra-SM overlapping (discussed later), the effective SM usage can drop to zero. TMA also avoids adding register pressure since it relies only on shared memory and does not occupy execution lanes. Plus, it naturally supports tile-granularity communication.</p>\n<p>TMA does have a few limitations, though. For example, it does not support in-network reduction operations, which are only possible through register-level instructions. Register instructions can also saturate bandwidth at element-wise granularity, as long as the memory accesses are properly coalesced.</p>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-17-fluffy-kittens/nvlink-bandwidth-2.png\" alt=\"Observed memory bandwidth utilization 2.\"><p><em>Figure 5: The number of SMs it takes to saturate NVLink bandwidth, using different communication mechanisms.</em></p></div>\n<p>It is therefore important to use these different inter-GPU transfer mechanisms deliberately, depending on the workload. Existing off-the-shelf libraries do not handle this for you! For example, NVSHMEM’s <code>put</code> and <code>put_nbi</code> APIs (which many other libraries build upon) ultimately rely on volatile <code>st</code> instructions for intra-node transfers. They also enforce issuing <code>__ldg</code> to access peer memory addresses and add thread-level synchronizations, which together increase latency and reduce NVLink bandwidth utilization.</p>\n<h4>Using the Right Overlapping Schedule</h4>\n<p>While the specific optimal schedule can vary depending on the target operator, we find that overlapping strategies generally fall into two categories: <em>intra-SM overlapping</em> and <em>inter-SM overlapping</em>. Each comes with its own strengths and weaknesses.</p>\n<p>In <em>intra-SM overlapping</em>, different warps or threads within the same SM handle compute and communication concurrently. The idea is simple: we dedicate one or more warps (or a few threads) to communication, just as we specialize warps for compute or memory operations in many modern kernels. In the best case, intra-SM overlapping yields zero loss: all tensor cores across all SMs stay busy, and inter-GPU communication is fully hidden, adding no extra overhead. We note that this ideal case is not merely theoretical. In our fused GEMM + reduce-scatter kernel, we were able to reduce the non-overlapped communication portion to under 1%, completely hiding NVLink transfers and atomic additions behind the tensor-core GEMM.</p>\n<p>The challenge with intra-SM overlapping, however, is that the communication pattern must somehow align with the computation to avoid interference. Whether communication uses TMA or register operations, it must operate on the same data used by the computation (e.g., inputs, intermediates, or outputs). For example, in GEMM + reduce-scatter, the communication pattern matches computation perfectly; the output tile of a tiled GEMM can immediately be sent to the destination device and atomically added.</p>\n<p>However, when the communication pattern cannot align with computation, the kernel ends up splitting resources like the register file or shared memory, resulting in poor overlap and reduced compute utilization. This makes <em>inter-SM overlapping</em> necessary.</p>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-17-fluffy-kittens/gemm-rs-ar-schedules.png\" alt=\"GEMM + reduce-scatter (RS) and GEMM + all-reduce (AR).\"><p><em>Figure 6: GEMM + reduce-scatter (RS) and GEMM + all-reduce (AR) performance on 8xH100s across overlapping schedules.</em></p></div>\n<p><em>Inter-SM overlapping</em> dedicates entire SMs almost exclusively to either compute or communication tasks. This approach becomes especially useful when communication patterns that minimize NVLink traversal are difficult to achieve with intra-SM overlapping. Two main factors drive this advantage: <em>in-network acceleration</em> and <em>remote L2 caching behavior</em>.</p>\n<p><em>In-network acceleration</em> is enabled by modern high-bandwidth interconnects such as NVSwitch. As discussed in our previous post, NVSwitch integrates compute capabilities directly into the interconnect fabric, allowing in-network reductions. Efficient use of this capability requires inter-SM overlapping, since in-network acceleration relies on synchronous, register-based execution that increases register pressure and limits thread occupancy. For example, in workloads like fused GEMM + all-reduce, an effective approach is to accumulate partial results in local HBM, signal completion after each local write, and dedicate a few specialized SMs to perform a single in-network all-reduce once all devices have finished.</p>\n<p><em>Remote L2 caching behavior</em> is shown in the figure below from our previous post. Because remote HBM access bypasses the local L2 cache, data is cached only on the remote peer’s L2. This design simplifies inter-GPU memory consistency but makes every remote access bottlenecked by NVLink bandwidth. For operators where multiple thread blocks need to access the same remote data for computation (such as attention), a more effective strategy is to use inter-SM overlapping. For example, a few dedicated SMs can manage data transfers from peer HBMs to local HBM, allowing compute SMs to fully utilize local L2 cache bandwidth instead of repeatedly traversing NVLink.</p>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-17-fluffy-kittens/nvlink-data-path.png\" alt=\"The NVLink data path.\"><p><em>Figure 7: The NVLink data path, shown in red line. Note that the L2 cache is far-sided: data travels through the L2 cache of the source HBM, not the local one.</em></p></div>\n<p>For more discussion of the trade-offs in inter-GPU data transfer mechanisms and scheduling strategies, you can check out our <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2511.13940\" target=\"_blank\">paper</a>.</p>\n<h4>Using the Tiles 😎</h4>\n<p>And of course, tiles remain just as effective for writing communication kernels as they did for <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-05-12-tk\" target=\"_blank\">single-GPU kernels</a>. One potential concern is that tiling could reduce inter-GPU bandwidth utilization due to its fine granularity. However, our benchmarks show that this is not the case, provided we use TMA or properly coalesce remote HBM accesses. Tiles simply make everything easier. Really, much easier. Every kernel we are releasing today adds fewer than 50 lines of device code on top of the original single-GPU kernel (for example, GEMM) to support communication.</p>\n<h2>New Kernels</h2>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-17-fluffy-kittens/fluffy-kittens-3.png\" alt=\"The kittens gather for a fruitful day of labor.\"><p><em>Figure 8: The kittens gather for a fruitful day of <s>nap</s> labor.</em></p></div>\n<p>To demonstrate the power of the new ThunderKittens multi-GPU API and the ideas discussed above, we implemented a handful of compute-communication kernels on both Hopper and Blackwell platforms. We organize them by popular parallelization strategies: data, tensor, sequence, and expert parallelism, each of which we present below.</p>\n<h4>Data and Tensor Parallelism</h4>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-17-fluffy-kittens/dp-kittens.png\" alt=\"Data-parallel kittens.\"><p><em>Figure 9: Data-parallel kittens.</em></p></div>\n<p>For data and tensor parallelism, we optimize for a typical setup where we start with a batch-sharded input, perform an all-gather (AG), run the first GEMM with column-sharded weights, apply a nonlinear activation, then run the second GEMM with row-sharded weights, followed by a reduce-scatter (RS) or all-reduce (AR). We overlap communication and computation by pairing AG with the first GEMM (AG + GEMM) and the second GEMM with RS or AR (GEMM + RS, GEMM + AR). The performance for each configuration is shown in the figures below. As a note, we denote the GEMM shape as M x N x K, where the first operand has dimensions M x K and the second K x N.</p>\n<p>In our comparisons, cuBLAS + NCCL serves as the non-overlapped baseline, Triton Distributed represents the compiler-based overlapping approach, and Flux and CUTLASS are the hand-optimized kernels. PK (“ParallelKittens”) is our approach. Note that Flux and CUTLASS do not provide a GEMM + AR kernel, so they are not included in the GEMM + AR results.</p>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-17-fluffy-kittens/ag-gemm.png\" alt=\"AG + GEMM performance on 8xH100s.\"><p><em>Figure 10: AG + GEMM performance on 8xH100s. Local GEMM size is N x N/8 x N, with N given in the X-axis.</em></p></div>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-17-fluffy-kittens/gemm-rs.png\" alt=\"GEMM + RS performance on 8xH100s.\"><p><em>Figure 11: GEMM + RS performance on 8xH100s. Local GEMM size is N x N x N/8, with N given in the X-axis.</em></p></div>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-17-fluffy-kittens/gemm-ar.png\" alt=\"GEMM + AR performance on 8xH100s. \"><p><em>Figure 12: GEMM + AR performance on 8xH100s. Local GEMM size is N x N x N/8, with N given in the X-axis.</em></p></div>\n<h4>Sequence Parallelism</h4>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-17-fluffy-kittens/sp-kittens.png\" alt=\"Sequence-parallel kittens.\"><p><em>Figure 13: Sequence-parallel kittens.</em></p></div>\n<p>For sequence parallelism, we implemented two popular strategies for distributing the sequence dimension across multiple GPUs and computing self-attention: <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.01889\" target=\"_blank\">Ring Attention</a> and <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2309.14509\" target=\"_blank\">DeepSpeed-Ulysses</a>. We used xDiT and YunChang as their most efficient open-source reference implementations and compared our results against them. In the figures, the X-axis represents the total sequence length, which is evenly distributed across 8 H100 GPUs.</p>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-17-fluffy-kittens/ring-attn.png\" alt=\"Ring Attention performance on 8xH100s.\"><p><em>Figure 14: Ring Attention performance on 8xH100s across sequence lengths (B = 16, H = 16, D = 128).</em></p></div>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-17-fluffy-kittens/ulysses-attn.png\" alt=\"DeepSpeed-Ulysses attention layer performance on 8xH100s.\"><p><em>Figure 15: DeepSpeed-Ulysses attention layer performance on 8xH100s across sequence lengths (B = 16, H = 128, D = 128).</em></p></div>\n<h4>Expert Parallelism</h4>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-17-fluffy-kittens/ep-kittens.png\" alt=\"Expert-parallel kittens.\"><p><em>Figure 16: Expert-parallel kittens.</em></p></div>\n<p>In Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) layers with experts distributed across multiple GPUs (expert parallelism), token exchange accounts for a large portion of the total runtime (<a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.19811\" target=\"_blank\">up to 50%</a>). To reduce this overhead, <a href=\"https://github.com/deepseek-ai/DeepEP\" target=\"_blank\">many</a> <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.19811\" target=\"_blank\">approaches</a> overlap token dispatch with the first expert GEMM and the last expert GEMM with token combination. In this post, we focus on the first half of the MoE computation (overlapped token dispatch and GEMM), and compare our implementation against Comet, the state-of-the-art fine-grained overlapping kernel for expert parallelism.</p>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-17-fluffy-kittens/moe-dispatch-gemm.png\" alt=\"Expert-parallel token dispatch + GEMM performance on 8xH100s.\"><p><em>Figure 17: Expert-parallel token dispatch + GEMM performance on 8xH100s (top_k = 8, num_experts = 256, D = 7168, D_expert = 2048).</em></p></div>\n<p>You can explore even more kernels, including those for Blackwell, <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/ThunderKittens/tree/main/kernels/parallel\" target=\"_blank\">here</a>.</p>\n<h2>Coming Soon</h2>\n<p>Building efficient multi-GPU kernels with ThunderKittens has been exciting, but there is still loads and loads more to explore. We’re thrilled to soon present:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>Inter-node communication over InfiniBand with ThunderKittens</li>\n<li>NVFP4 support for ThunderKittens</li>\n<li>Integration of the above two with our <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-09-28-tp-llama-main\" target=\"_blank\">Megakernel framework</a> to build multi-node Mixture-of-Experts megakernels, supporting all commonly used precisions (BF16, MXFP8, NVFP4)</li>\n</ul>\n<p>Looking ahead, we are especially excited for the next wave of architectures (like NVL144), which signal a shift from scale-out to scale-up designs. These systems introduce hundreds of terabytes of on-device HBM, deeply hierarchical and massive on-chip memory structures, and new challenges in fault tolerance. These characteristics will invalidate many of the efficiency assumptions embedded in existing model designs, opening up fresh co-design opportunities. We hope ThunderKittens will help enable that exploration, and we will also be diving right in ourselves!</p>\n<p>As always, if you'd like to learn more or contribute, feel free to reach out to Stuart at <a href=\"mailto:ssul@cs.stanford.edu\" target=\"_blank\">ssul@cs.stanford.edu</a>. And huge thanks to <a href=\"https://cursor.com/home\" target=\"_blank\">Cursor</a> and <a href=\"https://www.together.ai/\" target=\"_blank\">Together AI</a> for making this work possible!</p>\n<p><strong>Links: <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-17-pk\" target=\"_blank\">Intro</a> | <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-09-22-pgl\" target=\"_blank\">Part 1</a> | <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-17-fluffy-kittens\" target=\"_blank\">Part 2</a> (this post) | <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/ThunderKittens\" target=\"_blank\">Code</a> | <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2511.13940\" target=\"_blank\">Paper</a></strong> <br></p>,Links: Intro | Part 1 | Part 2 (this post) | Code | Paper Figure 1: The kittens gather for a family photo.\n\nA couple of months ago, we…,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-17-fluffy-kittens,<a href=\"https://stuartsul.com/\">Stuart Sul</a>, <a href=\"https://arorasimran.com/\">Simran Arora</a>, <a href='https://benjaminfspector.com/'>Benjamin Spector</a>, <a Ré</a>,2026-07-22T18:37:38.418Z,2025-11-17T07:00:00.385Z,[{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-17-fluffy-kittens/fluffy-kittens-1.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1024,\"height\":784,\"blurhash\":\"LIL}4?IUR*WB_3%M%LWB~qWBxuWB\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-17-fluffy-kittens/fluffy-kittens-2.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1024,\"height\":1024,\"blurhash\":\"LQN,-%D*%gWA_3-p%MM{~qofj[WB\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-17-fluffy-kittens/hgx-b200.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1686,\"height\":1142,\"blurhash\":\"LCSF-9o#%OofyZWAVrjZ^kbHS2WV\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-17-fluffy-kittens/nvlink-bandwidth-1.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1342,\"height\":830,\"blurhash\":\"L3S$ovoJS}9Z~WD%D%f,NGxu-;%2\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-17-fluffy-kittens/nvlink-bandwidth-2.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1848,\"height\":738,\"blurhash\":\"L1Ss50xaxa%h^,IUoe9Zt*-;bH_2\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-17-fluffy-kittens/gemm-rs-ar-schedules.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1324,\"height\":816,\"blurhash\":\"LTR31R#AyF.TyFRjaxS4.AS~aJMw\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-17-fluffy-kittens/nvlink-data-path.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":2578,\"height\":932,\"blurhash\":\"LBRp8.x^x^xw18RjWARP-pR*R*Rk\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-17-fluffy-kittens/fluffy-kittens-3.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1536,\"height\":1024,\"blurhash\":\"L8ON8$D%%MM{.8t7%Mxu~qM{%Max\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-17-fluffy-kittens/dp-kittens.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1536,\"height\":1024,\"blurhash\":\"LIPZr$IU%MIU~qt7Rjt7%MayWBay\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-17-fluffy-kittens/ag-gemm.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":2640,\"height\":866,\"blurhash\":\"LTR{iK.AWAROyDRPM_ozyEMco#tR\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-17-fluffy-kittens/gemm-rs.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":2622,\"height\":840,\"blurhash\":\"LTR{fC.TWAVs%hRiMxozx^Mdo#tR\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-17-fluffy-kittens/gemm-ar.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1356,\"height\":874,\"blurhash\":\"LMRp2n_4g2Mx.9MxMxxu%NIAxux]\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-17-fluffy-kittens/sp-kittens.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1536,\"height\":1024,\"blurhash\":\"LSQ0XGRjt7Ri-;xvM{Rj~qM{t7xu\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-17-fluffy-kittens/ring-attn.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1304,\"height\":772,\"blurhash\":\"LYRC^}xw%On%xwayRiWB-@RiRhbI\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-17-fluffy-kittens/ulysses-attn.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1332,\"height\":792,\"blurhash\":\"LaR3TD%4xwn*t9j^RjWA-@W9V=a{\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-17-fluffy-kittens/ep-kittens.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1536,\"height\":1024,\"blurhash\":\"LUQ]+wIUaeay%Mj[j[of~qxuayay\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-17-fluffy-kittens/moe-dispatch-gemm.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1344,\"height\":992,\"blurhash\":\"LQR3G?%h-?o3?cWCM{WA.AROV?fk\"}],163778663386687488,1211296081845936137,Intelligence Per Watt: A Study of Local Intelligence Efficiency,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-11-ipw,<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0;margin-bottom:2em\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-11-ipw/tldr_figure.png\" style=\"width:100%\"></figure>\n<p><strong>TL;DR:</strong></p>\n<p>AI demand is growing exponentially, creating unprecedented pressure on data center infrastructure. While data centers dominate AI workloads due to superior compute density and efficiency, they face scaling constraints: years-long construction timelines, massive capital requirements, and energy grid limitations.</p>\n<p>History suggests an alternative path forward. From 1946-2009, computing efficiency (performance-per-watt) doubled every 1.5 years (Koomey et al.), enabling a <strong>redistribution of computing workloads</strong> from data center mainframes to personal computers (PCs). Critically, this transition didn't occur because PCs surpassed mainframes in raw performance: efficiency improvements enabled computing capable of meeting end-user needs within the power constraints of personal devices.</p>\n<p>We are at a similar inflection point. Local language models (LMs), with ≤20B active parameters, are <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2508.10925\" target=\"_blank\">surprisingly capable</a>, and local accelerators (e.g., M4 Max with 128GB unified memory) run LMs at <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2508.10925\" target=\"_blank\">interactive latencies</a>. Just as <em>compute efficiency</em> defined the transition to personal computing, we propose that <em>intelligence efficiency</em> defines the transition to local inference. To this end, we introduce <strong>intelligence per watt (IPW):</strong> task accuracy per unit of power. IPW is a unified metric for measuring <em>intelligence efficiency</em>, capturing both the intelligence delivered (capabilities) and power required (efficiency).</p>\n<p>We evaluate the current state and trajectory of local inference efficiency through two questions: (1) Are local LMs capable of accurately servicing a meaningful portion of today’s workloads? and (2) Given local power budgets, how efficiently do local accelerators convert watts into intelligence? We conduct a large-scale study across real-world single-turn chat and reasoning tasks, revealing:</p>\n<ul>\n<li><strong>Local LMs are capable and improving rapidly:</strong> <em>Local LMs can accurately respond to <strong>88.7%</strong> of single-turn chat and reasoning queries, with accuracy improving 3.1× from 2023-2025.</em></li>\n<li><strong>Local accelerator efficiency has room for improvement:</strong> Inference on a local accelerator (Qwen3-32B on an M4 Max) demonstrates 1.5x lower intelligence-per-watt vs. inference of the same local LM on an enterprise grade accelerator (i.e., NVIDIA B200).</li>\n<li><strong>Local intelligence efficiency is improving 5.3x from 2023 to 2025:</strong> 3.1x from model improvements (thanks to advances in model architectures, pretraining, post-training, and distillation) and 1.7x from accelerator improvements.</li>\n</ul>\n<p><strong>Call to Action:</strong> As AI demand grows exponentially, we must find better approaches to turn energy into intelligence. We envision a world where intelligence is everywhere and in every device: in earbuds providing instant translation, in glasses offering real-time visual assistance, and in pocket assistants solving graduate-level problems. By prioritizing intelligence-per-watt in both model design and hardware acceleration, we can bring powerful AI to billions of edge devices and make intelligence truly ubiquitous. We release a profiling harness to enable systematic benchmarking of intelligence per watt as local LMs and accelerators evolve.</p>\n<h2>🏢 The Mainframe Era — Demand is exploding faster than infrastructure can scale</h2>\n<p>Demand is surging, with Google Cloud reporting a 1300× increase in monthly token processing (see Figure 1), and NVIDIA noting a 10× year-over-year increase. Today, most AI queries are serviced by monolithic inference infrastructure: massive, centralized data centers with frontier LMs processing most queries, much like the time-sharing mainframes of the 1960s (<a href=\"https://youtu.be/LCEmiRjPEtQ\" target=\"_blank\">h/t Andrej Karpathy</a>).</p>\n<p>To keep up with this exploding demand, the industry is calling for unprecedented infrastructure buildouts spanning GPUs, energy projects and massive data center buildouts (<a href=\"https://www.theinformation.com/articles/sam-altman-wants-250-gigawatts-power-possible\" target=\"_blank\">Sam wants 250GW</a> 😱). Frontier labs are right to build large models: the path to AGI demands it.  But we're using that same infrastructure to answer \"write me an email\" and \"summarize this document.”</p>\n<p>Internal <a href=\"https://cdn.openai.com/pdf/a253471f-8260-40c6-a2cc-aa93fe9f142e/economic-research-chatgpt-usage-paper.pdf\" target=\"_blank\">ChatGPT telemetry data</a> shows 77% of requests are practical guidance, information seeking, or writing — tasks that don't require frontier capabilities. Put simply, <strong>user demand</strong> <strong>≠ frontier LM capabilities.</strong> This raises the question of whether these simpler tasks can be served by smaller, cheaper models locally.</p>\n<p>While centralized inference offers advantages like high-throughput batching and consistent utilization*,* offloading lower-complexity tasks <em>to</em> local compute <em><strong>could complement centralized systems</strong></em> preserving frontier infrastructure for tasks that truly demand them.</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:left;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-11-ipw/figure_1.png\" style=\"width:100%\">Figure #1: <strong>Exponential Increase in Inference Demand for Google Models in 2024-2025.</strong></figure>\n<h2>💻 Dawn of the PC AI Era — Local inference as a path to redistribute compute</h2>\n<p>Excitingly, we're seeing the emergence of two parallel trends across the model and hardware layers that allow for the possibility of redistributing AI workloads to local resources.</p>\n<p><strong>Better small models:</strong> Local LMs (≤20B active parameters) such as Qwen3 4B-14B, gpt-oss-20b, Gemma3 4B-12B, and IBM Granite 4.0 1B-7B are improving quickly, taking on an expanding range of tasks once limited to frontier‑scale models (Kumar et al., IBM, Yang et. al. (2025), Deepak et. al.,).</p>\n<p><strong>More powerful local accelerators:</strong> Local accelerators — from smartphone NPUs to laptop accelerators (Apple M4) to desktop GPUs (NVIDIA DGX Spark) — now run models once confined to data centers (see Figure 2). Consumer laptops with 128GB unified memory can run models approaching <a href=\"https://www.apple.com/newsroom/2024/10/apple-introduces-m4-pro-and-m4-max/\" target=\"_blank\">200B parameters</a> , desktop GPUs enable 200B parameter models with <a href=\"https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/products/workstations/dgx-spark/\" target=\"_blank\">quantization</a>, and smartphone <a href=\"https://itsfoss.com/android-on-device-ai/\" target=\"_blank\">NPUs run 7B models at over 8 tokens/second</a>.</p>\n<p>This raises a critical question: <strong>What role can local inference play in redistributing inference demand?</strong></p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:left;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-11-ipw/figure_2.png\" style=\"width:100%\">Figure #2: <strong>Improvement of Local Accelerator Memory Capacity (GB) over Time.</strong> Over the past decade, local accelerator memory capacity has increased drastically driven by technical advances, such as high-bandwidth memory (HBM) and unified memory architectures.</figure>\n<h2>🧠 / ⚡ Intelligence-per-watt: measuring intelligence efficiency</h2>\n<p>We answer this by measuring how effectively inference systems convert energy into useful computation, examining two key dimensions:</p>\n<ul>\n<li><strong>Capability</strong>: What % of today's single-turn chat and reasoning LLM queries can local LMs answer correctly?</li>\n<li><strong>Efficiency</strong>: How much useful compute (i.e., accurate inference) do we get per watt when running local LMs on local hardware? Energy efficiency is paramount for local inference given constrained power budgets—we benchmark against the same local LM running on enterprise accelerators.</li>\n</ul>\n<p>To answer these questions systematically, we introduce a new metric: <strong>intelligence-per-watt</strong>—a metric that quantifies how effectively inference systems convert energy into useful computation. IPW uses accuracy as a proxy for intelligence and measures task performance relative to power consumption. While accuracy is an <strong>imperfect proxy</strong> for model capabilities, <em>it provides a standardized way to compare inference efficiency across different model-hardware configurations.</em></p>\n<blockquote>\n<p>IPW = (mean accuracy across tasks) / (mean power draw during inference)</p>\n</blockquote>\n<p><em>Accuracy is expressed as a percentage; power draw is measured in watts (W).</em></p>\n<blockquote>\n</blockquote>\n<p>A higher IPW indicates greater inference efficiency—more “intelligence” delivered per watt of power consumed. We measure IPW by: <strong>(1)</strong> evaluating each model on diverse tasks to compute mean accuracy, <strong>(2)</strong> measuring power draw while processing each query, then averaging these measurements across queries, and <strong>(3)</strong> computing their ratio.</p>\n<h2>❓Studying Local AI Efficiency through Intelligence-Per-Watt</h2>\n<p>Our study focuses on <strong>single-query inference</strong> (i.e., batch size = 1), allowing us to isolate intrinsic <strong>model-hardware efficiency</strong> from system-level inference time optimizations. IPW captures gains from both <strong>model architecture</strong> (e.g., achieving higher accuracy with fewer parameters) and <strong>hardware design</strong> (e.g., more compute per watt), making it well-suited for comparing various model–accelerator pairings.</p>\n<p>To understand local AI efficiency, we conducted a <strong>large-scale empirical evaluation</strong>, benchmarking <strong>20+ state-of-the-art local LMs</strong> across diverse hardware setups. Our dataset includes <strong>1 million real-world queries</strong>, for which we rigorously measured both accuracy and energy usage. Below, we detail our experimental design and measurement methodology.</p>\n<h3><strong>Study Design</strong></h3>\n<figure style=\"text-align:left;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-11-ipw/figure_3.png\" style=\"width:100%\">Figure #3: <strong>Overview of IPW Study Components: Models, Hardware, Metrics, and Tasks.</strong></figure>\n<p><strong>Models:</strong> 20+ state-of-the-art local LMs (Qwen3 4B-14B, gpt-oss-20b/120b, Gemma3 4B-12B, IBM Granite 4.0 1B-7B)</p>\n<p><strong>Hardware:</strong> 3 local accelerators from 2023-2025. To establish upper bounds on intelligence-per-watt for local LMs, we benchmark the same models on 5 enterprise-grade accelerators (2023-2025) including Nvidia, AMD and Sambanova.</p>\n<p><strong>Workloads:</strong> 1M queries spanning three complementary data sources: (1) real-world ChatGPT queries (WildChat) spanning mathematics, code, and other domains that match Claude.ai's usage patterns (5 of 6 top occupational categories); (2) general reasoning tasks (Natural Reasoning); and (3) standardized benchmarks measuring knowledge breadth (MMLU-Pro) and expert-level reasoning (SuperGPQA). Our study focuses on a subset of LLM workloads: single-turn mainstream chat queries that represent a substantial portion of frontier model traffic (Deng et. al). We don't perform evaluations on agentic tool use (e.g., TauBench, TerminalBench), long-horizon planning, or long-document processing (e.g., FinanceBench) where local LMs are known to lag frontier LM performance by up to 45 percentage points. We leave this as future work.</p>\n<p><strong>Evaluation:</strong> We measure accuracy, energy consumption, latency, and compute under single-query inference conditions (batch size = 1), the <a href=\"https://developers.redhat.com/articles/2025/09/30/vllm-or-llamacpp-choosing-right-llm-inference-engine-your-use-case#conclusion__the_right_tool_for_the_job\" target=\"_blank\">standard deployment setting for local inference</a> benchmarking (Hao et al.). We leave evaluations of larger batch sizes to future work.</p>\n<h3>Measurement Methodology</h3>\n<p><strong>Energy Measurement</strong>: We follow standard energy measurement practices (Samsi et al., Fernandez et al., Wilkins et al.): querying NVML's energy counter for NVIDIA GPUs and using the <em>powermetrics</em> utility with power integration over time for Apple Silicon. However, software-based power measurements can introduce inaccuracies of 10-15%, with variations distributed across different hardware components due to architectural differences in workloads between CPUs, GPUs, and NPUs (Yang et al. 2023). Even hardware wattage meters may fall short for milliwatt-level precision, though our approach aligns with established practices and provides consistent relative comparisons across configurations. Compared to previous methodologies, we differ in two key aspects: (1) we sample at 50ms intervals for higher temporal resolution (compared to 100ms (Samsi et al.) or 15 seconds (Fernandez et al.)), and (2) for multi-GPU configurations, we aggregate energy counters from each GPU individually rather than extrapolating from a single device (Samsi et al.).</p>\n<p><strong>Accuracy Measurement</strong>: For chat queries where there is no ground truth answer we measure accuracy using LLM-as-a-judge evaluation established by Zheng et al. (2023) computing win/tie rates over frontier LMs (GPT-5 and Gemini-2.5-Pro). For reasoning and knowledge tasks where a ground truth answer exists, we use exact match to determine correctness.</p>\n<h2>🚀  Findings: Local AI is viable and improving</h2>\n<figure style=\"text-align:left;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-11-ipw/figure_4.png\" style=\"width:100%\">Figure #4: <strong>Rapid Improvement of Local LMs across Chat and Reasoning Queries</strong></figure>\n<figure style=\"text-align:left;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-11-ipw/figure_5.png\" style=\"width:100%\">Figure #5: <strong>Local LMs approach Frontier Cloud LM Performances.</strong> When considering the best-of-local ensemble (routing to the best local model for each query), and the best-of-cloud baseline (routing to the best frontier model), we see that local inference outperforms cloud inference on 3/4 tasks. For our Cloud LMs, we consider Gemini 2.5 Pro (the current SOTA LM on LMArena), Claude 4.5 Sonnet, and GPT-5.</figure>\n<p>Our study reveals three key findings:</p>\n<ol>\n<li><em><strong>Local LMs are capable and improving rapidly</strong>:</em> Local LMs accurately respond to 88.7% of the collected single-turn chat and reasoning queries with accuracy improving 3.1× from 2023-2025.  When considering the best-of-local ensemble (routing to the best local model for each query), local routing surpasses cloud routing on three of four benchmarks (see Figure 4).</li>\n<li><em><strong>Local accelerator efficiency has room for improvement</strong></em>: Local IPW can be improved by at least 1.5x to match enterprise-grade accelerators. We benchmarked IPW of Qwen3-32B across 5 enterprise accelerators (2023-2025) and found that inference on the M4 Max (local) achieved 1.5x lower IPW compared to the B200 (enterprise), revealing significant efficiency headroom in local accelerators.  </li>\n<li><strong>Local Intelligence efficiency is improving 5.3x over two years:</strong> IPW is improving at 2.3x year-over-year, driven by 3.1x from model innovations (better architecture, pretraining, post-training, and distillation techniques) and 1.7x from hardware advances (see Figure 5).</li>\n</ol>\n<figure style=\"text-align:left;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-11-ipw/figure_6.png\" style=\"width:100%\">Figure #6: <strong>Increase in Intelligence per Watt for Local LMs and Accelerators</strong>: Accuracy per watt has improved over 5.3x in two years by selecting the optimal local LM-accelerator pair at each time point. Improvements are driven by advances in both model architectures (from Mixtral-8x7B to gpt-oss-120b) and accelerator hardware (NVIDIA Quadro RTX 6000 to Apple M4 Max). Evaluated on chat queries and reasoning queries.</figure>\n<p>These findings demonstrate that just as computing shifted from time-shared mainframes to personal computers, LLM inference can shift from centralized clouds to local devices—with smaller, efficient models handling a growing share of queries. Prioritizing IPW in both model design and hardware acceleration will expand the frontier of viable local inference.</p>\n<h2>✅ Limitations and Future Work</h2>\n<p>Our study is a first step towards understanding the viability of offloading portions of frontier LM traffic to local compute. We see several important directions for future work:</p>\n<ul>\n<li><strong>Task Coverage</strong>: Our study focuses on mainstream chat and reasoning queries. It does not yet include complex or specialized workloads such as long-horizon agentic tasks (e.g., tool use, web navigation) or enterprise use cases (i.e., customer support management). Future iterations will expand the study to account for a full range of LLM deployment scenarios including tasks such as Snorkel Finance and Snorkel Underwriting.</li>\n<li><strong>Hardware Diversity:</strong> While we profile a large subset of hardware backends, we aim to extend support to a broader range of local accelerators — including more NPUs, desktop laptops (Nvidia DGX Spark), and cell phones.</li>\n<li><strong>Intelligence Efficiency in the Cloud:</strong>  Our work focuses on local inference efficiency. A natural extension is measuring intelligence-per-watt for cloud-scale deployments.</li>\n<li><strong>Systems-Level Inference Optimizations</strong>: We evaluate the local model and accelerator as integrated components in a single-query setting. Future work will expand IPW to isolate the contribution of individual sub-components of models and accelerators respectively on intelligence efficiency:\n<ul>\n<li><strong>Local LM</strong> —> Architecture, Pretraining, Post-training, Distillation, etc.</li>\n<li><strong>Local Accelerator</strong> —> Systems Architecture, Inference Kernels, Quantization Efficiency, Attention Variations, etc.</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n</ul>\n<h2>🚀 Towards Intelligence Efficiency</h2>\n<p>We're witnessing a fundamental shift from centralized \"mainframe era\" inference to distributed \"PC era\" inference. Local LMs running on local accelerators can accurately answer 88.7% of single-turn chat and reasoning queries—a meaningful subset of total LLM workloads. Intelligence efficiency has improved by 5.3x over the past two years, with local LMs achieving 3.1× accuracy gains and local accelerator efficiency improving 1.7× with substantial headroom for optimization.</p>\n<p>The path forward is clear: intelligence per watt should be one of the north star metrics for both model architecture and hardware accelerator design. Just as performance-per-watt guided the mainframe-to-PC transition, intelligence-per-watt will guide AI's transition to the edge—expanding the frontier of queries we can redistribute from data centers to local devices.</p>\n<p>To accelerate this transition, we release a hardware-agnostic profiling harness (Nvidia, AMD, Apple Silicon) for measuring intelligence efficiency across model-accelerator configurations. By establishing a standardized measurement framework, developers can systematically optimize models and hardware vendors can hill-climb on intelligence efficiency rather than raw performance alone.</p>\n<ul>\n<li>If you are a <strong>hardware developer</strong>: we’d love to collaborate in extending our profiling harness to support your new hardware accelerators!</li>\n<li>If you are a <strong>model developer</strong>: we’d love to study your new models and understand whether they improve intelligence-per-watt locally!</li>\n<li>If you are a <strong>curious human</strong>: please join our mission to improve intelligence efficiency!</li>\n</ul>\n<h2>🔗 Additional Links</h2>\n<blockquote>\n<p>📄<a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2511.07885\" target=\"_blank\">Paper</a><br>\n💻<a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/intelligence-per-watt\" target=\"_blank\">GitHub</a></p>\n</blockquote>\n<h1>Acknowledgements</h1>\n<p>We are thankful to the SambaNova, Ollama, SnorkelAI, and IBM for their support. We’re also grateful to Alex Atallah from OpenRouter, Sharon Zhou from AMD, and TogetherAI for generously providing compute resources and credits. Lastly, thanks to the many folks who have provided invaluable feedback on this work - Simran Arora, Kush Bhatia, Bradley Brown, Rishi Bommasani, Mayee Chen, Owen Dugan, Sabri Eyuboglu, Neel Guha, Simon Guo, Braden Hancock, Jordan Juravsky, Omar Khattab, Andy Konwinski, Jerry Liu, Kunle Olukotun, Christopher Rytting, Benjamin Spector, Shayan Talaei, and Dan Zhang.</p>\n<h3>Citations</h3>\n<p>Chatterji, A., Cunningham, T., Deming, D. J., Hitzig, Z., Ong, C., Shan, C. Y., &#x26; Wadman, K. (2025). <em>How people use ChatGPT</em> (NBER Working Paper No. 34255). Early ChatGPT user portrait through the lens of data. In <em>2023 IEEE International Conference on Big Data (BigData)</em> (pp. 5581-5583). L., Sheng, Y., Zhuang, S., Wu, Z., Zhuang, Y., Lin, Z., Li, Z., Li, D., Xing, E., Zhang, H., Gonzalez, J. E., &#x26; Stoica, I. (2023). Judging LLM-as-a-judge with MT-bench and chatbot arena.
