While data centers dominate AI…,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-11-ipw,Jon Saad-Falcon*, Avanika Narayan*, John Hennessy, Azalia Mirhoseini, Chris Ré,2026-07-22T18:37:38.417Z,2025-11-11T07:00:00.658Z,[{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-11-ipw/tldr_figure.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":2580,\"height\":2012,\"blurhash\":\"L8SF-C8_00D%-VxtbHj]DixZ%MtR\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-11-ipw/figure_1.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":4124,\"height\":2634,\"blurhash\":\"L6SY~z9FRjM{9EROtSxu00M|xu-p\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-11-ipw/figure_2.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":4134,\"height\":2634,\"blurhash\":\"L1S$ow^%V=4o~p-hD-M{x7~q9K?Z\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-11-ipw/figure_3.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1088,\"height\":801,\"blurhash\":\"L7S6Pl-;_3I:slj[ogx]~qRikXj]\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-11-ipw/figure_4.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":7169,\"height\":2970,\"blurhash\":\"L2Ss50[mNy0h~VMcNeS%MxAIxv~W\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-11-ipw/figure_5.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":5916,\"height\":2316,\"blurhash\":\"LLPjMt^+-oS6~Wt6M{Rj_3M|M|s.\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-11-ipw/figure_6.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":2316,\"height\":1716,\"blurhash\":\"LRQJiz~pIVxt-;t7IUoe?HE1xuWC\"}],163778663386687488,1211296081845936138,HipKittens: Fast and Furious AMD Kernels,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-09-hk,<p><strong>Team</strong>: William Hu, Drew Wadsworth, Sean Siddens, Stanley Winata, Daniel Fu, Ryan Swann, Muhammad Osama, Christopher Ré, Simran Arora<br>\n<strong>Links</strong>: <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2511.08083\" target=\"_blank\">Arxiv</a> | <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/HipKittens\" target=\"_blank\">Code</a></p>\n<p><strong>AI is gated by hardware. We think that opening up AI’s compute landscape is one of the most important problems to be working on right now.</strong> Building towards this goal, we present HipKittens: SoTA AMD kernels and a collection of opinionated programming primitives to make AMD kernel dev easier!</p>\n<p><em>Named after AMD's CUDA equivalent, called <a href=\"https://www.google.com/url?q=https://rocm.docs.amd.com/projects/HIP/en/docs-develop/what_is_hip.html&#x26;sa=D&#x26;source=docs&#x26;ust=1762797198251657&#x26;usg=AOvVaw0nJDptNvO-LI1BHu_fKZMT\" target=\"_blank\">HIP</a>.</em></p>\n<h3>Building towards multi-silicon AI systems</h3>\n<p>While AI has largely <a href=\"https://hc2023.hotchips.org/assets/program/conference/day2/Keynote%202/Keynote-NVIDIA_Hardware-for-Deep-Learning.pdf\" target=\"_blank\">used a single hardware vendor</a> to get to its current stage, AMD GPU hardware now offers state-of-the-art peak compute and memory bandwidth. However, this performance is locked away from AI workflows due to the lack of mature AMD software.</p>\n<table><thead><tr><th>Spec</th><th>NVIDIA B200 SXM5</th><th>AMD MI355X OAM</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td>BF16 matrix / tensor</td><td>2.2 PFLOPs</td><td>2.5 PFLOPs</td></tr><tr><td>MXFP8 matrix / tensor</td><td>4.5 PFLOPs</td><td>5.0 PFLOPs</td></tr><tr><td>MXFP6 matrix / tensor</td><td>4.5 PFLOPs</td><td>10.1 PFLOPs</td></tr><tr><td>MXFP4 matrix / tensor</td><td>9.0 PFLOPs</td><td>10.1 PFLOPs</td></tr><tr><td>Memory capacity</td><td>180 GB</td><td>288 GB</td></tr><tr><td>Memory bandwidth</td><td>8.0 TB/s</td><td>8.0 TB/s</td></tr></tbody></table>\n<p>Table 1: <strong>Hardware overview.</strong> Peak memory and compute speeds for the latest generation GPU platforms.</p>\n<p>The AMD software ecosystem includes <a href=\"https://github.com/ROCm/aiter\" target=\"_blank\">AITER</a>, a high performance AI kernel library; PyTorch and a few compilers (<a href=\"https://github.com/ROCm/triton\" target=\"_blank\">Triton</a>, <a href=\"https://github.com/modular/modular\" target=\"_blank\">Mojo</a>, <a href=\"https://github.com/tile-ai/tilelang\" target=\"_blank\">TileLang</a>); and <a href=\"https://github.com/ROCm/composable_kernel\" target=\"_blank\">Composable Kernel (CK)</a>, AMD's C++ based programming model for writing kernels. However, despite <a href=\"https://openai.com/index/openai-amd-strategic-partnership/\" target=\"_blank\">gigawatt-scale AMD deployments</a>, the software remains brittle.</p>\n<ol>\n<li><strong>The existing software offerings fail to consistently achieve peak performance.</strong> CK kernels frequently underperform (see our evaluations below). AITER and PyTorch are volatile; for instance, AITER and PyTorch SDPA Llama GQA backwards kernels achieve just 30% and 24% of SoTA performance respectively on AMD MI355X GPUs. And the compilers currently significantly sacrifice performance and have not yet demonstrated reusable programming primitives for AMD. Further, we find that some critical aspects of hardware functionality around bank conflict avoidance are undocumented in the <a href=\"https://www.amd.com/content/dam/amd/en/documents/instinct-tech-docs/instruction-set-architectures/amd-instinct-cdna4-instruction-set-architecture.pdf\" target=\"_blank\">CDNA ISA</a>, discussed in <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-09-amd-brr\" target=\"_blank\">our technical deep dive blog</a>.</li>\n</ol>\n<details><summary>Details: expand to learn more about our current compiler observations</summary><p>We expand on a few observations about current compilers:</p><ul>\n<li>\n<p>Mojo's <a href=\"https://github.com/modular/modular/tree/main/max/kernels/benchmarks/gpu\" target=\"_blank\">MHA forwards kernel suffers from bank conflicts</a> and offers just 50% of peak performance on the MI355X (e.g., 430 TFLOPs at B=16, H=16, N=2048, D=128).</p>\n</li>\n<li>\n<p>TileLang <a href=\"https://openreview.net/pdf?id=Jb1WkNSfUB\" target=\"_blank\">is currently limited to CDNA3</a> and the MHA kernel <a href=\"https://openreview.net/pdf?id=Jb1WkNSfUB\" target=\"_blank\">“is competitive with PyTorch”</a>, which is amongst the slowest baselines (see results below). TileLang lacks many features that we believe are important for AMD kernels--multiple <a href=\"https://github.com/tile-ai/tilelang/blob/85218bd97309deeff5a7ad5dc1f111d5366244d9/src/tl_templates/hip/gemm.h#L12\" target=\"_blank\">matrix core shapes like 32x32x16</a>, <a href=\"https://github.com/tile-ai/tilelang/blob/85218bd97309deeff5a7ad5dc1f111d5366244d9/src/tl_templates/hip/copy.h#L69\" target=\"_blank\">buffer_load_dwordx4</a>, XCD chiplet swizzling--and also has <a href=\"https://github.com/tile-ai/tilelang/blob/85218bd97309deeff5a7ad5dc1f111d5366244d9/src/tl_templates/hip/reduce.h#L15\" target=\"_blank\">dependencies on Composable Kernel</a>, adding complexity to the library.</p>\n</li>\n<li>\n<p>On AMD, Triton struggles with register lifetime tracking and lowering memory accesses to the most performant intrinsics. For example, it may fail to <a href=\"https://github.com/ROCm/tritonBLAS/blob/98c7d7d43ce831b30240389687f0417ff4b8fcc4/include/tritonblas/internal/streamk_matmul.py#L206-L265\" target=\"_blank\">reclaim registers</a> or <a href=\"https://github.com/ROCm/iris/blob/ec2dd919536ff56ce731020ac879a32430983291/iris/iris.py#L1456-L1462\" target=\"_blank\">lower vectorized loads</a>. In our evaluations, we find that Triton kernels from AMD’s <a href=\"https://github.com/ROCm/triton\" target=\"_blank\">ROCm/Triton</a> tend to underperform (even on a vanilla BF16 GEMM).</p>\n</li>\n</ul><p>While the Pythonic interface and portability of such compilers is helpful, our goal is to identify the principles that lead to peak performance as well.</p></details>\n<ol start=\"2\">\n<li><strong>As a result, AMD's most performant AI kernels need to be hand-optimized by experts <strong>in raw assembly</strong>.</strong> It is very difficult to scale to the breadth of AI workloads and as a result, most widely used AI workloads are unsupported/under-optimized on AMD (e.g., we see this on some attention problem shapes, non-causal gqa backwards pass, memory bound kernels).</li>\n</ol>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:5px\"><img style=\"width:60%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-hk/asm.png\"><p>Figure: what is raw assembly? can't understand it? that's the point!</p></figure>\n<p><strong>With all of this, it remains up in the air what the best path forwards is for multi-silicon kernel development!</strong></p>\n<p>As a result, the AI community says that there’s a CUDA moat in AI software: <a href=\"https://x.com/jxmnop/status/1887898002188103796\" target=\"_blank\">tweet #1</a>, <a href=\"https://x.com/SemiAnalysis_/status/1960070677379133949\" target=\"_blank\">tweet #2</a>, <a href=\"https://www.linkedin.com/posts/appenz_flash-attention-4-was-announced-today-at-activity-7365856788727939072-QI5B\" target=\"_blank\">tweet #3</a> and <a href=\"https://x.com/search?q=cuda%20moat&#x26;src=typed_query\" target=\"_blank\">many more</a>.</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:5px\"><img style=\"width:80%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-hk/moat.png\"></figure>\n<p>But that being said, developing performant NVIDIA kernels was also painstakingly tedious a few years ago. Using low level CUDA/CUTLASS, it took two years between the H100 GPU’s release and the release of <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.08608\" target=\"_blank\">peak performance open-source attention kernels</a>.  Compilers and <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.10517\" target=\"_blank\">LLMs-for-kernel-development</a> on NVIDIA have so far sacrificed performance for simplicity and <a href=\"https://x.com/difficultyang/status/1968027448622379387\" target=\"_blank\">struggled to quickly support new hardware features</a>.</p>\n<p><strong>Opinionated primitives are simplifying the process of writing performant NVIDIA kernels!</strong>  Amazingly through the community’s effort on all these DSLs and AI assistants, NVIDIA kernel development is starting to get easier! Last year, we shared our opinionated take on kernel DSLs — keep the familiar PyTorch feel, but make the primitives C++ embedded to get peak performance, simplicity and extensibility to hardware platforms and AI workloads. In May 2024, we shared <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-05-12-quick-tk\" target=\"_blank\">ThunderKittens (TK)</a>, and have been excited to see its ideas used in a wave of frameworks this year like <a href=\"https://docs.nvidia.com/cutlass/media/docs/pythonDSL/cute_dsl.html\" target=\"_blank\">CuTe DSL in Sept 2025</a>, <a href=\"https://x.com/__tinygrad__/status/1976084605141909845\" target=\"_blank\">Tiny Grad “tinykittens”</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2504.17577\" target=\"_blank\">TileLang in April 2025</a> and <a href=\"https://github.com/triton-lang/triton/tree/main/python/tutorials/gluon\" target=\"_blank\">Gluon in June 2025</a>. It’s been fun to see TK used in companies like <a href=\"https://www.together.ai/blog/thunderkittens\" target=\"_blank\">Together AI</a>, Jump Trading, and <a href=\"https://cursor.com/en-US/blog/kernels\" target=\"_blank\">Cursor</a> and in academic research.</p>\n<p><strong>So then we were curious whether entirely new programming primitives are needed to simplify AMD kernel development, or whether existing primitives suffice.</strong> It wasn't obvious to us where this exploration would end up; <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.08608\" target=\"_blank\">most</a> <a href=\"https://openreview.net/pdf?id=fGgQS5VW09\" target=\"_blank\">modern</a> <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2412.19437\" target=\"_blank\">kernels</a> are designed around NVIDIA-specific hardware features. AMD hardware differs meaningfully (no <em>wgmma/tcgen05</em> pipelined async matmuls, no <em>tma</em>, no <em>mbarriers</em>, no register reallocation, smaller shared memory, chiplet instead of monolithic, etc.) and we weren't sure where performance would end up nor how different the primitives might look compared to NVIDIA frameworks.</p>\n<p>Our exploration resulted in HipKittens, a minimal, opinionated collection of C++ embedded programming primitives for fast AMD kernels. We find:</p>\n<ol>\n<li><strong>The tile abstraction generalizes across architectures.</strong> The core tile-based primitives we identified as effective on NVIDIA GPUs—including tile types, PyTorch-like bulk compute operators over tiles, and composable load/store interfaces—translate naturally to AMD.</li>\n<li><strong>Backend implementations are architecture-specific.</strong> The underlying memory access patterns (e.g., swizzling schemes, register scheduling) that realize the tile interface differ between AMD and NVIDIA due to hardware differences.</li>\n<li><strong>Scheduling strategies adapt to hardware constraints.</strong> The scheduling patterns both within a processor and across processors differ on AMD compared to NVIDIA, reflecting fundamental architectural distinctions. Wave specialization underperforms on CDNA3 and CDNA4. However, reasoning about schedules at the tile granularity—rather than at the level of individual registers or memory transactions—continues to simplify development, maintain code readability, and enable peak performance.</li>\n</ol>\n<p>Ultimately, we see that tile-based abstractions remain general across architectures, providing evidence that a unified, performant programming model for AI accelerators is achievable. The key insight is separating the interface (tiles and operations on tiles) from the implementation (how tiles map to hardware), allowing the same high-level programming model to target diverse GPU architectures.</p>\n<h3>Climbing out of the CUDA moat: Introducing HipKittens</h3>\n<p>We first explored <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/ThunderKittens\" target=\"_blank\">ThunderKittens for NVIDIA</a>, then <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-11-28-tk-mlx\" target=\"_blank\">ThunderMittens on Apple Silicon</a> and now we’re excited to share <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/HipKittens/tree/main\" target=\"_blank\">HipKittens (HK) for AMD</a>!</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:5px\"><img style=\"width:50%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-hk/cinematic.png\"><p>Figure: The Kittens cinematic universe! Towards multi-silicon AI!</p></figure>\n<p>HK kernels are performant, while remaining easy to read and modify! We might not need raw assembly for peak performance AMD kernels any more! Life is good!</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:5px\"><img style=\"width:60%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-hk/hk_wave.png\"><p>Figure: HipKittens riding the ~wave~ (not warp).</p></figure>\n<p>Let's go through the results:</p>\n<ol>\n<li>Our attention forwards kernels are written in <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/HipKittens/blob/ec9fd64195d10bf5fa06d2170fd5c68a457a77ab/kernels/attn/gqa/kernel.cpp#L102\" target=\"_blank\">~500 lines of code</a> and outperform all of AMDs baselines on average, including the AITER kernels which are written in hand-optimized assembly! We show different head dimensions (<span><span><span><math>DD</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">D</span></span></span></span></span>) and sequence lengths <span><span><span><math>NN</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span></span></span></span></span>, for both causal and non-causal settings.</li>\n</ol>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:5px\"><img style=\"width:49%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-hk/mi355x_gqa_causal_fwd_attn_plot.png\"><img style=\"width:49%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-hk/mi355x_d64_gqa_causal_fwd_attn_d64_plot.png\"><img style=\"width:49%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-hk/mi355x_gqa_non_causal_fwd_attn_plot.png\"><img style=\"width:49%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-hk/mi355x_d64_gqa_non_causal_fwd_attn_d64_plot.png\"></figure>\n<ol start=\"2\">\n<li>Our GEMM kernel features a <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/HipKittens/blob/ec9fd64195d10bf5fa06d2170fd5c68a457a77ab/kernels/gemm/bf16fp32/mi350x/256_256_64_32_with16x32.cpp#L113\" target=\"_blank\">hot loop</a> that's <span><span><span><math>&#x3C;100&#x3C;100</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.5782em;vertical-align:-0.0391em\"></span><span>&#x3C;</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>100</span></span></span></span></span> lines of code and achieves peak performance. Again, AITER and HipBLASLT kernels are programmed in ... raw assembly!</li>\n</ol>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:5px\"><img style=\"width:49%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-hk/mi355x_bf16_gemm_plot.png\"><img style=\"width:49%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-hk/mi355x_fp8_gemm_plot.png\"></figure>\n<ol start=\"3\">\n<li>We also get speedy attention backwards pass, rotary, and fused dropout-residual-layernorm kernels compared to the strongest available baselines! These results use head dimension <span><span><span><math>128128</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>128</span></span></span></span></span> and we vary the sequence length.</li>\n</ol>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:5px\"><img style=\"width:49%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-hk/mi355x_gqa_non_causal_bkwd_plot.png\"><img style=\"width:49%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-hk/mi355x_gqa_causal_bkwd_plot.png\"><img style=\"width:49%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-hk/mi355x_norm_plot.png\"><img style=\"width:49%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-hk/mi355x_rotary_plot.png\"></figure>\n<h3>Multi-silicon AI is coming!</h3>\n<p>Realizing AI's full potential requires diverse, open hardware.<sup id=\"user-content-fnref-1\"><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-09-hk#fn-1\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">1</a></sup> Today, that means making AMD GPUs truly accessible.</p>\n<p>We want more AI in the world. AI has relied on and innovated on a single hardware provider, but we need to be able to use and experiment with all the kinds of compute we can. We need to be able to use the fastest hardware out there. We’re happy to help address these problems with HipKittens!</p>\n<p>Checkout <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-09-amd-brr\" target=\"_blank\">part two</a> for a technical deep dive on HK.</p>\n<p><strong>Links</strong>: <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2511.08083\" target=\"_blank\">Arxiv</a> | <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/HipKittens\" target=\"_blank\">Code</a></p>\n<div>\n<hr>\n<ol>\n<li id=\"user-content-fn-1\">Alongside offering peak hardware capabilities, AMD's software stack is also open-source!<a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-09-hk#fnref-1\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">↩</a></li>\n</ol>\n</div>,Team: William Hu, Drew Wadsworth, Sean Siddens, Stanley Winata, Daniel Fu, Ryan Swann, Muhammad Osama, Christopher Ré, Simran Arora Links: A…,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-09-hk,<a href=\"https://willhu-jpg.github.io/\">William Hu</a>, <a href=\"https://www.linkedin.com/in/wadsworthstephen\">Drew Wadsworth</a>, <a href=\"https://cs.stanford.edu/people/chrismre/\">Chris Ré</a>, <a href=\"https://arorasimran.com/\">Simran Arora</a>,2026-07-22T18:37:38.416Z,2025-11-11T07:00:00.466Z,[{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-hk/asm.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1076,\"height\":464,\"blurhash\":\"L69tJv~qt8RjV@M{t7t7NG%MR*Rj\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-hk/moat.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":2466,\"height\":632,\"blurhash\":\"L7RMe=IU-=M{Rjt7Rjj[_4WBayay\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-hk/cinematic.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1482,\"height\":1420,\"blurhash\":\"LFEe[JEO0hM{}RxY9at7ROn$V[t7\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-hk/hk_wave.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1668,\"height\":1116,\"blurhash\":\"LRN^VeIU~qay%MM{t7t7?bt7ofxa\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-hk/mi355x_gqa_causal_fwd_attn_plot.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":2975,\"height\":1786,\"blurhash\":\"LPQ9+g_3%goI?^RiIUWB?vITV[oz\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-hk/mi355x_d64_gqa_causal_fwd_attn_d64_plot.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":2974,\"height\":1786,\"blurhash\":\"LLQ,2a?byDxZ?^RjMxV[?vIUadkC\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-hk/mi355x_gqa_non_causal_fwd_attn_plot.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":2975,\"height\":1786,\"blurhash\":\"LVPP_v-;.8xa.SWBM_WB~qM{M{WX\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-hk/mi355x_d64_gqa_non_causal_fwd_attn_d64_plot.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":2974,\"height\":1786,\"blurhash\":\"LUQJG-%M%gxu%#WBRjWB_NRQRPWB\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-hk/mi355x_bf16_gemm_plot.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":2975,\"height\":1786,\"blurhash\":\"LQQm0K~qx^IA?cRPIUjs-=D%jF%M\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-hk/mi355x_fp8_gemm_plot.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":2976,\"height\":1780,\"blurhash\":\"LKRV@A_4o~RP%hWBMxV@%NIAt6x]\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-hk/mi355x_gqa_non_causal_bkwd_plot.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":2974,\"height\":1786,\"blurhash\":\"LJRMVm-p%Mxt?^RPMxWB$4RjkCbH\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-hk/mi355x_gqa_causal_bkwd_plot.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":2974,\"height\":1786,\"blurhash\":\"LFRV;4?bx^xa_NM|MxRk-;M{n$kB\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-hk/mi355x_norm_plot.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":2975,\"height\":1786,\"blurhash\":\"LSPjc6%2-qxa_4WBRjWV^kNGWoWV\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-hk/mi355x_rotary_plot.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":2975,\"height\":1786,\"blurhash\":\"LKQ0joxa-;-:~qM{M{a{~CR*R*M|\"}],163778663386687488,1211296081845936139,AMD GPUs go brrr,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-09-amd-brr,<p><strong>Team</strong>: William Hu, Drew Wadsworth, Sean Siddens, Stanley Winata, Daniel Fu, Ryan Swann, Muhammad Osama, Christopher Ré, Simran Arora<br>\n<strong>Links</strong>: <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2511.08083\" target=\"_blank\">Arxiv</a> | <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/HipKittens\" target=\"_blank\">Code</a></p>\n<p>AI is <a href=\"https://openai.com/index/five-new-stargate-sites/\" target=\"_blank\">compute</a> <a href=\"https://www.bloomberg.com/news/newsletters/2025-08-24/why-is-manhattan-being-crushed-by-this-giant-meta-data-center\" target=\"_blank\">hungry</a>. So <a href=\"https://www.together.ai/blog/based\" target=\"_blank\">we've</a> <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-05-12-tk\" target=\"_blank\">been</a> <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2023-12-11-truly-subquadratic\" target=\"_blank\">asking</a>: How do we build AI from the hardware up? How do we lead AI developers to do what the hardware prefers?</p>\n<p>AMD GPUs are now offering state-of-the-art speeds and feeds. However, this performance is locked away from AI workflows due to the <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-09-hk\" target=\"_blank\">lack of mature AMD software</a>. We share HipKittens, an opinionated collection of programming primitives to help developers realize the hardware's capabilities: optimized register tiles, 8-wave and 4-wave kernel patterns instead of wave-specialization to schedule work within processors, and chiplet-optimized cache reuse patterns to schedule work across processors.</p>\n<p>Checkout <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-09-hk\" target=\"_blank\">part one of this series</a> for an intro to HipKittens and checkout this post for a technical deep dive.</p>\n<h3>What do AMD CDNA GPUs look like? A lay of the land.</h3>\n<p>An AMD MI355X GPU has 256 processors called “compute units” (CUs) and a CU contains four SIMDs. A SIMD has different execution units. A 64-thread “wave” (contrasting a 32-thread warp on NVIDIA) occupies a single SIMD. We show the MI355X memory hierarchy below.</p>\n<p>Unsurprisingly, making AMD GPUs go brr boils down to keeping the “matrix cores” (tensor cores on NVIDIA) fed. There are a few differences in how we think about this hardware:</p>\n<ol>\n<li><strong>What it's not.</strong> An MI355X has 70% the SRAM of a B200 (165KB instead of 228KB), lacks asynchronous matrix multiplication instructions that operate on inputs in shared or tensor memory (wgmma, tcgen05), lacks register reallocation (the ability for some waves to give their registers to others), lacks tensor memory acceleration (dedicated hardware for global memory access), and lacks first class mbarrier primitives (for fine-grained synchronization).</li>\n<li><strong>What it is.</strong> On the other hand, AMD GPUs have a 2x larger register file per processor than the B200 and offers 60% more processors per GPU (256 compute units versus 160 streaming multiprocessors). AMD offers tiny and fine-grained matrix core instructions, while NVIDIA tensor cores instructions are generally called with large input operands. AMD has a TMA-like direct global to shared memory loads via <span><span><span><math>buffer_load_dword\\verb|buffer_load_dword|</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7063em;vertical-align:-0.0951em\"></span><span><span>buffer_load_dword</span></span></span></span></span></span> instructions, which bypass the register file.</li>\n<li><strong>Towards chiplet architectures.</strong> AMD is also leading the charge in the shift from monolithic grids to chiplets. AMD splits the 256 processors into 8 chiplets called “XCDs” of 32 CUs. NVIDIA B200s include 2 chips. The AMD cache is disaggregated: an AMD XCD has a private L2 cache and there is an extra last level cache (LLC) that sits between the L2 and HBM memory.</li>\n</ol>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:5px\"><img style=\"width:49%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-amd-brr/memory.png\"><img style=\"width:50%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-amd-brr/chip.png\"></figure>\n<table><thead><tr><th>Spec</th><th>NVIDIA B200 SXM5</th><th>AMD MI355X OAM</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td>BF16 matrix / tensor</td><td>2.2 PFLOPs</td><td>2.5 PFLOPs</td></tr><tr><td>MXFP8 matrix / tensor</td><td>4.5 PFLOPs</td><td>5.0 PFLOPs</td></tr><tr><td>MXFP6 matrix / tensor</td><td>4.5 PFLOPs</td><td>10.1 PFLOPs</td></tr><tr><td>MXFP4 matrix / tensor</td><td>9.0 PFLOPs</td><td>10.1 PFLOPs</td></tr><tr><td>Memory capacity</td><td>180 GB</td><td>288 GB</td></tr><tr><td>Memory bandwidth</td><td>8.0 TB/s</td><td>8.0 TB/s</td></tr></tbody></table>\n<p>Table 1: <strong>Hardware overview.</strong> Peak memory and compute speeds for the latest generation GPU platforms.</p>\n<p>These differences impact the ways in which we design kernels on AMD.</p>\n<ol>\n<li><strong>Optimized memory access:</strong> Of course this matters on NVIDIA, but AMD’s layouts, HIPCC compiler limitations, and (undocumented) quirky behaviors of different I/O instructions yields new challenges.</li>\n<li><strong>Scheduling within processors:</strong> We need to rely on our register file and small matrix core instructions instead of shared memory and bulky wgmma/tcgen05 instructions to establish deep pipelines and hide memory costs. Wave specialization / producer consumer, which reigns supreme in NVIDIA kernels, is not the right answer on AMD.</li>\n<li><strong>Scheduling across processors:</strong> We need to start thinking about NUMA effects at the cache level as we schedule work across thread blocks.</li>\n</ol>\n<p>We walk through these three topics next.</p>\n<h3>HipKittens memory access patterns</h3>\n<p>As in <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-05-12-tk\" target=\"_blank\">ThunderKittens</a>, in HK, developers program using tiles as the basic data structure. Tiles exist in shared or register memory, and are parametrized by a data type, size dimensions (a multiple of the matrix core instruction shape), and layout. HK provides a library of PyTorch-like functions that operate on tiles, for instance <span><span><span><math>exp\\verb|exp|</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6528em;vertical-align:-0.2222em\"></span><span><span>exp</span></span></span></span></span></span>, <span><span><span><math>mma\\verb|mma|</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span><span>mma</span></span></span></span></span></span>, <span><span><span><math>sub\\verb|sub|</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6111em\"></span><span><span>sub</span></span></span></span></span></span>, <span><span><span><math>add\\verb|add|</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6111em\"></span><span><span>add</span></span></span></span></span></span>, <span><span><span><math>row_max\\verb|row_max|</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.5257em;vertical-align:-0.0951em\"></span><span><span>row_max</span></span></span></span></span></span> compute ops and load, store memory ops. A tile is collectively owned by threads in a wave (warp). The functions use template metaprogramming to generalize to different input tiles and are lightweight, directly wrapping assembly (PTX, CDNA ISA) and C++.</p>\n<p>A memory layout determines how data elements map to thread ownership. A matrix core instruction expects a particular register layout depending on the data type and instruction shape. We also want to maximize the granularity and coalescing of global memory loads. Between registers and HBM, shared memory is split into banks (4-byte regions) that can serve data simultaneously. If threads from a wave request data from the same bank at the same time, their accesses are serialized; efficient kernels use “swizzle patterns” to organize data in a way that avoids these bank conflicts.</p>\n<p>A few challenges for memory access in HK include:</p>\n<ul>\n<li><strong>Register scheduling:</strong> A core tenant of HK and TK is to give developers full control over register allocation by remaining C++ embedded. Compilers like Triton prevent register management altogether, but surprisingly we find that even the HIPCC compiler imposes severe limitations (no wonder AMD uses raw assembly!).<sup id=\"user-content-fnref-1\"><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-09-amd-brr#fn-1\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">1</a></sup> For instance, 4-wave (1-wave per SIMD) kernels compiled via HIPCC cannot use data held in certain types of registers as inputs to matrix instructions. This motivated us to add explicit register scheduling to HK, where developers pin specific registers when creating registers tiles, effectively replacing the compiler’s register management capabilities. Developers thus have the control necessary to write peak performance kernels!</li>\n</ul>\n<details><summary>Learn more about explicit register scheduling</summary><p>When a single wave is mapped per SIMD, the 512 registers are actually divided into 256 accumulator general purpose registers (AGPRs) and 256 vector general purpose registers (VGPRs). AGPRs have fundamental hardware limitations (e.g., vector arithmetic instructions cannot operate on them), but they can still crucially serve as the input or outputs for MFMA instructions. HIPCC, however, cannot generate code that uses AGPRs as input to MFMA instructions, leading to inefficient register management for register heavy workloads and redundant accvgpr_read/write instructions that move data between VGPRs and AGPRs.</p></details>\n<ul>\n<li><strong>Register layouts</strong>: NVIDIA tensor core layouts are regular – as we vary the data type or matrix shape, the layout is composed of an underlying “core matrix” structure. Thus, frameworks like TK and Gluon can apply a unified swizzling rule to avoid bank conflicts. However, AMD layouts differ significantly based on the data type and matrix shape. In fact, we show that it’s not possible to use a single swizzle pattern for all layouts. Further, sometimes we want to use multiple matrix shapes within the same kernel meaning that our swizzle pattern needs to be compatible with multiple types of layouts concurrently.</li>\n</ul>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:5px\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-amd-brr/matrix.png\"><p>Figure: AMD register layouts for matrix instructions are less structured. NVIDIA layouts are all composed from an underlying core matrix structure.</p></figure>\n<ul>\n<li><strong>Instruction phases:</strong> Waves (and NVIDIA warps) execute shared memory read/write instructions <a href=\"https://forums.developer.nvidia.com/t/how-to-understand-the-bank-conflict-of-shared-mem/260900\" target=\"_blank\">in phases</a>, where a subset of threads in the wave access shared memory concurrently. NVIDIA instructions sequentially assign threads to phases (e.g., threads 0-7 in phase one, 8-15 in phase two). However, the phase groups are both non-sequential and differ entirely based on AMD CDNA memory instruction. For example, we found that a <span><span><span><math>ds_read_b128\\verb|ds_read_b128|</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7063em;vertical-align:-0.0951em\"></span><span><span>ds_read_b128</span></span></span></span></span></span> instruction reading 128 bits for each thread executes across 4 phases and has access to 64 banks. On the other hand, a <span><span><span><math>ds_write_b64\\verb|ds_write_b64|</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7063em;vertical-align:-0.0951em\"></span><span><span>ds_write_b64</span></span></span></span></span></span> instruction writing 64 bits for each thread executes across 4 phases and has access to only 32 banks. This behavior is not well-documented even within AMD(! 😔), so we created and release solvers that reverse-engineer this behavior.</li>\n</ul>\n<details><summary>Learn why we can't use a single swizzle pattern for all AMD layouts.</summary><p>Proof by contradiction:</p><p>To show why a single swizzling pattern is insufficient across different register tile\nshapes and layouts on AMD GPUs, consider the following two access patterns that surface in attention backwards:</p><ol>\n<li>A row-layout 16x16 bf16 tile is written to shared memory. For this tile configuration, each thread holds 4 contiguous bf16 values - 64 bits in memory - and the most optimal instruction to issue this write is <span><span><span><math>ds_write_b64\\verb|ds_write_b64|</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7063em;vertical-align:-0.0951em\"></span><span><span>ds_write_b64</span></span></span></span></span></span>. Avoiding bank conflicts for this access requires a swizzle pattern that respects the phase ordering and bank behavior previously mentioned. In this case, a swizzle that abides by these constraints is computed as <span><span><span><math>offset\\mathrm{offset}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span><span>offset</span></span></span></span></span></span> ^ <span><span><span><math>=((offset%512)>>7)&#x3C;&#x3C;3= ((\\mathrm{offset} \\% 512) >> 7) &#x3C;&#x3C; 3</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3669em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>((</span><span><span>offset</span></span><span>%512</span><span>)</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>>></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>7</span><span>)</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>&#x3C;&#x3C;</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>3</span></span></span></span></span>, where 64-bit chunks of memory is shifted around memory using an XOR swizzle.</li>\n<li>A row-layout 16x32 bf16 tile is read from shared memory. For this tile, each thread holds 8 contiguous bf16 values - 128 bits in memory - and the most optimal instruction to issue this read is <span><span><span><math>ds_read_b128\\verb|ds_read_b128|</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7063em;vertical-align:-0.0951em\"></span><span><span>ds_read_b128</span></span></span></span></span></span>.</li>\n</ol><p>Regardless of the swizzling pattern required for <span><span><span><math>ds_read_b128\\verb|ds_read_b128|</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7063em;vertical-align:-0.0951em\"></span><span><span>ds_read_b128</span></span></span></span></span></span>, the granularities of these two instructions are in conflict with each other. <span><span><span><math>ds_read_b128\\verb|ds_read_b128|</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7063em;vertical-align:-0.0951em\"></span><span><span>ds_read_b128</span></span></span></span></span></span> requires at least 128 bits of memory to be contiguous in shared memory, and the swizzle pattern for <span><span><span><math>ds_write_b64\\verb|ds_write_b64|</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7063em;vertical-align:-0.0951em\"></span><span><span>ds_write_b64</span></span></span></span></span></span> breaks apart memory into 64-bit chunks. As a result, different swizzling patterns need to be used for each.</p></details>\n<ul>\n<li><strong>Address generation:</strong> AMD GPUs support direct asynchronous HBM to shared memory loads. Like TMA, these loads bypass the register file. The instruction takes as input per-thread addresses in HBM from which each thread will read data. While DSLs like TK directly swizzle the shared memory addresses, swizzling shared memory on AMD is instead accomplished by swizzling on the HBM addresses.</li>\n</ul>\n<p>We provide developers with optimized tile layouts and memory access patterns by default within HK. Checkout our paper to learn more about how we implement solutions to the above challenges.</p>\n<h3>HipKittens schedules within a processor</h3>\n<p>Ideally we would have simple, reusable patterns for scheduling the compute and memory within kernels that generalize across AI workloads. Wave specialization / producer consumer serves this purpose on NVIDIA, but what about on AMD?</p>\n<p><strong>Wave specialization struggles on AMD.</strong> Wave specialization is the dominant paradigm for achieving high occupancy on modern NVIDIA GPUs. Producer waves focus on memory movement while consumer waves focus on computation. This strategy underpins today’s state-of-the-art AI kernels—including <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.08608\" target=\"_blank\">FlashAttention-3</a>, <a href=\"https://openreview.net/forum?id=fGgQS5VW09\" target=\"_blank\">COMET for MOE models</a>, and <a href=\"https://research.colfax-intl.com/nvidia-hopper-gemm-cutlass/\" target=\"_blank\">high-performance GEMMs</a> —as well as kernel DSLs such as <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/ThunderKittens/blob/main/kernels/matmul/H100/matmul.cu\" target=\"_blank\">ThunderKittens LSCF</a> and <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2504.17577\" target=\"_blank\">TileLang</a>.</p>\n<p>But, we show that wave specialization underperforms on AMD due to the lack of register reallocation. On the MI355X, registers are statically divided across all waves. Producer waves that only need a few registers for address calculation are allocated more registers than they need; consumer waves cannot recoup those registers and must either spill registers to scratch memory or run at a lower arithmetic intensity. Both are disastrous for performance. Wave specialization limits the output tile size and makes our kernels more memory bound.  For GEMMs, data loaded from memory is O(MK + NK) while compute is O(MNK). Decreasing the M or N in our per thread block output tile size lowers arithmetic intensity.\n<sup id=\"user-content-fnref-2\"><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-09-amd-brr#fn-2\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">2</a></sup></p>\n<table><thead><tr><th># P / # C</th><th>MFMA Shape</th><th>Output</th><th>TFLOPS</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td>HK 4 / 8</td><td>16×16×32</td><td>128×256</td><td>893</td></tr><tr><td>HK 4 / 12</td><td>16×16×32</td><td>192×256</td><td>1278</td></tr><tr><td>HK 0 / 8</td><td>16×16×32</td><td>192×256</td><td>1281</td></tr><tr><td>HK 0 / 8</td><td>16×16×32</td><td>256×256</td><td><strong>1605</strong></td></tr><tr><td>TK</td><td>256×256×16</td><td>256×256</td><td>1538</td></tr><tr><td>CUTLASS</td><td>256×256×16</td><td>256×256</td><td>1570</td></tr></tbody></table>\n<p>Figure: <strong>Wave specialization underperforms on AMD GPUs.</strong> We benchmark AMD GEMMs on the MI355X using different numbers of producers (P) and consumer (C) waves. We report the matrix core intrinsic shape, output tile size computed per thread block, and TFLOPs (500 iterations warmup / 100 iterations measured). The CUTLASS GEMM is selected and tuned using the CUTLASS profiler tool on a B200 GPU.</p>\n<p>As an aside, it might be surprising that AMD matches NVIDIA GEMM performance without all the bells and whistles of wgmma/tma, producer consumer, TMA, mbarriers, large shared memory for deep multi-stage pipelining etc. But… AMD has a <span><span><span><math>2×2\\times</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>2</span><span>×</span></span></span></span></span> larger register file and AMD’s smaller tensor core shapes (e.g., <span><span><span><math>16×16×3216\\times16\\times32</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>16</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>×</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>16</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>×</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>32</span></span></span></span></span>) provide an alternative path to establish deep pipelines by using finer-granularity load and compute stages.</p>\n<p>Scheduling patterns for AMD. Our attempt to use wave specialization - a strategy that works well on NVIDIA GPUs - did not yield the expected speedups on AMD hardware. All is not lost! We found two scheduling patterns that consistently yield high occupancy AMD GPUs, while using tile programming primitives (no raw assembly)!</p>\n<ol>\n<li><strong>8-wave ping-pong:</strong> We assign two waves per SIMD and at any given time, one is executing a cluster of memory instructions while the other wave executes a cluster of compute instructions. The waves swap at the end of cluster execution. With this approach, the developer can use large HK tiles since a thread issues many of the same instructions at once! This draws inspiration from <a href=\"https://github.com/triton-lang/triton/pull/5018\" target=\"_blank\">ideas by AMD's Triton team</a>, and adapts it to make it performant.</li>\n<li><strong>4-wave interleave:</strong> We assign one wave per SIMD and threads in this wave finely switch between issuing memory and compute operations. Here, the developer uses small HK tiles (essentially matching the size of the matrix core instruction shape) to achieve the fine-grained schedule.</li>\n</ol>\n<p>These two patterns tradeoff programmability and performance, where 8-wave and its large tile primitives lead to compact code and 4-wave fine-grained interleaving expands code size. Surprisingly, the 8-wave schedule is sufficient to achieve SoTA-level performance on GEMMs and attention forwards. For GQA non-causal attention backwards, 8-wave also outperforms all AMD baselines by <span><span><span><math>1.8×1.8\\times</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>1.8</span><span>×</span></span></span></span></span>, and our HK 4-wave further outperforms by <span><span><span><math>2.3×2.3\\times</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>2.3</span><span>×</span></span></span></span></span>.</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:5px\"><img style=\"width:80%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-amd-brr/8_wave.png\"><p>Figure: <strong>HK 8-wave ping pong pattern.</strong> We include a profiler snippet of the HK BF16 GEMM.</p></figure>\n<h3>HipKittens schedules across processors</h3>\n<p>Modern GPUs are moving toward chiplet-based architectures, shifting away from traditional monolithic dies. AMD’s MI355X, for instance, integrates eight chiplets (XCDs), each with its own L2 cache, while NVIDIA’s B200 pairs two dies together. This shift enables higher scalability and yield but introduces a new performance challenge: disaggregated memory hierarchies. Each cluster of compute units now has local caches, and memory locality is no longer uniform across the chip.</p>\n<p>On AMD GPUs, thread blocks are scheduled to chiplets in a round-robin fashion, meaning that the order in which blocks are launched—the grid schedule—directly affects how effectively data is reused in cache. Even perfectly tuned kernels can lose bandwidth if their grid layout is cache-unfriendly.</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:5px\"><img style=\"width:32%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-amd-brr/default.png\"><img style=\"width:32%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-amd-brr/w5.png\"><img style=\"width:32%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-amd-brr/w7.png\"><img style=\"width:32%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-amd-brr/14592_default.png\"><img style=\"width:32%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-amd-brr/14592_w8_c0.png\"><img style=\"width:32%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-amd-brr/14592_w8_c64.png\"><p>Figure: <strong>Visualization of three different grid schedules for the output matrix of a BF16 GEMM.</strong> The color represents the XCD assignment for the first set of thread blocks scheduled across the GPU's 256 processors. <strong>Top row</strong> is for a <span><span><span><math>9216×9216×92169216\\times9216\\times9216</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>9216</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>×</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>9216</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>×</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>9216</span></span></span></span></span> shaped GEMM and the <strong>bottom row</strong> is for a <span><span><span><math>14592×14592×1459214592\\times14592\\times14592</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>14592</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>×</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>14592</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>×</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>14592</span></span></span></span></span> shaped GEMM. The <strong>left most column</strong> shows the assignments under a naive row-major layout, the <strong>middle column</strong> shows an approach that optimizes L2 cache reuse, and the <strong>right column</strong> shows the output from our algorithm, balancing L2 and LLC cache reuse.</p></figure>\n<table><thead><tr><th>Block Order</th><th>L2 %</th><th>LLC %</th><th>Mem. BW</th><th>TFLOPS</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><strong>Matrix Multiply (M=N=K=9216)</strong></td><td></td><td></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td>Row-major</td><td>55%</td><td>95%</td><td>15.1 TB/s</td><td>1113</td></tr><tr><td>XCD (W 7/C 216)</td><td>79%</td><td>24%</td><td>14.9 TB/s</td><td>991</td></tr><tr><td>XCD (W 5/C 25)</td><td>75%</td><td>93%</td><td>18.3 TB/s</td><td>1145</td></tr><tr><td><strong>Matrix Multiply (M=N=K=14592)</strong></td><td></td><td></td><td></td><td></td></tr><tr><td>Row-major</td><td>36%</td><td>76%</td><td>10.7 TB/s</td><td>900</td></tr><tr><td>XCD (W 8/C 542)</td><td>79%</td><td>7%</td><td>13.9 TB/s</td><td>980</td></tr><tr><td>XCD W 8/C 64</td><td>78%</td><td>55%</td><td>16.6 TB/s</td><td>1068</td></tr></tbody></table>\n<p>Table: Performance results corresponding to the above chiplet swizzling figures.</p>\n<p>Above for a GEMM <span><span><span><math>D=AB+CD=AB + C</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">D</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.7667em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>A</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05017em\">B</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>+</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">C</span></span></span></span></span>, we show different patterns for assigning thread blocks the responsibility of computing different tiles of the output matrix <span><span><span><math>DD</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">D</span></span></span></span></span>. When thread blocks are scheduled in naíve row-major order, cache reuse is suboptimal (<span><span><span><math>≈55%\\approx55\\%</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4831em\"></span><span>≈</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8056em;vertical-align:-0.0556em\"></span><span>55%</span></span></span></span></span>) because blocks that share the same L2 cache often load different, non-overlapping tiles of A and B. Further, optimizing purely for L2 locality can cause each XCD to fetch disjoint portions of A and B, leading to redundant loads at the next cache level.</p>\n<p>To address this, HipKittens introduces a chiplet-aware scheduling strategy that reorganizes the grid launch order to better exploit locality at both the L2 (per-chiplet) and LLC (shared) cache levels for GEMM workloads. The key idea is to group thread blocks that operate on nearby regions of the output matrix so that they naturally reuse overlapping tiles of input data across cache hierarchies.</p>\n<h3>Putting it all together</h3>\n<p>Let's take a look at a few kernels written in HK.</p>\n<ol>\n<li>First, here's the hot loop of our attention forwards pass kernel (the entire kernel is <span><span><span><math>≈500\\approx 500</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4831em\"></span><span>≈</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>500</span></span></span></span></span> lines of code). We can see that the kernel uses HK's 8-wave ping pong schedule where waves alternate between compute instruction clusters and memory clusters.</li>\n</ol>\n<div><pre><code class=\"language-cpp\"><span>// hot loop</span>\n<span><span>#</span><span>pragma</span> <span>unroll</span></span>\n<span>for</span> <span>(</span><span>int</span> j <span>=</span> <span>3</span><span>;</span> j <span>&#x3C;</span> num_tiles <span>-</span> <span>1</span><span>;</span> j <span>+=</span> <span>2</span><span>)</span> <span>{</span>\n    <span>// Cluster 0: Compute</span>\n    <span>//      QK1 (QK multiplication for tile 1 of keys)</span>\n    <span>zero</span><span>(</span>att_block<span>[</span><span>1</span><span>]</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>swap_layout_and_transpose</span><span>(</span>k_reg_transposed<span>,</span> k_reg<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>mma_AtB</span><span>(</span>att_block<span>[</span><span>1</span><span>]</span><span>,</span> k_reg_transposed<span>,</span> q_reg_transposed<span>,</span> att_block<span>[</span><span>1</span><span>]</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>//      Finish softmax for QK0 (Softmax w/r/t tile 0 of keys, which we started in the prologue)</span>\n    <span>exp2</span><span>(</span>att_block<span>[</span><span>0</span><span>]</span><span>.</span>tiles<span>[</span><span>1</span><span>]</span><span>[</span><span>0</span><span>]</span><span>,</span> att_block<span>[</span><span>0</span><span>]</span><span>.</span>tiles<span>[</span><span>1</span><span>]</span><span>[</span><span>0</span><span>]</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>mul</span><span>(</span>norm_vec<span>,</span> norm_vec<span>,</span> scale_vec<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>col_sum</span><span>(</span>norm_vec<span>,</span> att_block<span>[</span><span>0</span><span>]</span><span>,</span> norm_vec<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>copy</span><span>(</span>att_block_bf16<span>,</span> att_block<span>[</span><span>0</span><span>]</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    att_block_bf16_in <span>=</span> <span>*</span><span><span>reinterpret_cast</span><span><span>&#x3C;</span>attn_tile<span>&#x3C;</span>D<span>,</span> bf16<span>,</span> col_l<span>,</span> rt_16x32_4_s<span>></span><span>*</span><span>></span></span></span><span>(</span><span>&#x26;</span>att_block_bf16<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span><span>sched_barrier_exp_pairs</span><span><span>&#x3C;</span><span>6</span><span>,</span> <span>3</span><span>,</span> <span>1</span><span>></span></span></span><span>(</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span><span>sched_barrier_pairs</span><span><span>&#x3C;</span><span>10</span><span>,</span> <span>5</span><span>,</span> <span>1</span><span>></span></span></span><span>(</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>__builtin_amdgcn_sched_barrier</span><span>(</span><span>0</span><span>)</span><span>;</span> <span>__builtin_amdgcn_s_barrier</span><span>(</span><span>)</span><span>;</span> <span>__builtin_amdgcn_sched_barrier</span><span>(</span><span>0</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n\n    <span>// Cluster 1: Memory</span>\n    <span>//      Load K3 into shared (Tile 3 of keys)</span>\n    G<span>::</span><span><span>load</span><span><span>&#x3C;</span><span>1</span><span>,</span> <span>false</span><span>></span></span></span><span>(</span>k_smem<span>[</span><span>1</span><span>]</span><span>,</span> g<span>.</span>Kg<span>,</span> <span>{</span>batch_idx<span>,</span> j<span>,</span> head_idx_kv<span>,</span> <span>0</span><span>}</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>//      Load V0 into registers (Prepare for multiplication by tile 0 of values)</span>\n    <span>load</span><span>(</span>v_reg<span>,</span> v_smem<span>[</span><span>0</span><span>]</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>asm</span> <span>volatile</span><span>(</span><span>\"s_waitcnt lgkmcnt(0)\"</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>asm</span> <span>volatile</span><span>(</span><span>\"s_waitcnt vmcnt(2)\"</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>__builtin_amdgcn_sched_barrier</span><span>(</span><span>0</span><span>)</span><span>;</span> <span>__builtin_amdgcn_s_barrier</span><span>(</span><span>)</span><span>;</span> <span>__builtin_amdgcn_sched_barrier</span><span>(</span><span>0</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n\n    <span>// Cluster 2: Compute</span>\n    <span>//      A0V0</span>\n    <span>__builtin_amdgcn_s_setprio</span><span>(</span><span>1</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>mma_AtB</span><span>(</span>o_reg<span>,</span> v_reg<span>,</span> att_block_bf16_in<span>,</span> o_reg<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>//      Partial softmax for QK1</span>\n    <span>col_max</span><span>(</span>max_vec<span>,</span> att_block<span>[</span><span>1</span><span>]</span><span>,</span> max_vec_prev<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>sub</span><span>(</span>scale_vec<span>,</span> max_vec_prev<span>,</span> max_vec<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>copy</span><span>(</span>max_vec_prev<span>,</span> max_vec<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>exp2</span><span>(</span>scale_vec<span>,</span> scale_vec<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n max_vec<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n att_block<span>[</span><span>1</span><span>]</span><span>.</span>tiles<span>[</span><span>0</span><span>]</span><span>[</span><span>0</span><span>]</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n <span>2</span><span>></span></span></span><span>(</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span><span>sched_barrier_exp_pairs</span><span><span>&#x3C;</span><span>6</span><span>,</span> <span>3</span><span>,</span> <span>2</span><span>></span></span></span><span>(</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n scale_vec<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>__builtin_amdgcn_s_setprio</span><span>(</span><span>0</span><span>)</span><span>;</span> \n <span>__builtin_amdgcn_sched_barrier</span><span>(</span><span>0</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n\n    <span>// Cluster 3: Memory</span>\n    <span>//      Load V2 into shared</span>\n    G<span>::</span><span><span>load</span><span><span>&#x3C;</span><span>1</span><span>,</span> <span>false</span><span>></span></span></span><span>(</span>v_smem<span>[</span><span>0</span><span>]</span><span>,</span> g<span>.</span>Vg<span>,</span> <span>{</span>batch_idx<span>,</span> j <span>-</span> <span>1</span><span>,</span> head_idx_kv<span>,</span> <span>0</span><span>}</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>//      Load K2 into registers</span>\n <span>__builtin_amdgcn_s_barrier</span><span>(</span><span>)</span><span>;</span> <span>__builtin_amdgcn_sched_barrier</span><span>(</span><span>0</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n\n    <span>// Cluster 4: Compute</span>\n    <span>//      QK2</span>\n    <span>zero</span><span>(</span>att_block<span>[</span><span>0</span><span>]</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n att_block<span>[</span><span>0</span><span>]</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>//      Finish softmax for QK1</span>\n att_block<span>[</span><span>1</span><span>]</span><span>,</span> norm_vec<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>copy</span><span>(</span>att_block_bf16<span>,</span> <span>5</span><span>,</span> <span>3</span><span>></span></span></span><span>(</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n <span>__builtin_amdgcn_sched_barrier</span><span>(</span><span>0</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n\n    <span>// Cluster 5: Memory</span>\n    <span>//      Load K4 into shared</span>\n <span>false</span><span>></span></span></span><span>(</span>k_smem<span>[</span><span>0</span><span>]</span><span>,</span> g<span>.</span>Kg<span>,</span> <span>{</span>batch_idx<span>,</span> j Cluster 6: Compute</span>\n    <span>//      A1V1</span>\n    <span>__builtin_amdgcn_s_setprio</span><span>(</span><span>1</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n <span>3</span><span>,</span> <span>4</span><span>></span></span></span><span>(</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n scale_vec<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>__builtin_amdgcn_s_setprio</span><span>(</span><span>0</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n <span>__builtin_amdgcn_sched_barrier</span><span>(</span><span>0</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n\n    <span>// Cluster 7: Memory</span>\n    <span>//      Load V3 into shared</span>\n    G<span>::</span><span><span>load</span><span><span>&#x3C;</span><span>1</span><span>,</span> <span>false</span><span>></span></span></span><span>(</span>v_smem<span>[</span><span>1</span><span>]</span><span>,</span> g<span>.</span>Vg<span>,</span> <span>{</span>batch_idx<span>,</span> <span>volatile</span><span>(</span><span>\"s_waitcnt lgkmcnt(0)\"</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>asm</span> <span>volatile</span><span>(</span><span>\"s_waitcnt vmcnt(2)\"</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>__builtin_amdgcn_sched_barrier</span><span>(</span><span>0</span><span>)</span><span>;</span> <span>__builtin_amdgcn_s_barrier</span><span>(</span><span>)</span><span>;</span> <span>__builtin_amdgcn_sched_barrier</span><span>(</span><span>0</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n<span>}</span>\n</code></pre></div>\n<ol start=\"2\">\n<li>Here's the core hot loop structure for our BF16 GEMM kernel. Again, we can see that waves alternate between compute clusters and memory clusters using HK's 8-wave ping pong schedule.</li>\n</ol>\n<div><pre><code class=\"language-cpp\"><span><span>#</span><span>pragma</span> <span>unroll</span></span>\n<span>for</span> <span>(</span><span>int</span> tile <span>=</span> <span>0</span><span>;</span> tile <span>&#x3C;</span> num_tiles <span>-</span> <span>2</span><span>;</span> <span>++</span>tile<span>,</span> tic<span>^=</span><span>1</span><span>,</span> toc<span>^=</span><span>1</span><span>)</span> <span>{</span>\n    <span>// Cluster 0: Memory</span>\n    <span>// load a sub-tile of the current A and B tiles from SMEM to register</span>\n    <span>auto</span> st_subtile_b <span>=</span> <span><span>subtile_inplace</span><span><span>&#x3C;</span>HALF_REG_BLOCK_N<span>,</span> K_STEP<span>></span></span></span><span>(</span>Bs<span>[</span>tic<span>]</span><span>[</span><span>0</span><span>]</span><span>,</span> <span>{</span>warp_col<span>,</span> <span>0</span><span>}</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>load</span><span>(</span>B_tile_0<span>,</span> st_subtile_b<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>auto</span> st_subtile_a <span>=</span> <span><span>subtile_inplace</span><span><span>&#x3C;</span>HALF_REG_BLOCK_M<span>,</span> K_STEP<span>></span></span></span><span>(</span>As<span>[</span>tic<span>]</span><span>[</span><span>0</span><span>]</span><span>,</span> <span>{</span>warp_row<span>,</span> <span>0</span><span>}</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>load</span><span>(</span>A_tile<span>,</span> st_subtile_a<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>// load a portion of the future A tile into the shared memory buffer</span>\n    <span>G</span><span>::</span><span>load</span><span>(</span>As<span>[</span>toc<span>]</span><span>[</span><span>1</span><span>]</span><span>,</span> g<span>.</span>a<span>,</span> <span>{</span><span>0</span><span>,</span> <span>0</span><span>,</span> row<span>*</span><span>2</span> <span>+</span> <span>1</span><span>,</span> tile <span>+</span> <span>1</span><span>}</span><span>,</span> swizzled_offsets_A<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>asm</span> <span>volatile</span><span>(</span><span>\"s_waitcnt lgkmcnt(8)\"</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>__builtin_amdgcn_s_barrier</span><span>(</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n\n    <span>// Cluster 1: Compute</span>\n    <span>// mfma on the sub-tile</span>\n    <span>asm</span> <span>volatile</span><span>(</span><span>\"s_waitcnt lgkmcnt(0)\"</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>__builtin_amdgcn_s_setprio</span><span>(</span><span>1</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>mma_ABt</span><span>(</span>C_accum<span>[</span><span>0</span><span>]</span><span>[</span><span>0</span><span>]</span><span>,</span> A_tile<span>,</span> B_tile_0<span>,</span> C_accum<span>[</span><span>0</span><span>]</span><span>[</span><span>0</span><span>]</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>__builtin_amdgcn_s_setprio</span><span>(</span><span>0</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>__builtin_amdgcn_s_barrier</span><span>(</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>__builtin_amdgcn_sched_barrier</span><span>(</span><span>0</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n\n    <span>// Cluster 2: Memory</span>\n    <span>// load next sub-tile of B from SMEM to register </span>\n    st_subtile_b <span>=</span> <span><span>subtile_inplace</span><span><span>&#x3C;</span>HALF_REG_BLOCK_N<span>,</span> K_STEP<span>></span></span></span><span>(</span>Bs<span>[</span>tic<span>]</span><span>[</span><span>1</span><span>]</span><span>,</span> <span>{</span>warp_col<span>,</span> <span>0</span><span>}</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>load</span><span>(</span>B_tile_1<span>,</span> st_subtile_b<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>G</span><span>::</span><span>load</span><span>(</span>Bs<span>[</span>tic<span>]</span><span>[</span><span>0</span><span>]</span><span>,</span> g<span>.</span>b<span>,</span> <span>{</span><span>0</span><span>,</span> <span>0</span><span>,</span> col<span>*</span><span>2</span><span>,</span> tile <span>+</span> <span>2</span><span>}</span><span>,</span> swizzled_offsets_B<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>__builtin_amdgcn_s_barrier</span><span>(</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n\n   <span>// Cluster 3: Compute </span>\n    <span>asm</span> <span>volatile</span><span>(</span><span>\"s_waitcnt lgkmcnt(0)\"</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>__builtin_amdgcn_s_setprio</span><span>(</span><span>1</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>mma_ABt</span><span>(</span>C_accum<span>[</span><span>0</span><span>]</span><span>[</span><span>1</span><span>]</span><span>,</span> A_tile<span>,</span> B_tile_1<span>,</span> C_accum<span>[</span><span>0</span><span>]</span><span>[</span><span>1</span><span>]</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>__builtin_amdgcn_s_setprio</span><span>(</span><span>0</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>__builtin_amdgcn_s_barrier</span><span>(</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n\n    <span>// Cluster 4: Memory</span>\n    st_subtile_a <span>=</span> <span><span>subtile_inplace</span><span><span>&#x3C;</span>HALF_REG_BLOCK_M<span>,</span> K_STEP<span>></span></span></span><span>(</span>As<span>[</span>tic<span>]</span><span>[</span><span>1</span><span>]</span><span>,</span> <span>{</span>warp_row<span>,</span> <span>0</span><span>}</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>load</span><span>(</span>A_tile<span>,</span> st_subtile_a<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>G</span><span>::</span><span>load</span><span>(</span>As<span>[</span>tic<span>]</span><span>[</span><span>0</span><span>]</span><span>,</span> g<span>.</span>a<span>,</span> <span>{</span><span>0</span><span>,</span> <span>0</span><span>,</span> row<span>*</span><span>2</span><span>,</span> tile <span>+</span> <span>2</span><span>}</span><span>,</span> swizzled_offsets_A<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>__builtin_amdgcn_s_barrier</span><span>(</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n\n    <span>// Cluster 5: Compute</span>\n    <span>asm</span> <span>volatile</span><span>(</span><span>\"s_waitcnt lgkmcnt(0)\"</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>__builtin_amdgcn_s_setprio</span><span>(</span><span>1</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>mma_ABt</span><span>(</span>C_accum<span>[</span><span>1</span><span>]</span><span>[</span><span>0</span><span>]</span><span>,</span> A_tile<span>,</span> B_tile_0<span>,</span> C_accum<span>[</span><span>1</span><span>]</span><span>[</span><span>0</span><span>]</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>__builtin_amdgcn_s_setprio</span><span>(</span><span>0</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>__builtin_amdgcn_s_barrier</span><span>(</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>__builtin_amdgcn_sched_barrier</span><span>(</span><span>0</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n\n    <span>// Cluster 6: Memory</span>\n    <span>// load a portion of the future B tile into the shared memory buffer</span>\n    <span>G</span><span>::</span><span>load</span><span>(</span>Bs<span>[</span>tic<span>]</span><span>[</span><span>1</span><span>]</span><span>,</span> g<span>.</span>b<span>,</span> <span>{</span><span>0</span><span>,</span> <span>0</span><span>,</span> col<span>*</span><span>2</span> <span>+</span> <span>1</span><span>,</span> tile <span>+</span> <span>2</span><span>}</span><span>,</span> swizzled_offsets_B<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>asm</span> <span>volatile</span><span>(</span><span>\"s_waitcnt vmcnt(6)\"</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>__builtin_amdgcn_s_barrier</span><span>(</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    \n    <span>// Cluster 7: Compute</span>\n    <span>__builtin_amdgcn_s_setprio</span><span>(</span><span>1</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>mma_ABt</span><span>(</span>C_accum<span>[</span><span>1</span><span>]</span><span>[</span><span>1</span><span>]</span><span>,</span> A_tile<span>,</span> B_tile_1<span>,</span> C_accum<span>[</span><span>1</span><span>]</span><span>[</span><span>1</span><span>]</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>__builtin_amdgcn_s_setprio</span><span>(</span><span>0</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>__builtin_amdgcn_s_barrier</span><span>(</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n<span>}</span>\n</code></pre></div>\n<h3>Multi-silicon AI is coming!</h3>\n<p>HipKittens delivers competitive performance on AMD CDNA3 and CDNA4 through three key insights: optimized memory access, AMD-centric wave scheduling patterns within a processor, and chiplet-aware grid scheduling across processors to exploits AMD's disaggregated cache hierarchy. Our kernels consistently achieve peak performance amongst AMD baselines across workloads (and compete with peak Blackwell kernels as well).</p>\n<p>Realizing AI's full potential requires diverse, open hardware.<sup id=\"user-content-fnref-1\"><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-09-amd-brr#fn-1\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">1</a></sup> Today, that means making AMD GPUs truly accessible.</p>\n<p>We want more AI in the world. AI has relied on and innovated on a single hardware provider, but we need to be able to use and experiment with all the compute we can. We need to be able to use the fastest hardware out there. We’re happy to help address these problems with HipKittens!</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:5px\"><img style=\"width:40%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-hk/hk_wave.png\"><p>Figure: Surfing towards multi-silicon AI!</p></figure>\n<p><strong>Links</strong>: <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2511.08083\" target=\"_blank\">Arxiv</a> | <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/HipKittens\" target=\"_blank\">Code</a></p>\n<div>\n<hr>\n<ol>\n<li id=\"user-content-fn-1\">We believe that the HIPCC register scheduling is one of the most important areas for improvement in AMD's kernel software stack.<a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-09-amd-brr#fnref-1\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">↩</a></li>\n<li id=\"user-content-fn-2\">We hope these findings lead to hardware changes that support wave specialization or guide AMD kernel development; for instance,  Mojo currently provides a <a href=\"https://github.com/modular/modular/blob/main/max/kernels/src/linalg/matmul/gpu/amd/warp_spec_matmul.mojo\" target=\"_blank\">warp-specialized matmul kernel as of 11/06/2025</a> even though AMD CDNA doesn’t have register reallocation.<a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-09-amd-brr#fnref-2\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">↩</a></li>\n</ol>\n</div>,Team: William Hu, Drew Wadsworth, Sean Siddens, Stanley Winata, Daniel Fu, Ryan Swann, Muhammad Osama, Christopher Ré, Simran Arora Links: A…,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-09-amd-brr,<a href=\"https://willhu-jpg.github.io/\">William Hu</a>, <a href=\"https://www.linkedin.com/in/wadsworthstephen\">Drew Wadsworth</a>, <a href=\"https://arorasimran.com/\">Simran Arora</a>,2026-07-22T18:37:38.415Z,2025-11-11T07:00:00.074Z,[{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-amd-brr/memory.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1656,\"height\":1276,\"blurhash\":\"LtJ*be~p%gXTjYoLogWBKPNanNr=\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-amd-brr/chip.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":2102,\"height\":1260,\"blurhash\":\"LKH.l[:6YQ0{*|#Sb^NwCRF_$k-U\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-amd-brr/matrix.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":2554,\"height\":734,\"blurhash\":\"LcOMa2vn2{rv8waLOZjG.9SdwHbF\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-amd-brr/8_wave.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":2184,\"height\":1266,\"blurhash\":\"LWP?,UaekDf6_4RjaeWB-=ofWBWB\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-amd-brr/default.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":4884,\"height\":4884,\"blurhash\":\"LEB:BDjbWVjI~Cjba|jb^*jbazjb\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-amd-brr/w5.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":4884,\"height\":4884,\"blurhash\":\"LDB:BDR:#mN4~Vai+]N4^*J:#lVz\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-amd-brr/w7.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":4884,\"height\":4884,\"blurhash\":\"L6B:BD#txUBrBs4[-BtT{wWJ~CnB\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-amd-brr/14592_default.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":0,\"height\":0,\"blurhash\":\"\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-amd-brr/14592_w8_c0.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":0,\"height\":0,\"blurhash\":\"\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-amd-brr/14592_w8_c64.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":0,\"height\":0,\"blurhash\":\"\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-11-09-hk/hk_wave.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1668,\"height\":1116,\"blurhash\":\"LRN^VeIU~qay%MM{t7t7?bt7ofxa\"}],163778663386687488,1211296081845936140,We Bought the Whole GPU, So We're Damn Well Going to Use the Whole GPU,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-09-28-tp-llama-main,<p><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-09-28-tp-llama-intro\" target=\"_blank\"><strong>Intro Post</strong></a> | <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/Megakernels/tree/throughput\" target=\"_blank\"><strong>Code</strong></a> | <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-05-27-no-bubbles\" target=\"_blank\"><strong>Low-Latency Megakernels</strong></a> | <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-05-12-tk\" target=\"_blank\"><strong>Brr</strong></a></p>\n<p><strong>TLDR:</strong> We're releasing a throughput-optimized megakernel for tensor-parallel inference with Llama-70B on H100s. Our kernel can aggressively overlap compute, memory, and communication ops in order to simultaneously use the different hardware resources available on a GPU. When integrated into the Tokasaurus inference engine, our megakernel can outperform SGLang by <strong>>22%</strong> on end-to-end throughput (measured as time to finish 65,536 prompts from the ShareGPT benchmark). We're releasing the code <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/Megakernels/tree/throughput\" target=\"_blank\">here</a>; please be warned that this <em>really</em> is research code; it is sensitive to compiler versions, GPU setup, and sometimes even being looked at the wrong way, and we have no intention whatsoever of supporting it. We hope you'll find the ideas and results interesting nonetheless!</p>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-09-28-tp-llama-main/results.png\" alt=\"Performance comparison graph\"><p><em>Figure 1: Zoooommmm</em></p></div>\n<p>A few months ago, we <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-05-27-no-bubbles\" target=\"_blank\">showed</a> how we could fuse an entire model forward pass into a single \"megakernel\" in order to deliver low-latency inference with Llama-1B. In that post, we teased that many of the same concepts we introduced would also be useful for optimizing for throughput. We're now excited to bring receipts and release a new megakernel optimized for high-throughput inference with Llama-70B.</p>\n<p>The inference workloads targeted by our low-latency and high-throughput megakernels are quite different and require distinct optimizations. Our low-latency megakernel targeted inference using Llama-1B when running on a single GPU with batch size one. This workload was entirely memory bound, and our focus was therefore on eliminating stalls that delayed loading model weights from global memory.</p>\n<p>With large-batch Llama-70B inference, our workload is much more heterogeneous. Large portions of it (e.g. matrix multiplies, attention prefill) are compute-bound. Other parts (e.g. attention decode, RMS norm) are still bottlenecked by global memory bandwidth. Additionally, by distributing our model across multiple GPUs, we now need to perform cross-GPU communication that throttles the NVLink connections between devices. By running these components sequentially, we've paid for the whole GPU, but are only using little bits and pieces of it at a time. :(</p>\n<p>Overall, these different operations in our model each make use of different resources available on the GPU (e.g. tensor cores, non-matmul compute units, HBM bandwidth, NVLink bandwidth) in unique ways. Therefore, a key area for optimizing this high-throughput workload is to <em>overlap multiple kinds of work</em> in order to simultaneously use more of the GPU's resources. We want to do this across many levels of the GPU -- within an individual SM, across multiple SMs, and even across GPUs.</p>\n<p>Existing approaches to overlapping include <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2408.12757v2\" target=\"_blank\">assigning different SMs to different ops</a>, developing <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2410.18038\" target=\"_blank\">custom kernels to run prefill and decode simultaneously</a>, and <a href=\"https://discuss.pytorch.org/t/distributed-w-torchtitan-introducing-async-tensor-parallelism-in-pytorch/209487\" target=\"_blank\">running kernels in parallel with cross-gpu memory copying operations</a>. Here, we show that the same simple, interpreter-based megakernel patterns <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-05-27-no-bubbles\" target=\"_blank\">we previously introduced</a> can also achieve all of these fine-grained overlapping patterns -- and more! Most excitingly, despite the significant differences between our low-latency and high-throughput workloads, our core megakernel abstraction (a pipelined instruction interpreter that runs on each SM) is highly transferable across both domains.</p>\n<p>In the rest of this blog, we will:</p>\n<ol>\n<li>Give a brief recap on the design of our megakernels from our last, low-latency post.</li>\n<li>Walk through the details of the tensor-parallel Llama forward pass that we map into our megakernel, including a novel approach to communicating intermediate results across GPUs right after running attention.
