At the coarsest level is the no-overlap version, which performs a full matrix multiplication and then a full reduce-scatter at the end. A finer-grained version is asynchronous tensor-parallel matrix multiplication, which performs reduce-scatter on the previous 1/N chunk of output matrix while computing the current chunk. With PGLs, we can push this to the finest level by overlapping the compute of the current tile (e.g., 128x128) with reduce-scatter of the previous tile within a single kernel. A pseudo-code for such would look as follows:</p>\n<div><pre><code class=\"language-cpp\">__global__ <span>void</span> <span>kernel</span><span>(</span><span>.</span><span>.</span><span>.</span><span>)</span> <span>{</span>\n\n    <span>if</span> <span>(</span>warpgroup_id <span>&#x3C;</span> <span>2</span><span>)</span> <span>{</span> <span>// Consumer (2 warpgroups)</span>\n\n        <span>for</span> <span>(</span><span>int</span> i <span>=</span> <span>0</span><span>;</span> i <span>&#x3C;</span> num_tiles<span>;</span> i<span>++</span><span>)</span> <span>{</span>\n            <span>mbarrier_wait_for_final_matmul_completion</span><span>(</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n            <span>async_load_from_TMEM</span><span>(</span>reg<span>,</span> TMEM<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n            <span>wait_for_load_completion</span><span>(</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n            <span>store_to_SMEM</span><span>(</span>SMEM<span>,</span> reg<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n        <span>}</span>\n\n    <span>}</span> <span>else</span> <span>if</span> <span>(</span>warpgroup_id <span>==</span> <span>2</span><span>)</span> <span>{</span>\n\n        <span>if</span> <span>(</span>warp_id <span>==</span> <span>0</span> <span>&#x26;&#x26;</span> lane_id <span>==</span> <span>0</span><span>)</span> <span>{</span> <span>// TMA Loader</span>\n\n            <span>for</span> <span>(</span><span>int</span> i <span>=</span> <span>0</span><span>;</span> i <span>&#x3C;</span> num_tiles<span>;</span> i<span>++</span><span>)</span> <span>{</span>\n                <span>for</span> <span>(</span><span>int</span> j <span>=</span> <span>0</span><span>;</span> j <span>&#x3C;</span> num_iters<span>;</span> j<span>++</span><span>)</span> <span>{</span>\n                    <span>mbarrier_wait_for_matmul_completion</span><span>(</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n                    <span>TMA_async_load</span><span>(</span>SMEM<span>,</span> HBM<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n                <span>}</span>\n            <span>}</span>\n\n        <span>}</span> <span>else</span> <span>if</span> <span>(</span>warp_id <span>==</span> <span>1</span> <span>&#x26;&#x26;</span> lane_id <span>==</span> <span>0</span><span>)</span> <span>{</span> <span>// Tensor Core Launcher</span>\n\n            <span>for</span> <span>(</span><span>int</span> i <span>=</span> <span>0</span><span>;</span> i <span>&#x3C;</span> num_tiles<span>;</span> i<span>++</span><span>)</span> <span>{</span>\n                <span>mbarrier_wait_for_TMEM_clear</span><span>(</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n                <span>for</span> <span>(</span><span>int</span> j <span>=</span> <span>0</span><span>;</span> j <span>&#x3C;</span> num_iters<span>;</span> j<span>++</span><span>)</span> <span>{</span>\n                    <span>mbarrier_wait_for_input_SMEM_load</span><span>(</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n                    <span>launch_tc_matmul</span><span>(</span>TMEM<span>,</span> SMEM<span>,</span> SMEM<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n                <span>}</span>\n            <span>}</span>\n\n        <span>}</span> <span>else</span> <span>if</span> <span>(</span>warp_id <span>==</span> <span>2</span> <span>&#x26;&#x26;</span> lane_id <span>==</span> <span>0</span><span>)</span> <span>{</span> <span>// TMA Storer</span>\n\n            <span>for</span> <span>(</span><span>int</span> i <span>=</span> <span>0</span><span>;</span> i <span>&#x3C;</span> num_tiles<span>;</span> i<span>++</span><span>)</span> <span>{</span>\n                <span>mbarrier_wait_for_output_SMEM_load</span><span>(</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n                <span>multimem_async_reduce_add_with_TMA</span><span>(</span>MULTICAST_OBJECT<span>,</span> SMEM<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n            <span>}</span>\n\n        <span>}</span>\n    <span>}</span>\n<span>}</span>\n</code></pre></div>\n<p>With this design, the reduce-scatter is hidden by every tiled tensor-core matrix multiplication at the intra-SM level.</p>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-09-22-pgl/gemm-reduce-scatter.png\" alt=\"GEMM + Reduce-Scatter\"><p><em>Figure 13: GEMM + Reduce-Scatter (<a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/ThunderKittens/tree/main/kernels/parallel/matmul_rs\" target=\"_blank\">source code</a>)</em></p></div>\n<p>In addition, this approach is exactly what we used in our <strong>8-GPU LLaMA-70B megakernel</strong>. By overlapping the multi-GPU residual add of the previous output projection tile with the compute of the current tile, we were able to hide most of the NVLink communication during 8-GPU LLaMA-70B decoding. You can read more about this in the upcoming LLaMA-70B megakernel post!</p>\n<h2>Moving On…</h2>\n<p>The addition of PGLs opens up a wide range of new kernels we can implement with ThunderKittens, and we are eager to explore more opportunities to improve multi-GPU performance. So far, all of our experiments have been on 8-GPU platforms, and we are curious to see how these techniques scale on Grace-Blackwell NVL72 systems with 72 GPUs interconnected through NVLink. We are also working on extending ThunderKittens beyond NVLink to include InfiniBand, enabling multi-node kernels in the same programming model.</p>\n<p>For now, the tile abstraction appears just as effective for multi-GPU communication as it was for single-GPU kernels <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-05-12-tk\" target=\"_blank\">when we introduced it a year ago</a>. We found PGLs to be very neat for implementing performant multi-GPU kernels within an NVSwitch domain. Please try it out and let us know what multi-GPU kernels you are able to build!</p>\n<p>And if you'd like to learn more or contribute, feel free to reach out to Stuart at <a href=\"mailto:ssul@cs.stanford.edu\" target=\"_blank\">ssul@cs.stanford.edu</a>.</p>\n<p>Lastly, huge thanks to <a href=\"https://cursor.com/\" target=\"_blank\">Cursor</a> for supporting this work!</p>\n<p><strong>Links: <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-17-pk\" target=\"_blank\">Intro</a> | <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-09-22-pgl\" target=\"_blank\">Part 1</a> (this post) | <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-11-17-fluffy-kittens\" target=\"_blank\">Part 2</a> | <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/ThunderKittens\" target=\"_blank\">Code</a> | <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2511.13940\" target=\"_blank\">Paper</a></strong> <br></p>,Links: Intro | Part 1 (this post) | Part 2 | Code | Paper AI models are now too large to fit on a single GPU, or even a single node.\n\nA few…,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-09-22-pgl,<a href=\"https://stuartsul.com/\">Stuart Sul</a>, <a href=\"https://www.linkedin.com/in/dylanllim/\">Dylan Lim</a>, <a href='https://benjaminfspector.com/'>Benjamin Spector</a>, <a href=\"https://cs.stanford.edu/people/chrismre/\">Chris Ré</a>,2026-07-22T18:37:38.412Z,2025-09-22T07:00:00.730Z,[{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-09-22-pgl/hgx-b200.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1686,\"height\":1142,\"blurhash\":\"LCSF-9o#%OofyZWAVrjZ^kbHS2WV\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-09-22-pgl/cuda-ipc-flow.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1576,\"height\":1432,\"blurhash\":\"LCSr}#TzkWrB9YE0axxuH;OZkCrq\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-09-22-pgl/vmm-flow.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1920,\"height\":1728,\"blurhash\":\"LCSia6PWxum*9YE0ayxvH;OsWBr;\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-09-22-pgl/nvswitch-acceleration.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":2176,\"height\":994,\"blurhash\":\"LJPs^ibEt7R~53ayayfREIayofoM\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-09-22-pgl/multicast-creation.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1650,\"height\":1032,\"blurhash\":\"L9S6MY53CTslofWAWVWBxaR.%0oz\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-09-22-pgl/multicast-hierarchy.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1532,\"height\":1310,\"blurhash\":\"L8S6Ma%h%g%goiWAaxWA}5IqIpNx\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-09-22-pgl/nvlink-data-path.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":2578,\"height\":932,\"blurhash\":\"LBRp8.x^x^xw18RjWARP-pR*R*Rk\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-09-22-pgl/all-reduce.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":3600,\"height\":2400,\"blurhash\":\"LSQc#Xs:%Lxa~oR*M|WV?GofR*R*\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-09-22-pgl/all-gather.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":3600,\"height\":2400,\"blurhash\":\"LLQJ+W-V-oxa~ofkRjR*=yS2W;R*\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-09-22-pgl/reduce-scatter.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":3600,\"height\":2400,\"blurhash\":\"LLQJ+W-V-oxa~oa|RjR*=yS2X7R*\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-09-22-pgl/all2all.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":3600,\"height\":2400,\"blurhash\":\"LKQJ$P-V-pxa~oWWRjR*=fS2X8R*\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-09-22-pgl/ulysses.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":3600,\"height\":2400,\"blurhash\":\"LPPZ.S?H?GxG?aofM|R*~VIpM|bG\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-09-22-pgl/gemm-reduce-scatter.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":3600,\"height\":2400,\"blurhash\":\"LPQc#X~V%LM{_1RjIVWB-oD*ofxt\"}],163778663386687488,1211296081845936143,BWLer 🎳 (Part 2): Navigating a Precision-Conditioning Tradeoff for PINNs,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-07-07-bwler-p2,<details><summary><strong>TL;DR (click to expand):</strong></summary><p>Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) offer a flexible, mesh-free way to solve PDEs, but standard architectures struggle with precision. Even on simpler interpolation tasks, without the challenging PDE constraints, we find multi-layer perceptrons (MLPs) consistently plateau at <span><span><span><math>10−810^{-8}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"></span><span>1</span><span><span>0</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>−</span><span>8</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> RMSE, far from float64's machine precision (around <span><span><span><math>10−1610^{-16}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"></span><span>1</span><span><span>0</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>−</span><span>16</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>). We introduce <strong>BWLer</strong>, a drop-in replacement for neural networks that models the PDE solution as a barycentric polynomial interpolant. BWLer can either augment a neural network (<em>BWLer-hat</em>) or fully replace it (<em>explicit BWLer</em>), cleanly separating function representation from derivative computation. This lets us disentangle precision bottlenecks caused by MLPs from those due to PDE conditioning. Across three benchmark PDEs, <strong>BWLer-hatted MLPs</strong> reduce RMSE by up to <span><span><span><math>1800×1800\\times</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>1800</span><span>×</span></span></span></span></span> over standard MLPs. <strong>Explicit BWLer</strong> models can do even better — reaching <span><span><span><math>10−1210^{-12}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"></span><span>1</span><span><span>0</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>−</span><span>12</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> RMSE, up to <strong>10 billion times</strong> improvement over standard MLPs — but they require heavier-duty, second-order optimizers. BWLer exposes a fundamental <strong>precision–conditioning tradeoff</strong>, and gives us tools to navigate it — one step toward building precise, flexible, and scalable scientific ML systems.</p></details>\n<blockquote>\n<p>⏮️ <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-07-07-bwler-p1\" target=\"_blank\">Part 1 of the blog post</a><br>\n📄 <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.23024\" target=\"_blank\">Paper</a><br>\n💻 <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/bwler\" target=\"_blank\">GitHub</a></p>\n</blockquote>\n<p><strong>Full team</strong>: Jerry Liu, Yasa Baig, Denise Hui Jean Lee, Rajat Vadiraj Dwaraknath, Atri Rudra, Chris Ré</p>\n<p><strong>Corresponding author*</strong>: <a href=\"mailto:jwl50@stanford.edu\" target=\"_blank\">jwl50@stanford.edu</a></p>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:95%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-07-07-bwler/banner2.png\" alt=\"A friendly BWLer\"></div>\n<p>In <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-07-07-bwler-p1\" target=\"_blank\">Part 1</a> of this two-part blog post, we introduced the physics-informed framework and motivated the need for better, high-precision architectures — leading to <strong>BWLer</strong> (<strong>B</strong>arycentric <strong>W</strong>eight <strong>L</strong>ay<strong>er</strong>). In Part 2, we'll dive deeper into how BWLer works, show how it achieves high-precision PDE solutions, and share what we're excited to explore next.</p>\n<h2>🎳 BWLer: Spectral interpolation meets deep learning</h2>\n<p>BWLer enhances or replaces the physics-informed neural network (PINN) with a high-precision polynomial. Instead of learning a black-box function that approximates the PDE solution everywhere, we define the solution globally using a <a href=\"https://people.maths.ox.ac.uk/trefethen/barycentric.pdf\" target=\"_blank\">barycentric polynomial interpolant</a> — a classical tool from numerical analysis known for its stability and spectral accuracy.</p>\n<p>The idea is simple: rather than using a neural network to represent the solution across the domain, we fix a grid of interpolation nodes and represent the solution entirely by its values at those nodes. BWLer then interpolates the solution using a closed-form formula, and computes derivatives using efficient spectral methods. The model remains fully differentiable and can be trained with standard ML optimizers like Adam.</p>\n<p>BWLer supports two modes (see Figure 1):</p>\n<ul>\n<li>In a <strong>BWLer-hatted MLP</strong>, the MLP predicts the node values, and BWLer handles interpolation and differentiation downstream.</li>\n<li>In <strong>explicit BWLer</strong>, we discard the MLP entirely and directly optimize the node values as parameters.</li>\n</ul>\n<p>By separating representation from differentiation, BWLer exposes new knobs for controlling precision and numerical conditioning — while remaining fully compatible with the physics-informed training.</p>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:95%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-07-07-bwler/fig2.png\" alt=\"BWLer vs. PINN architecture schematic\"><p><em><strong>Figure 1</strong>: Standard PINNs evaluate a neural network everywhere. BWLer defines a solution globally using interpolation at a fixed grid of nodes.</em></p></div>\n<details><summary><strong>If you're interested: how BWLer works under the hood</strong></summary><p>BWLer uses <strong>barycentric Lagrange interpolation</strong>, specifically the second barycentric formula:</p><div><span><span><span><math>f(x)=∑jwjx−xjfj∑jwjx−xjf(x) = \\frac{\\sum_j \\frac{w_j}{x - x_j} f_j}{\\sum_j \\frac{w_j}{x - x_j}}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10764em\">f</span><span>(</span><span>x</span><span>)</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:2.9821em;vertical-align:-1.241em\"></span><span><span></span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.741em\"><span style=\"top:-2.357em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.0714em\"><span style=\"height:2.5em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2819em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"top:-3.23em\"><span style=\"height:3em\"></span><span style=\"border-bottom-width:0.04em\"></span></span><span style=\"top:-3.5073em\"><span style=\"height:3em\"></span><span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02691em\">w</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3281em\"><span style=\"top:-2.357em;margin-left:-0.0269em;margin-right:0.0714em\"><span style=\"height:2.5em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.1076em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2861em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1.241em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></div><p>Here, <span><span><span><math>{xj}\\{x_j\\}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.0361em;vertical-align:-0.2861em\"></span><span>{</span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2861em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>}</span></span></span></span></span> are interpolation nodes, <span><span><span><math>{fj}\\{f_j\\}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.0361em;vertical-align:-0.2861em\"></span><span>{</span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10764em\">f</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.1076em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2861em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>}</span></span></span></span></span> are the function values at those nodes, and <span><span><span><math>{wj}\\{w_j\\}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.0361em;vertical-align:-0.2861em\"></span><span>{</span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02691em\">w</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0269em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2861em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>}</span></span></span></span></span> are precomputed barycentric weights. When the nodes are well-chosen (e.g. Chebyshev nodes), this formula is stable and accurate even for high-degree polynomials, and requires no explicit basis functions.</p><p>To compute PDE losses, BWLer differentiates the interpolant using:</p><ul>\n<li><a href=\"https://epubs.siam.org/doi/book/10.1137/1.9780898719598\" target=\"_blank\"><em>Spectral derivatives</em></a>, such as FFTs or Chebyshev differentiation matrices.</li>\n<li>Or <a href=\"https://www.colorado.edu/amath/sites/default/files/attached-files/mathcomp_88_fd_formulas.pdf\" target=\"_blank\"><em>finite-difference approximations</em></a>, when better conditioning is needed.</li>\n</ul><p>All operations remain fully differentiable with respect to the node values <span><span><span><math>{fj}\\{f_j\\}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.0361em;vertical-align:-0.2861em\"></span><span>{</span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10764em\">f</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.1076em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2861em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>}</span></span></span></span></span>, so BWLer integrates seamlessly into any physics-informed training loop.</p></details>\n<h2>🎩 BWLer-hatted PINNs: boosting precision with a single extra layer</h2>\n<p>Our first experiment with BWLer kept the neural network, but changed how we used it. Instead of evaluating the MLP everywhere in the domain and relying on autodiff to compute derivatives, we ask the MLP to predict the solution at a fixed grid of interpolation nodes. BWLer then takes over: it interpolates the solution across the domain and computes PDE residuals using spectral derivatives. This yields a <strong>BWLer-hatted PINN</strong> — same MLP, same optimizer, but with representation and differentiation now cleanly decoupled (see Figure 2).</p>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:95%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-07-07-bwler/alg.png\" alt=\"PINN vs BWLer-hatted schematic\"><p><em><strong>Figure 2</strong>: Standard PINN (left) vs. BWLer-hatted PINN (right). BWLer implements forward and derivative computations using spectral routines.</em></p></div>\n<p>Importantly, this setup remains plug-and-play: you can drop BWLER on top of an existing PINN with minimal changes. But this extra polynomial layer leads to large improvements on smooth problems. Across three benchmark PDEs — convection, reaction, and wave equations — BWLer-hatted models reduce relative error by <span><span><span><math>10×10\\times</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>10</span><span>×</span></span></span></span></span> to <span><span><span><math>1800×1800\\times</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>1800</span><span>×</span></span></span></span></span> compared to standard PINNs, with no extra supervision or architectural tuning.</p>\n<p>Why does this work? BWLer-hatting imposes global structure in the way it treats the PDE. Unlike with standard MLPs, where derivative constraints are enforced locally, the interpolant's derivatives tie all points together, encouraging the network to learn globally coherent, smooth solutions. We find empirical support for the smoother convergence behavior: BWLer-hatted models consistently exhibit better-conditioned loss landscapes with lower Hessian eigenvalues.</p>\n<table><thead><tr><th style=\"text-align:left\">PDE</th>\n<th style=\"text-align:center\">MLP</th>\n<th style=\"text-align:center\">BWLer-hatted MLP</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td>Convection</td>\n<td><span><span><span><math>1.14×1001.14 \\times 10^{0}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>1.14</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>×</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"></span><span>1</span><span><span>0</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>0</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></td>\n<td><span><span><span><math>3.91×10−2\\mathbf{3.91 \\times 10^{-2}}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8974em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span><span>3.91</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>×</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>1</span><span><span>0</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>−</span><span>2</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> <br><span style=\"font-size:0.9em;color:#666\">(<span><span><span><math>29.2×29.2\\times</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>29.2</span><span>×</span></span></span></span></span> better)</span></td></tr><tr><td>Reaction</td>\n<td><span><span><span><math>4.02×10−34.02 \\times 10^{-3}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>4.02</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>×</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"></span><span>1</span><span><span>0</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>−</span><span>3</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></td>\n<td><span><span><span><math>3.91×10−4\\mathbf{3.91 \\times 10^{-4}}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8974em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span><span>3.91</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>×</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>1</span><span><span>0</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>−</span><span>4</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> <br><span style=\"font-size:0.9em;color:#666\">(<span><span><span><math>10.3×10.3\\times</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>10.3</span><span>×</span></span></span></span></span> better)</span></td></tr><tr><td>Wave</td>\n<td><span><span><span><math>5.22×10−15.22 \\times 10^{-1}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>5.22</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>×</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"></span><span>1</span><span><span>0</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>−</span><span>1</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></td>\n<td><span><span><span><math>2.88×10−4\\mathbf{2.88 \\times 10^{-4}}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8974em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span><span>2.88</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>×</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>1</span><span><span>0</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>−</span><span>4</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> <br><span style=\"font-size:0.9em;color:#666\">(<span><span><span><math>1800×1800\\times</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>1800</span><span>×</span></span></span></span></span> better)</span></td></tr></tbody></table>\n<p style=\"text-align:center\"><em><strong>Table 1</strong>: BWLer-hatted MLP improves precision (by <span><span><span><math>1010</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>10</span></span></span></span></span>-<span><span><span><math>1800×1800\\times</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>1800</span><span>×</span></span></span></span></span>) on three benchmark PDEs.</em></p>\n<h2>🔬 Explicit BWLer: reaching machine precision by removing the neural network</h2>\n<p>As a proof of concept, we next took the BWLer approach one step further by fully decoupling function representation from derivative computation. In <strong>explicit BWLer</strong>, we treat the solution values at a fixed grid of interpolation nodes as directly learnable parameters. The model becomes a pure polynomial interpolant — a spectral approximation that’s fully differentiable and trained directly against the physics loss. No MLPs, no hidden layers; just node values and fast, precise derivatives.</p>\n<p>This setup eliminates the representational limitations of neural networks and exposes the precision bottleneck sourcing from the poorly-conditioned PDE constraints. We find that we're able to push through these challenges using a heavier-duty, second-order optimizer (in our case, a method called <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.01868\" target=\"_blank\">Nyström-Newton-CG</a>).</p>\n<p>The results are striking. On the same benchmark PDEs, explicit BWLer achieves relative errors as low as <span><span><span><math>10−1210^{-12}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"></span><span>1</span><span><span>0</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>−</span><span>12</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>, outperforming prior PINNs by up to <span><span><span><math>1010×10^{10}\\times</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8974em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>1</span><span><span>0</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>10</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>×</span></span></span></span></span> in accuracy. As far as we know, these are some of the first PINN results to reach near machine precision, even on simple problems over 2D domains.</p>\n<p>Crucially, we note that our results are not time- or parameter-matched comparisons! While explicit BWLer doesn’t yet match traditional solvers in runtime, our results show that machine-precision solutions are possible within the flexible physics-informed framework. We’re optimistic and excited about improving its efficiency and scaling it to more complex problems.</p>\n<table><thead><tr><th style=\"text-align:left\">PDE</th>\n<th style=\"text-align:center\">Literature</th>\n<th style=\"text-align:center\">Explicit BWLer</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td>Convection (<span><span><span><math>c=40c = 40</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span>c</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"font-size:0.9em;color:#666\">(<span><span><span><math>1.43×1081.43 \\times 10^8</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>1.43</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>×</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>8</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> better)</span></td></tr></tbody></table>\n<p style=\"text-align:center\"><em><strong>Table 2</strong>: Explicit BWLer improves precision by up to 10 billion relative MSE on benchmark PDEs. Improvements are relative to the best published values (to our knowledge).</em></p>\n<h2>⚖️ The precision–conditioning tradeoff</h2>\n<p>Explicit BWLer unlocks a new regime of high precision, but it also surfaces a key tension at the heart of physics-informed learning: <strong>higher accuracy often comes at the cost of worse numerical conditioning</strong>.</p>\n<p>Here’s the basic idea. Increasing the number of interpolation nodes makes the model more expressive and reduces approximation error, a hallmark of spectral methods. But there’s a catch: the resulting derivative matrices become more ill-conditioned, especially for high-order PDEs, which slows down optimization and can stall convergence. See Figure 3 for an example.</p>\n<p>To understand this tension, we can break down total error into three components:</p>\n<ul>\n<li><strong>Expressivity error</strong>: how well the interpolant can represent the true solution. Improves with more nodes.</li>\n<li><strong>Bias error</strong>: how well the discretized PDE approximates the true one. Less accurate finite difference schemes introduce more bias than spectral derivatives.</li>\n<li><strong>Optimization error</strong>: how close training gets to the best solution. Tied directly to how well-conditioned the loss landscape is.</li>\n</ul>\n<p>These sources of error often pull in different directions. Spectral derivatives reduce bias but worsen conditioning. Finite differences improve conditioning but limit achievable precision by adding bias. Adding more nodes helps expressivity, but can worsen optimization if we aren't careful.</p>\n<p>What makes BWLer useful here is that this tradeoff becomes explicit. We can swap in finite differences when needed, reduce node count, use heavier-duty optimizers like Nyström-Newton-CG, or warm-start from coarser solutions. These tools give us leverage to navigate the tradeoff in a controllable way, letting us push toward higher precision.</p>\n<p>We suspect (and <a href=\"https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2022/file/46f0114c06524debc60ef2a72769f7a9-Paper-Conference.pdf\" target=\"_blank\">prior work suggests</a>) that standard PINNs face a similar tradeoff — but they’re harder to reason about, because the MLP parameterization affects how derivatives for PDE constraints are computed. This may help explain why training techniques like <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2109.01050\" target=\"_blank\">curriculum learning</a> or <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.10771\" target=\"_blank\">time marching</a> work, and why so many <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2001.04536\" target=\"_blank\">optimization pathologies</a> persist across the literature.</p>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:95%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-07-07-bwler/fig3.png\" alt=\"Precision-conditioning tradeoff in PINNs\"><p><em><strong>Figure 3</strong>: The precision–conditioning tradeoff becomes explicit with BWLer: finite differences improve conditioning (optimization) but adds bias (misspecification).</em></p></div>\n<h2>⚠️ Limitations of BWLer</h2>\n<p>While BWLer enables new levels of precision in scientific ML, it also inherits the known limitations of spectral methods. In particular, when the target solution exhibits discontinuities (e.g. PDEs for shock modeling) or is defined over irregular geometries, the PDE loss becomes significantly more ill-conditioned. We’re still able to match the precision of prior PINN results on these more difficult problems, but doing so requires heavier-duty optimization and much longer training times. For example, on Burgers’ equation and 2D Poisson, our current explicit BWLer setup required <em>hours</em> of wall-clock time to train, compared to just a few minutes for standard MLPs.</p>\n<p>We view these results as a proof of concept — and just the beginning! One natural next step is a hybrid approach, where we start training with a rapidly-converging model (like a standard MLP) and switch to BWLer when higher precision is needed. We also suspect that many existing optimization techniques used to accelerate PINNs, like adaptive sampling or curriculum learning, could be repurposed to make BWLer more efficient.</p>\n<p>See the paper for details, and stay tuned: we’re actively exploring ways to make BWLer more robust and efficient!</p>\n<table><thead><tr><th style=\"text-align:left\">PDE</th>\n<th style=\"text-align:center\">Literature<br>RMSE</th>\n<th style=\"text-align:center\">Explicit BWLer<br>RMSE (Improvement)</th>\n<th style=\"text-align:center\">Explicit BWLer<br>Wall-clock Time</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td>Burgers (1D-C)</td>\n<td><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.08827\" target=\"_blank\">(Hao et al.)</a><br><span><span><span><math>1.33×10−21.33 \\times 10^{-2}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>1.33</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>×</span><span \\times 10^{-3}}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8974em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span><span>4.63</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>×</span><span style=\"font-size:0.9em;color:#666\">(<span><span><span><math>2.87×2.87\\times</math></span><span><span><span style=\"font-size:0.9em;color:red\"><span><span><span><math>100×100\\times</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>100</span><span>×</span></span></span></span></span> slower</span></td></tr><tr><td>Poisson (2D-C)</td>\n<td><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.08827\" target=\"_blank\">(Hao et al.)</a><br><span><span><span><math>1.23×10−21.23 \\times 10^{-2}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>1.23</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>×</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"></span><span>1</span><span><span>0</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>−</span><span>2</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></td>\n<td><span><span><span><math>1.08×10−2\\mathbf{1.08 \\times 10^{-2}}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8974em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span><span>1.08</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>×</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>1</span><span><span>0</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>−</span><span>2</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><br><span style=\"font-size:0.9em;color:#666\">(<span><span><span><math>1.14×1.14\\times</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>1.14</span><span>×</span></span></span></span></span> better)</span></td>\n<td><span style=\"font-size:0.9em;color:red\"><span><span><span><math>100×100\\times</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>100</span><span>×</span></span></span></span></span> slower</span></td></tr></tbody></table>\n<p style=\"text-align:center\"><em><strong>Table 3</strong>: Explicit BWLer can match performance of standard PINNs on PDE problems with sharp solutions or irregular domains, but training takes <em>much</em> longer: when training from scratch, up to <span><span><span><math>100×100\\times</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>100</span><span>×</span></span></span></span></span> longer wall-clock time.</em></p>\n<h2>🔭 Looking ahead</h2>\n<p>BWLer shows that classical numerical methods still have a lot to teach modern machine learning, especially in scientific domains where precision matters. From how models capture local and global structure to how they navigate ill-conditioned losses, we believe there's still enormous room to improve the ML architectures and optimization algorithms we use for scientific modeling. Scientific ML is still in its early days — and BWLer is one step along that path.</p>\n<h3>📈 Near-term directions for BWLer</h3>\n<p>There’s plenty we’re already working on:</p>\n<ul>\n<li><strong>Faster training</strong>: Spectral methods and second-order optimizers are accurate but slow. We're exploring better kernels, preconditioners, and data-efficient training strategies to speed things up.</li>\n<li><strong>Sharper solutions and complex domains</strong>: We're developing techniques to better handle discontinuities and irregular geometries, including adaptive node placement, hybrid methods, and domain decomposition.</li>\n<li><strong>Better generalization</strong>: We're exploring ways to combine BWLer with neural operator-style models to move beyond single-PDE solves. We're hoping to learn data-to-data mappings and to achieve generalization across problem instances.</li>\n</ul>\n<h3>📡 Farther out: beyond axioms?</h3>\n<p>But perhaps the most exciting possibilities lie even farther out.</p>\n<p>PDEs have been a tremendously successful modeling paradigm for over a century, capturing everything from fluid dynamics to quantum mechanics. But can we move beyond them?</p>\n<p>We’ve written on this blog about the <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-11-18-evo-axioms\" target=\"_blank\">limitations of axiomatic knowledge</a> and explored what it might mean to use machine learning to uncover new, non-axiomatic models of the world. What if modern ML could move beyond our human-designed notions of structure in science? Instead of encoding physics into our models, could our models discover their own?</p>\n<p>These questions raise deep challenges: What does generalization even mean when the governing equations themselves are unknown? Can ML systems reveal principles that go beyond anything humans have formalized?</p>\n<p><a href=\"https://openreview.net/pdf?id=FsJ2FdQ19C\" target=\"_blank\">We’ve started exploring this</a>, but we’re just scratching the surface. There’s a ton to do, and we’re excited to keep pushing!</p>\n<h2>Acknowledgements</h2>\n<p>Thank you to Owen Dugan, Roberto Garcia, Junmiao Hu, and Atri Rudra for helpful feedback on this blogpost.</p>\n<h2>🛠️ Code &#x26; Resources</h2>\n<blockquote>\n<p>⏮️ <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-07-07-bwler-p1\" target=\"_blank\">Part 1 of the blog post</a><br>\n📄 <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.23024\" target=\"_blank\">Paper</a><br>\n💻 <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/bwler\" target=\"_blank\">GitHub</a><br>\n📧 Questions? Reach out to <a href=\"mailto:jwl50@stanford.edu\" target=\"_blank\">jwl50@stanford.edu</a></p>\n</blockquote>,TL;DR (click to expand): Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) offer a flexible, mesh-free way to solve PDEs, but standard architectures…,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-07-07-bwler-p2,<a href=\"https://jerrywliu.github.io/\">Jerry Liu*</a>, <a href=\"https://scholar.google.com/citations?hl=en&user=JS3KBYSfsU4C\">Yasa Baig</a>,2026-07-22T18:37:38.411Z,2025-07-07T07:00:00.411Z,[{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-07-07-bwler/banner2.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1536,\"height\":1024},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-07-07-bwler/fig2.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-07-07-bwler/alg.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-07-07-bwler/fig3.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"}],163778663386687488,1211296081845936144,BWLer 🎳 (Part 1): PDEs, PINNs, and the Precision Gap,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-07-07-bwler-p1,<p><strong>TL;DR</strong><br>\nPhysics-informed neural networks (PINNs) offer a flexible, mesh-free way to solve PDEs, but standard architectures struggle with precision. Even on simpler interpolation tasks, without the challenging PDE constraints, we find multi-layer perceptrons (MLPs) consistently plateau at <span><span><span><math>10−810^{-8}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"></span><span>1</span><span><span>0</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>−</span><span>8</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> RMSE, far from float64's machine precision (around <span><span><span><math>10−1610^{-16}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"></span><span>1</span><span><span>0</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>−</span><span>16</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>). We introduce <strong>BWLer</strong>, a drop-in replacement for neural networks that models the PDE solution as a barycentric polynomial interpolant. BWLer can either augment a neural network (<em>BWLer-hat</em>) or fully replace it (<em>explicit BWLer</em>), cleanly separating function representation from derivative computation. This lets us disentangle precision bottlenecks caused by MLPs from those due to PDE conditioning. Across three benchmark PDEs, <strong>BWLer-hatted MLPs</strong> reduce RMSE by up to <span><span><span><math>1800×1800\\times</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>1800</span><span>×</span></span></span></span></span> over standard MLPs. <strong>Explicit BWLer</strong> models can do even better — reaching <span><span><span><math>10−1210^{-12}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"></span><span>1</span><span><span>0</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>−</span><span>12</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> RMSE, up to <strong>10 billion times</strong> improvement over standard MLPs — but they require heavier-duty, second-order optimizers. BWLer exposes a fundamental <strong>precision–conditioning tradeoff</strong>, and gives us tools to navigate it — one step toward building precise, flexible, and scalable scientific ML systems.</p>\n<blockquote>\n<p>⏭️ <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-07-07-bwler-p2\" target=\"_blank\">Part 2 of the blog post</a><br>\n📄 <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.23024\" target=\"_blank\">Paper</a><br>\n💻 <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/bwler\" target=\"_blank\">GitHub</a></p>\n</blockquote>\n<p><strong>Full team</strong>: Jerry Liu, Yasa Baig, Denise Hui Jean Lee, Rajat Vadiraj Dwaraknath, Atri Rudra, Chris Ré</p>\n<p><strong>Corresponding author*</strong>: <a href=\"mailto:jwl50@stanford.edu\" target=\"_blank\">jwl50@stanford.edu</a></p>\n<hr>\n<p>Recently, we've been thinking a lot about how we can get machine learning models to reach <em>high precision</em>. High precision seems to be a fundamental challenge for modern AI's <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.12295\" target=\"_blank\">workhorse architectures and algorithms</a> — and is especially crucial to build ML methods that are useful for scientific applications (e.g. nonlinear phenomena that require lots of time-stepping like modeling <a href=\"https://www.cambridge.org/highereducation/books/turbulence/FD8C5E35E5F1CA850E017461942A59AC\" target=\"_blank\">turbulence</a>). As one small step to that end, we're excited to share <strong>BWLer</strong> (<strong>B</strong>arycentric <strong>W</strong>eight <strong>L</strong>ay<strong>er</strong>), a new architecture for solving PDEs with machine learning. In this first half of a two-part blog post, we'll introduce the physics-informed framework for PDEs, which BWLer builds upon, and we'll focus on the motivation: why standard PINN architectures struggle, and why architecture (not just optimization) deserves more attention. In <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-07-07-bwler-p2\" target=\"_blank\">Part 2</a>, we’ll dive deeper into the BWLer architecture and how we're able to get to (near-)machine-precision PDE solutions using neural networks.</p>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:95%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-07-07-bwler/banner.png\" alt=\"A well-behaved BWLer-hatted MLP aiming precisely at bowling PINNs\"><p><em>A well-behaved BWLer-hatted MLP aiming precisely at bowling PINNs.</em></p></div>\n<h2>🧩 The precision gap in physics-informed learning</h2>\n<p>Machine learning offers a new way to approach PDE solving, with the potential for more scalable and flexible methods. One of the simplest and most elegant frameworks is the <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/1711.10561\" target=\"_blank\">physics-informed neural network (PINN)</a>. This approach represents the PDE solution with a neural network and enforces the equation as a loss function, which can be computed via auto-differentiation and minimized using ML optimizers. Unlike standard numerical methods, PINNs require no meshing and can naturally handle irregular geometries. PINNs provide a unified interface for many PDE problems, and their flexibility has made them a widely explored tool in scientific ML.</p>\n<p>At the same time, PINNs have well-documented limitations. The main issue is their <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.07218\" target=\"_blank\">failure to converge to high precision</a> — a critical requirement for many scientific applications. PINNs can be <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2308.08468\" target=\"_blank\">sensitive to hyperparameters</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2205.14249\" target=\"_blank\">slow to train</a>, and often <a href=\"https://academic.oup.com/imamat/article/89/1/143/7680268\" target=\"_blank\">struggle to match the accuracy of classical numerical methods</a>. Although conceptually appealing, many practitioners have concluded that <a href=\"https://www.understandingai.org/p/i-got-fooled-by-ai-for-science-hypeheres\" target=\"_blank\">PINNs remain unreliable in practice</a>.</p>\n<p>Prior work has largely focused on the optimization challenges stemming from the PDE: researchers have proposed new <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.01868\" target=\"_blank\">optimizers</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2104.06217\" target=\"_blank\">loss functions</a>, and training techniques (e.g. <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2109.01050\" target=\"_blank\">curriculum learning</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.10771\" target=\"_blank\">time marching</a>) to improve convergence. But we argue there’s a deeper question at play: are these precision bottlenecks inherent to the PDE optimization, or are they a consequence of the neural network architectures we use to represent PDE solutions?</p>\n<h2>🚧 Multi-layer perceptrons (MLPs) hit a precision wall</h2>\n<p>To understand where the precision ceiling comes from, we first removed the PDE entirely and asked a simpler question: can a neural network just interpolate a smooth 1D function precisely (i.e. to machine precision)?</p>\n<p>Surprisingly, MLPs seem to struggle even on basic interpolation tasks! For example, fitting <span><span><span><math>sin⁡(4x)\\sin(4x)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>sin</span><span>(</span><span>4</span><span>x</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span>, we find that MLPs consistently stall around <span><span><span><math>10−810^{-8}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"></span><span>1</span><span><span>0</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>−</span><span>8</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> relative error — eight orders of magnitude worse than float64's machine epsilon. Furthermore, scaling the network’s width and depth (by up to <span><span><span><math>1000×1000\\times</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>1000</span><span>×</span></span></span></span></span> more parameters) improves performance by just 1–2 orders of magnitude, still far from machine precision.</p>\n<p>In contrast, polynomial interpolants (a classical method with <a href=\"https://people.maths.ox.ac.uk/trefethen/ATAP/first6.pdf\" target=\"_blank\">decades of theory</a>) reach machine precision when trained with gradient descent with just tens of parameters. This suggests a core bottleneck: MLPs themselves are a poor fit for high-precision approximation, even before any PDE constraints are added.</p>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:95%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-07-07-bwler/fig1.png\" alt=\"MLP vs. BWLer-hatted MLP precision comparison\"><p><em>Multi-layer perceptrons (MLPs) plateau around <span><span><span><math>10−810^{-8}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"></span><span>1</span><span><span>0</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>−</span><span>8</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> relative error on 1D interpolation. BWLer-hatted MLP improves relative error by <span><span><span><math>10,000×10,000\\times</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8389em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span>10</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>000</span><span>×</span></span></span></span></span>.</em></p></div>\n<h2>🎳 BWLer: Barycentric Weight Layer</h2>\n<p>Given polynomials perform so well on interpolation, why not directly incorporate them into a physics-informed model? That's exactly what our BWLer architecture is! Essentially, BWLer implements a <a href=\"https://epubs.siam.org/doi/book/10.1137/1.9780898719598\" target=\"_blank\">spectral method</a> into the auto-differentiable, physics-informed framework for PDEs, using <em>polynomial interpolation</em> as the core building block. BWLer supports two modes:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>In a <strong>BWLer-hatted MLP</strong>, BWLer acts like an extra layer atop a standard neural network.</li>\n<li>In <strong>explicit BWLer</strong>, BWLer replaces the neural network entirely and becomes an alternative architecture to MLPs.</li>\n</ul>\n<p>This architecture shift yields dramatic improvements. We'll discuss the results in more detail in <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-07-07-bwler-p2\" target=\"_blank\">Part 2</a>, but as a sneak peek, across three standard PDE benchmarks:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>BWLer-hatted MLPs reduce RMSE by up to <span><span><span><math>1800×1800\\times</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>1800</span><span>×</span></span></span></span></span>.</li>\n<li>Explicit BWLer reaches up to <strong><span><span><span><math>10−1210^{-12}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"></span><span>1</span><span><span>0</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>−</span><span>12</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></strong> RMSE — almost <strong><span><span><span><math>1010×10^{10}\\times</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8974em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>1</span><span><span>0</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>10</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>×</span></span></span></span></span> better</strong> than standard MLPs.</li>\n</ul>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:95%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-07-07-bwler/bwler_vs_mlp.png\" alt=\"MLP vs. BWLer precision comparison\"><p><em><strong>Sneak peek</strong>: on PDEs, BWLer outperforms standard MLPs — by up to 10 billion times relative MSE!</em></p></div>\n<h2>⏭️ What’s next in Part 2</h2>\n<p>So far, we’ve looked at the motivation behind BWLer, and seen that even basic function approximation is a challenge for standard PINNs. In <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-07-07-bwler-p2\" target=\"_blank\">Part 2</a> of this post, we’ll dive deeper into the technical details:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>How BWLer actually works under the hood</li>\n<li>What makes it more precise than traditional MLPs</li>\n<li>How it exposes and helps navigate a precision-conditioning tradeoff</li>\n</ul>\n<p>We think there’s a lot more to explore here: from building better ML architectures for modeling PDEs, to designing systems that achieve higher precision, to maybe even <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-11-18-evo-axioms\" target=\"_blank\">moving beyond PDEs to fundamentally new tools for scientific modeling</a>. BWLer is just a first step, and we’re excited to see what others might build from these ideas! Whether you’re trying BWLer out, adapting it to new problems, or just thinking about similar questions, we'd love to hear from you — please reach out and let us know what you're thinking about!</p>\n<h2>🛠️ Code &#x26; Resources</h2>\n<blockquote>\n<p>⏭️ <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-07-07-bwler-p2\" target=\"_blank\">Part 2 of the blog post</a><br>\n📄 <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.23024\" target=\"_blank\">Paper</a><br>\n💻 <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/bwler\" target=\"_blank\">GitHub</a><br>\n📧 Questions? Reach out to <a href=\"mailto:jwl50@stanford.edu\" target=\"_blank\">jwl50@stanford.edu</a></p>\n</blockquote>,TL;DR Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) offer a flexible, mesh-free way to solve PDEs, but standard architectures struggle with…,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-07-07-bwler-p1,<a href=\"https://jerrywliu.github.io/\">Jerry Liu*</a>, <a href=\"https://scholar.google.com/citations?hl=en&user=JS3KBYSfsU4C\">Yasa Baig</a>,2026-07-22T18:37:38.410Z,2025-07-07T07:00:00.216Z,[{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-07-07-bwler/banner.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1536,\"height\":1024},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-07-07-bwler/fig1.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-07-07-bwler/bwler_vs_mlp.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"}],163778663386687488,1211296081845936145,Weaver: Closing the Generation-Verification Gap with Weak Verifiers,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-06-18-weaver,<p><strong>TL;DR</strong> Large language models often generate correct answers but struggle to reliably distinguish their correct responses from incorrect ones, a costly challenge known as the <strong>generation-verification gap</strong>. Weaver closes this gap without relying on expensive frontier models. Instead, it aggregates multiple smaller, weak verifiers (like reward models and LM judges) without labeled data, reaching accuracy comparable to o3-mini on explored math, science, and general reasoning tasks (MATH500, GPQA Diamond, and MMLU Pro, respectively).\nCrucially, Weaver further distills the ensemble of weak verifiers into a <em>compact 400M model</em> that fits on your laptop, preserving up to <strong>98.7%</strong> of the ensemble performance while <em>reducing verification inference FLOPs by up to 99.97%</em>. Weaver isn't about building bigger models, it's about smarter aggregation. Weaver efficiently transforms multiple weak verifiers into a single, powerful verification engine.</p>\n<blockquote>\n<p>📄 <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.18203\" target=\"_blank\">Paper</a><br>\n💻 <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/scaling-verification\" target=\"_blank\">GitHub</a><br>\n🤗 <a href=\"https://huggingface.co/collections/hazyresearch/weaver-683798010b39c9653ddb9bd8\" target=\"_blank\">Datasets and Models</a></p>\n</blockquote>\n<p><em>Full team</em>: Jon Saad‑Falcon*, E. Kelly Buchanan*, Mayee F. Chen*, Tzu-Heng Huang, Brendan McLaughlin, Tanvir Bhathal, Shang Zhu, Ben Athiwaratkun, Frederic Sala, Scott Linderman, Azalia Mirhoseini, Christopher Ré</p>\n<hr>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:80%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-06-18-weaver/Figure_1.png\" alt=\"Weaver overview diagram\"><p><em><strong>Figure 1</strong>: The Beaver Weaver</em></p></div>\n<h2>🔥 The Problem: The Generation-Verification Gap</h2>\n<p>Here's a frustrating reality: your LM often already \"knows\" the correct answer to your question (see <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.21787\" target=\"_blank\">LLMonkeys</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2408.03314\" target=\"_blank\">Scaling TTC</a>, and <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2409.15254\" target=\"_blank\">Archon</a>). If you repeatedly query the LM—for example, 100 times—it'll usually generate the correct answer at least once. But the problem is, <em>it doesn't know which of its responses is correct.</em></p>\n<p>To demonstrate this clearly, we ran an experiment using Llama 3.3 70B on the GPQA Diamond dataset—a challenging benchmark of PhD-level science questions for evaluating reasoning performance. We generated 100 responses per query and analyzed the results using three different methods:</p>\n<ul>\n<li><strong>Pass@100 (Oracle)</strong>: 82.8% — Checks if the ground-truth answer is among the 100 generated answers, without needing to identify which one.</li>\n<li><strong>Majority Voting</strong>: 45.5% — Selects the most frequently generated answer.</li>\n<li><strong>First Sample</strong>: 42.9% — Simply takes the first generated answer.</li>\n</ul>\n<p><strong>Note:</strong> While GPQA Diamond uses multiple choice format, the PhD-level scientific reasoning required makes even oracle performance challenging, as evidenced by the 82.8% ceiling rather than near-perfect scores.</p>\n<p>These results highlight a significant gap—<strong>37.3%</strong>—between the model's potential capability (oracle accuracy) and what straightforward selection methods (Majority Voting on the generated answers) actually deliver. <em>While this isn't the largest gap we observe, it illustrates how much quality is lost in selection alone.</em></p>\n<p>This discrepancy is known as the <strong>generation-verification gap</strong>: the model generates correct answers but struggles to reliably identify them. It's like having a student who can guess the correct answer but struggles to confidently explain why they wrote it or consistently identify it among distractors (see <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.02674\" target=\"_blank\">Mind the Gap</a>).</p>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-06-18-weaver/Figure_2.png\" alt=\"Generation-verification gap across datasets\"><p><em><strong>Figure 2</strong>: Verification Accuracy Lags Behind Generation Capacity<br>Across Models and Tasks</em></p></div>\n<p>This gap exists across many tasks:</p>\n<table><thead><tr><th align=\"center\"><strong>Model</strong></th><th align=\"center\"><strong>Dataset</strong></th><th align=\"center\"><strong>Oracle<br>(Pass@100)</strong></th><th align=\"center\"><strong>Majority<br>Voting</strong></th><th align=\"center\"><strong>Generation<br>Verification<br>Gap</strong></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td align=\"center\"><strong>Llama 3.3<br>70B Instruct</strong></td><td align=\"center\">GPQA<br>Diamond</td><td align=\"center\">82.8%</td><td align=\"center\">45.5%</td><td align=\"center\"><strong>37.3%</strong></td></tr><tr><td align=\"center\"></td><td align=\"center\">MATH500</td><td align=\"center\">98.6%</td><td align=\"center\">82.3%</td><td align=\"center\"><strong>16.3%</strong></td></tr><tr><td align=\"center\"></td><td align=\"center\">MMLU Pro</td><td align=\"center\">92.0%</td><td align=\"center\">74.4%</td><td align=\"center\"><strong>17.6%</strong></td></tr><tr><td align=\"center\"><strong>Llama 3.1<br>8B Instruct</strong></td><td align=\"center\">GPQA<br>Diamond</td><td align=\"center\">95.2%</td><td align=\"center\">30.7%</td><td align=\"center\"><strong>64.5%</strong></td></tr><tr><td align=\"center\"></td><td align=\"center\">MATH500</td><td align=\"center\">99.2%</td><td align=\"center\">69.8%</td><td align=\"center\"><strong>29.4%</strong></td></tr><tr><td align=\"center\"></td><td align=\"center\">MMLU Pro</td><td align=\"center\">89.0%</td><td align=\"center\">56.5%</td><td align=\"center\"><strong>32.5%</strong></td></tr></tbody></table>\n<h2>🧠 Weak Verifiers, Strong Together</h2>\n<h3>⚖️ The Wisdom of Imperfect Verifiers</h3>\n<p>At the heart of Weaver is a simple idea with surprisingly broad power: when you can't trust any single verifier, trust the <strong>pattern of their agreement</strong>.</p>\n<p>Like a jury, individual verifiers may be noisy or biased, but their collective agreement tends to be accurate. To see this, we used 30+ verifiers, namely reward models from 7B–72B, and LM judges from 27B–72B, and had them judge responses to GPQA Diamond questions.</p>\n<p>Each verifier scored candidate responses, and we recorded which response each verifier judged as best.</p>\n<ul>\n<li><strong>Individual verifier accuracy</strong>: each verifier correctly judged samples with 43-62% accuracy—barely better than flipping a coin</li>\n<li><strong>High verifier agreement</strong>: on samples where 20+ verifiers agreed, their collective decision had 91% accuracy</li>\n<li><strong>Low verifier agreement</strong>: on samples where verifiers were split evenly, their collective decision had 48% accuracy, reverting back to random chance</li>\n</ul>\n<blockquote>\n<p>📘 Example:<br>\n<em>Question:</em> Two quantum states with lifetimes of 10⁻⁹ and 10⁻⁸ seconds need to be resolved. What could be their energy difference?<br>\n<em>Verifier votes:</em></p>\n<ul>\n<li><strong>Answer B (correct):</strong> 18 votes</li>\n<li>Answer A: 7</li>\n<li>Answer C: 3</li>\n<li>Answer D: 2</li>\n</ul>\n<p><em>Weaver's confidence:</em> 89%</p>\n</blockquote>\n<p>The key insight: <strong>verifier agreement is a strong signal of verifier correctness</strong>. We don't need to know which verifiers are accurate beforehand—their agreement patterns tell us. Even on challenging questions, it's uncommon for many weak verifiers to confidently agree on the same wrong answer. Weaver builds on this insight by using a statistical aggregation technique that learns which verifiers are most reliable from their rates of agreement. By weighting verifiers according to their estimated reliability, Weaver goes beyond simple consensus, as in the example above, and captures more sophisticated patterns—turning noisy judgments into confident decisions.</p>\n<h2>🚀 Why This Matters Now</h2>\n<p>New model ecosystems and statistical tools make this approach feasible at scale.</p>\n<h3>🤖 The Explosion of Available Verifiers</h3>\n<p>We're swimming in models and tools that can act as verifiers:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 <strong>Reward models:</strong> Hundreds on HuggingFace, trained on human preferences and reasoning generations (see <a href=\"https://huggingface.co/spaces/allenai/reward-bench\" target=\"_blank\">RewardBenchV2</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2501.03124\" target=\"_blank\">ProcessRewardBench</a>)</li>\n<li>🧑‍⚖️ <strong>LM judges:</strong> Practically every chat LM can evaluate answers with binary verdicts (see <a href=\"https://huggingface.co/spaces/lmarena-ai/chatbot-arena-leaderboard\" target=\"_blank\">ChatBotArena</a>)</li>\n<li>🧮 <strong>Specialized critics:</strong> Math checkers, code validators, safety filters (see <a href=\"https://lean-lang.org/\" target=\"_blank\">LeanProver</a>)</li>\n<li>🧪 <strong>Unit tests:</strong> Manually or synthetically generated binary checks (see <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.18181\" target=\"_blank\">Yang et al. (2024)</a>)</li>\n</ul>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-06-18-weaver/Figure_3.png\" alt=\"Verifier types overview\"><p><em><strong>Figure 3</strong>: Growth of Open-Source RMs and LM Judges</em></p></div>\n<h3>🧬 Weak Supervision Comes to Weak Verifiers</h3>\n<p>So, we've seen an explosion in the number of available verifiers. But this raises a natural question: how should we combine their outputs—is a simple unweighted average sufficient?</p>\n<p>While naive aggregation can perform better than the first sample and majority voting baselines, they often fail to capture the varying reliability of individual verifiers. As a result, we find that weighted aggregation—such as ensembling over the top-K models or fitting a logistic regression model to combine outputs—significantly outperforms naive aggregation.</p>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-06-18-weaver/MV_vs_Naive_vs_Weighted_Fig.png\" alt=\"Verifier types overview\"><p><em><strong>Figure 4</strong>: Weighted Verifier Ensembles Outperform Naive Verifier Ensembles</em></p></div>\n<p>However, determining the optimal weights for aggregation typically requires large amounts of labeled data, which can be time-consuming and expensive to obtain. How can we learn the weights without labeled data?</p>\n<p>This is where <strong>Weak Supervision</strong> comes in—a classical statistical framework for aggregating the outputs of multiple noisy \"voters\" (e.g., heuristics, models, crowdworkers) in settings where ground-truth labels are scarce. Originally developed for data labeling (e.g. <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/1711.10160\" target=\"_blank\">Snorkel</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/1810.02840\" target=\"_blank\">MeTaL</a>, <a href=\"https://rss.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.2307/2346806\" target=\"_blank\">Dawid-Skene Model</a>),  Weak Supervision infers true labels by examining the rates of agreement among noisy voters. These techniques have since been successfully adapted to LLM settings (e.g. <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.07402\" target=\"_blank\">Ask-Me-Anything</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.04692\" target=\"_blank\">Smoothie</a>), where LLM outputs serve as noisy signals of response correctness.</p>\n<p>These recent developments create a timely opportunity: we have an abundance of imperfect verifiers, increasingly cheap inference costs, and well-established statistical aggregation tools. Weaver brings these elements together, and its capabilities are poised to only grow stronger as the ecosystem of models continues to expand.</p>\n<h2>⚙️ How Weaver Works: From Noisy Signals to Consensus</h2>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-06-18-weaver/Figure_4.png\" alt=\"Weaver pipeline\"><p><em><strong>Figure 5</strong>: Weaver Framework</em></p></div>\n<h3>🧊 Step 1: Binarize (and Drop)</h3>\n<p>Before we can combine verifier outputs, we need them on the same scale.</p>\n<p>Verifiers come in many forms—reward models (which output continuous scores), LM judges (which give discrete ratings), and rule-based critics (e.g., math or code checkers). Weaver first normalizes and binarizes all verifier signals, such that their outputs reflect a simple question: Is this response likely correct or not?</p>\n<p>This makes different verifier types comparable and lets us treat them equally in the next step.</p>\n<p>But not all verifiers are helpful. Some are too noisy, too biased, or too redundant. So Weaver drops them with a lightweight filtering pass, customized per dataset:</p>\n<ul>\n<li><strong>Adaptive marginal filtering</strong>: Weaver drops verifiers with extreme positive/negative rates based on dataset difficulty. For easier datasets (>80% correct samples), it removes pessimistic verifiers that rarely predict positive. For harder datasets (&#x3C;20% correct samples), it filters optimistic verifiers that predict positive too often. For medium-difficulty datasets, both extremes are pruned.</li>\n<li><strong>Smart binarization thresholds</strong>: Using just 1% of the data as a labeled development set, Weaver estimates optimal thresholds for converting continuous reward model scores to binary signals—maximizing the signal-to-noise ratio rather than using naive 0.5 cutoffs.</li>\n</ul>\n<p>By effectively binarizing verifier signals and filtering out low-quality signals, we have a cleaner ensemble to aggregate.</p>\n<h3>🧼 Step 2: Denoise with Weak Supervision</h3>\n<p>Once we have a binarized matrix of verifier votes, the next challenge is <em>assigning trust</em>—determining how much weight to give each verifier's opinion based on their estimated accuracy. Weaver tackles this by modeling response correctness as a latent variable, using tools from weak supervision to estimate how accurate each verifier is—without any access to ground-truth labels.</p>\n<p>This is formalized as a <strong>latent variable graphical model</strong>, where:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>The correctness of a response (<strong>latent variable</strong>) is predicted from multiple binary verifier votes (<strong>observed variables</strong>)</li>\n<li>A <strong>conditional independence assumption</strong> is made: verifiers behave independently given the true label</li>\n<li>Weaver then applies a <a href=\"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Method_of_moments_(statistics)\" target=\"_blank\"><strong>method-of-moments estimator</strong></a> that matches second-order statistics of verifier co-voting patterns to infer each verifier's true/false positive rates, based on the conditional independence assumption</li>\n</ul>\n<p>These inferred accuracy parameters become the weights used in aggregation—quantifying how much to trust each verifier based solely on unlabeled agreement structure.</p>\n<p>Crucially, this estimation remains robust across domains, model types, and verifier formats—even when verifiers vary widely in calibration, score ranges, or quality.</p>\n<p>For deeper technical details on weak supervision methods, see <a href=\"https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper/2016/hash/6709e8d64a5f47269ed5cea9f625f7ab-Abstract.html\" target=\"_blank\">Data Programming</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/1711.10160\" target=\"_blank\">Snorkel</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/1810.02840\" target=\"_blank\">MeTaL</a>, and recent LLM applications like <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.07402\" target=\"_blank\">Ask-Me-Anything</a> and <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.04692\" target=\"_blank\">Smoothie</a>.</p>\n<h3>📊 Step 3: Aggregate Smartly</h3>\n<p>With verifier weights in hand, Weaver computes a posterior probability that each candidate answer is correct. This final score represents the model's belief over correctness—a soft estimate of the latent label behind noisy verifier observations.</p>\n<p>Unlike majority vote or averaging, this aggregation step is grounded in <strong>probabilistic inference</strong>:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>It combines scores using learned <strong>verifier reliabilities</strong></li>\n<li>It naturally downweights low-quality or redundant signals</li>\n<li>And it produces a <strong>confidence score</strong> for each response that is both statistically principled and empirically calibrated</li>\n</ul>\n<p>This latent-variable-based aggregation makes Weaver more than just an ensemble—it's a full probabilistic verifier that adapts to verifier diversity, dataset difficulty, and scaling constraints.</p>\n<h2>📊 The Payoff: Shrinking the Generation-Verification Gap</h2>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-06-18-weaver/Figure_5.png\" alt=\"Benchmark performance\"><p><em><strong>Figure 6</strong>: Weaver's Verification Boost Across Benchmarks as Generations Increase</em></p></div>\n<p>Weaver helps us close the generation-verification gap across four challenging benchmarks—MATH500, MMLU Pro, MMLU College, and GPQA Diamond. Weaver boosts first-sample accuracy by <strong>11.2-27.8 percentage points</strong> across tasks, matching what only the most expensive frontier models can do.</p>\n<p>As the number of generations increases, naive methods plateau early. Majority vote on the generations and unweighted ensembling of verifier outputs barely budge past 20–30 generations. In contrast, Weaver continues climbing—<strong>pushing 72.1% on GPQA Diamond</strong>, and closing in on the oracle Pass@100 upper bound. This ability to scale with generation count is key for high-stakes domains like math and science reasoning.</p>\n<p>Weaver reaches <strong>87.7% average accuracy across all tasks explored</strong>—<em>1.0% above o3-mini, a closed, post-trained reasoning model.</em> It also outperforms GPT-4o (69.0%) and Claude 3.7 Sonnet (70.4%)—<em>without any parameter updates, preference tuning, or alignment data.</em></p>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-06-18-weaver/Figure_6.png\" alt=\"Model comparison\"><p><em><strong>Figure 7</strong>: Weaver Outperforms Baseline Verification Methods<br>and Shrinks Gap with Frontier LMs</em></p></div>\n<h2>🪶 Making It Practical: Distilling Verification to 400M Parameters</h2>\n<blockquote>\n<p>✅ One 400M verifier = 98.7% of ensemble accuracy<br>\n🚀 Fits on a laptop. No human labels needed for training.</p>\n</blockquote>\n<div style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-06-18-weaver/Figure_7.png\" alt=\"Distillation process\"><p><em><strong>Figure 8</strong>: Distilling the Weaver Verifier Ensemble to a Compact Cross-Encoder</em></p></div>\n<p>While Weaver's ensemble verifier achieves strong performance, it incurs significant computational overhead—querying 30+ models per response is not always feasible in latency- or resource-constrained settings.</p>\n<p>To address this, we distill the full verifier ensemble into a single 400M-parameter cross-encoder. This model is trained to predict Weaver's final verification scores directly from the query and candidate response, treating ensemble outputs as soft supervision. No human annotations are required to train the cross-encoder.</p>\n<p>This approach yields a verifier that:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>Retains up to <strong>98.7%</strong> of the ensemble's selection accuracy</li>\n<li>Reduces verification inference compute by <strong>99.97%</strong> compared to Weaver</li>\n<li>Outperforms majority voting by <strong>23.2 percentage points</strong></li>\n<li>Runs efficiently on a <strong>single H100</strong> or smaller consumer-grade GPU</li>\n</ul>\n<p>We emphasize that the generation model remains entirely frozen.
