To win the race, we need the ability to flexibly deploy our talent–and trust them in their bets!</p>\n<p><strong>There are already bright spots in Industry</strong></p>\n<div style=\"display:flex;justify-content:center;align-items:center\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-03-25-ai-usa/zuck.png\" alt=\"Mark Zuckerberg\" style=\"width:40%\"></div>\n<p>There are a few people who I think deserve special praise. </p>\n<ul>\n<li>\n<p>Elon is very popular in my house. He is our best chance to go to Mars, and my kids love watching SpaceX launches together. When he took up the mantle of open source AI, I was thrilled. I subscribed to Grok (and everything else!). I hope he pushes this and builds more community, he can do it! </p>\n</li>\n<li>\n<p>Meta and Mark Zuckerberg made a commitment to open source and the Llama models that put American opensource in the game and started the wave our friends at Hugging Face started! I also still love his above July 4th post (maybe that title robot is already here?!), which is also popular in my house.  We need more great leaders with technical vision. We have so many more form factors for intelligence to make sure the world runs on American AI rails. </p>\n</li>\n<li>\n<p>I’m personally grateful to Vipul Ved Prakash, the CEO of Together, and Ce Zhang the CTO who lead with many others building the cloud for open source AI – and believed in it when it sounded insane to most people. Now, we’re at the cusp of helping to build that American foundation–shill or money where my mouth is? Let’s make it unambiguous: try <a href=\"https://chat.together.ai/\" target=\"_blank\">the chat app</a>! </p>\n</li>\n</ul>\n<p><strong>Open Source provides a way forward</strong></p>\n<p>We need a holistic AI effort from academia, industry, and the US government to have the best shot at a freer, better educated, and healthier world. I’m a mega bull on the US and open source AI. Maybe we’re cooking something bigger… stay tuned or contact us.</p>\n<p>Our Discord channel: <a href=\"https://discord.gg/mvbk62T7CC\" target=\"_blank\">https://discord.gg/mvbk62T7CC</a></p>\n<div style=\"display:flex;justify-content:center\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-03-25-ai-usa/mega-bull.png\" alt=\"Mega Bull on US and Open Source AI\" style=\"width:50%\"></div>\n<div>\n<hr>\n<ol>\n<li id=\"user-content-fn-1\">The author has been involved in several companies and projects that are mentioned here. You can view this as being a shill or that I put my money and time where my mouth is. Either is fine with me, but you should be aware of it.  Also, care with AI’s future is warranted, but if you’re worried about AI launching nukes, get rid of the nukes, not the technology that might cure cancer, educate our children. Fwiw, I think nuclear power is great.<a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-03-25-ai-usa#fnref-1\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">↩</a></li>\n<li id=\"user-content-fn-2\">Open source models (including Chinese) that run on American services benefit all of us. Soon, we need even more compelling American models to keep pace. Putting our heads in the sand about their models, any of which quickly commoditize, is not a way to run faster. Using Chinese services is fraught.<a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-03-25-ai-usa#fnref-2\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">↩</a></li>\n</ol>\n</div>,Which do you want? Awesome and free surfer AIs? Dreary coal-shoveling AIs? We’ve been given a machine that turns energy into intelligence…,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-03-25-ai-usa,<a href='https://cs.stanford.edu/~chrismre/'>Chris Ré</a>,2026-07-22T18:37:38.405Z,2025-03-24T07:00:00.892Z,[{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-03-25-ai-usa/free-surfer.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":512,\"height\":512},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-03-25-ai-usa/coal-shoveling.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-03-25-ai-usa/muga.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-03-25-ai-usa/zuck.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-03-25-ai-usa/mega-bull.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"}],163778663386687488,1211296081845936150,BASED ✌️: our one year retrospective,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-03-24-based-retro,<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:60%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-03-24-based-retro/retro.jpeg\">A walk down memory lane.</figure>\n<p>It’s been a bit over a year since we released BASED ✌️ at NeurIPS 2023 and we’ve been humbled by the response! This post is a quick retrospective of what’s stood out to us — beyond learning that using the word “BASED” can get you blacklisted on Twitter...</p>\n<p><strong>TL;DR</strong>: Over the past year, BASED ✌️ quietly reshaped the landscape of efficient architectures. Its fingerprints are all over today’s fastest and most capable efficient LMs — even when they go by different names (Mamba-v2, RWKV-v5/v6, MetaLA, MiniMax, Liger attention). In this post, we reflect on what made BASED work and how its ideas spread.</p>\n<p><strong><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.18668\" target=\"_blank\">BASED Paper</a></strong> with Sabri*, Michael*, Aman, Silas, Dylan, James, Atri, and Chris!</p>\n<hr>\n<p>Today's Transformer-based language models are just way too inefficient and a key question is whether we can get our AI capabilities using asymptotically cheaper modeling choices. Our work on BASED tackles this objective. As a reminder, BASED  is a high-quality, efficient recurrent architecture built from two key principles:</p>\n<ol>\n<li>a <em>short</em> mixing operation (e.g., convolution, sliding window attention) to precisely compute the interactions between locally spaced, nearby words in text, and</li>\n<li>a linear attention with <em>large state sizes</em> to approximately compute the expensive interactions between far apart words</li>\n</ol>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-03-24-based-retro/based.png\">BASED ✌️ overview.</figure>\n<h2>Key advances of BASED</h2>\n<figure style=\"float:right;width:50%;margin-left:1em;margin-bottom:1em\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-03-24-based-retro/hype.png\" alt=\"Hype headline\"></figure>\n<p>While the BASED recipe is simple, we needed to get a lot of tricky pieces right to make it work. <strong>We had to map the true efficiency frontier — and cut through the <em>hype</em>:</strong> This year, headlines like the one on the right <em>“efficient models outperform expensive Transformers everywhere”</em> created excitement, but also a lot of <a href=\"https://huggingface.co/blog/infini-attention\" target=\"_blank\">confusion</a> in the field. Our <a href=\"https://openreview.net/forum?id=e93ffDcpH3\" target=\"_blank\">No free lunch studies</a> helped add clarity — showing where efficient models shine, and where the tradeoffs really are.</p>\n<figure style=\"float:right;width:40%;margin-left:1em;margin-bottom:1em\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-03-24-based-retro/frontier.png\" alt=\"Hype headline\"></figure>\n<p>In particular, we showed: <strong>there is a fundamental tradeoff between the amount of information a model can recall from its context during inference and the amount of memory it utilizes during inference, called its \"state size\".</strong> Some models are better than others at using limited state. Transformers use massive state sizes making it trivial to recall info, gated state space models struggle in recall quality compared to alternatives, and BASED expanded the Pareto frontier.</p>\n<p>The whole game of efficient models is figuring out how to use limited state as effectively as possible and many have since built on our work to push even further. We’ve been thrilled to see our tools for evaluating model recall abilities – like the <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/zoology\" target=\"_blank\">MQAR synthetic</a> and <a href=\"https://huggingface.co/collections/hazyresearch/evaporate-suite-67e218d4175b4ffc0a870501\" target=\"_blank\">EVAPORATE task suite</a> – become the <strong>de-facto evaluation standards</strong> in both academia and industry’s work on pushing this Pareto frontier for efficient models.</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:70%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-03-24-based-retro/mqar.png\">A few architectures &#x26; teams that used MQAR this year!</figure>\n<p>Once we cleaned up our understanding -- state size is the true fundamental tradeoff -- it became clear how to push forward with the BASED design. There were two key principles we used:</p>\n<figure style=\"float:right;width:50%;margin-left:1em;margin-bottom:1em\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-03-24-based-retro/data.png\" alt=\"Hype headline\"></figure>\n<ul>\n<li>\n<p><strong>We built architectures from the data up!</strong> We observed and exploited the power-law distribution in language. Nearby words are often related — that’s where the precise local mixers in BASED help. Far-apart words may be related too, but infrequently — that’s where global approximate linear attention comes in.</p>\n<p>BASED proved that efficient doesn’t have to mean weird. Before BASED, many efficient modeling efforts relied on choices like gated long convolutions (<a href=\"https://www.together.ai/blog/stripedhyena-7b\" target=\"_blank\">Together’s StripedHyena</a> and <a href=\"https://www.rwkv.com/\" target=\"_blank\">RWKVs</a>) or state space models (<a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2312.00752\" target=\"_blank\">Mambas</a> and <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.19427\" target=\"_blank\">Deepmind’s Griffin</a>) with complex initializations and hard-to-optimize inductive biases, or <em>large</em> sliding window attentions which are hard to make hardware efficient (<a href=\"https://mistral.ai/\" target=\"_blank\">Mistral</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.07522\" target=\"_blank\">Microsoft Samba</a>). BASED showed that <strong>familiar attention-like primitives suffice</strong> for both high quality and efficiency. This was great, since the community has already spent a decade studying attention!</p>\n</li>\n<li>\n<p><strong>We built architectures from the hardware up!</strong> Prior architectures like Mamba and Griffin offered the quality, but ran into efficiency bottlenecks since they require <a href=\"https://sustcsonglin.github.io/blog/2024/deltanet-2/\" target=\"_blank\">tensor-core-unfriendly parallel scans</a>. A lot of AI tries to take high-quality ideas and make them run fast — but that’s a tricky proposition. In our work, we were excited about flipping that status quo and starting from what the hardware prefers. This allowed us to use <span><span><span><math>8×8 \\times</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>8</span><span>×</span></span></span></span></span> the state size of Mamba, while still running at <span><span><span><math>5×5\\times</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>5</span><span>×</span></span></span></span></span> the hardware utilization1</p>\n<p>A year later, our BASED kernels remain the <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/ThunderKittens/blob/main/kernels/based/lin_attn_h100.cu\" target=\"_blank\">fastest linear attention kernels we know of</a>! We demonstrated how to get linear attention to run fast on modern hardware by:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>Leveraging advanced hardware features: H100 WGMMA instructions alleviate pressure on precious register memory by allowing us to compute matrix multiplies directly from shared memory. H100 TMA instructions help us efficiently load/store data from/to HBM.</li>\n<li>Choosing hardware-efficient algorithms: We approximated the softmax kernel using the first 2 terms of a Taylor expansion of attention’s softmax <span><span><span><math>exp⁡(x)\\exp(x)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>exp</span><span>(</span><span>x</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span>. This structure gives great data reuse: polynomial terms share data across terms, letting us grow the state size while keeping HBM reads minimal. To put this another way, as the number of terms increases --- e.g., in <span><span><span><math>exp⁡(x)=[1,x,x2/2!,...]\\exp(x) = [1, x, x^2/2!, ...]</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>exp</span><span>(</span><span>x</span><span>)</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1.0641em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>[</span><span>1</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>x</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>2</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>/2</span><span>!</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>...</span><span>]</span></span></span></span></span> --- this increases the recurrent state size (size of the resultant tensor), but each term reuses <em>the same input <span><span><span><math>xx</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span>x</span></span></span></span></span></em> (data reuse)! We found <span><span><span><math>k=2k=2</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>2</span></span></span></span></span> (a second order polynomial) was a sweet spot for running BASED on H100s — balancing quality and kernel throughput.</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n</ul>\n<p>To dive even deeper into the technical details, check out some of our <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2023-12-11-zoology0-intro\" target=\"_blank\">older</a> <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-03-03-based\" target=\"_blank\">blogposts</a> <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-06-22-ac\" target=\"_blank\">here</a>!</p>\n<h2>The impact</h2>\n<p>The BASED ✌️ blueprint has quietly become the new standard along multiple dimensions:</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:60%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-03-24-based-retro/blueprint.jpeg\">Following the blueprint.</figure>\n<ul>\n<li>\n<p><strong>BASED featured in a slew of new architectures this year</strong> — like <a href=\"https://www.minimax.io/\" target=\"_blank\">MiniMax</a> and <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.01496\" target=\"_blank\">Liger attention</a>! Within the 6-8 months from BASED ’s NeurIPS 2023 debut, teams that previously used state space models (Mamba-v1, RWKV-v4) have switched to BASED-inspired structures — linear attention with large states + local mixers — in Mamba-v2, RWKV-v5/v6, MetaLA, and more. Mamba-v2 has been used in Tencent’s <a href=\"https://x.com/TXhunyuan/status/1903121005809373386\" target=\"_blank\">Hunyuan-T1</a>, <a href=\"https://t.co/NYfx14nduS\" target=\"_blank\">Nemotron-H</a>, <a href=\"https://t.co/6kmF2TOsMb\" target=\"_blank\">Vamba</a>, and more! <em>These models aren’t just Mambas — they’ve embraced the BASED philosophy!</em></p>\n</li>\n<li>\n<p><strong>BASED ideas crushed in quality!</strong> In work led by <a href=\"https://michaelzhang.xyz/\" target=\"_blank\">Michael Zhang</a>, we released <strong>state-of-the-art 8B parameter Transformer-free models</strong>. We showed that we could <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-10-14-lolcats-p1\" target=\"_blank\">convert Transformer checkpoints to BASED style models</a> with <strong>minimal loss — or even gains — in quality and major speedups, without training from scratch</strong>.\nOur models outperformed efforts by <a href=\"https://research.nvidia.com/publication/2024-06_empirical-study-mamba-based-language-models\" target=\"_blank\">Nvidia Mamba</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.19427\" target=\"_blank\">Google Deepmind Griffin</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2408.15237\" target=\"_blank\">Together AI Mamba-2</a>, Together AI <a href=\"https://www.together.ai/blog/stripedhyena-7b\" target=\"_blank\">StripedHyena</a>, and <a href=\"https://www.tri.global/research/linearizing-large-language-models\" target=\"_blank\">Toyota Research SUPRA</a> ... with 35%–103% higher 5-shot MMLU accuracy across the board.</p>\n<p>We scaled our recipe to <strong>50x the size</strong> of other Transformer-free models, releasing the <strong>largest such models to date</strong> at 405B parameters — on an <strong>academic budget</strong>. What do we mean by academic budget? Well we trained our 405B models using 16 GPUs for 2 days contrasting the 16000 GPUs for 54+ days, which Meta used for Llama 405B!!!</p>\n</li>\n<li>\n<p><strong>We needed to build new systems and tools along the way!</strong> To help ourselves and others build AI from the hardware up, we spent the last year developing <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-10-29-tk2\" target=\"_blank\">ThunderKittens (TK)</a> — our opinionated DSL for AI kernel programming. TK makes writing AI kernels <strong>fun and fast</strong>. Turns out others agreed — it’s now used in industry (GPU Mode, Meta, Cruise, Jump Trading, Together AI, etc.) and academia (Berkeley, Stanford, University of Italy, UCSD, and more)...  Emphasizing the impact, now one year after TK’s Mother’s Day 2024 release, <strong>NVIDIA GTC 2025</strong> was all about TK-inspired DSLs like CuTile and Cutlass 4.0, which adopt TK’s Pythonic design and tile-based abstractions!</p>\n</li>\n</ul>\n<h2>Signing off</h2>\n<p>So to summarize the lessons, we strongly recommend that future work on efficient models: think more about designing efficiency optimizations based on <strong>downstream workload properties</strong> (like the power law distribution of recall in language); embrace <strong>simple, effective operations</strong> like the short sliding windows in BASED to avoid overly complex solutions; and build architectures <strong>from the hardware up</strong>! Of course, there's still no free lunch with BASED and we need to push hard to keep expanding the Pareto frontier. We've explored <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-07-01-jrt\" target=\"_blank\">some creative solutions</a> as well.</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:60%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-03-24-based-retro/signing.jpeg\"></figure>\n<p>Looking forward to continuing to build on this line of work and to seeing what the community uses it to create! Where BASED ✌️ Could Help You Next:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>\n<p><strong>On-device models</strong>: Eliminate the KV-cache for resource-constrained hardware</p>\n</li>\n<li>\n<p><strong>Speculative decoding</strong>: Use BASED as fast draft models</p>\n</li>\n<li>\n<p><strong>High-throughput data processing</strong>: Classify, extract from, or answer questions over millions of documents orders of magnitude faster than Transformers</p>\n</li>\n</ul>\n<p>Keep us posted and reach out to collaborate! We’re so excited 💫</p>,A walk down memory lane. It’s been a bit over a year since we released BASED ✌️ at NeurIPS 2023 and we’ve been humbled by the response! This…,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-03-24-based-retro,<a href='https://arorasimran.com/'>Simran Arora</a>,2026-07-22T18:37:38.404Z,2025-03-24T07:00:00.660Z,[{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-03-24-based-retro/retro.jpeg\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1024,\"height\":1024},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-03-24-based-retro/based.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-03-24-based-retro/hype.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-03-24-based-retro/frontier.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-03-24-based-retro/mqar.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-03-24-based-retro/data.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-03-24-based-retro/blueprint.jpeg\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-03-24-based-retro/signing.jpeg\",\"type\":\"photo\"}],163778663386687488,1211296081845936151,ThunderKittens Now on Blackwells!,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-03-15-tk-blackwell,<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-03-15-tk-blackwell/kitten.jpeg\">A black ThunderKitten climbing out of a well.</figure>\n<p><a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/ThunderKittens/tree/blackwell/kernels/matmul\" target=\"_blank\"><strong>GEMM Kernels</strong></a> | <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/ThunderKittens/blob/blackwell/kernels/attn/b200/b200.cu\" target=\"_blank\"><strong>Attention Kernel</strong></a> | <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-10-29-tk2\" target=\"_blank\"><strong>ThunderKittens Blog</strong></a></p>\n<hr>\n<p>With our collaborators at Together AI, we’ve been having fun playing around with some NVIDIA Blackwell GPUs over the past few weeks and reading about all the exciting new features. The cool thing is – turns out the new features, from 5th-generation tensor cores, to Tensor Memory and CTA pairs, fit pretty well into TK’s existing tile-based abstractions. It’s all about dataflow!</p>\n<p>Today, we’re releasing a few new kernels for the NVIDIA Blackwell architecture, written in ThunderKittens:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>BF16 and FP8 ThunderKittens GEMM kernels – running at or near cuBLAS speeds, and up to 2x faster than cuBLAS GEMMs on H100.</li>\n<li>Attention forwards and backwards – both running at near-cuDNN speeds on B200, and up to 2x faster than FA3 on H100.</li>\n</ul>\n<p>In the remainder of this blog, we’re going to take a deep dive into how we use the new hardware features for these kernels, as well as how we adapt kernels written for the NVIDIA Hopper architecture to the new NVIDIA Blackwell architecture. By the end of this blog, you’ll learn all about these new features and how they make attention go vroom!</p>\n<h1>It's All About the Dataflow</h1>\n<p>In our experience, writing performant kernels on NVIDIA Blackwell GPUs feels a lot more like programming a dataflow-machine than writing traditional (circa ~2022) CUDA kernels. It’s all about loading in enough data at a high enough throughput to keep the tensor cores hot. In the H100, the main mechanism for doing this was using warp-specialized TMA loads to asynchronously fetch data while the tensor cores are doing their computation (e.g., in attention, you asynchronously load the next tiles of <span><span><span><math>KK</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">K</span></span></span></span></span> and <span><span><span><math>VV</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.22222em\">V</span></span></span></span></span> while computing the current <span><span><span><math>QKTQK^T</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.0358em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span>Q</span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">K</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8413em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">T</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> tiles and online softmax).</p>\n<p>On the B200, this is even more important – the tensor cores now have 2–2.5x the power of those on the H100. And to fully utilize all that compute, we need to be loading in a lot more data all at once. Luckily, the new hardware features make it easier to build deeper pipelines on B200.</p>\n<h2>Matrix Multiplication</h2>\n<p>Of all of the kernels one can run, a matrix multiply kernel has the <em>least</em> excuse for bubbles in the data pipeline. It turns out with a little bit of care, one can eliminate just about all of them!</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-03-15-tk-blackwell/pipes.png\">Top: TK’s Tensor Pipe PM Sampling – almost no bubbles!\nBottom: cuBLAS Tensor Pipe PM Sampling for reference.</figure>\n<p>Our new matrix multiplication kernel has a few tricks up its sleeve that are different from Hopper:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>We launch threadblock clusters to take advantage of the CTA pair mechanism (more on that later) – this increases reuse and reduces bandwidth requirements on shared memory.</li>\n<li>We reserve two producer warps to launch the matrix multiplies for each consumer warpgroup. Consumers no longer launch their own matrix multiplies!</li>\n<li>MMA instructions directly signal to the producer load warps that pipeline stages are freed and ready to be filled.</li>\n<li>Producers signal consumers that output accumulators are finished and ready.</li>\n<li>Consumers pipeline output accumulators into registers, into shared memory, and then out into HBM.\n<ul>\n<li>We even serialize the consumer warpgroups and force one to load tensor memory into registers and signal the producers before the other can load its tensor memory, so that these loads are pipelined, too.</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li>We adopt a persistent kernel, so that we can pipeline the next inputs while the previous outputs are being written out.\n<ul>\n<li>In fact, we can even launch the next matrix multiply accumulate block while the previous is still in tensor memory.</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n</ul>\n<p>The end result is that there is only one bubble in the whole tensor pipeline: when the first consumer warpgroup reads its output accumulator into registers. We think this takes about 140 ns every few hundred microseconds; the rest is all tensor cores.</p>\n<h2>Attention</h2>\n<p>One important optimization turns out to be launching the AV MMA’s from the previous iteration of the attention loop while starting the QK MMA of the iteration, and loading the K and V tiles of the next iteration. In pseudocode, this looks like:</p>\n<div><pre><code class=\"language-cpp\"><span>// Producer warpgroup</span>\n<span>if</span> <span>(</span>warpgroup<span>::</span><span>is_producer</span><span>(</span><span>)</span><span>)</span> <span>{</span>\n  <span>if</span> <span>(</span>warpgroup<span>::</span><span>warpid</span><span>(</span><span>)</span> <span>==</span> <span>0</span><span>)</span> <span>{</span>\n    <span>// do QK.T matmul</span>\n  <span>}</span>\n  <span>if</span> <span>(</span>warpgroup<span>::</span><span>warpid</span><span>(</span><span>)</span> <span>==</span> <span>1</span><span>)</span> <span>{</span>\n    <span>// do AV matmul</span>\n  <span>}</span>\n  <span>if</span> <span>(</span>warpgroup<span>::</span><span>warpid</span><span>(</span><span>)</span> <span>==</span> <span>2</span><span>)</span> <span>{</span>\n    <span>// load next K</span>\n  <span>}</span>\n  <span>if</span> <span>(</span>warpgroup<span>::</span><span>warpid</span><span>(</span><span>)</span> <span>==</span> <span>3</span><span>)</span> <span>{</span>\n    <span>// load next V</span>\n  <span>}</span>\n<span>}</span>\n<span>// Consumer warpgroups</span>\n<span>else</span> <span>{</span>\n  <span>// Do O online softmax while signaling next AV</span>\n<span>}</span>\n</code></pre></div>\n<p>Below, we’ll go into each of the new hardware features that enable these pipelines in more detail.</p>\n<h2>Fifth-Generation Tensor Cores</h2>\n<p>A major new feature of the B200 is the larger, faster tensor cores. These run around ~2–2.5x faster than the tensor cores in the H100, and they’re a major source of speedups on the new generation of hardware.</p>\n<p>For review: tensor cores are on-chip compute units that compute large GEMMs: the computation <span><span><span><math>D=A@B+DD = A @ B + D</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">D</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.7778em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>A</span><span>@</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05017em\">B</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>+</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">D</span></span></span></span></span>, where <span><span><span><math>AA</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span>A</span></span></span></span></span>, <span><span><span><math>BB</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05017em\">B</span></span></span></span></span>, and <span><span><span><math>DD</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">D</span></span></span></span></span> are all matrices. In this blog, we’ll use an <span><span><span><math>M×N×KM \\times N \\times K</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7667em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">M</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>×</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.7667em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>×</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">K</span></span></span></span></span> notation for GEMMs, meaning that <span><span><span><math>AA</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span>A</span></span></span></span></span> has shape <span><span><span><math>M×KM \\times K</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7667em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">M</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>×</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">K</span></span></span></span></span>, <span><span><span><math>BB</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05017em\">B</span></span></span></span></span> has shape <span><span><span><math>K×NK \\times N</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7667em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">K</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>×</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span></span></span></span></span>, and <span><span><span><math>DD</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">D</span></span></span></span></span> has shape <span><span><span><math>M×NM \\times N</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7667em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">M</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>×</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span></span></span></span></span>.</p>\n<p>The B200 tensor cores aren’t just faster than the H100 tensor cores – they’re also much larger. From our microbenchmarking, they seem to behave like <span><span><span><math>128×128128 \\times 128</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>128</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>×</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>128</span></span></span></span></span> systolics. That means – in order to get the full FLOP utilization out of the tensor cores, you want <span><span><span><math>MM</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">M</span></span></span></span></span> and <span><span><span><math>NN</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span></span></span></span></span> to be 128 (or larger multiples of 128). Smaller values of <span><span><span><math>MM</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">M</span></span></span></span></span> and <span><span><span><math>NN</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span></span></span></span></span> run at the corresponding fraction of 128; e.g., a <span><span><span><math>64×64×6464 \\times 64 \\times 64</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>64</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>×</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>64</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>×</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>64</span></span></span></span></span> GEMM will run at one-quarter the FLOP rate of a <span><span><span><math>128×128×64128 \\times 128 \\times 64</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>128</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>×</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>128</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>×</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>64</span></span></span></span></span> GEMM. This is a bit of a departure from the H100, where smaller GEMM shapes were enough to max out the tensor cores.</p>\n<p>In ThunderKittens, the new operations are nicely abstracted:</p>\n<div><pre><code class=\"language-cpp\"><span>using</span> <span>namespace</span> kittens<span>;</span>\ntt<span>&#x3C;</span><span>float</span><span>,</span> <span>128</span><span>,</span> <span>128</span><span>></span> d<span>;</span> <span>// 128 x 128 FP32 tensor memory tile</span>\n__shared__ st_bf<span>&#x3C;</span><span>128</span><span>,</span> <span>64</span><span>></span> a<span>,</span> b<span>;</span> <span>// 128 x 64 BF16 shared tile</span>\n__shared__ semaphore sem<span>;</span> <span>// semaphore (mbarrier)</span>\n<span>// init...</span>\n<span><span>mma</span><span><span>&#x3C;</span>transpose<span>::</span>N<span>,</span> transpose<span>::</span>T<span>></span></span></span><span>(</span>d<span>,</span> a<span>,</span> b<span>,</span> sem<span>)</span><span>;</span> <span>// do it</span>\n</code></pre></div>\n<h2>Tensor Memory</h2>\n<p>There’s also a new layer of the memory hierarchy: tensor memory, a set of registers specifically allocated for tensor core instructions. This is nice for kernels because it gives us an extra 256KB of register memory to play with, in addition to the 256KB we had already, and up to 227KB of shared memory.</p>\n<p>This is especially useful for building more complex dataflow pipelines, which are always trading off the degree of preloading we can accomplish, vs. how much SRAM is available on each SM. Tensor memory gives us more room to work!</p>\n<p>In the TK attention kernel, we make extensive use of tensor memory, especially during the backwards pass (which has a higher footprint due to the need to store gradient values):</p>\n<div><pre><code class=\"language-cpp\"><span>// The tensor memory used by a warpgroup in attention backwards.</span>\n<span>struct</span> <span>wg_tmem_t</span> <span>{</span>\n    tt<span>&#x3C;</span><span>float</span><span>,</span> <span>64</span><span>,</span> <span>128</span><span>></span> <span>&#x26;</span>kg<span>;</span>\n    tt<span>&#x3C;</span><span>float</span><span>,</span> <span>64</span><span>,</span> <span>128</span><span>></span> <span>&#x26;</span>vg<span>;</span>\n    tt<span>&#x3C;</span><span>float</span><span>,</span> <span>64</span><span>,</span> <span>64</span><span>></span>  <span>&#x26;</span>sb<span>;</span>\n    tt<span>&#x3C;</span><span>float</span><span>,</span> <span>64</span><span>,</span> <span>64</span><span>></span>  <span>&#x26;</span>dp<span>;</span>\n    tt<span>&#x3C;</span>bf16<span>,</span>  <span>64</span><span>,</span> <span>64</span><span>></span>  <span>&#x26;</span>pb_bf<span>;</span>\n    tt<span>&#x3C;</span>bf16<span>,</span>  <span>64</span><span>,</span> <span>64</span><span>></span>  <span>&#x26;</span>dp_bf<span>;</span>\n    semaphore <span>*</span>mma_sem<span>;</span>\n<span>}</span><span>;</span>\n</code></pre></div>\n<h2>CTA Pairs</h2>\n<p>There’s another nice set of abstractions that allows for deeper coordination between different CUDA thread blocks – CTA pairs.</p>\n<p>Brief review: NVIDIA’s CUDA programming model has a notion of “thread blocks” – sets of threads that work together to execute a kernel. In classic (pre-2022) CUDA, thread blocks executed independently; but starting with the NVIDIA Hopper architecture, thread blocks in the same “cluster” were able to start to coordinate with each other. CTA pairs are deeply related to these notions – CTA stands for “Cooperative Thread Array,” the PTX implementation of a thread block.</p>\n<p>The NVIDIA Blackwell platform introduces the notion of CTA pairs – two CTAs operating within the same cluster, scheduled on the same or adjacent SM's. Two CTAs can coordinate to execute tensor core instructions, accessing the Tensor Memory of both of the CTAs within the CTA pair. The <a href=\"https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/parallel-thread-execution/#tcgen05-cta-pair\" target=\"_blank\">NVIDIA PTX guide</a> has more details for those interested.</p>\n<p>You’ve actually already seen the ThunderKittens abstraction for CTA pairs in this very blog post! If the <code>ncta</code> variable in the <code>mma</code> template is set to <code>2</code>, we can have two CTAs on a single SM coordinate to do a larger GEMM:</p>\n<div><pre><code class=\"language-cpp\"><span>using</span> <span>namespace</span> kittens<span>;</span>\n<span>template</span><span>&#x3C;</span><span>int</span> trans_a<span>,</span> <span>int</span> n_trans_b<span>,</span> ducks<span>::</span>tt<span>::</span>all D<span>,</span> ducks<span>::</span>st_descriptor<span>::</span>input A<span>,</span> ducks<span>::</span>st_descriptor<span>::</span>input B<span>,</span> <span>int</span> acc<span>=</span><span>1</span><span>,</span> <span>int</span> ncta<span>=</span><span>1</span><span>></span>\n__device__ <span>static</span> <span>inline</span> <span>void</span> <span>mma</span><span>(</span>D <span>&#x26;</span>d<span>,</span> <span>const</span> A <span>&#x26;</span>a<span>,</span> <span>const</span> B <span>&#x26;</span>b<span>,</span> semaphore <span>&#x26;</span>sem<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n</code></pre></div>\n<h1>Conclusion</h1>\n<p>We hope you enjoyed this whirlwind of new features and how you can use them to write blazing fast attention kernels!</p>\n<p>Check out our kernels and read more about ThunderKittens below!</p>\n<ul>\n<li><a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/ThunderKittens/blob/blackwell/kernels/attn/b200/b200.cu\" target=\"_blank\">Attention Kernel</a></li>\n<li><a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/ThunderKittens/blob/blackwell/kernels/matmul/B200/matmul.cu\" target=\"_blank\">BF16 GEMM Kernel</a></li>\n<li><a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/ThunderKittens/blob/blackwell/kernels/matmul/FP8_B200/matmul.cu\" target=\"_blank\">FP8 GEMM Kernel</a></li>\n<li><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-10-29-tk2\" target=\"_blank\">ThunderKittens Blog</a></li>\n</ul>\n<p>If you’d like to learn more about these kernels or work with us on developing the next set of updates, please reach out to Ben or Dan!</p>\n<ul>\n<li>Ben: <a href=\"mailto:bfs@stanford.edu\" target=\"_blank\">bfs@stanford.edu</a></li>\n<li>Dan: <a href=\"mailto:danfu@ucsd.edu\" target=\"_blank\">danfu@ucsd.edu</a></li>\n</ul>\n<p>And thanks again to Together AI for collaborating with us and helping us get running with NVIDIA B200’s to write these kernels!</p>\n<p>Finally, if you'd like to learn how to build kernels like this and contribute to the cutting edge, please reach out to work with us! Dan is building the kernels team at Together AI and is <a href=\"https://job-boards.greenhouse.io/togetherai/jobs/4188119007\" target=\"_blank\">actively hiring</a>, and we are always looking for great academic collaborators! Come build with us!</p>\n<p>Watch this space for more updates soon!</p>,A black ThunderKitten climbing out of a well. GEMM Kernels | Attention Kernel | ThunderKittens Blog\n\nWith our collaborators at Together AI…,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-03-15-tk-blackwell,<a href='https://benjaminfspector.com/'>Benjamin Spector</a>, <a href=\"https://www.aaryan-singhal.com/\">Aaryan Singhal</a>, <a href=\"https://danfu.org/\">Dan Fu</a>, <a href=\"https://cs.stanford.edu/people/chrismre/\">Chris Ré</a>,2026-07-22T18:37:38.403Z,2025-03-15T07:00:00.342Z,[{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-03-15-tk-blackwell/kitten.jpeg\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1024,\"height\":768},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-03-15-tk-blackwell/pipes.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"}],163778663386687488,1211296081845936152,ThunderMLA: FlashMLA, Faster and Fused-er!,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-03-04-thundermla,<p><a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/ThunderKittens/blob/mla/kernels/attn/demo/mla_decode/template_mla_decode.cu\" target=\"_blank\"><strong>Code</strong></a> | <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-10-29-tk2\" target=\"_blank\"><strong>TK Part 2</strong></a> | <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-05-12-quick-tk\" target=\"_blank\"><strong>TK Part 1</strong></a> | <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-05-12-tk\" target=\"_blank\"><strong>Brr</strong></a></p>\n<hr>\n<p>We've been playing with some new schedulers to deal with variable length sequences. (This is a common case in LLM inference, when you're serving requests from many different users.) Like everyone else, we were really excited about DeepSeek MLA so we decided to look into it!</p>\n<p>We're excited to introduce ThunderMLA, our response to the performance challenges of large language model inference. ThunderMLA is a completely fused \"megakernel\" for decode that's 20-35% faster than DeepSeek's FlashMLA on diverse workloads. It turns out some simple scheduling tricks can get you pretty far! And although this release is focused on attention decoding, we think these techniques apply much more broadly.</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-03-04-thundermla/smoothbrain_tldr.png\">Figure 0: A caricature of the main message of this post. Fused, scheduled megakernels (B) can be much more performant than separate kernels (A)!</figure>\n<p>Please be warned: this release is more art than product, although to our surprise, people are already using it in production!</p>\n<h3>Kernel Launches Are the Natural Predator of Performance</h3>\n<p>Attention decode kernels can struggle on variable-prompt workloads. Whereas prefill usually allows for a single kernel to fill the GPU, parallelizing across batch and sequence, when decoding small batches and just a few queries at a time, one needs to use other tricks like FlashInfer to keep the GPU full, but incurs other costs in doing so. The core issue lies in the substantial overhead created by:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>Running two separate kernel launches</li>\n<li>Tail effects between kernel executions</li>\n<li>Limited batch sizes when running large reasoning models (which appear, at the present, to be the future of AI)</li>\n</ul>\n<p>These constraints can severely limit performance. For intuition, consider the following decode scenario with imbalanced inputs:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>A batch of 4 prompts, of lengths [4641, 45118, 1730, 1696]</li>\n<li>Generating 4 new tokens (e.g., for a speculator)</li>\n<li>8-way tensor parallel -- that is, 16 heads per GPU for DeepSeek R1</li>\n</ul>\n<p>With 4 new tokens, FlashMLA runs in 52 us on an SXM H100, achieving just 144 TFLOPS and 1199 GB/s (out of the 939 TFLOPS / 3300 GB/s advertised by NVIDIA).</p>\n<p>As a partial solution, we're introducing ThunderMLA, a completely fused mega-kernel that represents the first step in our broader effort to rethink CUDA programming; on the same workload, ThunderMLA runs in just 41 us, achieving 183 TFLOPs and 1520 GB/s. Check it out <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/ThunderKittens/blob/mla/kernels/attn/demo/mla_decode/template_mla_decode.cu\" target=\"_blank\">here</a>!</p>\n<p>ThunderMLA applies classical computing ideas (like job scheduling) to modern AI workloads through:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>A novel interpreter template for building megakernels</li>\n<li>The actual ThunderMLA megakernel</li>\n<li>Two simple schedulers</li>\n<li>Advanced timing infrastructure</li>\n</ul>\n<h3>ThunderKittens Templates are the Natural Predator of Kernel Launches</h3>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-03-04-thundermla/scary_predator.png\"><i>Its presence masked by the faint hum of a GPU, a juvenile feline predator assumes the unmistakable hunting posture—tail twitching with anticipation, pupils dilated to their fullest extent. The tiny ThunderKitten's focus is absolute, fixed upon a single pale kernel that has strayed from the safety of its bucket. After three weeks stalking its prey, the kitten calculates distance and trajectory, its compact body coiled like a spring. The kernel, blissfully unaware of its impending doom, remains motionless on the silicon plains. Suddenly, the deadly ballet commences -- the kitten's haunches wiggle in that characteristic pre-pounce tremor, and in an explosive burst of primal energy, it launches forward with astonishing speed. Time seems to slow as paws extend, claws unsheathe, and the apex predator connects with its quarry, sending the kernel skittering across the floor in a desperate bid for survival that will, almost certainly, prove futile against nature's most perfectly designed hunting machine.</i></figure>\n<p>Traditionally, GPU workloads are written as sequences of kernels that exchange data through global memory (L2 cache or HBM). While this approach allows kernels to be reused, it introduces significant overhead, illustrated in Figure 1:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>Kernels must be set up and torn down; each of these can consume several microseconds</li>\n<li>Single-wave tail effects can often be substantial in these small-batch, fast-decoding scenarios</li>\n<li>Using multiple kernels prevents data reuse, thereby increasing memory bandwidth required by the GPU</li>\n</ul>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-03-04-thundermla/stylized_gantt.png\">Figure 1: a stylized Gantt chart of various kernel overheads. Sometimes, these costs are negligible, but often times they're not!</figure>\n<p>Our solution eliminates these kernel launches by creating a simple virtual instruction set on the GPU. Instead of launching separate kernels, we fuse them into a single \"megakernel,\" and then pass this megakernel its instructions at runtime through an instruction tensor, which in turn can dramatically modify its behavior. Within the megakernel, GPU SMs read from this instruction tensor, decide what work to perform next, and then execute it. Although we're initially focused on these megakernels for attention decoding scenarios, we think they're an important idea in general -- stay tuned!</p>\n<p>We've implemented this through an interpreter template within ThunderKittens, allowing arbitrary simple kernels to be plugged together into a megakernel, each with its own opcode and instructions. Key benefits include:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>Vastly simplified pipelining within instructions, across loop boundaries</li>\n<li>Asynchronous fetching of the future instructions, minimizing instruction overhead</li>\n<li>Deep pipelines that hide data fetch latencies within and across instructions</li>\n</ul>\n<p>Our implementation adapts a slot attention kernel and token reduction kernel into this interpreter template with just a few hundred lines of device code, and uses a global tensor as a semaphore to synchronize dependencies across instructions. Once both the two constituent kernels (\"partial\" and \"reduction\") of the megakernel for FlashMLA are implemented, the megakernel can be instantiated as <code>interpreter::kernel&#x3C;config, partial_template, reduction_template></code>. Altogether, this fused megakernel is just 250 lines of device code in ThunderKittens.</p>\n<p>The results speak for themselves. In realistic scenarios, our Thunder MLA can be 20-35% faster than the corresponding FlashMLA implementation released by DeepSeek. On the representative workload above, ThunderMLA runs in just 41 us, achieving 183 TFLOPs and 1520 GB/s.</p>\n<p>Here's a few other workloads we've tested with different batch sizes, KV sequence lengths, and Q tokens:</p>\n<table><thead><tr><th>Kernel</th><th>B 1, Seq 64k, Q 1</th><th>B 64, Seq 256-1024 (random), Q 4</th><th>B 64, Seq 512 Q 2</th><th>B 132, Seq 4k, Q 4</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><strong>FlashMLA (μs, TFLOPs, GB/s)</strong></td><td>55.0, 42, 1378</td><td>47.0, 124, 1212</td><td>39.0, 59, 1092</td><td>226.0, 333, 2839</td></tr><tr><td><strong>ThunderMLA (μs, TFLOPs, GB/s)</strong></td><td>44.5, 52, 1700</td><td>39.5, 147, 2022</td><td>28.6, 80, 1489</td><td>210.0, 358, 3055</td></tr><tr><td><strong>Speedup (%)</strong></td><td><strong>23.6%</strong></td><td><strong>19.0%</strong></td><td><strong>36.3%</strong></td><td><strong>7.6%</strong></td></tr></tbody></table>\n<h3>But How to Instruction Tensor?????</h3>\n<p>We've explored multiple approaches to scheduling work and generating the instruction tensors, which we'll provide more detail on in our upcoming technical report. If you're interested in collaborating, we'd love to know more about any interesting workloads you might have that could benefit from these megakernels; please send them <a href=\"mailto:bfs@stanford.edu\" target=\"_blank\">here</a>. In the mean time, here are two simple schedulers we've tested for ThunderMLA:</p>\nStatic Scheduler\n<p>Our first approach uses a simple static scheduler that:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>Divides jobs into small pieces</li>\n<li>Synthesizes a k-way reduction tree to merge operations</li>\n<li>Uses a heap-based priority queue to allocate work to available SMs</li>\n</ul>\n<p>This yields good performance across various settings, though it struggles to optimally overlap reduction and compute because it doesn't consider task timing when creating instructions. An example of a proposed schedule from the static scheduler can be seen in figure 2A.</p>\nMakespan Backwards Scheduler\n<p>Our second, more advanced scheduler improves performance by optimizing the makespan more aggressively:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>We know the very last event of the kernel is a single reduction task for each token, so we can work backwards from it</li>\n<li>We then run heuristic rollouts to determine optimal execution path</li>\n<li>Assigns processor IDs to each task</li>\n</ul>\n<p>Taken together, this can shave an additional 10% off execution time, as visualized in figure 2B.</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-03-04-thundermla/simulated_gantt.png\">Figure 2: Two simulated Gantt charts from our schedulers. (A) our simple static scheduler. Although this approach doesn't overlap very well, it nonetheless reduces both tail effects and kernel launch times. (B) Our makespan-focused backwards scheduler. Of particular note is that the schedule is determined relative to the end of the kernel, rather than its beginning.</figure>\n<p>Note that while these schedulers are currently slower than the kernel itself (currently requiring 1-2ms), this approach remains viable because:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>Schedules can be reused across dozens of layers on large models</li>\n<li>Generation can happen asynchronously on the CPU while previous batches are running</li>\n<li>Schedules between similar batch sizes change infrequently (e.g., a batch size of 4 only needs schedule regeneration at most every 8 forward passes on average)</li>\n<li>Even when schedules change, they usually change in simple enough ways that a simple update is possible</li>\n</ul>\n<h3>Profiling MegaKernels Requires New Tools, but Good Thing We Thought of That Too</h3>\n<p>During development, we realized that NVIDIA's tools are increasingly inadequate for understanding the performance of heterogeneous and dataflow-driven kernels. NCU, while an excellent tool we love very much, isn't as well-suited for analyzing these kernels where hardware resources are more constraining than thread execution.</p>\n<p>To address this, we've developed custom timing infrastructure that produces detailed Gantt charts of kernel execution, tracking events such as:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>Consumer setup phases</li>\n<li>Producer load and consumer compute phases</li>\n<li>Synchronization delays in the production tree</li>\n</ul>\n<p>These tools have already proven invaluable for improving our kernels, infrastructure, and schedulers. Our infrastructure embeds a pipeline for writing out timing data, to ensure that overhead is minimal. For example, these visualizations have made it easy to understand data pipeline formation, and to track and eliminate latencies that otherwise plague these kernels. To illustrate, figure 3 has the measured performance of each of the schedules shown in the previous section:</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-03-04-thundermla/actual_gantt.png\">Figure 3: Measured profiles from an H100 of the schedules from figure 1. Yellow bars correspond to partial instructions, and teal bars to reduction operations. Black events represent setup and finish operations, blue dots show producer loads, and red diamonds show when consumers begin compute phases. For example, one can clearly see the formation of three-stage and four-stage pipelines in the partial and reduction operations, respectively. Most importantly, one can see that the backwards scheduler in (B) is able to overlap reduction and compute much more effectively than the static scheduler (A).</figure>\n<p>As a simple example, one optimization that these traces enabled was to remove approximately 2 us of kernel latency from the partial operations by delaying filling the second and third stages of the three-stage KV cache pipeline until after the Q matrix is loaded -- otherwise, the out-of-order loads can further delay the compute phase from beginning. These sorts of optimizations have been very difficult to otherwise identify.</p>\n<h3>What Next?????</h3>\n<p>While we've focused on MLA with this release, these techniques apply equally well to:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>Standard multi-headed attention and grouped query attention</li>\n<li>Overlapping compute and NCCL communication in tensor parallel</li>\n<li>Other AI workloads (e.g., overlapping router and expert phases in mixture-of-experts models)</li>\n</ul>\n<p>We plan to continue improving pipelining across instruction boundaries, which remains an important source of overhead (though still less than kernel launches). A full iterator model, inspired by traditional database theory, would be a promising next step.</p>\n<p>Ultimately, we're excited about further virtualizing NVIDIA's programming model into a cleaner, more performant set of abstractions. We are convinced dataflow is the appropriate paradigm for modern AI workloads, and we aim to apply the full arsenal of classical solutions—parallel job scheduling, latency hiding, asymmetric memory costs—to these increasingly important problems.</p>,Code | TK Part 2 | TK Part 1 | Brr We've been playing with some new schedulers to deal with variable length sequences. (This is a common…,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-03-04-thundermla,<a href='https://benjaminfspector.com/'>Benjamin Spector</a>, <a href=\"https://www.aaryan-singhal.com/\">Aaryan Singhal</a>, <a href=\"https://danfu.org/\">Dan Fu</a>, <a href=\"https://cs.stanford.edu/people/chrismre/\">Chris Ré</a>,2026-07-22T18:37:38.402Z,2025-03-04T07:00:00.357Z,[{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-03-04-thundermla/smoothbrain_tldr.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1744,\"height\":1488},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-03-04-thundermla/scary_predator.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-03-04-thundermla/stylized_gantt.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-03-04-thundermla/simulated_gantt.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-03-04-thundermla/actual_gantt.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"}],163778663386687488,1211296081845936153,Minions: the rise of small, on-device LMs,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-02-24-minions,<h3>Embracing small LMs, shifting compute on-device, and cutting cloud costs in the process</h3>\n<blockquote>\n<p>📄<a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.15964\" target=\"_blank\">Paper</a><br>\n💻<a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/Minions\" target=\"_blank\">GitHub</a></p>\n</blockquote>\n<p><strong>TL;DR</strong> \nWe shift a substantial portion of LLM workloads to consumer devices by having small on-device models collaborate with frontier models in the cloud. By only reading long contexts locally, we reduce cloud costs with minimal or no quality degradation. We imagine a future where an “intelligence layer” running persistently on-device interacts with frontier models in the cloud to deliver applications with cost-effective, “always-on” intelligence.</p>\n<h2>🌬️ The Tailwinds: Frontier Models Cost $$$, While Local Hardware Collects Dust</h2>\n<figure style=\"float:right;width:30%;margin-left:1em;margin-bottom:1em\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-02-24-minions/dust.jpg\" style=\"width:100%\"></figure>\n<p>Compute is everywhere—our  <a href=\"https://www.apple.com/newsroom/2024/05/apple-introduces-m4-chip/\" target=\"_blank\">laptops</a>, <a href=\"https://www.apple.com/apple-intelligence/\" target=\"_blank\">phones</a>, <a href=\"https://www.trendforce.com/news/2024/05/03/news-tsmc-reportedly-commences-production-of-teslas-next-generation-dojo-chips-anticipates-40x-increase-in-computing-power-in-3-years/\" target=\"_blank\">cars</a>, and even <a href=\"https://www.qualcomm.com/products/mobile/snapdragon/xr-vr-ar/snapdragon-ar1-gen-1-platform\" target=\"_blank\">eyeglasses</a> (<a href=\"https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-cqwXxUo_q8\" target=\"_blank\">shoutout to Chris</a>, and no, not our advisor ❤️) — and the amount of compute on these consumer devices is growing rapidly (<em>e.g.</em> Nvidia just announced <a href=\"https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/project-digits/\" target=\"_blank\">a desktop</a> with Blackwell GPUs). Thanks to incredible packages like <a href=\"https://ollama.com/\" target=\"_blank\">Ollama</a> 😊 (along with many others), consumer devices can now run surprisingly capable small LMs. While we’re calling them “small”, remember that we’re talking about models that have billions of parameters and can tackle real tasks. And they are improving rapidly.</p>\n<p>With models of that caliber on-device, we can envision a world where laptops host always-on coding assistants that look for bugs and refactor our code, personal agents that are tirelessly indexing and organizing our local documents, and smart security systems that detect anomalies before they escalate. We’re getting there—but not quite yet. Today’s local LMs handle more humble tasks, like auto-complete and text summarization. Any moderately challenging task is handled by frontier models on the cloud. However, these models are not cheap. Consider our code refactoring dream; asking <strong>OpenAI’s o1 a single question on a respectable code repository (with one million tokens) will cost us >$15</strong>!!!</p>\n<h2>❓ The Big Question: Can Local LMs Step Up?</h2>\n<figure style=\"float:right;width:30%;margin-left:1em;margin-bottom:1em\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-02-24-minions/dancing.jpg\" style=\"width:100%\"></figure>\n<p>Small LMs are improving rapidly. Perhaps even more rapidly than their frontier counterparts. But it’s undeniable that for some higher order/domain-specific reasoning tasks (i.e., medical reasoning) frontier models have a clear advantage. Instead of entirely replacing big models with small ones, we ask: can small models on-device collaborate with larger devices in the cloud to solve these challenging tasks at reduced cost? </p>\n<p>To mimic the dream use cases mentioned above – of continuous grunt work on device – we analyze three data-intensive tasks, that involve retrieving and integrating data from long, domain-specific documents:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>\n<p><strong>Financial analysis</strong> over 100-page 10-K reports (<a href=\"https://github.com/patronus-ai/financebench/tree/main\" target=\"_blank\"><strong>FinanceBench</strong></a>)</p>\n</li>\n<li>\n<p><strong>Medical reasoning</strong> over patient records (<a href=\"https://github.com/kbressem/LongHealth\" target=\"_blank\"><strong>LongHealth</strong></a>)</p>\n</li>\n<li>\n<p><strong>Question answering</strong> over scientific papers (<a href=\"https://huggingface.co/datasets/allenai/qasper/viewer/qasper/train\" target=\"_blank\"><strong>QAsper</strong></a>)</p>\n</li>\n</ul>\n<h2>💻 The Naive Attempt: Minion</h2>\n<figure style=\"float:right;width:30%;margin-left:1em;margin-bottom:1em\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-02-24-minions/handshake.jpg\" style=\"width:100%\"></figure>\n<p>We first tried a simple approach: letting the on-device LM (between 1-8B parameters), which has access to the long documents, chat freely (see Figure 1 below) with a cloud model—like a junior analyst consulting a senior expert. The models choose what information to share and when to terminate the conversation. Since the long document is not sent to the cloud, we <strong>pay only 3.3% of the cloud cost while maintaining 87%</strong> of cloud model performance. Not bad at all! </p>\n<p>But why do we lose 13 points? It seems like performance takes a hit due to two limitations of the current generation of small LMs:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>\n<p><strong>Long Context Struggles</strong> – As document length increases, small LMs perform worse.</p>\n</li>\n<li>\n<p><strong>Multi-Step Confusion</strong> – As instructions become more complex and multipart, small LMs struggle to follow them reliably.</p>\n</li>\n</ul>\n<p>Beyond these challenges, <strong>hardware utilization (on consumer GPU like 4090) remained low,</strong> leaving potential accuracy gains on the table  (h/t <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.21787\" target=\"_blank\">Large language</a> 🐒’s). A back-and-forth chat does not take advantage of batching on-device, which is crucial for achieving high utilization.</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-02-24-minions/minion.png\" style=\"width:75%\">Figure 1: Sample chat using the minion protocol</figure>\n<h2>🎩 The Sophisticated Attempt: Minions</h2>\n<figure style=\"float:right;width:30%;margin-left:1em;margin-bottom:1em\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-02-24-minions/sophisticated.jpg\" style=\"width:100%\"></figure>\n<p>The lessons we learned led us to the enhanced Minions protocol (emphasis on plural S). Minions introduces a <strong>decomposition-execution-aggregation</strong> loop that addresses the weaknesses of small LMs, while making them work harder (in Minions, we work harder *and* smarter). </p>\n<p>Here is how it works:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>\n<p><strong>Decompose</strong> – The remote LM decomposes the task into smaller, single-step subtasks to be executed on chunks of the context. But how can it decompose the task and chunk the context without seeing it? To this end, we have the remote LM generate code for task decomposition and chunking, that is executed on-device where the context is available. </p>\n</li>\n<li>\n<p><strong>Execute</strong> – The local LM runs the subtasks in parallel, filtering only the most relevant outputs and communicating them to the remote LM.</p>\n</li>\n<li>\n<p><strong>Aggregate</strong> – The remote LM combines the local outputs, finalizing the answer or looping and requesting another round of subtasks.</p>\n</li>\n</ul>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-02-24-minions/minions.png\" style=\"width:100%\">Figure 2: Description of Minions Protocol</figure>\n<p>Minions delivers <strong>97.9% of the accuracy of remote-only solutions while costing just 17.5% as much</strong>!</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-02-24-minions/tradeoff.png\" style=\"width:100%\">Figure 3: Cost-Accuracy Tradeoff</figure>\n<h2>🔍 Tuning The Minions</h2>\n<p>Digging deeper, we found key levers that allow us to navigate the cost-accuracy tradeoff:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>\n<p><strong>Model Choice Matters</strong> – Currently, local models below <strong>3B parameters are not effective for the Minion/Minions protocols.</strong> By fixing the remote model and varying the local model (exploring different sizes and generations), we show that the <strong>Minions</strong>  protocol wouldn’t have been feasible until mid-2024 (until the release of gpt4-turbo and llama3.1-8B)!</p>\n</li>\n<li>\n<p><strong>Scaling Inference-Time Compute Improves Performance</strong> – Techniques like <strong>repeated sampling, finer-grained decomposition, and context chunking</strong> (see Figure 4)  at the execution stage boost quality—but at the cost of longer runtimes on laptops and increased remote usage. This is illustrated with the scaling curves in the figure below. </p>\n</li>\n<li>\n<p><strong>Sequential Communication Helps</strong> – <strong>Allowing for more</strong> <strong>communication rounds</strong> generally improves accuracy at the cost of compute. </p>\n</li>\n</ul>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-02-24-minions/scaling-samples.png\" style=\"width:100%\">Figure 4: Effects of Scaling Local Compute.</figure>\n<h2>🚀 To infinity and Beyond</h2>\n<figure style=\"float:right;width:30%;margin-left:1em;margin-bottom:1em\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-02-24-minions/flying.jpg\" style=\"width:100%\"></figure>\n<p>Minions is a first step towards establishing a communication protocol between small on-device LMs and frontier LMs. As GPUs become ubiquitous across consumer and edge devices, we need smarter ways to distribute AI workloads—reducing reliance on expensive cloud APIs while unlocking powerful local applications. If you are a practitioner, researcher, or hacker, we’d love to get you involved with Minions:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>\n<p><strong>Practitioners</strong>: we encourage you to apply Minions to your own workloads — please see our GitHub repository to quickstart. </p>\n</li>\n<li>\n<p><strong>Researchers</strong>: we see significant opportunities for further research, including improving communication efficiency through better co-design of local and remote models, alternative communication signals beyond natural language, and enhancing inference speed via systems and algorithmic advances.</p>\n</li>\n<li>\n<p><strong>Hackers</strong>: we are just getting started with Minions, and are excited to continue building. If you are excited by our vision, we’d love to see PRs and new projects that expand our community!</p>\n</li>\n</ul>\n<h2>📚 Additional Resources</h2>\n<ul>\n<li>How do you use Minions? Check out our Github here for sample scripts: <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/minions\" target=\"_blank\">https://github.com/HazyResearch/minions</a></li>\n</ul>\n<iframe width=\"560\" height=\"315\" src=\"https://www.youtube.com/embed/70Kot0_DFNs\" title=\"YouTube video player\"></iframe>\n<ul>\n<li>Want to learn more? Read our paper here: <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.15964\" target=\"_blank\">https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.15964</a></li>\n</ul>\n<h2>🙏 Acknowledgements</h2>\n<p>A huge thank you to Jeff Morgan, Michael Chiang, Patrick Devine, and the entire Ollama team for their support throughout this release. We’re also grateful to Together AI for generously providing compute resources and credits. Lastly, thanks to the many folks who have provided invaluable feedback on this work - Aditi Jha, Andrew Drozdov, Cody Blakeney, Jordan Juravsky, Jon Saad-Falcon, Ben Viggiano, Mayee Chen, Neel Guha, Jerry Liu, Kelly Buchanan and Michael Zhang.</p>,Embracing small LMs, shifting compute on-device, and cutting cloud costs in the process 📄Paper\n 💻GitHub\n\nTL;DR  We shift a substantial…,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2025-02-24-minions,<a href='http://www.sabrieyuboglu.com/'>Sabri Eyuboglu*</a>, <a href='https://dan-biderman.netlify.app/'>Dan Biderman*</a>, and <a href='https://www.linkedin.com/in/avanika-narayan-a35a02a3/'>Avanika Narayan*</a>.,2026-07-22T18:37:38.401Z,2025-02-24T07:00:00.063Z,[{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-02-24-minions/dust.jpg\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1024,\"height\":1024},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-02-24-minions/dancing.jpg\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-02-24-minions/handshake.jpg\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-02-24-minions/minion.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-02-24-minions/sophisticated.jpg\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-02-24-minions/minions.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-02-24-minions/tradeoff.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-02-24-minions/scaling-samples.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2025-02-24-minions/flying.jpg\",\"type\":\"photo\"}],163778663386687488,1211296081845936154,Hazy Alumni Life Updates (2024),https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-12-11-alumni-updates,<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/dancing_dinos.png\" style=\"width:80%\"></figure>\n<p>With the annual celebration of machine learning that is NeurIPS underway, we thought it would be fun to celebrate the amazing accomplishments of our own Hazy alumni! 🤩</p>\n<p>We're constantly amazed by what they have accomplished -- from landing on the inaugural <strong>Time's 100 Most Influential People in AI (2x!)</strong> to founding <strong>unicorn startups (several!)</strong> to winning <strong>NSF CAREER Awards (4x!)</strong> to earning a spot on <strong>Forbes 30 Under 30 (3x!)</strong> and too many <strong>orals, spotlights, and best papers</strong> to count, our former lab members have been busy leaving their mark on the AI landscape.</p>\n<p>But beyond their individual accomplishments, what makes us most proud is the work they’ve done to shape the future of AI as professors, entrepreneurs, researchers, and engineers. So please join us in celebrating their amazing accomplishments!</p>\n<p>Table of contents:</p>\n<div style=\"display:flex;flex-wrap:wrap;gap:20px\"><div style=\"flex:1 1 30%\"><ul><li><a target=\"_blank\"></a>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/albert_gu.png\" style=\"width:30%\"></figure>\n<h4>Albert Gu</h4>\n<p><em>Cartesia | CSO</em><br>\n<em>CMU | Assistant Professor</em></p>\n<p>What can we say that <a href=\"https://time.com/7012853/albert-gu/\" target=\"_blank\">Time didn’t already cover in its profile</a> of Albert as part of the inaugural Time Top 100 in AI? Before joining CMU as an assistant professor next year, Albert has been leading the SSM revolution while serving as Chief Scientific Officer of <a href=\"https://www.cartesia.ai/\" target=\"_blank\">Cartesia</a>.</p>\n<p><strong>Notable Papers</strong></p>\n<ul>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2312.00752\" target=\"_blank\">Mamba: Linear-Time Sequence Modeling with Selective State Spaces | COLM 2024</a></li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2111.00396\" target=\"_blank\">Efficiently Modeling Long Sequences with Structured State Spaces</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Oral @ ICLR 2022</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2008.07669\" target=\"_blank\">Hippo: Recurrent memory with optimal polynomial projections</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Spotlight @ NeurIPS 2020</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2012.14966\" target=\"_blank\">Kaleidoscope: An efficient, learnable representation for all structured linear maps</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Spotlight @ ICLR 2020</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n</ul>\n<p><strong>Awards</strong></p>\n<ul>\n<li>2024 Time’s 100 Most Influential People in AI</li>\n<li>2023 Amazon Research Award</li>\n</ul>\n<hr>\n<a name=\"user-content-alex_ratner\" target=\"_blank\"></a>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/alex_ratner.png\" style=\"width:30%\"></figure>\n<h4>Alex Ratner</h4>\n<p><em>Snorkel AI | CEO</em><br>\n<em>University of Washington | Affiliate Assistant Professor</em></p>\n<p>Currently on leave from <a href=\"https://ajratner.github.io/\" target=\"_blank\">his lab at UW</a> to continue leading <a href=\"https://snorkel.ai/\" target=\"_blank\">Snorkel AI</a> on its journey from humble academic research project to multi-billion dollar company making data labeling 100x faster for enterprises!</p>\n<hr>\n<a name=\"user-content-arjun_desai\" target=\"_blank\"></a>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/arjun_desai.png\" style=\"width:30%\"></figure>\n<h4>Arjun Desai</h4>\n<p><em>Cartesia | Co-Founder</em></p>\n<p>Arjun was recently selected to be on Forbes 30 Under 30 and is helping take SSMs to the moon at <a href=\"https://www.cartesia.ai/\" target=\"_blank\">Cartesia</a> after graduating from the lab last year! 🎉</p>\n<hr>\n<a name=\"user-content-armin_thomas\" target=\"_blank\"></a>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/armin_thomas.png\" style=\"width:30%\"></figure>\n<h4>Armin      Thomas</h4>\n<p><em>Liquid AI | Technical Staff</em></p>\n<p>Joined <a href=\"https://www.liquid.ai/\" target=\"_blank\">Liquid AI</a> as a technical staff member working on novel ML architectures.</p>\n<hr>\n<a name=\"user-content-arun_kumar\" target=\"_blank\"></a>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/arun_kumar.png\" style=\"width:30%\"></figure>\n<h4>Arun Kumar</h4>\n<p><em>RapidFire AI | Cofounder and CTO</em><br>\n<em>University of California, San Diego | Associate Professor (on leave)</em></p>\n<p>Arun has been piling up awards over the past few years for his work on DL systems, and the apple doesn’t fall far from the tree -- his first PhD student, Supun Nakandala, won the 2023 ACM SIGMOD Jim Gray Doctoral Dissertation Award.</p>\n<p><strong>Awards</strong></p>\n<ul>\n<li>2024 VLDB Early Career Research Contribution Award</li>\n<li>2024 ACM SIGMOD Distinguished Associate Editor</li>\n<li>2021: NSF CAREER Award</li>\n<li>2021: IEEE TCDE Rising Star Awards</li>\n</ul>\n<hr>\n<a name=\"user-content-beidi_chen\" target=\"_blank\"></a>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/beidi_chen.png\" style=\"width:30%\"></figure>\n<h4>Beidi Chen</h4>\n<p><em>CMU | Assistant Professor</em><br>\n<em>RapidFire AI | Research Scientist</em></p>\n<p>Started the <a href=\"https://keroro824.github.io/lab-page/\" target=\"_blank\">InfiniAI lab</a> at CMU, selected as a Rising Star in EECS by MIT, and just had <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.06126\" target=\"_blank\">two</a> <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.12374\" target=\"_blank\">papers</a> accepted to NeurIPS 2024 as spotlights!\n👀 <a href=\"https://x.com/BeidiChen/status/1854325012108648685\" target=\"_blank\">Beidi is currently looking for new students</a> -- if you’re applying to the CMU PhD program, please reach out to her!</p>\n<p><strong>Notable Papers</strong></p>\n<ul>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.06126\" target=\"_blank\">Learn To be Efficient: Build Structured Sparsity in Large Language Models | NeurIPS 2024</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Spotlight @ NeurIPS 2024</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.12374\" target=\"_blank\">Sequoia: Scalable, Robust, and Hardware-aware Speculative Decoding | NeurIPS 2024</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Spotlight @ NeurIPS 2024</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://openreview.net/forum?id=wWK7yXkULyh\" target=\"_blank\">MONGOOSE: A Learnable LSH Framework for Efficient Neural Network Training | ICLR 2021</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Oral @ ICLR 2021</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://openreview.net/forum?id=RRntzKrBTp\" target=\"_blank\">FlexGen: High-Throughput Generative Inference of Large Language Models with a Single GPU | ICML 2023</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Oral @ ICML 2023</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2206.01288\" target=\"_blank\">Decentralized training of foundation models in heterogeneous environments | NeurIPS 2022</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Oral @ NeurIPS
