2022</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n</ul>\n<p><strong>Awards</strong></p>\n<ul>\n<li>2022 ICML Outstanding Paper Runner Up</li>\n<li>2021 MIT EECS Rising Stars</li>\n<li>2019 UIUC EECS Rising Stars</li>\n</ul>\n<hr>\n<a name=\"user-content-braden_hancock\" target=\"_blank\"></a>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/braden_hancock.png\" style=\"width:30%\"></figure>\n<h4>Braden Hancock</h4>\n<p><em>Meta | Director of AI</em></p>\n<p>After co-founding <a href=\"https://snorkel.ai/\" target=\"_blank\">Snorkel AI</a>, found a new home this year at Meta as the Director of AI, leading the centralized Evaluations org responsible for evaluation strategy, development, tooling, and execution for all GenAI foundation models (e.g. Llama).</p>\n<hr>\n<a name=\"user-content-brandon_yang\" target=\"_blank\"></a>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/brandon_yang.png\" style=\"width:30%\"></figure>\n<h4>Brandon Yang</h4>\n<p><em>Cartesia | Co-Founder</em></p>\n<p>After co-founding Cartesia, also earned a spot on Forbes 30 Under 30 this year!</p>\n<hr>\n<a name=\"user-content-ce_zhang\" target=\"_blank\"></a>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/ce_zhang.png\" style=\"width:30%\"></figure>\n<h4>Ce Zhang</h4>\n<p><em>University of Chicago | Associate Professor</em><br>\n<em>Together AI | CTO</em></p>\n<p>When not serving as the CTO of one of the <a href=\"https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5RylG1wt0eM\" target=\"_blank\">fastest growing</a> VC-backed <a href=\"https://www.reuters.com/technology/together-ai-valued-125-bln-salesforce-led-funding-round-2024-03-13/\" target=\"_blank\">unicorns</a> (Together AI), Ce runs a <a href=\"https://zhangce.github.io/docs/publications.html\" target=\"_blank\">lab</a> at the University of Chicago focused on distributed learning and efficient inference.</p>\n<p><strong>Notable Papers</strong></p>\n<ul>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2206.01288\" target=\"_blank\">Decentralized training of foundation models in heterogeneous environments | NeurIPS 2022</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Oral @ NeurIPS 2022</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n</ul>\n<p><strong>Awards</strong></p>\n<ul>\n<li>Co Editor-in-Chief of the new publication <a href=\"https://data.mlr.press/\" target=\"_blank\">DMLR</a></li>\n<li>2018 Google Focused Research Award</li>\n</ul>\n<hr>\n<a name=\"user-content-chris_desa\" target=\"_blank\"></a>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/chris_desa.png\" style=\"width:30%\"></figure>\n<h4>Christopher De Sa</h4>\n<p><em>Cornell | Professor</em><br>\n<em>Together | Advisor</em></p>\n<p>Chris recently received tenure as an associate professor at Cornell while leading the <a href=\"https://relax-ml.cs.cornell.edu/team/\" target=\"_blank\">Relax ML Lab</a>  🎉, staying just one step ahead of his amazing students like Ruqi and Cooper who’ve joined the faculty at Purdue and Yale. Chris recently served as Program Chair of MLSys 2024 and won a DARPA grant for decentralized fine-tuning of compressed foundation models.</p>\n<p><strong>Notable Papers</strong></p>\n<ul>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2301.11562\" target=\"_blank\">Is My Prediction Arbitrary? The Confounding Effects of Variance in Fair Classification Benchmarks</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Best Paper Award Honorable Mention for the AI for Social Impact track @ AAAI 2024</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.13304\" target=\"_blank\">QuIP: 2-Bit Quantization of Large Language Models With Guarantees</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Spotlight @ NeurIPS 2023</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://proceedings.mlr.press/v162/zhang22ag.html\" target=\"_blank\">Low-Precision Stochastic Gradient Langevin Dynamics</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Spotlight @ ICML 2022</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://openreview.net/forum?id=7gWSJrP3opB\" target=\"_blank\">A General Analysis of Example-Selection for Stochastic Gradient Descent</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Spotlight @ ICLR 2022</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://icml.cc/virtual/2021/poster/8893\" target=\"_blank\">Optimal Complexity in Decentralized Training</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Oral @ ICML 2021</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2006.11677\" target=\"_blank\">Asymptotically Optimal Exact Minibatch Metropolis-Hastings</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Spotlight @ NeurIPS 2020</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper/2020/file/42299f06ee419aa5d9d07798b56779e2-Paper.pdf\" target=\"_blank\">Random Reshuffling is Not Always Better</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Spotlight @ NeurIPS 2020</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2007.02203\" target=\"_blank\">Regulating Accuracy-Efficiency Trade-Offs in Distributed Machine Learning Systems</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Oral @ LML 2020: ICML Workshop on Law and Machine Learning</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2019/file/82c2559140b95ccda9c6ca4a8b981f1e-Paper.pdf\" target=\"_blank\">Numerically Accurate Hyperbolic Embeddings Using Tiling-Based Models</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Spotlight @ NeurIPS 2019</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://proceedings.mlr.press/v97/acharya19b.html\" target=\"_blank\">Distributed Learning with Sublinear Communication Long oral</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Oral @ ICML 2019</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2019/file/361440528766bbaaaa1901845cf4152b-Paper.pdf\" target=\"_blank\">Poisson-Minibatching for Gibbs Sampling with Convergence Rate Guarantees</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Spotlight @ NeurIPS 2019</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n</ul>\n<p><strong>Awards</strong></p>\n<ul>\n<li>2024 keynote at the International Conference on AI-ML Systems</li>\n<li>2024 DARPA grant - decentralized fine-tuning of compressed foundation models</li>\n<li>2021 Google Research Scholar Award</li>\n<li>2021 NSF CAREER Award</li>\n</ul>\n<hr>\n<a name=\"user-content-dan_fu\" target=\"_blank\"></a>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/dan_fu.png\" style=\"width:30%\"></figure>\n<h4>Dan Fu</h4>\n<p><em>UCSD | Assistant Professor</em><br>\n<em>Together AI | Research Scientist</em></p>\n<p>Pending his ability to fulfill basic PhD requirements, Dan will soon join UCSD as the newest member of the computer science faculty 🎉!</p>\n<p>Also recently adopted a new kitten! <img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/dan_fu_cat.png\" style=\"width:50px;display:inline\"></p>\n<p><strong>Notable Papers</strong></p>\n<ul>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2311.05908\" target=\"_blank\">FlashFFTConv: Efficient Convolutions for Long Sequences with Tensor Cores</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Oral @ ENLSP Workshop at NeurIPS 2023</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.12109\" target=\"_blank\">Monarch Mixer: A Simple Sub-Quadratic GEMM-Based Architecture</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Oral @ NeurIPS 2023</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2212.14052\" target=\"_blank\">Hungry hungry hippos: Towards language modeling with state space models</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Spotlight @ ICLR 2023</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2205.14135\" target=\"_blank\">FlashAttention: Fast and memory-efficient exact attention with IO-awareness</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Best Paper @ Hardware Aware Efficient Training Workshop at ICML 2022</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2411.12372\" target=\"_blank\">RedPajama: an Open Dataset for Training Large Language Models</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Spotlight @ NeurIPS Datasets 2024</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2302.10866\" target=\"_blank\">Hyena Hierarchy: Towards Larger Convolutional Language Models</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Oral @ ICML 2023</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2203.13270\" target=\"_blank\">Shoring Up the Foundations: Fusing Model Embeddings and Weak Supervision</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Oral @ UAI 2022</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n</ul>\n<p><strong>Awards</strong></p>\n<ul>\n<li>Stanford Open Source Software Prize 2024</li>\n<li>NDSEG Award for Exemplary Impact and Relevance</li>\n</ul>\n<hr>\n<a name=\"user-content-feng_niu\" target=\"_blank\"></a>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/feng_niu.png\" style=\"width:30%\"></figure>\n<h4>Feng Niu</h4>\n<p><em>Evidently | CEO</em></p>\n<p>Founded <a href=\"https://evidently.com/\" target=\"_blank\">Evidently</a> to empower clinicians to make better decisions, leading to happier clinicians, healthier patients, and improved financial ROI for hospitals</p>\n<hr>\n<a name=\"user-content-fred_sala\" target=\"_blank\"></a>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/fred_sala.png\" style=\"width:30%\"></figure>\n<h4>Fred Sala</h4>\n<p><em>UW Madison | Assistant Professor</em><br>\n<em>Snorkel AI | Chief Scientist</em></p>\n<p>When not serving as Chief Scientist of <a href=\"https://snorkel.ai/\" target=\"_blank\">Snorkel AI</a>, Fred has kept himself busy winning the 2024 DARPA Young Faculty Award for work on data-efficient decentralized foundation models and leading his <a href=\"https://pages.cs.wisc.edu/~fredsala/\" target=\"_blank\">lab at UW Madison</a> focused on developing data-driven systems for machine learning.</p>\n<p><strong>Notable Papers</strong></p>\n<ul>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2407.11004.pdf\" target=\"_blank\">The AlCHEmist: Automated Labeling 500x CHEaper than LLM Data Annotators | NeurIPS 2024</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Spotlight @ NeurIPS 2024</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2211.12620.pdf\" target=\"_blank\">Promises and Pitfalls of Threshold-based Auto-labeling | NeurIPS 2023</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Spotlight @ NeurIPS 2023</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2307.14430.pdf\" target=\"_blank\">Skill-it! A Data-Driven Skills Framework for Understanding and Training Language Models | NeurIPS 2023</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Spotlight @ NeurIPS 2023</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2303.07527.pdf\" target=\"_blank\">Domain Generalization via Nuclear Norm Regularization | CPAL 2023</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Spotlight @ CPAL 2023</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2309.04344.pdf\" target=\"_blank\">Foundation Models Can Robustify Themselves, For Free | Neurips 2023</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Best Paper Award Honorable Mention @ NeurIPS R0-FoMo Workshop 2023</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2203.13270.pdf\" target=\"_blank\">Shoring Up the Foundations: Fusing Model Embeddings and Weak Supervision</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Oral @ UAI 2022</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n</ul>\n<p><strong>Awards</strong></p>\n<ul>\n<li>2024 DARPA Young Faculty Award</li>\n</ul>\n<hr>\n<a name=\"user-content-ines_chami\" target=\"_blank\"></a>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/ines_chami.png\" style=\"width:30%\"></figure>\n<h4>Ines Chami</h4>\n<p><em>Numbers Station | Cofounder</em></p>\n<p>Graduated from the lab 🎉 and cofounded <a href=\"https://numbersstation.ai/\" target=\"_blank\">Numbers Station</a> to apply LLMs to structured data; before that, won the 2021 Stanford Gene Golub Doctoral Dissertation Award!</p>\n<hr>\n<a name=\"user-content-jaeho_shin\" target=\"_blank\"></a>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/jaeho_shin.png\" style=\"width:30%\"></figure>\n<h4>Jaeho Shin</h4>\n<p><em>Evidently | Chief Engineer</em></p>\n<p>Cofounded <a href=\"https://evidently.com/\" target=\"_blank\">Evidently</a> to build tools that doctors and nurses love to use to improve the care they deliver to patients</p>\n<hr>\n<a name=\"user-content-jared_dunnmon\" target=\"_blank\"></a>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/jared_dunnmon.png\" style=\"width:30%\"></figure>\n<h4>Jared Dunnmon</h4>\n<p><em>Chief Scientist | Stealth Startup</em><br>\n<em>Former Technical Director for AI | Defense Innovation Unit</em></p>\n<p>Served as the first Technical Director for Artificial Intelligence at the <a href=\"https://www.diu.mil/\" target=\"_blank\">Defense Innovation Unit (DIU)</a>, during which he ran the DIU xView3 program (a public challenge resulting in ML models currently used to fight illegal fishing around the world) and built out the Emerging Capabilities Policy Office at the Pentagon. Recently cofounded a company focused on rebuilding the U.S. maritime industry.</p>\n<hr>\n<a name=\"user-content-jason_fries\" target=\"_blank\"></a>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/jason_fries.png\" style=\"width:30%\"></figure>\n<h4>Jason Fries</h4>\n<p><em>Research Scientist | Stanford University</em><br>\n<em>Snorkel AI | Advisor</em></p>\n<p>Working at the intersection of multimodal foundation models and healthcare, Jason has developed methods and datasets for unlocking the full potential of generative AI in real-world medical settings.</p>\n<p><strong>Notable Papers</strong></p>\n<ul>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2308.14089\" target=\"_blank\">MedAlign: A Clinician-Generated Dataset for Instruction Following with Electronic Medical Records</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Best Thematic Paper Award @ AHLI Machine Learning for Health Symposium 2023</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2301.03150\" target=\"_blank\">MOTOR: A Time-To-Event Foundation Model For Structured Medical Records</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Spotlight @ ICLR 2024</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.02028\" target=\"_blank\">EHRSHOT: An EHR Benchmark for Few-Shot Evaluation of Foundation Models</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Spotlight @ NeurIPS Datasets 2023</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n</ul>\n<p><strong>Awards</strong></p>\n<ul>\n<li>2021 HAI-AIMI Partnership Grant for developing multimodal patient embeddings</li>\n</ul>\n<hr>\n<a name=\"user-content-karan_goel\" target=\"_blank\"></a>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/karan_goel.png\" style=\"width:30%\"></figure>\n<h4>Karan Goel</h4>\n<p><em>Cartesia | CEO</em></p>\n<p>Taking SSMs to the moon as CEO of <a href=\"https://www.cartesia.ai/\" target=\"_blank\">Cartesia</a> after graduating from the lab last year! 🎉 Currently looking for smart ML researchers (<a href=\"https://x.com/krandiash/status/1851031250511020526\" target=\"_blank\">preferably named Brandon</a>)</p>\n<hr>\n<a name=\"user-content-khaled_saab\" target=\"_blank\"></a>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/khaled_saab.png\" style=\"width:30%\"></figure>\n<h4>Khaled Saab</h4>\n<p><em>Google DeepMind | Research Scientist</em></p>\n<p>Graduated from the lab 🎉 before joining Google DeepMind to push the state-of-the-art of using foundation models to improve healthcare through leading efforts like <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.18416\" target=\"_blank\">Med-Gemini</a> and <a href=\"https://research.google/blog/amie-a-research-ai-system-for-diagnostic-medical-reasoning-and-conversations/\" target=\"_blank\">AMIE</a>.</p>\n<p><strong>Notable Papers</strong></p>\n<ul>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.18416\" target=\"_blank\">Med-Gemini: Capabilities of Gemini Models in Medicine</a></li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2401.05654\" target=\"_blank\">AMIE: Towards Conversational Diagnostic AI</a></li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2212.14052\" target=\"_blank\">Hungry hungry hippos: Towards language modeling with state space models</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Spotlight @ ICLR 2023</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2211.11176\" target=\"_blank\">Spatiotemporal Modeling of Multivariate Signals With Graph Neural Networks and Structured State Space Models</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Best Paper @ CHIL 2023</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2203.14960\" target=\"_blank\">Domino: Discovering Systematic Errors with Cross-Modal Embeddings</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Oral @ ICLR 2022</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://khaledsaab.github.io/media/MICCAI_2019.pdf\" target=\"_blank\">Doubly Weak Supervision of Deep Learning Models for Head CT</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Oral @ MICCAI 2019</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://khaledsaab.github.io/media/ICLR_LLD_2019.pdf\" target=\"_blank\">Improving Sample Complexity with Observational Supervision</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Spotlight @ ICLR 2019 Limited Labeled Data Workshop</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n</ul>\n<hr>\n<a name=\"user-content-kun_yu\" target=\"_blank\"></a>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/kun_yu.png\" style=\"width:30%\"></figure>\n<h4>Kun-Hsing Yu</h4>\n<p><em>Harvard Medical School | Assistant Professor</em></p>\n<p>Published many <a href=\"https://yulab.hms.harvard.edu/\" target=\"_blank\">amazing papers with his lab</a> on AI and digital pathology, developed numerous tools for cancer <a href=\"https://news.harvard.edu/gazette/story/2024/09/new-ai-tool-can-diagnose-cancer-guide-treatment-predict-patient-survival/\" target=\"_blank\">diagnosis</a> and <a href=\"https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2023-07-07/ai-offers-key-information-for-treating-brain-cancer-study-finds\" target=\"_blank\">treatment</a>, and co-founded a biotech company for cancer pathology diagnosis.</p>\n<p><strong>Notable Papers</strong></p>\n<ul>\n<li><a href=\"https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-024-07894-z\" target=\"_blank\">A Pathology Foundation Model for Cancer Diagnosis and Prognostic Prediction | Nature 2024</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Nature</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://www.nejm.org/doi/10.1056/NEJMra2214183?url_ver=Z39.88-2003&#x26;rfr_id=ori:rid:crossref.org&#x26;rfr_dat=cr_pub%20%200pubmed\" target=\"_blank\">Medical Artificial Intelligence and Human Values | NEJM 2024</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 NEJM</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n</ul>\n<p><strong>Awards</strong></p>\n<ul>\n<li>NIH R01 and R35 grants</li>\n<li>Department of Defense (DoD) Career Development Award</li>\n<li>American Cancer Society Research Award</li>\n<li>Google Research Scholar Award</li>\n</ul>\n<hr>\n<a name=\"user-content-kush_bhatia\" target=\"_blank\"></a>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/kush_bhatia.png\" style=\"width:30%\"></figure>\n<h4>Kush Bhatia</h4>\n<p><em>Google Deepmind | Research Scientist</em></p>\n<p>Finished his postdoc at Hazy Research 🎉 and joined Google Deepmind</p>\n<p><strong>Notable Papers</strong></p>\n<ul>\n<li><a href=\"https://openreview.net/forum?id=h5VySv1O4q\" target=\"_blank\">TART: A plug-and-play Transformer module for task-agnostic reasoning</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Spotlight @ R0-FoMo at NeurIPS 2023</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://openreview.net/forum?id=IoizwO1NLf\" target=\"_blank\">Skill-it! A data-driven skills framework for understanding and training language models</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Spotlight @ NeurIPS 2023</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://openreview.net/forum?id=hJqGbUpDGV\" target=\"_blank\">On the Sensitivity of Reward Inference to Misspecified Human Models</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Spotlight @ ICLR 2023</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n</ul>\n<p><strong>Awards</strong></p>\n<ul>\n<li>JP Morgan PhD Fellowship 2020</li>\n</ul>\n<hr>\n<a name=\"user-content-nancy_xu\" target=\"_blank\"></a>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/nancy_xu.png\" style=\"width:30%\"></figure>\n<h4>Nancy Xu</h4>\n<p><em>Moonhub | CEO</em><br>\n<em>Xu Ventures | Founder, General Partner</em></p>\n<p>Another <a href=\"https://time.com/collection/time100-ai/6310644/nancy-xu/\" target=\"_blank\">Time 100 AI</a> honoree! Built the world's first AI Recruiter as the founder of <a href=\"https://www.moonhub.ai/\" target=\"_blank\">Moonhub</a> and is a WEF Technology Pioneer</p>\n<p><strong>Awards</strong></p>\n<ul>\n<li>2024 Time’s 100 Most Influential People in AI</li>\n<li>WEF Technology Pioneer</li>\n</ul>\n<hr>\n<a name=\"user-content-nimit_sohoni\" target=\"_blank\"></a>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/nimit_sohoni.png\" style=\"width:30%\"></figure>\n<h4>Nimit Sohoni</h4>\n<p><em>Cartesia | Researcher</em></p>\n<p>Joined <a href=\"https://www.cartesia.ai/\" target=\"_blank\">Cartesia</a> as an early member of the research staff after working at Citadel for 2 years!</p>\n<hr>\n<a name=\"user-content-sarah_hooper\" target=\"_blank\"></a>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/sarah_hooper.png\" style=\"width:30%\"></figure>\n<h4>Sarah Hooper</h4>\n<p><em>National Institutes of Health | Research Scientist</em></p>\n<p>Graduated from the lab! 🎉 Decided being an academic didn’t involve enough bureaucracy and is now a research scientist at the NIH’s Office of AI Research.</p>\n<p><strong>Notable Papers</strong></p>\n<ul>\n<li><a href=\"https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/file/ad6a3bd12095fdca71c306871bdec400-Paper-Conference.pdf\" target=\"_blank\">A case for reframing automated medical image classification as segmentation | NeurIPS 2023</a></li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2308.11194\" target=\"_blank\">Fine-Grained Vision-Language Representation Learning from Real-World Data | ICCV 2023</a></li>\n<li><a href=\"https://proceedings.mlr.press/v182/saab22a/saab22a.pdf\" target=\"_blank\">Reducing Reliance on Spurious Features in Medical Image Classification with Spatial Specificity | ML4H 2022</a></li>\n</ul>\n<p><strong>Awards</strong></p>\n<ul>\n<li>2023 Human-Centered AI and GCP Research Proposal</li>\n<li>Hertz Foundation Fellowship</li>\n</ul>\n<hr>\n<a name=\"user-content-sharon_li\" target=\"_blank\"></a>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/sharon_li.png\" style=\"width:30%\"></figure>\n<h4>Sharon Li</h4>\n<p><em>UW-Madison | Assistant Professor</em></p>\n<p>Recently named “2023 Innovator of the Year” by MIT Technology Review after running out of other awards to win. <a href=\"https://pages.cs.wisc.edu/~sharonli/group.html\" target=\"_blank\">Her lab at UW Madison</a> focuses on algorithmic and theoretical foundations of reliable machine learning, addressing challenges in both model development and deployment in the open world!</p>\n<p><strong>Notable Papers</strong></p>\n<ul>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2409.17504\" target=\"_blank\">HaloScope: Harnessing Unlabeled LLM Generations for Hallucination Detection</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Spotlight @ NeurIPS 2024</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2311.03524\" target=\"_blank\">A Graph-Theoretic Framework for Understanding Open-World Semi-Supervised Learning</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Spotlight @ NeurIPS 2023</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2303.05809\" target=\"_blank\">Distributionally Robust Optimization with Probabilistic Group</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Oral @ AAAI 2023</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2208.09083\" target=\"_blank\">Out-of-distribution Detection via Frequency-regularized Generative Models</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Spotlight @ WACV 2023</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2210.14707\" target=\"_blank\">Is Out-of-Distribution Detection Learnable?</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Outstanding Paper Award @ NeurIPS 2022</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2206.13687\" target=\"_blank\">POEM: Out-of-Distribution Detection with Posterior Sampling</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Oral @ ICML 2022</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2203.03800\" target=\"_blank\">Unknown-Aware Object Detection: Learning What You Don’t Know from Videos in the Wild</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Oral @ CVPR 2022</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://openreview.net/forum?id=EhYjZy6e1gJ\" target=\"_blank\">PiCO: Contrastive Label Disambiguation for Partial Label Learning</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Outstanding Paper Award Honorable Mention @ ICLR 2022</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2112.00787\" target=\"_blank\">Provable Guarantees for Understanding Out-of-distribution Detection</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Oral @ AAAI 2022</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2109.05642\" target=\"_blank\">On the Impact of Spurious Correlation for Out-of-distribution Detection</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Oral @ AAAI 2022</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n</ul>\n<p><strong>Awards</strong></p>\n<ul>\n<li>2023 Innovator of the Year by MIT Technology Review</li>\n<li>2023 NSF CAREER Award</li>\n<li>2022 AFOSR Young Investigator Program (YIP) Award</li>\n<li>2022 Amazon Research Award</li>\n<li>2021 Google-Initiated Focused Research Award</li>\n<li>2021 Facebook Research Award</li>\n<li>2021 JP Morgan Early-career Faculty Award</li>\n<li>2020 Forbes 30 Under 30</li>\n<li>2019 30 Under 30 Rising Stars in AI</li>\n</ul>\n<hr>\n<a name=\"user-content-silas_alberti\" target=\"_blank\"></a>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/silas_alberti.png\" style=\"width:30%\"></figure>\n<h4>Silas Alberti</h4>\n<p><em>Cognition AI | Founding Engineer</em></p>\n<p>Automating himself out of a job while building Devin as a founding engineer at <a href=\"https://www.cognition.ai/\" target=\"_blank\">Cognition AI</a></p>\n<hr>\n<a name=\"user-content-stephen_bach\" target=\"_blank\"></a>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/stephen_bach.png\" style=\"width:30%\"></figure>\n<h4>Stephen Bach</h4>\n<p><em>Brown | Assistant Professor</em><br>\n<em>Snorkel AI | Advisor</em></p>\n<p>In addition to leading the <a href=\"https://cs.brown.edu/people/sbach/\" target=\"_blank\">BATS research group</a> at Brown, co-led one of the teams that proposed instruction tuning, released the T0 family of models, and achieved top performance on zero-shot image classification in DARPA's Learning with Less Labeling program.</p>\n<p><strong>Notable Papers</strong></p>\n<ul>\n<li><a href=\"https://openreview.net/forum?id=9Vrb9D0WI4\" target=\"_blank\">T0: Multitask prompted training enables zero-shot task generalization</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Spotlight @ ICLR 2022</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.02446\" target=\"_blank\">Low-Resource Languages Jailbreak GPT-4</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Best Paper Award @ NeurIPS Workshop on Socially Responsible Language Modeling Research 2023</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/1711.10160\" target=\"_blank\">Snorkel: Rapid training data creation with weak supervision</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Best of VLDB 2018</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n</ul>\n<p><strong>Awards</strong></p>\n<ul>\n<li>Co-PI on DARPA grant “Tasks Algorithmically Given Labels Established via Transferred Symbols”</li>\n<li>NSF grant to develop an LLM-backed research assistant for comprehensive climate assessments</li>\n</ul>\n<hr>\n<a name=\"user-content-theo_rekatsinas\" target=\"_blank\"></a>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/theo_rekatsinas.png\" style=\"width:30%\"></figure>\n<h4>Theo Rekatsinas</h4>\n<p><em>Apple | Research Scientist</em></p>\n<p>Currently at Apple enabling smaller generative models to have the capabilities of frontier models via data selection methods after his data cleaning company, Inductiv, was acquired. In a previous life, was a Professor at UW Madison and ETH working on a $5M DARPA grant for scientific discovery and semantic analytics</p>\n<hr>\n<a name=\"user-content-tri_dao\" target=\"_blank\"></a>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/tri_dao.png\" style=\"width:30%\"></figure>\n<h4>Tri Dao</h4>\n<p><em>Princeton | Assistant Professor</em><br>\n<em>Together AI | Chief Scientist</em></p>\n<p>Starting <a href=\"https://tridao.me/\" target=\"_blank\">a new lab</a> at Princeton next year 🎉 after doing a stint as Chief Scientist of <a href=\"https://together.ai/\" target=\"_blank\">Together AI</a>! Made transformers significantly more scalable with FlashAttention 1/2/3, then decided to burn it all down with Mamba.</p>\n<p><strong>Notable Papers</strong></p>\n<ul>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2312.00752\" target=\"_blank\">Mamba: Linear-Time Sequence Modeling with Selective State Spaces | COLM 2024</a></li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2205.14135\" target=\"_blank\">FlashAttention: Fast and memory-efficient exact attention with IO-awareness</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Best Paper @ Hardware Aware Efficient Training Workshop at ICML 2022</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2302.10866\" target=\"_blank\">Hyena hierarchy: Towards larger convolutional language models</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Oral @ ICML 2023</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2204.00595\" target=\"_blank\">Monarch: Expressive structured matrices for efficient and accurate training</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Outstanding Paper runner-up @ ICML 2022</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2206.01288\" target=\"_blank\">Decentralized training of foundation models in heterogeneous environments</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Oral @ NeurIPS 2022</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2212.14052\" target=\"_blank\">Hungry hungry hippos: Towards language modeling with state space models</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Spotlight @ ICLR 2023</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2112.00029\" target=\"_blank\">Pixelated butterfly: Simple and efficient sparse training for neural network models</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Spotlight @ ICLR 2022</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2012.14966\" target=\"_blank\">Kaleidoscope: An efficient, learnable representation for all structured linear maps</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Spotlight @ ICLR 2020</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2008.07669\" target=\"_blank\">Hippo: Recurrent memory with optimal polynomial projections</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Spotlight @ NeurIPS 2020</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n</ul>\n<p><strong>Awards</strong></p>\n<ul>\n<li>Stanford Open Source Software Prize 2024</li>\n</ul>\n<hr>\n<a name=\"user-content-vincent_chen\" target=\"_blank\"></a>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/vincent_chen.png\" style=\"width:30%\"></figure>\n<h4>Vincent Chen</h4>\n<p><em>Snorkel AI | Founding Engineer, VP</em></p>\n<p>Still working on <a href=\"https://snorkel.ai/\" target=\"_blank\">Snorkel AI</a>!</p>\n<hr>\n<a name=\"user-content-virginia_smith\" target=\"_blank\"></a>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/virginia_smith.png\" style=\"width:30%\"></figure>\n<h4>Virginia Smith</h4>\n<p><em>Carnegie Mellon University | Associate Professor</em></p>\n<p>When not busy running <a href=\"https://www.cs.cmu.edu/~smithv/\" target=\"_blank\">her lab at CMU</a> focused on federated learning and privacy-preserving ML, won an NSF CAREER Award and Sloan Research Fellowship and came in 1st place in the 2023 UK-US Privacy-Enhancing Technologies pandemic forecasting challenge</p>\n<p><strong>Notable Papers</strong></p>\n<ul>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2203.10190\" target=\"_blank\">Fair Federated Learning via Bounded Group Loss</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Best Paper Award @ ICLR 2022 Socially Responsible ML Workshop</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2211.15458\" target=\"_blank\">Validating Large Language Models with ReLM</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Outstanding Paper Award @ MLSys 2023</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2012.04221\" target=\"_blank\">Ditto: Fair and Robust Federated Learning Through Personalization</a>\n<ul>\n<li>🏆 Best Paper Award @ ICLR 2021 Secure ML Workshop</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n</ul>\n<p><strong>Awards</strong></p>\n<ul>\n<li>NSF CAREER Award</li>\n<li>Sloan Research Fellowship</li>\n<li>2023 Samsung AI Researcher of the Year</li>\n<li>2022 Intel Rising Star</li>\n<li>MIT Technology Review's 35 Innovators Under 35</li>\n<li>1st place in the 2023 UK-US Privacy-Enhancing Technologies pandemic forecasting challenge</li>\n</ul>\n<hr>\n<p>If you're an alum and want to be added to this list or modify your information, please shoot Michael (<a href=\"mailto:mwornow@stanford.edu\" target=\"_blank\">mwornow@stanford.edu</a>) an email or Slack message!</p>,With the annual celebration of machine learning that is NeurIPS underway, we thought it would be fun to celebrate the amazing…,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-12-11-alumni-updates,<a href=\"https://cs.stanford.edu/~chrismre/\">Christopher Ré</a>,2026-07-22T18:37:38.400Z,2024-12-11T07:00:00.156Z,[{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/dancing_dinos.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1834,\"height\":1834},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/albert_gu.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/alex_ratner.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/arjun_desai.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/armin_thomas.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/arun_kumar.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/beidi_chen.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/braden_hancock.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/brandon_yang.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/ce_zhang.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/chris_desa.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/dan_fu.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/dan_fu_cat.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/feng_niu.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/fred_sala.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/ines_chami.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/jaeho_shin.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/jared_dunnmon.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/jason_fries.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/karan_goel.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/khaled_saab.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/kun_yu.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/kush_bhatia.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/nancy_xu.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/nimit_sohoni.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/sarah_hooper.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/sharon_li.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/silas_alberti.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/stephen_bach.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/theo_rekatsinas.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/tri_dao.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/vincent_chen.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-11-alumni-updates/virginia_smith.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"}],163778663386687488,1211296081845936155,Smoothie: a label-free approach for inference-time LLM routing,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-12-10-smoothie,<p><strong>TLDR</strong>: We study algorithms for LLM-routing in the inference-time setting: given open-ended generations from <em>K</em> different LLMs for an input, can we learn to select the best generation, without needing any labeled data? Our algorithm, <strong>Smoothie,</strong> accomplishes this, and we show it can achieve substantial gains over other unsupervised baselines for generation tasks (e.g., instruction-following, NLG, and more)–by up to 27 points.</p>\n<p>[<a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.04692\" target=\"_blank\">Paper (NeurIPS ‘24)</a> | <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/smoothie\" target=\"_blank\">Code</a>]</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:75%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-10-smoothie/smoothie_logo.jpg\"></figure>\n<h1>Motivation</h1>\n<p>Our work builds on two trends in LLM development and usage over the last twelve months.</p>\n<ul>\n<li>The first is the mechanism of <strong>routing</strong>. Routing leverages the idea that specific “expert” submodules, each with their own strengths and weaknesses, can work together effectively when samples are directed to the most appropriate submodule. This approach, in contrast to monolithic AI systems, enables systems to harness the collective intelligence of its components.</li>\n<li>The second is the paradigm of <strong>test-time compute</strong>.<sup id=\"user-content-fnref-1\"><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-12-10-smoothie#fn-1\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">1</a></sup> In this regime, developers are able and willing to allocate more resources at inference-time to produce high quality generations. In particular, they can produce multiple outputs from a single LLM–or multiple LLMs–for a single input.</li>\n</ul>\n<p>Recent work has begun exploring the intersection of these areas–a paradigm known as <strong>inference-time routing</strong>. For example, rather than producing a response from one model for an instruction, developers will (1) produce responses from multiple different models, and then (2) identify the best response to return to the user.  Typically, this requires labeled training data—either to simply identify the best model or to train a router, such as an MLP classifier or a KNN retrieval model. However, acquiring this labeled data in the context of model routing can be resource-intensive. For each training sample’s input, we first need to generate the responses from all the LLMs in the ensemble. Then, we must annotate each LLM generation’s quality, or write a reference output and use some scoring mechanism against this reference to score each generation (using metrics like ROUGE, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2106.11520\" target=\"_blank\">BARTScore</a>, or <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.05685\" target=\"_blank\">LLM-as-a-judge</a>. This can be time-intensive for open-ended generation—imagine manually writing out a summary of a Dostoevsky novel or comparing multiple LLM-generated summaries of it!<sup id=\"user-content-fnref-2\"><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-12-10-smoothie#fn-2\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">2</a></sup></p>\n<p>Our work introduces an algorithm for inference-time routing, which we call Smoothie. Given a test set of inputs and generations from K different LLMs for each sample in this test set, Smoothie “selects” which LLM’s generation should be used for each sample. We think Smoothie is exciting for three reasons:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>Smoothie works for open-ended generations: instruction-following, summarization, data-to-text, and more! It’s not constrained to purely classification tasks. The key innovation is that while ensembling techniques are well-established for classification, our approach can ensemble outputs for open-ended generative tasks.</li>\n<li>Smoothie does not require labels! No data needs to be annotated, compared, or ranked.</li>\n<li>Smoothie is extremely efficient, and just requires computing embeddings of every LLM’s generations with a small encoder (e.g., BERT)! No training is required!</li>\n</ul>\n<p>The remainder of this blogpost describes Smoothie at a high level, and some of our experimental results. If you’d like the more detailed version, we recommend you check out the paper!</p>\n<h1>Smoothie</h1>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-10-smoothie/smoothie.png\"></figure>\n<p>At a high-level, Smoothie treats the LLM generations for each test sample as “voters” on the unknown ground-truth label. Using an approach inspired by <a href=\"https://ai.stanford.edu/blog/weak-supervision/\" target=\"_blank\">Weak Supervision</a>, which uses probabilistic graphical models to aggregate noisy labels for annotating training datasets, Smoothie estimates a quality score for each LLM by looking at the generations’ embedding similarities (“voter agreement”). We then route the test sample to the LLM with the highest estimated score.</p>\n<p><strong>Intuition: majority vote on classification tasks.</strong> The intuition for Smoothie is rooted in the rule-of-thumb that *<em>agreement*</em> among many voters often translates into high-accuracy voters—a principle present in majority vote as well as weak supervision. As an example, suppose that we wished to determine what LLM to route to for a binary classification task like sentiment analysis. If we had 5 models and they generated <code>['positive', 'negative', 'positive', 'positive', 'positive']</code> as their responses, then a majority vote tells us that it’s likely that the sentence does have positive sentiment. Then, we could select the first, third, fourth, or fifth model to use for this sample, since they all generate the majority output. We could improve on this by using a “weighted majority vote” via <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2002.11955\" target=\"_blank\">Weak Supervision algorithms</a>, which are able to learn these weights without labeled data. As an example of intuition behind the weighted vote, suppose that model #2 tends to disagree with all other models; then, there’s a good chance it’s low-quality, and we’d want to downweight its vote.</p>\n<p><strong>Smoothie’s insight: how to vote on open-ended generation tasks.</strong> The above method described only works when we work with classification tasks, where a notion of agreement among LLM generations is simple. I.e., two models “agree” when they produce identical class predictions. But how do we extend this to generation tasks? How do we determine how much two LLM-generated summaries of Dostoevsky agree with each other?</p>\n<p>Our insight is to utilize model embeddings<sup id=\"user-content-fnref-3\"><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-12-10-smoothie#fn-3\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">3</a></sup>. Smoothie embeds each LLM generation (using an off-the-shelf embedding model), and then uses the euclidean distances between embeddings to express agreement. Smoothie determines the quality score of each LLM by using the following idea: LLM generations that have low Euclidean distances with all other generations (i.e., are close to the ‘centroid’ of all the generations) are assigned high quality scores, while outlier LLM generations that are far from other generations are assigned low quality scores.</p>\n<p>Finally, we construct two versions of Smoothie, Smoothie-Global and Smoothie-Local, where the former produces just one quality score per LLM by averaging agreements over the entire test dataset, while the latter produces a per-sample score by using just the k-nearest neighbor samples (where k=1 when we just have one test sample available). Which version to use largely depends on practitioner preference—are you happy with just figuring out the best LLM for your task, which might be easier to integrate with your pipeline, or do you want to optimize farther to find the best LLM for each task sample?</p>\n<p><strong>Bonus: the math behind Smoothie.</strong> The description above provides high-level intuition regarding Smoothie, but the more formal details are in our paper. For statistics fans though, here’s the core idea: Smoothie’s estimated quality scores are actually a consistent, method-of-moments parameter estimator for a latent variable graphical model—specifically, a multivariate Gaussian random vector. In this model, we treat the vector difference between each LLM response embedding and the unknown ground-truth embedding as a Gaussian variable with mean 0 and a diagonal covariance matrix. The covariance matrix has entries that are inversely proportional to the LLM quality score, reflecting the assumption that each LLM’s generation quality is independent. To estimate the quality scores, we draw on great work from <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2112.03865\" target=\"_blank\">Shin et. al.</a>, which exploits independence assumptions in the graphical model and allows us to bypass the need for labels to determine quality.</p>\n<h1>Results</h1>\n<p>We encourage taking a look at the full paper to learn more about our experiments, but here are some quick highlights.</p>\n<ul>\n<li><a href=\"https://tatsu-lab.github.io/alpaca_eval/\" target=\"_blank\">AlpacaEval</a> is an instruction-following benchmark, with generations of many leading LLMs available on its leaderboard. When we apply Smoothie-Global to route AlpacaEval samples to one of five LLMs, we achieve an <strong>average of 15 points improvement—and a maximum of 27 points</strong>—in win-rate against GPT-4 references, compared to randomly selecting one of the LLMs.</li>\n<li>We created a multi-task dataset (SQuAD, TriviaQA, and Definition Extraction) and applied Smoothie-Local to it using an ensemble of five 7B models. Smoothie improves over random routing by <strong>9.6 points accuracy</strong>, and also outperforms labeled baselines.</li>\n<li>Smoothie-Local routed to the highest-accuracy LLM per sample <strong>over 75% of the time</strong> across the multi-task dataset.</li>\n</ul>\n<div style=\"display:flex;justify-content:center;align-items:center;gap:20px\"><figure style=\"text-align:center;margin:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-10-smoothie/alpaca_selection_results_win_rate_diff.png\"></figure><figure style=\"text-align:center;margin:0\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-10-smoothie/acc_group_7b_ensemble_rank_relative_frequency.png\"></figure></div>\n<table><thead><tr><th>Method</th><th>Multi-task routing accuracy</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td>Random</td><td>65.4</td></tr><tr><td>PairRM</td><td>71.8</td></tr><tr><td>Labeled KNN</td><td>71.7</td></tr><tr><td>Best Model</td><td>73.2</td></tr><tr><td>Smoothie-Local</td><td><strong>75.0</strong></td></tr></tbody></table>\n<p>Check out our paper for more results, including: 1) evaluation on additional datasets like <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.02561\" target=\"_blank\">Mix-Instruct</a>, GSM8K, and summarization tasks; 2) a more efficient variant of Smoothie that does not require obtaining all the generations per test sample in advance; and 3) an application of Smoothie to prompt selection.</p>\n<h1>Future directions</h1>\n<p>If you’re interested in chatting more, please reach out to Neel Guha (<a href=\"mailto:nguha@stanford.edu\" target=\"_blank\">nguha@stanford.edu</a>) and Mayee Chen (<a href=\"mailto:mfchen@stanford.edu\" target=\"_blank\">mfchen@stanford.edu</a>)! We also encourage taking a look at <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/smoothie\" target=\"_blank\">our code</a>, especially <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/smoothie/blob/main/tutorials/tutorial.ipynb\" target=\"_blank\">this simple tutorial</a> we put together for how to easily apply Smoothie to your routing workflows.<br>\nWe’re really excited about this direction, and applying classical statistical tools to new versions of problems as they manifest in the current age of generative AI. We see Smoothie as the first step, and believe there’s room to get significantly better! For example:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>Beyond just embeddings, how can we incorporate other forms of signal to determine LLM response quality, such as reward models and unit tests?</li>\n<li>How do we make Smoothie better for structured tasks with a clearer notion of correctness? We observe some promising results on GSM8K, but we’d like to investigate this further....</li>\n</ul>\n<p>Lastly, we’d like to thank Simran Arora and Jon Saad-Falcon for their helpful feedback on this post!</p>\n<div>\n<hr>\n<ol>\n<li id=\"user-content-fn-1\">Check out some other work by our lab here, on test-time compute <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.21787\" target=\"_blank\">scaling curves</a> and leveraging test-time compute for <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2409.15254\" target=\"_blank\">compound systems</a>!<a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-12-10-smoothie#fnref-1\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">↩</a></li>\n<li id=\"user-content-fn-2\">Aside from manual annotation, using LLMs to label the quality of the training data for routing might be less time-consuming. However, it can still be costly and can also introduce additional biases, such as <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.04475\" target=\"_blank\">preferring longer generations</a>.<a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-12-10-smoothie#fnref-2\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">↩</a></li>\n<li id=\"user-content-fn-3\">Using model embeddings to improve weak supervision approaches has been explored previously for classification tasks, such as <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2203.13270\" target=\"_blank\">Liger</a> and <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.11031\" target=\"_blank\">Embroid</a>.<a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-12-10-smoothie#fnref-3\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">↩</a></li>\n</ol>\n</div>,TLDR: We study algorithms for LLM-routing in the inference-time setting: given open-ended generations from K different LLMs for an input…,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-12-10-smoothie,<a href='https://neelguha.github.io/'>Neel Guha</a>, <a href=\"https://mayeechen.github.io/\">Mayee F. Chen</a>, <a href=\"https://tmychow.com/\">Trevor Chow</a>, <a href=\"https://iskhare.github.io/\">Ishan S. Khare</a>, <a href=\"https://cs.stanford.edu/~chrismre/\">Christopher Ré</a>,2026-07-22T18:37:38.399Z,2024-12-10T07:00:00.062Z,[{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-10-smoothie/smoothie_logo.jpg\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1024,\"height\":1024},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-10-smoothie/smoothie.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-10-smoothie/alpaca_selection_results_win_rate_diff.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-12-10-smoothie/acc_group_7b_ensemble_rank_relative_frequency.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"}],163778663386687488,1211296081845936156,ThunderMittens For Your ThunderKittens,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-11-28-tk-mlx,<p><strong>Repo</strong>: <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/ThunderKittens\" target=\"_blank\">https://github.com/HazyResearch/ThunderKittens</a><br>\n<strong>With team</strong>: <a href=\"https://benjaminfspector.com/\" target=\"_blank\">Benjamin Spector</a>, <a href=\"https://arorasimran.com/\" target=\"_blank\">Simran Arora</a>, <a href=\"https://danfu.org/\" target=\"_blank\">Daniel Y. Fu</a>, <a href=\"https://cs.stanford.edu/people/chrismre/\" target=\"_blank\">Chris Ré</a></p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:75%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-11-28-tk-mlx/mittens.jpeg\"></figure>\n<p>AI has grown tremendously through the teraflops of compute that bigger and bigger GPUs have brought online. With ThunderKittens (TK), we've explored how we can write performant and cute AI kernels on the biggest and baddest AI hardware out there.</p>\n<p>While hardware utilization is undeniably crucial for large-scale AI in data centers, there's an equally exciting and transformative frontier on the other end of the spectrum: on-edge training and inference. Efficiently running ML on-edge enables greater amounts of user privacy, unlocks the development of models tailored to user-specific preferences, and invites a broader community to accelerate AI research.</p>\n<p>Inspired by these possibilities, we wanted to write AI kernels for Apple Silicon and thought it would be fun to build a TK port for MSL (Metal Shading Language). This seemed like the perfect test for TK. The general vision for the DSL was to design around fundamental hardware properties - now, we wanted to explore how these primitives would hold up when put to the test on a completely different race track: the Apple M2 Pro.</p>\n<h2>Understanding The Apple GPUs</h2>\n<p>First, we decided to take a deep dive into hardware properties:</p>\n<p>The M2 Pro has very high memory bandwidth relative to compute: ~200GB/s memory bandwidth and ~6.5 TFLOPs of compute. For comparison, the closest consumer-facing NVIDIA GPUs - the RTX 4090s - have ~1000GB/s memory bandwidth and 82.58 TFLOPs of compute. The 4090s get 2.5x flops per byte loaded!</p>\n<p>For the M2 Pros, this broadly means:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>Shared memory isn't as crucial. ALUs can be kept active by directly loading values from HBM into registers, almost never leveraging shared memory for things like reuse.</li>\n<li>Incredibly simple kernels (without fancy producer/consumer asynchrony, for example) get the job done.</li>\n<li>Swizzling isn't worth the hassle (for now). ALU ops are too precious to be used for faster memory loads, so the simple and reasonable solution to bank conflicts is padding.</li>\n<li>Limited support for bf16. The compiler generally struggles to optimize bf16 ops. This led us down the unfortunate path of using some, shall we say, \"aesthetically challenged\" code to force the compiler's hand (i.e., meta template loop unrolling).</li>\n<li>Occupancy matters. A lot. Writing a fast FA kernel for D=128 is no walk in the park. Register use goes up, occupancy goes down, and performance takes a nosedive (yes, it's a delicate balancing act).</li>\n</ul>\n<h2>Porting ThunderKittens To Metal</h2>\n<p>So, we present... 🥁🥁🥁 ThunderMittens!!! The Apple Metal port for ThunderKittens</p>\n<p>Most of TK was developed on H100s and most of its kernels are optimized for H100s. While the H100s and the Apple M2 Pro might seem like they're from different worlds, we were surprised to see how well our primitives held up.</p>\n<p>How many major abstraction changes did we have to make?\nOne.\nWait, really?\nYep, that's it.</p>\n<p>The only major change a TK user needs to pay attention to is going from 16x16 base tiles to 8x8 base tiles. We do this because the M2 Pro can maximally allocate 128 registers per thread. The 8x8 base tiles also work nicely with the <code>metal::simd_matrix&#x3C;T,8,8></code> intrinsic.</p>\n<p>Under the hood, relevant internals are modified/removed:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>Register layouts. The metal::simdgroup_multiply_accumulate intrinsic expects different layouts from the NVIDIA WGMMA instructions. Fortunately, a little shuffle dance does the trick.</li>\n<li>Swizzling. As discussed above, swizzling is disabled in ThunderMittens—the address calculations just can't be justified given the limited compute and speedy memory.</li>\n<li>WGMMA &#x26; TMA: These instructions are NVIDIA specific.</li>\n<li>Async loads/stores: These are currently deprecated on the M2s—we got things working without them.</li>\n</ul>\n<p>With these changes, writing SOTA/near-SOTA performance Apple Metal kernels is a breeze. The true differences in hardware shine through not in the abstractions themselves, but in how they're used!</p>\n<h3>GEMM Kernel Implementation</h3>\n<p>See our GEMM kernel here:</p>\n<div><pre><code class=\"language-cpp\"><span>namespace</span> mittens <span>{</span>\n<span>template</span><span>&#x3C;</span><span>typename</span> <span>T</span><span>,</span> <span>unsigned</span> N_BLOCK<span>,</span> <span>unsigned</span> K_BLOCK<span>,</span> <span>unsigned</span> M_BLOCK<span>></span>\nkernel <span>void</span> <span>matmul</span><span>(</span>device T<span>*</span> D <span>[</span><span>[</span><span>buffer</span><span>(</span><span>0</span><span>)</span><span>]</span><span>]</span><span>,</span>\n                   device T<span>*</span> A <span>[</span><span>[</span><span>buffer</span><span>(</span><span>1</span><span>)</span><span>]</span><span>]</span><span>,</span>\n                   device T<span>*</span> B <span>[</span><span>[</span><span>buffer</span><span>(</span><span>2</span><span>)</span><span>]</span><span>]</span><span>,</span>\n                   <span>const</span> constant <span>int</span> <span>&#x26;</span>N <span>[</span><span>[</span><span>buffer</span><span>(</span><span>3</span><span>)</span><span>]</span><span>]</span><span>,</span>\n                   <span>const</span> constant <span>int</span> <span>&#x26;</span>K <span>[</span><span>[</span><span>buffer</span><span>(</span><span>4</span><span>)</span><span>]</span><span>]</span><span>,</span>\n                   <span>const</span> constant <span>int</span> <span>&#x26;</span>M <span>[</span><span>[</span><span>buffer</span><span>(</span><span>5</span><span>)</span><span>]</span><span>]</span><span>,</span>\n                   uint3 tg_id        <span>[</span><span>[</span>threadgroup_position_in_grid<span>]</span><span>]</span><span>,</span>\n                   uint  simd_lane_id <span>[</span><span>[</span>thread_index_in_simdgroup<span>]</span><span>]</span><span>)</span> <span>{</span>\n    <span>using</span> global_layout <span>=</span> gl<span>&#x3C;</span>T<span>,</span> <span>1</span><span>,</span> <span>1</span><span>,</span> <span>-</span><span>1</span><span>,</span> <span>-</span><span>1</span><span>></span><span>;</span>\n    global_layout <span>gl_a</span><span>(</span>A<span>,</span> <span>nullptr</span><span>,</span> <span>nullptr</span><span>,</span> N<span>,</span> K<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    global_layout <span>gl_b</span><span>(</span>B<span>,</span> <span>nullptr</span><span>,</span> <span>nullptr</span><span>,</span> K<span>,</span> M<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    global_layout <span>gl_d</span><span>(</span>D<span>,</span> <span>nullptr</span><span>,</span> <span>nullptr</span><span>,</span> N<span>,</span> M<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    rt<span>&#x3C;</span>T<span>,</span>     N_BLOCK <span>*</span> TILE_DIM<span>,</span> K_BLOCK <span>*</span> TILE_DIM<span>></span> a_reg<span>;</span>\n    rt<span>&#x3C;</span>T<span>,</span>     K_BLOCK <span>*</span> TILE_DIM<span>,</span> M_BLOCK <span>*</span> TILE_DIM<span>></span> b_reg<span>;</span>\n    rt<span>&#x3C;</span><span>float</span><span>,</span> N_BLOCK <span>*</span> TILE_DIM<span>,</span> M_BLOCK <span>*</span> TILE_DIM<span>></span> d_reg<span>;</span>\n    <span>zero</span><span>(</span>d_reg<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span><span>#</span><span>pragma</span> <span>clang loop <span>unroll</span><span>(</span>full<span>)</span></span></span>\n    <span>for</span> <span>(</span><span>int</span> k <span>=</span> <span>0</span><span>;</span> k <span>&#x3C;</span> K <span>/</span> <span>(</span>K_BLOCK <span>*</span> TILE_DIM<span>)</span><span>;</span> k<span>++</span><span>)</span> <span>{</span>\n        kittens<span>::</span>ore<span>::</span><span>load</span><span>(</span>a_reg<span>,</span> gl_a<span>,</span> <span>{</span><span>0</span><span>,</span> <span>0</span><span>,</span> <span>(</span><span>int</span><span>)</span>tg_id<span>.</span>y<span>,</span> k<span>}</span><span>,</span> simd_lane_id<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n        kittens<span>::</span>ore<span>::</span><span>load</span><span>(</span>b_reg<span>,</span> gl_b<span>,</span> <span>{</span><span>0</span><span>,</span> <span>0</span><span>,</span> k<span>,</span> <span>(</span><span>int</span><span>)</span>tg_id<span>.</span>x<span>}</span><span>,</span> simd_lane_id<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n        <span>mma_AB</span><span>(</span>d_reg<span>,</span> a_reg<span>,</span> b_reg<span>,</span> d_reg<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>}</span>\n    <span>store</span><span>(</span>gl_d<span>,</span> d_reg<span>,</span> <span>{</span><span>0</span><span>,</span> <span>0</span><span>,</span> <span>(</span><span>int</span><span>)</span>tg_id<span>.</span>y<span>,</span> <span>(</span><span>int</span><span>)</span>tg_id<span>.</span>x<span>}</span><span>,</span> simd_lane_id<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n<span>}</span>\n<span>}</span>\n</code></pre></div>\n<h3>Attention Inference Kernel</h3>\n<p>And our standard attention inference kernel:</p>\n<div><pre><code class=\"language-cpp\"><span>template</span><span>&#x3C;</span><span>int</span> D<span>></span>\nkernel <span>void</span> <span>attend_ker</span><span>(</span>constant <span>unsigned</span> <span>&#x26;</span>N <span>[</span><span>[</span><span>buffer</span><span>(</span><span>0</span><span>)</span><span>]</span><span>]</span><span>,</span> constant <span>unsigned</span> <span>&#x26;</span>H <span>[</span><span>[</span><span>buffer</span><span>(</span><span>1</span><span>)</span><span>]</span><span>]</span><span>,</span>\n                       device bf16<span>*</span> q <span>[</span><span>[</span><span>buffer</span><span>(</span><span>2</span><span>)</span><span>]</span><span>]</span><span>,</span> device bf16<span>*</span> k <span>[</span><span>[</span><span>buffer</span><span>(</span><span>3</span><span>)</span><span>]</span><span>]</span><span>,</span> \n                       device bf16<span>*</span> v <span>[</span><span>[</span><span>buffer</span><span>(</span><span>4</span><span>)</span><span>]</span><span>]</span><span>,</span> device bf16<span>*</span> o <span>[</span><span>[</span><span>buffer</span><span>(</span><span>5</span><span>)</span><span>]</span><span>]</span><span>,</span>\n                       uint3 blockIdx <span>[</span><span>[</span>threadgroup_position_in_grid<span>]</span><span>]</span><span>,</span> \n                       uint laneId <span>[</span><span>[</span>thread_index_in_simdgroup<span>]</span><span>]</span><span>)</span> <span>{</span>\n    <span>static_assert</span><span>(</span>D <span>==</span> <span>64</span> <span>||</span> D <span>==</span> <span>128</span><span>,</span> <span>\"D must be 64 or 128\"</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>using</span> global_layout <span>=</span> kittens<span>::</span>ore<span>::</span>gl<span>&#x3C;</span>bfloat<span>,</span> <span>1</span><span>,</span> <span>-</span><span>1</span><span>,</span> <span>-</span><span>1</span><span>,</span> D<span>></span><span>;</span>\n    global_layout <span>gl_q</span><span>(</span>q<span>,</span> <span>nullptr</span><span>,</span> H<span>,</span> N<span>,</span> <span>nullptr</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    global_layout <span>gl_k</span><span>(</span>k<span>,</span> <span>nullptr</span><span>,</span> H<span>,</span> N<span>,</span> <span>nullptr</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    global_layout <span>gl_v</span><span>(</span>v<span>,</span> <span>nullptr</span><span>,</span> H<span>,</span> N<span>,</span> <span>nullptr</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    global_layout <span>gl_o</span><span>(</span>o<span>,</span> <span>nullptr</span><span>,</span> H<span>,</span> N<span>,</span> <span>nullptr</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    rt_bf<span>&#x3C;</span><span>8</span><span>,</span> D<span>></span> q_reg<span>,</span> v_reg<span>;</span>\n    rt_bf<span>&#x3C;</span><span>8</span><span>,</span> D<span>,</span> ducks<span>::</span>rt_layout<span>::</span>col<span>></span> k_reg<span>;</span>\n    rt_fl<span>&#x3C;</span><span>8</span><span>,</span> <span>8</span><span>></span> att_block<span>;</span>\n    rt_fl<span>&#x3C;</span><span>8</span><span>,</span> D<span>></span> o_reg<span>;</span>\n    rt_fl<span>&#x3C;</span><span>8</span><span>,</span> <span>8</span><span>></span><span>::</span>col_vec max_vec<span>,</span> max_vec_last<span>,</span> norm_vec<span>;</span>\n    \n    <span>const</span> <span>int</span> block <span>=</span> blockIdx<span>.</span>z<span>,</span> head <span>=</span> blockIdx<span>.</span>y<span>;</span>\n    <span>const</span> <span>int</span> q_seq <span>=</span> blockIdx<span>.</span>x<span>;</span>\n    <span>const</span> <span>int</span> kv_blocks <span>=</span> N <span>/</span> v_reg<span>.</span>rows<span>;</span>\n    <span>load</span><span>(</span>q_reg<span>,</span> gl_q<span>,</span> <span>{</span>block<span>,</span> head<span>,</span> q_seq<span>,</span> <span>0</span><span>}</span><span>,</span> laneId<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>neg_infty</span><span>(</span>max_vec<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>zero</span><span>(</span>norm_vec<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>zero</span><span>(</span>o_reg<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>constexpr</span> <span>const</span> bf16 q_mul <span>=</span> <span>(</span><span>(</span>D <span>==</span> <span>128</span><span>)</span> <span>?</span> <span>0.08838834764</span>bf <span>:</span> <span>0.125</span>bf<span>)</span> <span>*</span> <span>1.44269504089</span>bf<span>;</span>\n    <span>mul</span><span>(</span>q_reg<span>,</span> q_reg<span>,</span> q_mul<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span><span>#</span><span>pragma</span> <span>clang loop <span>unroll</span><span>(</span>full<span>)</span></span></span>\n    <span>for</span><span>(</span><span>auto</span> kv_idx <span>=</span> <span>0</span><span>;</span> kv_idx <span>&#x3C;</span> kv_blocks<span>;</span> kv_idx<span>++</span><span>)</span> <span>{</span>\n        <span>load</span><span>(</span>k_reg<span>,</span> gl_k<span>,</span> <span>{</span>block<span>,</span> head<span>,</span> kv_idx<span>,</span> <span>0</span><span>}</span><span>,</span> laneId<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n        <span>zero</span><span>(</span>att_block<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n        <span>mma_ABt</span><span>(</span>att_block<span>,</span> q_reg<span>,</span> k_reg<span>,</span> att_block<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n        <span>copy</span><span>(</span>max_vec_last<span>,</span>  max_vec<span>,</span> laneId<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n        <span>row_max</span><span>(</span>max_vec<span>,</span> att_block<span>,</span> max_vec<span>,</span> laneId<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n        <span>sub</span><span>(</span>max_vec_last<span>,</span> max_vec_last<span>,</span> max_vec<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n        <span>exp2</span><span>(</span>max_vec_last<span>,</span> max_vec_last<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n        <span>sub_row</span><span>(</span>att_block<span>,</span> att_block<span>,</span> max_vec<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n        <span>exp2</span><span>(</span>att_block<span>,</span> att_block<span>)</span><span>;</span>        \n        <span>mul</span><span>(</span>norm_vec<span>,</span> norm_vec<span>,</span> max_vec_last<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n        <span>row_sum</span><span>(</span>norm_vec<span>,</span> att_block<span>,</span> norm_vec<span>,</span> laneId<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n        <span>mul_row</span><span>(</span>o_reg<span>,</span> o_reg<span>,</span> max_vec_last<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n        <span>load</span><span>(</span>v_reg<span>,</span> gl_v<span>,</span> <span>{</span>block<span>,</span> head<span>,</span> kv_idx<span>,</span> <span>0</span><span>}</span><span>,</span> laneId<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n        <span>mma_AB</span><span>(</span>o_reg<span>,</span> att_block<span>,</span> v_reg<span>,</span> o_reg<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>}</span>\n    <span>div_row</span><span>(</span>o_reg<span>,</span> o_reg<span>,</span> norm_vec<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>store</span><span>(</span>gl_o<span>,</span> o_reg<span>,</span> <span>{</span>block<span>,</span> head<span>,</span> q_seq<span>,</span> <span>0</span><span>}</span><span>,</span> laneId<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n<span>}</span>\n</code></pre></div>\n<h2>Performance Results</h2>\n<p>So, how do these perform? The attention kernel is within +- 15% of MLX's implementation and our GEMM is ~9% faster on most sizes.</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-11-28-tk-mlx/attn_64.png\"></figure>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-11-28-tk-mlx/attn_128.png\"></figure>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-11-28-tk-mlx/gemm.png\"></figure>\n<p>Importantly, the kernels continue to be simple to write. While the <a href=\"https://github.com/ml-explore/mlx/blob/main/mlx/backend/metal/kernels/steel/gemm/kernels/steel_gemm_fused.h\" target=\"_blank\">MLX GEMM implementation</a> takes over a hundred lines of code to write, ours gets the job done in 11. The DSL's abstractions coalesce the differences between Metal and CUDA, such that our attention kernels on 4090s and the M2s are effectively identical (<a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/ThunderKittens/blob/main/kernels/attn/demo/4090.cu\" target=\"_blank\">4090 attention</a>). Since both kernels are implementations of the FA algorithm, they consist of the same set of TK tile-based ops. However, the NVIDIA 4090s and M2 Pro GPU are also very different from each other, and that's alright: in TK, you can do things like swap in HBM-to-register loads for Metal (vs HBM-SMEM loads for 4090s). The DSL acknowledges that different pieces of hardware may very well want to run the same algorithm differently.</p>\n<h2>Writing Metal Kernels</h2>\n<p>Despite the similarities in our TK kernels, there are notable differences in how we write kernels in Apple Vs NVIDIA. Most development was done in XCode. This gave us easy access to profiling through tools like the XCode Metal GPU Debugger. While distinct from NCU, the debugger has proven invaluable for ThunderMittens. We attach an example below of what we were staring at for 90% of our dev time.</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-11-28-tk-mlx/profile.png\"></figure>\n<p>We often found that the statistics provided to us by the debugger were not enough for thorough profiling. Repetitive trial and error seemed to be best for detailed understandings of our primitives.</p>\n<h2>More To Come...</h2>\n<p>Even with our notable results, the Apple MLX port is still in its early stages. There are many more architectures (Based linear attention, FFTConv, Mamba) that we’d like to write kernels for in ThunderMittens. We’re also excited about how the primitives change with the M3s and M4s. We’re always open to and would love to support open-source contributions to the repo!</p>,Repo: https://github.com/HazyResearch/ThunderKittens With team: Benjamin Spector, Simran Arora, Daniel Y. Fu, Chris Ré\n\nAI has grown…,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-11-28-tk-mlx,<a href='https://www.linkedin.com/in/conner-takehana/'>Conner Takehana</a>, <a href=\"https://www.aaryan-singhal.com/\">Aaryan Singhal</a>,2026-07-22T18:37:38.398Z,2024-11-28T07:00:00.046Z,[{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-11-28-tk-mlx/mittens.jpeg\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1024,\"height\":1024},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-11-28-tk-mlx/attn_64.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-11-28-tk-mlx/attn_128.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-11-28-tk-mlx/gemm.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-11-28-tk-mlx/profile.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"}],163778663386687488,1211296081845936157,ThunderKittens: Bringing fp8 to theaters near you,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-11-27-tk-fp8,<p><strong>Repo</strong>: <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/ThunderKittens\" target=\"_blank\">https://github.com/HazyResearch/ThunderKittens</a><br>\n<strong>With team</strong>: <a href=\"https://benjaminfspector.com/\" target=\"_blank\">Benjamin Spector</a>, <a href=\"https://www.aaryan-singhal.com/\" target=\"_blank\">Aaryan Singhal</a>, <a href=\"https://danfu.org/\" target=\"_blank\">Daniel Y. Fu</a>, <a href=\"https://cs.stanford.edu/people/chrismre/\" target=\"_blank\">Chris Ré</a></p>\n<p>Feature update! We’ve gotten a lot of requests for supporting quantized data types in ThunderKittens. Today, we’re happy to bring you fp8 support and fp8 kernels! This post dives into our approach.</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:30%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-11-27-tk-fp8/cat_fire.jpeg\"></figure>\n<h2>fp8 gemm</h2>\n<p>1500 TFLOPS in 95 lines of code. Checkout our kernel <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/ThunderKittens/tree/main/kernels/matmul/FP8\" target=\"_blank\">here</a>, <code>make &#x26;&#x26; ./matmul</code>!</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-11-27-tk-fp8/gemm_fp8_len2tflops.png\">fp8 gemm in tk</figure>\n<h2>implementation details</h2>\n<p>The main goal for TK, has always been to make it easier to write kernels for and do research on new architectures. That being said, there are key differences across the data types (fp8, fp16, ...) that could make things messy. Rather than versioning our TK library functions for each data type and requiring developers to think about each data type differently when programming, we want to find a way of incorporating fp8, while maintaining a simple, unified programming experience. For those of you who are interested in learning a bit about our approach to implementing fp8 support, let’s dive in!</p>\n<p><strong>Refresher on TK’s tile data structure</strong>: The most basic data structure in TK is a <span><span><span><math>16×1616\\times16</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>16</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>×</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>16</span></span></span></span></span> tile. We have tiles for both shared and register memory. Our primary goal, given TK’s focus on supporting AI use cases, is to make the tile compatible with tensor core instructions. A tensor core executes GEMMs. A GEMM (matrix-multiply accumulate, MMA) is:</p>\n<div align=\"center\"><p><span><span><span><math>D=AB+CD = AB + C</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">D</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.7667em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>A</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05017em\">B</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>+</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">C</span></span></span></span></span>,</p></div>\n<p>for accumulator matrix <span><span><span><math>DD</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">D</span></span></span></span></span>, and input matrices <span><span><span><math>AA</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span>A</span></span></span></span></span> (row-major), <span><span><span><math>BB</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05017em\">B</span></span></span></span></span> (column-major), and <span><span><span><math>CC</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">C</span></span></span></span></span> (row-major). Typically, <span><span><span><math>DD</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">D</span></span></span></span></span> is in a higher precision, like fp32 or fp16.</p>\n<h3>launching warp-level gemms</h3>\n<p>Let's start with the basic MMA instruction, where warps / groups of 32 threads execute GEMMs on matrices held in register memory. The GPU instruction expects particular layouts for matrices <span><span><span><math>A,B,C,A, B, C,</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8778em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span>A</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05017em\">B</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">C</span><span>,</span></span></span></span></span> and <span><span><span><math>DD</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">D</span></span></span></span></span>.
