2024-10-29-tk2/sunny.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"}],163778663386687488,1211296081845936160,LoLCATs Blog Part 2: How to Linearize LLMs for Me and You,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-10-14-lolcats-p2,<p><strong>Full team</strong>: <a href=\"https://michaelzhang.xyz/\" target=\"_blank\">Michael</a>, <a href=\"https://arorasimran.com/\" target=\"_blank\">Simran</a>, <a href=\"https://chalamala.com/\" target=\"_blank\">Rahul</a>, <a href=\"https://alanwu.xyz/\" target=\"_blank\">Alan</a>, <a href=\"https://benjaminfspector.com/\" target=\"_blank\">Ben</a>,  <a href=\"https://www.aaryan-singhal.com/\" target=\"_blank\">Aaryan</a>, <a href=\"https://x.com/KrithikTweets\" target=\"_blank\">Krithik</a>, and <a href=\"https://cs.stanford.edu/people/chrismre/\" target=\"_blank\">Chris Ré</a>.</p>\n<p><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2410.10254\" target=\"_blank\"><strong>Paper</strong></a> | <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/lolcats\" target=\"_blank\"><strong>Code</strong></a> | <a href=\"https://huggingface.co/collections/hazyresearch/lolcats-670ca4341699355b61238c37\" target=\"_blank\"><strong>Models</strong></a> | <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-10-14-lolcats-p1\" target=\"_blank\"><strong>Part 1</strong></a></p>\n<hr>\n<p>In <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-10-14-lolcats-p1\" target=\"_blank\">Part 1</a>, we shared LoLCATs, a new way to linearize high-quality LLMs on much more accessible compute. We’ll now share more about the method and our results.</p>\n<h3>What’s linearizing? And is linearizing all we need?</h3>\n<p>When we started working on LoLCATs, we really just wanted 7B+ LLMs with three things: competitive quality, subquadratic efficiency, and the ability for us to actually create them.</p>\n<p>There's been a ton of exciting recent work on high quality and highly-efficient architectures, but we still don't have ways to easily scale these up, especially to modern LLM regimes.\nDoing things the typical way---i.e., training new architectures from scratch---would mean training 7B+ parameters on trillions of tokens (so far, 1 - 15 trillion!).\nWe didn't have this compute budget lying around (remember, we're fighting for one of <a href=\"https://x.com/tsarnick/status/1789052769032138786\" target=\"_blank\">64 A100s</a>).</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-10-14-lolcats/low-gpus.png\"></figure>\n<p>Alternatively, we could \"linearize\" (also known before as <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2103.13076\" target=\"_blank\">\"linear conversion\"</a> or <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2004.05150\" target=\"_blank\">\"continued pretraining\"</a>).\nWe'd start with existing pretrained Transformers, replace their self-attentions with efficient analogs (such as linear attentions), and continue training to adjust to the transplanted layers.\nIn our own work, we started exploring linearizing LLMs in <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.04347\" target=\"_blank\">Hedgehog</a>, linearizing Llama 2 7B for a summarization task. Excitingly, folks at TRI showed how to\nlinearize Llama 2 7B and Mistral 7B to recover <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2405.06640\" target=\"_blank\"><em>general zero-shot</em> LM capabilities</a>! And our lab alums more recently shared <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2408.10189\" target=\"_blank\">similar</a> <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2408.15237\" target=\"_blank\">work</a>\ndistilling 1.3B and 8B Transformer LLMs into their favorite <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2312.00752\" target=\"_blank\">Mamba</a> <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2405.21060\" target=\"_blank\">architectures</a>.</p>\n<p>Unfortunately, linearizing was not all we needed just yet. The proposed ways to linearize LLMs were still too expensive and left quality on the table. All prior methods involved fully training 7B parameter LLMs on billions of tokens after swapping attentions. Their linearized models also underperformed the original Transformers by 1.7 to 8.0 points on popular LM Eval tasks (up to 28.2 points on MMLU!). Could we close this gap?</p>\n<hr>\n<h3>LoLCATs: adding more L's to get more W's</h3>\n<p>In short, we could. Our main idea was to add three simple concepts to linearizing Transformers:</p>\n<ol>\n<li>Learnable (Linear) Attentions</li>\n<li>Low-rank Adaptation</li>\n<li>Layer-wise Optimization</li>\n</ol>\n<h4>1. Learning (Linear) Attentions</h4>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0;padding-bottom:0\"><img style=\"width:80%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-10-14-lolcats/softmax_to_linear.png\"></figure>\n<p>We first had to pick what to swap out softmax attentions for. To make linearizing easier, we wondered if we could simply use <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2006.16236\" target=\"_blank\">linear attentions</a>, which would give us subquadratic efficiency while also being architecturally similar.</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0;padding-bottom:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-10-14-lolcats/linear_attn_memes.png\"></figure>\n<p style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:-10px;color:grey\"></p><p>If you know... you know.</p><p></p>\n<p>While simple, prior works have suggested this might not be enough, where linear attentions often struggle to match softmax attention in expressivity <sup id=\"user-content-fnref-1\"><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-10-14-lolcats-p2#fn-1\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">1</a></sup><sup id=\"user-content-fnref-2\"><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-10-14-lolcats-p2#fn-2\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">2</a></sup></p>\n<p>However, we found we could first simply <em>train</em> subquadratic attentions to mimic and replace softmax attention.\nThis \"attention transfer\" was inspired by our earlier <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.04347\" target=\"_blank\">Hedgehog work</a>.\nThere, instead of manually designing linear attention feature maps <span><span><span><math>ϕ\\phi</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8889em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span>ϕ</span></span></span></span></span> to be good softmax approximators,\nwe just set them as learnable layers. Surprisingly(?), we found we could explicitly train these layers to\nlearn whatever functions were important to produce good softmax attention approximations.</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:50%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-10-14-lolcats/hedgehog_llamas.png\"></figure>\n<p style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:-10px;color:grey\"></p><p>This is the critical picture of what we're doing here. One linearized Llama 8B is just 32 hedgehogs trained in a trenchcoat.</p><p></p>\n<p></p>\n<p>For LoLCATs, we made two simple improvements to linearize LLMs:</p>\n<ol>\n<li>While in Hedgehog, we only used learnable linear attentions, with LoLCATs we generalized to learnable linear-attention-and-sliding-window hybrids. This was inspired by our earlier <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.18668\" target=\"_blank\">Based</a> work, where some local softmax attention helped improve quality. But now we unify the linear and softmax attentions in a single fully subquadratic layer. And train these layers to approximate softmax attention as whole. For an <span><span><span><math>NN</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span></span></span></span></span>-token sequence, the first <span><span><span><math>WW</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">W</span></span></span></span></span> tokens get softmax attention, the remaining <span><span><span><math>N−WN-W</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7667em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>−</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">W</span></span></span></span></span> get linear attention, and the values get combined as a learned weighted sum.</li>\n</ol>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:50%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-10-14-lolcats/lolcats_linear_swa.png\"></figure>\n<p style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0;color:grey\"></p><p>We support two sliding window implementations. (a) The usual implementation, and (b) a “terraced” window that exploits the <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-05-12-quick-tk\" target=\"_blank\">ThunderKittens DSL</a>’s primitives for fast CUDA kernels. Here we prefer contiguous blocks of size 64.</p><p></p>\n<ol start=\"2\">\n<li>While in Hedgehog we trained the feature maps to match on attention weights (via a KL divergence), we found we could also use an MSE loss on the outputs of attention layers. This gets around a limitation of Hedgehog where we needed to instantiate all <span><span><span><math>N2N^2</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>2</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> attention weights as \"targets\" to supervise. Instead, we can now use <a href=\"https://github.com/Dao-AILab/flash-attention\" target=\"_blank\">FlashAttention</a> to compute softmax attention outputs, and keep attention transfer in <span><span><span><math>O(N)O(N)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> memory land.</li>\n</ol>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-10-14-lolcats/sample_attention_weights.png\"></figure>\n<p style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0;color:grey\"></p><p>We really love our mathematical ways to approximate attention, but sometimes it pays off to just put our deep learning hats on. Like here, where we found we could simply learn how to approximate softmax with trainable linear attentions.</p><p></p>\n<p>In all of the above, we create the linearized LLMs by simply inserting these feature maps into each existing attention. We only train these feature maps while freezing all other weights, amounting to just 0.2% of a 7B LLM's parameter counts.</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:80%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-10-14-lolcats/lolcats_step_0_1.png\"></figure>\n<h4>2. Low-rank Adaptation</h4>\n<p></p>\n<p>With attention transfer we could train linear attentions to approximate softmax attentions. However, we still needed to do a bit more finetuning to \"reconnect the wires\" and get a coherent model.\nFortunately, with LoLCATS we could do so by simply applying low-rank adaptation (<a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2106.09685\" target=\"_blank\">LoRA</a>) to attention QKVO weights. Keeping all else frozen, we train the LoRA weights so\nthe LLM outputs minimize a next-token prediction loss over some natural language data. Then we’re done!</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:50%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-10-14-lolcats/lolcats_step_2.png\"></figure>\n<h4>3. Layer-wise Optimization</h4>\n<p>At least for most cases. To help scale up LoLCATs to 405B LLMs, we had to add a third \"L\". While we could successfully linearize 7B+ and 70B LLMs by simply optimizing all layers jointly during attention transfer,\nthis led to later layers having much higher attention MSEs than earlier ones. The larger MSEs escalated into a real problem for Llama 3.1 405B.</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:80%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-10-14-lolcats/layerwise_mse.png\"></figure>\n<p>To resolve this and improve layer-wise attention transfer, we used a finer-grained \"block-by-block\" (or <em>cria-by-cria</em> <sup id=\"user-content-fnref-3\"><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-10-14-lolcats-p2#fn-3\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">3</a></sup>) training setup. We split Llama 3.1 405B into blocks of <span><span><span><math>kk</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span></span></span></span></span> layers and jointly trained the attentions only within each block.\nTo train all blocks in parallel, with some linearizing data we simply precomputed LLM hidden states every <span><span><span><math>kk</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span></span></span></span></span> layers, giving us the inputs for each block.</p>\n<p>We pick <span><span><span><math>kk</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span></span></span></span></span> to balance the speed of parallel training with the memory of precomputing and saving hidden states to disk.\nNo fancy cost models here, but if we wanted to linearize with 50M tokens:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>At <span><span><span><math>kk</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span></span></span></span></span> = 1, we’d need 2 bytes <span><span><span><math>×\\times</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6667em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>×</span></span></span></span></span> 126 layers <span><span><span><math>×\\times</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6667em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>×</span></span></span></span></span> 50M tokens <span><span><span><math>×\\times</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6667em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>×</span></span></span></span></span> 16384 hidden size = 200TB of disk space to store the hidden states!</li>\n<li>At <span><span><span><math>kk</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span></span></span></span></span> = 9, we cut this disk space down to just 22 TB, while still being able to train each 9-layer block on its own single GPU in parallel.</li>\n</ul>\n<p>The latter sounded better to us. By splitting Llama 3.1 405B's 126 layers into 14 9-layer blocks, we could parallelize attention transfer on 14 different GPUs. This took just 5 hours. Then we stitched them all together with LoRA to get the final model.</p>\n<hr>\n<h3>Results</h3>\n<figure></figure>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:40%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-10-14-lolcats/lolcat_results_open.png\"></figure>\n<p>We now share some of our results, where LoLCATs improves the quality, training efficiency, and scalability of linearizing LLMs.</p>\n<h4>Closing the linearizing quality gap</h4>\n<p>As a first test, we evaluated how LoLCATS compared to other linearizing methods at the popular 7B+ LLM scale.\nAcross Mistral 7B v0.1 and Llama 3 8B LLMs, despite only training 0.2% of the model parameters on\n40M tokens, LoLCATs closes <span><span><span><math>>></math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.5782em;vertical-align:-0.0391em\"></span><span>></span></span></span></span></span>80% of the linearizing quality gap averaged across popular LM Evaluation\nHarness tasks. This outperforms concurrent methods needing 500 - 2500<span><span><span><math>×\\times</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6667em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>×</span></span></span></span></span> the tokens. For the first time,\nLoLCATs further outperforms linearized hybrids with 50% of their layers being full softmax attention,\nwhile also closing the gap with the original 100% softmax attention Transformers on non-MMLU tasks.</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-10-14-lolcats/lolcats_comparison_linearize.png\"></figure>\n<p><strong>Pushing linearized LLMs into state-of-the-art territory</strong><br>\nAlong the way, we also found that LoLCATs could create state-of-the-art subquadratic LLMs in general. By converting available Transformers like Llama 3 8B or Mistral 7B, we created subquadratic LLMs that outperformed strong pretrained 7B Transformer alternatives by 1.2 to 9.9 higher LM Eval points (averaged over tasks). Furthermore, rather than pretrain on trillions of tokens, we got there with the same cost as a parameter-efficient finetune, using only 40M tokens no less (a 7,500 to 35,500<span><span><span><math>×\\times</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6667em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>×</span></span></span></span></span> boost in \"tokens-to-model\" efficiency).</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:80%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-10-14-lolcats/lolcats_quality_efficiency.png\"></figure>\n<h3>Drastically reducing linearizing costs</h3>\n<p>As alluded to above, with LoLCATs we could create state-of-the-art subquadratic LLMs with a fraction of prior training costs. Linearizing 7B and 8B LLMs required training just 0.2% of their model parameter counts on 40M tokens, doable in ~5 hours on a single 40GB A100. In our paper, we found that finding close softmax attention approximators was crucial to this efficiency boost. Across various prior linear attentions, by first training to approximate softmax attentions via attention transfer, we could rapidly speed up recovering linearized language modeling quality. We also found attention transfer alone wasn't enough. But fortunately only a few LoRA updates were all we needed to get the models talking!</p>\n<figure></figure>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-10-14-lolcats/lora_recovery.png\"></figure>\n<h3>Scaling up linearizing to 70B and 405B LLMs</h3>\n<figure></figure>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-10-14-lolcats/linearized_llama3_1_fam.png\"></figure>\n<p>Finally, to really just see what these quality and training efficiency gains could do, we used LoLCATs to linearize the complete Llama 3.1 8B family. In the process, we created the first linearized 70B and 405B LLMs. Notably, linearizing Llama 3.1 70B took only 18 hours on a single 8x80GB H100. Linearizing Llama 3.1 405B still took less GPU hours than what <a href=\"https://github.com/jxiw/MambaInLlama/blob/main/mamba_zephyr/README.md\" target=\"_blank\">prior methods used</a> for 8B LLMs. LoLCATs also offers significant progress in quality, to tackle these big LLMs with reasonable compute. Compared with following the prior approach of just swapping attentions before training, we were able to close <span><span><span><math>>></math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.5782em;vertical-align:-0.0391em\"></span><span>></span></span></span></span></span>78% of the performance gap to original Transformers on tasks like 5-shot MMLU.</p>\n<figure></figure>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-10-14-lolcats/lolcats_comparison_large.png\"></figure>\n<hr>\n<h2>What's next?</h2>\n<p>In summary, we made LoLCATs, a new method for linearizing LLMs with state-of-the-art quality and orders of magnitude less compute. While we’re excited to share our progress so far, we're also jazzed about the potential opportunities linearizing unlocks. Two such directions below:</p>\n<p><strong>Unlocking new capabilities with cheaper test-time compute</strong></p>\n<figure></figure>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:80%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-10-14-lolcats/linearized_thruput_memory.png\"></figure>\n<p>We're particularly excited about how linearizing LLMs lets us bring complexity-level improvements in efficiency---like\nlinear-time and constant-memory generation---to readily-available and state-of-the-art LLMs.\nOur paper discusses this more, but by replacing each attention layer with a linear or recurrent\nalternative, we no longer need to deal with growing KV caches and their associated memory pains.\nInstead, we can dedicate that memory to more fun use-cases, such as achieving higher throughput\nwith larger batch sizes.</p>\n<p>Especially with recent attention on inference scaling laws---and <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.21787\" target=\"_blank\">improving\nanswer quality</a> <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2408.03314\" target=\"_blank\">by generating many responses</a>---we're curious about\nfurther enabling these ideas, e.g., by now generating 2048 parallel responses at the prior cost of 32.\nBy significantly bringing down the costs of test-time compute, could linearizing unlock further reasoning demonstrations and improve open-source model quality?</p>\n<p><strong>Democratizing Subquadratic LLM Development</strong><br>\nWe’re also excited about how low-rank linearizing can scale up efficient architecture research.\nWhile we stuck to simple linear attentions with LoLCATs, a big motivator was the ability to allow\nanyone to scale up a subquadratic candidate into competitive 7B+ LLMs that are all the rage these days.\nWe've been fortunate that our <a href=\"https://wiki.rwkv.com/\" target=\"_blank\">RWKV</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2312.06635\" target=\"_blank\">Gated</a> <a href=\"https://github.com/sustcsonglin/flash-linear-attention\" target=\"_blank\">Linear Attention</a>, and <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2312.00752\" target=\"_blank\">Mamba</a> friends (among others!)\ncontinue to cook up many exciting architectural developments—often on academic compute no less!</p>\n<p>How can techniques like linearizing help take their ideas to bigger and\nbadder models? While the effects might not be the same as pretraining from scratch, it seems\nlike a great opportunity to use the open-weight ecosystem available today as a research testbed.\nCan we open up new avenues of efficient architecture development?</p>\n<p>For both these pursuits, we’ve tried to make our training repo for LoLCATs helpful at <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/lolcats\" target=\"_blank\">https://github.com/HazyResearch/lolcats</a>. Linearized Llamas for all!</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:60%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-10-14-lolcats/oprah-you-get-llamas.gif\"></figure>\n<div>\n<hr>\n<ol>\n<li id=\"user-content-fn-1\">The Hedgehog &#x26; the Porcupine: Expressive Linear Attentions with Softmax Mimicry, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.04347\" target=\"_blank\">https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.04347</a><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-10-14-lolcats-p2#fnref-1\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">↩</a></li>\n<li id=\"user-content-fn-2\">On The Computational Complexity of Self-Attention, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2209.04881\" target=\"_blank\">https://arxiv.org/abs/2209.04881</a><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-10-14-lolcats-p2#fnref-2\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">↩</a></li>\n<li id=\"user-content-fn-3\">Where a cria is a <a href=\"https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/cria\" target=\"_blank\">baby llama, alpaca, vicuña, or guanaco</a><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-10-14-lolcats-p2#fnref-3\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">↩</a></li>\n</ol>\n</div>,Full team: Michael, Simran, Rahul, Alan, Ben, Aaryan, Krithik, and Chris Ré. Paper | Code | Models | Part 1\n\nIn Part 1, we shared LoLCATs…,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-10-14-lolcats-p2,<a href='https://michaelzhang.xyz/'>Michael Zhang</a>, <a href=\"https://arorasimran.com/\">Simran Arora</a>,2026-07-22T18:37:38.394Z,2024-10-14T07:00:00.791Z,[{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-10-14-lolcats/low-gpus.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":4531,\"height\":1080},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-10-14-lolcats/softmax_to_linear.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-10-14-lolcats/linear_attn_memes.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-10-14-lolcats/hedgehog_llamas.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-10-14-lolcats/lolcats_linear_swa.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-10-14-lolcats/sample_attention_weights.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-10-14-lolcats/lolcats_step_0_1.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-10-14-lolcats/lolcats_step_2.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-10-14-lolcats/layerwise_mse.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-10-14-lolcats/lolcat_results_open.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-10-14-lolcats/lolcats_comparison_linearize.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-10-14-lolcats/lolcats_quality_efficiency.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-10-14-lolcats/lora_recovery.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-10-14-lolcats/linearized_llama3_1_fam.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-10-14-lolcats/lolcats_comparison_large.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-10-14-lolcats/linearized_thruput_memory.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-10-14-lolcats/oprah-you-get-llamas.gif\",\"type\":\"photo\"}],163778663386687488,1211296081845936161,Linearizing LLMs with LoLCATs,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-10-14-lolcats-p1,<p><strong>Full team</strong>: <a href=\"https://michaelzhang.xyz/\" target=\"_blank\">Michael</a>, <a href=\"https://arorasimran.com/\" target=\"_blank\">Simran</a>, <a href=\"https://chalamala.com/\" target=\"_blank\">Rahul</a>, <a href=\"https://alanwu.xyz/\" target=\"_blank\">Alan</a>, <a href=\"https://benjaminfspector.com/\" target=\"_blank\">Ben</a>,  <a href=\"https://www.aaryan-singhal.com/\" target=\"_blank\">Aaryan</a>, <a href=\"https://x.com/KrithikTweets\" target=\"_blank\">Krithik</a>, and <a href=\"https://cs.stanford.edu/people/chrismre/\" target=\"_blank\">Chris Ré</a>.</p>\n<p><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2410.10254\" target=\"_blank\"><strong>Paper</strong></a> | <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/lolcats\" target=\"_blank\"><strong>Code</strong></a> | <a href=\"https://huggingface.co/collections/hazyresearch/lolcats-670ca4341699355b61238c37\" target=\"_blank\"><strong>Models</strong></a></p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-10-14-lolcats/lolcats_and_llamas_resized.png\"></figure>\n<p>In addition to making models go fast, we’re excited about creating fast models <em>faster</em>.\nAnd so in this first part of a <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-10-14-lolcats-p2\" target=\"_blank\">two-post</a> release, we’re happy to share <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2410.10254\" target=\"_blank\"><strong>LoLCATs</strong></a> (<strong>Lo</strong>w-rank <strong>L</strong>inear\n<strong>C</strong>onversion via <strong>A</strong>ttention <strong>T</strong>ran<strong>s</strong>fer), a new approach for quickly creating subquadratic LLMs\nfrom <em>existing</em> Transformers.</p>\n<p>Rather than invent and pretrain new architectures from scratch, LoLCATs builds on a recent playbook <sup id=\"user-content-fnref-1\"><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-10-14-lolcats-p1#fn-1\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">1</a></sup><sup id=\"user-content-fnref-2\"><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-10-14-lolcats-p1#fn-2\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">2</a></sup><sup id=\"user-content-fnref-3\"><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-10-14-lolcats-p1#fn-3\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">3</a></sup><sup id=\"user-content-fnref-4\"><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-10-14-lolcats-p1#fn-4\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">4</a></sup><sup id=\"user-content-fnref-5\"><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-10-14-lolcats-p1#fn-5\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">5</a></sup>\nthat simply (1) <em>swaps out</em> softmax attentions for efficient alternatives (e.g., linear attentions),\nbefore (2) further training the model to recover from this layer swapping.\nThis \"linearizing\" lets us bring the joys of <strong>linear-time</strong> and <strong>constant-memory</strong> generation to powerful and\npopular open-source LLMs (cheaper test-time compute, more reasoning for all!).</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:70%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-10-14-lolcats/linearize_setup.png\"></figure>\n<p>However, we developed LoLCATs to make linearizing even more painless and quality-preserving.\nAs our own test, LoLCATS let us create linear versions of the <strong>complete Llama 3.1 family</strong>\n(8B, 70B, and 405B) for the first time, doing so no less on the same budget of a\n<em>parameter-efficient finetune</em>.</p>\n<p>To do so, we found that we could do two simple things:</p>\n<ol>\n<li>\n<p><strong>Make swapping more seamless</strong>: replacing softmax attentions with linear attentions trained to approximate their softmax counterparts (\"attention transfer\").</p>\n</li>\n<li>\n<p><strong>Make recovery cheaper</strong>: avoiding full model training after swapping, and recovering quality by only adjusting with parameter-efficient finetuning (e.g., low-rank adaptation).</p>\n</li>\n</ol>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-10-14-lolcats/lolcats_step_0_1_2.png\"></figure>\n<p>With this release, we’re sharing our paper, code, and reference checkpoints, so you can linearize your own LLMs too!</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-10-14-lolcats/learn_attn_quality.png\"></figure>\n<p style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:-10px;color:grey\"></p><p>Left: Rather than try to design expressive linear attentions, we simply learn them!\nRight: With minimal training (just LoRA!), this lets us recover LLM yapping abilities.</p><p></p>\n<figure></figure>\n<hr>\n<p>In <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-10-14-lolcats-p2\" target=\"_blank\">Part 2</a>, we'll go deeper into some details and results of LoLCATs. But for some quick highlights,\nLoLCATs lets us:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>\n<p><strong>Achieve state-of-the-art linearized quality</strong>: improving the zero-shot accuracy of linearizing popular 7B and 8B LLMs (Mistral 7B, Llama 3 8B) by 6.7 to 8.8 points on average over standard LM Eval zero-shot tasks (20+ points on 5-shot MMLU), while also outperforming strong subquadratic models pretrained from scratch (like <a href=\"https://huggingface.co/TRI-ML/mamba-7b-rw\" target=\"_blank\">Mamba 7B</a>, <a href=\"https://huggingface.co/RWKV/rwkv-6-world-7b\" target=\"_blank\">RWKV-6 World</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.14995\" target=\"_blank\">TransNormer 7B</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.19427\" target=\"_blank\">Hawk 7B, Griffin 7B</a>, and <a href=\"https://www.together.ai/blog/stripedhyena-7b\" target=\"_blank\">StripedHyena-7B</a>). LoLCATs LLMs further <em>matched</em> original Transformer-based LLMs on zero-shot LM Eval tasks.</p>\n</li>\n<li>\n<p><strong>Drastically reduce linearizing costs</strong>: getting this quality by training under 0.2% of the parameters in prior linearizing methods, while using only 40 million training tokens. This amounts to a 2,500x improvement in tokens-to-model efficiency versus prior linearizing methods, and a 35,000x improvement versus pretraining strong 7B subquadratic models from scratch).</p>\n</li>\n<li>\n<p><strong>Scale up linearizing to 70B and 405B LLMs</strong>: using these advances to linearize the complete Llama 3.1 family (8B, 70B, and 405B). We created the first linearized 70B and 405B LLMs, and all on \"academic compute\" (Llama 3.1 405B took <a href=\"https://github.com/jxiw/MambaInLlama/blob/main/mamba_llama/README.md#hybrid-mamba-50-attention--16-attention-layer\" target=\"_blank\">less GPU hours</a> than recent methods used for 50x smaller 8B LLMs).</p>\n</li>\n</ul>\n<p>For even more details, please check out our paper, along with the LoLCATs method repo and some sample checkpoints.\nWhile we started with simple linear attentions, we hope that our findings + code can help you linearize your\nLlamas, Mistrals, Gemmas or whatevers into the architectures of your hearts’ desires.</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:50%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-10-14-lolcats/subquadratics.png\"></figure>\n<ul>\n<li>Paper: <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2410.10254\" target=\"_blank\">(arXiv)</a></li>\n<li>Method + Training Repo: <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/lolcats\" target=\"_blank\">https://github.com/HazyResearch/lolcats</a></li>\n<li>Checkpoints: <a href=\"https://huggingface.co/collections/hazyresearch/lolcats-670ca4341699355b61238c37\" target=\"_blank\">(Hugging Face Collection)</a></li>\n<li>Part 2: <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-10-14-lolcats-p2\" target=\"_blank\">link</a></li>\n</ul>\n<div>\n<hr>\n<ol>\n<li id=\"user-content-fn-1\">Finetuning Pretrained Transformers into RNNs <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2103.13076\" target=\"_blank\">https://arxiv.org/abs/2103.13076</a><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-10-14-lolcats-p1#fnref-1\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">↩</a></li>\n<li id=\"user-content-fn-2\">The Hedgehog &#x26; the Porcupine: Expressive Linear Attentions with Softmax Mimicry <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.04347\" target=\"_blank\">https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.04347</a><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-10-14-lolcats-p1#fnref-2\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">↩</a></li>\n<li id=\"user-content-fn-3\">Linearizing Large Language Models <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2405.06640\" target=\"_blank\">https://arxiv.org/abs/2405.06640</a><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-10-14-lolcats-p1#fnref-3\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">↩</a></li>\n<li id=\"user-content-fn-4\">Transformers to SSMs: Distilling Quadratic Knowledge to Subquadratic Models <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2408.10189\" target=\"_blank\">https://arxiv.org/abs/2408.10189</a><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-10-14-lolcats-p1#fnref-4\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">↩</a></li>\n<li id=\"user-content-fn-5\">The Mamba in the Llama: Distilling and Accelerating Hybrid Models <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2408.15237\" target=\"_blank\">https://arxiv.org/abs/2408.15237</a><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-10-14-lolcats-p1#fnref-5\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">↩</a></li>\n</ol>\n</div>,Full team: Michael, Simran, Rahul, Alan, Ben, Aaryan, Krithik, and Chris Ré. Paper | Code | Models\n\nIn addition to making models go fast…,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-10-14-lolcats-p1,<a href='https://michaelzhang.xyz/'>Michael Zhang</a>, <a href=\"https://arorasimran.com/\">Simran Arora</a>,2026-07-22T18:37:38.393Z,2024-10-14T07:00:00.782Z,[{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-10-14-lolcats/lolcats_and_llamas_resized.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1907,\"height\":1080},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-10-14-lolcats/linearize_setup.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-10-14-lolcats/lolcats_step_0_1_2.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-10-14-lolcats/learn_attn_quality.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-10-14-lolcats/subquadratics.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"}],163778663386687488,1211296081845936162,Just read twice: closing the recall gap for recurrent language models,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-07-01-jrt,<p><strong>Full team</strong>: <a href=\"https://arorasimran.com/\" target=\"_blank\">Simran</a>, <a href=\"https://openreview.net/profile?id=~Aman_Timalsina1\" target=\"_blank\">Aman</a>, <a href=\"https://www.aaryan-singhal.com/\" target=\"_blank\">Aaryan</a>, <a href=\"https://benjaminfspector.com/\" target=\"_blank\">Ben</a>, <a href=\"https://sabrieyuboglu.com/\" target=\"_blank\">Sabri</a>, <a href=\"https://www.linkedin.com/in/xinyi-zhao-47253b1b8/\" target=\"_blank\">Xinyi (Jojo)</a>, <a href=\"https://www.linkedin.com/in/ashishprao/\" target=\"_blank\">Ashish</a>, <a href=\"https://cse.buffalo.edu/faculty/atri/\" target=\"_blank\">Atri</a>, and <a href=\"https://cs.stanford.edu/people/chrismre/\" target=\"_blank\">Chris Ré</a>.</p>\n<p><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.05483\" target=\"_blank\"><strong>ArXiv</strong></a> | <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/prefix-linear-attention\" target=\"_blank\"><strong>Code</strong></a> | <a href=\"https://huggingface.co/collections/hazyresearch/jrt-666296fb18c35a31a3c364b9\" target=\"_blank\"><strong>Models</strong></a></p>\n<p><strong>TLDR</strong> Today's efficient ML architectures struggle to perform a fundamental skill called associative recall (AR) as well as the de-facto Transformer architecture. AR requires an LM to use info provided in long contexts when generating tokens. The issue is that efficient LMs need to predict what info from long contexts to store in a limited amount of memory. We observe that if prompts are ordered in \"the right way\" or we use non-causal variants of efficient LMs to process prompts, the prediction problem can become much easier! Our work departs from the modern <em>causal</em> language modeling orthodoxy, where LMs process text left-to-right in a fixed order, to close AR gaps between memory efficient and Transformer LMs.</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:35%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-07-01-jrt/jrt_logo.png\"></figure>\n<h2>Introduction</h2>\n<p>Recent work has made rapid progress in developing fixed-memory recurrent architectures (e.g., <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2312.00752\" target=\"_blank\">Mamba</a>\nand <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.13048\" target=\"_blank\">RWKV</a>) that are competitive with attention in language modeling perplexity. During inference,\nthese models are more memory efficient and asymptotically faster than attention! Amazing, we have quality <em>and</em> efficiency! But what are we missing? As the saying goes, there's no free lunch!</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:35%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-07-01-jrt/free_lunch.jpg\"></figure>\n<p>While these LMs are efficient, they make a tradeoff in <a href=\"https://transformer-circuits.pub/2022/in-context-learning-and-induction-heads/index.html\" target=\"_blank\">\"associative recall\" (AR) ability</a>: they struggle to use information provided in-context when generating responses. Why does this matter? Well while the greater efficiency might be helpful for unlocking long-context applications, such as generating code given an <em>entire codebase</em>, if they can't actually refer to specific functions or variables in context, then that efficiency effectively doesn't matter. <em>We find a <a href=\"https://huggingface.co/state-spaces/mamba-2.8b\" target=\"_blank\">popular 2.8Bn parameter Mamba LM trained on 300 Bn tokens of the Pile</a> underperforms a <a href=\"https://huggingface.co/hazyresearch/attn-1b-50bn\" target=\"_blank\">1.3Bn parameters (2.2× smaller) Transformer LM trained on 50 Bn tokens (6× fewer tokens)</a> by 5 points, averaged across a suite of benchmarks that require AR abilities.</em></p>\n<p>Unfortunately, this fundamental issue for memory-limited LMs: we prove models <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.18668\" target=\"_blank\">require <span><span><span><math>Ω(N)\\Omega(N)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>Ω</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> space</a>, in input length N, to solve associative recall tests like the one below. We have a \"context\" of key-value token pair mappings on the left and \"questions\" on the right for which the model should output answers <span><span><span><math>44</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>4</span></span></span></span></span>, <span><span><span><math>66</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>6</span></span></span></span></span>, <span><span><span><math>11</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>1</span></span></span></span></span>, <span><span><span><math>22</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>2</span></span></span></span></span>, <span><span><span><math>33</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>3</span></span></span></span></span>:</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:55%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-07-01-jrt/mqar.jpg\"></figure>\n<p>Is all lost? <em>This begs the question of whether we can actually rely on the LMs with O(1)-memory recurrent state sizes for language modeling...</em></p>\n<p>Luckily, models often do not need to remember <em>all information</em> provided in-context to excel at a task! The challenge is predicting which subset of information is useful to store. LMs have gotten a lot better at this through innovations on architectural inductive biases like input-dependent decays (e.g., <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2312.00752\" target=\"_blank\">Mamba</a>), <a href=\"https://www.bioinf.jku.at/publications/older/2604.pdf\" target=\"_blank\">LSTM</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/1610.06258\" target=\"_blank\">fast weights</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2011.07831\" target=\"_blank\">delta rule</a>, etc. Other work like the <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.18668\" target=\"_blank\">Based architecture</a>, instead increases the recurrent state size in hardware-efficient and math-guided ways! <sup id=\"user-content-fnref-1\"><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-07-01-jrt#fn-1\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">1</a></sup> <sup id=\"user-content-fnref-2\"><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-07-01-jrt#fn-2\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">2</a></sup> Efficient LMs have continued to extend the Pareto frontier of the AR-quality and efficiency tradeoff space, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.18668\" target=\"_blank\">but leave a lot to be desired</a>.</p>\n<p><strong>Our insights.</strong> We make a simple observation that the order in which a recurrent LM reads input text drastically impacts the difficulty of predicting what to store in the limited memory. Suppose we ask questions <span><span><span><math>QQ</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8778em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span>Q</span></span></span></span></span> (e.g., <em>“When did Galileo move to Florence?”</em>), over documents <span><span><span><math>DD</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">D</span></span></span></span></span> (e.g., the detailed <a href=\"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Galileo_Galilei\" target=\"_blank\">Wikipedia for Galileo Galilei</a>). The model needs to remember just one fact from <span><span><span><math>DD</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">D</span></span></span></span></span> if the prompt is ordered <span><span><span><math>[Q,D][Q, D]</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>[</span><span>Q</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">D</span><span>]</span></span></span></span></span>, but needs to remember all facts when it is <span><span><span><math>[D,Q][D, Q]</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>[</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">D</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>Q</span><span>]</span></span></span></span></span>. <em>The issue is that essentially all our modern LLMs view text \"causally\", i.e. they process text in a fixed order – left-to-right.</em> They need to predict the future (predicting that I’ll ask about Galileo’s move to Florence) when deciding what to store (reading Galileo’s page). Note that people don't read like this! We go back and forth and jump around - think back to SAT reading comprehension tests!</p>\n<p><strong>Our work goes beyond the causal-LM orthodoxy to close AR gaps to Transformers using efficient recurrent LMs!!</strong> We propose two methods in this vein, detailed in the rest of this blogpost:</p>\n<ol>\n<li>\n<p><strong>Just read twice prompting (JRT-Prompt):</strong> This method repeats the prompt context multiple times before the model needs to generate the answer. It works with any off-the-shelf causal or non-causal model, without modification! With this repeated prompt strategy we can do better than zero-shot Transformers in quality, but with comparable efficiency. You might think the extra context repetition makes things slow, but because we're linear and hardware-efficient we actually end up being faster than the zero-shot Tranformer! We apply JRT-Prompt to <span><span><span><math>1616</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>16</span></span></span></span></span> off-the-shelf LMs overall (spanning <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.18668\" target=\"_blank\">Based</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2312.06635\" target=\"_blank\">GLA</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2312.00752\" target=\"_blank\">Mamba-1</a>, and <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2405.21060\" target=\"_blank\">Mamba-2</a>) resulting in <span><span><span><math>11.0±1.311.0 \\pm 1.3</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>11.0</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>±</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>1.3</span></span></span></span></span> points improvement on average across the benchmarks. Based LMs with JRT Prompt can process <span><span><span><math>3267832678</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>32678</span></span></span></span></span> length sequences, <span><span><span><math>1616</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>16</span></span></span></span></span> batch size <span><span><span><math>11.9×11.9\\times</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>11.9</span><span>×</span></span></span></span></span> faster than Transformers with FlashAttention-2.</p>\n</li>\n<li>\n<p><strong>Just read twice recurrent architecture (JRT-RNN):</strong> This architecture uses an encoder-decoder structure to non-causally process prompts then causally decode the answer. JRT-RNN provides <span><span><span><math>99%99\\%</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8056em;vertical-align:-0.0556em\"></span><span>99%</span></span></span></span></span> of Transformer quality at <span><span><span><math>360360</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>360</span></span></span></span></span>M params./<span><span><span><math>3030</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>30</span></span></span></span></span>Bn tokens, averaged across the recall-intensive ICL benchmarks. This represents <span><span><span><math>46.7%46.7\\%</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8056em;vertical-align:-0.0556em\"></span><span>46.7%</span></span></span></span></span> improvement over Based and <span><span><span><math>78.8%78.8\\%</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8056em;vertical-align:-0.0556em\"></span><span>78.8%</span></span></span></span></span> over Mamba. JRT-RNN provides <span><span><span><math>96%96\\%</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8056em;vertical-align:-0.0556em\"></span><span>96%</span></span></span></span></span> of Transformer quality at <span><span><span><math>1.31.3</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>1.3</span></span></span></span></span>Bn params. / <span><span><span><math>5050</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>50</span></span></span></span></span>Bn tokens, representing <span><span><span><math>16.2%16.2\\%</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8056em;vertical-align:-0.0556em\"></span><span>16.2%</span></span></span></span></span> improvement over Based and <span><span><span><math>34.5%34.5\\%</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8056em;vertical-align:-0.0556em\"></span><span>34.5%</span></span></span></span></span> over Mamba on average. Benchmarking our custom CUDA implementation, prefix linear attention can process <span><span><span><math>3267832678</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>32678</span></span></span></span></span> length sequences, <span><span><span><math>1616</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>16</span></span></span></span></span> batch size <span><span><span><math>19.2×19.2\\times</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>19.2</span><span>×</span></span></span></span></span> faster than FlashAttention-2.</p>\n</li>\n</ol>\n<p>We think there's a lot to do in this space and are interested to hear your feedback on these ideas! At a high level, why are we directly trying to mimic the way we use Transformers with our efficient LMs? We can exploit the asymmetries! Multiple linear passes is asymptotically better than quadratic Transformers!</p>\n<p>You can try out our models and hardware-efficient kernels: <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/prefix-linear-attention\" target=\"_blank\">https://github.com/HazyResearch/prefix-linear-attention</a>.</p>\n<h2>Understanding the role of data order on efficient models</h2>\n<p>To demonstrate the sensitivity of recurrent LMs to the data ordering, we started by considering a simplified toy task: the model is given input sequences that contain elements from two sets A and B, and needs to output the tokens in the intersection of <span><span><span><math>AA</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span>A</span></span></span></span></span> and <span><span><span><math>BB</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05017em\">B</span></span></span></span></span>. E.g., the correct output below would be 6:</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:40%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-07-01-jrt/example_sd.jpg\"></figure>\n<p>Note, that the \"set intersection\" problem tested by this toy task is a <a href=\"https://www.tcs.tifr.res.in/~arkadev/commSurvey.pdf\" target=\"_blank\">quintessential problem in communication complexity theory that has been studied for decades</a>. The \"hardness\" of many problems reduces to the hardness of set disjointness (are the two sets disjoint or not)? We also formally show that the associative recall problem reduces to set disjointness problem in our paper!</p>\n<p>In evaluating LMs on the toy task, we consider two slices of the test set: when <span><span><span><math>∣A∣>∣B∣|A| > |B|</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>∣</span><span>A</span><span>∣</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>∣</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05017em\">B</span><span>∣</span></span></span></span></span> (“long <span><span><span><math>AA</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span>A</span></span></span></span></span> to short <span><span><span><math>BB</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05017em\">B</span></span></span></span></span>”) and when <span><span><span><math>∣B∣>∣A∣|B| > |A|</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>∣</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05017em\">B</span><span>∣</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>∣</span><span>A</span><span>∣</span></span></span></span></span> (“short <span><span><span><math>AA</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span>A</span></span></span></span></span> to long <span><span><span><math>BB</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05017em\">B</span></span></span></span></span>”). We find that causal models achieve better quality when <span><span><span><math>∣B∣>∣A∣|B| > |A|</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>∣</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05017em\">B</span><span>∣</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>∣</span><span>A</span><span>∣</span></span></span></span></span>, than when <span><span><span><math>∣A∣>∣B∣|A| > |B|</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>∣</span><span>A</span><span>∣</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>∣</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05017em\">B</span><span>∣</span></span></span></span></span>, which is intuitive since  the LM needs to store all elements in set A to find the intersection with B. Meanwhile, non-causal LMs outperform when <span><span><span><math>∣A∣>∣B∣|A| > |B|</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>∣</span><span>A</span><span>∣</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>∣</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05017em\">B</span><span>∣</span></span></span></span></span> and match the causal case when <span><span><span><math>∣B∣>∣A∣|B| > |A|</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>∣</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05017em\">B</span><span>∣</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>∣</span><span>A</span><span>∣</span></span></span></span></span>! The causal LMs are far more sensitive to the data-ordering (highlighted in the right-hand-side panel).</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-07-01-jrt/synthetic_sd.jpg\"></figure>\n<p>We theoretically formalize the synthetic observations, pointing to two paths forward to build more reliable <span><span><span><math>O(1)O(1)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span><span>(</span><span>1</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span>-memory recurrent LMs:</p>\n<ol>\n<li>\n<p>The causal LM needs <span><span><span><math>Ω(min(∣A∣,∣B∣))\\Omega(\\mathrm{min}(|A|, |B|))</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>Ω</span><span>(</span><span><span>min</span></span><span>(</span><span>∣</span><span>A</span><span>∣</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>∣</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05017em\">B</span><span>∣</span><span>))</span></span></span></span></span> bits of memory to solve set disjointness. Broadly, we should put data in “the right order” in-context (e.g., placing the set with <span><span><span><math>min(∣A∣,∣B∣)\\mathrm{min}(|A|, |B|)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span><span>min</span></span><span>(</span><span>∣</span><span>A</span><span>∣</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>∣</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05017em\">B</span><span>∣</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> first in context). We use this idea in our first proposal, Just read twice prompting.</p>\n</li>\n<li>\n<p>We prove non-causal LMs can solve set disjointness in <span><span><span><math>Ω(min(∣A∣,∣B∣))\\Omega(\\mathrm{min}(|A|, |B|))</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>Ω</span><span>(</span><span><span>min</span></span><span>(</span><span>∣</span><span>A</span><span>∣</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>∣</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05017em\">B</span><span>∣</span><span>))</span></span></span></span></span> bits of memory, regardless of the data order in context. We use this idea in our second proposal, the Just read twice recurrent architecture.</p>\n</li>\n</ol>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-07-01-jrt/main_fig_jrt1.png\"></figure>\n<h2>Just read twice prompting</h2>\n<p>Just read twice is very simple: we just repeat the prompt (e.g., document and question) multiple times in context before the model generates an answer. Intuitively, in the second (or subsequent) pass over the context, the LM can condition on the full context to decide what to store in the state -- we get around the issue of needing the data to be in \"the right order\"!</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:80%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-07-01-jrt/prompt_scheme.jpg\"></figure>\n<p>We take <span><span><span><math>1616</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>16</span></span></span></span></span> pretrained recurrent LMs from Hugging Face and evaluate them using standard zero-shot prompting versus JRT-prompts on six recall-intensive tasks (document question answering, information extraction). Each cell below shows the Standard / JRT scores respectively.</p>\n<table><thead><tr><th>Architecture</th><th>Params</th><th>Tokens</th><th><a href=\"https://huggingface.co/datasets/hazyresearch/based-fda\" target=\"_blank\">FDA</a></th><th><a href=\"https://huggingface.co/datasets/hazyresearch/based-swde\" target=\"_blank\">SWDE</a></th><th><a href=\"https://huggingface.co/datasets/hazyresearch/based_nq_1024\" target=\"_blank\">NQ</a></th><th><a href=\"https://huggingface.co/datasets/hazyresearch/based-squad\" target=\"_blank\">SQUADv2</a></th><th><a href=\"https://huggingface.co/datasets/hazyresearch/based_triviaqa\" target=\"_blank\">TriviaQA</a></th><th><a href=\"https://huggingface.co/datasets/hazyresearch/based_drop\" target=\"_blank\">Drop</a></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><a
