/ <strong>40.5</strong></td><td>42.9 / <strong>59.6</strong></td><td><strong>63.8</strong> / 62.4</td><td>23.2 / <strong>36.6</strong></td></tr><tr><td><a href=\"https://huggingface.co/state-spaces/mamba2-2.7b\" target=\"_blank\">Mamba2</a> </td><td>2.7B</td><td>300B</td><td>68.7 / <strong>81.6</strong></td><td>55.2 / <strong>60.8</strong></td><td>34.4 / <strong>41.7</strong></td><td>45.4 / <strong>59.4</strong></td><td>66.4 / <strong>66.5</strong></td><td>23.0 / <strong>42.5</strong></td></tr></tbody></table>\n<p>This results in <span><span><span><math>11.0±1.311.0 \\pm 1.3</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>11.0</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>±</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>1.3</span></span></span></span></span> points of accuracy improvement on average for non-Transformer LMs across the benchmarks. Checkout our paper for more analysis. We show JRT outperforms few-shot prompting, and the full repetition also appears to be more helpful than only repeating the question.<sup id=\"user-content-fnref-3\"><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-07-01-jrt#fn-3\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">3</a></sup> These results highlight that LMs are brittle across varied data orderings in context.</p>\n<p>JRT prompt with two repetitions does double the context length, but using recurrent LMs, this is still asymptotically more efficient than the quadratic scaling of Transformers. Interestingly, a lot of our work on efficient LMs tries to directly mimic the experience of using Transformers, but we could exploit the asymmetry: multiple linear passes can still be better than one quadratic pass in many regimes. We're excited for future methods along this line of thinking.</p>\n<p>Benchmarking our hardware-efficient implementation of Based, written in the <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/ThunderKittens\" target=\"_blank\">ThunderKittens</a> framework, JRT-Prompt applied to Based models can provide 11.9X higher throughput than FlashAttention-2 at sequence length <span><span><span><math>3276832768</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>32768</span></span></span></span></span> and batch size <span><span><span><math>1616</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>16</span></span></span></span></span> on an NVidia H100 GPU. It is also worth re-emphasizing that JRT-Prompt can be used with <em>any off-the-shelf LM</em>.</p>\n<h2>Just read twice recurrent architecture</h2>\n<p>We have shown that the recall quality of causal recurrent LMs varies depending on the order in which the information appears in context, making them brittle for in-context learning. In our set disjointness analysis, we found that non-causal recurrent LMs could help! If a LM can non-causally view the entire prompt context when deciding what information to store or discard, the LM may be able to make better selection decisions.</p>\n<p>A <a href=\"https://deeplearning.cs.cmu.edu/S24/document/readings/Bidirectional%20Recurrent%20Neural%20Networks.pdf\" target=\"_blank\">long</a> <a href=\"https://www.cs.toronto.edu/~graves/ijcnn_2005.pdf\" target=\"_blank\">line</a> of work has demonstrated the strength of non-causal \"bidirectional\" neural networks in language modeling.\nHowever, it is challenging to use them for fast text generation because the context <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2205.05131\" target=\"_blank\">must</a> <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/1905.03197\" target=\"_blank\">be</a> <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2209.14500\" target=\"_blank\">re-processed</a> for each generated token to achieve good quality.\n<a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/1910.10683\" target=\"_blank\">Encoder-decoder architectures</a> with a bidirectional encoder and causal decoder offer a way to achieve fast causal generation while reaping the benefits of bidirectional LMs. <em>Nonetheless, decoder-only causal autoregressive LMs remain the norm and encoder-decoder architectures\nhave received little attention in the context of sub-quadratic efficient LMs.</em></p>\n<p>We propose a simple encoder-decoder architecture, JRT-RNN, that goes beyond the de-facto causal modeling! We'll start by defining some baseline efficient LMs for the causal setting and then describe our approach.</p>\n<h2>Preliminaries in the causal case</h2>\n<p>Sequence mixers are the part of an LM that determines how words in input <span><span><span><math>x∈RN×dx \\in \\mathbb{R}^{N \\times d}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.5782em;vertical-align:-0.0391em\"></span><span>x</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>∈</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"></span><span><span>R</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>×</span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> for sequence length <span><span><span><math>NN</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span></span></span></span></span> and model dimension <span><span><span><math>dd</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span>, interact with each other when computing output representations <span><span><span><math>y∈RN×dy \\in \\mathbb{R}^{N \\times d}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7335em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">y</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>∈</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"></span><span><span>R</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>×</span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> for the subsequent layer of a deep neural network.</p>\n<p>First we'll start with the de-facto <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/1409.0473\" target=\"_blank\">attention (Bahdanau et al., 2014)</a> sequence mixer and then describe a popular category of efficient LMs, which uses <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2006.16236\" target=\"_blank\">linear attention (Katharopoulos et al., 2020)</a>. Linear attention is <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2405.21060\" target=\"_blank\">closely related</a> to another popular category of efficient LMs based on <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2111.00396\" target=\"_blank\">state space models (Gu et al., 2021)</a>.</p>\n<p><em><strong>Attention models</strong></em> mix tokens as follows:</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:70%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-06-22-ac/attn.jpg\"></figure>\n<p>Attention takes <span><span><span><math>O(N2d)O(N^2d)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.0641em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span><span>(</span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>2</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>d</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> compute and linear <span><span><span><math>O(Nd)O(Nd)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>d</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> memory during training. In inference, remember we generate one token at a time <span><span><span><math>tt</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6151em\"></span><span>t</span></span></span></span></span> -- each new query <span><span><span><math>QtQ_t</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8778em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span><span>Q</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.2806em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>t</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> needs to interact with all the prior <span><span><span><math>K0:tK_{0:t}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8333em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">K</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0715em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>0</span><span>:</span><span>t</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> and <span><span><span><math>V0:tV_{0:t}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8333em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.22222em\">V</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.2222em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>0</span><span>:</span><span>t</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> so if we cache those keys and values, compute and memory to generate a token scales with <span><span><span><math>tt</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6151em\"></span><span>t</span></span></span></span></span>.</p>\n<p><em><strong>Linear attention models</strong></em> replace the <span><span><span><math>exp⁡\\exp</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.625em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span>exp</span></span></span></span></span> with a “feature map” <span><span><span><math>ϕ:RN×d′→RN×D\\phi: \\mathbb{R}^{N \\times d'} \\rightarrow \\mathbb{R}^{N \\times D}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8889em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span>ϕ</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>:</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.9425em\"></span><span><span>R</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.9425em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>×</span><span><span>d</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8278em\"><span style=\"top:-2.931em;margin-right:0.0714em\"><span style=\"height:2.5em\"></span><span><span><span>′</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>→</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8413em\"></span><span><span>R</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8413em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>×</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">D</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>, with “feature dimension” <span><span><span><math>DD</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">D</span></span></span></span></span>. As before, <span><span><span><math>dd</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span> is the model dimension, but we’ll decouple <span><span><span><math>d’d’</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span>d</span><span>’</span></span></span></span></span> so <span><span><span><math>QQ</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8778em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span>Q</span></span></span></span></span> and <span><span><span><math>KK</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">K</span></span></span></span></span> can be projected to a different dimension than <span><span><span><math>VV</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.22222em\">V</span></span></span></span></span> as needed.</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:70%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-06-22-ac/lin_attn.jpg\"></figure>\n<p>Shown in the figure, this is now <span><span><span><math>O(NDd)O(NDd)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">D</span><span>d</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> linear in <span><span><span><math>NN</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span></span></span></span></span> during training. During inference, by summing prior <span><span><span><math>ht=∑i=0tϕ(KtT)Vth_t = \\sum_{i=0}^{t} \\phi(K_t^T) V_t</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8444em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span>h</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.2806em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>t</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1.2332em;vertical-align:-0.2997em\"></span><span><span style=\"position:relative;top:0em\">∑</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.9335em\"><span style=\"top:-2.4003em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>i</span><span>=</span><span>0</span></span></span></span><span style=\"top:-3.2029em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>t</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2997em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>ϕ</span><span>(</span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">K</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8413em\"><span style=\"top:-2.453em;margin-left:-0.0715em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>t</span></span></span><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">T</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.247em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>)</span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.22222em\">V</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.2806em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.2222em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>t</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> such that <span><span><span><math>ht∈R1×Ddh_t \\in \\mathbb{R}^{1 \\times Dd}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8444em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span>h</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.2806em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>t</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>∈</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"></span><span><span>R</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>1</span><span>×</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">D</span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>, then multiplying this with <span><span><span><math>QtQ_t</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8778em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span><span>Q</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.2806em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>t</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>, observe our compute and memory to generate a token is constant <span><span><span><math>O(1)O(1)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span><span>(</span><span>1</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> as <span><span><span><math>tt</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6151em\"></span><span>t</span></span></span></span></span> grows!</p>\n<h3>Our approach</h3>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:80%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-07-01-jrt/rnn_scheme.jpg\"></figure>\n<p><strong>Prefix linear attention.</strong> We first uses what we refer to as <em>prefix</em> linear attention (PLA), inspired by the class of <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/1910.10683\" target=\"_blank\">prefix LM architectures</a>. We split the sequence into two regions: a non-causal encoder region of length <span><span><span><math>MM</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">M</span></span></span></span></span>, <span><span><span><math>M&#x3C;NM &#x3C; N</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7224em;vertical-align:-0.0391em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">M</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>&#x3C;</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span></span></span></span></span> and a causal decoder region of length <span><span><span><math>N−MN-M</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7667em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>−</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">M</span></span></span></span></span>. Adding projections to compute <span><span><span><math>AeA_e</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8333em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span>A</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.1514em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>e</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>, <span><span><span><math>BeB_e</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8333em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05017em\">B</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.1514em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0502em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>e</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> on the encoder side, we compute outputs as:</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:65%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-07-01-jrt/pla.jpg\"></figure>\n<p>Like standard linear attention, PLA is agnostic to the choice of feature map <span><span><span><math>ϕ(⋅)\\phi(\\cdot)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>ϕ</span><span>(</span><span>⋅</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span>. Observe that PLA retains the compute complexity and recurrent state size of causal linear attention during decoding, but processes the \"prompt region\" (first <span><span><span><math>MM</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">M</span></span></span></span></span> tokens) twice during prefill.</p>\n<p>We introduce a hardware efficient CUDA implementation using the <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/ThunderKittens\" target=\"_blank\">ThunderKittens</a> CUDA library for PLA, which takes just <span><span><span><math>1.24×1.24\\times</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>1.24</span><span>×</span></span></span></span></span> the time of the baseline decoder linear attention LM. In benchmarking, this implementation provides <span><span><span><math>19.2×19.2\\times</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>19.2</span><span>×</span></span></span></span></span> higher <span><span><span><math>19.2×19.2\\times</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>19.2</span><span>×</span></span></span></span></span> higher throughput than FlashAttention-2 for prefill of length <span><span><span><math>32K32K</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span>32</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">K</span></span></span></span></span>, batch size <span><span><span><math>1616</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>16</span></span></span></span></span>!</p>\n<p><strong>Pretraining Loss.</strong> Decoder-LMs are trained using a next token prediction loss: i.e, at each position <span><span><span><math>ii</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6595em\"></span><span>i</span></span></span></span></span> in the sequence, the LM uses tokens <span><span><span><math>0−i0-i</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>0</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>−</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6595em\"></span><span>i</span></span></span></span></span> to predict token <span><span><span><math>i+1i+1</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7429em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>i</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>+</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>1</span></span></span></span></span>. In PLA, we can’t compute a NTP loss for tokens 1 through M since the model views them non-causally. Instead, we use a masked language modeling loss for tokens in the encoder region  (i.e., replacing / \"masking\" some words in the sequence and measuring how well the model  predicts the original word that got masked) plus next token prediction loss in the causal decoder region (last <span><span><span><math>N−MN-M</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7667em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>−</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">M</span></span></span></span></span> tokens). Note that if we mask roughly <span><span><span><math>15%15\\%</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8056em;vertical-align:-0.0556em\"></span><span>15%</span></span></span></span></span> of tokens, let <span><span><span><math>M=N2M = \\frac{N}{2}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">M</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1.2173em;vertical-align:-0.345em\"></span><span><span></span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8723em\"><span style=\"top:-2.655em\"><span style=\"height:3em\"></span><span><span><span>2</span></span></span></span><span style=\"top:-3.23em\"><span style=\"height:3em\"></span><span style=\"border-bottom-width:0.04em\"></span></span><span style=\"top:-3.394em\"><span style=\"height:3em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.345em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span></span></span></span></span>, and normalizing for the total number of input sequences, the non-causal JRT LM would compute losses on roughly <span><span><span><math>65%65\\%</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8056em;vertical-align:-0.0556em\"></span><span>65%</span></span></span></span></span> the number of tokens of the causal LMs. However, how best to normalize training across causal vs. non-causal models is an open question that we hope future work will continue to engage with.</p>\n<h3>Results</h3>\n<p>On the set of recall-intensive benchmarks, JRT-RNN provides consistent improvements over strong decoder recurrent LM baselines, achieving 96% the quality of the very strong Transformer ++ (<a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2302.13971\" target=\"_blank\">Llama architecture</a>) baseline! We compare to two very strong <span><span><span><math>O(1)O(1)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span><span>(</span><span>1</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span>-memory recurrent LM baselines: JRT gives a <span><span><span><math>16.2%16.2\\%</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8056em;vertical-align:-0.0556em\"></span><span>16.2%</span></span></span></span></span> improvement over the Based baseline and <span><span><span><math>34.5%34.5\\%</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8056em;vertical-align:-0.0556em\"></span><span>34.5%</span></span></span></span></span> improvement over the Mamba baseline at <span><span><span><math>1.31.3</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>1.3</span></span></span></span></span>Bn parameters and <span><span><span><math>5050</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>50</span></span></span></span></span>Bn tokens. Checkout our paper for lots more results and analysis!</p>\n<table><thead><tr><th>Architecture</th><th>Params/Tok</th><th>FDA <span><span><span><math>N=512N=512</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>512</span></span></span></span></span></th><th>FDA <span><span><span><math>N=512N=512</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>512</span></span></span></span></span></th><th>SWDE <span><span><span><math>N=512N=512</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>512</span></span></span></span></span></th><th>SWDE <span><span><span><math>N=512N=512</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>512</span></span></span></span></span></th><th>NQ <span><span><span><math>N=512N=512</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>512</span></span></span></span></span></th><th>NQ <span><span><span><math>N=512N=512</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>512</span></span></span></span></span></th><th>SQUADv2</th><th>TriviaQA</th><th>Drop</th><th>Avg.</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td>Transformer</td><td>1.3B/50B</td><td>85.6</td><td><strong>83.5</strong></td><td><strong>55.7</strong></td><td><strong>56.0</strong></td><td>33.4</td><td><strong>29.9</strong></td><td>40.1</td><td><strong>56.6</strong></td><td>21.4</td><td><strong>51.4</strong></td></tr><tr><td>Mamba</td><td>1.3B/50B</td><td>55.4</td><td>40.1</td><td>44.0</td><td>33.7</td><td>27.6</td><td>23.2</td><td>32.2</td><td>54.5</td><td>20.7</td><td>36.8</td></tr><tr><td>Based</td><td>1.3B/50B</td><td>69.3</td><td>58.8</td><td>47.6</td><td>40.4</td><td>29.1</td><td>24.4</td><td>38.5</td><td>54.3</td><td>20.8</td><td>42.6</td></tr><tr><td>JRT-RNN</td><td>1.3B/50B</td><td><strong>86.7</strong></td><td>67.7</td><td>49.4</td><td>45.7</td><td><strong>38.3</strong></td><td>25.4</td><td><strong>50.4</strong></td><td>53.0</td><td><strong>29.3</strong></td><td>49.5</td></tr></tbody></table>\n<p>Overall, nearly all the focus on efficient attention alternatives LMs has been on decoder-only LMs. We're very excited about the potential of (1) non-causal modeling and (2) making multiple <em>linear</em> passes over the input prompt as two ways to help make efficient LMs competitive with Transformers on the important recall skill, and think there are so many more ideas to consider along these directions. <sup id=\"user-content-fnref-5\"><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-07-01-jrt#fn-5\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">5</a></sup></p>\n<p>If you’re interested in chatting more about any of these topics, please feel free to reach out at: <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/simarora@stanford.edu\" target=\"_blank\">simarora@stanford.edu</a>. We'd like to thank Hazy Research, <a href=\"https://cartesia.ai/\" target=\"_blank\">Cartesia</a>, and <a href=\"https://www.together.ai/\" target=\"_blank\">Together</a> for supporting our work!</p>\n<p>Finally, big thank you to labmates including Michael Zhang, Dan Fu, Neel Guha, and Jon Saad-Falcon on helpful feedback on this blogpost and the overall work!</p>\n<div>\n<hr>\n<ol>\n<li id=\"user-content-fn-1\"><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2405.21060\" target=\"_blank\">Mamba-2</a> recently followed this approach as well!<a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-07-01-jrt#fnref-1\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">↩</a></li>\n<li id=\"user-content-fn-2\">There are also several combinations of linear recurrent models and attention layers –- <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2212.14052\" target=\"_blank\">H3</a>, <a href=\"https://www.ai21.com/jamba\" target=\"_blank\">Jamba</a>, <a href=\"https://www.together.ai/blog/stripedhyena-7b\" target=\"_blank\">Striped Hyena</a>, <a href=\"https://www.together.ai/blog/evo\" target=\"_blank\">Evo</a>, <a href=\"https://mistral.ai/news/announcing-mistral-7b/\" target=\"_blank\">Mistral</a>, and <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.19427\" target=\"_blank\">Griffin</a> -- resorting to <span><span><span><math>O(N)O(N)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> KV-caches, with smaller constants.<a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-07-01-jrt#fnref-2\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">↩</a></li>\n<li id=\"user-content-fn-3\"><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.15449\" target=\"_blank\">Echo embeddings</a> is another very exciting paper you should check out along this line! That work repeats context prior to extracting text embeddings from autoregressive LMs, leading to improvements over the case without repetition.<a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-07-01-jrt#fnref-3\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">↩</a></li>\n<li id=\"user-content-fn-5\"><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/pdf/1409.3215\" target=\"_blank\">Ilya's seminal seq-to-seq paper</a> exploits the observation that data order matters in the context of encoder-decoder recurrent LMs, proposing to reverse the order of the tokens source text!<a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-07-01-jrt#fnref-5\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">↩</a></li>\n</ol>\n</div>,Full team: Simran, Aman, Aaryan, Ben, Sabri, Xinyi (Jojo), Ashish, Atri, and Chris Ré. ArXiv | Code | Models\n\nTLDR Today's efficient ML…,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-07-01-jrt,<a href=\"https://arorasimran.com/\">Simran Arora</a>,2026-07-22T18:37:38.392Z,2024-07-07T07:00:00.376Z,[{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-07-01-jrt/jrt_logo.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1024,\"height\":1024},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-07-01-jrt/free_lunch.jpg\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-07-01-jrt/mqar.jpg\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-07-01-jrt/example_sd.jpg\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-07-01-jrt/synthetic_sd.jpg\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-07-01-jrt/main_fig_jrt1.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-07-01-jrt/prompt_scheme.jpg\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-06-22-ac/attn.jpg\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-06-22-ac/lin_attn.jpg\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-07-01-jrt/rnn_scheme.jpg\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-07-01-jrt/pla.jpg\",\"type\":\"photo\"}],163778663386687488,1211296081845936163,Efficient language models as arithmetic circuits,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-06-22-ac,<p><em>This blogpost was written as a resource in conjunction with our talk at <a href=\"https://sites.google.com/view/algos-in-llms-stoc24/home\" target=\"_blank\">STOC 2024</a>. It focuses on work done with the following full team</em>: Michael Zhang, Aman Timalsina, Michael Poli, Isys Johnson, Dylan Zinsley, Silas Alberti, James Zou, Christopher Ré.</p>\n<hr>\n<p>The Transformer architecture, which powers most language models (LMs) in industry, requires compute and memory to grow quickly with the input size during training and inference, precluding dreams of modeling inputs containing millions of lines of code or the 3.2Bn nucleotide pairs in genome sequences. Alternative architectures (e.g. <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2312.00752\" target=\"_blank\">Mamba</a>, <a href=\"https://www.rwkv.com/\" target=\"_blank\">RWKV</a>), which seek to reduce the compute and memory requirements relative to Transformers, are emerging at a rapid pace. However, deviating from the Transformer orthodoxy is a major risk: models are served to millions of users and take billions of dollars to train, and we have limited insight into how switching to these alternatives might impact quality.</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-06-22-ac/landscape.jpg\"></figure>\n<p>Thus, we need frameworks to reason about which architectural changes matter and why. We’ve been writing a series of papers that use arithmetic circuits to make some sense of the landscape of architectures (<a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2312.04927\" target=\"_blank\">Zoology</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.18668\" target=\"_blank\">Based</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.05483\" target=\"_blank\">JRT</a>). Our general approach is to view each architecture as different a <em>arithmetic circuit</em>, a composition of sums <span><span><span><math>(+)(+)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>(</span><span>+</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> and products <span><span><span><math>(×)(\\times)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>(</span><span>×</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span>, and to reason about the efficiency of different LMs in terms of the number of operations required to perform tasks of interest. This post describes our theoretical results.</p>\n<h2>Part 1: A single skill, associative recall, causes a lot of trouble</h2>\n<p>It is difficult to reason about language modeling quality given the breadth of skills involved (e.g. fact memorization, in-context learning) and the many different levels of abstraction for skills (e.g. <a href=\"https://gluebenchmark.com/\" target=\"_blank\">some work evaluates LM’s grammar skills</a> while <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.17567\" target=\"_blank\">others evaluate an LM’s ability to “emotionally self regulate”</a>). We decided to train a bunch of LMs across architectures and do error analysis to see what we’d find!<sup id=\"user-content-fnref-1\"><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-06-22-ac#fn-1\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">1</a></sup></p>\n<h3>Error analysis across efficient LM architectures</h3>\n<p>Surprisingly, we found that a single skill called associative recall (AR) glaringly stood out in the error analysis: Transformers crushed it and the efficient LMs struggled. An LM performs AR if it recalls information that it has seen earlier in the input. For instance, LMs need to refer to functions or variables that already exist in the inputted codebase when generating subsequent lines of code. Our prior posts <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2023-12-11-zoology1-analysis\" target=\"_blank\">here</a> and <a href=\"https://www.together.ai/blog/based\" target=\"_blank\">here</a> dive more into our empirical observations. As a recap:</p>\n<ol>\n<li><em><strong>Gated convolutions.</strong></em> We found 80%+ of the language modeling quality difference between Transformers vs. the class of “gated convolution” LMs (built from gating and convolution operations e.g., H3, Hyena, RWKV) is attributed to AR. We find these LMs perform abysmally on in-context learning (ICL) tasks that require performing recall (e.g., document question answering, information extraction).</li>\n</ol>\n<p>Next, training other classes of LM architectures, including <em>selective state space</em> LMs (e.g. Mamba, GateLoop) and <em>linear attention</em> LMs (e.g. GLA, Based), at 1.3Bn parameters, 50Bn tokens, and evaluating on ICL tasks that require performing recall (<a href=\"https://www.nature.com/articles/s41591-021-01312-x\" target=\"_blank\">FDA</a>, <a href=\"https://paperswithcode.com/dataset/swde\" target=\"_blank\">SWDE</a>, <a href=\"https://ai.google.com/research/NaturalQuestions/\" target=\"_blank\">NQ</a>), we found:</p>\n<ol start=\"2\">\n<li><em><strong>Selective state space models.</strong></em> For 1k and 2k length documents, Mamba underperforms the Transformer by 42% and 55% on average across tasks.</li>\n<li><em><strong>Linear attention.</strong></em> There is excitment around linear attention since they <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.18668\" target=\"_blank\">extend the Pareto frontier of the efficiency-recall quality tradeoff space beyond the above architecture classes</a>. Still, Based underperforms the Transformer by 27% and 41% at 1k and 2k length documents on average.</li>\n</ol>\n<p>This blogpost focuses on how we theoretically explore what's going on with AR.</p>\n<h3>Formalizing the associative recall problem</h3>\n<p><strong>Based on our analysis, we defined the MQAR problem:</strong> An LM needs to refer back to key-value token mappings (on the left) to answer questions (on the right). Here the answers are 4, 6, 1, 2.</p>\n<p><span><span><span><math>A 4 B 3 C 6 F 1 E 2→A ? C ? F ? E ?\\textrm{A 4 B 3 C 6 F 1 E 2} \\rightarrow \\textrm{A ? C ? F ? E ?}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span><span>A 4 B 3 C 6 F 1 E 2</span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>→</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span><span>A ? C ? F ? E ?</span></span></span></span></span></span></p>\n<p>Formally, given an input sequence <span><span><span><math>x=x0,...,xN−1x = {x_0, . . . , x_{N−1}}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span>x</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6389em;vertical-align:-0.2083em\"></span><span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>0</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>...</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3283em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>−</span><span>1</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2083em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> where each <span><span><span><math>xi∈Cx_i \\in C</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6891em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>∈</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">C</span></span></span></span></span> is a token drawn from a vocabulary of size <span><span><span><math>c=∣C∣c = |C|</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span>c</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>∣</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">C</span><span>∣</span></span></span></span></span>. The task is to check, for every query <span><span><span><math>1≤i&#x3C;N1 \\leq i &#x3C; N</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7804em;vertical-align:-0.136em\"></span><span>1</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>≤</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6986em;vertical-align:-0.0391em\"></span><span>i</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>&#x3C;</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span></span></span></span></span>,\nwhether there exists a <span><span><span><math>0≤j&#x3C;i0 \\leq j &#x3C; i</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7804em;vertical-align:-0.136em\"></span><span>0</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>≤</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.854em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>&#x3C;</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6595em\"></span><span>i</span></span></span></span></span> such that <span><span><span><math>xi≡xjx_i ≡ x_j</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6138em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>≡</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.7167em;vertical-align:-0.2861em\"></span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2861em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>. If so, output <span><span><span><math>xj+1x_{j+1}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7167em;vertical-align:-0.2861em\"></span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span><span>+</span><span>1</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2861em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>.<sup id=\"user-content-fnref-2\"><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-06-22-ac#fn-2\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">2</a></sup></p>\n<p>Theoretically reasoning about open-ended problems like language modeling is complicated, but the simplified MQAR task helped us make some progress. When we train LMs on synthetic MQAR data (like the example above), the trends correlate with real language modeling gaps between architectures trained to large scale. Prior work has extensively explored AR over multiple decades (<a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/1410.5401\" target=\"_blank\">Graves et al., 2014</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/1610.06258\" target=\"_blank\">Ba et al., 2016</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.11174\" target=\"_blank\">Schlag et al., 2021</a>, etc.). We find MQAR quality correlates better with real language modeling quality than previously used formulations (discussed in more detail <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2023-12-11-zoology1-analysis\" target=\"_blank\">in our prior blogpost</a>).</p>\n<hr>\n<h2>Part 2: Background</h2>\n<p>Here we'll briefly recap the definitions of different sequence mixers and intuition on why our efficient LMs might struggle to perform MQAR.</p>\n<p>Sequence mixers are the part of an LM that determines how words in input <span><span><span><math>x∈RN×dx \\in \\mathbb{R}^{N \\times d}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.5782em;vertical-align:-0.0391em\"></span><span>x</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>∈</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"></span><span><span>R</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>×</span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> for sequence length <span><span><span><math>NN</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span></span></span></span></span> and model dimension <span><span><span><math>dd</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span>, interact with each other when computing output representations <span><span><span><math>y∈RN×dy \\in \\mathbb{R}^{N \\times d}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7335em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">y</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>∈</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"></span><span><span>R</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>×</span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> for the subsequent layer of a deep neural network.</p>\n<p>First we'll start with the de-facto attention sequence mixer, and then describe two (closely related) categories of efficient LMs: linear attention and state space models. The LMs shown in Figure 1 above all fall in these two categories.</p>\n<p><em><strong>Attention models</strong></em> mix tokens as follows:</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:70%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-06-22-ac/attn.jpg\"></figure>\n<p>Attention takes <span><span><span><math>O(N2d)O(N^2d)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.0641em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span><span>(</span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>2</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>d</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> compute and linear <span><span><span><math>O(Nd)O(Nd)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>d</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> memory during training. During inference, remember we generate one token at a time <span><span><span><math>tt</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6151em\"></span><span>t</span></span></span></span></span> -- each new query <span><span><span><math>QtQ_t</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8778em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span><span>Q</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.2806em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>t</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> needs to interact with all the prior <span><span><span><math>K0:tK_{0:t}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8333em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">K</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0715em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>0</span><span>:</span><span>t</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> and <span><span><span><math>V0:tV_{0:t}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8333em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.22222em\">V</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.2222em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>0</span><span>:</span><span>t</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> so if we cache those keys and values, compute and memory to generate a token scales with <span><span><span><math>tt</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6151em\"></span><span>t</span></span></span></span></span>.</p>\n<p><em><strong>Linear attention models</strong></em>, proposed by <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2006.16236\" target=\"_blank\">Katharopoulos et al., 2020</a> replaces <span><span><span><math>exp⁡\\exp</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.625em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span>exp</span></span></span></span></span> with a “feature map” <span><span><span><math>ϕ:RN×d′→RN×D\\phi: \\mathbb{R}^{N \\times d'} \\rightarrow \\mathbb{R}^{N \\times D}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8889em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span>ϕ</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>:</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.9425em\"></span><span><span>R</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.9425em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>×</span><span><span>d</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8278em\"><span style=\"top:-2.931em;margin-right:0.0714em\"><span style=\"height:2.5em\"></span><span><span><span>′</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>→</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8413em\"></span><span><span>R</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8413em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>×</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">D</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>, with “feature dimension” <span><span><span><math>DD</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">D</span></span></span></span></span>. As before, <span><span><span><math>dd</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span> is the model dimension, but we’ll decouple <span><span><span><math>d’d’</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span>d</span><span>’</span></span></span></span></span> so <span><span><span><math>QQ</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8778em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span>Q</span></span></span></span></span> and <span><span><span><math>KK</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">K</span></span></span></span></span> can be projected to a different dimension than <span><span><span><math>VV</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.22222em\">V</span></span></span></span></span> as needed.</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:70%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-06-22-ac/lin_attn.jpg\"></figure>\n<p>Shown in the figure, this is now <span><span><span><math>O(NDd)O(NDd)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">D</span><span>d</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> linear in <span><span><span><math>NN</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span></span></span></span></span> during training. During inference, by summing prior <span><span><span><math>ht=∑i=0tϕ(KtT)Vth_t = \\sum_{i=0}^{t} \\phi(K_t^T) V_t</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8444em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span>h</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.2806em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>t</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1.2332em;vertical-align:-0.2997em\"></span><span><span style=\"position:relative;top:0em\">∑</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.9335em\"><span style=\"top:-2.4003em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>i</span><span>=</span><span>0</span></span></span></span><span style=\"top:-3.2029em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>t</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2997em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>ϕ</span><span>(</span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">K</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8413em\"><span style=\"top:-2.453em;margin-left:-0.0715em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>t</span></span></span><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">T</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.247em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>)</span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.22222em\">V</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.2806em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.2222em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>t</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> such that <span><span><span><math>ht∈R1×Ddh_t \\in \\mathbb{R}^{1 \\times Dd}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8444em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span>h</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.2806em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>t</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>∈</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"></span><span><span>R</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>1</span><span>×</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">D</span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>, then multiplying this with <span><span><span><math>QtQ_t</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8778em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span><span>Q</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.2806em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>t</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>, observe our compute and memory to generate a token is constant <span><span><span><math>O(1)O(1)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span><span>(</span><span>1</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> as <span><span><span><math>tt</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6151em\"></span><span>t</span></span></span></span></span> grows.</p>\n<p><em><strong>State space models</strong></em> The mixing proceeds as follows:</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:85%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-06-22-ac/ssm.jpg\"></figure>\n<p>And many SSM variants also use <strong>Hadamard product</strong> (elementwise multiplication between <span><span><span><math>22</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>2</span></span></span></span></span> vectors) operations in addition to the convolution.</p>\n<p>This ignores that SSMs are continuous objects, for a more precise definition <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2022-01-14-s4-1\" target=\"_blank\">see prior blogs</a>. Observe that we again have a recurrent view (<span><span><span><math>O(1)O(1)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span><span>(</span><span>1</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> compute and memory) we can use during inference and a sub-quadratic parallel convolution <a href=\"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fast_Fourier_transform#:~:text=A%20Fast%20Fourier%20Transform%20(FFT,frequency%20domain%20and%20vice%20versa.)\" target=\"_blank\"><span><span><span><math>O(dNlog⁡N)O(dN \\log N)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span><span>(</span><span>d</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>lo<span style=\"margin-right:0.01389em\">g</span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> with FFT</a> view we can use during training.</p>\n<p><strong>Intuition on why gated convolutions may struggle on MQAR</strong>\nBefore diving into the theory, let's make sure we have some high level intuition on why efficient LMs might struggle. MQAR requires us to compare tokens to find matching tokens, and then shift forwards the answer for the next token prediction.</p>\n<p>Attention computes inner products between all tokens, making it easy to find matches! In the above figure, note the convolution is more restricted, a Toeplitz matrix, so a token can only be compared to a token <span><span><span><math>44</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>4</span></span></span></span></span> positions prior if <em>all tokens</em> compare to the tokens <span><span><span><math>44</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>4</span></span></span></span></span> positions prior to them as well. The comparisons are accomplished by using a convolution filter that <em>shifts</em> the sequence <span><span><span><math>44</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>4</span></span></span></span></span> positions. Once we shift, the model can compare the shifted and original sequences to identify matches using the aforementioned Hadamard products.</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:70%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-06-22-ac/part1.jpg\"></figure>\n<p>In our convolution models, we apply a unique convolution filter for each of the <span><span><span><math>dd</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span> dimensions, so we can support <em>multiple</em> shifts. BUT, to support all <span><span><span><math>O(N)O(N)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> possible shifts in a sequence of length <span><span><span><math>NN</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span></span></span></span></span>, each hidden effectively needs to store information about all tokens to be able to perform the comparison.</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:70%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-06-22-ac/part2.jpg\"></figure>\n<p>Okay so we have some intuition on why convolutions might struggle -- the filters are input-independent and it's hard to support all the shifts with small model dimension. Attention's input-dependent mixing is helpful to find the matching tokens. We walk through this intuition more slowly in <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2023-12-11-zoology1-analysis\" target=\"_blank\">our prior blogpost</a>.</p>\n<hr>\n<h2>Part 3: Unifying the messy architectural landscape using arithmetic circuits</h2>\n<p>Above, we walked through a toy construction of how LMs might solve MQAR, but there's a messy landscape of LMs with subtle modifications from architecture to architecture. We use theory to reason about the landscape systematically.</p>\n<p>Towards this, we first observe our efficent LM classes (gated convolutions, linear attentions) can be written as polynomials in terms of the input variables! The models we consider are maps <span><span><span><math>M:RN×d→RN×dM:\\mathbb{R}^{N\\times d}\\to \\mathbb{R}^{N\\times d}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">M</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>:</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"></span><span><span>R</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>×</span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>→</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"></span><span><span>R</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>×</span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> and each of the <span><span><span><math>NdNd</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span> output values are polynomials in the <span><span><span><math>NdNd</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span> input variables <span><span><span><math>x[i,j]x[i,j]</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>x</span><span>[</span><span>i</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span><span>]</span></span></span></span></span> for <span><span><span><math>(i,j)∈[N]×[d](i,j)\\in [N]\\times [d]</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>(</span><span>i</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span><span>)</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>∈</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>[</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>]</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>×</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>[</span><span>d</span><span>]</span></span></span></span></span>.</p>\n<h3>Polynomial view of the primitive operations in efficient language models</h3>\n<p><em><strong>Gated convolutions.</strong></em> Let’s walk through how Hadamard product and convolution operations are expressed as polynomial operations. Suppose we have two vectors:</p>\n<p><span><span><span><math>x=[x0,x1,…,xN−1]x = [x_0, x_1, …, x_{N-1}]</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span>x</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>[</span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>0</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>1</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>…</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3283em\"><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>−</span><span>1</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2083em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>]</span></span></span></span></span></p>\n<p><span><span><span><math>v=[v0,v1,…,vN−1]v = [v_0, v_1, …, v_{N-1}]</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>[</span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0359em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>0</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0359em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>1</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>…</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3283em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0359em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>−</span><span>1</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2083em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>]</span></span></span></span></span></p>\n<p>First, recall that a Hadamard product between vectors <span><span><span><math>xx</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span>x</span></span></span></span></span> and <span><span><span><math>vv</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span></span></span></span></span> computes:\n<span><span><span><math>y=[x0v0,x1v1,…,xN−1vN−1] y = [x_0 v_0, x_1 v_1, …, x_{N-1} v_{N-1}]</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.625em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">y</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>[</span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>0</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0359em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>0</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>1</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0359em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>1</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>…</span><span
