style=\"height:0.2083em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>]</span></span></span></span></span>.\nNote that here the <span><span><span><math>NN</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span></span></span></span></span> outputs <span><span><span><math>xi⋅vix_i\\cdot v_i</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.5945em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>⋅</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.5806em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0359em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> is a degree <span><span><span><math>22</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>2</span></span></span></span></span> polynomial in the variables <span><span><span><math>xix_i</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.5806em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> and <span><span><span><math>viv_i</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.5806em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0359em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>.</p>\n<p>Next, a convolution between vectors <span><span><span><math>xx</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span>x</span></span></span></span></span> and <span><span><span><math>vv</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span></span></span></span></span> computes (for <span><span><span><math>0≤i≤2N−20\\le i\\le 2N-2</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7804em;vertical-align:-0.136em\"></span><span>0</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>≤</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.7955em;vertical-align:-0.136em\"></span><span>i</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>≤</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.7667em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>2</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>−</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>2</span></span></span></span></span>):</p>\n<p><span><span><span><math>(x∗v)[i]=∑j=0ixi−jvj(x \\ast v)[i] = \\sum_{j=0}^{i} x_{i-j}v_j</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>(</span><span>x</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>∗</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span><span>)</span><span>[</span><span>i</span><span>]</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1.4004em;vertical-align:-0.4358em\"></span><span><span style=\"position:relative;top:0em\">∑</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.9646em\"><span style=\"top:-2.4003em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span><span>=</span><span>0</span></span></span></span><span style=\"top:-3.2029em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.4358em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>i</span><span>−</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2861em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0359em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2861em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>.</p>\n<p>We can see that each of the <span><span><span><math>2N−12N-1</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7667em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>2</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>−</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>1</span></span></span></span></span> outputs are polynomials in the <span><span><span><math>2N2N</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span>2</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span></span></span></span></span> inputs variables in <span><span><span><math>xx</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span>x</span></span></span></span></span> and <span><span><span><math>vv</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span></span></span></span></span>. In models that use convolutions typically <span><span><span><math>vv</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span></span></span></span></span> is fixed (it is a learned convolution filter), so the convolution becomes a linear operator– i.e. for each of the output positions we compute a polynomial of degree at most <span><span><span><math>11</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>1</span></span></span></span></span>.</p>\n<p><em><strong>Linear attention models</strong></em> We can express each entry of the <span><span><span><math>(i,j)∈RN×d(i, j) \\in \\mathbb{R}^{N \\times d}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>(</span><span>i</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span><span>)</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>∈</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"></span><span><span>R</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>×</span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> linear attention output as a polynomial as follows. We let <span><span><span><math>DD</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">D</span></span></span></span></span> be the feature dimension and <span><span><span><math>Q,K∈RN×DQ, K \\in \\mathbb{R}^{N \\times D}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8778em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span>Q</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">K</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>∈</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8413em\"></span><span><span>R</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8413em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>×</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">D</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> and <span><span><span><math>V∈Rd×dV \\in \\mathbb{R}^{d \\times d}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7224em;vertical-align:-0.0391em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.22222em\">V</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>∈</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"></span><span><span>R</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>d</span><span>×</span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> be our embedding projections.</p>\n<p><span><span><span><math>hi,j(x)=∑m∈[D],n∈[N](WQx)[i,m]⋅(WKx)[n,m]⋅(WVx)[n,j]h_{i,j}(x) = \\sum_{m \\in [D], n \\in [N]} (W_Qx)[i, m] \\cdot (W_Kx)[n, m] \\cdot (W_Vx)[n,j]</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.0361em;vertical-align:-0.2861em\"></span><span><span>h</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>i</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2861em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>(</span><span>x</span><span>)</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1.2247em;vertical-align:-0.4747em\"></span><span><span style=\"position:relative;top:0em\">∑</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.2253em\"><span style=\"top:-2.4003em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>m</span><span>∈</span><span>[</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">D</span><span>]</span><span>,</span><span>n</span><span>∈</span><span>[</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>]</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.4747em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>(</span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">W</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3283em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.1389em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>Q</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2861em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>x</span><span>)</span><span>[</span><span>i</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>m</span><span>]</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>⋅</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>(</span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">W</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3283em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.1389em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">K</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>x</span><span>)</span><span>[</span><span>n</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>m</span><span>]</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>⋅</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>(</span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">W</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3283em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.1389em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.22222em\">V</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>x</span><span>)</span><span>[</span><span>n</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span><span>]</span></span></span></span></span></p>\n<p>We can see that this computes a degree <span><span><span><math>33</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>3</span></span></span></span></span> polynomial at each position (three variables from input <span><span><span><math>xx</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span>x</span></span></span></span></span> are being multiplied).</p>\n<p><strong>!! Note !!</strong> One limitation of the arithmetic circuit view is that we can’t exactly represent non-polynomial activation functions, like the <span><span><span><math>exp⁡\\exp</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.625em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span>exp</span></span></span></span></span> in attention or Mamba. However, foreshaddowing a bit, we will use functional approximation theory to approximate <span><span><span><math>exp⁡\\exp</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.625em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span>exp</span></span></span></span></span> with (small degree) Taylor polynomials for <span><span><span><math>exp⁡\\exp</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.625em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span>exp</span></span></span></span></span>.</p>\n<h3>Measuring the efficiency of models using arithmetic circuit complexity</h3>\n<p>All gated convolution models (<a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/1612.08083\" target=\"_blank\">Dauphin et al., 2016</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2212.14052\" target=\"_blank\">H3</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2212.10544\" target=\"_blank\">BiGS</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2302.10866\" target=\"_blank\">Hyena</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.13048\" target=\"_blank\">RWKV</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.12109\" target=\"_blank\">M2</a>, etc.) and linear attention models (with polynomial feature maps) are polynomials, which means they can be directly computed by arithmetic circuits! Efficient ML is all about the scaling needed to obtain some desired quality, and there are many ways to define efficiency e.g., data, compute, parameters: here we use the complexity of arithmetic circuits as our measure of efficiency. Arithmetic circuits provide a natural and powerful computational model for studying the complexity of polynomials, with <a href=\"https://link.springer.com/book/10.1007/978-3-662-03338-8\" target=\"_blank\">deep connections to fundamental problems in theoretical computer science</a>.  The complexity of a polynomial is determined by the size of the smallest arithmetic circuit that computes the polynomial.</p>\n<p><strong>What is an arithmetic circuit?</strong> An arithmetic circuit is a directed acyclic graph that represents a polynomial function of the circuit’s input variables. We can express our polynomial (gated convolution) as arithmetic circuits with our input <span><span><span><math>xx</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span>x</span></span></span></span></span> and model parameters <span><span><span><math>θ\\theta</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">θ</span></span></span></span></span> as inputs. Each node in the circuit is an addition (<span><span><span><math>++</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6667em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>+</span></span></span></span></span>) or multiplication (<span><span><span><math>×\\times</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6667em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>×</span></span></span></span></span>) operation between inputs.</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-06-22-ac/circuit.jpg\"></figure>\n<p>As an example, let’s express the Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) as a linear arithmetic circuit for an input <span><span><span><math>vv</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span></span></span></span></span> of length <span><span><span><math>n=4n=4</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span>n</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>4</span></span></span></span></span> (<a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2012.14966\" target=\"_blank\">Dao et al., 2020</a>). Linear means the circuit uses just linear gates (i.e. gates on input <span><span><span><math>xx</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span>x</span></span></span></span></span> and <span><span><span><math>yy</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.625em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">y</span></span></span></span></span> compute <span><span><span><math>ax+byax+by</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6667em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>a</span><span>x</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>+</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8889em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span>b</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">y</span></span></span></span></span> for constants <span><span><span><math>aa</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span>a</span></span></span></span></span> and <span><span><span><math>bb</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span>b</span></span></span></span></span>), contrasting general arithmetic circuits, which also have multiplication gates (i.e. on input <span><span><span><math>xx</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span>x</span></span></span></span></span> and <span><span><span><math>yy</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.625em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">y</span></span></span></span></span> compute <span><span><span><math>xyxy</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.625em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span>x</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">y</span></span></span></span></span>).</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-06-22-ac/dft_circuit.jpg\"></figure>\n<p>An arithmetic circuit is an <span><span><span><math>(n,s,Δ,ω)(n, s, \\Delta, \\omega)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>(</span><span>n</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>s</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>Δ</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">ω</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span>-circuit, if it takes <span><span><span><math>nn</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span>n</span></span></span></span></span> variables, has size <span><span><span><math>ss</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span>s</span></span></span></span></span>, depth at most <span><span><span><math>Δ\\Delta</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span>Δ</span></span></span></span></span> and width <span><span><span><math>ω\\omega</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">ω</span></span></span></span></span>. The size is the number of nodes in the circuit, depth is the length of the longest path between input and output nodes, and width is the maximum number of wires that cross any “horizontal cut” in the circuit. If we implement a DFT by doing the naive <span><span><span><math>O(n2)O(n^2)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.0641em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span><span>(</span><span><span>n</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>2</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> time algorithm, then for the order 4 DFT matrix, we have 4 rows (4 inner products).  To implement each inner product we need 3 linear gates for an overall 12 linear gate ops. But here, we show the <a href=\"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fast_Fourier_transform#Definition\" target=\"_blank\">FFT algorithm</a>, using fewer (8) linear gates.</p>\n<h3>Quick notes on arithmetic circuits, polynomials, and structured matrices</h3>\n<p><strong>Feel free to skip this section if you're familiar with these concepts</strong></p>\n<p>Again, when we talk about arithmetic circuits being equivalent to polynomials, we are talking about outputting <span><span><span><math>NdNd</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span> values <span><span><span><math>y[i,j]y[i,j]</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">y</span><span>[</span><span>i</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span><span>]</span></span></span></span></span> by computing polynomials with respect to the <span><span><span><math>NdNd</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span> input variables (e.g., <span><span><span><math>x[i,j]x[i,j]</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>x</span><span>[</span><span>i</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span><span>]</span></span></span></span></span>) in <span><span><span><math>x∈RN×dx \\in \\mathbb{R}^{N \\times d}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.5782em;vertical-align:-0.0391em\"></span><span>x</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>∈</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"></span><span><span>R</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>×</span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> . Each polynomial has a single output value, so the model/circuit computes <span><span><span><math>NdNd</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span> polynomials, which as mentioned before, are each of degree at most <span><span><span><math>22</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>2</span></span></span></span></span> in the case of convolutions plus Hadamard product, and degree <span><span><span><math>33</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>3</span></span></span></span></span> for linear attention.</p>\n<p>In our <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2023-12-11-conv-tutorial\" target=\"_blank\">prior tutorial blogpost on polynomials and convolutions</a>, we thought about polynomials in the <em><strong>coefficient space</strong></em>, whereas in all our discussion in this blogpost, our polynomials are in <em><strong>evaluation space</strong></em>. <em>Why?</em> Well, for the convolution case discussed in our prior blogpost, <em><strong>we were picking the evaluation points</strong></em>, namely we chose the <a href=\"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Root_of_unity\" target=\"_blank\"><em>complex roots of unity</em></a> as our evaluation points permitting us go back and forth between coefficient and evaluation spaces (we could take the DFT of the coefficient vector in order to evaluate the convolution (polynomial)).</p>\n<p>Why did we think about coefficients in the <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.12109\" target=\"_blank\">Monarch Mixer work</a>? Well, we cared about <em>causality</em>, which is fundamentally about the positions of different tokens governing which other tokens they can interact with in the context -- <em>so</em>, we chose a polynomial basis to help us analyze that property.</p>\n<details><summary><strong>Reminder of the polynomial basis considered in our prior work</strong></summary><p>Let’s also write <span><span><span><math>[x0,x1,…,xN−1][x_0, x_1, …, x_{N-1}]</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>[</span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>0</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>1</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>…</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3283em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>−</span><span>1</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2083em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>]</span></span></span></span></span> as coefficients of a polynomial <span><span><span><math>f(Z)f(Z)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10764em\">f</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">Z</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> and <span><span><span><math>[v0,v1,…,vN−1][v_0, v_1, …, v_{N-1}]</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>[</span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0359em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>0</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0359em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>1</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>…</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3283em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0359em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>−</span><span>1</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2083em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>]</span></span></span></span></span> coefficients of <span><span><span><math>g(Z)g(Z)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">g</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">Z</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span>:</p><p><span><span><span><math>f(Z)=x0+x1Z+….+xN−1ZN−1f(Z) = x_0 + x_1Z + …. + x_{N-1}Z^{N-1}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10764em\">f</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">Z</span><span>)</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.7333em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>0</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>+</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8333em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>1</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">Z</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>+</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6667em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>…</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>.</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>+</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1.0497em;vertical-align:-0.2083em\"></span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3283em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>−</span><span>1</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2083em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">Z</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8413em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span = v_0 + v_1Z + … + v_{N-1}Z^{N-1}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">g</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">Z</span><span>)</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.7333em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0359em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>0</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>+</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8333em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0359em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>1</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">Z</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>+</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6667em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>…</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>+</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1.0497em;vertical-align:-0.2083em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3283em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0359em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>−</span><span>1</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2083em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">Z</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8413em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>−</span><span>1</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></p><p>First, recall that a Hadamard product between vectors <span><span><span><math>xx</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span>x</span></span></span></span></span> and <span><span><span><math>vv</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span></span></span></span></span> computes:</p><p><span><span><span><math>y=[x0v0,x1v1,…,xN−1vN−1] y = [x_0 v_0, x_1 v_1, …, x_{N-1} v_{N-1}]</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.625em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">y</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>[</span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>0</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0359em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>0</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>1</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0359em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>1</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>…</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3283em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>−</span><span>1</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2083em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3283em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0359em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>−</span><span>1</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2083em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>]</span></span></span></span></span></p><p>By definition, the Hadamard product of two polynomials is obtained by element-wise multiplying the coefficients of the polynomials. I.e.:</p><p><span><span><span><math>f(Z)⊙g(Z)=x0k0+x1k1Z+⋯+xN−1kN−1ZN−1f(Z) \\odot g(Z) = x_0 k_0+x_1 k_1 Z+ \\dots+ x_{N-1} k_{N-1} Z^{N-1}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10764em\">f</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">Z</span><span>)</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>⊙</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">g</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">Z</span><span>)</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8444em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>0</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0315em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>0</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>+</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8444em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>1</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0315em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>1</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">Z</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>+</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6667em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>⋯</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>+</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1.0497em;vertical-align:-0.2083em\"></span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3283em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>−</span><span>1</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2083em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3283em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0315em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>−</span><span>1</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2083em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">Z</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8413em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>−</span><span>1</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></p><p>The <span><span><span><math>ZZ</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">Z</span></span></span></span></span> powers remain unaffected because they are part of the polynomial's structure (corresponding to the positions in the sequence), not the elements being operated on. The operation preserves the polynomial’s form, altering only the coefficients.</p><p>Second, a convolution between vectors <span><span><span><math>xx</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span>x</span></span></span></span></span> and <span><span><span><math>vv</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span></span></span></span></span> computes (for <span><span><span><math>0≤i≤2N−20\\le i\\le 2N-2</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7804em;vertical-align:-0.136em\"></span><span>0</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>≤</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.7955em;vertical-align:-0.136em\"></span><span>i</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>≤</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.7667em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>2</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>−</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>2</span></span></span></span></span>):</p><p><span><span><span><math>(x∗v)[i]=∑j=0ixi−jvj(x \\ast v)[i] = \\sum_{j=0}^{i} x_{i-j}v_j</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>(</span><span>x</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>∗</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span><span>)</span><span>[</span><span>i</span><span>]</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1.4004em;vertical-align:-0.4358em\"></span><span><span style=\"position:relative;top:0em\">∑</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.9646em\"><span style=\"top:-2.4003em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span><span>=</span><span>0</span></span></span></span><span style=\"top:-3.2029em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.4358em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>i</span><span>−</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2861em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0359em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2861em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></p><p>This is equivalent to multiplying the two polynomials whose coefficients are provided by <span><span><span><math>xx</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span>x</span></span></span></span></span> and <span><span><span><math>vv</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span></span></span></span></span> respectively:\n<span><span><span><math>h(Z)=f(Z)g(Z)h(Z) = f(Z)g(Z)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>h</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">Z</span><span>)</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10764em\">f</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">Z</span><span>)</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">g</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">Z</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span>,</p><p>or</p><p><span><span><span><math>h(Z)=(x0+x1Z+….+xN−1ZN−1)(v0+v1z+…+vN−1ZN−1)h(Z) = ( x_0 + x_1Z + …. + x_{N-1}Z^{N-1} ) ( v_0 + v_1z + … + v_{N-1}Z^{N-1} )</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>h</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">Z</span><span>)</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>(</span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>0</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>+</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8333em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:0.6667em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>…</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>.</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>+</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1.0913em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3283em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>−</span><span>1</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2083em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">Z</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8413em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>−</span><span>1</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>)</span><span>(</span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0359em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>0</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>+</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.7333em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0359em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>1</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.04398em\">z</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>+</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6667em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>…</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>+</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1.0913em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3283em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0359em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>−</span><span>1</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2083em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">Z</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8413em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>−</span><span>1</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span>,</p><p>Which is the same as</p><p><span><span><span><math>h(Z)=∑i=02N−2wiZih(Z) = \\sum{i=0}^{2N - 2} w_i Z^i</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>h</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">Z</span><span>)</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1.1408em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"position:relative;top:0em\">∑</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span><span>i</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>0</span></span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8908em\"><span style=\"top:-3.1124em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>2</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>−</span><span>2</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02691em\">w</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0269em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">Z</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8247em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>.</p><p>In the above, each coefficient is:</p><p><span><span><span><math>wi=(x∗v)[i]=∑j=0ixi−jvjw_i = (x \\ast v)[i] = \\sum_{j=0}^{i} x_{i-j}v_{j}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.5806em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02691em\">w</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0269em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>(</span><span>x</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>∗</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span><span>)</span><span>[</span><span>i</span><span>]</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1.4004em;vertical-align:-0.4358em\"></span><span><span style=\"position:relative;top:0em\">∑</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.9646em\"><span style=\"top:-2.4003em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span><span>=</span><span>0</span></span></span></span><span style=\"top:-3.2029em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.4358em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>i</span><span>−</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2861em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0359em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2861em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>.</p></details>\n<p>Meanwhile, arithmetic circuits need to work for <em><strong>all potential evaluation points</strong></em> - we can't pick our evalution points. Taking in the <span><span><span><math>NdNd</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span> input variables, arithmetic circuits can use arbitrary computations to compute the <span><span><span><math>NdNd</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span> outputs (sharing computation across the <span><span><span><math>NdNd</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span> polynomials within the circuit)!</p>\n<p>Another way to think about things. We can move between coefficient and evaluation space in general: polynomial evaluation at <span><span><span><math>NN</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span></span></span></span></span> points is equal to multiplying by a structured <a href=\"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vandermonde_matrix\" target=\"_blank\">Vandermonde Matrix</a> containing the polynomial coefficients! However, for Vandermonde matrices, the arithmetic circuit conversion has <a href=\"https://link.springer.com/book/10.1007/978-3-662-03338-8\" target=\"_blank\">complexity <span><span><span><math>O(Nlog⁡2N)O(N \\log^2 N)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.1484em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span>lo<span style=\"margin-right:0.01389em\">g</span></span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8984em\"><span style=\"top:-3.1473em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>2</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> arithmetic operations</a> -- this is a bad way to express the circuits since it's a large number of operations and could result in numerical stability issues. In general, Vandermonde matrices are ill-conditioned, but the DFT, a special case of Vandermonde matrices, has condition number <span><span><span><math>11</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>1</span></span></span></span></span> (i.e. is very well conditioned).</p>\n<p>Hopefully this helps you think about why we choose different polynomial bases across these works. Alright, let's get back to the main story!</p>\n<h3>Unifying efficient LM architectures via the polynomial view</h3>\n<p>This section unifies architectures using the polynomial view.</p>\n<p><em><strong>Gated convolutions.</strong></em> Using the polynomial view, we first prove that any gated convolution model (including H3, BiGS, Hyena, RWKV, M2, etc.) can be simulated by a single canonical representation, BaseConv, within tight (poly)logarithmic factors in model parameters and depth. Given an input <span><span><span><math>u∈RN×du \\in \\mathbb{R}^{N \\times d}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.5782em;vertical-align:-0.0391em\"></span><span>u</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>∈</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"></span><span><span>R</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>×</span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>, the BaseConv operator on the right is defined as:</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-06-22-ac/basconv_ac.jpg\"></figure>\n<p>where <span><span><span><math>h∈RN×dh \\in \\mathbb{R}^{N \\times d}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7335em;vertical-align:-0.0391em\"></span><span>h</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>∈</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"></span><span><span>R</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>×</span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> contains learnable filters, <span><span><span><math>W∈Rd×dW \\in \\mathbb{R}^{d \\times d}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7224em;vertical-align:-0.0391em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">W</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>∈</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"></span><span><span>R</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>d</span><span>×</span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> is a linear projection, and <span><span><span><math>b1,b2∈RN×db_1, b_2 \\in \\mathbb{R}^{N \\times d}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8889em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span><span>b</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>1</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span>b</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>2</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>∈</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"></span><span><span>R</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>×</span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> are bias terms. The <span><span><span><math>⊙\\odot</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6667em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>⊙</span></span></span></span></span> represents a Hadamard product and the <span><span><span><math>∗\\ast</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4653em\"></span><span>∗</span></span></span></span></span> represents a convolution. Each BaseConv layer is a polynomial of degree at most <span><span><span><math>22</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>2</span></span></span></span></span> since the left and right sides are linear (degree <span><span><span><math>11</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>1</span></span></span></span></span>) in input <span><span><span><math>uu</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span>u</span></span></span></span></span>, and taking their Hadamard product, two linear polynomials get multiplied together.</p>\n<p><em><strong>Linear attentions.</strong></em> We prove that an <span><span><span><math>(N,O(log2(Nd)),d,O(N(d+D)),O(max⁡(d,D)))(N, O(log2(Nd)), d, O(N(d + D)), O(\\max(d, D)))</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.01968em\">l</span><span>o</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">g</span><span>2</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>d</span><span>))</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>d</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>(</span><span>d</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>+</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">D</span><span>))</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span><span>(</span><span>max</span><span>(</span><span>d</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">D</span><span>)))</span></span></span></span></span>-BaseConv (using <span><span><span><math>(n,s,Δ,ω)(n, s, \\Delta, \\omega)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>(</span><span>n</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>s</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>Δ</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">ω</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span>-circuit notation) computes the output for a single linear attention layer with feature dimension D. This points to the relative efficiency of linear attention over gated convolutions: we need multiple BaseConv layers to represent one linear attention layer.</p>\n<p>Moreover, we can convert any arithmetic circuit into a BaseConv model with only a poly-log loss in parameters:</p>\n<p><strong>Theorem (Arithmetic circuit equivalency).</strong> For every low-depth arithmetic circuit of size <span><span><span><math>ss</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span>s</span></span></span></span></span>, depth <span><span><span><math>Δ\\Delta</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span>Δ</span></span></span></span></span>, that takes <span><span><span><math>u∈RN×du \\in \\mathbb{R}^{N \\times d}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.5782em;vertical-align:-0.0391em\"></span><span>u</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>∈</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"></span><span><span>R</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>×</span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> as input, there is an equivalent BaseConv operator that uses <span><span><span><math>O~(sΔ)\\tilde{O}(s\\Delta)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.1702em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.9202em\"><span style=\"top:-3em\"><span style=\"height:3em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span></span><span style=\"top:-3.6023em\"><span style=\"height:3em\"></span><span style=\"left:-0.1667em\"><span>~</span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>(</span><span>s</span><span>Δ</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> parameters and <span><span><span><math>O~(Δ)\\tilde{O}(\\Delta)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.1702em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.9202em\"><span style=\"top:-3em\"><span style=\"height:3em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span></span><span style=\"top:-3.6023em\"><span style=\"height:3em\"></span><span style=\"left:-0.1667em\"><span>~</span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>(</span><span>Δ</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> layers.<sup id=\"user-content-fnref-3\"><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-06-22-ac#fn-3\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">3</a></sup></p>\n<p>For specific instantiations of gated convolutions (e.g., Hyena models), we show in our paper that we can even get rid of the poly-log factor, where <span><span><span><math>O~(⋅)\\tilde{O}(\\cdot)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.1702em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.9202em\"><span style=\"top:-3em\"><span style=\"height:3em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span></span><span style=\"top:-3.6023em\"><span style=\"height:3em\"></span><span style=\"left:-0.1667em\"><span>~</span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>(</span><span>⋅</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> hides poly-logarithmic factors.</p>\n<p><strong>Why is this theorem exciting to us?</strong> Let's walk through a few implications of this theorem:</p>\n<ol>\n<li>\n<p><strong>Arithmetic circuits are non-differentiable objects.</strong> However, BaseConv <em>is</em> differentiable, allowing us to reason about how things actually learn!</p>\n</li>\n<li>\n<p><strong>Arithmetic circuits can do arbitrary computation.</strong> Meanwhile, notice that the left-hand-side (linear map) portion of BaseConv <em>only operates on the channel dimension</em> <span><span><span><math>dd</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span> of the <span><span><span><math>N×dN \\times d</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7667em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>×</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span> input and the right-hand-side (convolution) <em>only operates on the sequence dimension</em> <span><span><span><math>NN</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span></span></span></span></span>. Despite the relative restrictivity of BaseConv, it does not lose any power!</p>\n</li>\n<li>\n<p><strong>The theorem generalizes prior results.</strong> A prior paper from our group, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2012.14966\" target=\"_blank\">Kaleidoscope</a>, shows results for linear arithmetic circuits and Butterfly matrices; here we extend to general arithmetic circuits.</p>\n</li>\n</ol>\n<details><summary><strong>Expand for an overview of how we prove the above theorem.</strong></summary><p>First, recall an arithmetic circuit <span><span><span><math>CC</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">C</span></span></span></span></span> is layered with each layer being only <span><span><span><math>×\\times</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6667em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>×</span></span></span></span></span> or <span><span><span><math>++</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6667em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>+</span></span></span></span></span> gates. The <span><span><span><math>×\\times</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6667em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>×</span></span></span></span></span> gates we handle via the Hadamard product <span><span><span><math>⊙\\odot</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6667em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>⊙</span></span></span></span></span> operation in BaseConv. So in some sense all that remains is to handle the <span><span><span><math>++</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6667em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>+</span></span></span></span></span> gates – or more specifically, we need the capability to implement linear maps using BaseConv.</p><p>To handle linear maps, we use a result from [6] that shows that any linear map can be implemented as a <span><span><span><math>O~(1)\\tilde{O}(1)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.1702em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.9202em\"><span style=\"top:-3em\"><span style=\"height:3em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span></span><span style=\"top:-3.6023em\"><span style=\"height:3em\"></span><span style=\"left:-0.1667em\"><span>~</span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>(</span><span>1</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> product of Butterfly (factor) matrices (a classical type of structured matrix that e.g. arises in the FFT). These matrices can be represented as sum of shifts of 3 diagonal matrices, i.e. they are of the form <span><span><span><math>Si⋅D1+D2⋅Si+D3S^i\\cdot D_1+D_2\\cdot S^i+D_3</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8247em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05764em\">S</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8247em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>⋅</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8333em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">D</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0278em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>1</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>+</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8333em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">D</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0278em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>2</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>⋅</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.908em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05764em\">S</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8247em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>+</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8333em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">D</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0278em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>3</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> where <span><span><span><math>SS</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05764em\">S</span></span></span></span></span> is the shift matrix and <span><span><span><math>D1,D2,S3D_1,D_2,S_3</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8778em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">D</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0278em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>1</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">D</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0278em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>2</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05764em\">S</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0576em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>3</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> are diagonal matrices. Any of these three matrices can be implemented as <span><span><span><math>x⊙bx \\odot b</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6667em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>x</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>⊙</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span>b</span></span></span></span></span> for a suitably defined bias matrix <span><span><span><math>bb</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span>b</span></span></span></span></span>.<sup id=\"user-content-fnref-4\"><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-06-22-ac#fn-4\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">4</a></sup> There are other details that need to be handled (mainly being able to “remember” part of the input when applying a layer of BaseConv) but those can be done (see our paper [1] for the details).</p></details>\n<p>We looked at a the landscape of efficient LMs and distilled it to BaseConv. We did not lose any power (within poly-logarithmic factors) with our simplification and we can now reason about <em>classes of architectures</em> more easily! Let's do this next.</p>\n<hr>\n<h2>Part 3: Solving MQAR with different classes of efficient architectures</h2>\n<p>BaseConv is exciting: we can reason about the simple object as a proxy for the messy zoo of efficient LMs.</p>\n<p>Concretely, we can propose an arithmetic circuit that solves the MQAR task, draw an equivalence between the circuit and BaseConv, and derive an upper bound on the depth of BaseConv needed to represent the circuit.\nFor instance, a naive algorithm for MQAR would use a sequential pass over the sequence and put the keys and values into a hash map, then for queries, search the hash map.</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:90%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-06-22-ac/pseudocode.jpg\"></figure>\n<p>Unfortunately, converting this algorithm to a circuit requires massive <span><span><span><math>Ω(N)\\Omega(N)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>Ω</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> depth of BaseConv in sequence length <span><span><span><math>NN</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span></span></span></span></span>.</p>\n<p>We also came up with a more efficient parallel arithmetic circuit by drawing inspiration from the <a href=\"https://www.computer.org/csdl/journal/td/1990/02/l0247/13rRUwhHcQp\" target=\"_blank\">parallel binary search algorithm</a>, which has <span><span><span><math>O(log⁡N)O(\\log N)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span><span>(</span><span>lo<span style=\"margin-right:0.01389em\">g</span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> depth (<a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2312.04927\" target=\"_blank\">Algorithm 10</a>). This allows us to obtain a tighter upper bound for BaseConv and MQAR.
