Given the equivalence between BaseConv and arithmetic circuits, we could conclude a BaseConv model with <span><span><span><math>O~(Nd)\\tilde{O}(Nd)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.1702em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.9202em\"><span style=\"top:-3em\"><span style=\"height:3em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span></span><span style=\"top:-3.6023em\"><span style=\"height:3em\"></span><span style=\"left:-0.1667em\"><span>~</span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>d</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> parameters and <span><span><span><math>O~(1)\\tilde{O}(1)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.1702em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.9202em\"><span style=\"top:-3em\"><span style=\"height:3em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span></span><span style=\"top:-3.6023em\"><span style=\"height:3em\"></span><span style=\"left:-0.1667em\"><span>~</span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>(</span><span>1</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> layers can solve MQAR!</p>\n<p>So this is awesome, we have this single powerful BaseConv tool!! Why don’t we just do everything with this? It’s easy to reason about! The issue is: we're still losing poly-log factors on the depth in our bound (recall <span><span><span><math>O~()\\tilde{O}()</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.1702em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.9202em\"><span style=\"top:-3em\"><span style=\"height:3em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span></span><span style=\"top:-3.6023em\"><span style=\"height:3em\"></span><span style=\"left:-0.1667em\"><span>~</span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>(</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> hides poly-log factors) :( While poly-log is relatively tight in theory, in ML architectures, we pay a lot if the depth is too large — it makes things hard to optimize.\nWe really want to know if we can solve MQAR in <span><span><span><math>O(1)O(1)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span><span>(</span><span>1</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> layers, like we can do for attention.</p>\n<p>Can we do better?</p>\n<h3>Lower bound for BaseConv and MQAR</h3>\n<p>Unfortunately, we find can't get <span><span><span><math>O(1)O(1)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span><span>(</span><span>1</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> layers with BaseConv :( We show a lower bound on the depth that depends on <span><span><span><math>NN</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span></span></span></span></span>.</p>\n<p><em><strong>Recapping the situation for one-hot encodings.</strong></em> In our toy constructions in Part 2, we had used one-hot encodings for each token in the sequence. We showed both Attention and BaseConv could solve MQAR with these encodings, but the size of the one-hot encoding is <span><span><span><math>d=Nd=N</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span>d</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span></span></span></span></span>, which is much too large.</p>\n<details><summary><strong>Expand to recap attention and BaseConvs's one-hot encoding solutions again</strong></summary><p>Here we describe the Part 2 intuition with math notation :)</p><p>We will assume that our input matrix <span><span><span><math>x∈{0,1}N×dx\\in\\{0,1\\}^{N\\times d}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.5782em;vertical-align:-0.0391em\"></span><span>x</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>∈</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1.0991em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>{</span><span>0</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>1</span><span><span>}</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>×</span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> uses 1-hot encoding, i.e. each row is a standard basis vector in <span><span><span><math>{0,1}n\\{0,1\\}^n</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>{</span><span>0</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>1</span><span><span>}</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6644em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>n</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> (note that this implies <span><span><span><math>d=Nd=N</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span>d</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span></span></span></span></span>). Note that the attention-query-key inner product, <span><span><span><math>A[i,j]=⟨Q[i,:],K[j,:]⟩A[i,j]=\\langle Q[i,:],K[j,:]\\rangle</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>A</span><span>[</span><span>i</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span><span>]</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>⟨</span><span>Q</span><span>[</span><span>i</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>:</span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>]</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">K</span><span>[</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>:</span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>]⟩</span></span></span></span></span>, is <span><span><span><math>11</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>1</span></span></span></span></span> if <span><span><span><math>Q[i,:]=K[j,:]Q[i,:]=K[j,:]</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>Q</span><span>[</span><span>i</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>:</span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>]</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">K</span><span>[</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>:</span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>]</span></span></span></span></span> and <span><span><span><math>00</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>0</span></span></span></span></span> otherwise (note that we crucially used the fact that each row is 1-hot encoded here). <span><span><span><math>AA</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span>A</span></span></span></span></span> tells us the positions of matching tokens in MQAR. Given the matching MQAR-key tokens, we want to output the corresponding MQAR-value, so we can let our attention-values be <span><span><span><math>V=(S⋅x)V = (S \\cdot x)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.22222em\">V</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05764em\">S</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>⋅</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>x</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span>, where <span><span><span><math>SS</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05764em\">S</span></span></span></span></span> a matrix that shifts entries forwards by one position. We need to use ``positional encodings'' to implement the shift. We can compute <span><span><span><math>A⋅VA \\cdot V</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span>A</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>⋅</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.22222em\">V</span></span></span></span></span> to solve the MQAR problem. <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2023-12-11-zoology1-analysis\" target=\"_blank\">Visualize this construction.</a></p><p>Moving on to BaseConv, note that all of the above operations for attention are polynomial operations yielding an arithmetic circuit of size <span><span><span><math>O(N3)O(N^3)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.0641em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span><span>(</span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>3</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> and <span><span><span><math>O(1)O(1)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span><span>(</span><span>1</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> to solve the MQAR problem. This is because the circuit size is <span><span><span><math>O(N2d)O(N^2d)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.0641em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span><span>(</span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>2</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>d</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> but we also have <span><span><span><math>d=nd=n</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span>d</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span>n</span></span></span></span></span>, so <span><span><span><math>O(N3)O(N^3)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.0641em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span><span>(</span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>3</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> overall. If we use our arithmetic circuit equivalency theorem then we can get an <span><span><span><math>O~(1)\\tilde{O}(1)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.1702em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.9202em\"><span style=\"top:-3em\"><span style=\"height:3em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span></span><span style=\"top:-3.6023em\"><span style=\"height:3em\"></span><span style=\"left:-0.1667em\"><span>~</span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>(</span><span>1</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> layer BaseConv implementation with <span><span><span><math>O~(N3)\\tilde{O}(N^3)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.1702em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.9202em\"><span style=\"top:-3em\"><span style=\"height:3em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span></span><span style=\"top:-3.6023em\"><span style=\"height:3em\"></span><span style=\"left:-0.1667em\"><span>~</span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>(</span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>3</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> parameters.\nIn other words, for 1-hot-encoded inputs there is no difference between Attention and BaseConv: both can solve MQAR in <span><span><span><math>O(1)O(1)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span><span>(</span><span>1</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> layers! <em><strong>However, 1-hot encodings require model dimension to scale with sequence length <span><span><span><math>NN</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span></span></span></span></span>!</strong></em></p></details>\n<p><em><strong>We want more compresssed representations in ML...</strong></em> Things get interesting if we use a more compressed representation. For example, consider the case when <span><span><span><math>d=log⁡2∣C∣d=\\log_2{|C|}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span>d</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span><span>lo<span style=\"margin-right:0.01389em\">g</span></span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.207em\"><span style=\"top:-2.4559em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>2</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2441em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span>∣</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">C</span><span>∣</span></span></span></span></span></span> for vocab <span><span><span><math>CC</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">C</span></span></span></span></span>. Here we can still use Attention to solve MQAR in <span><span><span><math>O(1)O(1)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span><span>(</span><span>1</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> layers. This is because we have that <span><span><span><math>Q[i,:]=K[j,:]Q[i,:]=K[j,:]</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>Q</span><span>[</span><span>i</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>:</span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>]</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">K</span><span>[</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>:</span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>]</span></span></span></span></span> (for our <span><span><span><math>QKTQK^T</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.0358em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span>Q</span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">K</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8413em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">T</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> inner products) iff and only if <span><span><span><math>⟨Q[i,:],K[j,:]⟩=d\\langle Q[i,:],K[j,:]\\rangle=d</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>⟨</span><span>Q</span><span>[</span><span>i</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>:</span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>]</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">K</span><span>[</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>:</span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>]⟩</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span>. The latter is obtained with a “threshold” functionality, provided a linear map+ReLU or  Softmax/other non-polynomial activation functions. The rest of the Attention construction remains the same.</p>\n<p>However, in BaseConv, it is not possible to implement the above threshold “trick” with <span><span><span><math>O(1)O(1)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span><span>(</span><span>1</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> layers. In fact, we prove a lower bound in the even simpler case of checking if two vectors <span><span><span><math>y,x∈{0,1}log⁡∣C∣y,x \\in \\{0,1\\}^{\\log{|C|}}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7335em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">y</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>x</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>∈</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1.138em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>{</span><span>0</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>1</span><span><span>}</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.888em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span><span>l</span><span>o</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.01389em\">g</span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1952em\"></span><span><span>∣</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">C</span><span>∣</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> are equal (when <span><span><span><math>x,y∈Cx,y \\in C</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7335em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span>x</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">y</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>∈</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">C</span></span></span></span></span>). The basic takeaway from our proof ends up being that to represent equality <em>exactly</em>, we need a polynomial of degree <span><span><span><math>dd</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span>, but <span><span><span><math>O(1)O(1)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span><span>(</span><span>1</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> layer BaseConv can only represent constant degree polynomials.</p>\n<p>Note that this argument was tied to a specific encoding of <span><span><span><math>xx</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span>x</span></span></span></span></span>, but are also able to show that regardless of how <span><span><span><math>xx</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span>x</span></span></span></span></span> is encoded, with  <span><span><span><math>d≤2(log⁡N)1−ϵd\\le 2^{(\\log{N})^{1-\\epsilon}}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8304em;vertical-align:-0.136em\"></span><span>d</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>≤</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.9869em\"></span><span><span>2</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.9869em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>(</span><span><span>l</span><span>o</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.01389em\">g</span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1952em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span></span><span><span>)</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8913em\"><span style=\"top:-2.931em;margin-right:0.0714em\"><span style=\"height:2.5em\"></span><span><span><span>1</span><span>−</span><span>ϵ</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>, a BaseConv model requires the number of layers to solve MQAR to scale with <span><span><span><math>NN</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span></span></span></span></span>.</p>\n<details><summary><strong>Expand for an overview of the proof of this result.</strong></summary><p>This proof invokes the index problem. The index problem has two agents, Alice and Bob, where Alice has a string <span><span><span><math>x∈0,1nx \\in {0, 1}^n</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.5782em;vertical-align:-0.0391em\"></span><span>x</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>∈</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8932em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span><span><span>0</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>1</span></span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6987em\"><span style=\"top:-3.0973em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>n</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> and Bob has an index <span><span><span><math>i∈[n]i \\in [n]</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6986em;vertical-align:-0.0391em\"></span><span>i</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>∈</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>[</span><span>n</span><span>]</span></span></span></span></span>. The goal for the players is to output the <span><span><span><math>ithi^{th}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"></span><span><span>i</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>t</span><span>h</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> entry: <span><span><span><math>xix_i</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.5806em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>. We also require the communication to be one-way: only Alice is allowed to send a single message to Bob and Bob needs to output the answer. We reduce MQAR to the index problem (described in Part 4).</p><ol>\n<li>\n<p><strong>Properties of BaseConv</strong> First we note that an <span><span><span><math>LL</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span>L</span></span></span></span></span> layer BaseConv model that solves AR on input <span><span><span><math>x∈RN×dx\\in\\mathbb{R}^{N\\times d}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.5782em;vertical-align:-0.0391em\"></span><span>x</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>∈</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"></span><span><span>R</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>×</span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> is equivalent to a polynomial on <span><span><span><math>NdNd</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span> variables (one corresponding to each entry in the matrix <span><span><span><math>xx</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span>x</span></span></span></span></span>) and has degree at most <span><span><span><math>2L2^L</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8413em\"></span><span><span>2</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8413em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>L</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>. <sup id=\"user-content-fnref-5\"><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-06-22-ac#fn-5\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">5</a></sup> Recall each BaseConv layer is the same as a polynomial of degree at most <span><span><span><math>22</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>2</span></span></span></span></span>  -- composing <span><span><span><math>LL</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span>L</span></span></span></span></span> such layers gives us a polynomial of degree at most <span><span><span><math>2L2^L</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8413em\"></span><span><span>2</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8413em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>L</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>.</p>\n</li>\n<li>\n<p><strong>We next invoke the index problem.</strong> Alice has the input <span><span><span><math>a=(1,x1,…,n,xn)a=(1,x_1,\\dots,n,x_n)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span>a</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>(</span><span>1</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>1</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>…</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>n</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.1514em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>n</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> (encoded as a matrix in <span><span><span><math>R(N−1)×d\\mathbb{R}^{(N-1)\\times d}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.888em\"></span><span><span>R</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.888em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>−</span><span>1</span><span>)</span><span>×</span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>) and Bob has the input <span><span><span><math>b=i∈[n]b= i \\in [n]</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span>b</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6986em;vertical-align:-0.0391em\"></span><span>i</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>∈</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>[</span><span>n</span><span>]</span></span></span></span></span> (encoded as a vector in <span><span><span><math>Rd\\mathbb{R}^d</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"></span><span><span>R</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>). If there exists an <span><span><span><math>LL</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span>L</span></span></span></span></span> layer BaseConv model to solve AR, then there exists a polynomial <span><span><span><math>P(a,b)P(a,b)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">P</span><span>(</span><span>a</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>b</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> of degree at most <span><span><span><math>2L2^L</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8413em\"></span><span><span>2</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8413em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>L</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> that outputs <span><span><span><math>xix_i</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.5806em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>.  Alice knows everything except <span><span><span><math>bb</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span>b</span></span></span></span></span>. She can substitute her values <span><span><span><math>aa</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span>a</span></span></span></span></span>, to get a polynomial <span><span><span><math>Qa(Z)=P(a,Z)Q_a(Z)=P(a,Z)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span><span>Q</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.1514em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>a</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">Z</span><span>)</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">P</span><span>(</span><span>a</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">Z</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span>, where <span><span><span><math>ZZ</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">Z</span></span></span></span></span> has <span><span><span><math>dd</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span> variables as placeholders for Bob’s input and still, the degree of <span><span><span><math>Q(Z)≤deg⁡(P)≤2LQ(Z)\\le \\deg(P)\\le 2^L</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>Q</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">Z</span><span>)</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>≤</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>de<span style=\"margin-right:0.01389em\">g</span></span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">P</span><span>)</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>≤</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8413em\"></span><span><span>2</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8413em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>L</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>. Alice sends Bob the coefficients of <span><span><span><math>Qa(Z)Q_a(Z)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span><span>Q</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.1514em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>a</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">Z</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> and Bob outputs <span><span><span><math>Qa(b)=P(a,b)=xiQ_a(b)=P(a,b)=x_i</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span><span>Q</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.1514em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>a</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>(</span><span>b</span><span>)</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">P</span><span>(</span><span>a</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>b</span><span>)</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.5806em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>.</p>\n</li>\n<li>\n<p><strong>Lower bound</strong> Note that by the lower bound of the index problem, we can conclude that the number of bits Alice needs to encode all the coefficients is <span><span><span><math>Ω(N)\\Omega(N)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>Ω</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span>. Since <span><span><span><math>Q(Z)Q(Z)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>Q</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">Z</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> is a <span><span><span><math>dd</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span>-variate polynomial of degree at most <span><span><span><math>2L2^L</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8413em\"></span><span><span>2</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8413em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>L</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>, it has at most <span><span><span><math>d2Ld^{2^L}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.0064em\"></span><span><span>d</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.0064em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span><span>2</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.9191em\"><span style=\"top:-2.931em;margin-right:0.0714em\"><span style=\"height:2.5em\"></span><span><span>L</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> coefficients. If <span><span><span><math>BB</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05017em\">B</span></span></span></span></span> is the number of maximum number of bits we need to represent any of these coefficients, we have: <span><span><span><math>B⋅d2L≥Ω(N)B \\cdot d^{2^L} \\geq \\Omega(N)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05017em\">B</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>⋅</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1.1423em;vertical-align:-0.136em\"></span><span><span>d</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.0064em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span><span>2</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.9191em\"><span style=\"top:-2.931em;margin-right:0.0714em\"><span style=\"height:2.5em\"></span><span><span>L</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>≥</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>Ω</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span>.</p>\n<p>Using the fact that all of the original parameters in BaseConv model use <span><span><span><math>O(log⁡N)O(\\log{N})</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span><span>(</span><span>lo<span style=\"margin-right:0.01389em\">g</span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span></span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> bits (as well as the fact that <span><span><span><math>d≤2(log⁡N)1−ϵd\\le 2^{(\\log{N})^{1-\\epsilon}}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8304em;vertical-align:-0.136em\"></span><span>d</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>≤</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.9869em\"></span><span><span>2</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.9869em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>(</span><span><span>l</span><span>o</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.01389em\">g</span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1952em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span></span><span><span>)</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8913em\"><span style=\"top:-2.931em;margin-right:0.0714em\"><span style=\"height:2.5em\"></span><span><span><span>1</span><span>−</span><span>ϵ</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>), a little bit of algebraic manipulation gives us our claimed lower bound of</p>\n</li>\n</ol><p><span><span><span><math>L≥Ω(ϵ⋅log⁡log⁡N)L\\ge \\Omega\\left(\\epsilon\\cdot\\log\\log{N}\\right)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8193em;vertical-align:-0.136em\"></span><span>L</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>≥</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>Ω</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span style=\"top:0em\">(</span><span>ϵ</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>⋅</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>lo<span style=\"margin-right:0.01389em\">g</span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>lo<span style=\"margin-right:0.01389em\">g</span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span></span><span style=\"top:0em\">)</span></span></span></span></span></span>.</p></details>\n<h3>Linear attention and MQAR</h3>\n<p>We have seen that Transformers can represent MQAR in constant model width and layers, independent of the sequence length <span><span><span><math>NN</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span></span></span></span></span>, and the scaling needed for gated convolutions to solve MQAR is undesirable.<em>Can we mimic Transformers without the quadratic scaling in sequence length?</em> Enter linear attention models! These retain input-dependent mixing, making it easy to compare tokens in the sequence unlike convolutions, and give nice efficiency properties (sub-quadratic training and constant inference).</p>\n<p><em><strong>We love polynomials</strong></em>  In spirit of our polynomial view of efficient architectures, we consider using a natural option for the feature map, a polynomial approximation to the attention exponential function following <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.04347\" target=\"_blank\">Zhang et al., 2024</a>. Recall the <a href=\"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Taylor_series\" target=\"_blank\">Taylor approximation of <span><span><span><math>exp⁡(x)\\exp(x)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>exp</span><span>(</span><span>x</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span></a>:</p>\n<p><span><span><span><math>exp⁡(x)=1+x+x2!+…+xtt!…\\exp(x) = 1 + x + \\frac{x}{2!} + … + \\frac{x^t}{t!} …</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>exp</span><span>(</span><span>x</span><span>)</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>1</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>+</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6667em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>x</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>+</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1.0404em;vertical-align:-0.345em\"></span><span><span></span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6954em\"><span style=\"top:-2.655em\"><span style=\"height:3em\"></span><span><span><span>2</span><span>!</span></span></span></span><span style=\"top:-3.23em\"><span style=\"height:3em\"></span><span style=\"border-bottom-width:0.04em\"></span></span><span style=\"top:-3.394em\"><span style=\"height:3em\"></span><span><span><span>x</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.345em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>+</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6667em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>…</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>+</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1.3482em;vertical-align:-0.345em\"></span><span><span></span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.0032em\"><span style=\"top:-2.655em\"><span style=\"height:3em\"></span><span><span><span>t</span><span>!</span></span></span></span><span style=\"top:-3.23em\"><span style=\"height:3em\"></span><span style=\"border-bottom-width:0.04em\"></span></span><span style=\"top:-3.394em\"><span style=\"height:3em\"></span><span><span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8703em\"><span style=\"top:-2.931em;margin-right:0.0714em\"><span style=\"height:2.5em\"></span><span><span>t</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.345em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>…</span></span></span></span></span></p>\n<p>Let’s let <span><span><span><math>⊗\\otimes</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6667em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>⊗</span></span></span></span></span> represent an outer product operation and let the linear attention feature map, <span><span><span><math>ϕ(\\phi(</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>ϕ</span><span>(</span></span></span></span></span>\\cdot<span><span><span><math>):RN×d’→RN×D): \\mathbb{R}^{N \\times d’} \\rightarrow \\mathbb{R}^{N \\times D}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>)</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>:</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"></span><span><span>R</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>×</span><span>d</span><span>’</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>→</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8413em\"></span><span><span>R</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8413em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>×</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">D</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>, be the Taylor polynomial for <span><span><span><math>exp⁡\\exp</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.625em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span>exp</span></span></span></span></span> following <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.04347\" target=\"_blank\">Zhang et al., 2024</a>:</p>\n<p><span><span><span><math>ϕ(q)=Concatenation(1,q,(q⊗q)/2!,…)\\phi(q) = \\mathrm{Concatenation}(1, q, (q \\otimes q)/2!,…)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>ϕ</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">q</span><span>)</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span><span>Concatenation</span></span><span>(</span><span>1</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">q</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">q</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>⊗</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">q</span><span>)</span><span>/2</span><span>!</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>…</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span></p>\n<p><span><span><span><math>ϕ(k)=Concatenation(1,k,(k⊗k)/2!,…)\\phi(k) = \\mathrm{Concatenation}(1, k, (k \\otimes k)/2!, …)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>ϕ</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span><span>)</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span><span>Concatenation</span></span><span>(</span><span>1</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>⊗</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span><span>)</span><span>/2</span><span>!</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>…</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span></p>\n<p>Elementwise multiplying <span><span><span><math>ϕ(q)\\phi(q)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>ϕ</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">q</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> and <span><span><span><math>ϕ(k)\\phi(k)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>ϕ</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span>, we get for <span><span><span><math>q,k∈RN×d’q, k \\in \\mathbb{R}^{N \\times d’}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8889em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">q</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>∈</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"></span><span><span>R</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>×</span><span>d</span><span>’</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>:\n<span><span><span><math>exp⁡(qk)=ϕ(q)ϕ(k)=Concatenation(1,qk,qqkk/2!,…)\\exp(qk) = \\phi(q)\\phi(k) = \\mathrm{Concatenation}(1, qk, qqkk/2!,…)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>exp</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">q</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span><span>)</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>ϕ</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">q</span><span>)</span><span>ϕ</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span><span>)</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span><span>Concatenation</span></span><span>(</span><span>1</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">q</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">qq</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">kk</span><span>/2</span><span>!</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>…</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span></p>\n<p>While we would need infinite terms (i.e., an infinite dimension tensor and amount of GPU hardware memory consumption) to exactly represent attention’s <span><span><span><math>exp⁡qk\\exp{qk}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8889em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span>exp</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">q</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span></span></span></span></span></span>, we find that roughly order-<span><span><span><math>22</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>2</span></span></span></span></span> (feature dimension <span><span><span><math>D=1+d’+d’2D = 1 + d’ + d’^2</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">D</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>1</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>+</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.7778em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>d</span><span>’</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>+</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"></span><span>d</span><span><span>’</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>2</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>) seems to be empirically effective given the range of values that <span><span><span><math>qq</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.625em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">q</span></span></span></span></span> and <span><span><span><math>kk</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span></span></span></span></span> fall into in large language modeling experiments.</p>\n<p>Varying values of <span><span><span><math>d’d’</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span>d</span><span>’</span></span></span></span></span> changes the amount of meory (size of <span><span><span><math>hth_t</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8444em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span>h</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.2806em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>t</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>) the model uses. While arithmetic circuit complexity typically focuses on the total size and depth of the circuit computing polynomials, we also care about the amount of external <em>memory</em> a model uses to produce an answer from a hardware efficiency perspective. Keeping track of the memory starts to fall into the communication complexity model of computation, bringing us to our final section.</p>\n<hr>\n<h2>Part 4: Memory-limited learning</h2>\n<p>Efficient LMs seeks to reduce the memory requirement relative to Transformers, which require massive <a href=\"https://kipp.ly/transformer-inference-arithmetic/\" target=\"_blank\">“KV”-caches that grow in input length</a>.</p>\n<details><summary><strong>Expand to recap the recurrent memory use by architecture!</strong></summary><p>Let’s briefly review the total state size (GPU memory consumption) <span><span><span><math>ss</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span>s</span></span></span></span></span> by different architectures as they processes an <span><span><span><math>x∈RN×dx \\in \\mathbb{R}^{N \\times d}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.5782em;vertical-align:-0.0391em\"></span><span>x</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>∈</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"></span><span><span>R</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>×</span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> input (see <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.18668\" target=\"_blank\">Appendix E.2</a> for more discussion).</p><ul>\n<li>Attention: the size of the KV cache (<span><span><span><math>hKh_{K}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8444em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span>h</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3283em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">K</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> and <span><span><span><math>hVh_{V}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8444em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span>h</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3283em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.22222em\">V</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>) described above is: <span><span><span><math>s=2×d×Ns = 2 \\times d \\times N</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span>s</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>2</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>×</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.7778em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>d</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>×</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span></span></span></span></span>.</li>\n<li>Linear attention: the state size is determined by <span><span><span><math>hth_t</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8444em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span>h</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.2806em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>t</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> as discussed above: <span><span><span><math>s=dDs = dD</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span>s</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span>d</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">D</span></span></span></span></span>, for feature dimension <span><span><span><math>DD</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">D</span></span></span></span></span> and model dimension <span><span><span><math>dd</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span>.</li>\n<li>Gated convolution (H3): the state is determined by the number of heads <span><span><span><math>dstated_{state}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8444em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span>d</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.2806em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>s</span><span>t</span><span>a</span><span>t</span><span>e</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>: <span><span><span><math>s=d×dstates = d \\times d_{state}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span>s</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.7778em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>d</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>×</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8444em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span>d</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.2806em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>s</span><span>t</span><span>a</span><span>t</span><span>e</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>.</li>\n</ul><p>Specifically, note the efficient Transformer alternatives seek to use external memory that’s independent of sequence length <span><span><span><math>NN</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span></span></span></span></span>.</p></details>\n<p>Autoregressive language modeling should remind you of one-pass streaming algorithms. In this vein, our theoretical analysis of the memory-efficient LMs invokes a <a href=\"https://theoryofcomputing.org/articles/v004a006/v004a006.pdf\" target=\"_blank\">prior lower bound result for the index problem</a> in the one-pass streaming setting: the one-way randomized communication complexity of the index problem for a n-length input bit string is <span><span><span><math>Ω(n)\\Omega(n)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>Ω</span><span>(</span><span>n</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span>.</p>\n<p><em><strong>Index problem.</strong></em> The index problem has two agents, Alice and Bob, where Alice has a string <span><span><span><math>x∈0,1nx \\in {0, 1}^n</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.5782em;vertical-align:-0.0391em\"></span><span>x</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>∈</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8932em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span><span><span>0</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>1</span></span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6987em\"><span style=\"top:-3.0973em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>n</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> and Bob has an index <span><span><span><math>i∈[n]i \\in [n]</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6986em;vertical-align:-0.0391em\"></span><span>i</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>∈</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>[</span><span>n</span><span>]</span></span></span></span></span>. The goal for the players is to output the <span><span><span><math>ithi^{th}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"></span><span><span>i</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8491em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>t</span><span>h</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> entry: <span><span><span><math>xix_i</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.5806em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>. We also require the communication to be one-way: only Alice is allowed to send a single message to Bob and Bob needs to output the answer.</p>\n<p>There is a pretty straightforward reduction from indexing to MQAR (and in fact to the simpler version AR where the query comes at the end). Specifically given the input <span><span><span><math>x∈{0,1}nx\\in\\{0,1\\}^n</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.5782em;vertical-align:-0.0391em\"></span><span>x</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>∈</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>{</span><span>0</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>1</span><span><span>}</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6644em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>n</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> and <span><span><span><math>i∈[n]i\\in [n]</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6986em;vertical-align:-0.0391em\"></span><span>i</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>∈</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>[</span><span>n</span><span>]</span></span></span></span></span>, the corresponding input to AR is (a sequence; index pair): <span><span><span><math>1,x1,…,j,xj,…,n,xn;i1,x_1,\\dots,j,x_j,\\dots,n,x_n;i</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.9456em;vertical-align:-0.2861em\"></span><span>1</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>1</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>…</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2861em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>…</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>n</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.1514em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>n</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>;</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>i</span></span></span></span></span>. Note that if a model solves this AR instance, then it should match <span><span><span><math>ii</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6595em\"></span><span>i</span></span></span></span></span> to the earlier occurrence of <span><span><span><math>ii</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6595em\"></span><span>i</span></span></span></span></span> and output <span><span><span><math>xix_i</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.5806em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>.</p>\n<p><em><strong>Recurrent models and MQAR.</strong></em> We can easily conclude that any recurrent model <span><span><span><math>M\\mathcal{M}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span>M</span></span></span></span></span> that solves MQAR requires the state size to be at least <span><span><span><math>Ω(N)\\Omega(N)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>Ω</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> bits. To show this, suppose Alice runs <span><span><span><math>M\\mathcal{M}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span>M</span></span></span></span></span> on the portion of the AR input above she has (i.e. <span><span><span><math>1,x1,…,j,xj,…,n,xn1,x_1,\\dots,j,x_j,\\dots,n,x_n</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.9456em;vertical-align:-0.2861em\"></span><span>1</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>1</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>…</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2861em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>…</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>n</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.1514em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>n</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>) and sends the final state <span><span><span><math>hN−1h_{N-1}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.9028em;vertical-align:-0.2083em\"></span><span><span>h</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3283em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>−</span><span>1</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2083em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> to Bob, who using the state <span><span><span><math>hN−1h_{N-1}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.9028em;vertical-align:-0.2083em\"></span><span><span>h</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3283em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>−</span><span>1</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2083em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> and his input <span><span><span><math>ii</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6595em\"></span><span>i</span></span></span></span></span> can run <span><span><span><math>M\\mathcal{M}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span>M</span></span></span></span></span> to output the input value at position <span><span><span><math>ii</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6595em\"></span><span>i</span></span></span></span></span>. Since we have a lower bound for the index problem, this would present a contradiction and we conclude <span><span><span><math>Ω(N)\\Omega(N)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>Ω</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> bits are required.<sup id=\"user-content-fnref-6\"><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-06-22-ac#fn-6\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">6</a></sup></p>\n<p>Maybe all hope is not lost! <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.05483\" target=\"_blank\">Our subsequent work</a> designs multi-pass recurrent models, which can use less memory than autoregressive models depending on the order of information in the input sequences.</p>\n<hr>\n<h2>Part 5: Tying the theory to experiments!</h2>\n<p>We evaluate several efficient LMs, from the architecture classes discussed above, on the MQAR problem. We vary the recurrent state size, or amount of memory each architecture uses during inference, on the <span><span><span><math>xx</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span>x</span></span></span></span></span>-axis.</p>\n<p>We can see the theoretical scaling results reflected in the figure below.</p>\n<ol>\n<li><strong>Attention baseline</strong> Sliding window attention is a natural input-dependent and fixed-memory architecture. As we traverse the window size (memory use), it faces a stark quality tradeoff.</li>\n<li><strong>Input independent convolutions</strong> Gated convolutions (H3, Hyena) falle below the a (sliding window) attention baseline.</li>\n<li><strong>Input dependent</strong> Input-dependent mixers (Based linear attention, Mamba’s selective SSMs) expand the Pareto frontier relative to attention!</li>\n<li><strong>Memory limits</strong> All these recurrent LMs face a memory vs. recall-quality tradeoff. Some models are better than others at using their alotted memory.</li>\n</ol>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:90%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-06-22-ac/tradeoff_ac.jpg\"></figure>\n<p>You can find more discussion of our empirical results in our <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2023-12-11-zoology1-analysis\" target=\"_blank\">prior blogposts</a> and try out our MQAR synthetic here: <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/zoology\" target=\"_blank\">https://github.com/HazyResearch/zoology</a>. We used our insights to <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2023-12-11-zoology2-based\" target=\"_blank\">design new LM architectures</a>.</p>\n<h2>Postscript: Related Works!</h2>\n<p>If you're interested in this line of work, we've compiled a quick reading list of works on complexity and language models to check out. This is a relatively biased list, please reach out if you have more pointers!</p>\n<details><summary><strong>Expand for the related works discussion</strong></summary><p>Works related to arithmetic circuits and language models:</p><ul>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/1611.01569\" target=\"_blank\">A Two-pronged Progress in Structured Dense Matrix Vector Multiplication</a>: Our initial work on structured matrices. The most relevant result on the equivalence of linear arithmetic circuit complexity of <span><span><span><math>WxWx</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">W</span><span>x</span></span></span></span></span> and sparse decomposition of <span><span><span><math>WW</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">W</span></span></span></span></span> is in Appendix B.</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2012.14966\" target=\"_blank\">Kaleidoscope: An Efficient Learnable Representation For All Structured Linear Maps</a>: Proves any (small depth) arithmetic circuit for <span><span><span><math>WxWx</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">W</span><span>x</span></span></span></span></span> can be converted into a decomposition of <span><span><span><math>WW</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">W</span></span></span></span></span> into structured sparse matrices with only a poly-log loss in parameters. The structured matrices are Butterfly matrices that arise naturally in FFT and lead to a differentiable family of matrices.</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2206.12490\" target=\"_blank\">Arithmetic Circuits, Structured Matrices and (not so) Deep Learning</a>: A survey that covers the above two results and puts them in context of the constraints imposed by deep learning. If you do not have much background in deep learning, this could be a good starting point.</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2204.00595\" target=\"_blank\">Monarch: Expressive Structured Matrices for Efficient and Accurate Training</a>: This paper proposed Monarch matrices that inherit the nice expressivity properties of Butterfly matrices but are also hardware friendly. In addition, there is a simple solution to the \"projection\" problem– given an arbitrary matrix, find the closest Monarch matrix in the Frobenius norm.</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.12109\" target=\"_blank\">Monarch Mixer: A Simple Sub-Quadratic GEMM-Based Architecture</a>: The above work showed how to replace the MLP layer with structured matrices. This paper showed how they can be used to replace Attention layer as well (there is a bunch of polynomial goodness in the appendices to show that these can be implemented in a causal manner).</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://proceedings.mlr.press/v201/duman-keles23a/duman-keles23a.pdf\" target=\"_blank\">On The Computational Complexity of Self-Attention</a> Attention requires quadratic runtime in the worst-case assuming SETH.</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2312.04927\" target=\"_blank\">Zoology: Measuring and Improving Recall in Efficient Language Models</a>: Introduced the MQAR problem, the BaseConv architecture and showed how BaseConv can simulate any (small depth) arithmetic circuits (beyond linear arithmetic circuits from Kaleidoscope) with only a poly-log loss in parameters. <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.18668\" target=\"_blank\">Simple linear attention language models balance the recall-throughput tradeoff</a>: The theory results are mostly on proving limitations of BaseConv using connections to communication complexity.</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.05483\" target=\"_blank\">Just read twice: Closing the recall gap for
