recurrent language models</a>: The theory explores linear recurrent models in the multi-pass setting, whereas most of the work on sub-quadratic architectures focuses on causal single-pass streaming language modeling.</li>\n</ul><p>State space models:</p><ul>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2008.07669\" target=\"_blank\">HiPPO: Recurrent Memory with Optimal Polynomial Projections</a>: Formulated the online functional approximation problem and connected it to orthogonal polynomials. <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2110.13985\" target=\"_blank\">Combining Recurrent, Convolutional, and Continuous-time Models with Linear State Space Layers</a>: Connected linear state space models to HiPPO and explicitly pointed out the advantages of its recurrent and convolutional views.</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2111.00396\" target=\"_blank\">Efficiently Modeling Long Sequences with Structured State Spaces</a> introduced the exciting S4 state space model based architecture! and <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2206.12037\" target=\"_blank\">How to Train Your HiPPO: State Space Models with Generalized Orthogonal Basis Projections</a> provides some more theoretical grounding for choices in S4.</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2312.00752\" target=\"_blank\">Mamba: Linear-Time Sequence Modeling with Selective State Spaces</a> State space models with data dependent state space model parameters! This has created lots of buzz!</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2405.21060\" target=\"_blank\">Transformers are SSMs: Generalized Models and Efficient Algorithms Through Structured State Space Duality</a>: A nice theoretical framework that connects works on state space models to those in linear attention.</li>\n</ul><p>Connections to other “traditional” computation models\nThere is a rapidly growing body of work connecting language model architectures to well studied models of computation (other than arithmetic circuits, e.g. boolean circuits) mainly with the goal of showing limitations of these models. Since this body of work is slightly orthogonal to this blog post, we’ll just give a biased sample below:</p><ul>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2207.00729\" target=\"_blank\">Log-precision Transformers are in <span><span><span><math>TC0TC^0</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">T</span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">C</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>0</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></a>; <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/1901.03429\" target=\"_blank\">With infinite precision, Transformers are as powerful as Turing machines</a></li>\n<li><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2210.02671\" target=\"_blank\">Log-precision Transformers are in a (slight) generalization of first order logic statements</a></li>\n<li>A strong dichotomy result on runtime of Attention <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2302.13214\" target=\"_blank\">based on whether the precision is sub-logarithmic or not</a>.</li>\n<li>Commonly used instantiations of <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.08819\" target=\"_blank\">State Space Models are in <span><span><span><math>TC0TC^0</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">T</span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">C</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>0</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> as well!</a></li>\n</ul></details>\n<h2>Takeaways and future questions</h2>\n<p>We’ve had a ton of fun playing with MQAR and trying to build efficient LMs that solve it more efficiently over the past while. Overall, we hope the polynomial view of efficient LMs helps you reason about key ingredients like input-dependent sequence mixing and managing the quality-efficiency tradeoffs around recurrent state size.</p>\n<p>A couple of questions for that might be interesting to think about:</p>\n<ol>\n<li>What are other “toy tasks”, like MQAR, that both are empirically meaningful and enable theoretical analysis? Perhaps not only for the sequence mixer. Work from [11] shows that both Transformers and SSMs struggle with certain capabilities like state tracking. Can we better understand when state tracking is important in language modeling with simple synthetics?</li>\n<li>We also don’t use the full power of BaseConv to prove our results (we largely instantiate a few types of convolution kernels, like shift kernels). What are the minimal sufficient representations for the capabilities we need?</li>\n<li>Our results show that there exists BaseConv models that exactly solve MQAR. However, we do not have any proof that running Gradient Descent with MQAR will converge to a BaseConv model that can solve MQAR. It would be very nice to prove that this is indeed the case. As a first step, it would be nice to present any provable fast learning algorithm that given test data from MQAR learns a BaseConv model that solves the problem.</li>\n</ol>\n<p>Please feel free to reach out to us: <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/simran@cs.stanford.edu\" target=\"_blank\">simran@cs.stanford.edu</a> and <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/eyuboglu@stanford.edu\" target=\"_blank\">eyuboglu@stanford.edu</a>.</p>\n<div>\n<hr>\n<ol>\n<li id=\"user-content-fn-1\">One view is to ignore these fine-grained skills and equate quality to perplexity. Efficient LMs have increasingly closed the perplexity gaps to Transformers! But, we find it could be useful to look at more fine-grained skills.<a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-06-22-ac#fnref-1\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">↩</a></li>\n<li id=\"user-content-fn-2\">Perhaps of interest to theory folks, we also prove the hardness of MQAR reduces to the hardness of set disjointness, a <a href=\"https://www.tcs.tifr.res.in/~arkadev/commSurvey.pdf\" target=\"_blank\">quintessential and decodes-old problem in complexity theory</a>.<a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-06-22-ac#fnref-2\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">↩</a></li>\n<li id=\"user-content-fn-3\">We note that the above result is most interesting when <span><span><span><math>Δ=O~(1)\\Delta=\\tilde{O}(1)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span>Δ</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1.1702em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.9202em\"><span style=\"top:-3em\"><span style=\"height:3em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span></span><span style=\"top:-3.6023em\"><span style=\"height:3em\"></span><span style=\"left:-0.1667em\"><span>~</span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>(</span><span>1</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span>-- luckily for us, many of the well known fast transforms (like the FFT) have poly-log depth arithmetic circuits.<a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-06-22-ac#fnref-3\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">↩</a></li>\n<li id=\"user-content-fn-4\">A subtle but crucial point here is that the linear maps that we want to implement are defined on matrices of length <span><span><span><math>O(Nd)O(Nd)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>d</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span>, but BaseConv does not have the ability to implement arbitrary linear maps over <em>matrices</em> in one layer (we are only allowed linear maps on the <em>rows or columns</em> of these matrices <em>re-packaged</em> as <span><span><span><math>O(N)×O(d)O(N)\\times O(d)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>)</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>×</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span><span>(</span><span>d</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> matrices). However, since the matrices we need to compute maps on here are very structured (shift, diagonal matrices), we can use the bias matrices and Hadamard product to implement them.<a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-06-22-ac#fnref-4\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">↩</a></li>\n<li id=\"user-content-fn-5\">Discussed previously, this follows from the definition of BaseConv since each term in the parenthesis in the definition of BaseConv–  <span><span><span><math>(u⋅W+b1)⊙(h∗u+b2)(u\\cdot W+b_1)\\odot(h\\ast u+ b_2)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>(</span><span>u</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>⋅</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.7667em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">W</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>+</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span><span>b</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>1</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>)</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>⊙</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>(</span><span>h</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>∗</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6667em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>u</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>+</span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>1</span></span></span></span></span>) map (since <span><span><span><math>W,b1,hW,b_1,h</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8889em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">W</span><span>,</span><span Multiplying two linear map gives us a polynomial of degree at most <span><span><span><math>22</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>2</span></span></span></span></span>.<a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-06-22-ac#fnref-5\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">↩</a></li>\n<li id=\"user-content-fn-6\">We note that the above reduction style is standard in data streaming lower bounds and indeed the connection to communication complexity lower bounds have also been exploited in other works to prove similar lower bounds (<a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.11174\" target=\"_blank\">Schlag et al., 2021</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.01032\" target=\"_blank\">Jelassi et al., 2024</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.09347\" target=\"_blank\">Bhattamishra et al., 2024</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2405.16674#:~:text=Limits%20of%20Deep%20Learning%3A%20Sequence%20Modeling%20through%20the%20Lens%20of%20Complexity%20Theory,-Nikola%20Zubi%C4%87%2C%20Federico&#x26;text=Deep%20learning%20models%20have%20achieved,function%20composition%20and%20compositional%20tasks\" target=\"_blank\">Zubić et al., 2024</a>, etc.).<a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-06-22-ac#fnref-6\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">↩</a></li>\n</ol>\n</div>,This blogpost was written as a resource in conjunction with our talk at STOC 2024. It focuses on work done with the following full team: Mic…,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-06-22-ac,<a href=\"https://arorasimran.com/\">Simran Arora</a>, <a href=\"https://sabrieyuboglu.com/\">Sabri Eyuboglu</a>, <a href='https://cse.buffalo.edu/faculty/atri/'>Atri Rudra</a>,2026-07-22T18:37:38.391Z,2024-06-22T07:00:00.700Z,[{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-06-22-ac/landscape.jpg\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1254,\"height\":537},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-06-22-ac/attn.jpg\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-06-22-ac/lin_attn.jpg\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-06-22-ac/ssm.jpg\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-06-22-ac/part1.jpg\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-06-22-ac/part2.jpg\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-06-22-ac/circuit.jpg\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-06-22-ac/dft_circuit.jpg\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-06-22-ac/basconv_ac.jpg\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-06-22-ac/pseudocode.jpg\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-06-22-ac/tradeoff_ac.jpg\",\"type\":\"photo\"}],163778663386687488,1211296081845936164,Announcing LoCoV1 and the Latest M2-BERT Models,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-05-20-m2-bert-retrieval,<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:60%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-01-11-m2-bert-retrieval/monarch_in_library.png\"></figure>\n<p>We are thrilled to announce a new long-context retrieval benchmark, <strong>LoCoV1</strong>, as well as our newest long-context <strong>M2-BERT</strong> models! After hearing back from the community where long-context retrieval could be most useful, we are building on our earlier LoCoV0 and M2-BERT models with the release of:</p>\n<ul>\n<li><strong>LoCoV1</strong>: An expanded long-context benchmark consisting of 12 tasks drawn from law, medicine, science, finance, corporate governance, government reports, and more. LoCoV1 tasks use real-world datasets spanning diverse domains, including Tau Scrolls, QASPER, LongBench, the Legal Case Reports corpus, and more.</li>\n<li><strong>M2-BERT-V2 Models</strong>: Using LoCoV1 and our original pretrained M2-BERT checkpoints, we fine-tuned a new set of M2-BERT models for 128, 2k, 8k, and 32k input tokens. We include these inference checkpoints on HuggingFace and Together. We also provide our code base for developing your own long-context retrieval encoders with M2-BERT!</li>\n</ul>\n<h1>LoCoV1: Gauging Long-Context Handling in Modern Retrieval Systems</h1>\n<p>In our <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-01-11-m2-bert-retrieval\" target=\"_blank\">earlier blog post</a>, we asked for the use cases where long-context retrieval would be impactful. After many great conversations with researchers and practitioners, we are excited to present <strong>LoCoV1</strong>, which includes several new application domains such as law and programming.</p>\n<p>We hoped to encompass a new set of naturalistic, domain-specific retrieval tasks that reflect real-world use cases for long-context queries and documents. LoCoV1 draws from several existing long-context benchmarks, including <a href=\"https://huggingface.co/datasets/tau/scrolls\" target=\"_blank\">Tau Scrolls</a>, <a href=\"https://huggingface.co/datasets/THUDM/LongBench\" target=\"_blank\">LongBench</a>, and <a href=\"https://huggingface.co/datasets/allenai/qasper\" target=\"_blank\">QASPER</a>, as well as several domain-specific datasets not originally intended for retrieval, like <a href=\"https://www.courtlistener.com/help/api/bulk-data/\" target=\"_blank\">CourtListener</a>, the <a href=\"https://archive.ics.uci.edu/dataset/239/legal+case+reports\" target=\"_blank\">Australian Legal Court Reports</a> dataset, and the <a href=\"https://archive.org/details/stackexchange\" target=\"_blank\">StackOverflow</a> forum.</p>\n<p>Each dataset was selected for two main properties:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>Their longer, more complex formatting of queries and documents.</li>\n<li>Their ability to gauge long-context handling by containing relevant information throughout their queries and documents.</li>\n</ul>\n<p>We make the queries and documents for LoCoV1 available on HuggingFace!</p>\n<ul>\n<li><a href=\"https://huggingface.co/datasets/hazyresearch/LoCoV1-Queries\" target=\"_blank\">LoCoV1 Queries</a></li>\n<li><a href=\"https://huggingface.co/datasets/hazyresearch/LoCoV1-Documents\" target=\"_blank\">LoCoV2 Documents</a></li>\n</ul>\n<p>For an overview of the dataset, please see the table below. We also provide additional dataset information in <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2402.07440\" target=\"_blank\">our ArXiv release</a>, including:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>Query and document examples (Table 11)</li>\n<li>Distribution plots of the document lengths (Figure 5)</li>\n</ul>\n<table><thead><tr><th>Dataset</th><th>Source</th><th>Domain</th><th align=\"center\"># of Train  Queries</th><th align=\"center\"># of Train Documents</th><th align=\"center\"># of Test Queries</th><th align=\"center\"># of Test Documents</th><th align=\"center\">Avg. Query Length</th><th align=\"center\">Avg. Doc Length</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td>SummScreenFD</td><td>Tau Scrolls</td><td>Screenwriting</td><td align=\"center\">3673</td><td align=\"center\">3673</td><td align=\"center\">338</td><td align=\"center\">338</td><td align=\"center\">590</td><td align=\"center\">30,792</td></tr><tr><td>Gov. Report</td><td>Tau Scrolls</td><td>Government</td><td align=\"center\">17457</td><td align=\"center\">17457</td><td align=\"center\">972</td><td align=\"center\">972</td><td align=\"center\">3,871</td><td align=\"center\">55,280</td></tr><tr><td>QMSUM</td><td>Tau Scrolls</td><td>Corporate Management</td><td align=\"center\">1257</td><td align=\"center\">162</td><td align=\"center\">272</td><td align=\"center\">35</td><td align=\"center\">430</td><td align=\"center\">58,129</td></tr><tr><td>QASPER - Title to Full Text</td><td>QASPER</td><td>Science</td><td align=\"center\">888</td><td align=\"center\">888</td><td align=\"center\">416</td><td align=\"center\">416</td><td align=\"center\">71</td><td align=\"center\">22,315</td></tr><tr><td>QASPER - Abstract to Full Text</td><td>QASPER</td><td>Science</td><td align=\"center\">888</td><td align=\"center\">888</td><td align=\"center\">416</td><td align=\"center\">416</td><td align=\"center\">931</td><td align=\"center\">22,315</td></tr><tr><td>MultiFieldQA</td><td>LongBench</td><td>General Domain</td><td align=\"center\">120</td><td align=\"center\">120</td><td align=\"center\">30</td><td align=\"center\">30</td><td align=\"center\">62</td><td align=\"center\">29,465</td></tr><tr><td>2WikimQA</td><td>LongBench</td><td>General Domain</td><td align=\"center\">240</td><td align=\"center\">240</td><td align=\"center\">60</td><td align=\"center\">60</td><td align=\"center\">69</td><td align=\"center\">37,867</td></tr><tr><td>Passage Retrieval</td><td>LongBench</td><td>General Domain</td><td align=\"center\">240</td><td align=\"center\">240</td><td align=\"center\">60</td><td align=\"center\">60</td><td align=\"center\">840</td><td align=\"center\">35,814</td></tr><tr><td>CourtListener - Plain Text</td><td>CourtListener</td><td>Law</td><td align=\"center\">10000</td><td align=\"center\">10000</td><td align=\"center\">2000</td><td align=\"center\">2000</td><td align=\"center\">146</td><td align=\"center\">48,190</td></tr><tr><td>CourtListener - HTML</td><td>CourtListener</td><td>Law</td><td align=\"center\">10000</td><td align=\"center\">10000</td><td align=\"center\">2000</td><td align=\"center\">2000</td><td align=\"center\">146</td><td align=\"center\">47,028</td></tr><tr><td>Australian Legal Case Reports</td><td>Australian Legal Case Report</td><td>Law</td><td align=\"center\">3094</td><td align=\"center\">3094</td><td align=\"center\">770</td><td align=\"center\">770</td><td align=\"center\">14,986</td><td align=\"center\">47,536</td></tr><tr><td>StackOverflow</td><td>StackOverflow</td><td>Programming</td><td align=\"center\">1599</td><td align=\"center\">18005</td><td align=\"center\">400</td><td align=\"center\">7741</td><td align=\"center\">758</td><td align=\"center\">4,544</td></tr></tbody></table>\n<h1>The Latest Set of M2-BERT Embedding Encoders</h1>\n<p>With our new LoCoV1 benchmark, we fine-tune a new set of M2-BERT encoders for a broader diversity of long-context domains, starting with our pretrained checkpoints capable of handling 128, 2048, 8192, and 32768 input tokens. We include <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/m2\" target=\"_blank\">our latest code</a> here and our model releases on HuggingFace:</p>\n<ul>\n<li><a href=\"https://huggingface.co/hazyresearch/M2-BERT-128-Retrieval-Encoder-V1\" target=\"_blank\">M2-BERT-80M-128-Retrieval</a></li>\n<li><a href=\"https://huggingface.co/hazyresearch/M2-BERT-2k-Retrieval-Encoder-V1\" target=\"_blank\">M2-BERT-80M-2k-Retrieval</a></li>\n<li><a href=\"https://huggingface.co/hazyresearch/M2-BERT-8K-Retrieval-Encoder-V1\" target=\"_blank\">M2-BERT-80M-8k-Retrieval</a></li>\n<li><a href=\"https://huggingface.co/hazyresearch/M2-BERT-32K-Retrieval-Encoder-V1\" target=\"_blank\">M2-BERT-80M-32k-Retrieval</a></li>\n</ul>\n<h1>Results of New M2-BERT Encoders on LoCoV1</h1>\n<p>We compare our latest long-context M2-BERT models to the same state-of-the-art retrieval encoders from our original blog post: <a href=\"https://huggingface.co/intfloat/e5-mistral-7b-instruct\" target=\"_blank\">E5-Mistral</a>, <a href=\"https://huggingface.co/BAAI/bge-large-en-v1.5\" target=\"_blank\">BGE</a>, <a href=\"https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/embeddings\" target=\"_blank\">OpenAI Ada</a>, <a href=\"https://cohere.com/\" target=\"_blank\">Cohere</a>, and <a href=\"https://www.voyageai.com/\" target=\"_blank\">VoyageAI</a>. We also test additional retrieval baselines, including <a href=\"https://nlp.stanford.edu/IR-book/html/htmledition/okapi-bm25-a-non-binary-model-1.html\" target=\"_blank\">BM25</a>, <a href=\"https://github.com/stanford-futuredata/ColBERT\" target=\"_blank\">ColBERT</a>, and <a href=\"https://blog.vespa.ai/announcing-long-context-colbert-in-vespa/\" target=\"_blank\">LongColBERT</a>.</p>\n<p>For our long-context retrieval models, we implement the same fine-tuning procedure as our previous blog post:\nwe fine-tuned M2-BERT-80M-2K, -8K, and -32K on the training sets of these tasks using the orthogonal loss. These are our M2-BERT-80M-2K-retrieval, -8K-retrieval, and -32K-retrieval models.</p>\n<table><thead><tr><th align=\"center\">Model</th><th align=\"center\"># Params</th><th align=\"center\">Max. Seq. Len.</th><th align=\"center\">LoCo Score</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td align=\"center\">BGE-Large Zeroshot</td><td align=\"center\">335M</td><td align=\"center\">512</td><td align=\"center\">56.9</td></tr><tr><td align=\"center\">BGE-Large Finetuned</td><td align=\"center\">335M</td><td align=\"center\">512</td><td align=\"center\">65.0</td></tr><tr><td align=\"center\">e5-mistral 7b-instruct</td><td align=\"center\">7.11B</td><td align=\"center\">4096</td><td align=\"center\">73.0</td></tr><tr><td align=\"center\">Jina Embeds.</td><td align=\"center\">137M</td><td align=\"center\">8192</td><td align=\"center\">67.2</td></tr><tr><td align=\"center\">OpenAI Ada</td><td align=\"center\">N/A</td><td align=\"center\">8192</td><td align=\"center\">63.9</td></tr><tr><td align=\"center\">Voyage 001</td><td align=\"center\">N/A</td><td align=\"center\">4096</td><td align=\"center\">56.9</td></tr><tr><td align=\"center\">Cohere English-3.0</td><td align=\"center\">N/A</td><td align=\"center\">512</td><td align=\"center\">59.0</td></tr><tr><td align=\"center\">BM25</td><td align=\"center\">N/A</td><td align=\"center\">N/A</td><td align=\"center\">81.5</td></tr><tr><td align=\"center\">ColBERT</td><td align=\"center\">110M</td><td align=\"center\">512</td><td align=\"center\">54.3</td></tr><tr><td align=\"center\">LongColBERT</td><td align=\"center\">110M</td><td align=\"center\">N/A</td><td align=\"center\">68.0</td></tr><tr><td align=\"center\">M2-BERT 128</td><td align=\"center\">80M</td><td align=\"center\">128</td><td align=\"center\">69.7</td></tr><tr><td align=\"center\">M2-BERT 2048</td><td align=\"center\">80M</td><td align=\"center\">2048</td><td align=\"center\">81.4</td></tr><tr><td align=\"center\">M2-BERT 8192</td><td align=\"center\">80M</td><td align=\"center\">8129</td><td align=\"center\">88.9</td></tr><tr><td align=\"center\">M2-BERT 32768</td><td align=\"center\">80M</td><td align=\"center\">32768</td><td align=\"center\">95.2</td></tr></tbody></table>\n<p>We find that lexical approaches, such as BM25, perform particularly strongly on the LoCoV1 tasks, which is particularly impressive considering how computationally low-cost it is! We also find that our M2-BERT retrieval models can outperform much larger models on long-context tasks, surpassing fine-tuned models 4x larger and zero-shot neural models up to 85x larger!</p>\n<p>As we continue to make progress in understanding where long-context benchmarks and models are useful, we are excited to see how neural, lexical, and hybrid retrieval encoders continue to improve long-context retrieval performance.</p>\n<p>For our complete LoCoV1 results with task subscores, please see Table 13 in <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2402.07440\" target=\"_blank\">our ArXiv release</a>.</p>\n<h1>Limitations</h1>\n<p>We also explored the efficacy of M2-BERT in a reranker setting on the <a href=\"https://huggingface.co/datasets/Shitao/MLDR\" target=\"_blank\">MLDR</a> retrieval dataset, which has 800 queries and 200,000 long-context documents. Using exact search, we found that M2-BERT performance suffered, similar to other embedding approaches like <a href=\"https://huggingface.co/intfloat/e5-mistral-7b-instruct\" target=\"_blank\">E5-Mistral</a>, <a href=\"https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/embeddings\" target=\"_blank\">OpenAI Ada</a>, and <a href=\"https://huggingface.co/BAAI/bge-m3\" target=\"_blank\">M3</a>.\nHowever, we augmented M2-BERT by using it as a reranker on BM25 retrieval results.\nWith this approach, we were able to improve long-context retrieval performance and achieve 80.9 nDCG@10 compared to current SOTA of 77.5 by LongColBERT.\nIn future work, we would like to further explore how M2-BERT embeddings can be augmented or combined with lexical approaches to maximize both short and long document handling.</p>\n<h1>Acknowledgments</h1>\n<p>We would like to thank <a href=\"https://crfm.stanford.edu/\" target=\"_blank\">the Stanford Center for Research on Foundation Models (CRFM)</a>, and the <a href=\"https://ai.stanford.edu/\" target=\"_blank\">Stanford AI Laboratory</a> for supporting our research! Thank you to Alycia Lee for helping set up AutoModel for our models on HuggingFace.</p>\n<p>We would also like to thank our collaborators at <a href=\"https://www.together.ai/\" target=\"_blank\">Together AI</a> for their support in developing these models, and their help in hosting these models in their <a href=\"https://www.together.ai/blog/embeddings-endpoint-release\" target=\"_blank\">new embedding API</a>. Thanks to Together, we were able to test out early versions of these models at a Hackathon with <a href=\"https://www.together.ai/blog/rag-tutorial-mongodb\" target=\"_blank\">MongoDB</a>, and we’re looking forward to all the new use cases for long-context retrieval models including RAG integrations with <a href=\"https://www.together.ai/blog/rag-tutorial-langchain\" target=\"_blank\">LangChain</a> and <a href=\"https://www.together.ai/blog/rag-tutorial-llamaindex\" target=\"_blank\">LlamaIndex</a>.</p>\n<p>Check out their <a href=\"https://www.together.ai/blog/embeddings-endpoint-release\" target=\"_blank\">blog</a> <a href=\"https://www.together.ai/blog/rag-tutorial-mongodb\" target=\"_blank\">posts</a> on new <a href=\"https://www.together.ai/blog/rag-tutorial-langchain\" target=\"_blank\">usage</a> of these <a href=\"https://www.together.ai/blog/rag-tutorial-llamaindex\" target=\"_blank\">models</a>!</p>,We are thrilled to announce a new long-context retrieval benchmark, LoCoV1, as well as our newest long-context M2-BERT models! After…,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-05-20-m2-bert-retrieval,<a href='https://jonsaadfalcon.com/'>Jon Saad-Falcon</a>, <a href='https://danfu09.github.io/'>Dan Fu</a>, <a href=\"https://arorasimran.com/\">Simran Arora</a>,2026-07-22T18:37:38.390Z,2024-05-20T07:00:00.737Z,[{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-01-11-m2-bert-retrieval/monarch_in_library.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1024,\"height\":1024}],163778663386687488,1211296081845936165,ECLAIR: A Treat for the Enterprise,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-05-18-eclair,<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:5px\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-05-18-eclair/eclair_icon.jpeg\" style=\"width:70%\"></figure>\n<h2>👩‍💻 Death by 1,000 Clicks</h2>\n<p>Over <a href=\"https://nationalskillscoalition.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/02/NSC-DigitalDivide_report_Feb2023.pdf\" target=\"_blank\">90% of jobs</a> now require digital skills, with workers averaging <a href=\"https://www.automationanywhere.com/company/press-room/global-research-reveals-worlds-most-hated-office-tasks\" target=\"_blank\">3 hrs/day</a> <strong>doing repetitive digital tasks tangential to their core responsibilities</strong> -- a phenomenon referred to as <a href=\"https://kffhealthnews.org/news/death-by-a-thousand-clicks/\" target=\"_blank\">\"death by 1,000 clicks\"</a>.</p>\n<p>While <a href=\"https://www.technologyreview.com/2024/05/14/1092407/googles-astra-is-its-first-ai-for-everything-agent/\" target=\"_blank\">tons</a> of <a href=\"https://www.fastcompany.com/90945078/pi-woebot-personalai-ai-assistants\" target=\"_blank\">recent</a> <a href=\"https://techcrunch.com/2023/06/29/inflection-ai-lands-1-3b-investment-to-build-more-personal-ai/\" target=\"_blank\">attention</a> has focused on developing better <strong>personal assistants</strong>, automating these <strong>tedious enterprise workflows</strong> represents a <strong>much larger (but much harder!) opportunity.</strong></p>\n<p>McKinsey estimates that <a href=\"https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/mckinsey-digital/our-insights/the-economic-potential-of-generative-ai-the-next-productivity-frontier#key-insights\" target=\"_blank\">$4 trillion/year</a> in productivity gains could be realized at places like hospitals, government agencies, and corporations with high administrative burden and knowledge-intensive tasks.</p>\n<p>$4 trillion sounds like a lot -- So how do we get there?</p>\n<h2>⏰ The Promise of Robotic Process Automation (RPA)</h2>\n<p>Today, enterprises hoping to automate workflows typically purchase <strong>Robotic Process Automation (RPA)</strong> software from vendors like UIPath or Blue Prism.</p>\n<p>In RPA, a bot is <strong>hard-coded to follow a set of predefined rules</strong> for completing a workflow -- it’s essentially a decision tree built using a low-code editor, as shown in Figure #1.</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:5px\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-05-18-eclair/uipath.png\" style=\"width:90%\"><p>Figure 1: Screenshot of how an RPA bot is built using UIPath (a popular RPA vendor).</p></figure>\n<p>Narrowly-scoped RPA deployments can have <a href=\"https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/mckinsey-digital/our-insights/the-next-acronym-you-need-to-know-about-rpa\" target=\"_blank\">ROIs of 30-200%</a> and <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2311.11775\" target=\"_blank\">double the speed</a> of workflows. However, the <strong>adoption of RPA has been inhibited</strong> by three key failure modes that surfaced in our interviews with technology leaders at a hospital and B2B enterprise (see <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2405.03710\" target=\"_blank\">our paper</a> for full case studies):</p>\n<ol>\n<li><strong>High set-up costs</strong>: It typically takes 12-18 months to go from project kickoff to deployment, and often requires trained specialists to map workflows, write automation scripts, and integrate with IT infrastructure.</li>\n<li><strong>Unreliable execution</strong>: Since RPA relies on hard-coded rules, bots cannot adapt to slight variations in input. In our B2B case study, the RPA bot was initially only 60% accurate and took 6 months of improvements to reach 95%.</li>\n<li><strong>Burdensome maintenance</strong>: Deployments require continued human oversight to validate the RPA bot’s outputs and fix edge cases. In our B2B case study, the bot required 2 full-time equivalents (FTEs) worth of continued monitoring.</li>\n</ol>\n<p>The common cause of these shortcomings is the impossibility of enumerating all possible scenarios that a bot might encounter. Most enterprise knowledge is “<a href=\"https://www.nber.org/system/files/working_papers/w20485/w20485.pdf\" target=\"_blank\">tacit</a>” -- i.e. hard to explicitly articulate and almost never written down -- which makes a rule-based system like RPA fundamentally limited.</p>\n<p>Examples of such “tacit” knowledge include:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>Billing administrators at Stanford Hospital knowing that lapsed insurance coverage should never be deleted from a patient’s health record, but rather have its end date set to “Jan 1, 1901” for auditing purposes; or</li>\n<li>Customer support agents at a B2B enterprise knowing how far a conversation should go before offering a discount or escalating to a supervisor.</li>\n</ul>\n<p>These small bits of knowledge are typically acquired through observation and on-the-job experience rather than written documentation.</p>\n<p><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2401.01614\" target=\"_blank\">Recent</a> <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.03720\" target=\"_blank\">research</a> <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2311.07562\" target=\"_blank\">suggests</a> <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.07456\" target=\"_blank\">that</a> <strong>multimodal foundation models (FMs) such as GPT-4 can sidestep the failure modes of traditional RPA</strong>, just as deep learning eclipsed rule-based approaches over the past decade. Multimodal FMs have been shown to automate simple <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2401.13919\" target=\"_blank\">web navigation</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.07939\" target=\"_blank\">desktop</a>, and <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2312.13771\" target=\"_blank\">mobile</a> tasks by leveraging their visual understanding, real-time decision making, and generalized reasoning capabilities.</p>\n<p>However, a <strong>large gap still exists between these proof-of-concepts and enterprise-ready solutions</strong> -- it’s one thing for GPT-4 to order you a burger on Doordash, but would you trust it to coordinate your hip replacement surgery?</p>\n<p>Thus, we ask:</p>\n<div align=\"center\"><i>Can we design a system which uses multimodal foundation models to unlock “tacit” enterprise knowledge and automate complex enterprise workflows without any human oversight?</i></div>\n<h2>⚙️ ECLAIR: Enterprise sCaLe AI for woRkflows</h2>\n<p>We take the first natural step and apply multimodal FMs across all three stages of traditional RPA through a system called <strong><a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2405.03710\" target=\"_blank\">ECLAIR</a></strong> (“Enterprise s<strong>C</strong>a<strong>L</strong>e <strong>AI</strong> for wo<strong>R</strong>kflows”).</p>\n<p>Critically, we show that it is possible to automate all three stages of the RPA pipeline (from task specification to task auditing) <em>without</em> any human oversight, as shown below (see Figure 2):</p>\n<ol>\n<li><strong>Demonstrate</strong>: ECLAIR uses multimodal FMs to learn from human workflow expertise by watching video demonstrations and reading written documentation. This <strong><em>lowers set-up costs</em></strong> and <strong><em>technical barriers to entry</em></strong>.</li>\n<li><strong>Execute</strong>: ECLAIR observes the state of the screen to plan actions by leveraging the reasoning and visual understanding abilities of multimodal FMs in conjunction with the task-specific knowledge learned in the <strong>Demonstrate</strong> step. This <strong><em>improves robustness</em></strong> over traditional methods that require rigid APIs or hard-coding of rules.</li>\n<li><strong>Validate</strong>: ECLAIR utilizes multimodal FMs to self-monitor and error correct its actions in real-time. This <strong><em>reduces the need for human oversight</em></strong>.</li>\n</ol>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:5px\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-05-18-eclair/eclair_explanation.jpg\"><p>Figure 2:  ECLAIR vs. Traditional RPA. (left) ECLAIR decreases set-up costs by using FMs to learn expertise via passive observation of human demonstrations. (center) ECLAIR improves reliability by navigating interfaces without relying on rigid APIs or hard-coded rules. (right)  ECLAIR reduces the need for human oversight by recording itself executing the workflow and self-validating if the workflow was successfully completed.</p></figure>\n<h2>🏥 Real-World Healthcare Workflow Demo</h2>\n<p>Talk is cheap -- show me a demo!</p>\n<p>We applied ECLAIR to a <strong><em>real-world enterprise workflow</em></strong> sourced from Stanford Hospital -- placing a patient telesitter order in Epic on behalf of a nurse. See a full recording of ECLAIR completing the workflow <a href=\"https://drive.google.com/file/d/1In5Qo-MMIG33M5UrqQICC7thE_NZs3eJ/view?usp=sharing\" target=\"_blank\">here</a>.</p>\n<p><em>(Epic is the most popular electronic health record software used by hospitals, and “placing a patient telesitter order” means that a nurse has requested for a patient to be placed under continuous remote visual monitoring)</em></p>\n<ol>\n<li><strong>Demonstrate</strong>: Using ECLAIR, we record a nurse placing a patient sitter order. Our script runs in the background and captures a full screen recording and telemetry data such as clicks, keystrokes, and scrolls (Figure 3). ECLAIR then generates screenshots at key frames (i.e. frames of the video in which an action was taken) and writes a standard operating procedure (“SOP”) that details every step of the workflow in natural language (Figure 4).</li>\n</ol>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:5px\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-05-18-eclair/ECLAIR_RECORD.jpg\" style=\"width:90%\"><p>Figure 3: Capturing domain expertise via video and telemetry capture.</p></figure>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:5px\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-05-18-eclair/ECLAIR_WATCH.jpg\" style=\"width:90%\"><p>Figure 4: Translating a demonstration capture into a Standard Operating Procedure (“SOP”)</p></figure>\n<ol start=\"2\">\n<li><strong>Execute</strong>: At execution time, ECLAIR takes full control of the computer (Figure 5). Using a manually edited SOP and raw screenshots of the computer screen, ECLAIR decides the next action to take (i.e., “click on the submit button with location x=1241, y=74”). This action is then executed. ECLAIR repeats this process until it determines that the task is complete (Figure 6).</li>\n</ol>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:5px\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-05-18-eclair/ECLAIR_CONTROL.jpg\" style=\"width:90%\"><p>Figure 5: ECLAIR controls the computer by issuing pyautogui commands.</p></figure>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:5px\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-05-18-eclair/ECLAIR_EXECUTE.jpg\" style=\"width:90%\"><p>Figure 6: Task execution using vision understanding, guided by SOP.</p></figure>\n<ol start=\"3\">\n<li><strong>Validate</strong>: Finally, ECLAIR validates that the workflow was successfully completed (Figure 7). It does so by processing the action trace it generated during the <strong>Execution</strong> phase alongside the generated SOP from the <strong>Demonstrate</strong> step. ECLAIR also provides action-level and workflow-level validation during the <strong>Execution</strong> phase to detect and correct mistakes.</li>\n</ol>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:5px\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-05-18-eclair/ECLAIR_VALIDATE.jpg\" style=\"width:90%\"><p>Figure 7: Autonomous audits, guided by the SOP and recording of ECLAIR executing the workflow.</p></figure>\n<h2>⏭️ To Infinity and Beyond</h2>\n<p>ECLAIR is a <strong>first step</strong> towards applying FMs to enterprise workflow automation.</p>\n<p>There are significant <strong>opportunities for further research</strong>, such as improved error handling and monitoring to improve task completion rates, as well as a better understanding of how to incorporate human-in-the-loop review for workflows that require human signoff (e.g. a physician signing a medication order).</p>\n<p>We encourage users to apply ECLAIR to their own workflows and use cases -- Please see our <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/eclair-agents\" target=\"_blank\">code repository</a> for more details.</p>\n<p>We are currently <strong>looking for design partners to help us take ECLAIR to the next level</strong>! If you would like to see ECLAIR applied to your use case, please <strong>fill out this form to get 1-1 set-up support and customization for deploying ECLAIR: <a href=\"https://forms.gle/ndkGmyMQESGpdSSH6\" target=\"_blank\">ECLAIR Waitlist Form</a></strong></p>\n<h2>📚 Additional Resources</h2>\n<ul>\n<li>How do you use ECLAIR? Check out our Github here for sample scripts: <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/eclair-agents\" target=\"_blank\">https://github.com/HazyResearch/eclair-agents</a></li>\n<li>Want to learn more? Read our paper here: <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2405.03710\" target=\"_blank\">https://arxiv.org/abs/2405.03710</a></li>\n</ul>\n<h2>Acknowledgements</h2>\n<p>This work would not have been possible without amazing collaborators at Stanford Healthcare who selflessly shared their time and expertise with us. In particular, we are extremely grateful for the support of Stanford Healthcare’s TDS (Nikesh Kotecha, Aditya Sharma, Nigam Shah), AI-Cares Initiative (Minh Nguyen), and Nursing Innovation and Informatics (Nerissa Ambers and Darren Batara) for their incredible help with this project!</p>,👩‍💻 Death by 1,000 Clicks Over 90% of jobs now require digital skills, with workers averaging 3 hrs/day doing repetitive digital tasks…,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-05-18-eclair,<a href=\"https://twitter.com/avanika15\">Avanika Narayan*</a>, <a href=\"https://michaelwornow.net\">Michael Wornow*</a>, <a href='https://cs.stanford.edu/people/chrismre/'>Chris Ré</a>.,2026-07-22T18:37:38.389Z,2024-05-18T07:00:00.740Z,[{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-05-18-eclair/eclair_icon.jpeg\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1024,\"height\":1024},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-05-18-eclair/uipath.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-05-18-eclair/eclair_explanation.jpg\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-05-18-eclair/ECLAIR_RECORD.jpg\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-05-18-eclair/ECLAIR_WATCH.jpg\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-05-18-eclair/ECLAIR_CONTROL.jpg\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-05-18-eclair/ECLAIR_EXECUTE.jpg\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-05-18-eclair/ECLAIR_VALIDATE.jpg\",\"type\":\"photo\"}],163778663386687488,1211296081845936166,GPUs Go Brrr,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-05-12-tk,<p>AI uses an <a href=\"https://www.semianalysis.com/p/gpt-4-architecture-infrastructure\" target=\"_blank\">awful</a> <a href=\"https://www.instagram.com/p/C2QARHJR1sZ\" target=\"_blank\">lot</a> <a href=\"https://www.semianalysis.com/p/google-gemini-eats-the-world-gemini\" target=\"_blank\">of</a> <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2202.05924\" target=\"_blank\">compute</a>.</p>\n<p>In the last few years we’ve focused a great deal of our work on making AI use less compute (e.g. <a href=\"https://www.together.ai/blog/based\" target=\"_blank\">Based</a>, <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2023-12-11-truly-subquadratic\" target=\"_blank\">Monarch Mixer</a>, <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2023-01-20-h3\" target=\"_blank\">H3</a>, <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2023-06-08-hyena-safari\" target=\"_blank\">Hyena</a>, <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2022-06-11-simplifying-s4\" target=\"_blank\">S4</a>, among others) and run more efficiently on the compute that we have (e.g. <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2023-01-12-flashattention-long-sequences\" target=\"_blank\">FlashAttention</a>, <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2023-07-17-flash2\" target=\"_blank\">FlashAttention-2</a>, <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2023-11-13-flashfftconv\" target=\"_blank\">FlashFFTConv</a>). Lately, reflecting on these questions has prompted us to take a step back, and ask two questions:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>What does the hardware actually want?</li>\n<li>And how can we give that to it?</li>\n</ul>\n<p>This post is a mixture of practice and philosophy. On the practical side, we’re going to talk about what we’ve learned about making GPUs go brr -- and release an embedded DSL, <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/ThunderKittens\" target=\"_blank\">ThunderKittens</a>, that we’ve built to help us write some particularly speedy kernels (which we are also <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/ThunderKittens/tree/main/examples\" target=\"_blank\">releasing</a>). On the philosophical side, we’ll briefly talk about how what we’ve learned has changed the way we think about AI compute.</p>\n<h2>What's in an H100?</h2>\n<p>For this post, we’re going to focus on the NVIDIA H100 for two reasons. First, it represents an awful lot of new compute going online. Second, we think the trends it implies are going to continue in future generations, and probably from other manufacturers, too. But bear in mind (and we will repeat in case you forget) that most of this post applies in some form to other GPUs, too.</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:5px\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-05-12-tk/brr.png\"><p>Figure 1: brr</p></figure>\n<p>Advance apologies for restating the data sheet, but the details of the hardware are important for the discussion to come. An H100 SXM GPU contains, for our purposes:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>80 GB of HBM3 with 3 TB/s of bandwidth. (A bit less bandwidth in practice.)</li>\n<li>50 MB of L2 cache with 12 TB/s of bandwidth, split across the GPU into two 25MB sections connected by a crossbar. (The crossbar sucks.)</li>\n<li>132 streaming multiprocessors (SM’s), where each has:\n<ul>\n<li>up to 227 KB of shared memory within a 256 KB L1 cache. (Together, these have about 33 TB/s of bandwidth.)</li>\n<li>a tensor memory accelerator (TMA) -- a new chunk of hardware in Hopper that can do asynchronous address generation and fetch memory. It also does other things like facilitate the on-chip memory network (distributed shared memory) but we’re not going to focus on this much, today.</li>\n<li>4 quadrants, where each quadrant has:\n<ul>\n<li>A warp scheduler</li>\n<li>512 vector registers (each containing 32 4-byte words)</li>\n<li>A tensor core for matrix multiplies</li>\n<li>A bunch of built-in instructions like sums, multiplies, that operate in parallel on these vector registers.</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n</ul>\n</li>\n</ul>\n<p>There’s a lot of other stuff, too (memory controllers, instruction caches, etc) but we don’t care about any of that right now.</p>\n<p>All of the compute happens in the SM’s. <em>Most</em> of it happens in the registers.</p>\n<p><em>Great, how do I make it go brr?</em></p>\n<p>Keep the tensor core fed. That’s it.</p>\n<p><em>Wait, really?</em></p>\n<p>Yes. That’s the game.</p>\n<p>An H100 GPU has 989 TFLOPs of half-precision matrix multiply compute, and ~60 TFLOPs of “everything else”. So, every cycle the tensor core is in use, you’re getting at least 94% utilization of the hardware. And every cycle the tensor core is not in use, you’re getting no more than 6% utilization of the hardware. Put another way:</p>\n<div align=\"center\">% utilization H100 = % tensor cores active cycles +/- 6%.</div>\n<p>Now it turns out that keeping the tensor core fed is easier said than done. We’ve discovered a number of quirks to the hardware that are important to keeping the matrix multiplies rolling. Much of this also applies to non-H100 GPUs, but the H100 is particularly tricky to keep fed so we focus on it here. (The RTX 4090, by comparison, is very easy to work with as illustrated in figure 2.)</p>\n<ul>\n<li>WGMMA instructions are necessary but also really irritating to use.</li>\n<li>Shared memory is not actually that fast and also requires great care.</li>\n<li>Address generation is expensive.</li>\n<li>Occupancy remains helpful, and registers are generally the key resource.</li>\n</ul>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:5px\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-05-12-tk/spirits.png\"><p>Figure 2: NVIDIA GPUs (H100 and 4090) and their spirit animals (canadian goose and golden retriever puppy).</p></figure>\n<p>Let’s go through each of these in order.</p>\n<h3>WGMMA Instructions</h3>\n<p>The H100 has a new set of instructions called “warp group matrix multiply accumulate” (<code>wgmma.mma_async</code> in PTX, or <code>HGMMA</code>/<code>IGMMA</code>/<code>QGMMA</code>/<code>BGMMA</code> in SASS). To understand what makes them special, we need to look briefly at how you used to have to use tensor cores. The tensor core instructions available on previous GPUs were <code>wmma.mma.sync</code> and <code>mma.sync</code> instructions. With these instructions a warp of 32 threads on a single quadrant of an SM would synchronously feed their chunk of the data into the tensor core and await the result. Only then could they move on.</p>\n<p>Not so with <code>wgmma.mma_async</code> instructions. Here, 128 consecutive threads -- split across all quadrants of the SM -- collaboratively synchronize, and asynchronously launch a matrix multiply directly from shared memory (and optionally also registers.) These warps can then go do other things with their registers while the matrix multiply happens, and await the result whenever they want.</p>\n<p>In our microbenchmarks, we found that these instructions are necessary to extract the full compute of the H100. Without them, the GPU seems to top out around 63% of its peak utilization; we suspect this is because the tensor cores want a deep hardware pipeline to keep them fed, even from local resources.</p>\n<p>Unfortunately, the memory layouts for these instructions <a href=\"https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/parallel-thread-execution/index.html#asynchronous-warpgroup-level-matrix-register-fragment-wgmma-64n16\" target=\"_blank\">are</a> <a href=\"https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/parallel-thread-execution/index.html#asynchronous-warpgroup-level-matrix-shared-memory-layout\" target=\"_blank\">quite</a> <a href=\"https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/parallel-thread-execution/index.html#asynchronous-warpgroup-level-matrix-shared-memory-layout-swizzling-modes\" target=\"_blank\">complicated</a>. The unswizzled shared memory layouts suffer from very poor coalescing, and so they require substantial additional bandwidth from L2. The swizzled memory layouts are flat-out incorrectly documented, which took considerable time for us to figure out. They’re also brittle, in that they appear to only work for specific matrix shapes and do not play well with other parts of the wgmma.mma_async instructions. For example, the hardware can transpose sub-matrices on its way to the tensor cores -- but only if the layout is not swizzled.</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:5px\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-05-12-tk/lies.png\"><p>Figure 3: NVIDIA’s <span style=\"color:red\"><em>lies</em></span>. This is an extraordinarily misleading representation of the actual 128b swizzled wgmma layout. This diagram cost us three weeks of life that we will not get back, hence the public shaming.</p></figure>\n<p>We’ve also found that unswizzled wgmma layouts have both poor memory coalescing as well as bank conflicts. On kernels such as flash attention, TMA and the L2 cache are both fast enough so as to hide these problems reasonably well. But to make the full use of the hardware, memory request must be coalesced and bank conflicts avoided, and then controlling layouts very carefully becomes critical.</p>\n<p>Despite these pains, these instructions really are necessary to make full use of the H100. Without them, you’ve already lost 37% of the potential performance of the GPU!</p>\n<h3>Shared memory</h3>\n<p>Shared memory appears to have a single-access latency of around <a href=\"https://chipsandcheese.com/2023/07/02/nvidias-h100-funny-l2-and-tons-of-bandwidth/\" target=\"_blank\">30 cycles</a> (this matches our observations, too). That doesn’t sound like much, but in that time the SM’s tensor cores could have done almost two full 32x32 square matrix multiplies.</p>\n<p>In previous work (like Flash Attention), we’ve focused more on the HBM-SRAM bottleneck. And indeed: this really used to be the bottleneck! But as HBM has gotten faster and the tensor cores continue to grow out of proportion with the rest of the chip, even relatively small latencies like those from shared memory have also become important to either remove or hide.</p>\n<p>Shared memory can be tricky to work with because it is “banked” into 32 separate stores of memory. If one is not careful, this can lead to something called “bank conflicts”, where the same memory bank is being asked to simultaneously provide multiple different pieces of memory. This leads to requests being serialized, and in our experience this can disproportionately slow down a kernel -- and the register layouts required by wgmma and mma instructions would naively suffer from these bank conflicts. The solution is to rearrange shared memory with various “swizzling” patterns so as to avoid these conflicts, but it is an important detail to get right.</p>\n<p>More generally, we have found it very valuable to avoid movement between registers and shared memory when possible, and otherwise to use the built-in hardware (wgmma and TMA instructions) to do data movement asynchronously when possible. Synchronous movement using the actual warps is a worst-case fallback with the greatest generality.</p>\n<h3>Address Generation</h3>\n<p>One interesting quirk of the H100 is that the tensor cores and memory are both fast enough that merely producing the memory addresses to fetch takes a substantial fraction of the resources of the chip. (This is even more the case when complicated interleaved or swizzling patterns are added in.)</p>\n<p>NVIDIA appears to understand this, as they have bestowed on us the Tensor Memory Accelerator (or TMA, as it likes to be called). TMA allows you to specify a multi-dimensional tensor layout in global and shared memory, tell it to asynchronously fetch a subtile of that tensor, and trip a barrier when it’s done. This saves all of the address generation costs, and additionally makes it much easier to construct pipelines.</p>\n<p>We have found TMA to be, like wgmma.mma_async, completely indispensable in achieving the full potential of the H100. (Probably moreso than wgmma, in our experience.) It saves register resources and instruction dispatches, and also has useful features such as the ability to perform reductions onto global memory asynchronously, too -- this is particularly useful in complex backwards kernels. As with wgmma, the main quirk of it is that its swizzling modes are a bit difficult to decipher without some reverse engineering, but we had substantially less pain on this point.</p>\n<h3>Occupancy</h3>\n<p>For those newer to CUDA, occupancy refers to the number of co-scheduled threads on the exact same execution hardware. Each cycle, the warp scheduler on that quadrant of the SM will try to issue an instruction to a warp of threads that are ready for an instruction. NVIDIA uses this model because it can enable the hardware to be more easily kept full. For example, while one warp of threads is waiting for a matrix multiply, another can receive an instruction to use the fast exponential hardware.</p>\n<p>In some ways, the H100 is less reliant on occupancy than previous generations of the hardware. The asynchronous features of the chip mean that even a single instruction stream can keep many parts of the hardware busy -- fetching memory, running matrix multiplies, doing shared memory reductions, and still simultaneously running math on the registers.</p>\n<p>But occupancy is very good at hiding both sins and sync’s. A perfectly designed pipeline might run reasonably fast even without any additional occupancy, but our observations suggest that NVIDIA really has designed their GPUs with occupancy in mind. And there are enough synchronizations -- and enough ways to make mistakes -- that finding ways to increase occupancy has, in our experience, usually yielded good returns at increasing the realized utilization of the hardware.</p>\n<p>Finally, while occupancy is merely useful on the H100, we have found it to be increasingly important on the A100 and RTX 4090, respectively, likely because they rely increasingly on synchronous instruction dispatches, relative to the H100.</p>\n<h2>ThunderKittens</h2>\n<p>Based on the above, we asked ourselves how we might make it easier to write the kinds of kernels we care about while still extracting the full capabilities of the hardware. Motivated by a continuing proliferation of new architectures within the lab (and the fact that Flash Attention is like 1200 lines of code), we ended up designing a DSL embedded within CUDA -- at first for our own internal use.</p>\n<p>But then we decided it was useful enough that, with love in our hearts, we cleaned it up and have released it for you. ThunderKittens is that embedded DSL. It is named ThunderKittens because we think kittens are cute, and also we think it is funny to make you type <code>kittens::</code> in your code.</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:5px\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-05-12-tk/tk.png\"><p>Figure 4: A ThunderKitten. Look at her big eyes! Are you not be entranced!?!?</p></figure>\n<p>It is meant to be as simple as possible, and contains four templated types:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>Register tiles -- 2D tensors on the register file.</li>\n<li>Register vectors -- 1D tensors on the register file.</li>\n<li>Shared tiles -- 2D tensors in shared memory.</li>\n<li>Shared vectors -- 1D tensors in shared memory.</li>\n</ul>\n<p>Tiles are parameterized by a height, width, and layout. Register vectors are parameterized by a length and a layout, and shared vectors just by a length. (They don’t generally suffer from bank conflicts.)</p>\n<p>We also give operations to manipulate them, either at the warp level or at the level of a collaborative group of warps. Examples include:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>Initializers -- zero out a shared vector, for example.</li>\n<li>Unary ops, like exp</li>\n<li>Binary ops, like mul</li>\n<li>Row / column ops, like a row_sum</li>\n</ul>\n<p>Since ThunderKittens is embedded within CUDA (contrasting libraries like Triton which we also love very much and rely on heavily), the abstractions fail gracefully. If it’s missing something, just extend it to do what you want!</p>\n<p>To show an example of these primitives in action, consider Tri’s lovely flash attention -- a beautiful algorithm, but <a href=\"https://github.com/Dao-AILab/flash-attention/blob/main/csrc/flash_attn/src/flash_fwd_kernel.h\" target=\"_blank\">complicated</a> to implement in practice, even on top of NVIDIA’s wonderful Cutlass library.</p>\n<p>Here's a simple forward flash attention kernel for an RTX 4090, written in ThunderKittens.</p>\n<div><pre><code class=\"language-cpp\"><span><span>#</span><span>define</span> <span>NUM_WORKERS</span> <span><span>16</span> </span><span>// This kernel uses 16 workers in parallel per block, to help issue instructions more quickly.</span></span>\n<span>using</span> <span>namespace</span> kittens<span>;</span> <span>// this kernel only handles headdim=64 for simplicity. Also n should be a multiple of 256 here.</span>\n__global__ <span>void</span> <span>attend_ker64</span><span>(</span><span>int</span> n<span>,</span> <span>const</span> bf16<span>*</span> __restrict__ __q__<span>,</span> <span>const</span> bf16<span>*</span> __restrict__ __k__<span>,</span> <span>const</span> bf16<span>*</span> __restrict__ __v__<span>,</span> bf16<span>*</span> __o__<span>)</span> <span>{</span>\n\n    <span>auto</span> warpid        <span>=</span> kittens<span>::</span><span>warpid</span><span>(</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>auto</span> block_start   <span>=</span> blockIdx<span>.</span>x<span>*</span><span>(</span>n<span>*</span><span>64</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>const</span> bf16 <span>*</span>_q <span>=</span> __q__ <span>+</span> block_start<span>,</span> <span>*</span>_k <span>=</span> __k__ <span>+</span> block_start<span>,</span> <span>*</span>_v <span>=</span> __v__ <span>+</span> block_start<span>;</span>\n          bf16 <span>*</span>_o <span>=</span> __o__ <span>+</span> block_start<span>;</span>\n\n    <span>extern</span> __shared__ alignment_dummy __shm<span>[</span><span>]</span><span>;</span> <span>// this is the CUDA shared memory</span>\n    shared_allocator <span>al</span><span>(</span><span>(</span><span>int</span><span>*</span><span>)</span><span>&#x26;</span>__shm<span>[</span><span>0</span><span>]</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    \n    <span>// K and V live in shared memory -- this is about all that will fit.</span>\n    <span><span>st_bf_1x4</span><span><span>&#x3C;</span>ducks<span>::</span>st_layout<span>::</span>swizzle<span>></span></span></span> <span>(</span><span>&#x26;</span>k_smem<span>)</span><span>[</span>NUM_WORKERS<span>]</span> <span>=</span> al<span>.</span><span><span>allocate</span><span><span>&#x3C;</span>st_bf_1x4<span>&#x3C;</span>ducks<span>::</span>st_layout<span>::</span>swizzle<span>></span><span>,</span> NUM_WORKERS<span>></span></span></span><span>(</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span><span>st_bf_1x4</span><span><span>&#x3C;</span>ducks<span>::</span>st_layout<span>::</span>swizzle<span>></span></span></span> <span>(</span><span>&#x26;</span>v_smem<span>)</span><span>[</span>NUM_WORKERS<span>]</span> <span>=</span> al<span>.</span><span><span>allocate</span><span><span>&#x3C;</span>st_bf_1x4<span>&#x3C;</span>ducks<span>::</span>st_layout<span>::</span>swizzle<span>></span><span>,</span> NUM_WORKERS<span>></span></span></span><span>(</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n\n    <span>// Initialize all of the register tiles.</span>\n    rt_bf_1x4<span>&#x3C;</span><span>></span> q_reg<span>,</span> k_reg<span>,</span> v_reg<span>;</span> <span>// v_reg need to be swapped into col_l</span>\n    rt_fl_1x1<span>&#x3C;</span><span>></span> att_block<span>;</span>\n    rt_bf_1x1<span>&#x3C;</span><span>></span> att_block_mma<span>;</span>\n    rt_fl_1x4<span>&#x3C;</span><span>></span> o_reg<span>;</span>\n    rt_fl_1x1<span>&#x3C;</span><span>></span><span>::</span>col_vec max_vec_last<span>,</span> max_vec<span>;</span> <span>// these are column vectors for the attention block</span>\n    rt_fl_1x1<span>&#x3C;</span><span>></span><span>::</span>col_vec norm_vec_last<span>,</span> norm_vec<span>;</span> <span>// these are column vectors for the attention block</span>\n    \n    <span>int</span> qo_blocks <span>=</span> n <span>/</span> <span>(</span>q_reg<span>.</span>rows<span>*</span>NUM_WORKERS<span>)</span><span>,</span> kv_blocks <span>=</span> n <span>/</span> <span>(</span>q_reg<span>.</span>rows<span>*</span>NUM_WORKERS<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n\n    <span>for</span><span>(</span><span>auto</span> q_blk <span>=</span> <span>0</span><span>;</span> q_blk <span>&#x3C;</span> qo_blocks<span>;</span> q_blk<span>++</span><span>)</span> <span>{</span>\n\n        <span>// each warp loads its own Q tile of 16x64, and then multiplies by 1/sqrt(d)</span>\n        <span>load</span><span>(</span>q_reg<span>,</span> _q <span>+</span> <span>(</span>q_blk<span>*</span>NUM_WORKERS <span>+</span> warpid<span>)</span><span>*</span>q_reg<span>.</span>num_elements<span>,</span> q_reg<span>.</span>cols<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n        <span>mul</span><span>(</span>q_reg<span>,</span> q_reg<span>,</span> <span>__float2bfloat16</span><span>(</span><span>0.125f</span><span>)</span><span>)</span><span>;</span> <span>// temperature adjustment</span>\n\n        <span>// zero flash attention L, M, and O registers.</span>\n        <span>neg_infty</span><span>(</span>max_vec<span>)</span><span>;</span> <span>// zero registers for the Q chunk</span>\n        <span>zero</span><span>(</span>norm_vec<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n        <span>zero</span><span>(</span>o_reg<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n\n        <span>// iterate over k, v for these q's that have been loaded</span>\n        <span>for</span><span>(</span><span>auto</span> kv_idx <span>=</span> <span>0</span><span>;</span> kv_idx <span>&#x3C;</span> kv_blocks<span>;</span> kv_idx<span>++</span><span>)</span> <span>{</span>\n\n            <span>// each warp loads its own chunk of k, v into shared memory</span>\n            <span>load</span><span>(</span>v_smem<span>[</span>warpid<span>]</span><span>,</span> _v <span>+</span> <span>(</span>kv_idx<span>*</span>NUM_WORKERS <span>+</span> warpid<span>)</span><span>*</span>q_reg<span>.</span>num_elements<span>,</span> q_reg<span>.</span>cols<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n            <span>load</span><span>(</span>k_smem<span>[</span>warpid<span>]</span><span>,</span> _k <span>+</span> <span>(</span>kv_idx<span>*</span>NUM_WORKERS <span>+</span> warpid<span>)</span><span>*</span>q_reg<span>.</span>num_elements<span>,</span> q_reg<span>.</span>cols<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n            <span>__syncthreads</span><span>(</span><span>)</span><span>;</span> <span>// we need to make sure all memory is loaded before we can begin the compute phase</span>\n\n            <span>// now each warp goes through all of the subtiles, loads them, and then does the flash attention internal alg.</span>\n            <span>for</span><span>(</span><span>int</span> subtile <span>=</span> <span>0</span><span>;</span> subtile <span>&#x3C;</span> NUM_WORKERS<span>;</span> subtile<span>++</span><span>)</span> <span>{</span>\n\n                <span>load</span><span>(</span>k_reg<span>,</span> k_smem<span>[</span>subtile<span>]</span><span>)</span><span>;</span> <span>// load k from shared into registers</span>\n\n                <span>zero</span><span>(</span>att_block<span>)</span><span>;</span> <span>// zero 16x16 attention tile</span>\n                <span>mma_ABt</span><span>(</span>att_block<span>,</span> q_reg<span>,</span> k_reg<span>,</span> att_block<span>)</span><span>;</span> <span>// Q@K.T</span>\n\n                <span>copy</span><span>(</span>norm_vec_last<span>,</span> norm_vec<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n                <span>copy</span><span>(</span>max_vec_last<span>,</span>  max_vec<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n\n                <span>row_max</span><span>(</span>max_vec<span>,</span> att_block<span>,</span> max_vec<span>)</span><span>;</span> <span>// accumulate onto the max_vec</span>\n                <span>sub_row</span><span>(</span>att_block<span>,</span> att_block<span>,</span> max_vec<span>)</span><span>;</span> <span>// subtract max from attention -- now all &#x3C;=0</span>\n                <span>exp</span><span>(</span>att_block<span>,</span> att_block<span>)</span><span>;</span> <span>// exponentiate the block in-place.</span>\n\n                <span>sub</span><span>(</span>max_vec_last<span>,</span> max_vec_last<span>,</span> max_vec<span>)</span><span>;</span> <span>// subtract new max from old max to find the new normalization.</span>\n                <span>exp</span><span>(</span>max_vec_last<span>,</span> max_vec_last<span>)</span><span>;</span> <span>// exponentiate this vector -- this is what we need to normalize by.</span>\n                <span>mul</span><span>(</span>norm_vec<span>,</span> norm_vec<span>,</span> max_vec_last<span>)</span><span>;</span> <span>// and the norm vec is now normalized.</span>\n\n                <span>row_sum</span><span>(</span>norm_vec<span>,</span> att_block<span>,</span> norm_vec<span>)</span><span>;</span> <span>// accumulate the new attention block onto the now-rescaled norm_vec</span>\n                <span>div_row</span><span>(</span>att_block<span>,</span> att_block<span>,</span> norm_vec<span>)</span><span>;</span> <span>// now the attention block is correctly normalized</span>\n\n                <span>mul</span><span>(</span>norm_vec_last<span>,</span> norm_vec_last<span>,</span> max_vec_last<span>)</span><span>;</span> <span>// normalize the previous norm vec according to the new max</span>\n                <span>div</span><span>(</span>norm_vec_last<span>,</span> norm_vec_last<span>,</span> norm_vec<span>)</span><span>;</span> <span>// normalize the previous norm vec according to the new norm</span>\n\n                <span>copy</span><span>(</span>att_block_mma<span>,</span> att_block<span>)</span><span>;</span> <span>// convert to bf16 for mma_AB</span>\n\n                <span>load</span><span>(</span>v_reg<span>,</span> v_smem<span>[</span>subtile<span>]</span><span>)</span><span>;</span> <span>// load v from shared into registers.</span>\n                rt_bf_1x4<span>&#x3C;</span>ducks<span>::</span>rt_layout<span>::</span>col<span>></span> <span>&#x26;</span>v_reg_col <span>=</span> <span>swap_layout_inplace</span><span>(</span>v_reg<span>)</span><span>;</span> <span>// this is a reference and the call has invalidated v_reg</span>\n\n                <span>mul_row</span><span>(</span>o_reg<span>,</span> o_reg<span>,</span> norm_vec_last<span>)</span><span>;</span> <span>// normalize o_reg in advance of mma_AB'ing onto it</span>\n                <span>mma_AB</span><span>(</span>o_reg<span>,</span> att_block_mma<span>,</span> v_reg_col<span>,</span> o_reg<span>)</span><span>;</span> <span>// mfma onto o_reg with the local attention@V matmul.</span>\n            <span>}</span>\n            <span>__syncthreads</span><span>(</span><span>)</span><span>;</span> <span>// we need to make sure all warps are done before we can start loading the next kv chunk</span>\n        <span>}</span>\n\n        <span>store</span><span>(</span>_o <span>+</span> <span>(</span>q_blk<span>*</span>NUM_WORKERS <span>+</span> warpid<span>)</span><span>*</span>q_reg<span>.</span>num_elements<span>,</span> o_reg<span>,</span> q_reg<span>.</span>cols<span>)</span><span>;</span> <span>// write out o. compiler has an issue with register usage if d is made constexpr q_reg.rows :/</span>\n    <span>}</span>\n<span>}</span>\n</code></pre></div>\n<p>Altogether, this is about 60 lines of CUDA sitting at 75% hardware utilization -- and while it is fairly dense, most of the complexity is in the algorithm, rather than in swizzling patterns or register layouts. And what of all of the complexity of TMA, WGMMA, swizzling modes, and descriptors? Here’s a FlashAttention-2 forward pass for the H100, written with ThunderKittens.</p>\n<div><pre><code class=\"language-cpp\"><span>template</span><span>&#x3C;</span><span>int</span> D<span>></span>\n__global__  <span>__launch_bounds__</span><span>(</span><span>(</span>NUM_WORKERS<span>)</span><span>*</span>kittens<span>::</span>WARP_THREADS<span>,</span> <span>2</span><span>)</span>\n<span>void</span> <span>fwd_attend_ker_dim</span><span>(</span><span>int</span> N<span>,</span> <span>const</span> CUtensorMap<span>*</span> tma_q<span>,</span> <span>const</span> CUtensorMap<span>*</span> tma_k<span>,</span> <span>const</span> CUtensorMap<span>*</span> tma_v<span>,</span> CUtensorMap<span>*</span> tma_o<span>)</span> <span>{</span>\n    <span>extern</span> __shared__ <span>int</span> __shm<span>[</span><span>]</span><span>;</span> <span>// this is the CUDA shared memory</span>\n    tma_swizzle_allocator <span>al</span><span>(</span><span>(</span><span>int</span><span>*</span><span>)</span><span>&#x26;</span>__shm<span>[</span><span>0</span><span>]</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n\n    <span>constexpr</span> <span>int</span> tile_width <span>=</span> fwd_attend_ker_tile_dims<span>&#x3C;</span>D<span>></span><span>::</span>tile_width<span>;</span> <span>// constants</span>\n    <span>constexpr</span> <span>int</span> qo_height  <span>=</span> fwd_attend_ker_tile_dims<span>&#x3C;</span>D<span>></span><span>::</span>qo_height<span>;</span>\n    <span>constexpr</span> <span>int</span> kv_height  <span>=</span> fwd_attend_ker_tile_dims<span>&#x3C;</span>D<span>></span><span>::</span>kv_height<span>;</span>\n\n    <span><span>st_bf</span><span><span>&#x3C;</span>qo_height<span>,</span> tile_width<span>,</span> layout_q<span>></span></span></span>          <span>(</span><span>&#x26;</span>q_smem<span>)</span>   <span>[</span>NUM_WARPGROUPS<span>]</span> <span>=</span> al<span>.</span><span><span>allocate</span><span><span>&#x3C;</span>st_bf<span>&#x3C;</span>qo_height<span>,</span> tile_width<span>,</span> layout_q<span>></span><span>,</span>          NUM_WARPGROUPS<span>></span></span></span><span>(</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span><span>st_bf</span><span><span>&#x3C;</span>kv_height<span>,</span> tile_width<span>,</span> layout_k<span>></span></span></span>          <span>(</span><span>&#x26;</span>k_smem<span>)</span><span>[</span><span>2</span><span>]</span><span>[</span>NUM_WORKERS_KV<span>]</span> <span>=</span> al<span>.</span><span><span>allocate</span><span><span>&#x3C;</span>st_bf<span>&#x3C;</span>kv_height<span>,</span> tile_width<span>,</span> layout_k<span>></span><span>,</span> <span>2</span><span>,</span>       NUM_WORKERS_KV<span>></span></span></span><span>(</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span><span>st_bf</span><span><span>&#x3C;</span>kv_height<span>,</span> tile_width<span>,</span> layout_v<span>></span></span></span>          <span>(</span><span>&#x26;</span>v_smem<span>)</span><span>[</span><span>2</span><span>]</span><span>[</span>NUM_WORKERS_KV<span>]</span> <span>=</span> al<span>.</span><span><span>allocate</span><span><span>&#x3C;</span>st_bf<span>&#x3C;</span>kv_height<span>,</span> tile_width<span>,</span> layout_v<span>></span><span>,</span> <span>2</span><span>,</span>       NUM_WORKERS_KV<span>></span></span></span><span>(</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n\n    <span>int</span> tic <span>=</span> <span>0</span><span>,</span> toc <span>=</span> <span>1</span><span>;</span>\n \n    rt_fl<span>&#x3C;</span><span>1</span><span>,</span> kv_height<span>></span> att_block<span>;</span>\n    rt_bf<span>&#x3C;</span><span>1</span><span>,</span> kv_height<span>></span> att_block_mma<span>;</span>\n    rt_fl<span>&#x3C;</span><span>1</span><span>,</span> qo_height<span>></span> o_prev<span>;</span>\n    col_vec<span>&#x3C;</span>rt_fl<span>&#x3C;</span><span>1</span><span>,</span> kv_height<span>>></span> max_vec_last<span>,</span> max_vec<span>;</span>\n    col_vec<span>&#x3C;</span>rt_fl<span>&#x3C;</span><span>1</span><span>,</span> kv_height<span>>></span> norm_vec_last<span>,</span> norm_vec<span>;</span>\n\n    <span>int</span> warpid      <span>=</span> kittens<span>::</span><span>warpid</span><span>(</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>int</span> warpgroupid <span>=</span> warpid<span>/</span>kittens<span>::</span>WARPGROUP_WARPS<span>;</span>\n\n    <span>int</span> kv_blocks <span>=</span> N <span>/</span> <span>(</span>NUM_WORKERS_KV<span>*</span>k_smem<span>[</span><span>0</span><span>]</span><span>[</span><span>0</span><span>]</span><span>.</span>rows<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n\n    __shared__ <span>uint64_t</span> qsmem_barrier<span>,</span> kvsmem_barrier<span>;</span><span>//, vsmem_barrier;</span>\n\n    <span>int</span> q_phasebit <span>=</span> <span>0</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>int</span> kv_phasebit <span>=</span> <span>0</span><span>;</span>\n\n    <span>if</span> <span>(</span>threadIdx<span>.</span>x <span>==</span> <span>0</span><span>)</span> <span>{</span>\n        tma<span>::</span><span><span>init_barrier</span><span><span>&#x3C;</span>st_bf<span>&#x3C;</span>qo_height<span>,</span> tile_width<span>,</span> layout_q<span>></span><span>,</span> NUM_WARPGROUPS<span>></span></span></span><span>(</span>qsmem_barrier<span>,</span> <span>1</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n        tma<span>::</span><span><span>init_barrier</span><span><span>&#x3C;</span>st_bf<span>&#x3C;</span>kv_height<span>,</span> tile_width<span>,</span> layout_k<span>></span><span>,</span> NUM_WORKERS_KV<span>*</span><span>2</span><span>></span></span></span><span>(</span>kvsmem_barrier<span>,</span> <span>1</span><span>)</span><span>;</span> \n    <span>}</span>\n\n    <span>if</span> <span>(</span>warpid <span>==</span> <span>0</span><span>)</span> <span>{</span>\n        <span>for</span> <span>(</span><span>int</span> wg <span>=</span> <span>0</span><span>;</span> wg <span>&#x3C;</span> NUM_WORKERS<span>/</span>kittens<span>::</span>WARPGROUP_WARPS<span>;</span> wg<span>++</span><span>)</span> <span>{</span> <span>// load q</span>\n            <span>int</span> tile_idx <span>=</span> <span>(</span>blockIdx<span>.</span>y <span>*</span> NUM_WARPGROUPS <span>*</span> gridDim<span>.</span>x<span>)</span> <span>+</span> <span>(</span>blockIdx<span>.</span>x <span>*</span> NUM_WARPGROUPS<span>)</span> <span>+</span> wg<span>;</span>\n            tma<span>::</span><span>load_async</span><span>(</span><span>(</span>q_smem<span>[</span>wg<span>]</span><span>)</span><span>,</span> tma_q<span>,</span> qsmem_barrier<span>,</span> tile_idx<span>)</span><span>;</span> \n        <span>}</span>\n        <span>for</span> <span>(</span><span>int</span> w <span>=</span> <span>0</span><span>;</span> w <span>&#x3C;</span> NUM_WORKERS_KV<span>;</span> w<span>++</span><span>)</span> <span>{</span> <span>// load k, v      </span>\n            <span>int</span> tile_idx <span>=</span> <span>(</span>blockIdx<span>.</span>y <span>*</span> NUM_WORKERS_KV <span>*</span> kv_blocks<span>)</span> <span>+</span> <span>(</span><span>0</span> <span>*</span> NUM_WORKERS_KV<span>)</span> <span>+</span> w<span>;</span> \n
