 tma<span>::</span><span>load_async</span><span>(</span><span>(</span>k_smem<span>[</span>tic<span>]</span><span>[</span>w<span>]</span><span>)</span><span>,</span> tma_k<span>,</span> kvsmem_barrier<span>,</span> tile_idx<span>)</span><span>;</span> \n            tma<span>::</span><span>load_async</span><span>(</span><span>(</span>v_smem<span>[</span>tic<span>]</span><span>[</span>w<span>]</span><span>)</span><span>,</span> tma_v<span>,</span> kvsmem_barrier<span>,</span> tile_idx<span>)</span><span>;</span> \n        <span>}</span>\n    <span>}</span>\n\n    <span>neg_infty</span><span>(</span>max_vec<span>)</span><span>;</span> <span>// zero registers for the Q chunk</span>\n    <span>zero</span><span>(</span>norm_vec<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>zero</span><span>(</span>o_prev<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>__syncthreads</span><span>(</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n\n    tma<span>::</span><span>arrive_and_wait</span><span>(</span>qsmem_barrier<span>,</span> q_phasebit<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    q_phasebit <span>^=</span> <span>1</span><span>;</span>\n\n    <span>if</span> <span>constexpr</span> <span>(</span>D <span>==</span> <span>64</span><span>)</span> <span>{</span> warpgroup<span>::</span><span>mul</span><span>(</span>q_smem<span>[</span>warpgroupid<span>]</span><span>,</span> q_smem<span>[</span>warpgroupid<span>]</span><span>,</span> <span>__float2bfloat16</span><span>(</span><span>0.125f</span><span>)</span><span>)</span><span>;</span> <span>}</span> \n    <span>else</span> <span>{</span> warpgroup<span>::</span><span>mul</span><span>(</span>q_smem<span>[</span>warpgroupid<span>]</span><span>,</span> q_smem<span>[</span>warpgroupid<span>]</span><span>,</span> <span>__float2bfloat16</span><span>(</span><span>0.08838834764f</span><span>)</span><span>)</span><span>;</span> <span>}</span>\n\n    <span>for</span> <span>(</span><span>auto</span> kv_idx <span>=</span> <span>0</span><span>;</span> kv_idx <span>&#x3C;</span> kv_blocks<span>;</span> kv_idx<span>++</span><span>,</span> tic <span>^=</span> <span>1</span><span>,</span> toc <span>^=</span> <span>1</span><span>)</span> <span>{</span>\n        tma<span>::</span><span>arrive_and_wait</span><span>(</span>kvsmem_barrier<span>,</span> kv_phasebit<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n        kv_phasebit <span>^=</span> <span>1</span><span>;</span>\n\n        <span>__syncthreads</span><span>(</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n        <span>if</span> <span>(</span>warpid <span>==</span> <span>0</span><span>)</span> <span>{</span>\n            tma<span>::</span><span>set_bytes</span><span>(</span>kvsmem_barrier<span>,</span> <span>2</span> <span>*</span> NUM_WORKERS_KV <span>*</span> k_smem<span>[</span><span>0</span><span>]</span><span>[</span><span>0</span><span>]</span><span>.</span>num_elements <span>*</span> <span>sizeof</span><span>(</span>bf16<span>)</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n\n            <span>if</span> <span>(</span>kv_idx <span>+</span> <span>1</span> <span>&#x3C;</span> kv_blocks<span>)</span> <span>{</span>\n                <span>for</span> <span>(</span><span>int</span> w <span>=</span> <span>0</span><span>;</span> w <span>&#x3C;</span> NUM_WORKERS_KV<span>;</span> w<span>++</span><span>)</span> <span>{</span>        \n                    <span>int</span> tile_idx <span>=</span> <span>(</span>blockIdx<span>.</span>y <span>*</span> NUM_WORKERS_KV <span>*</span> kv_blocks<span>)</span> <span>+</span> <span>(</span><span>(</span>kv_idx <span>+</span> <span>1</span><span>)</span> <span>*</span> NUM_WORKERS_KV<span>)</span> <span>+</span> w<span>;</span> \n                    tma<span>::</span><span>load_async</span><span>(</span><span>(</span>k_smem<span>[</span>toc<span>]</span><span>[</span>w<span>]</span><span>)</span><span>,</span> tma_k<span>,</span> kvsmem_barrier<span>,</span> tile_idx<span>)</span><span>;</span> \n                    tma<span>::</span><span>load_async</span><span>(</span><span>(</span>v_smem<span>[</span>toc<span>]</span><span>[</span>w<span>]</span><span>)</span><span>,</span> tma_v<span>,</span> kvsmem_barrier<span>,</span> tile_idx<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n                <span>}</span>\n            <span>}</span>\n        <span>}</span>\n\n        warpgroup<span>::</span><span>mma_fence</span><span>(</span>att_block<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n        warpgroup<span>::</span><span>mm_ABt</span><span>(</span>att_block<span>,</span> q_smem<span>[</span>warpgroupid<span>]</span><span>,</span> k_smem<span>[</span>tic<span>]</span><span>[</span><span>0</span><span>]</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n        warpgroup<span>::</span><span>mma_commit_group</span><span>(</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n\n        <span>copy</span><span>(</span>norm_vec_last<span>,</span> norm_vec<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n        <span>copy</span><span>(</span>max_vec_last<span>,</span>  max_vec<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n\n        warpgroup<span>::</span><span>mma_async_wait</span><span>(</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n\n        <span>row_max</span><span>(</span>max_vec<span>,</span> att_block<span>,</span> max_vec<span>)</span><span>;</span> <span>// accumulate onto the max_vec</span>\n        <span>sub_row</span><span>(</span>att_block<span>,</span> att_block<span>,</span> max_vec<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n        <span>exp</span><span>(</span>att_block<span>,</span> att_block<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n\n        <span>sub</span><span>(</span>max_vec_last<span>,</span> max_vec_last<span>,</span> max_vec<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n        <span>exp</span><span>(</span>max_vec_last<span>,</span> max_vec_last<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n        <span>mul</span><span>(</span>norm_vec<span>,</span> norm_vec<span>,</span> max_vec_last<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n\n        <span>row_sum</span><span>(</span>norm_vec<span>,</span> att_block<span>,</span> norm_vec<span>)</span><span>;</span> <span>// accumulate onto the norm_vec</span>\n        <span>div_row</span><span>(</span>att_block<span>,</span> att_block<span>,</span> norm_vec<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n\n        <span>mul</span><span>(</span>norm_vec_last<span>,</span> norm_vec_last<span>,</span> max_vec_last<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n        <span>div</span><span>(</span>norm_vec_last<span>,</span> norm_vec_last<span>,</span> norm_vec<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n\n        <span>copy</span><span>(</span>att_block_mma<span>,</span> att_block<span>)</span><span>;</span> <span>// convert to bf16 for mma</span>\n        <span>mul_row</span><span>(</span>o_prev<span>,</span> o_prev<span>,</span> norm_vec_last<span>)</span><span>;</span> <span>// normalize o_prev in advance of mma'ing onto it</span>\n\n        warpgroup<span>::</span><span>mma_fence</span><span>(</span>o_prev<span>)</span><span>;</span>\n        warpgroup<span>::</span><span>mma_AB</span><span>(</span>o_prev<span>,</span> att_block_mma<span>,</span> v_smem<span>[</span>tic<span>]</span><span>[</span><span>0</span><span>]</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n        warpgroup<span>::</span><span>mma_commit_group</span><span>(</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    <span>}</span>\n\n    <span>auto</span> <span>(</span><span>*</span>o_smem<span>)</span> <span>=</span> <span><span>reinterpret_cast</span><span><span>&#x3C;</span><span><span>st_bf</span><span><span>&#x3C;</span>qo_height<span>,</span> tile_width<span>,</span> layout_o<span>></span></span></span><span>(</span><span>*</span><span>)</span><span>></span></span></span><span>(</span>q_smem<span>)</span><span>;</span> <span>// reuse q memory</span>\n    warpgroup<span>::</span><span>store</span><span>(</span>o_smem<span>[</span>warpgroupid<span>]</span><span>,</span> o_prev<span>)</span><span>;</span> \n    <span>__syncthreads</span><span>(</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n    \n    <span>if</span> <span>(</span>warpid <span>%</span> <span>4</span> <span>==</span> <span>0</span><span>)</span> <span>{</span> <span>// store o</span>\n        <span>int</span> tile_idx <span>=</span> <span>(</span>blockIdx<span>.</span>y <span>*</span> NUM_WARPGROUPS <span>*</span> gridDim<span>.</span>x<span>)</span> <span>+</span> <span>(</span>blockIdx<span>.</span>x <span>*</span> NUM_WARPGROUPS<span>)</span> <span>+</span> warpgroupid<span>;</span>\n        tma<span>::</span><span>store_async</span><span>(</span>tma_o<span>,</span> <span>(</span>o_smem<span>[</span>warpgroupid<span>]</span><span>)</span><span>,</span> tile_idx<span>)</span><span>;</span> \n        tma<span>::</span><span>store_commit_group</span><span>(</span><span>)</span><span>;</span> \n    <span>}</span>\n\n    tma<span>::</span><span>store_async_wait</span><span>(</span><span>)</span><span>;</span>\n<span>}</span>\n</code></pre></div>\n<p>So how does it do?</p>\n<p>This kernel is just 100 lines, and it actually outperforms FlashAttention-2 on the H100 by about 30%. ThunderKittens takes care of wrapping up the layouts and instructions, and gives you a mini-pytorch to play with on the GPU.</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:5px\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-05-12-tk/attn.png\"><p>Figure 5: FA2 (via Pytorch) versus TK for a wide range of configs on the H100 SXM.</p></figure>\n<p>We also release kernels for Based linear attention and other forthcoming architectures, too. Our Based linear attention kernel runs at 215 TFLOPs (or more than 300 TFLOPs when the recompute inherent in the algorithm is considered). And while linear attention is of course theoretically more efficient, historically, they have been dramatically less efficient on real hardware. So we feel this could open up a broad range of high-throughput applications -- more to come on this point later.</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:5px\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-05-12-tk/based.png\"><p>Figure 6: Linear attention can be quite quick with TK!</p></figure>\n<p>If this seems up your alley, feel free to play with it!</p>\n<h2>Tiles Seem Like a Good Idea</h2>\n<p>In our view, what has made ThunderKittens work well for us is that it does not try to do everything. CUDA is indeed far more expressive than ThunderKittens. ThunderKittens is small and dumb and simple.</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:5px\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-05-12-tk/theydontknow.png\"><p>Figure 7: the main message of this blog post.</p></figure>\n<p>But ThunderKittens has good abstractions -- small tiles -- that match where both AI and hardware are going. ThunderKittens doesn’t support any dimension less than 16. But in our view, this doesn’t really matter, since the hardware doesn’t particularly want to, either. And we ask: if your matrix multiply is smaller than 16x16, are you sure what you’re doing is AI?</p>\n<p>From a philosophical point of view, we think a frame shift is in order. A “register” certainly shouldn’t be a 32-bit word like on the CPUs of old. And a 1024-bit wide vector register, as CUDA uses, is certainly a step in the right direction. But to us a “register” is a 16x16 tile of data. We think AI wants this -- after all this time, it’s still just matrix multiplies, reductions, and reshapes. And we think the hardware wants this, too -- small matrix multiplies are just begging for hardware support beyond just the systolic mma.</p>\n<p>In fact, more broadly we believe we should really reorient our ideas of AI around what maps well onto the hardware. How big should a recurrent state be? As big can fit onto an SM. How dense should the compute be? No less so than what the hardware demands. An important future direction of this work for us is to use our learnings about the hardware to help us design the AI to match.</p>\n<h2>Tiles Seem Pretty General</h2>\n<p>Coming soon -- ThunderKittens on AMD hardware!</p>,AI uses an awful lot of compute. In the last few years we’ve focused a great deal of our work on making AI use less compute (e.g. Based, Mon…,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-05-12-tk,<a href=\"http://benjaminfspector.com/\">Benjamin Spector</a>, <a href=\"https://www.aaryan-singhal.com/\">Aaryan Singhal</a>, <a href=\"https://arorasimran.com/\">Simran Arora</a>, <a href=\"https://cs.stanford.edu/~chrismre/\">Chris Re</a>,2026-07-22T18:37:38.388Z,2024-05-12T07:00:00.005Z,[{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-05-12-tk/brr.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":878,\"height\":284},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-05-12-tk/spirits.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-05-12-tk/lies.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-05-12-tk/tk.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-05-12-tk/attn.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-05-12-tk/based.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-05-12-tk/theydontknow.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"}],163778663386687488,1211296081845936167,ThunderKittens: A Simple Embedded DSL for AI kernels,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-05-12-quick-tk,<p>(This post is an extended introduction to a <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-05-12-tk\" target=\"_blank\">longer post</a> we're releasing concurrently.)</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:5px\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-05-12-quick-tk/tk.png\"></figure>\n<p>We have a lot of fun building kernels for AI, and we’re proud of our open-source AI contributions. We’re less proud that people regard FlashAttention as alien technology. In a <a href=\"https://neurips.cc/virtual/2023/invited-talk/73990\" target=\"_blank\">NeurIPS keynote</a>, our group tried our best to make the key ideas simple – but the gap from the <a href=\"https://github.com/Dao-AILab/flash-attention/blob/main/assets/flashattn_banner.jpg\" target=\"_blank\">beautiful pictures</a> generated by Dan Fu to actual “gpu go brr” cuda is still <a href=\"https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/b/b1/Jimmy_McMillan_Blue_2_2011_Shankbone.jpg\" target=\"_blank\">too damn high</a>. So we set out to write a simple framework to make it really easy to express the key technical ideas. Relatively quickly, we had a small library (DSL?) that we called ThunderKittens that we hope lets us write simple-to-understand clean code that indeed makes gpus go brrr.<a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-05-12-quick-tk#footnote-1\" target=\"_blank\">[1]</a></p>\n<p>Our observations for TK are pretty simple:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>You want to keep tensor cores busy. These things are 94% of the compute on an H100, so if you don’t use them you’re capped at 6% utilization. In TK, we make the fundamental object a matrix (tile) of the same size that fits into the tensor core.</li>\n<li>We want something familiar to AI people. So we want an API that is PyTorch-like, because those folks are amazing and we love them!</li>\n<li>We wanted to get the full power of the host (cuda or hip), and we don’t want to hide how accelerators work. We think accelerators are awesome and more AI people should play around at this intersection. Pragmatically, AI processors are changing a lot – <a href=\"https://media.neurips.cc/Conferences/NIPS2018/Slides/Olukotun-NIPS-2018.pdf\" target=\"_blank\">they have to!</a> – but this means to take advantage of the latest and greatest you need to break out of software abstractions. So, TK is an embedded DSL somewhere between NVIDIA’s cute (in being embedded in raw cuda) and Triton (in being focused on AI kernels). If you know cuda, you can probably “compile” TK in your head–we try to make the structures transparent.</li>\n</ul>\n<p>You may have seen oblique references to TK in some of our earlier papers, e.g., <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-03-03-based\" target=\"_blank\">based</a>. We’ve been using it, so... why are we releasing it now? We don’t know honestly. But here are some things we think are interesting:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>On 4090s and A100s, TK matches FA2 performance in just a few lines of code.</li>\n<li>On H100s, TK is faster forward and backward than FA2 by quite a bit -- so there is no tradeoff of clean versus speed (in this case!)</li>\n</ul>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:5px\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-05-12-quick-tk/attn-mini.png\"></figure>\n<ul>\n<li>The lab folks wrote a <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/ThunderKittens/tree/main/examples\" target=\"_blank\">bunch</a> of other kernels in TK things like Based, hedgehog, and more! Some of them we couldn’t figure out how to get similar performance in Triton.</li>\n<li>AI folks who attended a two-hour cuda session have been able to write code, which is one step toward making this easier to use.</li>\n</ul>\n<p>We do want you to understand that this is an art project; if you like it, great! If you don’t, that’s ok too… feedback is great! But do not expect that we’ll do anything about your complaints in the code. For now, TK is for our fun and useful for us, and we hope it makes key ideas clear. To help in that way, we’re posting a version integrated with Andrej’s awesome <a href=\"https://github.com/karpathy/nanoGPT\" target=\"_blank\">NanoGPT</a> project, <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/nanoGPT-TK\" target=\"_blank\">NanoGPT-TK</a>. Andrej is one of the best communicators in AI and we’ve been using NanoGPT in <a href=\"https://cs229s.stanford.edu/fall2023/\" target=\"_blank\">teaching</a>, so we’re delighted to build on top.</p>\n<p>For a harder, better, faster, stronger, more detailed blog post, <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-05-12-tk\" target=\"_blank\">click!</a></p>\n<hr>\n<p id=\"user-content-footnote-1\">[1] Why ThunderKittens? We wanted something that was adorable and cute, and there are too many macho head games in the current names. Let’s be fun and welcoming!</p>,(This post is an extended introduction to a longer post we're releasing concurrently.) We have a lot of fun building kernels for AI, and we…,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-05-12-quick-tk,<a href=\"http://benjaminfspector.com/\">Benjamin Spector</a>, <a href=\"https://www.aaryan-singhal.com/\">Aaryan Singhal</a>, <a href=\"https://arorasimran.com/\">Simran Arora</a>, <a href=\"https://cs.stanford.edu/~chrismre/\">Chris Re</a>,2026-07-22T18:37:38.387Z,2024-05-12T07:00:00.927Z,[{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-05-12-quick-tk/tk.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1024,\"height\":1024},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-05-12-quick-tk/attn-mini.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"}],163778663386687488,1211296081845936168,Learning from DNA: a grand challenge in biology,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-03-14-evo,<p><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-03-14-evo#team\" target=\"_blank\">Full Evo team</a></p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:70%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-03-14-evo/robot-dna.jpeg\"></figure>\n<blockquote>\n<p>Evo is a long-context biological foundation model that generalizes across the fundamental modalities of biology: DNA, RNA, and proteins. It is capable of both prediction tasks and generative design, from molecular to whole genome scale (generating 650k+ tokens). Evo is trained at a single-nucleotide, byte-level resolution, on a large corpus of prokaryotic and phage genomic sequences.</p>\n</blockquote>\n<h2>Resources</h2>\n<p><a href=\"https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2024.02.27.582234v2\" target=\"_blank\">paper</a><br>\n<a href=\"https://github.com/evo-design/evo\" target=\"_blank\">repo</a><br>\n<a href=\"https://colab.research.google.com/github/evo-design/evo/blob/main/scripts/hello_evo.ipynb\" target=\"_blank\">colab</a><br>\n<a href=\"https://api.together.xyz/playground/language/togethercomputer/evo-1-131k-base\" target=\"_blank\">together playground</a> (browser tool)<br>\n<a href=\"https://evo.nitro.bio/\" target=\"_blank\">API wrapper</a> (by Nitro Bio)<br>\n<a href=\"https://huggingface.co/togethercomputer/evo-1-131k-base\" target=\"_blank\">huggingface (ckpts)</a><br>\n<a href=\"https://pypi.org/project/evo-model/0.1.0/\" target=\"_blank\">pip install</a></p>\n<h2>What can we learn from DNA?</h2>\n<p>We’ve long held the belief that biology is one of the primary domains that could truly benefit from long context models. Our first proof-of-concept, <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2023-06-29-hyena-dna\" target=\"_blank\">HyenaDNA</a>, showed it could process sequences up to 1M tokens at the byte-level for predictive tasks. We were thrilled to see such positive reception and interest from entities in and out of academia [<a href=\"https://www.forbes.com/sites/robtoews/2023/09/03/transformers-revolutionized-ai-what-will-replace-them/?sh=54465669c1fe\" target=\"_blank\">1</a>,<a href=\"https://time.com/6564430/ai-minister-uae/\" target=\"_blank\">2</a>,<a href=\"https://www.marktechpost.com/2023/07/04/stanford-researchers-introduce-hyenadna-a-long-range-genomic-foundation-model-with-context-lengths-of-up-to-1-million-tokens-at-single-nucleotide-resolution/\" target=\"_blank\">3</a>,<a href=\"https://www.cjco.com.au/article/news/ai-propels-genomics-forward-unveiling-hyenadnas-scalability-in-long-sequence-processing/\" target=\"_blank\">4</a>,<a href=\"https://cbirt.net/hyenadna-powers-long-range-genomic-sequence-modeling-at-single-nucleotide-resolution/\" target=\"_blank\">5</a>,<a href=\"https://www.jiqizhixin.com/articles/2023-7-7-28\" target=\"_blank\">6</a>]! As fun as this was, we knew we wanted to continue pushing capabilities of foundation models in biology.</p>\n<p>So we set our sights on a much harder challenge: <em><strong>generative</strong></em> DNA design. The problem was, a good number of biologists asked, “what would you even do if you can design DNA?” We spent 5 months searching for a partner that shared the same vision, and that’s when we found the Arc Institute, where we joined forces to create a model called <strong>Evo</strong>. Our goal - to be able to generate an entire (prokaryotic) genome from scratch using AI.</p>\n<p>Why? Understanding by creating was one motivation. But a real motivation that drove us here at Hazy Research, perhaps naively, was to create biomolecular machines using AI that can target cancer cells or any other disease we programmed it to. To create microbes for better biofuels, absorb carbon dioxide, or discover a new class of antibiotics. These were some of our dreams - and we didn’t succeed on any one of them.</p>\n<p>And yet, we’ve never been <em>more</em> excited about the potential for generative DNA and biology.</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:80%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-03-14-evo/ball-of-life-zoom-15.gif\"><p>Figure 1. Predicted protein structures from a single, Evo-generated sequence.</p></figure>\n<p>As we were training Evo, compiling the largest DNA pretraining dataset (we know of), it felt like we were observing a “GPT” moment in biology. A simple unsupervised task was getting competitive <strong>zero-shot</strong> performance by modeling across the <a href=\"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Central_dogma_of_molecular_biology\" target=\"_blank\">central dogma</a> of biology, and generalizing across DNA, RNA and protein modalities.</p>\n<p>One of the really exciting moments came when we were able to generate the world’s first AI designed CRISPR system (a complex of RNA and proteins), without requiring supervision. There were many other “firsts” that we observed throughout this project - but similar to natural language, there will be many more capabilities to emerge and yet to be discovered from the relatively untapped reservoir of biological data.</p>\n<p>And so these are some of the reasons why we believe learning from DNA sequences is the next “grand challenge” of biology. If this blog doesn’t convince some folks in machine learning to work in biology, we will not have done our job!</p>\n<p>It’s still very early days (think “blurry” GANs images), but the viability of scaling large biological foundation models is indeed promising. Let’s dive into the ML team \"director's cut\" on Evo.</p>\n<h2>Contents</h2>\n<p>Feel free to jump directly:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>Evo <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-03-14-evo#highlights\" target=\"_blank\">highlights</a></li>\n<li><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-03-14-evo#language\" target=\"_blank\">challenges</a> in modeling DNA</li>\n<li>DNA pretraining <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-03-14-evo#scaling\" target=\"_blank\">scaling laws</a></li>\n<li><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-03-14-evo#mad\" target=\"_blank\">Mechanistic Architecture Design</a></li>\n<li>CRISPR &#x26; gene essentiality <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-03-14-evo#results\" target=\"_blank\">results</a></li>\n<li><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-03-14-evo#future\" target=\"_blank\">future</a> directions</li>\n</ul>\n<a name=\"user-content-highlights\" target=\"_blank\"></a>\n<h2>Evo highlights</h2>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:5px\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-03-14-evo/arch.png\"><p>Figure 2. The Evo model architecture, based on StripedHyena.</p></figure>\n<p>With Hazy, Arc and Together AI, we trained Evo, a 7B parameter long context foundation model built to generate DNA sequences at single nucleotide resolution, and over 650k+ tokens long.</p>\n<ul>\n<li>\n<p>Evo is trained on 2.7M prokaryotic and phage genomes (un-annotated), using next token prediction. We compiled a dataset called OpenGenome, which we’re releasing open source, containing 300B tokens.</p>\n</li>\n<li>\n<p>Evo is based on the <a href=\"https://www.together.ai/blog/stripedhyena-7b\" target=\"_blank\">StripedHyena</a> architecture, an enhanced Hyena model that’s hybridized with rotary attention and trained in 2 stages using an efficient context extension, reaching a context of 131k tokens.</p>\n</li>\n<li>\n<p>Beyond the technical innovations of the model (more in <a href=\"https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2024.02.27.582234v2\" target=\"_blank\">paper</a>), the strength of the multimodal learning and generalization abilities of the model is what truly surprised us. Evo is able to learn across DNA, RNA and proteins, reaching competitive zero-shot performance on function prediction with state-of-the-art (SOTA) protein specific FMs like <a href=\"https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2022.07.20.500902v1.full.pdf\" target=\"_blank\">ESM2</a> and <a href=\"https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-022-01618-2\" target=\"_blank\">ProGen</a>, notably, without explicitly being shown protein coding regions.</p>\n</li>\n<li>\n<p>Evo understands at the whole genome level - using a gene essentiality test, Evo can predict which genes are essential to an organism’s survival based on small DNA mutations, also zero-shot (which currently is only possible in the wet lab).</p>\n</li>\n<li>\n<p>Evo excels at generation, where we showcase the generation of molecules, to systems, and to whole genomes scale. One of the key breakthroughs we highlight, Evo can design novel CRISPR systems (including genes and RNA), an exciting frontier for creating new forms of genome editing tools.</p>\n</li>\n</ul>\n<p>We describe the biological innovations more in our initial blog <a href=\"https://arcinstitute.org/news/blog/evo\" target=\"_blank\">release</a> and in greater detail in the <a href=\"https://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2024.02.27.582234v2\" target=\"_blank\">paper</a>, so we’ll focus on what makes modeling DNA challenging and an exciting frontier for ML.</p>\n<a name=\"user-content-language\" target=\"_blank\"></a>\n<h2>DNA as a language</h2>\n<p>Here, we largely treat DNA as a language, and so why haven’t we been able to effectively learn from DNA before? Aren’t Transformers great at language? This is something that initially blocked us as well. As we started scaling *thousands* of DNA models over the past year, several key lessons have emerged (obvious to many computational biologists).</p>\n<p><strong>DNA is not like natural language.</strong></p>\n<p>DNA is more like several languages or modalities in one. Typically, it’s believed that these biological languages (DNA, RNA, proteins) are so complex that they each require specialized models to effectively model them. But genomes carry an entire set of DNA to make a complete organism. And so it begs the question, “is DNA all you need”?</p>\n<a name=\"user-content-ppl\" target=\"_blank\"></a>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-03-14-evo/dogma.png\"><p>Figure 3.  The fundamental “languages” of biology DNA, RNA, and proteins, united in the central dogma. (Photo credit: Khan Academy)</p></figure>\n<p>A key technical set of challenges in learning from DNA, among many, has been the long-range modeling (e.g. human genome is 3B nucleotides) as well as the resolution required to capture the effects of single mutations throughout evolution, which occur one nucleotide at a time. This can lead to varying “signal” strengths over the different modalities in DNA (discussed more in our first DNA <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2023-06-29-hyena-dna\" target=\"_blank\">blog</a>). Though very long context models are emerging [<a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2403.05530\" target=\"_blank\">Gemini</a>, <a href=\"https://www.anthropic.com/news/claude-3-family\" target=\"_blank\">Claude</a>], no large-scale Transformer has been effectively used at single character / byte level. This turns out to be an “achilles heel” in Transformers for DNA language modeling.</p>\n<a name=\"user-content-scaling\" target=\"_blank\"></a>\n<h2>DNA scaling laws</h2>\n<p>To compare across potential architectures, we conducted the first DNA (and byte-level tokenization!) scaling laws experiments via the <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2203.15556\" target=\"_blank\">Chinchilla</a> protocol. This involved training 300 models across prevailing language and DNA models: Transformer++, Mamba, Hyena, and StripedHyena, where we sweep across model size (6M to 1B) and dataset size, compute budgets and hyperparameters.</p>\n<p>Scaling laws in natural language have helped propel LLM research forward by providing a guidebook on how to scale models and data with predictable performance. No such work has been done in DNA language modeling, and we hope to accelerate research in the field by releasing intermediate <a href=\"https://huggingface.co/togethercomputer/evo-1-131k-base/tree/main\" target=\"_blank\">checkpoints</a> during our training, hosted on HuggingFace. There’s a great deal of lessons learned that we hope to share with the community over time (perhaps a standalone blog?). There's so much we don't know about the best way to pretrain on DNA or with byte-level tokenization.</p>\n<a name=\"user-content-ppl\" target=\"_blank\"></a>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:70%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-03-14-evo/optimal2.png\"><p>Figure 5. Compute optimal scaling on DNA pretraining. Each point represents the lowest perplexity for a model after sweeping different compute allocations (model size and training tokens).</p></figure>\n<p>In DNA scaling, we found Transformer++ to yield significantly worse perplexity (a measure of next token prediction quality) at all compute budgets, a symptom of the inefficiency of the architecture at the byte resolution [<a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2106.12672\" target=\"_blank\">Charformer</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2103.06874\" target=\"_blank\">CANINE</a>]. State-space and deep signal processing architectures are observed to improve on the scaling rate over Transformer++, with Hyena and StripedHyena resulting in the best scaling rate. This suggests that at scale, the prevailing architecture in language may not easily transfer to DNA.</p>\n<a name=\"user-content-mad\" target=\"_blank\"></a>\n<h2>Mechanistic Architecture Design</h2>\n<p>In upcoming work on how we designed <a href=\"https://www.together.ai/blog/stripedhyena-7b\" target=\"_blank\">StripedHyena</a> and other hybrid models, we dive deeper into understanding their advantages on DNA data by using a new framework we call Mechanistic Architecture Design (MAD). MAD builds on previous work in the lab [<a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2212.14052\" target=\"_blank\">H3</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2302.10866\" target=\"_blank\">Hyena</a>, <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2023-12-11-zoology1-analysis\" target=\"_blank\">MQAR</a>] in using targeted synthetic tasks that allowed us to test new architecture design choices. With MAD, we are able to show for the first time a connection between syntethic performance across various tasks and scaling laws.</p>\n<p>These token manipulation tasks expand beyond previous recall-focused tasks, and include testing the ability to compress/aggregate multiple token representations (important in byte-level tokens) and to filter noise, where genomic sequences are known to contain a lot of “junk” DNA. Through synthetic tasks, we’re able to better understand where models do well or worse, and in turn, use this to drive the design of stronger models. Stay tuned.</p>\n<a name=\"user-content-results\" target=\"_blank\"></a>\n<h2>Zero-shot prediction and multimodal generation</h2>\n<a name=\"user-content-ppl\" target=\"_blank\"></a>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-03-14-evo/cas9_gen3.gif\"><p>Figure 6. Generative design of CRISPR-Cas molecular complexes with Evo.</p></figure>\n<p>As a preview, we highlight one of the generative design capabilities of Evo. Today, generative models for biology usually focus on a single modality (at a time)—for example, only on proteins or on RNA. Instead, Evo can perform multimodal design to generate novel CRISPR systems, a task that requires creating large functional complexes of proteins and ncRNA (non-coding RNA), and is out of reach for existing generative models.</p>\n<p>Typically, discovering new CRISPR systems requires searching through natural genomes for similar sequences that were literally taken from an organism. Promisingly, Evo enables a new approach to generating biological diversity by sampling sequences directly from a generative model, an exciting frontier for creating new forms of genome editing tools.</p>\n<p>Strikingly, Evo can also understand biological function at the whole genome level. Using an in silico gene essentiality test on 56 whole-genomes (at 66 kbp), Evo can predict which genes are essential to an organism’s survival based on small DNA mutations. It can do so <em><strong>zero-shot</strong></em> and with <em><strong>no supervision</strong></em>. For comparison, a gene essentiality experiment in the laboratory could require 6 months to a year of experimental effort. In contrast, we replace this with a few forward passes through a neural network.</p>\n<p>There are a range of capabilities (additional zero-shot predictions to generation of whole genome scale sequences) that we discuss in the <a href=\"https://arcinstitute.org/news/blog/evo\" target=\"_blank\">initial blog</a> and paper, and we encourage folks to check it out. But if that’s still too much time commitment for your comfort, we’re big fans of this <a href=\"https://x.com/JuliaBauman2/status/1765509688362492301?s=20\" target=\"_blank\">summary video</a> by Julia Bauman.</p>\n<a name=\"user-content-future\" target=\"_blank\"></a>\n<h2>Future directions</h2>\n<a name=\"user-content-ppl\" target=\"_blank\"></a>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:70%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-03-14-evo/hyena_scientist.png\"></figure>\n<p>We’re not working in a vacuum, there’s been a tremendous amount of fundamental research in biology (including computational, synthetic, bioengineering). Our goal is to accelerate the research where we can with the tools we know best, and hopefully motivating a few ML researchers along the way.</p>\n<p>In the spirit of foundation models in other domains, we believe that Evo and other models that emerge can serve as a foundation for building useful things on top.</p>\n<p>The potential to draw from the language modeling community is promising, as we incorporate prompt engineering and alignment techniques to design sequences with higher controllability and quality. Multimodal learning, and injecting domain specific knowledge are all things we’re bull-ish on.</p>\n<p>While data and models are certainly important, evaluations play an outsized importance in biology, and perhaps is the biggest barrier to entry for the ML community. How do you formulate a task, what pretraining and annotations are needed, and of course, what does it mean in terms of the mechanistic understanding in biology? These are just some of the many questions we continue to think about as we try to innovate, as well as respect the body of work before us.</p>\n<p>Even more challenging, how do we learn from eukaryotes (humans, mammals), which have <em><strong>far</strong></em> more complex genomes than prokaryotes. Is simply scaling one solution?</p>\n<p>We’re in the early innings of what we think is possible in designing DNA sequences. Dropping perplexity on the Pile is exciting, and can lead to better downstream benchmarks or chatbots. Better perplexity on DNA and other bio sequences can lead to creating new tools for countless scientific advances and the improvement of human health.</p>\n<p>We believe there's a big appetite in the ML community to work on meaningful problems (in bio or other) with cutting edge technology. If you've made it this far in the blog, you likely have that same hunger. Go after that grand challenge in biology, chemistry, climate tech, or whatever domain excites you. Tinker, collborate, and <strong>build the thing</strong>.</p>\n<a name=\"user-content-team\" target=\"_blank\"></a>\n<h2>Correspondence</h2>\n<p>Eric Nguyen, <a href=\"mailto:etnguyen@stanford.edu\" target=\"_blank\">etnguyen@stanford.edu</a><br>\nMichael Poli, <a href=\"mailto:polimic03@gmail.com\" target=\"_blank\">polimic03@gmail.com</a></p>\n<p>The full team also includes: Matt Durrant, Armin Thomas, Brian Kang, Jeremy Sullivan, Madelena Ng, Ashley Lewis, Aman Patel, Aarou Lou, Stefano Ermon, Stephen Baccus, Tina Hernandez-Boussard, Chris Ré, Patrick Hsu and Brian Hie.</p>\n<h2>Acknowledgements</h2>\n<p>We thank our labmates for their helpful feedback in writing this post: Jerry Liu, Michael Zhang, Jon Saad-Falcon, and Ben Viggiano.</p>,Full Evo team Evo is a long-context biological foundation model that generalizes across the fundamental modalities of biology: DNA, RNA…,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-03-14-evo,<a href=\"http://erictnguyen.com/\">Eric Nguyen</a>, <a href=\"https://zymrael.github.io/\">Michael Poli</a>,2026-07-22T18:37:38.386Z,2024-03-14T07:00:00.003Z,[{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-03-14-evo/robot-dna.jpeg\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":974,\"height\":971},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-03-14-evo/ball-of-life-zoom-15.gif\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-03-14-evo/arch.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-03-14-evo/dogma.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-03-14-evo/optimal2.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-03-14-evo/cas9_gen3.gif\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-03-14-evo/hyena_scientist.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"}],163778663386687488,1211296081845936169,Based: Simple linear attention language models balance the recall-throughput tradeoff,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-03-03-based,<p><strong>Full team</strong>: Simran*, Sabri*, Michael*, Aman, Silas, Dylan, James, Atri, Chris<br>\n<strong>Arxiv</strong>: <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.18668\" target=\"_blank\">https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.18668</a><br>\n<strong>Code</strong>: <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/based\" target=\"_blank\">https://github.com/HazyResearch/based</a></p>\n<p>In an <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2023-12-11-zoology1-analysis\" target=\"_blank\">ICLR paper</a> (and <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2023-12-11-zoology1-analysis\" target=\"_blank\">blogpost</a>) we posted towards the end of last year, we share the finding that many efficient architectures (e.g. <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2312.00752\" target=\"_blank\">Mamba</a>, <a href=\"https://github.com/BlinkDL/RWKV-LM\" target=\"_blank\">RWKV</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2302.10866\" target=\"_blank\">Hyena</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.08621\" target=\"_blank\">RetNet</a>) underperform Transformers on recall, the ability to ground generations on information seen in-context, which is critical for in-context learning and copying. We used this analysis to design a new architecture called Based (previewed in this <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2023-12-11-zoology2-based\" target=\"_blank\">blogpost</a>). We’re excited to share the latest progress in this line of work. </p>\n<p>Our recent work digs deeper into the recall challenge. We begin by illustrating a fundamental tradeoff between a model’s recall abilities and its memory consumption during generation. This analysis informs the design of Based, a simple recurrent architecture that outperforms prior sub-quadratic models on real-world recall-intensive tasks (information extraction, reading comprehension) and in-context learning. At the same time, Based offers fast generation speeds: Based is 56% and 44% faster at processing prompts than FlashAttention-2 and Mamba respectively. <strong>Based achieves 24x higher text generation throughput than FlashAttention-2.</strong></p>\n<p>We’re particularly excited about the <em>simplicity</em> of Based. Using just two well-known, attention-like building blocks, sliding window attention (with <em>tiny</em> window sizes) and linear attention (with Taylor series approximation of <span><span><span><math>exp⁡(QKT)\\exp(QK^T)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.0913em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>exp</span><span>(</span><span>Q</span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">K</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8413em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">T</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span>), we can outperform the strongest sub-quadratic architectures on language modeling and achieve massive speedups over optimized Transformers! </p>\n<p>This blogpost provides an overview of our 1) analysis on recall in sub-quadratic architectures that leads to the Based design and 2) how we make Based go brrrr! </p>\n<p><strong>Motivating analysis: the recall-memory tradeoff.</strong> The main question driving our exploration is: <em>can we drastically improve the real-world speed and memory consumption of language models without compromising on recall and in-context learning capability?</em> </p>\n<p>To begin answering this question, we had to first think about what slows architectures down. Efficient architectures (<em>e.g.</em> Mamba) are much faster than Transformers at inference time (<em>e.g.</em> 5x higher throughput) in large part because they have a reduced memory footprint. Smaller memory footprint means larger batch sizes and less I/O. However, it also makes intuitive sense that reducing memory footprint too much could hurt a model’s capacity to recall information seen earlier in the sequence. This looked to us like a classic “<em>no free lunch”</em> situation, so we took a number of popular architectures, varied the hyperparameters that affected the memory footprint, and evaluated performance on a challenging synthetic associative recall task.</p>\n<p><em>The recall-memory tradeoff.</em>  We found that all architectures obeyed a fundamental tradeoff: the less memory the model consumed during inference, the worse it did on associative recall. We focused on the <em>recurrent state size,</em> the number of bytes used to represent previously seen tokens when generating tokens one-by-one (<em>i.e.</em> recurrently). </p>\n<p>In attention, the <em>state</em> is commonly referred to as the KV-cache, and it grows with the length of the sequence. In the top right of Figure 1, we can see that attention performs recall perfectly, albeit at the cost of a huge recurrent state. Sliding window attention provides a way to cap the size of the KV-cache, but we found (unsurprisingly) that recall performance drops off rapidly as we reduce the size of the recurrent state (<em>e.g.</em> from 100% with 1MB recurrent state to 50% with a 65 KB recurrent state) (Figure 1, light blue). </p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-03-03-based/tradeoff.png\"></figure>\n<p>Excitingly, we found that Mamba expands the pareto frontier of the recall-memory tradeoff curve beyond sliding window attention. This means it is making <strong>better use of limited recurrent state size</strong> than approaches like sliding window attention. </p>\n<p>The natural question is: <em>are there other, perhaps simpler, models that can also expand the pareto frontier?</em></p>\n<p><strong>Based: a simple model at the pareto frontier.</strong> To answer this question, we started studying why the simplest alternatives to softmax attention fail to strike a favorable tradeoff. As a further design principle, we searched for primitives that could scale well on current and future hardware. For instance, it would be nice if our primitives could leverage GPU Tensor Cores, specialized hardware on modern GPUs that can perform matrix multiplications (GEMMs) 16x faster for 16x16 matrices than the default (CUDA cores)!</p>\n<p>In our <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2023-12-11-zoology1-analysis\" target=\"_blank\">ICLR paper</a>, we did a deep dive on why any model with a convolutional view (<em>e.g.</em> H3 or Hyena) will struggle on recall. Next, we considered two of the simplest efficient attention techniques out there: (1) <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2004.05150\" target=\"_blank\">sliding</a> <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2007.14062\" target=\"_blank\">window</a> <a href=\"https://mistral.ai/news/announcing-mistral-7b/\" target=\"_blank\">attention</a> and (2) <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2006.16236\" target=\"_blank\">linear attention</a> (<em>i.e.</em> attention without softmax).</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-03-03-based/based-design.png\"></figure>\n<p>Our experiments on real-world language modeling (up to 1.4bn parameters) and synthetic associative recall suggested to us that neither primitive alone would suffice to navigate the pareto frontier.</p>\n<ol>\n<li>\n<p>We found that pure linear attention models struggled to perform precise local token shifts and token comparisons, skills important in recall (Fu et al., 2023; Arora et al., 2023a), as well as dense attention. Expanding on our findings, we do find that our pure linear attention model improves over earlier sub-quadratic architectures. Focusing on the recall-intensive slice of the Pile test set (i.e. next token predictions that force the model to use the prior context vs. memorized knowledge), the 355M pure linear attention model outperforms RWKV-v5 by 0.1 ppl and H3 by 2.6 ppl (Table 1, paper). Pure linear attention is even comparable to the Mamba architecture on this recall-slice – 2.21 ppl for Mamba vs. 2.29 for pure linear attention! However, we observe a sizeable gap to Transformers, which achieve 1.87 ppl on the recall slice. </p>\n</li>\n<li>\n<p>In sliding window attention, models can only recall tokens within the sliding window (Figure 2, center). As we increase the window size, the recurrent state grows linearly and has a non-linear effect on speed during parallel training and inference (Figure 2, left).</p>\n</li>\n</ol>\n<p>However, we find the two primitives are complementary – linear attention for modeling long-range token interactions and sliding window for local token interactions in the sequence. We combined them into a single architecture, called Based (Figure 2, right). </p>\n<ol>\n<li>\n<p>Sliding window attention can perform the precise <em>local</em> shifts needed for associative recall. We use <em>tiny</em> window sizes (e.g. 64 in experiments) contrasting the larger window sizes in architectures like <a href=\"https://mistral.ai/news/announcing-mistral-7b/\" target=\"_blank\">Mistral-7B</a> and the recently proposed <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.19427\" target=\"_blank\">Griffin</a>. Intuitively more attention (larger window sizes) is nice from a quality perspective, but we’d like to balance quality and wall-clock speed. To balance these objectives, let’s take a look at the left plot in the above figure. Observe that the latency of matrix multiplication for 16x16 vs. 64x64 matrices are roughly equal, and beyond 64, latency grows non-linearly with the window size.  Note that the rough similarity between 16x16 and 64x64 is because the latter keeps the GPU tensor core occupancy high enough to saturate!</p>\n</li>\n<li>\n<p>Linear attention enables <em>global</em> token interactions, while maintaining a fixed size recurrent state. Unlike softmax attention, the size of linear attention’s recurrent state is a function of hyperparameters (<em>e.g.</em> choice of feature map) and not sequence length. This allows us to traverse the tradeoff space smoothly. We use a <strong>Taylor approximation of the exponential function as the feature map</strong>, that was first used in <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.04347\" target=\"_blank\">our prior work</a> on linear attentions!</p>\n</li>\n</ol>\n<p>Critically, the recurrent state size in Based does not grow with the sequence length, as it does in attention. Instead, it is determined by the linear attention feature dimension and the window size. <strong>By dialing these hyperparameters, we can tradeoff recall for throughput and navigate the pareto frontier in Figure 1.</strong>  </p>\n<p>Despite its simplicity, on real language modeling experiments (up to at least 1.3 billion parameters), Based is competitive with Mamba in terms of overall Pile perplexity and standard zero-shot benchmarks from the <a href=\"https://github.com/EleutherAI/lm-evaluation-harness\" target=\"_blank\">LM eval harness</a> (shown under Question Answering - Common). </p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-03-03-based/table.png\"></figure>\n<p>These commonly-used zero-shot benchmarks are limited to extremely short text, so they don’t stress test models’ recall capabilities. To address this shortcoming, we <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2304.09433\" target=\"_blank\">curated a small suite of <em>real world recall-intensive</em> benchmarks</a> that require recalling information from long documents (<em>e.g.</em> information extraction from <a href=\"https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21321283/https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21321283/\" target=\"_blank\">FDA documents</a> and <a href=\"https://paperswithcode.com/dataset/swde\" target=\"_blank\">raw HTML</a>, and reading comprehension). __Based is the strongest sub-quadratic architecture on these tasks, outperforming Mamba by 6.22 accuracy points on average. However, both Based and Mamba still underperform the strongest Transformer baseline, sometimes by large margins. This is consistent with our “no free lunch” observation above. </p>\n<p>It’s important to note that we don’t believe Based is the only architecture that can operate at this point on the tradeoff curve. For example, we show in our paper that we can replace the sliding window attention with short-convolutions (filter size 3) and achieve similar performance within 0.1 perplexity points. We suspect that there are lots of other architectures that can also match this pareto frontier and we’re hopeful there are even others that can even expand beyond it! </p>\n<p><strong>How we use our fixed-size recurrent state matters too!</strong> </p>\n<p>There are many recurrent architectures that might have the same hidden state size, but our work highlights how the featurization (e.g. linear attention feature map, state update mechanism) matters as well. Our choice for the map in Based is surprisingly simple (<em>high-school calculus is all you need)</em>: it approximates the exponential with a Taylor series. We compute <span><span><span><math>ϕ\\phi</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8889em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span>ϕ</span></span></span></span></span> such that <span><span><span><math>ϕ(q)ϕ(k)T≈exp⁡(qkT)\\phi(q) \\phi(k)^T \\approx \\exp (q k^T)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.0913em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>ϕ</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">q</span><span>)</span><span>ϕ</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span><span><span>)</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8413em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">T</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>≈</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1.0913em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>exp</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">q</span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8413em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">T</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span>. We use just the second-order Taylor series as in our prior work, where <span><span><span><math>exp⁡^(x)=1+x+x2/2\\hat{\\exp}(x) = 1 + x + x^2 / 2</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"><span style=\"top:-3em\"><span style=\"height:3em\"></span><span>exp</span></span><span style=\"top:-3em\"><span style=\"height:3em\"></span><span style=\"left:-0.25em\"><span>^</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.1944em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span><span>(</span><span>x</span><span>)</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>1</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>+</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6667em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>x</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>+</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1.0641em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>2</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>/2</span></span></span></span></span>! Note that if <span><span><span><math>xx</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span>x</span></span></span></span></span> has dimension <span><span><span><math>d’d’</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span>d</span><span>’</span></span></span></span></span> then the  <span><span><span><math>x2x^2</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"></span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>2</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> term will have dimension <span><span><span><math>d’2d’^2</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"></span><span>d</span><span><span>’</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>2</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>! The result of the key-value outer product (step 1 above) grows quickly in <span><span><span><math>d’d’</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span>d</span><span>’</span></span></span></span></span>, expanding the state size for Based. </p>\n<p><em>How much does our choice of featurization vs. the expanded state size matter when leading to the quality of Based?</em> The model’s ability to <em>use the state effectively</em> is key. Shown in the accuracy vs. recurrent state size tradeoff curves, several alternatives to the Taylor map fall <em>below</em> the pareto frontier. Below we compare to models that expand the state size using learned projections and then apply popular feature maps (<a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2009.14794\" target=\"_blank\">Performer</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2202.08791\" target=\"_blank\">CosFormer</a>, <a href=\"https://proceedings.mlr.press/v119/katharopoulos20a.html\" target=\"_blank\">PosELU</a>) from the literature. We train these models on the <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/zoology\" target=\"_blank\">MQAR synthetic</a> test for associative recall and sweep hyperparameters (learning rate) for all points shown in the plot below, finding that the Taylor map is most effective. This trend carries to real world experiments on the Pile language modeling corpus (see our paper for more).</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-03-03-based/feature_map.png\"></figure>\n<h2>IO and dataflow-aware implementation. <a id=\"user-content-io-and-dataflow-aware-implementation\" target=\"_blank\"></a></h2>\n<p>The next key question is how to make Based competitive in wall clock efficiency. Linear attention is theoretically more efficient than standard attention as a function of sequence length. However, existing implementations of linear attention methods are often <em>slower</em> than well-optimized attention implementations like <a href=\"https://github.com/Dao-AILab/flash-attention\" target=\"_blank\">FlashAttention</a>. </p>\n<p>In Based, we use the 2nd degree Taylor approximation, which expands the dimension of the keys, leading to large state sizes and large memory consumption <span><span><span><math>O(Nd’2d)O(Nd’^2d)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.0641em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>d</span><span><span>’</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>2</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>d</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span>, in sequence length <span><span><span><math>NN</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span></span></span></span></span>, key dimension <span><span><span><math>d’d’</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span>d</span><span>’</span></span></span></span></span>, and value dimension <span><span><span><math>dd</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span>d</span></span></span></span></span> (discussed above). The large resulting key-value state makes naïve implementations of Taylor linear attention quite slow. </p>\n<p>First let’s revisit a bit of context on how the hardware works. GPUs have small amounts of fast-to-access memory (thread-specific registers, shared memory at the warp/32-threads level using SRAM) and large amounts of slow-to-access memory (HBM). It is important to reduce the number of reads-and-writes between slow HBM and SRAM as well as SRAM and registers to unlock efficiency. We present new IO-aware algorithms for the Taylor linear attention forward pass and inference that reduce the HBM to SRAM data movement by <span><span><span><math>O(Nd’2)O(Nd’^2)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.0641em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>d</span><span><span>’</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>2</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> bytes and the SRAM to register data movement by <span><span><span><math>O(Nd’2d)O(Nd’^2d)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.0641em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>d</span><span><span>’</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>2</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>d</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> bytes. Our algorithm allows holding the KV state <em>in-thread-register</em> at feature dimension <span><span><span><math>d’d’</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span>d</span><span>’</span></span></span></span></span> = 16, which we use in experiments. </p>\n<p>Below we include a comparison between the naive Taylor attention forward pass, an implementation that leverages the popular linear attention kernels from <a href=\"https://github.com/idiap/fast-transformers/blob/master/fast_transformers/attention/causal_linear_attention.py\" target=\"_blank\">Fast Transformers</a>, and our custom kernels are shown below across batch size (sequence length 1024). </p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:50%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-03-03-based/micro.png\"></figure>\n<p>We then compare the end-to-end generation speeds of FlashAttention-2, Mamba, and Based 360M and 1.3Bn parameter models using our IO-aware algorithms. We hold the batch size to 2 for prefill, and generate 1024 tokens for next token prediction. Strikingly, Based achieves up to 24x higher throughput than FlashAttention-2! </p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-03-03-based/efficiency.png\"></figure>\n<p><em>Stay tuned!</em> These algorithms are implemented in an exciting new CUDA DSL called ThunderKittens, that’s being developed by our lab. Stay tuned for more on this soon – we hope the DSL improves the accessibility of CUDA development! In contrast to frameworks like Triton, which makes opinionated decisions about the supported scope of operations the user can perform, our DSL is <em>embedded</em> in C++. We’re really excited to share it and get your feedback! We’re cooking up more model artifacts alongside in the coming weeks, motivated by the question: <em>What models does the hardware want?</em> </p>\n<p>You can play with our checkpoints and evaluations on <a href=\"https://huggingface.co/collections/hazyresearch/based-65d77fb7a6f9c813c8b94339c\" target=\"_blank\">Hugging Face</a> and in this code repository:  <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/based\" target=\"_blank\">https://github.com/HazyResearch/based</a>! Huge thank you to<a href=\"https://www.together.ai/\" target=\"_blank\"> Together AI</a>, <a href=\"https://hai.stanford.edu/\" target=\"_blank\">Stanford HAI</a>, and <a href=\"https://crfm.stanford.edu/\" target=\"_blank\">Stanford CRFM</a> for supporting this work! Please send your feedback and questions to: Simran (<a href=\"mailto:simarora@stanford.edu\" target=\"_blank\">simarora@stanford.edu</a>), Sabri (<a href=\"mailto:eyuboglu@stanford.edu\" target=\"_blank\">eyuboglu@stanford.edu</a>), and Michael (<a href=\"mailto:mzhang@cs.stanford.edu\" target=\"_blank\">mzhang@cs.stanford.edu</a>).</p>,Full team: Simran*, Sabri*, Michael*, Aman, Silas, Dylan, James, Atri, Chris Arxiv: https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.18668\n Code: https://github…,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-03-03-based,<a href='https://sabrieyuboglu.com/'>Sabri Eyuboglu</a>*, <a href=\"https://arorasimran.com/\">Simran Arora</a>*, <a href='https://michaelzhang.xyz/'>Michael Zhang</a>*,2026-07-22T18:37:38.385Z,2024-03-03T07:00:00.430Z,[{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-03-03-based/tradeoff.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1369,\"height\":621},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-03-03-based/based-design.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-03-03-based/table.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-03-03-based/feature_map.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-03-03-based/micro.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-03-03-based/efficiency.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"}],163778663386687488,1211296081845936170,Long-Context Retrieval Models with Monarch Mixer,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-01-11-m2-bert-retrieval,<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:60%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-01-11-m2-bert-retrieval/monarch_in_library.png\"></figure>\n<p>Text embeddings are a critical piece of many pipelines, from search, to RAG, to vector databases and more. Most embedding models are BERT/Transformer-based and typically have short context lengths (e.g., 512). That’s only about two pages of text, but documents can be very long – books, legal cases, TV screenplays, code repositories, etc can be tens of thousands of tokens long (or more). Here, we’re taking a first step towards developing <strong>long-context retrieval models</strong>.</p>\n<p>We build on Monarch Mixer (M2), a recent model family developing attention- and MLP-free BERT models, which are enabling long-context BERT models. Today, we’re releasing a preview of a few models: long-context versions of M2-BERT up to 32K context length, as well as embedding versions fine-tuned for long-context retrieval. In this blog, you’ll hear about the changes to data mixture and loss function we needed to enable these new models.</p>\n<p>We’re also releasing the V0 of a long-context retrieval benchmark called LoCo, that we’ve started to use to evaluate the long-context properties of various retrieval models.</p>\n<p>Check out code <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/m2\" target=\"_blank\">here</a>, and models up on HuggingFace here:</p>\n<ul>\n<li><a href=\"https://huggingface.co/togethercomputer/m2-bert-80M-2k-retrieval\" target=\"_blank\">M2-BERT-80M-2k-retrieval</a>.</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://huggingface.co/togethercomputer/m2-bert-80M-8k-retrieval\" target=\"_blank\">M2-BERT-80M-8k-retrieval</a>.</li>\n<li><a href=\"https://huggingface.co/togethercomputer/m2-bert-80M-32k-retrieval\" target=\"_blank\">M2-BERT-80M-32k-retrieval</a>.</li>\n</ul>\n<p>These models are also available on Together AI’s new embedding service – check it out <a href=\"https://www.together.ai/blog/embeddings-endpoint-release\" target=\"_blank\">here</a>! Thanks to our collaborators at Together, we were able to beta-test these models at a <a href=\"https://www.together.ai/blog/rag-tutorial-mongodb\" target=\"_blank\">MongoDB hackathon</a>, and we already have early RAG integrations with <a href=\"https://www.together.ai/blog/rag-tutorial-langchain\" target=\"_blank\">LangChain</a> and <a href=\"https://www.together.ai/blog/rag-tutorial-llamaindex\" target=\"_blank\">LlamaIndex</a>.</p>\n<p>We’ll have a full paper out next month – but for now, we wanted to release a preview to get early feedback from the community. <strong>Here are some particular calls to action for feedback if you’re interested in long-context retrieval:</strong></p>\n<ul>\n<li>If you have long-context retrieval tasks, we would love to hear how the M2-BERT retrieval models perform in the wild!</li>\n<li>If you have public long-context retrieval tasks or datasets that you think would be good additions to LoCo, please let us know. We’ve only included a few retrieval tasks that have long documents, but we want to grow the benchmark to be more representative!</li>\n</ul>\n<p>In the rest of this blog post, we’ll give a brief overview of Monarch Mixer, discuss the technical challenges involved in developing a long-context retrieval model (data mixtures, loss functions, model evaluation), and go over some early results from LoCo V0!</p>\n<h1>Monarch Mixer (M2)</h1>\n<p>First, we’ll give a very brief overview of Monarch Mixer. Check out Dan’s <a href=\"https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IS59IwGLvVs\" target=\"_blank\">MLSys Seminar</a> and <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.12109\" target=\"_blank\">the paper</a> for more details!</p>\n<p>Monarch Mixer (M2) is a recent model that aims to improve the scaling properties of Transformers along two axes – sequence length and model dimension. For context, Transformers scale quadratically in sequence length (due to attention) and model dimension (due to MLP layers). That means that it’s expensive to scale Transformers (like BERT-based embedding models) to long sequences.  This also explains why scaling Transformers up results in so many parameters.</p>\n<p>M2 aims to change these scaling properties by replacing attention and MLPs with a single <strong>sub-quadratic primitive</strong>: Monarch matrices. Monarch matrices are a family of block-sparse matrices defined as block-diagonal matrices, interleaved with permutations:</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-01-11-m2-bert-retrieval/m2-diagram.png\"><p>Monarch Mixer uses Monarch matrices to mix data across the sequence and hidden dimension.</p></figure>\n<p>Monarch matrices are a sub-quadratic primitive (you can compute them in <span><span><span><math>O(N3/2)O(N^{3/2})</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.138em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span><span>(</span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.888em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>3/2</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span>) that are also hardware-efficient and expressive. The block-diagonal matrices map onto tensor cores, and the permutations generalize the Fast Fourier Transform. As a result, Monarch matrices can efficiently capture all sorts of structured linear transforms:</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-01-11-m2-bert-retrieval/linear-transforms.png\"><p>Monarch matrices can capture many structured linear transforms, including Toeplitz, Fourier, Hadamard, and more.</p></figure>\n<p>In M2, we use Monarch matrices to replace both attention and MLPs in Transformers. We replace attention by using Monarch matrices to construct a gated long convolution layer, similar to work like <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2212.14052\" target=\"_blank\">H3</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2302.10866\" target=\"_blank\">Hyena</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2206.13947\" target=\"_blank\">GSS</a>, and <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2212.10544\" target=\"_blank\">BiGS</a>. Specifically, Monarch matrices can implement the FFT, which can be used to compute a <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2023-12-11-conv-tutorial\" target=\"_blank\">long convolution</a> efficiently:</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:40%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-01-11-m2-bert-retrieval/m2-arch-sequence.png\"><p>M2 uses Monarch matrices to implement a gated long convolution, by implementing the convolution using FFT operations.</p></figure>\n<p>We replace the MLPs by replacing the dense matrices in the MLP with block diagonal matrices:</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:25%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-01-11-m2-bert-retrieval/m2-arch-dimension.png\"><p>M2 replaces dense matrices in an MLP with block-diagonal matrices.</p></figure>\n<p>Incidentally, this makes the model look similar to MoE models, without learned routing.</p>\n<p>Once we’ve replaced attention and MLP with these new primitives, we can use this architecture to train BERT models. In the M2 paper, we showed that we can match BERT quality with fewer parameters:</p>\n<table><thead><tr><th align=\"center\"><strong>Model</strong></th><th align=\"center\">Average GLUE Score</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td align=\"center\">BERT-base (110M)</td><td align=\"center\">79.6</td></tr><tr><td align=\"center\">M2-BERT-base (80M)</td><td align=\"center\">79.9</td></tr><tr><td align=\"center\">BERT-large (340M)</td><td align=\"center\">82.1</td></tr><tr><td align=\"center\">M2-BERT-base (260M)</td><td align=\"center\">82.2</td></tr></tbody></table>\n<p>And faster wall-clock time, especially for long sequences (throughput in terms of tokens/ms):</p>\n<table><thead><tr><th>Model</th><th>512</th><th>4096</th><th>8192</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td>HF BERT-base (110M)</td><td>206.1</td><td>39.0</td><td>OOM</td></tr><tr><td>FlashAttention BERT-base (110M)</td><td>367.4</td><td>179.1</td><td>102.4</td></tr><tr><td>M2-BERT-base (80M)</td><td><strong>386.3</strong></td><td><strong>353.9</strong></td><td><strong>320.1</strong></td></tr><tr><td>Speedup over HF</td><td>1.9<span><span><span><math>×\\times</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6667em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>×</span></span></span></span></span></td><td>9.1<span><span><span><math>×\\times</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6667em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>×</span></span></span></span></span></td><td>-</td></tr></tbody></table>\n<p>That OOM in HF BERT-base is particularly important (and FlashAttention BERT-base eventually OOMs as well). That means that <strong>any retriever with a Transformers-based BERT backbone</strong> will have trouble with long-context – that’s everything from sentence-BERT to ColBERT to BGE and more!</p>\n<p>Check out our <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2023-07-25-m2-bert\" target=\"_blank\">blog post</a> on M2-BERT for more!</p>\n<p>In this work, we’re going to take advantage of the better long-context throughput to train some long-context retrieval models!</p>\n<h1>Adapting M2-BERT for Long-Context Retrieval</h1>\n<p>In the original M2 paper, we only trained M2-BERT models on relatively short sequence lengths (128 sequence length), though we benchmarked the architecture’s efficiency on longer sequences. In this work, we found that simply extending the standard BERT training pipeline to longer sequence data was insufficient to train a good long-context retrieval model.</p>\n<p>In particular, we faced two concrete challenges:</p>\n<ul>\n<li><strong>Adapting the standard BERT pretraining pipeline to long sequences (data mixture).</strong> Our M2-BERT pretraining pipeline used C4, which has relatively short documents. If we want to pretrain a long-context M2-BERT model, we’ll need longer documents. We’ll also find that directly training on long-sequence data is hard – we need a mixture of sequence lengths or warmstarting from a shorter-context checkpoint.</li>\n<li><strong>Adapting the standard retrieval fine-tuning pipelines for long-context models (loss functions).</strong> Almost every dense retrieval model (see <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/1908.10084\" target=\"_blank\">1</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2112.01488\" target=\"_blank\">2</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2004.04906\" target=\"_blank\">3</a>, <a href=\"https://aclanthology.org/2023.findings-acl.761/\" target=\"_blank\">4</a>) uses a supervised contrastive loss function, which is very sensitive to batch size. This is a problem for long-sequence data, since batch sizes need to remain small for GPU memory limits. We need to change the loss functions.</li>\n</ul>\n<p>As an aside – these challenges point to a broader trend in the research area. There’s been a lot of work on designing new architectures for long sequences, but the architecture isn’t all it takes. There’s an emerging area on <strong>how to train</strong> models so they can actually use long sequences.</p>\n<p>We’ll dive more into each of these challenges now.</p>\n<h2>Long-Context Pretraining</h2>\n<p>Our first problem is how to adapt the BERT pretraining pipelines for long-context data. In the original M2 work, we performed masked language modeling pretraining at context length 128 using the standard C4 corpus. C4 contains documents with relatively short sequence lengths (dozens to low hundreds of tokens), so how can we adapt our pipeline for long-context pretraining and get 2K, 8K, and 32K pretrained M2-BERT models?</p>\n<p>A standard technique (which is common in GPT-style training) is to concatenate documents together until you reach your desired sequence length. Under this paradigm, each document the model sees is of a single fixed length. We tried this out with C4 at first, but found this resulted in poor performance – one intuition for why is that BERT-style models need to process documents and generate embeddings for all sorts of sequence lengths.</p>\n<p>Instead, we found that it was helpful to use a mixture of natural documents, of varied sequence lengths, with padding and concatenation. We also extended the pretraining corpus to include naturally-longer documents – in addition to C4, we added longer documents from Wikipedia and Books3 to our pretraining corpus.
