We used a weighted mixture of natural documents and concatenated documents to pretrain our M2 models:</p>\n<table><thead><tr><th align=\"center\">Data source</th><th align=\"center\">Proportion</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td align=\"center\">C4</td><td align=\"center\">10%</td></tr><tr><td align=\"center\">C4, concatenated</td><td align=\"center\">24%</td></tr><tr><td align=\"center\">Wikipedia</td><td align=\"center\">10%</td></tr><tr><td align=\"center\">Wikipedia, concatenated</td><td align=\"center\">23%</td></tr><tr><td align=\"center\">Books3</td><td align=\"center\">10%</td></tr><tr><td align=\"center\">Books3, concatenated</td><td align=\"center\">23%</td></tr></tbody></table>\n<p>We used these proportions to train our 2K and 8K models from scratch. For the 32K model, we found that it was helpful to warm-start using the 8K model, and simply copy over positional embeddings at initialization.</p>\n<p>With these techniques, we were able to pretrain three long-context M2-BERT models: <a href=\"https://huggingface.co/togethercomputer/m2-bert-80M-2k\" target=\"_blank\">M2-BERT-80M-2k</a>, <a href=\"https://huggingface.co/togethercomputer/m2-bert-80M-8k\" target=\"_blank\">M2-BERT-80M-8k</a>, and <a href=\"https://huggingface.co/togethercomputer/m2-bert-80M-32k\" target=\"_blank\">M2-BERT-80M-32k</a>.</p>\n<h2>Fine-Tuning for Long-Context Retrieval</h2>\n<p>Ok, so now we have some long-context M2-BERT models. But raw BERT models are actually not all that useful for retrieval – you have to fine-tune them to become good embedding models, a la a <a href=\"https://www.sbert.net/\" target=\"_blank\">sentence-transformers</a> pipeline.</p>\n<p>This brings us to our second training challenge. Most retrieval models are trained using a supervised contrastive loss (called “MultipleNegativesRankingLoss” in sentence-transformers), using corpora of queries and documents.</p>\n<p>In the data, you have pairs of queries and documents. The way the loss typically works is you load up a batch of queries and documents, and use a contrastive loss to bring the embeddings of the true query/document pair together (the “positive” pair), while pushing apart all the embeddings of all the other queries and documents (the “negative” pairs).</p>\n<p>You can see this written out mathematically here, for a batch of queries <span><span><span><math>QQ</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8778em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span>Q</span></span></span></span></span> and documents <span><span><span><math>DD</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">D</span></span></span></span></span>, where <span><span><span><math>(Qi,Di)(\\mathbf{Q_i}, \\mathbf{D_i})</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>(</span><span><span>Q</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3361em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span>D</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3361em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> are the embeddings of the positive pair:</p>\n<div><span><span><span><math>ℓ(Q,D)=∑icos_sim(Qi,Di)∑j,j≠icos_sim(Qi,Dj)\\ell(Q, D) = \\sum_i \\frac{\\text{cos\\_sim}(\\mathbf{Q_i}, \\mathbf{D_i})}{\\sum_{j, j \\neq i} \\text{cos\\_sim}(\\mathbf{Q_i}, \\mathbf{D_j})}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>ℓ</span><span>(</span><span>Q</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">D</span><span>)</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:2.7277em;vertical-align:-1.2777em\"></span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.05em\"><span style=\"top:-1.8723em;margin-left:0em\"><span style=\"height:3.05em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span><span style=\"top:-3.05em\"><span style=\"height:3.05em\"></span><span><span>∑</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1.2777em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span></span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.45em\"><span style=\"top:-2.314em\"><span style=\"height:3em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"position:relative;top:0em\">∑</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.1864em\"><span style=\"top:-2.4003em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span><span><span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8889em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span><span><span></span></span></span><span></span></span></span></span></span><span>=</span></span><span>i</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.4358em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span>cos_sim</span></span><span>(</span><span><span>Q</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3361em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span>D</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3361em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>j</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2861em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>)</span></span></span><span style=\"top:-3.23em\"><span style=\"height:3em\"></span><span style=\"border-bottom-width:0.04em\"></span></span><span style=\"top:-3.7em\"><span style=\"height:3em\"></span><span><span><span>cos_sim</span></span><span>(</span><span><span>Q</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3361em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span>D</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3361em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>)</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1.1218em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></div>\n<p>The key thing to understand about this is that the numerator pushes the embeddings of the positive pair <span><span><span><math>Qi,Di\\mathbf{Q_i}, \\mathbf{D_i}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8805em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span><span>Q</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3361em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span>D</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3361em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> towards each other, while the denominator pushes embeddings every negative pair <span><span><span><math>Qi,Dj\\mathbf{Q_i}, \\mathbf{D_j}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.9722em;vertical-align:-0.2861em\"></span><span><span>Q</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3361em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span>D</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3361em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>j</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2861em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> apart from each other (from <span><span><span><math>j≠ij \\neq i</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8889em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span><span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8889em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span><span><span></span></span></span><span></span></span></span></span></span><span>=</span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6595em\"></span><span>i</span></span></span></span></span>).</p>\n<p>This loss is very <strong>dependent on the batch size</strong>. If your batch is too small, you don’t get enough negative samples, and you get a bad embedding geometry.</p>\n<blockquote>\n<p>This poses a problem for training long-context retrieval models – when you’re fine-tuning on long documents, you are limited in batch size due to GPU memory limits (more tokens -> more memory).</p>\n</blockquote>\n<p>For context, we typically want batches of size 32 or greater with the contrastive loss – but we’re limited to size 1 at sequence length 32K.</p>\n<p>The computer vision <a href=\"https://lilianweng.github.io/posts/2021-05-31-contrastive/\" target=\"_blank\">literature</a> has known about this problem for a while and has developed a number of techniques – <a href=\"https://lilianweng.github.io/posts/2021-05-31-contrastive/#memory-bank\" target=\"_blank\">memory banks</a>, <a href=\"https://lilianweng.github.io/posts/2021-05-31-contrastive/#swav\" target=\"_blank\">prototypical losses</a>, and more. When we tried these on retrieval pipelines, most of them had poor performance. Our intuition is that they are poor approximations of the true contrastive loss.</p>\n<p>We then turned to a technique called <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2103.14021\" target=\"_blank\">orthogonal loss</a> (or “cosine similarity loss” in sentence-transformers parlance). This loss pushes the cosine similarity of positive pairs of embeddings to 1, and the cosine similarity of negative pairs of embeddings to 0 – or orthogonal vectors.</p>\n<p>And it’s simple to write down. For a given query embedding <span><span><span><math>Q0\\mathbf{Q_0}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8805em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span><span>Q</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>0</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>, its positive <span><span><span><math>D0\\mathbf{D_0}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8361em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span>D</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>0</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>, and <span><span><span><math>NN</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span></span></span></span></span> negatives <span><span><span><math>D1,…,DN\\mathbf{D_1}, \\dots, \\mathbf{D_N}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8805em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span><span>D</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>1</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>…</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span>D</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3303em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>N</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>, the loss is:</p>\n<div><span><span><span><math>ℓ(Q0,D0,…,DN)=(1−cos_sim(Q0,D0)+∑i=1Ncos_sim(Q0,Di))\\ell(\\mathbf{Q_0}, \\mathbf{D_0}, \\dots, \\mathbf{D_N}) = (1 - \\text{cos\\_sim}(\\mathbf{Q_0}, \\mathbf{D_0}) + \\sum_{i=1}^N \\text{cos\\_sim}(\\mathbf{Q_0}, \\mathbf{D_i}))</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>ℓ</span><span>(</span><span><span>Q</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>0</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span>D</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>0</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>…</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span>D</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3303em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>N</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>)</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>(</span><span>1</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>−</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1.06em;vertical-align:-0.31em\"></span><span><span>cos_sim</span></span><span>(</span><span><span>Q</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>0</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span>D</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>0</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>)</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>+</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:3.106em;vertical-align:-1.2777em\"></span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.8283em\"><span style=\"top:-1.8723em;margin-left:0em\"><span style=\"height:3.05em\"></span><span><span><span>i</span><span>=</span><span>1</span></span></span></span><span style=\"top:-3.05em\"><span style=\"height:3.05em\"></span><span><span>∑</span></span></span><span style=\"top:-4.3em;margin-left:0em\"><span style=\"height:3.05em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1.2777em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span>cos_sim</span></span><span>(</span><span><span>Q</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>0</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span>D</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3361em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>))</span></span></span></span></span></div>\n<p>The terms of this loss are independent from each other (c.w. the denominator of a contrastive loss needs all the negatives). As a result, <strong>this entire loss can be computed while only ever holding one query and one document in memory!</strong> That means that we are not sensitive to batch size limitations on GPU.</p>\n<p>Using this loss, we were able to unlock good long-context retrieval performance! Speaking of… how did we know how to evaluate those pipelines?</p>\n<h1>Results: Long-Context Retrieval with LoCo</h1>\n<p>So now we have these long-context retrieval models! This actually brought up another challenge: how do we actually evaluate these models? Most existing retrieval benchmarks like BEIR and MTEB are mostly full of short queries and documents.</p>\n<p>So in response, we put together a long-context retrieval benchmark called LoCo.</p>\n<blockquote>\n<p>LoCo is under active development – this is just a first pass. If you have tasks that you think would be good additions to the benchmark to evaluate on, please reach out to us via email!</p>\n</blockquote>\n<p>For now, LoCo just contains five retrieval tasks taken from public datasets. We selected these based on the length of the documents (we wanted long documents), and manual inspection to check that retrieval would actually require understanding of the whole document. Here are some statistics on the five datasets that are in LoCo V0 (scroll to see the whole table):</p>\n<table><thead><tr><th align=\"right\"><strong>Dataset</strong></th><th align=\"center\"><strong>Document Type</strong></th><th align=\"center\"><strong>Number of Documents</strong></th><th align=\"center\"><strong>Min Length (Characters / Words)</strong></th><th align=\"center\"><strong>Max Length (Characters / Words)</strong></th><th align=\"center\"><strong>Avg. Document Length (Characters / Words)</strong></th><th align=\"center\"><strong>Avg. Query Length (Characters / Words)</strong></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td align=\"right\"><strong><a href=\"https://huggingface.co/datasets/tau/scrolls#qmsum-zhong-et-al-2021\" target=\"_blank\">Tau Scrolls QMSum</a></strong></td><td align=\"center\">Meeting Transcripts</td><td align=\"center\">1257</td><td align=\"center\">6458 / 1035</td><td align=\"center\">147316 / 24651</td><td align=\"center\">55297 / 9748</td><td align=\"center\">434 / 71</td></tr><tr><td align=\"right\"><strong><a href=\"https://huggingface.co/datasets/tau/scrolls#govreport-huang-et-al-2021\" target=\"_blank\">Tau Scrolls Gov Report</a></strong></td><td align=\"center\">National Policy Reports</td><td align=\"center\">17457</td><td align=\"center\">2035 / 289</td><td align=\"center\">1268491 / 197506</td><td align=\"center\">52716 / 8154</td><td align=\"center\">3232 / 479</td></tr><tr><td align=\"right\"><strong><a href=\"https://huggingface.co/datasets/tau/scrolls#summscreenfd-chen--et-al-2021\" target=\"_blank\">Tau Scrolls Sum mScreen FD</a></strong></td><td align=\"center\">TV Episode Transcripts</td><td align=\"center\">3673</td><td align=\"center\">7514 / 1154</td><td align=\"center\">83873 / 13573</td><td align=\"center\">31346 / 5196</td><td align=\"center\">570 / 99</td></tr><tr><td align=\"right\"><strong><a href=\"https://huggingface.co/datasets/allenai/qasper\" target=\"_blank\">Qasper  Title to Document</a></strong></td><td align=\"center\">Scientific Research Papers</td><td align=\"center\">888</td><td align=\"center\">375 / 44</td><td align=\"center\">169214 / 26005</td><td align=\"center\">24537 / 3749</td><td align=\"center\">72 / 9</td></tr><tr><td align=\"right\"><strong><a href=\"https://huggingface.co/datasets/allenai/qasper\" target=\"_blank\">Qasper Abstract to Document</a></strong></td><td align=\"center\">Scientific Research Papers</td><td align=\"center\">888</td><td align=\"center\">375 / 44</td><td align=\"center\">169214 / 26005</td><td align=\"center\">24537 / 3749</td><td align=\"center\">982 / 144</td></tr></tbody></table>\n<p>Here’s a sample from one of the datasets called <a href=\"https://huggingface.co/datasets/tau/scrolls#qmsum-zhong-et-al-2021\" target=\"_blank\">QMSum</a>, where the documents are meeting transcripts.</p>\n<div style=\"font-family:&#x22;Courier New&#x22;, monospace;font-size:0.9em\"><p><strong>Query:</strong><br>\nThe meeting was mostly about the logistics of covering the legal bases around releasing meeting data. The team wanted to make sure that meeting participants would not sue for libel or releasing unwanted information. The team also went over the transcriptions that IBM had done as well as storage space, which was finally looking up. The meeting ended with a general discussion about the progress of the group and future directions.</p></div>\n<div style=\"font-family:&#x22;Courier New&#x22;, monospace;font-size:0.9em\"><p><strong>Document:</strong><br>\n...<br>\nPhD C: Yeah .<br>\nGrad F: But I figure also if they 're short agenda items , we could also do a little bit of each .<br>\nProfessor B: Yeah .<br>\nGrad F: So . I seem to be having difficulty getting this adjusted . Here we go .<br>\nProfessor B: OK .<br>\nGrad F: So , uh , as most of you should know , I did send out the consent form thingies and , uh , so far no one has made any {disfmarker} Ach ! {comment} {comment} any comments on them . So , no one has bleeped out anything .<br>\nProfessor B: Um . Yeah .<br>\nGrad F: So . I don't expect anyone to . But .<br>\nProfessor B: Um . {vocalsound} So , what follows ? At some point you go around and get people to sign something ?<br>\nGrad F: No . We had spoken w about this before<br>\nProfessor B: Yeah , but I 've forgotten .<br>\n...</p></div>\n<p>We found that these tasks were decently challenging, even for state-of-the-art retrieval models (see the bottom of the blog for full results).\nThe LoCo score is the average of the NDCG@10 score for all five tasks.</p>\n<table><thead><tr><th align=\"center\">Retrieval Encoders</th><th align=\"center\">Seq Len</th><th align=\"center\">LoCo Score</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td align=\"center\">intfloat/e5-mistral-7b-instruct (7.11B)</td><td align=\"center\">4096</td><td align=\"center\"><strong>88.5</strong></td></tr><tr><td align=\"center\">BGE-Large-en-v1.5 (335M) Zero-shot</td><td align=\"center\">512</td><td align=\"center\">77.2</td></tr><tr><td align=\"center\">BGE-Base-en-v1.5 (109M) Zero-shot</td><td align=\"center\">512</td><td align=\"center\">73.4</td></tr><tr><td align=\"center\">BGE-Small-en-v1.5 (33.4M) Zero-shot</td><td align=\"center\">512</td><td align=\"center\">70.6</td></tr><tr><td align=\"center\">Voyage Embeddings</td><td align=\"center\">4096</td><td align=\"center\">25.4</td></tr><tr><td align=\"center\">Cohere Embeddings</td><td align=\"center\">512</td><td align=\"center\">66.6</td></tr><tr><td align=\"center\">OpenAI Ada Embeddings</td><td align=\"center\">8192</td><td align=\"center\">52.7</td></tr></tbody></table>\n<p>Mistral-7B performs the best - it can technically run sequences longer than 4K, but the README doesn’t <a href=\"https://huggingface.co/intfloat/e5-mistral-7b-instruct#limitations\" target=\"_blank\">recommend it</a>. We also ran with the full 32K context (see the table at the end), but observed worse performance.</p>\n<p>For our long-context retrieval models, we fine-tuned M2-BERT-80M-2K, -8K, and -32K on the training sets of these tasks using the orthogonal loss. These are our <a href=\"https://huggingface.co/togethercomputer/m2-bert-80M-2k-retrieval\" target=\"_blank\">M2-BERT-80M-2K-retrieval</a>, <a href=\"https://huggingface.co/togethercomputer/m2-bert-80M-8k-retrieval\" target=\"_blank\">-8K-retrieval</a>, and <a href=\"https://huggingface.co/togethercomputer/m2-bert-80M-32k-retrieval\" target=\"_blank\">-32K-retrieval</a> models.</p>\n<p>For a baseline comparison, we took the three BGE models (small, base, large) and fine-tuned them a few ways using the same examples. We also include the best zero-shot model as reference:</p>\n<table><thead><tr><th align=\"center\">Retrieval Encoders</th><th align=\"center\">Seq Len</th><th align=\"center\">LoCo Score</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td align=\"center\">infloat/e5-mistral-7b-instruct (7.11B)</td><td align=\"center\">4096</td><td align=\"center\">88.5</td></tr><tr><td align=\"center\">BGE-Large-en-v1.5 (335M) Fine-tuned</td><td align=\"center\">512</td><td align=\"center\">85.0</td></tr><tr><td align=\"center\">BGE-Base-en-v1.5 (109M) Fine-tuned</td><td align=\"center\">512</td><td align=\"center\">83.0</td></tr><tr><td align=\"center\">BGE-Small-en-v1.5 (33.4M) Fine-tuned</td><td align=\"center\">512</td><td align=\"center\">81.2</td></tr><tr><td align=\"center\">M2-BERT-2048   (80M)</td><td align=\"center\">2048</td><td align=\"center\">83.6</td></tr><tr><td align=\"center\">M2-BERT-8192    (80M)</td><td align=\"center\">8192</td><td align=\"center\">85.9</td></tr><tr><td align=\"center\">M2-BERT-32768   (80M)</td><td align=\"center\">32768</td><td align=\"center\"><strong>92.5</strong></td></tr></tbody></table>\n<p>M2-BERT retrieval models can outperform much larger models on this benchmark – up to <strong>4x</strong> larger fine-tuned models and up to <strong>85x</strong> larger zero-shot models – which is pretty exciting and suggests that long-context models are beneficial for retrieval!</p>\n<p>We also tried hard to adapt SoTA short-context retrieval models to the long-context setting by changing the retrieval protocol. We chunked the long-context documents in the LoCo benchmarks and indexed the chunks. Given a query, we rank the documents based on the average similarity across the chunks in the document. We found that this protocol was sufficient to compete with long-context M2 models on 2 of 5 benchmark tasks. However, this protocol severely underperforms on the Tau Scrolls - QMSUM and QASPER tasks:</p>\n<table><thead><tr><th align=\"center\">Retrieval Encoders</th><th align=\"center\">Seq Len</th><th align=\"center\">LoCo Score</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td align=\"center\">infloat/e5-mistral-7b-instruct (7.11B)</td><td align=\"center\">4096</td><td align=\"center\">88.5</td></tr><tr><td align=\"center\">BGE-Large-en-v1.5 (335M)</td><td align=\"center\">512</td><td align=\"center\">85.0</td></tr><tr><td align=\"center\">BGE-Large-en-v1.5 (335M)  w/ Chunking</td><td align=\"center\">32768</td><td align=\"center\">72.5</td></tr><tr><td align=\"center\">M2-BERT-32768   (80M)</td><td align=\"center\">32768</td><td align=\"center\"><strong>92.5</strong></td></tr></tbody></table>\n<h2>Extending LoCo</h2>\n<p>Of course, these are only initial preliminary results – we don’t expect many people are searching up TV scripts via synopsis. When constructing this benchmark, we were primarily looking for datasets that a) had long documents, and b) where the query to passage mapping wasn’t immediately obvious from the first few sentences. For example, a dataset of movie titles and movie scripts, where the title is the first sentence of the script, wouldn’t be a very interesting long-context benchmark!</p>\n<p>We think there’s tons of exciting research to be done to figure out where long-context models matter most. That’s why we want <strong>your feedback</strong> on what tasks to add to this benchmark – this is only a first step towards really great long-context retrieval models!</p>\n<h1>What’s Next</h1>\n<p>This is an initial release of a few interesting artifacts via blog post – we’ll be releasing a full paper with all the details some time next month. In the meantime, you can check out our <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/m2\" target=\"_blank\">code</a>, download our models (<a href=\"https://huggingface.co/togethercomputer/m2-bert-80M-2k-retrieval\" target=\"_blank\">2k</a>, <a href=\"https://huggingface.co/togethercomputer/m2-bert-80M-8k-retrieval\" target=\"_blank\">8k</a>, <a href=\"https://huggingface.co/togethercomputer/m2-bert-80M-32k-retrieval\" target=\"_blank\">32k</a>), and try it on your own tasks via the <a href=\"https://www.together.ai/blog/embeddings-endpoint-release\" target=\"_blank\">Together embeddings API</a>.</p>\n<p>If you do, we really want to <strong>hear from you!</strong> We’d love to hear about:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>What long-context retrieval applications you’ve tried M2-BERT retrieval on, and how well they’ve performed</li>\n<li>What long-context tasks we should add to LoCo for the next version</li>\n</ul>\n<p>If you’re interested in this stuff, please reach out – we’re excited to hear from you!</p>\n<ul>\n<li>Jon Saad-Falcon: <a href=\"mailto:jonsaadfalcon@stanford.edu\" target=\"_blank\">jonsaadfalcon@stanford.edu</a></li>\n<li>Dan Fu: <a href=\"mailto:danfu@cs.stanford.edu\" target=\"_blank\">danfu@cs.stanford.edu</a></li>\n<li>Simran Arora: <a href=\"mailto:simarora@stanford.edu\" target=\"_blank\">simarora@stanford.edu</a></li>\n</ul>\n<h1>Acknowledgments</h1>\n<p>We would like to thank <a href=\"https://crfm.stanford.edu/\" target=\"_blank\">the Stanford Center for Research on Foundation Models (CRFM)</a>, and the <a href=\"https://ai.stanford.edu/\" target=\"_blank\">Stanford AI Laboratory</a> for supporting our research! Thank you to Alycia Lee for helping set up AutoModel for our models on HuggingFace.</p>\n<p>We would also like to thank our collaborators at <a href=\"https://www.together.ai/\" target=\"_blank\">Together AI</a> for their support in developing these models, and their help in hosting these models in their <a href=\"https://www.together.ai/blog/embeddings-endpoint-release\" target=\"_blank\">new embedding API</a>. Thanks to Together, we were able to test out early versions of these models at a Hackathon with <a href=\"https://www.together.ai/blog/rag-tutorial-mongodb\" target=\"_blank\">MongoDB</a>, and we’re looking forward to all the new use cases for long-context retrieval models including RAG integrations with <a href=\"https://www.together.ai/blog/rag-tutorial-langchain\" target=\"_blank\">LangChain</a> and <a href=\"https://www.together.ai/blog/rag-tutorial-llamaindex\" target=\"_blank\">LlamaIndex</a>.</p>\n<p>Check out their <a href=\"https://www.together.ai/blog/embeddings-endpoint-release\" target=\"_blank\">blog</a> <a href=\"https://www.together.ai/blog/rag-tutorial-mongodb\" target=\"_blank\">posts</a> on new <a href=\"https://www.together.ai/blog/rag-tutorial-langchain\" target=\"_blank\">usage</a> of these <a href=\"https://www.together.ai/blog/rag-tutorial-llamaindex\" target=\"_blank\">models</a>!</p>\n<h1>Full Results</h1>\n<table><thead><tr><th align=\"center\">Retrieval Encoders</th><th align=\"center\">Seq Len</th><th align=\"center\">Tau Scrolls Summ. Screen</th><th align=\"center\">Tau Scrolls Gov. align=\"center\">85.9</td></tr><tr><td align=\"center\">M2-BERT-32768 (80M)</td><td align=\"center\">32768</td><td align=\"center\"><strong>98.6</strong></td><td Fine-tuned</td><td align=\"center\">512</td><td align=\"center\">64.7</td><td align=\"center\">90.2</td><td align=\"center\">66.9</td><td align=\"center\">95.2</td><td align=\"center\">98.1</td><td align=\"center\">83.0</td></tr><tr><td align=\"center\">BGE-Large-en-v1.5 (335M) Fine-tuned</td><td align=\"center\">512</td><td align=\"center\">70.8</td><td align=\"center\">93.5</td><td Chunking</td><td align=\"center\">32768</td><td align=\"center\">88.6</td><td align=\"center\">97.7</td><td align=\"center\">17.8</td><td align=\"center\">72.9</td><td align=\"center\">85.5</td><td align=\"center\">72.5</td></tr><tr><td align=\"center\">OpenAI text-embedding-ada-002</td><td align=\"center\">8192</td><td align=\"center\">37.3</td><td align=\"center\">44.3</td><td align=\"center\">512</td><td align=\"center\">46.4</td><td align=\"center\">72.4</td><td align=\"center\">38.1</td><td align=\"center\">85.2</td><td align=\"center\">91.1</td><td align=\"center\">66.7</td></tr><tr><td align=\"center\">Voyage voyage-01</td><td align=\"center\">4096</td><td align=\"center\">1.95</td><td align=\"center\">39.4</td><td align=\"center\">15.2</td><td align=\"center\">38.4</td><td align=\"center\">32.2</td><td align=\"center\">25.41</td></tr><tr><td align=\"center\">infloat/e5-mistral-7b-instruct (7.11B) 4K</td><td align=\"center\">4096</td><td align=\"center\">95.9</td><td align=\"center\">98.3</td><td align=\"center\">46.8</td><td align=\"center\"><strong>98.4</strong></td><td align=\"center\"><strong>99.8</strong></td><td align=\"center\">87.8</td></tr><tr><td align=\"center\">infloat/e5-mistral-7b-instruct (7.11B)  32K</td><td align=\"center\">32768</td><td align=\"center\">70.7</td><td align=\"center\">51.8</td><td align=\"center\">7.4</td><td align=\"center\">96.6</td><td align=\"center\">97.9</td><td align=\"center\">64.9</td></tr></tbody></table>,Text embeddings are a critical piece of many pipelines, from search, to RAG, to vector databases and more. Most embedding models are BERT…,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2024-01-11-m2-bert-retrieval,<a href='https://jonsaadfalcon.com/'>Jon Saad-Falcon</a>, <a href='https://danfu09.github.io/'>Dan Fu</a>, <a href=\"https://arorasimran.com/\">Simran Arora</a>,2026-07-22T18:37:38.384Z,2024-01-11T07:00:00.090Z,[{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-01-11-m2-bert-retrieval/monarch_in_library.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1024,\"height\":1024},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-01-11-m2-bert-retrieval/m2-diagram.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-01-11-m2-bert-retrieval/linear-transforms.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-01-11-m2-bert-retrieval/m2-arch-sequence.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2024-01-11-m2-bert-retrieval/m2-arch-dimension.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"}],163778663386687488,1211296081845936171,Zoology (Blogpost 2): Simple, Input-Dependent, and Sub-Quadratic Sequence Mixers,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2023-12-11-zoology2-based,<h2><code>Based</code>: An Educational and Effective Sequence Mixer </h2>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:35%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2023-12-11-zoology2-based/image23.png\"></figure>\n<p>We’re excited to share our new ~ <code>Based </code>~ architecture, inspired by our theoretical and empirical analysis of how different architecture classes perform <em>associative recall</em> (AR), a key task predictive of language modeling and in-context learning capabilities.</p>\n<p><strong>Key takeaways:</strong> <code>Based</code> is a simple architecture that combines two familiar sub-quadratic operators: short convolutions and linear attention. These operators have complementary strengths and together enable high-quality language modeling with strong associative recall capabilities. At inference time, because <code>Based</code> only uses fixed-sized convolutions and linear attentions (computable as recurrences), we can decode with no KV-cache. This enables a <strong>4.5x</strong> throughput improvement over Transformers with Flash Attention 2.</p>\n<p>We demonstrate these properties along three axes, finding that <code>Based</code> provides:</p>\n<ol>\n<li><strong>Simple and intuitive understandings</strong>: we motivate <code>Based</code> with the view that simple convolutions and attentions are good at modeling different kinds of sequences. Instead of introducing new complexities to overcome their individual weaknesses, we can combine familiar versions of each (short 1D convolutions! “spiky” linear attentions!) in intuitive ways to get a best-of-both-worlds situation. (Section 1. Introducing the <code>Based</code> Models)</li>\n</ol>\n<ol start=\"2\">\n<li><strong>High quality modeling:</strong> despite its simplicity, in our evaluations we find <code>Based</code> outperforms full Llama-2 style Transformers (rotary embeddings, SwiGLU MLPs, etc.) and modern state-space models (Mamba, Hyena) in language modeling perplexity at multiple scales. (Section 2. <code>Based</code> outperforms Transformers in language model perplexity.)</li>\n</ol>\n<ol start=\"3\">\n<li><strong>Efficient high-throughput inference</strong>: when implemented in pure PyTorch, <code>Based</code> achieves <strong>4.5x higher inference throughput</strong> than competitive Transformers, e.g., a parameter-matched <a href=\"https://mistral.ai/news/announcing-mistral-7b/\" target=\"_blank\"><strong>Mistral</strong></a> with its sliding window attention and <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.08691\" target=\"_blank\"><strong>FlashAttention 2</strong></a>. High throughput is critical for enabling <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2023-04-12-batch\" target=\"_blank\"><strong>batch processing tasks</strong></a> with LLMs. (Section 3. <code>Based</code> is fast.)</li>\n</ol>\n<p>This blogpost is the first preview of <code>Based</code>, lots more to come! You can follow along with our downstream language modeling results <a href=\"https://api.wandb.ai/links/hazy-research/qnc0jdig\" target=\"_blank\">in this WandB report </a>and play with <code>Based</code> on our synthetic associative recall testbed <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/zoology\" target=\"_blank\">in this repository</a>.</p>\n<h3>Section 0. Recapping the lessons we learned in Zoology.<a id=\"user-content-section-0-recapping-the-lessons-we-learned-in-zoology\" target=\"_blank\"></a></h3>\n<p>In our previous blogpost, <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2023-12-11-zoology1-analysis\" target=\"_blank\"><em>Zoology: Measuring and Improving Recall in Efficient Language Models</em></a>, we discuss the analysis that motivated the design of Based. Here’s a quick summary of what we learned…</p>\n<p>We found that there is a small perplexity gap between recently proposed sub-quadratic gated-convolution architectures and Transformers, when training on fixed data (10B tokens of the Pile) and infrastructure (EleutherAI GPT-NeoX). However, after performing a fine-grained error analysis, we see there remains a significant gap on next-token predictions that require the model to deploy a skill called associative recall (AR).  </p>\n<p><em>What’s AR?</em> Consider the sequence: “She put vanilla extract in her strawberry smoothie … then she drank her strawberry ?” – the model needs to be able to look back at the prior context and <em>recall</em> that the next word should be “smoothie”. We find that the gated-convolution models’ poor performance on these sorts of token predictions account for >82% of the remaining quality gap to attention on average! A 70M attention model outperforms 1.4Bn Hyena on AR tokens.</p>\n<table><thead><tr><th></th><th align=\"right\"></th><th align=\"right\"></th><th align=\"right\"></th><th align=\"right\"></th><th align=\"right\"></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td></td><td align=\"right\">Attention</td><td align=\"right\">Long Conv</td><td align=\"right\">Hyena</td><td align=\"right\">H3</td><td align=\"right\">RWKV</td></tr><tr><td>All Tokens (PPL)</td><td align=\"right\">9.44</td><td align=\"right\">13.13</td><td align=\"right\">10.38</td><td align=\"right\">10.07</td><td align=\"right\">9.79</td></tr><tr><td>AR Tokens (PPL)</td><td align=\"right\">1.98</td><td align=\"right\">13.27</td><td align=\"right\">4.81</td><td align=\"right\">3.83</td><td align=\"right\">3.82</td></tr><tr><td>Other Tokens (PPL)</td><td align=\"right\">10.62</td><td align=\"right\">13.12</td><td align=\"right\">11</td><td align=\"right\">10.75</td><td align=\"right\">10.51</td></tr></tbody></table>\n<p>Table 1: Perplexity of 355 million parameter models trained for 10 billion tokens on the Pile. </p>\n<p>Yet, some subquadratic gated-convolutions match attention on the non AR slice! Can we capture the strengths of both gated convolutions and attention in one purely sub-quadratic architecture? </p>\n<p>We find the AR gap is because gated convolution models (<em>e.g.</em> Hyena, H3, RWKV, RetNet) need model dimension that scales in sequence length to solve associative recall. We can see this scaling on synthetic associative recall data:</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2023-12-11-zoology2-based/image19.png\"></figure>\n<p><strong>Is there a path forward without quadratic attention?</strong> Finally, we saw with theory and experiments that we need input-dependent sequence aggregation in our architecture to solve the proposed multi-query associative recall (MQAR) as efficiently as attention. Again, attention computes Softmax(QK<sup>T</sup>), where Q and K are functions of the input data <em>u</em>, to determine how to mix tokens. Convolutions use a fixed filter, defined by the model weights, across diverse inputs. </p>\n<h3>Section 1. Introducing the <code>Based</code> Models<a id=\"user-content-section-1-introducing-the-based-models\" target=\"_blank\"></a></h3>\n<p>We now introduce Based, a new approach to subquadratic sequence modeling. Motivated by the findings above, we set out to test a simple hypothesis: convolutions and attentions seem naturally complementary; if we combine their capabilities, can we get a simple architecture that outperforms both? </p>\n<p>To do so, we build <code>Based</code> around two key primitives: (1) short gated-convolutions and (2) spiky linear attentions. Intuitively, the standard convolutions in (1) are great for modeling local dependencies and settings where we might not expect to need associative recall (think building up <a href=\"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Morpheme\" target=\"_blank\">morphemes</a> [i.e., units of meaning] from individual tokens, similar to how in vision we build up higher-level features from neighboring pixels). Meanwhile, the linear attentions in (2) enable <code>Based</code> to do associative recall, e.g., by recovering the global “look-up” inductive bias of standard attention <sup id=\"user-content-fnref-1\"><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2023-12-11-zoology2-based#fn-1\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">1</a></sup>.</p>\n<p>As we’ll see, the combination of both enables <code>Based</code> to achieve high quality sequence modeling while remaining *fully sub-quadratic*. Aside from its aesthetic appeal, we’ll further see that keeping everything sub-quadratic unlocks other niceties of Based, such as its high-throughput generation capabilities via no KV cache. </p>\n<p>Finally, for those of you that insist on architectural purity, you’ll be pleased to find that we can also unify the modules of <code>Based</code> as two sides of the same architectural block. Under this view, each layer of <code>Based</code> can be viewed as a gated convolution, depending on how we parameterize the filter weights. </p>\n<p>In the remainder of this post, we’ll first go over the gated convolutions and linear attentions of <code>Based</code> under their intuitive interpretations for local sequence modeling and global look-ups / associative recall. We’ll then provide a short overview of the unified view of Based. We’ll finally present our <code>Based</code> evaluations, discussing its high-quality language modeling and high-throughput generation capabilities. </p>\n<h4>Building Block 1: A Simple Short Gated Convolution<a id=\"user-content-building-block-1-a-simple-short-gated-convolution\" target=\"_blank\"></a></h4>\n<p>To build <code>Based</code> models, we start with short convolutions, as we like their subquadratic efficiency and their ability to model local interactions. As a review, these standard short convolutions have fixed-length filters directly defined by model weights. By “sliding” these filters over an input sequence, we thus only scale linearly in sequence length. Furthermore, because these filter values *are* neural net weights, by training the layers we directly learn the local relations between neighboring tokens as neural net parameters.  </p>\n<p>Inspired by recent works and our own results showing improved performance with gating (especially on non-AR slices where some gated convolutions even outperformed attention), we further make these layers gated. Under first principles, this allows us to better model settings where neighboring token interactions are still somewhat context-specific; the “gate” modulates the weights based on its input token. </p>\n<div><pre><code class=\"language-python\"><span># No gating, relation defined by fixed k1, k2, k3 for all x</span>\ny3 <span>=</span> k1 <span>*</span> x3 <span>+</span> k2 <span>*</span> x2 <span>+</span> k1 <span>*</span> x1\n\n<span># Gating, relation dynamically modulated by x3</span>\ng3 <span>=</span> g <span>*</span> x3\ny3 <span>=</span> <span>(</span>g3 <span>*</span> k1<span>)</span> <span>*</span> x3 <span>+</span> <span>(</span>g3 <span>*</span> k2<span>)</span> <span>*</span> x2 <span>+</span> <span>(</span>g3 <span>*</span> k1<span>)</span> <span>*</span> x1\n</code></pre></div>\n<p>However, unlike these works, we leave the long-range modeling to the linear attention layers we describe next. In this way, we let convolutions do what they do best, and avoid the complexities of trying to get them to scale to longer sequences <sup id=\"user-content-fnref-2\"><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2023-12-11-zoology2-based#fn-2\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">2</a></sup>.</p>\n<p>In our experiments, we use two sets of filters of length 3 and 128. This lets us capture both higher and lower frequency features within local contexts (i.e., token patterns that repeat a lot versus those that occur more sparsely). </p>\n<div><pre><code class=\"language-python\">v <span>=</span> conv1D<span>(</span>u<span>)</span>          <span>//</span> <span>filter</span> size <span>3</span> <span>(</span><span>\"short\"</span><span>)</span>\nv <span>=</span> F<span>.</span>SiLU<span>(</span>v<span>)</span>          \nv <span>=</span> conv1D<span>(</span>v<span>)</span>          <span>//</span> <span>filter</span> size <span>128</span>\nw <span>=</span> Linear<span>(</span>u<span>)</span>          <span>//</span> projection on dimension\nw <span>=</span> F<span>.</span>SiLU<span>(</span>w<span>)</span>\ny <span>=</span> v <span>*</span> w              <span>//</span> gating\n</code></pre></div>\n<h4>Building Block 2: A Spiky Linear Attention<a id=\"user-content-building-block-2-a-spiky-linear-attention\" target=\"_blank\"></a></h4>\n<p>Next, we need a simple way to solve AR (and MQAR) sub-quadratically. We already know that attention is great at this; the softmax between queries and keys enables a kind of “look-up” perfect for doing this context-based recall that we’re after. </p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2023-12-11-zoology2-based/image24.png\"></figure>\n<p>However, even the latest-and-greatest versions of attention like <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.08691\" target=\"_blank\">FlashAttention2</a> are quadratic in time over sequence length (and further won’t enable our high-throughput generation discussed later on). </p>\n<p>So how do we enable this attention-like behavior while remaining subquadratic? We revisit the great ideas in *linear attention*. On our visit though, we add in a simple twist inspired by recovering this “look-up” capability that seems so important for associative recall (at this point it’s really just haunting us).</p>\n<p><strong>Linear attention - all you need?</strong></p>\n<p>We start by reviewing a really cool idea from <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2006.16236\" target=\"_blank\">Katharopoulos et al. 2020</a>. In this seminal linear attention paper, the authors note that if we take standard attention, but remove the softmax around our query-dot products, the resulting outputs can be computed in linear time! </p>\n<p>Just computing these scaled query-key dot products alone doesn’t seem to work, so folks usually further transform the queries and keys with element-wise “feature maps” <span><span><span><math>ϕ\\phi</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8889em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span>ϕ</span></span></span></span></span>. The key challenge is picking which feature maps to use. </p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2023-12-11-zoology2-based/image22.png\"></figure>\n<p>Digging into the efficiency a bit, by rearranging which matrix multiplications we compute first, we can compute attention in O(ndd’) time and space for sequences of length n, if queries and keys are size d’, and values are size d. </p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2023-12-11-zoology2-based/image25.png\"></figure>\n<p>Now this is great, because typically head dimensions are much smaller than the sequence length. However, there does lie a rub. </p>\n<p>Unfortunately, when we tried existing feature maps, we found they too struggled to learn associative recall. This was perhaps surprising, given the theoretical guarantees of past inspiring methods designed to approximate the exponential function in standard attention (e.g., random fourier features from <a href=\"https://papers.nips.cc/paper_files/paper/2007/hash/013a006f03dbc5392effeb8f18fda755-Abstract.html\" target=\"_blank\">Rahimi &#x26; Recht</a>, 2007, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2009.14794\" target=\"_blank\">Choromanski et al.</a>, 2020). Inspecting the actual linear attention maps or weights, we found that in practice, without scaling up head dimension the actual attention weights produced were much more uniform (higher entropy) than softmax, i.e., “less spiky”. Intuitively, this is bad because this prevents us from paying “attention” to tokens of interest versus, exactly what we want attention to do for AR!</p>\n<table><thead><tr><th></th><th></th><th></th><th></th><th></th><th></th><th></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td></td><td>Softmax </td><td>1 + ELU</td><td>ReLU</td><td>Performer</td><td>cosFormer</td><td>Taylor Exp (Based)</td></tr><tr><td>Acc (%)</td><td>100.0 (0.00)</td><td>17.07 (0.58)</td><td>10.88 (1.32)</td><td>15.14 (1.27)</td><td>15.82 (0.48)</td><td>100.0 (0.00)</td></tr></tbody></table>\n<p>Table 2. Associative Recall (AR) task performance for different linear attentions. Mean accuracy % (and stdevs) over 5 seeds. Previewing a bit, we make a Based linear attention that solves AR just like standard softmax attention!</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2023-12-11-zoology2-based/image16.png\"></figure>\n<p><strong>The Taylor Exponential - high-school calculus is all you need</strong></p>\n<p>In the midst of trying to get these prior methods to work better, we came across a surprisingly simple solution: approximating the exponential with a Taylor series. In short, by computing <span><span><span><math>ϕ\\phi</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8889em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span>ϕ</span></span></span></span></span> such that <span><span><span><math>ϕ(q)ϕ(k)T≈exp⁡(qkT)\\phi(q) \\phi(k)^T \\approx \\exp (q k^T)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.0913em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>ϕ</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">q</span><span>)</span><span>ϕ</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span><span><span>)</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8413em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">T</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>≈</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1.0913em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>exp</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">q</span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8413em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">T</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span>, we were able to compute attentions with similar spikiness to standard softmax, at head dimensions small enough to remain relatively efficient. </p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:40%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2023-12-11-zoology2-based/image18.png\"></figure>\n<p>In slightly more detail, the linear attention algorithm above is a bit like a “reverse” kernel trick. Instead of trying to come up with a function <span><span><span><math>ff</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8889em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10764em\">f</span></span></span></span></span> such that <span><span><span><math>f(qkT)=ϕ(q)ϕ(k)Tf (q k^T) = \\phi(q) \\phi(k)^T</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.0913em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10764em\">f</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">q</span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8413em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">T</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>)</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1.0913em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>ϕ</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">q</span><span>)</span><span>ϕ</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span><span><span>)</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8413em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">T</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> (saving us from computing a higher-dimensional dot product; over the head dimensions), we want to come up with a function <span><span><span><math>ϕ\\phi</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8889em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span>ϕ</span></span></span></span></span> such that computing <span><span><span><math>ϕ(q)ϕ(k)T=f(qkT)\\phi(q) \\phi(k)^T = f(q k^T)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.0913em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>ϕ</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">q</span><span>)</span><span>ϕ</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span><span><span>)</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8413em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">T</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1.0913em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10764em\">f</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">q</span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8413em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">T</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> (saving us from computing a large outer product; over the sequence length).</p>\n<p>Exactly computing <span><span><span><math>f=exp⁡f = \\exp</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8889em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10764em\">f</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.625em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span>exp</span></span></span></span></span> with <span><span><span><math>ϕ\\phi</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8889em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span>ϕ</span></span></span></span></span>’s requires <em>infinite</em>-dimensional features; no good. But with just the second-order Taylor series, where <span><span><span><math>exp⁡^(x)=1+x+x2/2\\hat{\\exp}(x) = 1 + x + x^2 / 2</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"><span style=\"top:-3em\"><span style=\"height:3em\"></span><span>exp</span></span><span style=\"top:-3em\"><span style=\"height:3em\"></span><span style=\"left:-0.25em\"><span>^</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.1944em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span><span>(</span><span>x</span><span>)</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>1</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>+</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6667em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>x</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>+</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1.0641em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>2</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>/2</span></span></span></span></span>, we can make this tractable! Our feature maps simply exploit the linearity of the dot products, where we first compute the feature maps for each Taylor series order before summing their dot products.</p>\n<p>In pseudocode:</p>\n<div><pre><code class=\"language-python\"><span># Compute zero-order terms</span>\nqk0 <span>=</span> <span>1</span>  <span># 1</span>\n\n<span># Compute first-order terms</span>\nq   <span>=</span> <span>[</span>q1<span>,</span> <span>.</span><span>.</span><span>.</span><span>,</span> qd<span>]</span>\nk   <span>=</span> <span>[</span>q1<span>,</span> <span>.</span><span>.</span><span>.</span><span>,</span> qd<span>]</span>\nqk1 <span>=</span> <span>(</span>q <span>*</span> k<span>)</span><span>.</span><span>sum</span><span>(</span><span>)</span>  <span># qk</span>\n\n<span># Compute second-order terms</span>\nq <span>=</span> <span>[</span>q1 <span>*</span> q1<span>,</span> <span>.</span><span>.</span><span>.</span><span>,</span> q1 <span>*</span> qd<span>,</span> <span>.</span><span>.</span><span>.</span><span>,</span> qd <span>*</span> q1<span>,</span> <span>.</span><span>.</span><span>.</span> qd <span>*</span> qd<span>]</span>  <span># flattened qq^T</span>\nk <span>=</span> <span>[</span>k1 <span>*</span> k1<span>,</span> <span>.</span><span>.</span><span>.</span><span>,</span> k1 <span>*</span> kd<span>,</span> <span>.</span><span>.</span><span>.</span><span>,</span> kd <span>*</span> k1<span>,</span> <span>.</span><span>.</span><span>.</span> kd <span>*</span> kd<span>]</span>  <span># flattened kk^T</span>\nqk2 <span>=</span> <span>0.5</span> <span>*</span> <span>(</span>q <span>*</span> k<span>)</span><span>.</span><span>sum</span><span>(</span><span>)</span>  <span># (qk)^2 / 2</span>\n\n<span># 2nd-order Taylor exp: 1 + qk + (qk)^2 / 2</span>\ny <span>=</span> qk0 <span>+</span> qk1 <span>+</span> qk2  \n</code></pre></div>\n<p>As above, if queries and keys are originally size <span><span><span><math>d’d’</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span>d</span><span>’</span></span></span></span></span>, then the resulting dot products take <span><span><span><math>O(d’2)O(d’^2)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.0641em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span><span>(</span><span>d</span><span><span>’</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>2</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> time and compute. The overall linear attention then takes <span><span><span><math>O(ndd’2)O(n d d’^2)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.0641em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span><span>(</span><span>n</span><span>dd</span><span><span>’</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>2</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> time and space. In practice, we simply choose a smaller head dimension for queries and keys to make this trade off favorable for more sequences. As shown below, we find this retains better-than-Transformer modeling quality with d = 16 and d = 24 in the model results below, making us 16x and 4x faster than naïve attention for seq len = 4096, (128x and 32x faster at seq len 32k if we’re counting).</p>\n<h3>All your <code>Based</code> are gated convolutions<a id=\"user-content-all-your-based-are-gated-convolutions\" target=\"_blank\"></a></h3>\n<p>While the hybrid view of <code>Based</code> above conveys its modeling quality, for the architectural purists we can also interpret the short convolutions and linear attentions as two instantiations of the same (generalized) gated convolution block.</p>\n<p>Note that a convolution takes in two input signals  and u (the \"kernel\" and \"input\") and produces an output signal y, denoted as <span><span><span><math>y=K∗uy = K * u</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.625em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">y</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">K</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>∗</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span>u</span></span></span></span></span>. The unified <code>Based</code> parameterizes overall kernel  K via learnable short filter <span><span><span><math>ff</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8889em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10764em\">f</span></span></span></span></span> queries <span><span><span><math>qq</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.625em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">q</span></span></span></span></span>, and keys <span><span><span><math>kk</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span></span></span></span></span>, and parameterizes inputs <span><span><span><math>uu</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span>u</span></span></span></span></span> as values <span><span><span><math>vv</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span></span></span></span></span>, where <span><span><span><math>qq</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.625em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">q</span></span></span></span></span>, <span><span><span><math>kk</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span></span></span></span></span>, and <span><span><span><math>vv</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span></span></span></span></span> are all linear projections of layer inputs <span><span><span><math>xx</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span>x</span></span></span></span></span>.</p>\n<p><span><span><span><math>y[n]=(q[n]∑i=0nf[n−1−i]k[i]v[i])y[n] = (q[n] \\sum_{i=0}^{n} f[n - 1 - i]k[i]v[i])</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">y</span><span>[</span><span>n</span><span>]</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1.104em;vertical-align:-0.2997em\"></span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">q</span><span>[</span><span>n</span><span>]</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span style=\"position:relative;top:0em\">∑</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8043em\"><span style=\"top:-2.4003em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>i</span><span>=</span><span>0</span></span></span></span><span style=\"top:-3.2029em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>n</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2997em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10764em\">f</span><span>[</span><span>n</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>−</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>1</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>−</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>i</span><span>]</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span><span>[</span><span>i</span><span>]</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span><span>[</span><span>i</span><span>])</span></span></span></span></span></p>\n<p>Where for <span><span><span><math>y=K∗uy=K*u</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.625em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">y</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">K</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>∗</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span>u</span></span></span></span></span>, let <span><span><span><math>K[n]=(q[n]∑i=0nf[n−1−i]k[i])K[n] = (q[n] \\sum_{i=0}^n f[n - 1 - i]k[i])</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.07153em\">K</span><span>[</span><span>n</span><span>]</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1.104em;vertical-align:-0.2997em\"></span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">q</span><span>[</span><span>n</span><span>]</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span style=\"position:relative;top:0em\">∑</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8043em\"><span style=\"top:-2.4003em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>i</span><span>=</span><span>0</span></span></span></span><span style=\"top:-3.2029em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>n</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2997em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10764em\">f</span><span>[</span><span>n</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>−</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.7278em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>1</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>−</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>i</span><span>]</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span><span>[</span><span>i</span><span>])</span></span></span></span></span> and <span><span><span><math>u[i]=v[i]u[i] = v[i]</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>u</span><span>[</span><span>i</span><span>]</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span><span>[</span><span>i</span><span>]</span></span></span></span></span>.</p>\n<p>For our first short convolution block. We set the first K filter values as learnable weights, setting the rest of the signal to <span><span><span><math>00</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>0</span></span></span></span></span>'s:\n<span><span><span><math>[f1,…,fk,0,…,0][f_1, …, f_k, 0, …, 0]</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>[</span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10764em\">f</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.1076em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>1</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>…</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10764em\">f</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3361em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.1076em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>0</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>…</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>0</span><span>]</span></span></span></span></span>\nWe \"ignore\" all queries and keys (setting them to ones or the equivalent constants for multiplicative identities), and set the value projection to the identity matrix, computing inputs u as the layer's inputs (model's hidden states).</p>\n<p>For our second linear attention block. For basic linear attention, we set the filter f to be the ones vector n:\n<span><span><span><math>[1,…,1][1, …, 1]</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>[</span><span>1</span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>…</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>1</span><span>]</span></span></span></span></span></p>\n<p>It's a bit weird at first, but this just highlights that vanilla linear attention does not permit position-dependent modeling. To add this in, as a nice connection to relative positional embeddings such as ALiBi, we can also cover these cases and include positional dependence by instantiating the filter as positional embeddings, e.g.,\n<span><span><span><math>[e−1,e−2,…,e−n][e^{-1}, e^{-2}, …, e^{-n}]</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.0641em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>[</span><span><span>e</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>−</span><span>1</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span>e</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>−</span><span>2</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>…</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span>e</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7713em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>−</span><span>n</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>]</span></span></span></span></span>\nto dictate some exponential decay. We then learn the query, key, and value projections.</p>\n<p>In this sense, linear attention is also a gated convolution. The dot products of pairwise queries and keys, each a projection of the original layer inputs, act as an input-dependent \"long convolution\" filter (stretching all the way back to the beginning of the sequence, <em>e.g.</em> when computing attention weights <span><span><span><math>ai=∑j=1iqikja_i = \\sum_{j = 1}^i q_i k_j</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.5806em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span>a</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1.4004em;vertical-align:-0.4358em\"></span><span><span style=\"position:relative;top:0em\">∑</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.9646em\"><span style=\"top:-2.4003em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span><span>=</span><span>1</span></span></span></span><span style=\"top:-3.2029em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.4358em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">q</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0359em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03148em\">k</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0315em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2861em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>). After multiplying with f (optionally enabling relative positional encoding), these then \"gate\" or interact with the layer's inputs via the values (the layer inputs transformed by the value projections, ala <span><span><span><math>vj=xjWvv_j = x_j W_v</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7167em;vertical-align:-0.2861em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0359em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2861em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.9694em;vertical-align:-0.2861em\"></span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2861em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">W</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.1514em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.1389em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">v</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> and <span><span><span><math>yi=∑j=1iqikjvjy_i = \\sum_{j = 1}^i q_i k_j v_j </math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.625em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">y</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0359em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1.4004em;vertical-align:-0.4358em\"></span><span><span style=\"position:relative;top:0em\">∑</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.9646em\"><span style=\"top:-2.4003em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span
