style=\"top:-2.011em\"><span style=\"height:3.816em\"></span><span><span>⎣</span></span></span><span style=\"top:-3.158em\"><span style=\"height:3.816em\"></span><span style=\"height:1.816em;width:0.6667em\"><svg xmlns=\"http://www.w3.org/2000/svg\" width=\"0.6667em\" height=\"1.816em\" style=\"width:0.6667em\" viewBox=\"0 0 666.67 1816\" preserveAspectRatio=\"xMinYMin\"><path d=\"M319 0 H403 V1816 H319z M319 0 H403 V1816 H319z\"></path></svg></span></span><span style=\"top:-5.6111em\"><span style=\"height:3.816em\"></span><span><span>⎡</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:2.45em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span><span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:2.98em\"><span style=\"top:-5.8275em\"><span style=\"height:3.6875em\"></span><span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>0</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"top:-4.6275em\"><span style=\"height:3.6875em\"></span><span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>1</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"top:-2.7675em\"><span style=\"height:3.6875em\"></span><span><span><span>⋮</span><span style=\"border-right-width:0em;border-top-width:1.5em;bottom:0em\"></span></span></span></span><span style=\"top:-1.5675em\"><span style=\"height:3.6875em\"></span><span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.1514em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>n</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:2.48em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:2.95em\"><span style=\"top:-2.011em\"><span style=\"height:3.816em\"></span><span><span>⎦</span></span></span><span style=\"top:-3.158em\"><span style=\"height:3.816em\"></span><span style=\"height:1.816em;width:0.6667em\"><svg xmlns=\"http://www.w3.org/2000/svg\" width=\"0.6667em\" height=\"1.816em\" style=\"width:0.6667em\" viewBox=\"0 0 666.67 1816\" preserveAspectRatio=\"xMinYMin\"><path d=\"M263 0 H347 V1816 H263z M263 0 H347 V1816 H263z\"></path></svg></span></span><span style=\"top:-5.6111em\"><span style=\"height:3.816em\"></span><span><span>⎤</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:2.45em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:5.46em;vertical-align:-2.48em\"></span><span><span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:2.95em\"><span style=\"top:-2.011em\"><span style=\"height:3.816em\"></span><span><span>⎣</span></span></span><span style=\"top:-3.158em\"><span style=\"height:3.816em\"></span><span style=\"height:1.816em;width:0.6667em\"><svg xmlns=\"http://www.w3.org/2000/svg\" width=\"0.6667em\" height=\"1.816em\" style=\"width:0.6667em\" viewBox=\"0 0 666.67 1816\" preserveAspectRatio=\"xMinYMin\"><path d=\"M319 0 H403 V1816 H319z M319 0 H403 V1816 H319z\"></path></svg></span></span><span style=\"top:-5.6111em\"><span style=\"height:3.816em\"></span><span><span>⎡</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:2.45em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span><span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:2.98em\"><span style=\"top:-5.8275em\"><span style=\"height:3.6875em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10764em\">f</span><span>(</span><span><span>a</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>0</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>)</span></span></span><span style=\"top:-4.6275em\"><span style=\"height:3.6875em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10764em\">f</span><span>(</span><span><span>a</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>1</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>)</span></span></span><span style=\"top:-2.7675em\"><span style=\"height:3.6875em\"></span><span><span><span>⋮</span><span style=\"border-right-width:0em;border-top-width:1.5em;bottom:0em\"></span></span></span></span><span style=\"top:-1.5675em\"><span style=\"height:3.6875em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10764em\">f</span><span>(</span><span><span>a</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.1514em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>n</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>)</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:2.48em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:2.95em\"><span style=\"top:-2.011em\"><span style=\"height:3.816em\"></span><span><span>⎦</span></span></span><span style=\"top:-3.158em\"><span style=\"height:3.816em\"></span><span style=\"height:1.816em;width:0.6667em\"><svg xmlns=\"http://www.w3.org/2000/svg\" width=\"0.6667em\" height=\"1.816em\" style=\"width:0.6667em\" viewBox=\"0 0 666.67 1816\" preserveAspectRatio=\"xMinYMin\"><path d=\"M263 0 H347 V1816 H263z M263 0 H347 V1816 H263z\"></path></svg></span></span><span style=\"top:-5.6111em\"><span style=\"height:3.816em\"></span><span><span>⎤</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:2.45em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></div>\n<p>This famous matrix has determinant <span><span><span><math>∏i=0n(a0−ai)\\prod_{i=0}^{n} (a_0 - a_i)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.104em;vertical-align:-0.2997em\"></span><span><span style=\"position:relative;top:0em\">∏</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8043em\"><span style=\"top:-2.4003em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>i</span><span>=</span><span>0</span></span></span></span><span style=\"top:-3.2029em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>n</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2997em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>(</span><span><span>a</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>0</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>−</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span><span>a</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>i</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> which is non-zero whenever all the points are distinct. If you’re a linear algebra nerd, you can see that the condition number may be a bit high if we’re not careful about how we pick points–more on that later.</p>\n<p>Why is the value representation useful for us? Well imagine we had two value representations of the polynomials <span><span><span><math>f(aj)f(a_j)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.0361em;vertical-align:-0.2861em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10764em\">f</span><span>(</span><span><span>a</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2861em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span>  and <span><span><span><math>g(aj)g(a_j)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.0361em;vertical-align:-0.2861em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">g</span><span>(</span><span><span>a</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2861em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> at the same points. Then, note that:</p>\n<p><span><span><span><math>h(aj)=f(aj)g(aj)h(a_j) = f(a_j)g(a_j)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.0361em;vertical-align:-0.2861em\"></span><span>h</span><span>(</span><span><span>a</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2861em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>)</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:1.0361em;vertical-align:-0.2861em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10764em\">f</span><span>(</span><span><span>a</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2861em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>)</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">g</span><span>(</span><span><span>a</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3117em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:0em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2861em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span></p>\n<p>This is a much simpler multiplication: it’s pointwise. Now to exactly reconstruct <span><span><span><math>hh</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6944em\"></span><span>h</span></span></span></span></span>, we need to evaluate <span><span><span><math>ff</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8889em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10764em\">f</span></span></span></span></span> and <span><span><span><math>gg</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.625em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">g</span></span></span></span></span> at <span><span><span><math>n+mn + m</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6667em;vertical-align:-0.0833em\"></span><span>n</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>+</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.4306em\"></span><span>m</span></span></span></span></span> points (more later).</p>\n<blockquote>\n<p>Multiplying polynomials can be much faster if they are in the value representation.</p>\n</blockquote>\n<p>So how do we use this? Well this is where the Fast Fourier Transform comes in!</p>\n<h1>Converting from Coefficients to Values and Back with the FFT</h1>\n<p>Note that we get to pick the evaluation points. What do we want from those points? Well ideally, we’d like that the transformation is maximally stable. The best we can hope for is that all the eigenvalues have norm as close to 1 as possible. This is where our friend the <a href=\"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Root_of_unity\" target=\"_blank\">roots of unity</a> come in:</p>\n<p><span><span><span><math>Wn=e2iπ/nW_n = e^{2i\\pi/n}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8333em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">W</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.1514em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.1389em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>n</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.888em\"></span><span><span>e</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.888em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>2</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.03588em\">iπ</span><span>/</span><span>n</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:40%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2023-12-11-conv-tutorial/fifth_roots.png\"><p>Visualization of roots of unity for n = 5, courtesy Wikipedia.</p></figure>\n<p>These characters are great! So we form the Vandermonde with them, and notice that <span><span><span><math>V(Wn0,Wn1,…,Wnn−1)V(W_n^0, W_n^1, \\dots, W_n^{n-1})</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.0641em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.22222em\">V</span><span>(</span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">W</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"><span style=\"top:-2.453em;margin-left:-0.1389em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>n</span></span></span><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>0</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.247em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">W</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"><span style=\"top:-2.453em;margin-left:-0.1389em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>n</span></span></span><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>1</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.247em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>…</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">W</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"><span style=\"top:-2.453em;margin-left:-0.1389em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>n</span></span></span><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>n</span><span>−</span><span>1</span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.247em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> has a very special name–it’s the discrete fourier transform (DFT) matrix. More on that later, but observe that it’s now possible to go back and forth with condition number that is effectively 1.</p>\n<p>Now the magic: The DFT can be implemented not by this matrix but by a series of great algorithms, perhaps most famously the Cooley-Tukey algorithm that computes <span><span><span><math>DfD_f</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.9694em;vertical-align:-0.2861em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">D</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3361em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.0278em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10764em\">f</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2861em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> in time <span><span><span><math>O(nlog⁡n)O(n \\log n)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span><span>(</span><span>n</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>lo<span style=\"margin-right:0.01389em\">g</span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>n</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> using a classic divide-and-conquer algorithm. This is the fast fourier transform (FFT). This gives us a really simple algorithm:</p>\n<ol>\n<li>Take the FFT of the sequence and the filter.</li>\n<li>Point wise multiply.</li>\n<li>Take the inverse FFT.</li>\n</ol>\n<p>This is an <span><span><span><math>O(nlog⁡n)O(n \\log n)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span><span>(</span><span>n</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>lo<span style=\"margin-right:0.01389em\">g</span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>n</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> algorithm. Pretty great! We worked hard to fuse this algorithm and make it efficient on modern hardware – check out <a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2023-11-13-flashfftconv\" target=\"_blank\">FlashFFTConv</a> for a primer there.</p>\n<h1>Causality in Transforms</h1>\n<p>One particular issue where this is important is causality. Basically the idea is that the value of a sequence element <span><span><span><math>x[j]x[j]</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span>x</span><span>[</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.05724em\">j</span><span>]</span></span></span></span></span> should depend only on elements that come earlier in the sequence (and not on later ones, as is common in so-called bidirectional models). These causal transformations have become really popular because of GPT’s left-to-right prediction models:</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:60%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2023-12-11-conv-tutorial/transformer_next_token.png\"><p>Left-to-right next token prediction with language models.</p></figure>\n<p>Now, observe that multiplying polynomials gets this for free! If we multiply a polynomial by convolution then notice that degree k only depends on degrees of the input that are smaller than it (and the filter, which isn’t strictly speaking necessary)!</p>\n<p>There’s a catch. Recall from earlier that if we multiply a polynomial of degree d and m together we can get a polynomial of degree d+m. This means we have more coefficients than you might expect. This becomes important when we start using the FFT to do convolutions.</p>\n<p>It turns out that there are a few things we can do with those extra terms, and they correspond to different convolution operations we can compute:</p>\n<ul>\n<li><strong>Make the sequence longer.</strong> We don’t typically do this in machine learning models because we want to keep the sequence length the same per layer, but we could do this.</li>\n<li><strong>Throw them away.</strong> This is equivalent to operating \"mod <span><span><span><math>xnx^n</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6644em\"></span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6644em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>n</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>\", if you’re a math nerd. But this is often done.</li>\n<li><strong>Wrap those coefficients around.</strong> Here we set <span><span><span><math>xn=1x^{n} = 1</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6644em\"></span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6644em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>n</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>1</span></span></span></span></span> and so <span><span><span><math>xn+mx^{n+m}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7713em\"></span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.7713em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>n</span><span>+</span><span>m</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> gets added to <span><span><span><math>xnx^{n}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6644em\"></span><span><span>x</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6644em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>n</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>. These are sometimes called circular convolutions.</li>\n</ul>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2023-12-11-conv-tutorial/convolution_lti_three_options.gif\"><p>Three options for what to do when multiplying polynomials, and what it means for the resulting convolution.</p></figure>\n<p>Thus, to make fourier models GPT-like, we need to adopt the “make it longer” or “chop it off” version of convolutions above (we choose the latter)--not the circular convolution. However, for BERT-style models we might be able to use the wrap-around model. For example, M2-BERT and DiffuSSM use the wrap-around properties to compute a bidirectional convolution, by using a kernel that’s twice as long as the input.<sup id=\"user-content-fnref-1\"><a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2023-12-11-conv-tutorial#fn-1\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">1</a></sup></p>\n<h1>What's Next</h1>\n<p>This is really just a first primer into convolutions and their connections to polynomials. The nice thing is – once we’ve made this connection, we can bring the tools of polynomial theory to bear on understanding these ML layers:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>In <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.12109\" target=\"_blank\">Monarch Mixer</a>, we used this connection to figure out how to make Monarchs causal.</li>\n<li>In some work to release this week, Simran and Sabri take this polynomial view to the extreme, and show how polynomial circuits can reason about what is computable with convolutions.</li>\n</ul>\n<p>This blog is a series of surveys and tutorials on building blocks for AI Systems, associated with an upcoming NeurIPS keynote. If you like this style of work and want to learn more, check out the community we’re building over on this <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/aisys-building-blocks\" target=\"_blank\">GitHub</a>, and come on in!</p>\n<p>Dan Fu: <a href=\"mailto:danfu@cs.stanford.edu\" target=\"_blank\">danfu@cs.stanford.edu</a></p>\n<div>\n<hr>\n<ol>\n<li id=\"user-content-fn-1\">The pure circular version could lead to potentially even faster convolutions based on the discrete cosine transform which is 4x faster since it’s real and it wraps around naturally.<a href=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2023-12-11-conv-tutorial#fnref-1\" class=\"\" target=\"_blank\">↩</a></li>\n</ol>\n</div>,We’ve been writing a series of papers (1, 2, 3) that have at their core so-called long convolutions, with an aim towards enabling longer…,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2023-12-11-conv-tutorial,<a href=\"https://cs.stanford.edu/~chrismre/\">Chris Ré</a>, <a href='https://danfu09.github.io/'>Dan Fu</a>.,2026-07-22T18:37:38.379Z,2023-12-11T07:00:00.164Z,[{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2023-12-11-conv-tutorial/convolution_edge.gif\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2023-12-11-conv-tutorial/convolution_lti_continuous.gif\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2023-12-11-conv-tutorial/fifth_roots.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2023-12-11-conv-tutorial/transformer_next_token.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2023-12-11-conv-tutorial/convolution_lti_three_options.gif\",\"type\":\"photo\"}],163778663386687488,1211296081845936176,FlashFFTConv: Efficient Convolutions for Long Sequences with Tensor Cores,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2023-11-13-flashfftconv,<blockquote>\n<p>Convolution models with long filters now have state-of-the-art reasoning abilities in many long-sequence tasks, from long-range language modeling to audio analysis and DNA modeling. But they lag behind the most optimized Transformers in wall-clock time. A major bottleneck is the Fast Fourier Transform (FFT), which allows long convolutions to run in <span><span><span><math>O(Nlog⁡N)O(N \\log N)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>lo<span style=\"margin-right:0.01389em\">g</span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> time in sequence length <span><span><span><math>NN</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span></span></span></span></span> but has poor hardware utilization. We propose FlashFFTConv, a new algorithm for efficiently computing the FFT convolution on GPUs. FlashFFTConv speeds up convolutions by up to 7.93x over PyTorch and achieves up to 4.4x speedup end-to-end. Starting at sequence length 2K, FlashFFTConv starts to match the performance of FlashAttention-v2 – and outperforms it for longer sequences, achieving up to 62% MFU.</p>\n</blockquote>\n<p><strong>Paper:</strong> FlashFFTConv: Efficient Convolutions for Long Sequences with Tensor Cores. Daniel Y. Fu*, Hermann Kumbong*, Eric Nguyen, Christopher Ré. <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2311.05908\" target=\"_blank\">arXiv link</a>.</p>\n<p><strong>Try it now:</strong> <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/flash-fft-conv\" target=\"_blank\">https://github.com/HazyResearch/flash-fft-conv</a></p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2023-11-13-flashfftconv/logo.png\"><p>FlashFFTConv: Efficient Convolutions for Long Sequences with Tensor Cores</p></figure>\n<p>Over the past few years, we’ve seen convolutional sequence models take the world of long-sequence modeling by storm. From <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2111.00396\" target=\"_blank\">S4</a> and follow-ups like <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2208.04933\" target=\"_blank\">S5</a> and <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2203.14343\" target=\"_blank\">DSS</a>, to gated architectures like <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.12109\" target=\"_blank\">Monarch Mixer</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2212.10544\" target=\"_blank\">BiGS</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2302.10866\" target=\"_blank\">Hyena</a>, and <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2206.13947\" target=\"_blank\">GSS</a>, to hybrid architectures like <a href=\"https://github.com/facebookresearch/mega\" target=\"_blank\">Mega</a>, <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2212.14052\" target=\"_blank\">H3</a>, and <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2209.12951\" target=\"_blank\">Liquid-S4</a> – long convolutional models have had a major impact and are here to stay.</p>\n<p>These models are exciting because they are sub-quadratic in sequence length – thanks to the <a href=\"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Convolution_theorem\" target=\"_blank\">FFT convolution algorithm</a>, they can be computed in <span><span><span><math>O(Nlog⁡N)O(N \\log N)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span><span>(</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span>lo<span style=\"margin-right:0.01389em\">g</span></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> time in sequence length <span><span><span><math>NN</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span></span></span></span></span>:</p>\n<div><pre><code class=\"language-python\"><span>def</span> <span>conv</span><span>(</span>u<span>,</span> k<span>)</span><span>:</span>\n    u_f <span>=</span> torch<span>.</span>fft<span>.</span>fft<span>(</span>u<span>)</span>\n    k_f <span>=</span> torch<span>.</span>fft<span>.</span>fft<span>(</span>k<span>)</span>\n    y_f <span>=</span> u_f <span>*</span> k_f\n    <span>return</span> torch<span>.</span>fft<span>.</span>ifft<span>(</span>y_f<span>)</span>\n</code></pre></div>\n<p>Compare this against Transformers, which have compute that grows in <span><span><span><math>O(N2)O(N^2)</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.0641em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span><span>(</span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8141em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>2</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> in sequence length <span><span><span><math>NN</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span></span></span></span></span>, and you’ve got something that scales better than attention! We’re especially excited about the downstream applications that are being enabled by these new architectures, like audio generation from <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2202.09729\" target=\"_blank\">raw waveforms</a> and DNA modeling directly from <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.15794\" target=\"_blank\">base pairs</a>.</p>\n<p>But despite this asymptotically better scaling, these convolutions can actually be <em>slower</em> than the most optimized Transformers! That’s because – as is often the case in computer science, the big-O scaling is better, but the constants hiding behind the big-O notation are significant. Why do this work now?</p>\n<blockquote>\n<p>ML workloads have different bottlenecks than traditional FFT workloads, and modern hardware like GPUs has different characteristics than the hardware that was available when traditional FFT algorithms were designed. This necessitates a different approach to the FFT.</p>\n</blockquote>\n<p>We have developed FlashFFTConv, a new algorithm for efficiently computing FFT convolutions on GPUs that builds on a set of <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2212.14052\" target=\"_blank\">insights</a> we’ve been <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2302.06646\" target=\"_blank\">studying</a> over the past two years.</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2023-11-13-flashfftconv/banner.png\"><p>FlashFFTConv uses a Monarch decomposition to fuse the steps of the FFT convolution and use tensor cores on GPUs.</p></figure>\n<p>The main insight of our work is that a Monarch decomposition of the FFT allows us to fuse the steps of the FFT convolution – even for long sequences – and allows us to efficiently use the tensor cores available on modern GPUs.</p>\n<p>In this rest of this blog post, we’ll go over the key bottlenecks behind the FFT convolution, and then discuss how the Monarch decomposition allows us to overcome those bottlenecks – if we balance the FLOPs and I/O correctly. Then we’ll end with some highlights from our evaluation.</p>\n<p>As always, you can check out our <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2311.05908\" target=\"_blank\">paper</a> for more details, such as a deep dive into how FlashFFTConv enables exploration of new sparse primitives for convolutions. Check out our <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/flash-fft-conv\" target=\"_blank\">code</a> to try it out yourself – as well as our kernel for the fastest depthwise convolutions for short kernels (7x faster than Conv1D!).</p>\n<h1>Bottlenecks of the FFT Convolution in ML</h1>\n<p>If you’re familiar with the FFT, you’re probably asking yourself – <em>Why do we even need a specialized algorithm for the FFT convolution? Didn’t we solve this problem ages ago?</em></p>\n<p>There’s a grain of truth to this – the FFT algorithm has been described as <a href=\"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fast_Fourier_transform#:~:text=the%20most%20important%20numerical%20algorithm%20of%20our%20lifetime\" target=\"_blank\">the most important numerical algorithm of our lifetime</a>, and people have been optimizing it for ages. The FFT algorithm that you were taught in class, and the one that you get from <a href=\"https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=spUNpyF58BY\" target=\"_blank\">YouTube</a> explainers, dates back to <a href=\"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cooley%E2%80%93Tukey_FFT_algorithm\" target=\"_blank\">1965</a> (or <a href=\"https://www.cis.rit.edu/class/simg716/Gauss_History_FFT.pdf\" target=\"_blank\">1805</a> if you go back to Gauss). But this narrative hides a great deal of complexity behind the FFT <a href=\"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fast_Fourier_transform#Other_FFT_algorithms\" target=\"_blank\">algorithms</a> that are used in practice, from <a href=\"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bailey%27s_FFT_algorithm\" target=\"_blank\">Bailey’s FFT</a> algorithm, to <a href=\"https://eng.libretexts.org/Bookshelves/Electrical_Engineering/Signal_Processing_and_Modeling/Fast_Fourier_Transforms_(Burrus)/06%3A_Winograd's_Short_DFT_Algorithms/6.02%3A_Winograd_Fourier_Transform_Algorithm_(WFTA)\" target=\"_blank\">Winograd FFT</a>, <a href=\"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rader%27s_FFT_algorithm\" target=\"_blank\">Rader’s algorithm</a>, and many <a href=\"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fast_Fourier_transform#Other_FFT_algorithms\" target=\"_blank\">others</a>. The key behind all these developments is that they’re closely tied to the <strong>compute</strong> and <strong>memory</strong> paradigms of the day.</p>\n<p>For example, there was a time when multiplying numbers was more expensive than adding them -- so the Winograd FFT algorithm tries to minimize multiplication. There’s even a <a href=\"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hexagonal_fast_Fourier_transform\" target=\"_blank\">Hexagonal FFT</a> designed for data sampled on hexagonal grids!</p>\n<p>So what are the bottlenecks in ML hardware? There are two critical trends to understand, which can you read from two charts:</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:40%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2023-11-13-flashfftconv/memory_hierarchy.png\"><p>The GPU memory hierarchy, and bandwidth between different layers.</p></figure>\n<p>First – there are order-of-magnitude differences in bandwidth between reading from different sections of the GPU memory hierarchy.</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:80%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2023-11-13-flashfftconv/matmul_flops.png\"><p>FLOPs available for matmul (green and yellow) compared to general arithmetic (red and blue).</p></figure>\n<p>Second – matrix-matrix multiply (the green and yellow lines) is a lot faster than general floating point operations. And the trend is accelerating – matrix-matrix multiply was 8x faster than general arithmetic on V100 in 2018, but is now <strong>16x</strong> faster on A100 and H100.</p>\n<blockquote>\n<p>On A100 and H100, multiplying two 16x16 matrices in tensor cores is 16x faster than doing it one operation at a time using general arithmetic operations.</p>\n</blockquote>\n<p>These trends bring two key bottlenecks with them:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>Compute has improved much faster than memory I/O – so I/O is now a major bottleneck.</li>\n<li>Matrix-matrix multiply is significantly faster than general floating point operations.</li>\n</ul>\n<p>So what does that mean for FFT algorithms? It’s now much more important to <strong>carefully account for I/O</strong> than it is to purely count FLOPs, and it’s critical to <strong>use matrix multiply operations</strong> whenever we can.</p>\n<h1>Addressing these Bottlenecks with the Monarch Decomposition</h1>\n<p>In FlashFFTConv, we address these bottlenecks with a Monarch decomposition of the FFT. In brief, the Monarch decomposition allows us to do two things:</p>\n<ul>\n<li>It allows us to break the FFT into <strong>matrix-matrix multiply operations</strong>, which lets us use tensor cores.</li>\n<li>It allows us to <strong>fuse</strong> multiple steps of the FFT convolution together, even for long sequences.</li>\n</ul>\n<h2>Monarch for Matrix-Matrix Multiply</h2>\n<p>Here’s a simple illustration of the Monarch FFT decomposition:</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2023-11-13-flashfftconv/monarch_decomposition.png\"><p>Monarch decomposition of the FFT. Also known as Bailey's FFT, or the four-step FFT.</p></figure>\n<p>Given an FFT of length <span><span><span><math>N=N1N2N = N_1N_2</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6833em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.8333em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.109em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>1</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.109em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>2</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>, the Monarch decomposition lets us compute the FFT by reshaping the input into an <span><span><span><math>N1xN2N_1 x N_2</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8333em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.109em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>1</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>x</span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.109em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>2</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>, compute the FFT on the columns, adjust with the outputs, compute the FFT on the rows, and then transpose the output.</p>\n<p>But the FFT operation itself can be expressed as a matrix-matrix multiply operation! So given this mathematical formulation, we can break up the FFT convolution into a series of matrix-matrix multiply operations:</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:50%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2023-11-13-flashfftconv/conv_as_matmul.png\"><p>FFT convolution using Monarch decomposition.</p></figure>\n<p>What we’re doing here is taking Fourier matrices <span><span><span><math>FN1,FN2F_{N_1}, F_{N_2}</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.9334em;vertical-align:-0.2501em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">F</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3283em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.1389em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3173em\"><span style=\"top:-2.357em;margin-left:-0.109em;margin-right:0.0714em\"><span style=\"height:2.5em\"></span><span><span>1</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.143em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2501em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>,</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.1667em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">F</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3283em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.1389em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3173em\"><span style=\"top:-2.357em;margin-left:-0.109em;margin-right:0.0714em\"><span style=\"height:2.5em\"></span><span><span>2</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.143em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.2501em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>, computing the FFT over the “columns” or “rows” of the input via matrix-matrix multiply, and adjusting with pointwise operations in between. This means that we can use much <strong>faster</strong> FLOPs than you might otherwise find in traditional FFT algorithms!</p>\n<h2>Recursion to Reduce I/O</h2>\n<p>The Monarch decomposition buys us something else – it allows us to fuse more operations into a single kernel.</p>\n<ul>\n<li>The key bottleneck is the <strong>size of SRAM</strong> – we can only fit around 32K half-precision (complex) values in SRAM at a time. So if the sequence is longer than 32K, we need to cache intermediate outputs to HBM.</li>\n<li>The Monarch decomposition gives us a tool to attack this – higher-order decompositions require us to <strong>store less of the input in SRAM</strong> at any given time.</li>\n</ul>\n<p>Higher-order decompositions are also nice because they reduce the total amount of FLOPs that we need to compute – a second-order (2-way) decomposition requires <span><span><span><math>O(N3/2)O(N^{3/2})</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.138em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span><span>(</span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.888em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>3/2</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span> FLOPs, third-order <span><span><span><math>O(N4/3)O(N^{4/3})</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:1.138em;vertical-align:-0.25em\"></span><span style=\"margin-right:0.02778em\">O</span><span>(</span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.10903em\">N</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.888em\"><span style=\"top:-3.063em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span><span>4/3</span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span><span>)</span></span></span></span></span>, etc.</p>\n<p>But there’s also a <strong>tradeoff</strong> here: on modern GPUs, the tensor cores are designed to multiply two 16x16 matrices – so if your matrices are too small, they’re inefficient on tensor cores. For example, using a three-way decomposition for an FFT of length <span><span><span><math>4096=16∗16∗164096 = 16*16*16</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>4096</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>16</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>∗</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>16</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>∗</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>16</span></span></span></span></span> is fine, but using it for an FFT of length <span><span><span><math>1024=8∗8∗161024 = 8 * 8 * 16</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>1024</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span><span>=</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2778em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>8</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>∗</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>8</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span><span>∗</span><span style=\"margin-right:0.2222em\"></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.6444em\"></span><span>16</span></span></span></span></span> is going to be slow.</p>\n<p>We can model this with a simple cost model that takes both compute and I/O into account:</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:center;padding-top:0\"><img style=\"width:100%\" src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2023-11-13-flashfftconv/cost_model.png\"><p>Cost of various different order-<span><span><span><math>pp</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.625em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span>p</span></span></span></span></span> FFT convolutions for different sequence lengths.</p></figure>\n<ul>\n<li>You can see that for short sequences (shorter than 4K), an order-2 decomposition is fine, since it allows us to break the FFT down into 16x16 matmuls – but quickly grows more expensive for longer sequences.</li>\n<li>Meanwhile, higher-order decompositions can’t fully use the tensor cores for short sequences – but better asymptotic scaling kicks in for longer sequences.</li>\n<li>You can also see the kink in the orange line that arises from the SRAM limit – the 4th-order Monarch allows us to break past the SRAM limit!</li>\n</ul>\n<h1>Speeding Up Convolutional Models with FlashFFTConv</h1>\n<p>So what does this change in algorithm buy you? Faster convolutions, and faster convolutional sequence models! We’ll present some brief highlights here, but check out <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2311.05908\" target=\"_blank\">our paper</a> for more details!</p>\n<h3>Faster End-to-End Models</h3>\n<p>We benchmark end-to-end speedup with FlashFFTConv. This table shows throughput in sequences per second, between PyTorch and FlashFFTConv on a single H100. FlashFFTConv speeds up convolutional sequence models end-to-end – up to 4.4X for HyenaDNA!</p>\n<table><thead><tr><th align=\"right\"><strong>Model (size, seqlen)</strong></th><th align=\"center\"><strong>PyTorch</strong></th><th align=\"center\"><strong>FlashFFTConv</strong></th><th align=\"center\"><strong>Speedup</strong></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td align=\"right\"><strong>M2-BERT-base (110M, 128)</strong></td><td align=\"center\">4480</td><td align=\"center\"><strong>8580</strong></td><td align=\"center\">1.9x</td></tr><tr><td align=\"right\"><strong>Hyena-s-4K (155M, 4K)</strong></td><td align=\"center\">84.1</td><td align=\"center\"><strong>147</strong></td><td align=\"center\">1.7x</td></tr><tr><td align=\"right\"><strong>HyenaDNA (6M, 1M)</strong></td><td align=\"center\">0.69</td><td align=\"center\"><strong>3.03</strong></td><td align=\"center\">4.4x</td></tr></tbody></table>\n<p>Check out the <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/flash-fft-conv/tree/main/examples\" target=\"_blank\">examples</a> in our code base for examples of how to adapt these models for FlashFFTConv. FlashFFTConv has already been integrated into a number of research codebases, and we’ll be excited to announce a number of new models that were trained with FlashFFTConv, coming out soon!</p>\n<h3>Speedups over FlashAttention-v2</h3>\n<p>Thanks to these optimizations, convolutional language models are now starting to match the most optimized Transformers end-to-end, even for sequences as short as 2K.</p>\n<p>Here, we compare the throughput in toks/ms, and FLOP utilization of Hyena running FlashFFTConv compared to FlashAttention-v2 for some 3B-equivalent models across sequence lengths:</p>\n<table><thead><tr><th align=\"right\"><strong>Model</strong></th><th align=\"center\"><strong>2K</strong></th><th align=\"center\"><strong>8K</strong></th><th align=\"center\"><strong>16K</strong></th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td align=\"right\"><strong>GPT-2.7B, FA-v2 Throughput</strong></td><td align=\"center\">33.8</td><td align=\"center\">27.8</td><td align=\"center\">21.6</td></tr><tr><td align=\"right\"><strong>Hyena-2.7B, FlashFFTConv Throughput</strong></td><td align=\"center\"><strong>35.2</strong></td><td align=\"center\"><strong>35.2</strong></td><td align=\"center\"><strong>32.3</strong></td></tr><tr><td align=\"right\"><strong>FA-v2 FLOP Util</strong></td><td align=\"center\">65.7</td><td align=\"center\">72.1</td><td align=\"center\">78.5</td></tr><tr><td align=\"right\"><strong>FlashFFTConv Flop Util</strong></td><td align=\"center\">62.3</td><td align=\"center\">61.9</td><td align=\"center\">56.5</td></tr><tr><td align=\"right\"><strong>Speedup</strong></td><td align=\"center\">1.1x</td><td align=\"center\">1.3x</td><td align=\"center\">1.5x</td></tr></tbody></table>\n<p>FlashFFTConv achieves up to 62% MFU – and is faster than FlashAttention-v2 end-to-end due to a lower FLOP cost!</p>\n<h1>What's Next</h1>\n<p>You can try out FlashFFTConv <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/flash-fft-conv/tree/main\" target=\"_blank\">now</a> - we welcome feedback about where it’s useful, or where it could be further improved. We’re super excited about this intersection of new architectures and new systems algorithms to support them on modern hardware.</p>\n<p>Check out our paper for a deeper dive into the algorithms – and some discussion about how this systems advance can also push the boundaries of ML algorithms, with sparse convolutions that are naturally enabled by FlashFFTConv (we just skip parts of the matrices).</p>\n<p>And watch for more work releasing soon that uses FlashFFTConv!</p>\n<p>If you’re excited by these directions, please reach out – we would love to chat!</p>\n<p>Dan Fu: <a href=\"mailto:danfu@cs.stanford.edu\" target=\"_blank\">danfu@cs.stanford.edu</a>; Hermann Kumbong: <a href=\"mailto:kumboh@stanford.edu\" target=\"_blank\">kumboh@stanford.edu</a></p>,Convolution models with long filters now have state-of-the-art reasoning abilities in many long-sequence tasks, from long-range language…,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2023-11-13-flashfftconv,<a href='https://danfu09.github.io/'>Dan Fu</a>*, <a href=\"https://twitter.com/kumbonghermann\">Hermann Kumbong</a>*, <a href=\"http://erictnguyen.com/\">Eric Nguyen</a>, <a href=\"https://cs.stanford.edu/~chrismre/\">Chris Ré</a>,2026-07-22T18:37:38.378Z,2023-11-13T07:00:00.528Z,[{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2023-11-13-flashfftconv/logo.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1792,\"height\":1024},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2023-11-13-flashfftconv/banner.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2023-11-13-flashfftconv/memory_hierarchy.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2023-11-13-flashfftconv/matmul_flops.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2023-11-13-flashfftconv/monarch_decomposition.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2023-11-13-flashfftconv/conv_as_matmul.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2023-11-13-flashfftconv/cost_model.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"}],163778663386687488,1211296081845936177,A Paradigm Shift in ML Validation: Evaluating Workflows, Not Tasks,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2023-08-21-workflows,<p>It’s no surprise that recent advances in machine learning are starting to change how we think about the role of ML in applied domains. Doctors are using foundation models to <a href=\"https://www.tiktok.com/@mycareact/video/7177374136442834218\" target=\"_blank\">write insurance letters</a> to accelerate approval of life-saving care. Computational biologists are discovering new protein structures <a href=\"https://www.deepmind.com/blog/alphafold-reveals-the-structure-of-the-protein-universe\" target=\"_blank\">faster than ever</a>. And teachers can finally <a href=\"https://www.pbs.org/newshour/education/some-educators-embrace-chatgpt-as-a-new-teaching-tool\" target=\"_blank\">design their lesson plans</a> without sacrificing multiple nights of sleep.</p>\n<p><em>But as applications of ML become more pervasive, how do we make sure these models work the way we want them to? Are our current validation methods sufficient?</em></p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:left\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2023-08-21-workflows/overview.png\" style=\"width:100%\"><p>Workflows, like those in medical imaging, are composed of multiple tasks. The quality of later (<em>downstream</em>) stages of the workflow (e.g. analysis, treatment) depend on earlier (<em>upstream</em>) stages (e.g. how well images were acquired and reconstructed). Historically, machine learning benchmarks have been built for many of these tasks in silo, and thus, are not designed to evaluate methods as a part of an end-to-end workflow.</p></figure>\n<blockquote>\n<p>Over the past few years, we have been quite interested in how we can reimagine ML validation to be focused on user workflows rather than individual tasks. In this blog, we’ll dive into the bottlenecks and exciting directions we’ve explored for designing datasets &#x26; benchmarks for workflow-centric validation, particularly in medical imaging.</p>\n</blockquote>\n<h2>A brief history of ML validation</h2>\n<p>Over the years, the ML community <a href=\"https://blog.neurips.cc/2021/04/07/announcing-the-neurips-2021-datasets-and-benchmarks-track/\" target=\"_blank\">has rallied</a> around building open-source benchmarks spanning a wide array of applications. While data and tasks across these domains are heterogenous, ML benchmarks have helped unify them by standardizing tasks and validation procedures. This standardization has simplified comparison and made it easier to build and test ML methods in applied domains.</p>\n<p>However, in practice, these tasks are just small steps of much larger pipelines. For example, a doctor could use ML-assisted tools for detecting pneumonia in an X-ray. But it is the <em>combination</em> of this detection with other analyses (patient history, lab results, previous scans, etc.) that determines how the doctor treats the patient.</p>\n<p>The problem is that evaluating performance on a specific task is often not a good indicator of the quality of end-to-end workflows (e.g. in <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2203.06823.pdf\" target=\"_blank\">medical imaging</a>). In these scenarios, we would want validation protocols that not only evaluate if a model is good for some task(s), but also evaluate how the model impacts a user’s end-to-end workflow.</p>\n<blockquote>\n<p>One way to make ML systems useful in practice is to shift ML validation from being <strong>task-centric</strong> to being <strong>workflow-centric</strong>.</p>\n</blockquote>\n<h2>What is a workflow?</h2>\n<p>A <em><strong>workflow</strong></em> can be (loosely) defined as a sequence of tasks (x → y → z) performed to extract information relevant to a user’s decision making. In this paradigm, tasks are just small components that can be composed together as part of a larger end-to-end process. These tasks can be performed by different <em>operators</em> (e.g. ML models, human experts), each with different operating capabilities (e.g. speed, quality) and error profiles. Naturally, the quality of <em>downstream tasks</em>, tasks performed at later stages of the workflow, is dependent on the quality of <em>upstream tasks</em>, those performed at earlier stages. For example, in the medical imaging workflow shown above, the quality of the reconstructed image will influence the quality of our analysis, and thus, the treatment we administer to the patient.</p>\n<p>Workflows are typically designed to give outputs that are interpretable by the user (e.g. a domain expert). This allows the user to make decisions based on metrics that they care about. For example, in cancer care, oncologists would be interested in identifying traits such as the size of a tumor, how far the cancer has spread (i.e. metastasis), and what treatment solution (chemotherapy, radiation, etc.) would be most potent.</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:left\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2023-08-21-workflows/metrics.png\" style=\"width:100%\"><p>Standard metrics used for benchmarking ML algorithms for knee MRI segmentation — e.g. Dice (DSC) and average symmetric surface distance (ASSD) — are poorly correlated (p) with clinically-relevant biomarkers, such as <span><span><span><math>T2T_2</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8333em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">T</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.1389em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>2</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span> estimates in MRI.</p></figure>\n<h2>Why can evaluating workflows help?</h2>\n<p>Workflow-centric validation shifts the focus from evaluating the ML model to evaluating how successful a user’s workflow would be if they were to use the model. In other words, we care about the quality of the full workflow rather than only the quality of a single or a few tasks. This perspective can give us a realistic working template of how ML models can be <em>used</em> successfully in practice.</p>\n<p>By thinking of a workflow as a series of tasks, we can also study error propagation — i.e. how do errors in upstream tasks impact quality among downstream tasks? This paradigm allows us to better understand how these errors affect the overall quality of the workflow. For example, we can ask: “How much do errors in my reconstructed image affect the treatment I prescribe to my patient?”</p>\n<p>Relatedly, understanding user workflows is also critical for understanding what outcomes matter to the ML users. Historically, ML benchmarking has evolved to focus on generalist metrics, like accuracy, AUROC, or Dice, but these metrics <a href=\"https://pubs.rsna.org/doi/full/10.1148/ryai.2021200078\" target=\"_blank\">may not be correlated</a> with the user-relevant outcomes. This makes it difficult to gauge how ML models will translate from benchmarks to the quality metrics users care about in the real world.</p>\n<h2>Designing workflow-centric datasets and benchmarks in medical imaging</h2>\n<p>In light of these challenges, we have been quite excited by how we can enable workflow-centric validation, specifically in the context of how we design datasets &#x26; benchmarks. We believe there are two fundamental principles that differentiate workflow-centric datasets from standard ML datasets:</p>\n<ol>\n<li>The dataset mirrors user workflows. This means data is collected from different stages of the workflow and models can be built to benchmark at each stage independently and on the workflow as a whole.</li>\n<li>The dataset allows us to measure the quality of user-relevant outcomes. In other words, we can define new metrics that are more aligned with what the user cares about.</li>\n</ol>\n<p>A few years ago, we applied these principles when building the <a href=\"https://github.com/StanfordMIMI/skm-tea\" target=\"_blank\">Stanford Knee MRI with Multi-Task Evaluation</a> (SKM-TEA [<em>pronounced: skim tē</em>]) dataset, a 1.6TB dataset of clinical knee MRIs consisting of raw data, reconstructions, segmentations, detection, and classification labels. By including ground truth labels at different stages of the MRI pipeline, SKM-TEA enables model developers to benchmark MRI workflows in an end-to-end manner, from acquisition, to reconstruction, to analysis. The dataset also complements standard ML benchmarking metrics with a new clinical-relevant metric based on a workflow for estimating <span><span><span><math>T2T_2</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8333em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">T</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.1389em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>2</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>, a biomarker that can help characterize biochemical properties of tissues.</p>\n<figure style=\"text-align:left\"><img src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2023-08-21-workflows/skmtea.png\" style=\"width:100%\"><p>An example knee MRI workflow in SKM-TEA for quantifying biochemical properties of tissues and detecting pathology. Raw data must first be reconstructed into images (red arrow), which then can be used for downstream analysis tasks like pathology detection/classification (green arrow) and tissue segmentation (blue arrow). Reconstructed images and tissue segmentations can also be used to estimate regional <span><span><span><math>T2T_2</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8333em;vertical-align:-0.15em\"></span><span><span style=\"margin-right:0.13889em\">T</span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.3011em\"><span style=\"top:-2.55em;margin-left:-0.1389em;margin-right:0.05em\"><span style=\"height:2.7em\"></span><span><span>2</span></span></span></span><span></span></span><span><span style=\"height:0.15em\"><span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></span>, a clinically-relevant biomarker for measuring biochemical changes in different regions of the knee.</p></figure>\n<p>Since the release of SKM-TEA, we’ve been very excited to see new methods being benchmarked in an end-to-end way. New MRI reconstruction methods are being designed to be <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2304.12507\" target=\"_blank\">aware</a> of downstream tasks, and model developers are <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.11238\" target=\"_blank\">starting to use</a> biomarker-based metrics as a routine endpoint for evaluation. With this dataset, we’ve even been able to run <a href=\"https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1002/jmri.28365\" target=\"_blank\">larger retrospective studies</a> for looking at diverse clinical biomarkers across different stages of the MRI pipeline.</p>\n<h2>What are the challenges?</h2>\n<p>Despite the advantages of workflow-centric validation, building and adopting these benchmarks is not easy, for many reasons:</p>\n<ol>\n<li><strong>Workflows aren’t public:</strong> In many cases, clear definitions or code for workflows are seldom available. If they are available, they are rarely built for public use (e.g. not modular, no clear entry points). Like traditional benchmarking, this makes it challenging for creating transparent understanding where the gaps in performance are.</li>\n<li><strong>Analysis workflows are not standardized:</strong> In any domain, users have particular, often personalized, workflows, which makes it difficult to standardize what workflows warrant benchmarking. In fact, these workflows are so unstandardized that the FDA (and other regulatory agencies) rely on companies asking for ML approval to <a href=\"https://www.fda.gov/media/145022/download\" target=\"_blank\">define their own testing procedure</a>.</li>\n<li><strong>Too much work:</strong> Frankly, validation can be hard to orchestrate. Labeled data for benchmarking each task of a workflow is not always available and is often expensive to collect. Additionally, evaluating models when the workflows are vague and hard to benchmark make it even less appealing to do.</li>\n<li><strong>Metrics <span><span><span><math>≠\\ne</math></span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8889em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span><span><span><span><span><span style=\"height:0.8889em;vertical-align:-0.1944em\"></span><span><span><span></span></span></span><span></span></span></span></span></span><span>=</span></span></span></span></span></span> qualitative utility:</strong> User interaction is inherently a qualitative process, and it’s no different when we think about how users would interact with ML tools. However, current metrics are not designed to capture the nuances of how users would <em>feel</em> when interacting with these ML systems.</li>\n</ol>\n<h2>A path forward</h2>\n<p>Fortunately for us, solutions to these challenges are rooted in actions that the ML community has excelled at:</p>\n<ol>\n<li><strong>Facilitate open-source:</strong> Work with domain experts to develop and distribute their workflows and associated user-informed metrics. This can help establish some user-led consensus on end-to-end workflows where ML methods can be helpful for their fields.</li>\n<li><strong>Make workflows usable</strong>: Build benchmarking suites so that with minimal code, users can get up and running with endpoint-driven workflows. This can reduce the barrier on entry for both ML experts with limited domain knowledge and domain experts with limited ML knowledge.</li>\n<li><strong>Show user utility</strong>: Demonstrate that evaluating models in a workflow-centric way actually leads to downstream outcomes for patients (less cost, better prognosis, etc.). Making these benchmarks visible to the public ensures there is transparency, which is critical for facilitating ML adoption.</li>\n<li><strong>Build interactive tools:</strong> Use user interfaces to ground the qualitative nature of user-model interactions in more quantitative metrics (time to success, rate of retention, etc.). Libraries like <a href=\"https://streamlit.io/\" target=\"_blank\">Streamlit</a> and <a href=\"https://www.gradio.app/\" target=\"_blank\">Gradio</a> have considerably simplified building applications for ML models. More recently, we built <a href=\"https://github.com/HazyResearch/meerkat\" target=\"_blank\">Meerkat</a> to focus on bridging the gap between ML models and data interfaces, which can help with large scale data <a href=\"https://twitter.com/jundesai/status/1638925988003463170\" target=\"_blank\">visualization</a> and <a href=\"https://twitter.com/krandiash/status/1648065372015448066\" target=\"_blank\">exploration</a>, and <a href=\"https://twitter.com/jundesai/status/1664278016011440129\" target=\"_blank\">qualitative validation studies</a> for new ML tools.</li>\n</ol>\n<figure style=\"text-align:left\"><video width=\"100%\"><source src=\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2023-08-21-workflows/reader-study.mp4\" type=\"video/mp4\"><p>Your browser does not support the video tag.</p></video><p>A Meerkat interface for conducting reader studies to evaluate image reconstruction quality.</p></figure>\n<p>We are excited to keep pushing the boundaries of applied ML validation, especially with the rapid proliferation of new application-driven foundation models and their benchmarking. If you’re interested in these directions, please <a href=\"mailto:arjundd@stanford.edu\" target=\"_blank\">reach out</a>!</p>\n<p><strong>Aside:</strong> While this blog post has been about medical imaging, it is far from the only setting where workflow-centric validation can be a huge unlock. We are starting to see similar trends emerging in a variety of different applications in medicine (e.g. <a href=\"https://www.nature.com/articles/s41746-023-00879-8\" target=\"_blank\">analyzing electronic health records</a>), <a href=\"https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.09237\" target=\"_blank\">law</a>, <a href=\"https://www.fda.gov/media/167973/download\" target=\"_blank\">drug discovery</a>, among others. Studying how different fields handle the validation and (at later stages) approval for using this ML tools will be critical for improving validation generally for applied ML domains.</p>\n<h2>Acknowledgements</h2>\n<p>Many thanks to Chris Ré, Shreya Rajpal, Brandon Yang, Karan Goel, Sabri Eyuboglu, Akshay Chaudhari, and Parth Shah for their feedback on this post.</p>,It’s no surprise that recent advances in machine learning are starting to change how we think about the role of ML in applied domains…,https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog/2023-08-21-workflows,<a href=\"https://ad12.github.io/\">Arjun Desai</a>,2026-07-22T18:37:38.377Z,2023-08-21T07:00:00.341Z,[{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2023-08-21-workflows/overview.png\",\"type\":\"photo\",\"width\":1792,\"height\":392},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2023-08-21-workflows/metrics.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2023-08-21-workflows/skmtea.png\",\"type\":\"photo\"},{\"url\":\"https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/static/posts/2023-08-21-workflows/reader-study.mp4\",\"type\":\"video\",\"width\":100}]",
```json
      "id": "163778663386687488",
      "image": null,
      "ownerUserId": null,
      "siteUrl": "https://hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog",
      "title": "Hazy Research Blog",
      "type": "feed",
      "url": "rsshub://stanford/hazyresearch/blog"
    }
  ],
  "url": "hazyresearch.stanford.edu/blog"
}
```
